cuisine: かまど炊きご飯と奈良食材の定食（食堂）
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. Rice is cooked in a traditional wood-fired kamado and the meals center on local Nara rice, vegetables, tofu and miso, but soy sauce and miso typically contain wheat, and some dishes include wheat gluten (麸/fu, e.g. the Yamato beef hot pot with fu). The venue lists "allergy accommodations upon consultation," so a gluten-restricted diner should ask in advance; there is no documented gluten-free menu or dedicated handling for celiac safety.
vegan_detail: Plant-forward but not vegan by default. The kitchen leans heavily on Yamato (local Nara) vegetables, tofu, and a kombu-based dashi for the miso soup, which makes a vegetable-centered, partly vegetarian meal realistic. However the rotating set menus also include meat (e.g. Yamato beef hot pot) and fish-based items, and soy sauce/miso are standard. A vegan should ask which sets are free of animal ingredients; accommodation may be possible on request but is not guaranteed or formalized.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市井上町11 鹿の舟
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 奈良県奈良市井上町11; coordinates [135.830917, 34.674202] fall within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: ならまち南端・京終（奈良市井上町）
seats_text: About 20 seats — roughly 10 counter seats facing the open kamado and 10 table seats; no private rooms. (Tabelog describes it as counter-style; the accessibility guide gives 20 total.)
hours_raw: 11:00～16:00（食事は～15:00、ごはんがなくなり次第終了）、水曜定休
closed_days: 水曜日
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and the staff can discuss allergies on request, so a foreign student can manage comfortably with a friendly greeting and a few words of Japanese.
caveats: Lunch only (11:00-16:00, food service until ~15:00) and the kitchen closes once the day's rice runs out, so arrive early. No reservations and counter-heavy seating, so expect a wait at peak times. Menus rotate and include both vegetable/tofu sets and meat dishes, so vegans and gluten-restricted diners must check the day's offerings. Hours note: some older write-ups mention a morning breakfast service, but current official and listing sources show lunch-only 11:00-16:00.

bio.background: 竈 (Kamado) is the dining hall of 鹿の舟 (Shika no Fune), a three-part complex on the southern edge of Naramachi near Kyobate that opened in 2015. It was produced by Yukiko Ishimura, the founder of the long-beloved Nara café and lifestyle shop Kuruminoki (くるみの木), which opened in 1984. Alongside the Kamado dining hall, the complex holds an information center (繭 Mayu) and a café (囀 Saezuri).
bio.specialty: Rice cooked the old way in a wood-fired kamado built into the counter, served as a seasonal set (teishoku) with miso soup, tofu, a vegetable side, and pickles. Documented dishes include nasu dengaku (eggplant with miso), seasonal tempura, nameko-and-greens miso soup, and a Yamato beef hot pot with tofu and wheat gluten.
bio.philosophy: Hyper-local sourcing built around Nara: low-pesticide/organic local rice, Yamato vegetables, neighboring artisan condiments, and Yoshino cedar firewood, served in a calm, light-filled room that pairs rustic kamado cooking with quiet modern design.

anecdotes.text: The kamado sits right at the counter so guests watch and smell rice being cooked over firewood; reviewers describe the local rice as having an unpretentious sweetness and umami that comes through as you chew.
The space was conceived by Yukiko Ishimura of Kuruminoki as part of the 2015 Shika no Fune complex, designed around a 'laid-back yet beautifully modern' worldview in southern Naramachi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Meals are built on miso, soy sauce, and tofu, and at least one set (Yamato beef hot pot) includes wheat gluten (fu), so wheat is present across the standard menu.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menus rotate and include meat (Yamato beef) and fish-based items alongside vegetable and tofu dishes, so a strictly vegan meal is not guaranteed and would need to be confirmed per day.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The venue lists allergy accommodations available upon consultation and offers an English menu.

positives.text: The miso soup and base preparations use a kombu-based dashi rather than fish dashi, making a vegetable-and-tofu-centered meal realistic for those avoiding fish stock.
Cooking centers on local Yamato vegetables, tofu, and kamado-cooked rice, so plant-forward options are a core part of the menu.

cuisine: Tearoom / café — seasonal fruit desserts, toasts on artisan bread, and Yamato tea, inside the Shika no Fune complex
gf_detail: This is a café built around wheat: signature offerings include seasonal toasts on campagne bread, cakes, and brûlée-style desserts with homemade custard. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated prep, or celiac accommodation. Not safe for celiacs; a gluten-sensitive visitor could potentially have a fruit dessert or a pot of Yamato tea but should ask directly.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Desserts feature dairy (homemade custard cream, vanilla ice cream) and the toasts use bread that may contain dairy/egg. A vegan could likely order a pot of organic Yamato tea, and possibly a plain seasonal fruit item if available, but cakes and signature desserts are not vegan. Ask staff about specific ingredients.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市井上町11（鹿の舟内）
geocode_note: Geocoded from 奈良市井上町11 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: ならまち南端・京終（鹿の舟内）
seats_text: Seat count not documented; a tearoom-style café space within the renovated Taisho-era building, with adjacent shelving displaying ceramics and crafts.
hours_raw: 11:30–17:00 (last order 16:30). Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays per the official Saezuri page.
closed_days: Tuesdays and Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A calm, design-conscious tearoom where pointing at the menu and a friendly konnichiwa will get you through; English support is not documented, so learning the names of a couple of desserts or teas helps.
caveats: The provided Tabelog URL currently resolves to an unrelated butcher shop (精肉 肉よし) rather than this café — the café was confirmed via the official Kuruminoki/Shika no Fune site and Nara guide sources. Hours and closed days differ across listings (some show only Wednesday closed, some show Tue+Wed, with last-order times of 15:00 or 16:30); the official Saezuri page (Tue+Wed closed, LO 16:30) was used. Verify on arrival.

bio.chef_name: Ishimura Yukiko (founder of Kurumi no Ki)
bio.background: Saezuri is the tearoom of Shika no Fune, a complex housed in a renovated Taisho-era building near the southern edge of Naramachi in the Kyobate area. The whole project is produced by Kurumi no Ki, the beloved Nara café-and-crafts company founded by Ishimura Yukiko; Shika no Fune also includes a tourist information space (Mayu) and a kamado-cooked rice restaurant (Kamado).
bio.specialty: Seasonal fruit desserts that use whole fruits, signature plates like a fresh banana and nuts brulee with homemade custard and torched caramel, roasted whole apple with vanilla ice cream and caramel in season, seasonal toasts on Kyoto YENTA campagne bread (e.g. zucchini-and-plum, fresh ginger), and organic Yamato tea from the Tsukigase region of Nara.
bio.philosophy: The founder describes the wish behind the name as everyone — visitors and locals alike — riding together in one big boat heading toward the future; the room is designed so that outdoor greenery flows naturally into a bright, unhurried space for enjoying seasonal, Nara-grown ingredients at leisure.

anecdotes.text: The architecture was designed by Nakamura Yoshifumi and the logo by artist Mochizuki Michiharu, part of the carefully curated design identity of the space.
Desserts are built around whole seasonal fruit — 'using whole seasonal fruit to make delicious desserts' — a hallmark of the Kurumi no Ki kitchen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is central to the menu (campagne-bread toasts, cakes), so heavy gluten presence and cross-contact in a small café kitchen should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature desserts use homemade custard cream and vanilla ice cream (dairy/egg), so dessert prep surfaces are not vegan-isolated.

positives.text: Organic Yamato tea from Nara's Tsukigase region is available and is naturally gluten-free and vegan as a drink.
Desserts emphasize whole seasonal fruit, so a fresh-fruit option may be available for restricted diners — ask on the day.

cuisine: オーガニック・ヴィーガン／ベジタリアンカフェ（魚・肉・乳製品・小麦不使用、グルテンフリー有機玄米麺）
gf_detail: The kitchen states all menus are made without wheat/gluten and without additives ("添加物・グルテン不使用"), and the signature noodle dish uses a house-developed 100% organic brown-rice (玄米) noodle that is gluten-free rather than a wheat noodle. Staff develop allergy-compatible menus in-house, so gluten avoidance is a core part of the concept rather than an afterthought. No explicit statement about a dedicated fryer or certified celiac-level cross-contamination controls was found, so highly sensitive celiacs should confirm preparation directly. Not flagged as a formally certified gluten-free facility.
vegan_detail: All menus are vegetarian/vegan-oriented and use no fish, meat, or dairy. Dishes are plant-based: tofu hamburger plate with sprouted brown rice, three vegetable side dishes, salad, seasonal vegetable miso soup, plus brown-rice rice balls, brown-rice noodles, and banana cake. The owner develops vegan, vegetarian, allergy-compatible, and therapeutic ("食事による食事療法") menus independently using Nara-prefecture ingredients and no animal products or additives.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町5-1 井上ビル2F-A・B
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 奈良市東向南町5-1 via GSI; first feature coordinates [135.828918, 34.682663], inside the Nara box. Building is on the 2nd floor above Yamazakiya in the Higashimuki shopping arcade, ~2-3 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station.
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺・ならまち寄り（東向商店街）
seats_text: Seat count not published; small 2nd-floor café-style space.
hours_raw: 09:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00); offers morning, lunch, dinner and takeout. Sources vary (one lists 9:00-22:00, the wellness company page lists 10:00-18:00 reception). Evening hours can vary; reservation recommended.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closing days)
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run organic café where some basic Japanese helps, but the entirely plant-based, wheat-free menu means a vegan or gluten-avoiding foreign student can order comfortably with a few polite words and pointing.
caveats: Hours differ between sources (café 9:00-21:30/22:00 vs. the parent wellness company's 10:00-18:00 reception listing); closing days are irregular, so confirm before visiting. Cash only. Seat count and dedicated-fryer/cross-contamination protocols are not documented, so highly sensitive celiacs should confirm directly.

bio.background: KURURU is an owner-run organic café in the Higashimuki shopping arcade just a few minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station, sitting on the second floor above the long-standing Yamazakiya. It bills itself as a food-therapy specialty café, the cooking arm of a wider wellness operation that also runs a salon, school and studio offering aromatherapy, herbal treatments, foot baths and yoga.
bio.specialty: The most-loved dish is the tofu hamburger plate, served on a single tray with shiso tea, carrot purée, three small vegetable side dishes, salad, sprouted brown rice and a seasonal vegetable miso soup. The other signature is the house-developed additive-free organic brown-rice (玄米) noodle, made from Nara-prefecture pesticide-free brown rice blended with nutrient-dense superfoods and served cold as a noodle soup in spring and summer.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen is built on the idea of healing the body through food: every menu is plant-based and made without fish, meat, dairy, wheat/gluten or additives, using Nara-prefecture ingredients and simple cooking that lets the vegetables speak. The owner develops vegan, vegetarian, allergy-compatible and therapeutic menus in-house, tying the food into a broader well-being and SDGs mission.

anecdotes.text: The original additive-free brown-rice noodles are made from 100% Nara-prefecture pesticide-free brown rice combined with high-nutrient superfoods, developed through the owner's own research and now shipped nationwide by mail order.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner develops vegan, vegetarian, allergy-compatible and therapeutic meal menus in-house, so the kitchen is set up to accommodate dietary restrictions.

positives.text: All menus are made without fish, meat, dairy, wheat/gluten or additives, removing the most common animal and gluten triggers from the entire kitchen.
The signature noodle dish uses a 100% organic brown-rice noodle instead of wheat noodles, so it is gluten-free by design.

lead: エニー ビー&ビー+コーヒー（ANY B&B + COFFEE）
name_ja: エニー ビー&ビー+コーヒー
name_en: ANY B&B+COFFEE (Enii Bii ando Bii purasu Koohii)
cuisine: Specialty self-roasted coffee cafe with all-day breakfast; homemade granola, hot sandwiches, and gluten-free / vegan sweets; B&B lodging upstairs
gf_detail: Specific items are made dedicated gluten-free and vegan: the house granola and the chia pudding are explicitly free of wheat, dairy, animal products and refined sugar (granola sold as グルテンフリー&ビーガン and described as allergy-friendly, made with oats, walnuts, pumpkin seeds, coconut, canola oil and maple syrup). These are reliable celiac-conscious choices. However, the wider menu includes hot sandwiches (bread = wheat) and the kitchen is shared, so this is a cafe with genuine GF options rather than a dedicated GF kitchen. No documented dedicated fryer or formal cross-contamination protocol; ask staff for the GF items specifically.
vegan_detail: Several clearly vegan items: house granola (no animal products or dairy), chia pudding made with soy milk, and rotating plant-based pastries (some sourced from a nearby vegan/GF bakery, Somi Sweets & Coffee). They also sell plant-based packaged coffee beans. Not a fully vegan cafe though, as cafe lattes use dairy and hot sandwiches may contain animal products; plant milk substitution and the vegan pastries make it very workable for vegans. Confirm milk choice and which pastries are vegan on the day.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西城戸町23-4
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 西城戸町23-4; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: Along Yasuragi-no-michi, midway between Kintetsu Nara and JR Nara stations (about an 8-minute walk from either), near Naramachi
seats_text: 11 seats
hours_raw: 8:00-17:00 (food L.O. 13:00, dine-in L.O. 15:30); official site lists 8am-4pm. Closed Wednesdays and Thursdays.
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: The owners trained as barista and roaster in Melbourne and run an international-minded specialty coffee cafe, so English-speaking foreign students should feel very comfortable ordering here.
caveats: A third-party blog lists this cafe as closed (閉店), but the official website shows active May and June 2026 coffee-bean listings and operating schedules, so it is treated as currently operating. The owners' separate satellite coffee stand (ANY COFFEE BREW BAR inside BONCHI) does appear to have closed; this main cafe/B&B is the operating location. Hours on Tabelog (to 17:00) and the official site (to 16:00) differ slightly; food last order is around 13:00.

bio.background: ANY B&B+COFFEE is run by a husband-and-wife team, Yutaro and Chiharu, who gained their barista and coffee-roasting experience in Melbourne, Australia before returning to Japan and opening the cafe in 2019. The ground floor is a sunlit specialty-coffee cafe and roastery, while the upper floor offers bed-and-breakfast lodging for a single group at a time.
bio.origin: Nara, Japan (opened 2019); owners trained in Melbourne, Australia
bio.specialty: Self-roasted specialty coffee with a focus on bright-but-not-too-light roasts, often featuring African beans from Kenya and Ethiopia; an all-day breakfast built around homemade granola; hot sandwiches; vegan and gluten-free sweets such as chia pudding and rotating plant-based pastries; and a spiced PRANA CHAI made with Ceylon tea and Australian honey.
bio.philosophy: The owners describe their space as a place that weaves together stories between people, prioritizing human connection, individuality and intangible value over things. They are committed to sustainability, using compostable bags, reusing glass bottles, and running zero-waste initiatives alongside their coffee subscription and wholesale work.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's distinctive thick, handleless cups are handmade by a Scottish-born, Melbourne-based ceramic artist named Daisy, a friend of the owner's wife; the pieces are inspired by the Scottish countryside and designed so guests can cradle them for tactile warmth.

Their homemade granola is baked with simple, gentle flavors using oats, walnuts, pumpkin seeds, coconut, canola oil and maple syrup, with no animal products, dairy or refined sugar, making it both gluten-free and vegan and friendly to people with allergies.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The house granola is marketed as gluten-free and vegan and described as suitable for people with allergies, indicating the owners formulate specific products with dietary restrictions in mind.

positives.text: The granola and chia pudding are explicitly made free of wheat, dairy, animal products and refined sugar, giving gluten-free and vegan guests reliable, clearly-labeled options.
Rotating plant-based pastries are offered, some sourced from a nearby vegan and gluten-free bakery, and plant-milk-based drinks are available.

lead: 堀内果実園 奈良三条店
name_ja: 堀内果実園 奈良三条店
cuisine: フルーツパーラー／果樹農家直営フルーツカフェ。季節のフルーツパフェ、フルーツサンド、スムージー、かき氷など
neighborhood: ならまち・三条通り近く／近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約3〜4分
gf_detail: フルーツパーラーで、看板メニューのフルーツサンドは小麦のパンを使用。明示的なグルテンフリー対応や専用調理体制の記載はなく、セリアック対応の店ではない。フルーツそのものやスムージー、パフェの一部は素材的に小麦不使用の可能性があるが、店としての保証はないため要確認。
vegan_detail: 果樹農家直営のフルーツカフェで、フルーツ単体・フレッシュジュース・スムージーなどベジタリアン向きのメニューは多い。ただしパフェには生クリームやアイス、フルーツサンドにはパンと生クリーム（乳）が使われ、完全ビーガン対応の明示はない。乳製品を含まない品目は限られるため、ビーガンの場合は内容を確認する必要がある。
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市角振町23
geocode_note: GSIジオコーディングで「奈良県奈良市角振町23番地」を取得。Nara box内（lat 34.682289, lng 135.827164）。
seats_text: 16席（テーブル席）
closed_days: 無休（特定の定休日の記載なし／年中無休として営業）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, tourist-friendly fruit parlor near Kintetsu Nara Station where ordering is simple and pointing at the picture menu works fine, though staff communication is mainly in Japanese.
caveats: 看板はフルーツパフェ・フルーツサンドで、メニューは旬により変動する。ビーガン・グルテンフリーの明示的な専用対応はなく、該当する場合は来店時に内容確認が必要。座席数は16席と小規模で、観光シーズンは混み合うことがある。

roles: フルーツパーラー直営店
bio.background: 堀内果実園は1903年（明治36年）創業、奈良・吉野の山あいで六代続く果樹農家。吉野の杉・檜などの地域資源を生かして栄養豊かな土づくりを行い、果樹を育ててきた。
bio.specialty: 旬のフルーツを主役にしたフルーツパフェ、フルーツサンド、スムージー、フレッシュジュース。夏はかき氷、秋冬はあたたかいスイーツなど季節替わりのメニュー。ドライフルーツや干し柿、柿バターなどの加工品も手がける。
bio.philosophy: 完熟・最高の状態で収穫した果実を、フレッシュなまま、また加工品として『いろいろなかたちで』届けることを大切にしている。

anecdotes.text: 創業1903年、吉野で六代続く果樹農家が直営するフルーツパーラーで、自園の旬の果物をパフェやフルーツサンド、スムージーで味わえる。
近鉄奈良駅から徒歩圏の三条通り近くにあり、旬の果物が主役のパフェやフルーツサンドが楽しめるフルーツパーラーとして紹介されている。

positives.text: 自園で育てた旬のフルーツやフレッシュジュース・スムージーが中心で、果物そのものを楽しめる品目が多く、ベジタリアン向けの選択肢が豊富。

lead: 吉野本葛 天極堂 奈良本店
name_ja: 吉野本葛 天極堂 奈良本店
cuisine: Yoshino kuzu (arrowroot) specialty house — kuzu mochi, kuzu kiri, kuzu udon, kuzu porridge and other kuzu-based dishes and sweets; regional Nara cuisine and cafe
gf_detail: Kuzu (本葛 / Yoshino arrowroot starch) is itself naturally gluten-free, and several signature items are starch-based: kuzu mochi, kuzu kiri, and kuzu yu. However this is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen. The signature 葛うどん (kuzu udon) is made with thin wheat-flour noodles that have kuzu worked into them, so it is NOT gluten-free, and many savory dishes use dashi and soy-based seasonings. A celiac diner would need to ask carefully and stick to the pure-starch sweets; no documented allergen protocols or dedicated equipment.
vegan_detail: Some sweet items appear plant-based in principle (kuzu mochi, kuzu kiri and kuzu yu are made from kuzu starch and typically served with kinako/brown-sugar syrup), but this is not a vegan establishment. Savory kuzu dishes such as kuzu udon and kuzu porridge are commonly finished with fish dashi, and no vegan certification or ingredient disclosure is documented. A vegan would need to confirm dashi and toppings dish by dish.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市押上町1-6
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.8349, 34.687271] for 奈良県奈良市押上町1; within the central Nara bounding box.
neighborhood: Oshiage-cho, beside the former Todai-ji West Gate (Saidaimon) site, near Nara Park; about a 12-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: 54 seats, including private tatami rooms (up to 8 people); second-floor dining overlooks the former Todai-ji West Gate park
hours_raw: 10:00-19:30 (L.O. 19:00); closed Tuesdays (open and closed the following day if Tuesday falls on a national holiday)
closed_days: Tuesdays (if Tuesday is a national holiday, closed the following day instead)
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming traditional sweets house and cafe near Todai-ji where you can order by pointing at picture menus; a few words of Japanese help, but the relaxed cafe setting makes it easy for a foreign student to visit.
caveats: 葛うどん (kuzu udon) contains wheat noodles and is not gluten-free; savory dishes commonly use fish dashi, so they are not vegan. No documented allergen-handling protocols — confirm directly for celiac or strict vegan needs.

bio.background: Tengyokudo is the dining and confection arm of Inoue Tengyokudo, a Nara kuzu maker established in 1870 that has produced Yoshino honkuzu (traditionally refined arrowroot starch) without interruption ever since. The company controls everything from raw material to finished product. This main store sits in Oshiage-cho beside the former West Gate (Saidaimon) site of Todai-ji, with a second-floor dining room that looks out over the park — ginkgo in autumn and cherry blossoms in spring.
bio.specialty: Signature offerings include freshly made kuzu mochi (made to order and served within about ten minutes, cool on the outside and warm within), kuzu kiri, kuzu yu, and Yoshino udon — smooth thin noodles with kuzu worked into them, finished in a kuzu-thickened sauce. Pure kuzu products and kuzu yu are also sold to take home.
bio.philosophy: The company expresses its outlook as never forgetting gratitude for the blessings of heaven and earth, and wishing to deliver delicious, enriching everyday living to its customers — now and in the future.

anecdotes.text: Kuzu has been prized since ancient times as both medicine and nourishing food; the traditionally made form is called Yoshino honkuzu and is held to have special value.
The kuzu mochi is prepared fresh to order and arrives within about ten minutes, with a contrast of a cool exterior and a warm interior.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen; the house specialty 葛うどん (kuzu udon) is made with wheat noodles, so wheat is handled on site.

positives.text: Pure kuzu (Yoshino arrowroot starch) is naturally gluten-free, and several signature items — kuzu mochi, kuzu kiri and kuzu yu — are starch-based sweets rather than wheat-based.

cuisine: Japanese-style Western (yōshoku)
gf_detail: This is a classic Japanese yōshoku (Western) restaurant whose core dishes are inherently wheat-based and breaded: hamburger steak with demi-glace sauce (flour-thickened), breaded tonkatsu and shrimp fry (panko/wheat coating), and hayashi rice (roux-based). There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer information, and no celiac accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood-focused Western diner; signature items are beef hamburger steak, pork tonkatsu, Yamato beef/pork dishes and shrimp fry. House pudding uses dairy and the tartar sauce/dressings contain animal products. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市公納堂町14
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 奈良県奈良市公納堂町14 to [135.83287, 34.677406], within the central Nara box. Note: the actual address is Kōnōdō-chō (公納堂町), in Naramachi near Sarusawa Pond, not Nishiterabayashi-chō as listed in the target hint.
neighborhood: Naramachi (ならまち), near Sarusawa Pond / Mochiidono, about a 10-15 minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: 23 seats total: 13 table/chair seats plus a tatami room (about 10 seats) overlooking the garden
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (last order). Lunch only — evening service is temporarily suspended. Closed Tuesday and Wednesday (open on public holidays).
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday (open on public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed neighborhood lunch spot in an old townhouse where ordering is simple by pointing at the popular hamburger or shrimp-fry sets, but staff likely speak limited English and it is cash-only, so bring yen and a little patience.
caveats: Lunch only (11:00-15:00); evening service is currently suspended. Reservations are not taken for lunch, so expect to queue at peak times. Cash only. The address is in Kōnōdō-chō, not Nishiterabayashi-chō as in the original target note.

bio.background: Yoshoku Haru opened in October 2011 inside a townhouse said to be more than 120 years old in the historic Naramachi district, just off Sarusawa Pond. The dining space keeps the old machiya character, with Western table seating on one side and a large tatami room that looks out onto a Japanese garden.
bio.specialty: Handmade nostalgic Western dishes: a juicy double-ground hamburger steak served sizzling on an iron plate with demi-glace sauce, often paired with a large whole-head shrimp fry that is crisp enough to eat head and tail; also tonkatsu over hayashi rice and Yamato-beef rice (gyūmeshi). House-made pudding uses milk from Uemura Ranch in Nara, and the kitchen makes an original tartar sauce with carefully chosen Nara pickles and a house soy-based dressing.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen emphasizes local Nara ingredients (Yamato beef, Yamato pork) and aims for comforting food that three generations, from grandparents to grandchildren, can enjoy together with peace of mind.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant occupies a townhouse over 120 years old, with a garden-view tatami room and a corridor leading through the garden to the restroom, giving it the feel of dining in an old family home.
The most talked-about plate is a whole-head shrimp fry so crisply fried that the head is crunchy and even the tail can be eaten, served alongside the iron-plate hamburger.
House-made pudding is made with milk from Uemura Ranch in Nara, and the tartar sauce uses local Nara pickles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are breaded and deep-fried (tonkatsu, whole shrimp fry) in shared oil, and the hamburger and hayashi rice use flour-based sauces, so heavy gluten exposure and cross-contamination should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The restaurant uses a house soy-based dressing; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so soy-derived seasonings here should be treated as containing wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around beef, pork and seafood with dairy (house pudding from Uemura Ranch milk) and egg-based tartar sauce; no separated vegan preparation is documented.

cuisine: Japanese sweets cafe (甘味処) specializing in Yoshino hon-kuzu (arrowroot) desserts — kuzukiri and kuzumochi, made to order
neighborhood: Naramachi (Takamikadomachi), about 10 min walk south of Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高御門町2
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.8293, 34.677406] for 奈良県奈良市高御門町2, within the Nara bounding box.
seats_text: Small machiya interior with both table and counter seating; tatami atmosphere. Exact seat count not documented.
hours_raw: 10:00–17:00; closed Wednesdays and Thursdays (open on holidays)
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, traditional family-run machiya teahouse where English is unlikely, so a foreign student would be most comfortable with a guide or a translation app and a willingness to point at the simple kuzu menu.
gf_detail: The signature kuzukiri and kuzumochi are made from 100% Yoshino hon-kuzu (arrowroot starch), which is naturally gluten-free. However, this is a traditional sweets shop with no documented allergen handling, and some kuzu confections elsewhere are blended with wheat or served with sweetened soy/kinako that may carry gluten. There is no information confirming dedicated equipment or cross-contamination controls, so a celiac diner must ask directly about ingredients and preparation before ordering.
vegan_detail: The core kuzukiri and kuzumochi are plant-based (arrowroot starch served with kuromitsu black-sugar syrup and/or matcha), which can be vegan. But honey, kinako, or dairy/cream may appear in some seasonal items and accompaniments, and ingredients are not documented for vegans. Confirm syrups and toppings before ordering.

bio.background: Sakura is a kuzu sweets shop (甘味処) opened by a kimono merchant (呉服店) whose business dates to the Meiji era, set in the historic Naramachi district near Kintetsu Nara Station.
bio.specialty: Desserts made from 100% rare Yoshino hon-kuzu (true Yoshino arrowroot): kuzukiri (translucent arrowroot noodles) and kuzumochi (arrowroot mochi), served plain or with matcha and kuromitsu syrup.
bio.philosophy: Because true hon-kuzu turns cloudy and changes flavor as time passes, every order is prepared fresh by hand only after the customer orders, preserving the clarity and delicate texture of the arrowroot.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a traditional machiya townhouse roughly 150–160 years old, lending a calm, historic setting in the Naramachi quarter.
The confectionery grew out of a kimono shop founded in the early Meiji period, tying it to Nara's textile and craft heritage.

positives.text: Signature dishes are made from 100% Yoshino hon-kuzu (arrowroot), a naturally gluten-free and plant-based starch, prepared fresh to order.

caveats: Seat count is not documented in available sources. Allergen and cross-contamination practices are not published; GF/vegan diners should confirm ingredients and accompaniments (syrups, kinako, honey) directly. Payment is cash with QR-code payments accepted; credit cards and electronic money are not accepted.

lead: 奈良町豆腐庵 こんどう
name_ja: 奈良町豆腐庵 こんどう
cuisine: Tofu cuisine (yudofu, agedashi, dengaku, yuba) made with 100% domestic soybeans, served in a ~180-year-old registered cultural-property machiya
neighborhood: Naramachi / Nishi-shinya-cho (near Kintetsu Nara Station and JR Kyobate Station)
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The kitchen is tofu- and soy-centered, and set meals include explicitly wheat-based items such as fu (wheat gluten) and dengaku miso, plus dashi and soy sauce that typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or celiac-safe protocol is documented. A celiac traveler would need to ask in detail and should not assume safety.
vegan_detail: Tofu-focused and plant-forward (yudofu, oboro tofu, fresh yuba, okara, ganmodoki), so there is genuine vegan potential, but it is not a vegan restaurant. Set meals are built around dashi and soy sauce that are commonly fish/bonito-based, and items like hirousu/ganmodoki and clear soup may contain fish dashi. No documented vegan menu; a vegan diner must confirm the dashi and broth ingredients before ordering.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西新屋町44
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市西新屋町44 returned [135.829605, 34.676605], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 20 seats: roughly 12 table seats plus around 8 on tatami in private rooms.
hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Fri 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Sat, Sun 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00, dinner by reservation). Closed Tuesdays.
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, traditional tofu-cuisine townhouse where English is unlikely, so a foreign student will be most comfortable arriving with a guide or translation app and a willingness to point at the set menu.
caveats: Hours follow the restaurant's current listing; weekend dinner is by reservation only. An older blog listed closure on both Monday and Tuesday, but current listings show closure on Tuesday only, so confirm before a Monday visit.

bio.background: Naramachi Tofu-an Kondo is the sit-down restaurant of Kondo Tofu Shop, a tofu maker based in Tenri, who opened this place to let visitors taste their tofu cooked many ways. It occupies a roughly 180-year-old townhouse in the old merchant quarter of Naramachi that is registered as a Tangible Cultural Property, complete with an interior courtyard garden.
bio.specialty: Tofu set meals built around yudofu (hot tofu in a soy-milk style broth), oboro tofu eaten with sea salt, dashi soy sauce or yuzu pepper, fresh yuba and oboro sashimi, agedashi tofu, dengaku, okara and hirousu, often finished with a sweet.
bio.philosophy: Cherishing the natural flavor of the ingredient: tofu is made only from carefully selected 100% domestic soybeans (such as Kyushu Fukuyutaka and Hokuriku Enrei) set with mineral-rich Izu Oshima sea nigari, to bring out a deep, mellow bean taste.

anecdotes.text: The historic machiya was used as a filming location for director Naomi Kawase's 2003 film Shara (Sharasoju).
The site notes that tofu first reached Japan via Buddhist monks traveling to Tang-dynasty China during the Nara period, tying the dish to the city's history.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Set meals are documented to include fu (wheat gluten) and dengaku with miso, so wheat is present in the kitchen and in standard courses.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oboro tofu and other dishes are served with dashi soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented, so standard soy sauce (typically containing wheat) should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses include items such as ganmodoki/hirousu and clear soup; the dashi base is not documented as plant-based, so fish/bonito dashi should be assumed unless confirmed.

positives.text: Tofu is made from 100% domestic soybeans and sea nigari with no documented additives, and many courses are plant-based tofu, yuba and vegetable preparations.

cuisine: 茶粥・万葉粥（月替わりの季節野菜と昆布だし白味噌）、柿の葉寿司付きの大和名物膳、参道の甘味処
vegan_detail: The signature 万葉粥 (manyo porridge) is made with Hokkaido kombu dashi and Nara white miso — no fish dashi — so it can be a plant-based option, and there are sweets like Yoshino kuzumochi and matcha. However this is not a vegan kitchen: the popular set meals include saba (mackerel) persimmon-leaf sushi, white miso may not be confirmed dashi-free in every preparation, and there is no formal vegan menu. A vegan would order the plain manyo porridge and sweets and should confirm ingredients when ordering.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. Core offerings contain wheat: the summer 春日素麺 (somen) is wheat noodles, the kuzumochi and other cakes contain wheat, and the persimmon-leaf sushi involves soy. White miso and the seasoning are typical wheat-containing Japanese condiments. There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and a celiac visitor cannot eat safely here. Even the rice porridge risks cross-contact in a shared kitchen serving noodles and cakes.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市春日野町160
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市春日野町160 returned [135.844284, 34.682671], within the central Nara box. Location is on the Kasuga Taisha approach within the shrine grounds.
neighborhood: 奈良公園・春日大社境内
seats_text: About 50 seats total, including roughly 15 seats in the open-air garden surrounded by the Kasuga forest.
hours_raw: 10:30–16:00 (L.O. around 16:00). Closed Mondays in Jan–Mar, Jun–Sep, and Dec; open daily in Apr, May, Oct, and Nov. (Some sources list 10:30–16:30, L.O. 16:00.)
closed_days: Mondays in January–March, June–September and December; open every day in April, May, October and November (peak shrine-visiting months).
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed open-air teahouse on the shrine approach where you can point at the porridge or sweets on a simple menu, but staff likely speak limited English, so a foreign student should be ready to gesture or use a translation app.
caveats: Hours and closed days vary by season and sources differ slightly (10:30–16:00 vs 10:30–16:30); confirm before a special trip. The 春日素麺 is summer-only and 大和名物膳 includes mackerel sushi. No reservations taken.

bio.background: Kasuga Ninaijaya is a garden teahouse set right along the wooded approach to Kasuga Taisha, near the Manyo Botanical Garden, where pilgrims and park visitors have long stopped to rest. The name 荷茶屋 ("carried teahouse") recalls the old custom of vendors who carried tea and refreshments to shrine-goers, and the shop carries that tradition into a quiet spot among the seasonal flowering trees of the Kasuga forest.
bio.origin: On the grounds of Kasuga Taisha, 奈良公園, central Nara.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is 万葉粥 (manyo porridge), a gentle rice porridge simmered in Hokkaido kombu dashi and Nara white miso with monthly-changing seasonal vegetables inspired by the Man'yoshu poetry anthology, made with carefully chosen Hinohikari rice. It anchors the 大和名物膳 set (porridge, two pieces of saba persimmon-leaf sushi, seasonal kuzumochi, a side and pickles), and in summer the shop serves traditional Kasuga 春日素麺 eaten the old way with umeboshi and mustard. Sweets include Yoshino-kuzu kuzumochi, matcha with Yamato tea, and seasonal zenzai and kakigori.
bio.philosophy: A seasonal, place-rooted menu that lets diners taste the changing year — vegetables and dishes rotate month by month, served in a garden where the forest seasons are part of the meal.
roles: teahouse / 甘味処 on the Kasuga Taisha approach

anecdotes.text: The monthly manyo porridge is made with Hokkaido kombu dashi and Nara white miso and premium Hinohikari rice, with handmade in-house accompaniments that rotate each month.
Guests dine in garden seating surrounded by the seasonal flowering trees of the Kasuga forest, making it a favorite rest stop after visiting Kasuga Taisha.
A visitor praised the manyo porridge as 'absolutely delicious,' especially the chilled summer version, and described a welcoming setting where they shared a table with an international couple.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen serves wheat somen noodles and wheat-containing cakes alongside the rice porridge, so shared-equipment cross-contact is likely; there is no gluten-free handling.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The set meals include saba persimmon-leaf sushi (contains soy), and the menu notes wheat in the noodles and cakes; standard Japanese white miso and seasonings apply.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish (saba) sushi is prepared in the same kitchen as the vegetable porridge; while the porridge uses kombu dashi rather than fish dashi, shared preparation means a vegan should ask about cross-contact.

positives.text: The signature manyo porridge is made with kombu dashi and white miso rather than fish stock, so its base broth contains no fish.

cuisine: エスプーマかき氷専門店（甘味処・カフェ）
gf_detail: A kakigori (shaved ice) specialist. Plain shaved ice with fruit syrups can be naturally gluten-free, but the signature dishes use milk/yogurt espuma and varied seasonal syrups/toppings, and there is no documented gluten-free protocol or allergen labeling. Anyone with celiac disease should ask about each component before ordering, as ingredients change seasonally and cross-contact in a small kitchen cannot be ruled out.
vegan_detail: The signature creations are built on dairy: milk espuma and yogurt espuma are central to the menu, so the headline dishes are not vegan. A simple fruit-syrup shaved ice without the dairy foam might be possible, but there is no documented vegan option. Ask staff whether a dairy-free version can be made.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町47
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町47 via GSI; coordinates [135.82869, 34.680351] fall within the central Nara bounding box. Note the shop relocated in 2018 within the Mochiidono shopping street from the original もちいどの夢CUBE (No.12) to its current location at No.47.
neighborhood: もちいどのセンター街・餅飯殿町
seats_text: About 36 seats across counter, tables, and sunken-floor seating in the post-2018 relocated shop (some listings still show the older 24-seat figure).
hours_raw: 10:00-13:00, 14:00-16:50 (Mon, Tue, Wed, Fri); 10:00-13:00, 14:00-17:20 (Sat, Sun); closed Thursdays. Web reservation only, opening from 21:00 the day before.
closed_days: Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, beloved local shop that runs entirely on a same-day-eve web reservation system in Japanese, so a foreign student should book ahead online and be ready to communicate mostly in Japanese.
caveats: Reservations are essentially mandatory: bookings open via the web system from 21:00 the night before, and walk-ins are very limited. Cash only. Seat count and hours have shifted over the years (24 vs 36 seats appear in different listings); the current shop is the 2018 relocation within the same shopping street.

bio.background: Houseki-bako opened in March 2015 inside the Mochiidono Yume CUBE, helping to launch Nara's now-famous kakigori (shaved ice) boom. Demand grew so intense that the original shop had to restrict entry, and in March 2018 it relocated to a roomier space further along the same Mochiidono shopping arcade.
bio.specialty: Espuma kakigori: finely shaved ice crowned with airy foam ("espuma") made from milk or yogurt, layered with seasonal fruit syrups and toppings. The contrast between the delicate ice and the creamy foam is the shop's signature.
bio.philosophy: The owner, Okada-san, shaves the ice for every order himself, believing that the hand doing the shaving directly affects the texture of the finished ice.

anecdotes.text: Okada-san insists on personally shaving the ice for each serving because, in his view, the person shaving changes the texture of the finished dish.
The shop pioneered Nara's kakigori boom after opening in the Mochiidono Yume CUBE in 2015, and was so popular it relocated to a larger 36-seat space within the same arcade in 2018.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature dishes are built on dairy-based milk and yogurt espuma foams, so the core menu is not dairy-free.

positives.text: As a shaved-ice shop, the base is simply ice with fruit syrups, so a non-dairy fruit version may be possible if requested, though this is not documented.

cuisine: Kakigori (shaved ice) and tea cafe — signature espuma (mousse-style) shaved ice using yogurt and seasonal fruit, plus Yamato tea drinks and light cafe plates
caveats: IDENTITY/CUISINE MISMATCH IN SOURCE RECORD: The target name "housekibaco" (ほうせき箱) and address 餅飯殿町47 both unambiguously identify a kakigori (shaved ice) and tea cafe — confirmed by the Kintetsu Naramachi official shop map, Tabelog, and Instagram. The supplied cuisine description (gluten-free/vegan lunch with rice-flour focaccia and a seasonal veggie salad set) does NOT belong to this shop; it matches a different Nara business, "onwa" (オンワ), located at 三条大宮町3-23 near JR Nara Station. This record documents the actual entity named housekibaco at 餅飯殿町47. It is a shaved-ice dessert cafe, NOT a gluten-free/vegan lunch destination.
gf_detail: No gluten-free menu or claims found on any fetched source. It is a kakigori (shaved ice) and tea cafe; shaved ice itself is naturally wheat-free but syrups, toppings (e.g. kinako-dusted matcha items), espuma and any cafe plates have unverified ingredients and there is no documented allergen handling. A celiac would need to ask in person; treat as not confirmed safe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. The signature espuma shaved ice is yogurt-based and milk espuma appears in featured items (e.g. matcha DX with milk espuma), so dairy is central to the menu. No vegan options are documented. Anyone strictly plant-based would need to ask, and most signature items are unsuitable.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町47
geocode_note: GSI address search returned a single feature for 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町47番地 at [135.82869, 34.680351], within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: もちいどのセンター街 / 餅飯殿町 (Mochiidono Center Street), about 8 minutes on foot from Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: 24 seats total (10 counter, one 8-seat large table, two 4-seat tables). Online reservation slots are offered for 1- and 2-person parties.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Wed, Fri 10:00-13:00 / 14:00-16:50; Sat, Sun 10:00-13:00 / 14:00-17:20. Closed Thursdays. (Sources also describe the morning service ending around 11:50-12:50 with afternoon service from 13:00-14:00; times vary by source and seasonal operation.)
closed_days: Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: A hugely popular local shaved-ice cafe that runs almost entirely on a Japanese-language online reservation system opening at 9pm the night before, so a foreign student should be ready to book in Japanese (or have help) and arrive on time for their slot; staff service is in Japanese and it is cash only.

bio.background: Housekibaco is a small, independent shaved-ice and tea shop on Nara's Mochiidono Center Street arcade, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It became one of the shops that helped spark Nara's modern kakigori boom and now draws long waits in season, running on a tight online-reservation system that opens the night before.
bio.specialty: Artisanal kakigori built around airy espuma (a light mousse-like cream made with yogurt) layered over delicately shaved ice and seasonal fruit; signature and seasonal bowls include a yogurt-and-grape (Pione) ice and a 'grown-up' matcha bowl made with Yamato tea matcha syrup, milk espuma and kinako. The shop also pours Yamato tea and serves light cafe plates.
bio.philosophy: The shop highlights local Nara ingredients such as Yamato tea in its ice, presenting kakigori as a year-round craft dessert rather than a summer novelty.

anecdotes.text: The name 'housekibaco' comes from 'houseki,' a Nara-area dialect word meaning a snack or treat, reflecting the shop's idea of kakigori as an everyday delight.
Its signature item is an espuma-style shaved ice using yogurt, praised for an airy, melt-on-the-tongue texture that still keeps a fresh tartness.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop's signature espuma shaved ice is yogurt-based and milk espuma is used in featured bowls, so dairy is integral to the core menu; no vegan options are documented.

cuisine: Showa-retro junkissa serving nostalgic café food and drinks
neighborhood: Kitamachi / Naijihara-cho (a roughly 5-minute walk north from Kintetsu Nara Station along Yasuragi-no-Michi, near the south gate of Nara Women's University)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature dishes are wheat-based — the Kitamachi Napolitan is spaghetti and the dry curry is served with rice but the menu centers on retro café cooking with no documented gluten-free options or allergen accommodation. A celiac traveler should not rely on this kitchen.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. Signature items include sausage, fried egg, parmesan, and dairy-based ice cream (Uemura Farm milk), and there is no documented vegan menu. A flexible vegetarian might find a drink or a simple item, but anyone strict should ask in advance and not count on it.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市内侍原町13
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.826447, 34.68639] for 奈良県奈良市内侍原町13番地; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 12 seats — a small, intimate counter-and-table space.
closed_days: Mondays and Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, owner-run retro coffee house where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed pace go a long way; menus and signage are mainly Japanese, so a foreign student should be comfortable pointing and trying simple phrases.
caveats: Sources differ slightly on the closed days (Tabelog lists Mondays only); the operator-managed listing shows closed Mondays and Thursdays, which is reflected here. Credit cards are not accepted, but PayPay is. Hours are short (11:30 to 18:30) and seating is very limited at 12.

bio.background: Tucked into Nara's old Kitamachi district along the Yasuragi-no-Michi promenade, near the south gate of Nara Women's University, Bardot is a small owner-run coffee house that wears its Showa-era nostalgia proudly. Antique furnishings, 1980s magazines left out to read, and a French touch — red awnings, French flags, and a wall of Brigitte Bardot photos that gives the shop its name — set the mood, while jazz and bossa nova drift through the room.
bio.philosophy: The owners aim to offer guests a leisurely, unhurried stretch of time in an intimate setting, leaning into old-fashioned, nostalgic flavors rather than chasing trends.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is the Kitamachi Napolitan: al dente spaghetti tossed with sausage, onion, bell pepper, and mushrooms in a rich tomato sauce, crowned with a fried egg and finished with parmesan and Tabasco. The café is also known for its soy-sauce ice cream — Uemura Farm ice cream drizzled with a special soy sauce from Oita (with a salt-topped version using Uyuni salt-lake salt) — and a Nara-only 'Kofun' (burial-mound shaped) dry curry. The cafe ole is made with unhomogenized Uemura Farm milk.

anecdotes.text: The shop doubles as a curated little boutique of one-of-a-kind imported French goods, sold alongside the coffee.
Its soy-sauce ice cream has been featured in magazines including SAVVY.
The name and decor pay homage to French actress Brigitte Bardot, with her photos lining the retro interior.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen centers on wheat pasta (Napolitan) and shares a small space with no documented allergen separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy sauce is used as a featured ingredient (the signature soy-sauce ice cream), and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature dishes include sausage, fried egg, parmesan, and dairy-based ice cream, so vegan separation is not maintained.

cuisine: Retro Japanese kissaten (coffee shop) — omurice, homemade cakes, morning sets
neighborhood: Mochiidono Center-gai, Naramachi
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町39
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町39 via GSI; coordinates within central Nara box. Note: official shop page lists the chō as 餅飯殿町12, Tabelog lists 39 — same arcade block.
seats_text: 25 seats
hours_raw: 9:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:30) per official shop page; Tabelog lists 9:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30). Sunday morning service until 11:00.
closed_days: No regular closing day listed (open daily)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional neighborhood coffee shop where staff likely speak mostly Japanese; a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you through ordering coffee, omurice, or a morning set.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. This is a classic kissaten whose core menu — omurice (often served with wheat-based demi/ketchup sauces), sandwiches, cakes, and morning toast sets — is wheat-heavy. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: No vegan options indicated. The signature dishes center on egg (omurice), dairy (homemade cakes, custard pudding, coffee with milk), and sandwiches. Nothing on the documented menu is plant-based, and no vegan accommodation is mentioned.

bio.background: Kissa Nanpu opened in 1998 inside the covered Mochiidono Center-gai arcade, a historic shopping street running south from Kintetsu Nara Station toward Naramachi. It is a classic, family-style Japanese coffee shop that has become a reliable rest stop for visitors exploring the old town.
bio.specialty: Known for fluffy egg omelette rice (omurice, including omu-pilaf variations), homemade cakes, custard pudding, and morning service sets (from around 520 yen, with Sunday morning service offered until 11:00).
bio.philosophy: Welcomes tired sightseers to pause and refresh while wandering Nara's historic streets.

anecdotes.text: The shop describes itself warmly on its arcade profile: 'This is Kissa Nanpu, which opened in Heisei 10 (1998). Thanks to your support we are now in our 19th year.'

caveats: Hours differ between sources: official arcade page lists 9:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:30) while Tabelog lists 9:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30); verify on arrival. Address chō number differs (餅飯殿町39 on Tabelog vs 12 on official page) but both place it in the same arcade block. No documented dietary accommodation.

cuisine: Museum cafe / Western-style cafe (curry rice, hamburger sets, baumkuchen and other sweets, house-roasted coffee)
neighborhood: Nara Park area, inside the Nara National Museum (Noboriojicho)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市登大路町50 奈良国立博物館 B1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 奈良県奈良市登大路町50 (Nara National Museum). Result lng 135.835114, lat 34.684093 falls within the Nara box.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The menu centers on curry rice, hamburger and fried-chicken sets, baumkuchen and cakes, and other Western-style cafe fare, all of which typically involve wheat (roux, breading, batter, flour-based sweets). There is no indication of celiac-safe preparation or allergen handling. Travelers needing strict gluten-free should not rely on this venue.
vegan_detail: No explicitly vegan menu is documented. The cafe serves meat dishes (hamburger, fried chicken), curry, and dairy/egg-based sweets (baumkuchen made with local eggs). Drinks include house-roasted coffee, teas, and herbal infusions, which may suit a vegan in a pinch, but no plant-based meals are advertised. A vegan would need to ask, and options are likely very limited.
seats_text: Spacious interior with individual tables plus a 4-person terrace seating area (terrace typically closed in summer); exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (food last order 16:00 / drinks last order ~16:30). Closed Mondays (next day if Monday is a holiday). Hours extend to 19:00 on museum evening-opening days and to 18:00 during the Shosoin Exhibition period.
closed_days: Mondays (the following day if Monday is a public holiday; also closed the day after consecutive holidays). Hours follow the Nara National Museum schedule and vary during exhibitions.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed museum cafe where you can drop in without buying a museum ticket; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a coffee and curry comfortably.
caveats: IMPORTANT: The target row's metadata (Kintetsu Nara Station / Higashimuki shopping street, nyumen / tom yum nyumen) does NOT match this venue. The Tabelog URL 29003614 and the name カフェ・葉風泰夢 actually refer to the Western-style cafe in the basement of the Nara National Museum, run by the Tokokuro (桃谷樓) hospitality group. It serves curry, hamburger sets, baumkuchen, and coffee — not nyumen, and it is not on Higashimuki. A separate Tabelog entry (29007536), a related mobile-vendor gelato stand of the same name in front of the museum, is currently 掲載保留 (listing on hold). Hours follow the museum's calendar and change for special exhibitions, so confirm before visiting.

bio.background: Cafe Half Time sits one level below the galleries of the Nara National Museum, a quiet stop where you can pause between looking at Buddhist art and treasures of the old capital. You don't need a museum ticket to come in, so it works as a calm coffee break in the middle of Nara Park. It is operated by the Tokokuro group, a local hospitality company that also runs a Chinese restaurant and a bakery in the city.
bio.specialty: House specialties include a special curry rice served with soup, a French-style hamburger and fried-chicken set, and a baumkuchen made with pesticide-free tea leaves from Yamabe and specialty eggs from Yamato-Koriyama. Coffee is roasted in-house under the group's 'mamejicafe' label.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's signature baumkuchen draws on the museum's setting: its name and the calligraphy for it were given by the chief abbot (choro) of Todai-ji Temple, Kokei Kitagawara.
Coffee is roasted in-house and sold under the group's own 'mamejicafe' label.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built around wheat-containing dishes (curry roux, breaded/fried items, flour-based baumkuchen and cakes); no gluten-free preparation or allergen control is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen handles meat (hamburger, fried chicken) and egg/dairy sweets; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

cuisine: Cafe / kissaten in a converted 1920s lactic-acid-drink factory — Yamato tea, breakfast sets, and croquette bread
neighborhood: きたまち (Shibatsuji-cho, northwest of Todai-ji Kaidan-do; ~15 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station)
gf_detail: This is a Japanese cafe built around bread, dango, and tea. Signature items — the croquette bread (コロッケパン), fermented-butter toast, sandwiches, and white dango (mochi rice flour) — are wheat- or gluten-containing, and there is no documented gluten-free menu or celiac protocol. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: A drink-and-light-food cafe rather than a vegan destination. Plant-based options are incidental: Yamato tea (大和茶), persimmon-leaf tea (柿の葉茶), and a fig compote are vegan-friendly, but the hojicha latte and butter toast contain dairy and breakfast/lunch sets are not designed to be vegan. No documented vegan menu; ask staff about specific items.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市芝辻町543
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市芝辻町543 returned [135.836105, 34.689774], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 20 seats — 13 table seats, 5 counter seats, and a 7-mat tatami area (counts vary slightly by source).
hours_raw: Fri 11:00-18:00; Sat/Sun/holidays 09:00-18:00 (Tabelog lists Fri 11:00-17:30 and Sat/Sun/hol 08:30-17:30). Breakfast served Sat/Sun/holidays. Closed Mon-Thu.
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, individually-run heritage cafe where a few words of Japanese and a calm, respectful manner go a long way; English menus are not guaranteed, so pointing and basic phrases help.

bio.background: The building was raised in 1925 by Kazuo Kita's great-grandfather as the Chojukai Bacterial Research Institute, a factory that researched lactic-acid bacteria and produced a probiotic drink called 'Futurmin,' along with studies of edible fungi and bread yeast. It was designed by master carpenter Yoshitaro Oki, who also worked on Nara's Anglican church, and features hand-cut glass lamps by craftsman Koji Fujimoto. After the factory closed in the late 1970s it sat unused for more than three decades.
bio.specialty: Yamato tea and a weekend breakfast set, the much-loved croquette bread (a butcher's croquette with sweet sauce in fluffy bread, branded with a deer mark), fermented-butter toast, hojicha latte, and white dango.
bio.philosophy: Kita's guiding idea was to preserve the building by using it — 'preserve it while it lives,' so visitors can feel an atmosphere that cannot be recreated in anything new.

anecdotes.text: To reopen the long-shuttered factory as a cafe in 2009, Kita spent about a year cleaning it out, washing more than 10,000 glass bottles and sorting the old equipment by hand.
The croquette bread is stamped with a deer brand, a nod to Nara's famous deer, making it a small local keepsake as much as a snack.
In 2020 the former factory and its product storehouse were listed as a Registered Tangible Cultural Property of Japan.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bread (croquette bread, toast, sandwiches) and rice-flour dango are core menu items in a small shared kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy (butter, latte milk) is used throughout; vegan separation is not documented.

positives.text: Several plant-based drinks are offered, including Yamato tea and persimmon-leaf tea, plus a fig compote.

caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources: Tabelog lists Fri 11:00-17:30 and Sat/Sun/holidays 08:30-17:30, while co-trip and recent listings give Fri 11:00-18:00 and Sat/Sun/holidays 09:00-18:00; confirm before visiting. The official site URL (http://kojoato.main.jp/wp/) was surfaced via search and not directly fetched. Breakfast is served only on weekends/holidays and can be reserved by phone or website.

cuisine: Shōwa-retro local teahouse and food stall — curry udon, nyūmen, house-made warabi mochi, and shaved ice (kakigori)
neighborhood: Nara Park / Todai-ji grounds (Zōshi-chō), near Nigatsu-dō, central Nara
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市雑司町407-4
geocode_note: Geocoded from full address via GSI; coordinates [135.85025, 34.688213] fall within the Nara box and match the location inside Nara Park near Todai-ji's Nigatsu-dō. Note: the target's area description (三条通) is inaccurate — the shop is in the Todai-ji temple grounds (雑司町), about a 20-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: Small teahouse-style seating; one listing notes 8 counter seats and another lists 26 total — it is a small, casual spot with limited indoor/outdoor seating in the temple grounds.
hours_raw: 09:00-17:00 (general listing). Shaved ice (kakigori) served seasonally June–September, 10:00-16:30.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available, but this is a small old-fashioned local teahouse where a few words of Japanese and a friendly konnichiwa will go a long way.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature dishes — curry udon and nyūmen (thin somen-style noodles) — are wheat-based, and broths are dashi-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The udon and nyūmen broths rely on fish dashi, so the noodle dishes are not vegan. Some shaved ice (kakigori) options — such as those with kinako (roasted soybean) powder, honey/mizore syrups, and red bean paste — may be plant-based, but milk-based syrups are also used, so a vegan would need to ask which syrups and toppings are dairy-free. House-made warabi mochi is typically plant-based but should be confirmed.

bio.background: Rokumeien is a long-running, old-fashioned teahouse and food stall tucked into the wooded grounds of Todai-ji, on a quiet hill beyond the Great Buddha Hall near Nigatsu-dō. Surrounded by greenery in a free-to-enter part of the temple precinct, it has the unhurried, Shōwa-era character of a roadside tea shop — a place where visitors rest in the shade away from the crowds.
bio.specialty: Curry udon and pork-soup udon with a rich dashi broth, seasonal curry nyūmen, house-made warabi mochi served with home-cooked red beans, and shaved ice (kakigori). The signature kakigori is a rice-themed bowl combining amazake syrup, rice-flour dumplings, and concentrated red bean paste; the kinako (roasted soybean) milk ice with honey mizore is also a favorite, praised for fluffy, finely shaved ice and a clean, non-cloying finish.
bio.philosophy: A focus on simple, handmade, locally sourced fare — pure ice with house-made syrups and Nara ingredients, served in a calm setting away from the tourist crush.

anecdotes.text: Described by a visitor as a hidden gem of shaved ice deep within Todai-ji — surrounded by greenery, far from the lines that form at Nara's more famous kakigori shops, ideal for those who dislike waiting.

The kinako milk ice is served with fluffy, finely shaved ice and honey-infused mizore beneath a milk syrup, with the kinako powder brought separately so it doesn't spill — a small touch of care from the shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles (udon and nyūmen/somen) are central to the menu and are cooked in shared kitchen space; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths are dashi/soy-based; standard Japanese soy sauce containing wheat should be assumed. No wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Noodle broths use fish dashi and the menu mixes dairy (milk syrup) and animal-free items; vegans should ask which kakigori syrups and toppings are dairy- and animal-free.

positives.text: Shaved ice is made with pure ice and house-made syrups using Nara ingredients, with some plant-leaning options like kinako, honey/mizore syrups, red bean paste, and house-made warabi mochi that a careful diner can ask about.

caveats: Address is in Nara Park / Todai-ji grounds (雑司町), not 三条通 as the target note suggested — the Tabelog URL and shop identity match, but the area descriptor was inaccurate. Seat count differs between listings (8 counter vs. 26 total). Hours: general 09:00-17:00; kakigori is seasonal (roughly June–September, ~10:00-16:30). Closures are irregular (不定休). Official site (rokumeien.com) was unreachable (connection refused) at time of check, so history/founding details could not be confirmed from the source and are not stated.

lead: そば処 吟松 奈良町店
name_ja: そば処 吟松 奈良町店
cuisine: Hand-cut nihachi soba (80% Shinshu buckwheat / 20% wheat), with tempura fried to order; soba flour 100%/二八 buckwheat noodles, bonito-dashi broth
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The soba is nihachi (二八), an 80% buckwheat / 20% wheat-flour blend, so the noodles themselves contain wheat. Udon (pure wheat) is also offered, tempura is wheat-batter fried to order, and the broth uses soy-based tsuyu. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no allergen accommodation is documented. Unsuitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The soba dipping sauce and broth are made with bonito (katsuo) dashi, and the most popular dishes are tempura zaru soba and tendon. No documented vegetable-only broth or vegan options. A traveler could ask for plain zaru soba, but the standard tsuyu/dashi contains fish, so it cannot be assumed vegan.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西新屋町18
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 奈良市西新屋町18, within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: Naramachi (Nishi-Shinya-cho), near the Naramachi Shiryokan, about a 13-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: 24 seats; small machiya-style shop with mostly tables
hours_raw: 11:30–14:00 (closes when sold out) per the shop's own listing; some directories list 11:00–14:00. Closed Mondays and Fridays (irregular holidays) per the shop/Naramachi info site; some directories list Mondays only.
closed_days: Mondays and Fridays (irregular holidays); some directories list Mondays only
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, lunch-only local soba shop where a friendly greeting and pointing at the popular tempura zaru soba will get you by, though menus and staff are mostly Japanese, it is cash-only, and it can fill up when tour groups pass through.
caveats: Lunch only and closes as soon as the day's soba sells out, so go early. Cash only, no reservations, no cards or e-money. Closing days are reported inconsistently (the shop's own listing notes Mondays and Fridays as irregular holidays, while some directories show Mondays only) and posted hours vary between 11:00 and 11:30 opening — confirm before a special trip. The Shinshu-flour/nihachi detail comes from coverage of the same owner's main Takabatake shop, which also runs this Naramachi branch.

bio.background: A small machiya soba shop tucked into the historic Naramachi quarter near the Naramachi Shiryokan, Ginsho has been making soba in the neighborhood for around 32 years. Behind its nostalgic wooden lattice door and red noren, the room glows with warm lighting and Japanese knick-knacks, the kind of unhurried lunch spot locals have leaned on for decades. It is a branch of the family's main Ginsho shop over in the Takabatake area near Shin-Yakushi-ji.
bio.specialty: Thin, fragrant nihachi soba (80% buckwheat, 20% wheat) made from stone-ground Shinshu buckwheat flour, prized for its delicate texture and smooth throat-feel. The runaway favorite is tempura zaru soba — cold soba with crisp tempura (shrimp, pumpkin, eggplant, green beans, nori) fried to order — alongside tendon and a mini tempura rice bowl. The zaru is finished with a scattering of chopped nori, an unusual touch.
bio.philosophy: The shop keeps things simple and fresh: noodles and tempura made to order, a bonito-dashi broth left largely unadorned to let the soba speak, and a willingness to keep going only as long as the day's hand-cut soba lasts.

anecdotes.text: The owner writes that, supported by patrons, they have run a soba shop in Naramachi for about 32 years.
The cold zaru soba is traditionally topped with chopped seaweed, an uncommon preparation, and the dipping broth leans on a clean bonito dashi with minimal embellishment.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The soba is nihachi (二八), an 80% buckwheat / 20% wheat blend, so the noodles inherently contain wheat; udon (pure wheat) is also cooked here, and tempura is wheat-batter fried in shared oil.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with a bonito-dashi tsuyu/broth; standard Japanese soba tsuyu is soy-sauce based, which normally contains wheat. No wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth and dipping sauce use bonito (fish) dashi, and signature dishes are tempura and tendon, so dishes are prepared in a fish- and animal-using kitchen with no documented vegan separation.

lead: 蕎麦酒場 たかま家
name_ja: 蕎麦酒場 たかま家
cuisine: 本格手打ちそば＋京鴨・大和野菜・地酒を楽しむ小規模な蕎麦酒場
neighborhood: 奈良市中心部（近鉄奈良駅周辺・高天町）
address_ja: 〒630-8241 奈良県奈良市高天町45-6
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市高天町45-6 returned [135.826431, 34.683792], within the Nara box. Resolves to the 高天町 chō level near Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: Seat count not published. Described as a small place with a counter plus more table seats than the narrow frontage suggests; interior feels wider than the entrance implies.
closed_days: 火曜日（火曜が祝日の場合は振替）
cultural_comfort_note: A cozy neighborhood soba-and-sake spot tucked down a lane near Kintetsu Nara Station; some Japanese helps as menus and service lean local, but pointing at dishes and the cashless options make it manageable for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The house soba is hand-cut buckwheat made with roughly 20% binder (typically wheat flour), so the noodles contain wheat. Dishes also rely on soy sauce and fish dashi. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu centers on Kyoto duck, Yamato beef and pork, fresh fish/sashimi, and soba served in fish-dashi broths. Yamato vegetables appear as ingredients, but no documented dish is confirmed free of animal products, dashi, or fish; vegan accommodation is not mentioned.

bio.background: 蕎麦酒場 たかま家 occupies a Taisho-era (1912-1926) townhouse on a quiet lane in Takama-cho, a couple of minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station. The original exterior of the old machiya has been kept while the inside was renovated into a calm, surprisingly roomy space with a counter and table seating.
bio.specialty: Hand-cut soba made mostly from Hokkaido hikigurumi buckwheat flour with about a 20% binder, blended fresh each day to give thin noodles with a firm, chewy bite. Other signatures include Kyoto duck (京鴨) dishes, sashimi from fish the head chef selects himself, and Yamato vegetables, Yamato beef and Yamato pork, paired with a wide range of Nara local sake and regional sake served by the half cup.
bio.philosophy: A 地産地消 (local-production-for-local-consumption) approach built around ingredients found only in Nara, run as a soba shop by day and a soba-and-sake izakaya by night.

anecdotes.text: The shop is set in a converted Taisho-era townhouse down a hard-to-spot alley (a tucked-away nagaya); the entrance is easy to miss but the interior feels far wider than the narrow frontage suggests.
The kitchen raises and sources its Kyoto duck so quality and safety can be vouched for, and the head chef personally picks the day's fish for sashimi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba is made with roughly 20% binder (commonly wheat) and the kitchen also uses soy sauce and fish dashi, so wheat is present in the kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around duck, beef, pork, and fish with dashi-based broths; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

caveats: Seat count is not published on the official site, Tabelog, or gnavi. Day-by-day hours follow Tabelog's structured listing (Fri/Sat dinner to 23:00, otherwise to 22:00, last entry 21:00); the official access page lists the simpler 11:00-14:00 / 17:00-22:00 with Tuesday closed. One aggregator listed weekend lunch extending to 15:00, which is unconfirmed by the official site. No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan accommodation is documented anywhere; celiac and vegan diners should treat this as unsafe absent direct confirmation.

anecdotes.text: The owner-chef Kawada grew up in Nara and trained at the Kanazawa kaiseki house Tsuruko before opening his own restaurant in his hometown.

bio.background: Trained at Kanazawa's Tsuruko, then returned to Nara to open his own Japanese restaurant.
bio.specialty: Seasonal kaiseki of Yamato vegetables and the day's fish.
bio.philosophy: Letting ingredients' natural character shine through sure technique.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional kaiseki kitchen relying on soy sauce and fish dashi; no allergen-free prep documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses feature fish and shellfish and fish dashi broths.

cuisine: カレー（スパイスカレー専門店）
gf_detail: The official site states "gluten-free, pork-free menu options" are available, but no specific gluten-free dish is documented and there is no information on dedicated equipment or cross-contamination controls. Curry roux, curry bread (a signature item), and curry ramen are wheat-based, and the kitchen handles wheat heavily, so this is not a celiac-safe environment. Celiac travelers should ask directly about which curries are truly gluten-free and how they are prepared before ordering.
vegan_detail: There is a genuine meat- and fish-free veg line: the Veg Wakakusa Curry (¥1,250), a sesame-and-vegetable curry made for vegetarians, with a version available without garlic or onion for Buddhist-style diets. Veg variations of the baked (yaki) and omelet (omu) curries also exist. The standard menu is otherwise omnivore (chicken, Yamato beef, lamb). Strict vegans should confirm there is no dairy/egg (the omelet and baked versions use egg/cheese) and that the vegetable curry base contains no animal stock.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町38-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町38-1 returned [135.82869, 34.680351], within the Nara bounding box.
neighborhood: 餅飯殿センター街（近鉄奈良駅・ならまち方面）
seats_text: 26 seats total: 10 counter seats and 16 table seats (four tables of four)
hours_raw: 11:30-20:00 (L.O. 19:30) daily except Wednesday 11:30-15:30 (L.O. 15:00). Per the official site, Thursday operation begins June 1, 2026.
closed_days: No regular closing day stated; Wednesday is shortened (lunch only).
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, friendly counter-and-table curry spot in a covered shopping arcade where a smile and basic Japanese go a long way, though menu pointing works fine and card/QR payments make it easy for a foreign student.
caveats: No founder/owner backstory or philosophy is documented in the fetched pages, so the bio is limited to the place's specialty and concept. Gluten-free and vegan claims come from the shop's own listing without recipe-level detail; cross-contamination handling is unknown. The official site notes Thursday operation starting June 1, 2026, which is reflected in the weekly hours.

bio.background: Wakakusa Curry Honpo sits just south of Kintetsu Nara Station, tucked into the Mochiidono covered shopping arcade that links the station area to the old streets of Naramachi. It is a compact, independent spice-curry house known for healthy, vegetable-forward cooking.
bio.specialty: The house signature is the Wakakusa ('young grass') Curry, a spinach- and vegetable-heavy spiced curry, served alongside the shop's curry bread. The lineup spans a coconut-milk chicken curry, a mild Yamato beef curry that is popular with children, baked (yaki) curry, omelet (omu) curry, curry ramen, and a famously fiery lamb brown curry. A dedicated vegetarian Veg Wakakusa Curry of sesame and vegetables is offered, with an onion- and garlic-free version for Buddhist-style diets.
bio.philosophy: The shop's stated concept is building flavorful, nutritious dishes from abundant vegetables and aromatic spices.

anecdotes.text: The spinach-based 'young grass' curry is named after the vivid green of its vegetables, echoing Nara's Mount Wakakusa, and the shop pairs it with house-made curry bread.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen makes curry bread and curry ramen and uses wheat-based curry roux, so wheat is handled heavily; no dedicated gluten-free preparation or equipment is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetarian curries are cooked in a kitchen that also prepares chicken, beef, and lamb curries; shared equipment and stock are not addressed in available materials.

positives.text: Offers a purpose-made vegetarian Veg Wakakusa Curry of sesame and vegetables, including an onion- and garlic-free version for Buddhist-style diets.
Official site advertises vegetarian/vegan and gluten-free, pork-free menu options.

cuisine: Retro kissaten / yoshoku cafe and kakigori
neighborhood: Higashimuki / Sanjo-dori area near Kintetsu Nara Station, central Nara
gf_detail: This is a Western-style cafe whose menu is built around wheat: omurice with demi-glace and white (roux-based) sauces, pasta, doria, pizza, fried shrimp, and hamburg. Such dishes are wheat- and flour-heavy and the kitchen makes no documented gluten-free accommodation, so it is not suitable for celiacs. No gluten-free menu or handling is advertised.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-egg yoshoku cafe — the signature dish is a fluffy omelette over chicken rice, and other staples include hamburg steak, fried shrimp, and dairy-based sauces. Even the kakigori uses dairy/jelly toppings. There are no documented vegan dishes; this is not a vegan-friendly destination.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町28-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良市東向南町28; within the Nara box.
seats_text: 36 seats
hours_raw: 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00); closed Tuesdays
closed_days: Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, long-running family cafe where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the picture menu will carry you through comfortably.

bio.background: A retro Western-style cafe in the Higashimuki arcade near Kintetsu Nara Station that has been serving its signature omelette rice for over three decades, run by a master and his wife who handle everything by hand. Affectionately nicknamed 'Osha-kame' by regulars, it draws both Nara locals and visitors and has grown from an omurice specialist into a full casual cafe.
bio.specialty: Fluffy, creamy omelette rice over chicken rice, served tableside so diners cut the center to reveal a silky half-cooked egg, with a choice of sauces (demi-glace, white sauce, and more) and toppings such as hamburg. Beyond omurice the menu spans pasta, doria, fried shrimp, pizza, parfaits, and seasonal shaved ice with homemade syrups and rich house-made jelly.
bio.philosophy: Everything is made by hand — from sauces to the kakigori syrups and pudding — with the master and his wife taking time and care to chase flavor rather than expand endlessly.

anecdotes.text: The shop is named after a turtle from Kasuga's pond whose shell was said to resemble a mound of omelette rice; a turtle is displayed inside, reinforcing the playful 'chatty turtle' name.
It has been continuously making omelette rice for around 35 years, earning the local nickname 'Osha-kame'.
All dishes are homemade, including kakigori syrups and pudding, crafted by the master and his wife.

caveats: Cash only (PayPay QR accepted; no credit cards). No reservations. Closed Tuesdays. Not suitable for celiac or vegan diners — wheat-, egg-, meat-, and dairy-heavy Western cafe menu with no documented allergen accommodation. Coordinates geocoded to the chōme-level address via GSI.

cuisine: フルーツカフェ・甘味・サンドイッチ
neighborhood: 餅飯殿センター街・ならまち（近鉄奈良駅近く）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市角振町23
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市角振町23 returned [135.827164, 34.682289], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 16 table seats; a narrow shop with a takeout counter at the front and a small café seating area in back.
