closed_days: Irregular holidays (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual fruit café with a takeout counter and simple table seating where pointing at the picture menu works fine, so a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature items are fruit sandwiches on wheat bread (including a black-sugar bread), so the core menu is wheat-based. There is no documented gluten-free preparation or allergy handling; smoothies, mixed juice, and stone-baked sweet potato may be naturally wheat-free but cross-contact is not addressed.
vegan_detail: Largely not vegan. Headline items (fruit sandwiches, fruit zenzai, persimmon tiramisu) use fresh dairy cream and bread. Fresh fruit, mixed juice/smoothies, and the stone-baked sweet potato could be plant-based, but no vegan labeling or dairy-free options are documented, so confirm ingredients on site.

bio.origin: Nara (Yoshino) fruit orchard dating to the Meiji era
bio.background: Horiuchi Kajitsuen grew out of a family fruit orchard in Nara and Yoshino that has continued since the Meiji era. The growers opened a storefront to show off the quality of their tree-ripened, seasonal fruit, starting on Sanjo-dori in central Nara, and have since expanded to Osaka and Tokyo.
bio.specialty: Fruit sandwiches piled with fresh cream and ripe seasonal fruit (notably on a mildly sweet black-sugar bread), seasonal shaved ice made with fresh fruit (more than ten varieties in the May-October season), fruit zenzai, mixed juice and smoothies, stone-baked sweet potato, and a persimmon tiramisu.
bio.philosophy: Described as a shop where the farmers themselves propose ways to enjoy fruit: offering fresh, tree-ripened produce in a casual style, with the menu rotating according to what is in season and when each fruit is at its best.

anecdotes.text: Items such as the seasonal shaved ice and sandwiches are made to order so they are served fresh, and the shop's narrow space is split between a takeout counter at the front and a café area in the back.

positives.text: Plenty of fresh whole fruit, fruit juices and smoothies are on the menu, which gives plant-based and gluten-avoiding visitors some naturally simpler options to ask about.

caveats: Tabelog and the official site list this as the 奈良三条店 at 角振町23; the address sits one block from the Mochiidono / Sanjo-dori area near Kintetsu Nara Station. Hours are 10:00-19:00 but closures are irregular, so confirm before a special trip. It is part of a multi-branch operation (Nara, Osaka, Tokyo), so not a single independent shop. No gluten-free or vegan handling is documented; the core menu relies on wheat bread and dairy cream.

cuisine: Machiya-style cafe & bar — ancient-Nara-themed sweets, kakigori (shaved ice), light savory plates and craft beer
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on ancient-style sweets such as sakubei and magari (wheat-flour confections), pancakes/hotcakes, and pottery-served snacks; no dedicated GF preparation or allergen labeling appears on the official menu. Not suitable for celiacs without direct confirmation.
vegan_detail: Primarily an omnivore cafe-bar. The shaved ice and ancient-sweet sets are dessert-forward, but several signature kakigori use condensed milk and the "so" (蘇) plate is an ancient dairy cheese. Secondary listings mention seasonal dairy-free, egg-free hotcakes and canelés in the colder months, but this is not stated on the official menu, so plant-based eaters should ask in advance. No vegan menu or program is documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市小西町2 2F
geocode_note: GSI address search on 奈良県奈良市小西町2 returned [135.827957, 34.68293]; within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: 小西町・近鉄奈良駅南（ならまち方面）
seats_text: Seat count not published; described as an intimate second-floor space on a small alley between Konishi-dori and Higashimuki arcades — a compact cafe-bar setting.
hours_raw: Mon/Wed/Thu/Fri 14:00-20:00; Sat/Sun 11:00-21:00; closed Tuesday. (Some listings show weekday 14:00-19:30 and weekend from 12:00; hours shift seasonally, with reservation-only opening slots during shaved-ice season.)
closed_days: Tuesday (hours also vary seasonally; shaved-ice season uses reservation-only opening slots)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, locally-run themed cafe where a little Japanese helps, since menus and the ancient-Nara concept are mostly in Japanese; staff are used to curious visitors but English is limited.
caveats: Vegan/GF travelers should confirm in advance: the official menu lists no allergen labeling, and signature items include wheat-flour ancient sweets, condensed-milk kakigori, and an ancient dairy "so" plate. Seasonal dairy-free/egg-free hotcakes and canelés are mentioned only in secondary listings. Hours and menu rotate seasonally and shaved-ice season uses reservation-only opening times.

bio.background: Tucked on the second floor of a small alley linking Konishi-dori and the Higashimuki arcade, just a minute's walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, this cafe-bar occupies the former home of a long-shuttered kissaten called Akadama. It is the second outpost of Fulucoto (Furukoto), a shop devoted to books and goods about Nara, and it doubles as a cafe, a small bar, a retail nook of Nara-themed wares, and a venue for cultural events.
bio.specialty: Ancient-Nara-themed treats: a rotating cast of kakigori shaved ice named after Asuka and Tenpyo-era figures (such as Ooshiama with Yoshino kudzu and homemade granita, Chujohime with loquat-leaf tea and lotus seeds, and Ichihiko with Asuka Ruby strawberry syrup), plus 'ancient sweets' sets like sakubei and magari, the fermented dairy delicacy 'so' said to echo ancient cheese, and house Mahoroba craft beers.
bio.philosophy: Bringing the world of Heijo-kyo and the Tenpyo era to the table — naming dishes after historical figures and serving them in replica ancient vessels so guests taste Nara's deep history.

anecdotes.text: The shop reimagines kakigori as edible history, giving each shaved-ice creation the name of an ancient Nara figure and pairing it with locally sourced Nara ingredients.
It is the sister venue of Fulucoto, a Nara-focused books-and-goods shop, and sits in the renovated space of a former classic coffee house named Akadama.

positives.text: Some seasonal sweets are described in local listings as made without dairy or eggs, suggesting the kitchen can sometimes accommodate plant-based requests if asked in advance.

cuisine: Western-style diner (yoshoku) and café
neighborhood: Naramachi (Wakitedo-cho)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市脇戸町6
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 奈良県奈良市脇戸町6 to [135.829422, 34.677967], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 26 seats; reported as roughly five tables seating four, with generous spacing.
hours_raw: 10:30–19:00; closed Mondays (or the following day if Monday is a public holiday). Reported as currently lunch-focused, finishing around early/mid afternoon depending on the day.
closed_days: Mondays (open if a public holiday, then closed the following day)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed old-fashioned neighborhood diner where a little Japanese helps, but staff hand out photo menus so foreign visitors can order comfortably.
gf_detail: This is a traditional Western-style diner built around breaded, deep-fried dishes (chicken cutlet, beef cutlet, oyster fry, shrimp fry), hamburg with demi-glace, omurice, and curry — all wheat-heavy and not safe for celiacs. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat and seafood (Yamato chicken cutlet, hamburg, pork sauté, beef cutlet, fried oysters/shrimp), and set meals come with miso soup that typically uses fish dashi. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
caveats: No husband-and-wife/couple ownership could be confirmed from the pages fetched, though it is a small independent local eatery. Hours marked irregular because the kitchen has reportedly been running a shorter lunch-focused schedule rather than the full posted 10:30–19:00 window.

bio.background: Miyoshi is a long-established, unpretentious Western-style diner tucked into the historic Naramachi quarter, where most eateries lean expensive and trendy. It keeps a retro, Showa-era atmosphere with a Japanese-style glass-door exterior that opens onto a homey dining room of a handful of four-top tables, drawing both locals and visitors and often filling up at lunch.
bio.specialty: The kitchen is known for its Yamato chicken (大和肉鶏, Nara's brand chicken) cutlet, described as tender and juicy, and for a hamburg in a rich, grown-up demi-glace sauce. Combination lunch sets pair hamburg, chicken cutlet, and pork saute, and other favorites include a generous beef cutlet set with rich demi-glace, a special omurice with thin egg and fried prawn and chicken, and a winter oyster-fry set. Sets come with salad, small side dishes, miso soup, and rice.
bio.philosophy: The appeal is honest value and flavor over fashion: hearty, traditional yoshoku at modest prices in an area where that is increasingly rare.

anecdotes.text: The chicken cutlet uses Nara's branded Yamato chicken and is praised as soft and juicy, while the hamburg's demi-glace is described as a sophisticated, grown-up flavor.

Although Naramachi is a tourist area where dining tends to be pricey and trendy, Miyoshi stays reasonable and unfashionable in the best way, serving 20-plus set meals across both Japanese and Western styles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by breaded deep-fried items (chicken cutlet, beef cutlet, oyster fry, shrimp fry) sharing fryer oil; no gluten-free handling documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are meat- and seafood-based and sets include miso soup that typically uses fish dashi; no plant-based preparation documented.

cuisine: 和カフェ・甘味処（抹茶・米糀甘酒・発酵あんこ）
neighborhood: ならまち（西新屋町）
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment and no GF program is documented. The signature offerings — matcha, koji amazake, and red-bean anko — are naturally wheat-free, but the menu also includes mochi-based sweets and wagashi where wheat and cross-contact cannot be ruled out. A celiac diner would need to ask in person; nothing here is verified as celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Many core items are plant-based by nature: naturally cultivated Yamato-tea matcha, homemade rice-koji amazake, and sugar-free fermented-anko zenzai. However the cafe makes no vegan claim, and dishes such as the matcha kakigori include vanilla ice cream (dairy), so vegan diners should confirm which items are free of dairy and egg. Plenty of likely-vegan choices, but ask to be sure.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西新屋町12 ならまちHANARE館 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市西新屋町12 returned [135.830124, 34.676643], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 16 seats — four 4-person tables and four 2-person tables
hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (L.O. 17:00). Open Friday, Saturday, Sunday and public holidays. Closed Monday-Thursday (but open when a public holiday falls on those days).
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday (open on public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A serene, owner-run town-house tea cafe where the host prepares matcha tableside in kimono; it is welcoming but conducted largely in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app help a foreign student feel at ease.

bio.background: Opened on New Year's Day, January 1, 2021, in a renovated town house in the heart of Naramachi, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The proprietor greets every guest in full kimono and prepares matcha at the table.
bio.specialty: Naturally cultivated Yamato-tea matcha whisked tableside, homemade rice-koji amazake, and fermented red-bean anko. Signature items include a Matcha Set where guests choose their own Akahada-yaki tea bowl, a deer-themed matcha kakigori topped with four kinds of anko and a deer-shaped amber candy, and a sugar-free fermented-anko amazake zenzai.
bio.philosophy: The owner's wish is to offer a calm place where locals and travelers alike can relax over matcha and sweets, serving food that is both delicious and gentle on the body through traditional fermentation.

anecdotes.text: Guests select their own Akahada-yaki (a local Nara ceramic) tea bowl, and the host whisks the matcha in front of them.
The deer-themed shaved-ice dessert is finished with a deer-shaped amber candy, a nod to Nara's famous deer.

positives.text: Core offerings are built on naturally fermented, plant-forward ingredients — rice-koji amazake, fermented red-bean anko, and Yamato-tea matcha — several of which are inherently free of wheat and animal products.

caveats: Dietary details (GF/vegan, cross-contact, allergen handling) are not documented on any fetched page; the cafe makes no formal vegan or gluten-free claim, so all such guidance is inferred from ingredient descriptions and should be confirmed in person.

cuisine: 玄米菜食（マクロビオティック）・精進的な健康志向の和食。竈で炊く玄米と手づきの玄米餅が主役。
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Core dishes rely on wheat-containing seasonings (naturally brewed soy sauce / shoyu and miso) and the menu includes wheat-gluten "veggie meat" (グルテンミート系の主菜) and brown-rice mochi. Brown rice and vegetables themselves are GF, but cross-contact with soy sauce and wheat-based seasonings is pervasive. A strict celiac cannot rely on this kitchen; no dedicated GF handling is documented.
vegan_detail: Fully plant-based macrobiotic (genmai-saishoku) cuisine. One detailed local write-up states explicitly that no animal-derived foods are used ("動物性の食品を使わない"); the cooking centers on brown rice, seasonal Yamato/seasonal vegetables, seaweed, and fermented seasonings (miso, soy sauce, mirin). No meat, fish, or dairy. Dashi is plant-based (kombu/vegetable) rather than fish-based, consistent with macrobiotic practice. Soy-milk ice cream is offered as dessert. Vegans should still confirm specific dishes by phone, as the kitchen does not market itself with a formal vegan label.
address_ja: 〒636-0112 奈良県生駒郡斑鳩町法隆寺東1-3-24
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 奈良県生駒郡斑鳩町法隆寺東1-3-24 returned [lng 135.743317, lat 34.614613]. This is outside the central-Nara bounding box (lat 34.64–34.74, lng 135.76–135.90) because the restaurant is in Ikaruga (斑鳩町), about 5 minutes' walk from Horyu-ji and ~1.5 km from Horyuji Station — the southwestern edge of the Nara area, well outside Nara city / Kintetsu-Nara. Coordinates set null per the box rule, but the geocode itself appears correct.
neighborhood: 斑鳩町 法隆寺東（法隆寺周辺）
seats_text: 23 seats total; intimate, home-style dining room in a Kumano-cedar interior.
hours_raw: ランチ 11:00–15:00（L.O. 14:00）。夜は4名以上・3日前までの予約があった場合のみ営業。定休日：水曜（祝日の場合は翌日）。
closed_days: Wednesdays (moves to the next day if Wednesday is a public holiday). Dinner served only by advance reservation for groups of 4+.
cultural_comfort_note: A small family-run countryside spot near Horyu-ji where little to no English is likely, so a foreign student would do best visiting with a Japanese-speaking friend or guide and a few prepared phrases.
caveats: Located in Ikaruga (near Horyu-ji), NOT central Nara city — plan around train access to Horyuji Station. Cash only. Lunch-focused; dinner only by advance reservation for 4+. Closed Wednesdays. While the food is plant-based, it leans on wheat-containing soy sauce, miso, and gluten "veggie meat," so it is not suitable for gluten-free or celiac diners.

bio.chef_name: 松村薫 (Kaoru Matsumura) and 松村尚美 (Naomi Matsumura)
bio.background: Genmaian is run by the Matsumura couple, who studied macrobiotics out of a desire to serve food that is gentle on the body and supports good health. Husband Kaoru carries the unusual title of 玄米餅師 ('brown-rice mochi master') and fires brown rice every morning in a traditional kamado stove he designed himself; his wife Naomi grills the freshly pounded mochi over charcoal to order for each guest.
bio.specialty: Cooked-in-the-kamado brown rice with its signature plump, chewy, slightly popping texture; hand-pounded grilled brown-rice mochi; and seasonal lunch sets such as the 小鉢セット (small-bowl set) of seven vegetable dishes plus a main, mochi, brown rice, miso soup and pickles. Desserts include soy-milk ice cream and a brown-rice brulee.
bio.philosophy: Macrobiotic genmai-saishoku: brown rice and other grains as the staple, built around seasonal vegetables and seaweed, with naturally fermented miso, soy sauce and mirin and no animal products. The kitchen's stated core ingredients are simply brown rice, soy sauce, water and miso (玄米・醤油・水・味噌).

anecdotes.text: Kaoru Matsumura holds the title of 'brown-rice mochi master' (玄米餅師) and cooks the day's brown rice each morning in a traditional kamado he built himself, while his wife Naomi hand-grills the pounded mochi over charcoal for each customer.
The brown rice is served with a characteristic 'puchitto' pop and a fluffy, chewy finish from the kamado cooking, and a local write-up notes the kitchen uses no animal-derived foods ('動物性の食品を使わない').

gf_cross_contamination.text: Naturally brewed soy sauce (shoyu) and miso, both typically wheat-containing, are core seasonings used throughout the menu, and grilled mochi and gluten 'veggie meat' are featured — making wheat cross-contact pervasive. Not suitable for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kitchen identifies soy sauce (醤油) and miso among its four core ingredients (玄米・醤油・水・味噌); these are standard fermented Japanese seasonings that generally contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is plant-based macrobiotic with no meat, fish, or dairy used, so animal-product cross-contact risk is inherently low.

positives.text: Entirely plant-based macrobiotic cuisine — no meat, fish, or dairy — built on brown rice, seasonal vegetables, seaweed, and fermented seasonings; soy-milk ice cream offered for dessert.

cuisine: 茶道文化の甘味処・喫茶（季節の和菓子と日本茶、夏の白いかき氷）
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. Many Japanese wagashi (sweets) are rice/bean-based and naturally wheat-free, but some confections and accompaniments contain wheat, and no allergen handling is published. A celiac visitor would need to ask about each item; cross-contamination is not addressed anywhere on the site.
vegan_detail: This is a tea-culture café serving matcha, Japanese tea, and seasonal wagashi. Traditional wagashi and matcha are frequently plant-based (red bean, rice, sugar), so vegan options likely exist, but the shop publishes no vegan labeling. Seasonal sweets vary and some may contain dairy or egg, so a vegan visitor should confirm ingredients per item.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市元林院町31-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市元林院町31-1 returned [135.829636, 34.681026], within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: ならまち（元林院町、近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約8分）
seats_text: 28席（テーブル等18席、掘りごたつ10席）
closed_days: 第2火曜（祝日は翌日休み）
cultural_comfort_note: A calm, welcoming machiya tea space where staff patiently guide beginners through whisking their own matcha, so a foreign student can enjoy it with a few polite Japanese phrases and pointing at the menu.
caveats: Closed on the second Tuesday of each month (or the following day if it falls on a holiday). Café last order is 18:30 though the shop stays open to 19:00. Hours on different listings vary slightly; confirm before a late visit. No parking. The matcha-whisking and full tea-ceremony experiences are paid and may be best reserved ahead.

bio.background: 茶論（さろん）奈良町店は、奈良発祥の老舗・中川政七商店が手がける、茶道文化の入り口をテーマにした甘味処・喫茶です。ならまちの元林院町に残る伝統的な町家を生かし、床の間や季節の花、軸、手入れの行き届いた庭を備えた凛とした空間で、気軽にお茶と和菓子を楽しめます。
bio.specialty: 季節の和菓子と選りすぐりの日本茶、抹茶。喫茶では季節の和菓子と薄茶のセットのほか、自分で抹茶を点てる「自点て」体験、本格的なお茶席（季節の和菓子・濃茶・干菓子・薄茶）が用意され、夏には白いかき氷も供されます。テイクアウトの濃茶ラテなどもあります。
bio.philosophy: 「茶道は敷居が高い」と感じる人にも、作法に縛られず気軽に一服を楽しんでほしいという思いから、スタッフが丁寧に手ほどきし、自分の手で抹茶を点てられるようにしています。茶道文化を身近に体験できる入り口でありたいという姿勢が貫かれています。
roles: 茶道体験ワークショップ

anecdotes.text: 床の間に季節の花や軸をしつらえ、庭を望む町家の座敷で、スタッフの手ほどきを受けながら客自身が抹茶を点てる「自点て」体験ができる。
中川政七商店が運営し、抹茶や茶筅・茶碗などの茶道具販売、濃茶ラテのテイクアウト、茶道文化を気軽に体験できるワークショップも行っている。

positives.text: Seasonal Japanese wagashi and matcha are the core offering; many such sweets are rice- and bean-based, giving a starting point for plant-based diners who confirm ingredients with staff.

lead: キッシュ専門店 レ・カーセ
name_ja: キッシュ専門店　レ・カーセ
cuisine: 古民家のキッシュ専門店。無農薬・有機の奈良県産野菜を中心に、全粒粉・ライ麦粉・有精卵・発酵バターのタルト生地で焼く季節のキッシュ。
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The tart crust is explicitly made with whole wheat (全粒粉) and rye (ライ麦粉) flour, both of which contain gluten. Quiche is inherently a wheat-pastry dish, so this is not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoiding diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Although the shop emphasizes organic Nara-grown vegetables, the quiche is built on eggs (有精卵/fertilized eggs), fermented butter, cheese and cream custard. Signature items include conger eel, scallop and red-wine cheesecake. No documented vegan options.
address_ja: 〒630-8212 奈良県奈良市春日野町158（奈良公園内）
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良市春日野町158 returned [135.846161, 34.684265], within the central Nara box. Consistent with the documented location inside Nara Park near Kasuga Taisha.
neighborhood: 奈良公園・春日野町（春日大社近く、近鉄奈良駅 徒歩圏）
seats_text: About 14 seats total, including table seating and a tatami (座敷) room at the back.
hours_raw: 10:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:00). 10:00-11:30 takeout only; dine-in 11:30 onward in two seatings 11:30-13:00 and 13:30-15:00 (reservation priority). Closes early when quiches sell out. Closed Tuesdays, with occasional irregular closures posted on Instagram.
closed_days: Tuesdays; occasional irregular closures announced on Instagram; may close early when quiche sells out.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, owner-run quiche café in a converted old house where a friendly greeting and pointing at the showcase works fine, though menus and service are mostly in Japanese, so a basic phrase or two helps.
caveats: Dine-in runs in two reservation-priority seatings (11:30-13:00, 13:30-15:00); 10:00-11:30 is takeout only. Quiches can sell out and the shop may close early. No parking. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: Le Case (Italian for "the houses") opened in December 2011 in a renovated old folk house tucked inside Nara Park, surrounded by the wooded slopes of Kasuga Mountain and a short walk from Kasuga Taisha shrine. The shop is led by a chef who trained in Italy, and the building's table seats and a back tatami room give it a quiet, lived-in feel.
bio.specialty: Seasonal savory and dessert quiches, with around fifteen colorful varieties lined up in the showcase. Signature quiches include onion (Cipolla), conger eel and burdock, scallop and lemon cream, and an apple ('white apple') quiche, plus a red-wine cheesecake. The tart dough is made with fertilized eggs, whole-wheat and rye flour, and fermented butter.
bio.philosophy: The founder wants to show children how delicious vegetables can be, choosing ingredients that children can eat safely and that are high in nutritional value. Vegetables are organic and as much as possible grown within Nara Prefecture.

anecdotes.text: The vegetables are described as remarkably fresh and delicious, with produce sourced from local growers such as Uratsuji Farm's organic vegetables.
Vegetables are organic and grown as much as possible within Nara Prefecture, and the tart crust uses fertilized eggs, whole-wheat flour, rye flour and fermented butter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The tart crust is made with whole-wheat (全粒粉) and rye (ライ麦粉) flour, so wheat/gluten is a core ingredient throughout the kitchen; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Quiches are egg-, butter-, cheese- and cream-based and several use fish and shellfish (conger eel, scallop), so animal products are pervasive; no vegan options or separate vegan prep are documented.

positives.text: Vegetables are organic and largely grown within Nara Prefecture, which the shop highlights as ingredients chosen so children can eat them safely.

cuisine: 昭和レトロ純喫茶（きたまちナポリタン・醤油アイス）／フランス雑貨併設
neighborhood: きたまち・内侍原町（近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約5分）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市内侍原町13
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市内侍原町13; within Nara box.
seats_text: テーブル12席
hours_raw: 10:30-18:30（火～日）。定休日：月曜（情報源により月・木の記載もあり、移転前情報の可能性）
closed_days: 月曜（一部情報源では木曜も定休）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 12-seat Showa-era coffee house run by locals; menus and conversation are in Japanese, so a few phrases or a translation app help, but the cozy welcome makes it approachable for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The signature きたまちナポリタン is wheat-based spaghetti, and the café centers on coffee-house fare. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling is documented; celiac travelers should treat it as unsafe.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Signature items use dairy (Uemura Farm ice cream in cream soda and the soy-sauce ice cream) and meat (sausage in the Napolitan). No vegan options or plant-based substitutions are documented.
caveats: Business hours and closed days differ between sources: Tabelog (most current, post-relocation address 内侍原町13) lists Tue–Sun 10:30–18:30 closed Monday, while some café guides list closed Mon & Thu, and one search summary cited 11:30–18:30 — likely a mix of pre-relocation and night-café information. Confirm hours directly before visiting.

bio.background: Bardot is a small Showa-retro pure kissaten tucked into Nara's Kitamachi district, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station near the south gate of Nara Women's University. The 12-seat room is dressed in nostalgic vintage style with antique lamps and 1980s magazines you can flip through, and a small corner of imported French goods from Paris is displayed and sold alongside the café tables, giving it the feel of a hideaway that blends an old-fashioned coffee house with a curio shop. Jazz and bossa nova play in the background.
bio.specialty: Kitamachi Napolitan — a nostalgic café-style spaghetti in a deep tomato sauce with sausage, onion, green pepper and mushroom, topped with a fried egg and served with grated cheese and Tabasco — and its most distinctive signature, soy-sauce ice cream: Uemura Farm vanilla ice cream finished with a dedicated soy sauce from Oita. The cream soda also uses rich Uemura Farm ice cream.

anecdotes.text: The soy-sauce ice cream is the café's calling card — Uemura Farm vanilla ice cream eaten with a special soy sauce, an unusual sweet-savory pairing that has drawn magazine attention.
The Kitamachi Napolitan is the flagship dish, an al dente, old-fashioned nostalgic plate often ordered as a set with a drink.
Alongside the café, the shop sells mostly one-of-a-kind French décor pieces imported from Paris, described as charming and reasonably priced.

cuisine: Western-style grill (yoshoku) — handmade hamburg steak, teppan-grilled meats, Yamato beef
neighborhood: Naramachi / Kyobate area
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高御門町13-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市高御門町13-1 returned [135.8293, 34.677406], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 19 seats (table and counter)
hours_raw: 11:00-15:30 (L.O. 14:30) / 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:00). Closed Thursdays and the 2nd & 4th Wednesday of the month.
closed_days: Thursdays, plus 2nd and 4th Wednesday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run neighborhood grill where Japanese is expected; menus and service are in Japanese, so a translation app and a few basic phrases will help, though pointing at the photo menu works fine.
caveats: Children under elementary-school age are not admitted. Hours vary slightly between listings (lunch close reported as 15:00-15:30); confirm before a late lunch. The 10-portions-a-day limited Yamato-beef lunch sells out.
vegan_detail: This is a meat-focused Western grill: signature dishes are beef-and-pork hamburg steak, pork loin and chicken steaks, and Yamato beef. Sauces are meat- and soy-based (demi-glace, shariapin). No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented; sides like potage and salad are not confirmed animal-free.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or handling is documented. Core dishes carry wheat risk: hamburg steak typically uses breadcrumb binder, demi-glace sauces are wheat-thickened, shariapin sauce uses soy sauce (contains wheat), and breaded shrimp/scallop fries are battered. Not suitable for celiacs.

bio.background: Grill TALO opened in May 2023 on a quiet street in Nara's Naramachi/Kyobate district. The owner-chef trained at Western and grill restaurants in Osaka's Shinsaibashi and Kyoto's Gion before striking out on his own with this intimate 19-seat counter-and-table spot.
bio.specialty: Teppan-grilled dishes are the heart of the menu: a 100% domestic beef-and-pork hamburg steak (the signature grill set, ~170g), pork loin steak from Kagoshima, and grilled Bicchu-kogen chicken. The hamburg is coarsely ground to keep a meaty texture and seared on the hot plate so excess fat drips away for a crisp, fragrant crust. A daily 10-portion limited lunch showcases Nara's own Yamato beef and Yamato pork.
bio.philosophy: The chef wants guests to enjoy the performance of food being grilled right in front of them, pairing contemporary quality with a nostalgic, homey Western-diner feel.

anecdotes.text: Diners choose among three sauces — demi-glace, a soy-and-red-wine Japanese-style shariapin, and a ratatouille-with-basil sauce — with regulars said to favor the shariapin.
The chef trained at establishments in Shinsaibashi and Gion before opening here in May 2023.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shariapin sauce is described as a Japanese-style base made with soy sauce and red wine; standard soy sauce contains wheat.

cuisine: Hand-made juwari (100% buckwheat) soba; family-run soba shop with tempura and seasonal side dishes
neighborhood: Naramachi / Minami-fukuro-cho, between JR Nara and Kintetsu Nara stations along Yasuragi-no-michi
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南袋町1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良市南袋町1 returned [135.826126, 34.676487], inside the Nara box and consistent with the shop's location along Yasuragi-no-michi between JR Nara and Kintetsu Nara.
seats_text: About 20 seats; small space with a mix of 4-person and 2-person tables plus some shared/raised seating.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:30 daily (last order; closes earlier when the soba sells out). Dinner 17:30-20:00 (last order) on Friday/Saturday/Sunday only. Closed Wednesdays.
closed_days: Wednesdays (lunch-only on most weekdays; dinner served Fri/Sat/Sun only). Note: dinner availability and a possible Tuesday-evening closure vary across listings.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, quiet family-run soba counter where some basic Japanese helps; the entrance is easy to miss and English support is unlikely, so it suits a student comfortable ordering simply in Japanese.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. Although the house soba is described as juwari (100% buckwheat), the kitchen fries wheat-batter tempura and kakiage in the same small space, the dashi/dipping sauce is soy-sauce based, and there is no documented allergen control or dedicated equipment. Treat as unsafe for celiac.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The soba dipping/broth dashi is made from bonito (katsuo), aji/horse mackerel and Rausu kombu, so it contains fish; tempura and side dishes are not vegetarian-oriented. No documented vegan options.
caveats: Hours and dinner days differ between listings (some show daily lunch and Fri-Sun dinner; one shows a Tuesday-evening closure). Lunch can end early once the soba sells out. Cash or PayPay only — no credit cards. Phone 0742-23-5589. Verify current hours by phone before visiting.

bio.background: A husband-and-wife soba shop opened in 1989 (Heisei 1) on a quiet stretch of Yasuragi-no-michi in central Nara, roughly midway between JR Nara, Kintetsu Nara and Kyobate stations. The entrance is so understated that regulars warn it is easy to walk straight past. The chef built the place on skills honed in Japanese cuisine and traditional soba-making, and after more than three decades it has become a settled, homey neighborhood fixture.
bio.specialty: Hand-cut juwari (100% buckwheat) soba made from a custom Sarashina-style blend, served thin with an emphasis on smooth throat-feel. Signatures include zaru soba, oroshi (grated daikon) soba, kakiage soba, a limited daily wariko soba, and lightly fried seasonal tempura, finished with the house soba-yu.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen works under the idea of shinmi-ushin (true flavor made with sincerity), pairing carefully blended buckwheat with a refined dashi drawn from bonito, horse mackerel and Hokkaido Rausu kombu so the aroma of the soba comes through.

anecdotes.text: The shopfront is genuinely hard to spot from the street; diners note you can sail right past it if you are not paying attention.
Lunch service simply ends once the day's hand-made soba runs out, so the supply is finite each day.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-batter tempura and kakiage are fried in the same small kitchen as the soba, with no documented separation, so cross-contact with wheat is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The soba is served with a soy-sauce-based dashi (bonito, horse mackerel, Rausu kombu); standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and no gluten-free alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi/broth is fish-based (bonito and horse mackerel), so even the noodle dishes are not vegan.

lead: やたがらす 奈良店
name_ja: やたがらす 奈良店
cuisine: 大和肉鶏の焼き鳥・地鶏料理（地鶏専門居酒屋）
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（東向商店街・やすらぎの道沿い）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市林小路町13-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 奈良県奈良市林小路町13-1 via GSI AddressSearch; first feature coordinates [135.82637, 34.682892], within Nara box.
seats_text: 55 seats including counter, tables, and a tatami/sunken-kotatsu room with semi-private areas for groups of 3+.
closed_days: Mondays (note: one source mentioned a temporary Wednesday closure; primary/official listing states Monday)
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming local chicken izakaya where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you a warm reception; an English menu is not advertised, so a translation app helps.
gf_detail: This is a yakitori and jidori izakaya. Grilled skewers are basted in tare (a soy-sauce-based glaze that normally contains wheat) and dishes such as the Yamato soft-roe ponzu use soy/citrus sauces that typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: A chicken specialist built around Yamato jidori — grilled skewers, chicken sashimi/tataki, soft roe, chicken ramen and oyako-don. The menu is meat- and fish-centered with soy- and dashi-based seasonings; there is no vegan offering or documented vegetable-only option.

bio.background: An independent chicken-focused izakaya near Kintetsu Nara Station, along the quiet Yasuragi-no-michi just off the Higashimuki shopping street. The kitchen builds everything around Yamato jidori, Nara Prefecture's prized local chicken breed, sourced from a contracted poultry farm.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled skewers of morning-fresh chicken, chicken sashimi and tataki made possible by the daily-procured freshness, the signature Yamato shirako (soft roe) with ponzu, plus a chicken ramen and oyako-don to finish. House and seasonal Nara sake round out the table.
bio.philosophy: Staff travel to the poultry farm to pick up freshly dressed chicken each morning, prizing the breed for its sweetness, depth of umami, gentle springy texture, and balanced fat — fresh enough to serve raw.

anecdotes.text: The Yamato shirako (soft roe) with ponzu is highlighted as a rare, creamy, melt-in-the-mouth cut.
Daily morning trips to the contracted farm keep the chicken fresh enough to serve as sashimi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Yakitori is glazed with wheat-containing tare and shares a charcoal grill; ponzu-dressed dishes also typically contain wheat-derived soy sauce. No gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tare basting glaze and ponzu sauces used on signature dishes are soy-sauce based and normally contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is entirely chicken-centered; any vegetable dishes would be cooked alongside meat with fish/meat-based seasonings.

caveats: Closed-day data conflicts between sources: the official listing and Tabelog state Mondays, while the Nara gourmet guide says Wednesdays (the official site notes a seasonal switch from Wednesday to Monday around mid-March). Confirm before visiting. Seat count is 55, larger than a typical tiny mom-and-pop, but it is an independent, locally rooted jidori izakaya rather than a chain.

lead: 大和旬菜・魚・鶏 じゅん平
name_ja: 大和旬菜・魚・鶏 じゅん平
cuisine: 大和野菜・大和肉鶏・地魚を使った小規模な和食居酒屋（刺身・タタキ・炙りなど）
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約4〜5分（上三条町）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市上三条町7 メゾンドバンイノウエ1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search geocoded 奈良県奈良市上三条町7 to [135.826477, 34.68166]; within central Nara box.
seats_text: Around 24-26 seats: counter ~12 and tatami ~12, plus a separate banquet floor for up to ~26.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:00 / 17:00-00:00 (L.O. 23:30). Open daily; irregular holidays (不定休). Lunch service may currently be paused.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures); no fixed weekly closing day
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run Japanese izakaya where a few words of Japanese and a willingness to point at the menu go a long way; English support is not assured, so a friendly konnichiwa attitude helps.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a traditional izakaya built around dishes seasoned with regular (wheat-based) soy sauce, ponzu, and fish dashi; tempura/fried and grilled items, plus drinking-snack fare, make wheat pervasive. No documented gluten-free menu or celiac-safe handling, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Primarily an omnivore izakaya centered on Yamato chicken, local fish, and live horse mackerel. There is a notable vegetable focus — the owner sources seasonal Yamato vegetables directly from farms and serves a signature vegetable sashimi assortment (名物野菜刺身盛合せ) — but vegetable dishes are typically dressed with fish-based dashi, soy sauce, or accompaniments, so they are not reliably vegan. A vegan would need to ask carefully, and even then options would be very limited.

bio.background: Born in Nara in 1974, Junpei Hata first worked part-time at an izakaya while at university and earned his culinary license there. After graduation he briefly pursued a different career before returning to the food trade, working his way up at a local izakaya into management roles before opening his own place.
bio.specialty: Yamato hill chicken (大和肉鶏) grilled with precise heat control (あみやき), a signature vegetable sashimi assortment (名物野菜刺身盛合せ) using farm-fresh Yamato vegetables, and 'swimming aji' (泳ぎあじ造り) sashimi cut from horse mackerel kept live in the shop's tank until ordered.
bio.philosophy: Hata's guiding idea is to express Nara's charm through food: he personally visits farms to source seasonal organic vegetables, builds the menu around what he procures each day, and, as a certified sake taster (きき酒師), curates around ten Nara local sakes alongside selections from across Japan.

anecdotes.text: The shop keeps a water tank where guests can watch fish and shellfish swimming before they are deftly prepared to order, including aji sliced for sashimi moments after leaving the tank.
The owner goes out to farms himself to buy seasonal vegetables, and the menu changes daily depending on what he sources, reflecting his belief that seasonal ingredients deserve to be celebrated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Nara izakaya, dishes rely on standard soy sauce, ponzu, and fish dashi; no gluten-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable dishes such as the signature vegetable sashimi assortment are served in a kitchen centered on fish and chicken, and are typically dressed with fish-based dashi or soy sauce, so vegan separation is not documented.

positives.text: The owner sources seasonal Yamato vegetables directly from local farms and features a dedicated vegetable sashimi platter, so plant-forward dishes are genuinely available even if not certified vegan.

caveats: Lunch service (11:30-14:00) may currently be suspended per one listing; dinner 17:00-00:00 is the reliable service. Seat count varies by source (24 vs 26). No official website found; details drawn from Tabelog and Hitosara listings.

cuisine: Hand-made soba (buckwheat noodles)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core dish is soba (buckwheat noodles), which here are not certified pure-buckwheat and are typically blended with wheat flour; tempura and dipping broths add further wheat and wheat-based soy sauce exposure. The dashi-based broths and shared kitchen make this unsuitable for celiacs. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on duck, egg, tempura (shrimp, smelt), and bonito/fish dashi broths; the duck broth is explicitly noted as dashi-seasoned. A plain zaru soba might appear vegetarian, but the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is fish-dashi based, so it is not vegan. No documented vegan options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市春日野町16 ふれあい回廊 夢しるべ風しるべ内（東大寺門前 夢風ひろば内）
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 奈良県奈良市春日野町16 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Nara box at the Todaiji Monzen Yume-kaze Hiroba complex.
neighborhood: Kasugano-cho, near Todaiji Nandaimon (South Gate), inside the Todaiji Monzen Yume-kaze Hiroba complex; about 1 km east of Kintetsu Nara Station toward Todaiji.
seats_text: Approximately 30 seats, including private-room options; warm wood interior.
hours_raw: 11:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30) per official site; closed for maintenance Feb 24-26. One soba reviewer noted regular closure on Tuesdays except peak tourist seasons.
closed_days: Tuesdays (except peak tourist seasons, when it may open daily); annual maintenance closure around February 24-26.
cultural_comfort_note: Easy for a foreign student: it sits in a tourist complex by Todaiji's South Gate with multilingual menus in English, Chinese, and Korean, and staff are used to helping international visitors.
caveats: Hours and closed days vary by source; the official site lists 11:00-18:00 daily while a soba reviewer reported a regular Tuesday closure outside peak seasons. Cash only, no reservations. Can be very crowded with up to an hour wait during peak tourist periods.

bio.background: Warabe Kian brings the soba of Kian, a long-established soba restaurant in Osaka's Kita-Shinchi district, to the foot of Todaiji's South Gate inside the Yume-kaze Hiroba complex, a rest facility built around the concept of expressing Nara's food and living culture.
bio.specialty: Hand-made seasonal soba; signature dishes include kamo seiro (duck dipping soba), ten-zaru (tempura with cold soba), umetoji (plum and egg soba), and nameko soba.
bio.philosophy: The shop follows the soba ideal of freshly ground, freshly made, freshly boiled, sourcing premium buckwheat flour from across Japan and choosing the most aromatic and flavorful variety each season.
roles: branch of an established Osaka soba house

anecdotes.text: A visitor on December 30 found the shop packed with international guests and waited nearly an hour; an elderly hostess kindly walked them through how to prepare the soba broth, and the staff apologized for the crowding while serving efficiently. The duck broth was noted as well-seasoned with dashi and the new-crop buckwheat had a rustic, dark, thick-cut, chewy character.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba noodles here are not documented as pure buckwheat and are typically wheat-blended; tempura is fried in a shared kitchen, creating heavy gluten cross-contact.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba dipping broth (tsuyu) is soy-sauce and fish-dashi based; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are fish/bonito dashi based and the duck broth is explicitly dashi-seasoned; menu features duck, egg, and seafood tempura, so even soba dishes are not vegan.

positives.text: Multilingual menus (English, Chinese, Korean) are available, making it easier for international diners to identify dishes.

cuisine: Nara kyodo-ryori (regional home cooking): chagayu tea porridge, chameshi tea rice, Miwa somen, fresh warabi mochi
vegan_detail: The signature set "奈良の恵ごはん" (Nara no megumi gohan) is described by the restaurant as 精進的 (shojin-style) and explicitly 肉・卵・乳製品不使用 (no meat, egg, or dairy), built around chameshi/chagayu, Miwa somen and seasonal Nara vegetable dishes — a genuine plant-based option. The broader menu includes non-vegan items (e.g. egg-topped udon), and the dashi/seasoning base of each dish is not documented, so a vegan diner should order the "奈良の恵ごはん" set and confirm the broth/seasonings. Warabi mochi is made to order with real warabi powder and served with kinako/anko/kuromitsu, typically plant-based.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. Core offerings are wheat-heavy: Miwa somen and udon are wheat noodles, and chagayu/chameshi use hojicha and likely soy seasoning. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no allergen/cross-contamination information is documented. A celiac traveler should not rely on this place.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市春日野町158-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市春日野町158-1 returned [135.846161, 34.684265], within the Nara box; consistent with the location at the foot of Mt. Wakakusa near Kasuga Taisha.
neighborhood: 奈良公園・春日野町 (foot of Mt. Wakakusa, near Kasuga Taisha, east of Kintetsu Nara Station)
seats_text: Official shop page lists 28 table seats indoors plus 8 terrace seats (36 total). An English summary elsewhere cited a smaller "16 inside / 12 terrace" figure, so capacity may vary by season; confirm for a large party.
hours_raw: 11:30-16:30 (LO 16:00). Closed Thursdays & Fridays (open if a holiday/event day).
closed_days: Thursday and Friday (open on holidays/event days)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed traditional teahouse-style eatery near the park where a friendly greeting and pointing at the picture menu will get you by; an English website exists, but spoken English at the counter is limited, so learn the dish names (chagayu, chameshi) in advance.

bio.background: Satei Yusui sits at the very edge of Nara Park, at the foot of Mt. Wakakusa where the path into the Kasuga primeval forest begins. Run by owner Toshihiro Matsuoka, it is a small, green-surrounded teahouse-style eatery devoted to the old everyday foods of the Nara basin.
bio.specialty: The house specialties are chagayu (rice gently simmered in roasted hojicha tea) and chameshi (tea rice cooked with roasted soybeans), served alongside Miwa somen and seasonal local-vegetable side dishes. Warabi mochi is whisked up fresh to order from genuine warabi (bracken) starch and served with kinako, sweet bean and black-sugar syrup.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen frames its food as the bounty of Nara (奈良の恵ごはん) — a shojin-leaning, meat-, egg- and dairy-free way of cooking that leans on locally grown vegetables, rice and miso to express the taste of Nara through the seasons, from spring greenery to autumn leaves.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant describes its setting as the place surrounded by green, at the foot of Mt. Wakakusa where the trail into the Kasuga primeval forest begins, aiming to offer the best seasonal taste of Nara.
Its signature set is named 奈良の恵ごはん and is built from chameshi, Miwa somen and seasonal Nara-grown vegetables, prepared without meat, egg or dairy.
Coverage of the shop highlights the made-to-order warabi mochi using real bracken starch and the tea-based rice dishes as the draw for visitors strolling Nara Park.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles (Miwa somen and udon) are core menu items prepared in the same kitchen, so flour and gluten are pervasive; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The 奈良の恵ごはん set is stated to use no meat, egg or dairy (肉・卵・乳製品不使用), but the broth/dashi and seasonings are not itemized, so a vegan should confirm the soup and tsuyu base.

positives.text: The restaurant explicitly offers a shojin-style signature set that excludes meat, egg and dairy, making a plant-based meal genuinely available.
Warabi mochi is made fresh to order from real warabi starch with kinako, bean paste and black-sugar syrup, all typically plant-based.

caveats: Seat count differs between sources (official shop page: 28 indoor + 8 terrace; an English summary cited 16 + 12) — treat ~36 as the upper figure and confirm for large groups. "No meat/egg/dairy" is documented for the signature set but the dashi/seasoning base is not fully itemized; vegan diners should confirm the broth. Not suitable for celiacs due to wheat somen/udon. Hours and closed days (Thu/Fri) are consistent across the official site.

cuisine: 茶粥・にゅうめん・うどん・そば・甘味を出す昔ながらの茶屋（奈良郷土料理）
neighborhood: 東大寺境内・雑司町（三月堂・二月堂前）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市雑司町407 東大寺三月堂前
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市雑司町407 returned [135.85025, 34.688213], within the Nara box and consistent with the teahouse's location in front of Todai-ji Sangatsudo/Nigatsudo.
seats_text: Approximately 49 seats; a roomy traditional teahouse with large windows facing Nigatsudo.
hours_raw: 11:00～16:00（料理L.O. 15:00、ドリンクL.O. 15:00）。お水取り（3月）期間は特別延長。
closed_days: 無休（年中無休）。営業時間は天候・行事・客足により変動することがある。
cultural_comfort_note: A simple in-temple-grounds teahouse where pointing at the menu works; an English menu is not guaranteed, so a foreign student may find it easiest with a translation app or a Japanese-speaking friend.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The core menu is wheat-heavy — udon, soba (often containing wheat), and nyumen/somen are all wheat noodles, and pickles/sweets are typically prepared with regular soy sauce and dashi. The chagayu (tea porridge with rice) is itself wheat-free, but it is served with accompaniments (pickles, tsukudani) that commonly contain wheat-based soy sauce, and there is no indication of celiac-safe handling or a dedicated fryer. Treat as not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The chagayu (hojicha-roasted tea porridge with rice) and some pickled-vegetable sides (奈良漬, 柴漬け, 切り干し大根, 梅干し) are plant-based in themselves, but noodle dishes use fish/bonito dashi and the herring soba contains fish, so most cooked items are not vegan. A vegan could potentially order chagayu with vegetable pickles, but broth and seasoning details must be confirmed in person.

bio.background: A long-standing, modest teahouse tucked into the grounds of Todai-ji, sitting directly in front of the Sangatsudo and looking out through large windows toward the Nigatsudo hall. It is the kind of resting spot where temple pilgrims and Nara locals have long stopped to ease the tiredness of a stroll through the precincts.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is Nara-style chagayu (tea porridge): raw rice is slowly roasted in-house each morning until golden, then simmered in hojicha roasted tea into a light, fragrant porridge in which the natural sweetness of the rice comes through simply. It arrives with pickles and small side dishes — Nara-zuke, shibazuke, kombu tsukudani, umeboshi, and kiriboshi daikon — so diners can adjust the flavors to taste. Alongside the porridge the teahouse serves warm nyumen/somen, udon, soba and traditional sweets like zenzai.
bio.philosophy: While ornate multi-thousand-yen chagayu courses exist elsewhere, this teahouse leans into a humble, unadorned preparation, the kind that feels like what chagayu was always meant to be.

anecdotes.text: The rice for the chagayu is roasted from raw each morning in the shop until it turns a fox-brown color, then cooked in hojicha — a hands-on, daily ritual that gives the porridge its aroma.
Its position in front of the Sangatsudo, with windows framing the Nigatsudo, makes it a natural rest stop mid-walk through the Todai-ji grounds for both tourists and locals.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat noodles (udon, soba, nyumen/somen) are central to the menu and share a kitchen, so cross-contact for celiacs is likely; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Pickles and tsukudani accompaniments served with the chagayu are typically seasoned with standard (wheat-based) soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Noodle dishes use fish/bonito dashi and one menu item is herring soba, so animal products are present in the kitchen; vegan diners would need to confirm broth and seasoning for any dish.

positives.text: The chagayu is plain hojicha-roasted rice porridge, naturally free of wheat in the porridge itself, and comes with vegetable pickles that can be chosen to taste.

caveats: Hours and operation can vary with weather, temple events and foot traffic; during the March Omizutori festival hours are extended. Closed-days info is reported as 無休 (open year-round) but verify before a special trip. Menu noodle naming (soba/nyumen) and dashi/soy-sauce details were drawn from a Nara gourmet guide and a listing site, not an official restaurant site (no official site found).

cuisine: Hand-cut stone-milled soba (jūwari/100% buckwheat and nihachi blends)
neighborhood: Naramachi area, near Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市中筋町38
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市中筋町38, within the Nara box.
seats_text: 14 seats: a large single-plank communal counter (about 10) plus two 2-person tables; all non-smoking.
hours_raw: Mon, Thu, Fri 11:30-14:30; Sat, Sun (and holidays) 11:30-14:30, 17:30-19:30. Closed Tue & Wed. Hand-ground 100% buckwheat soba limited to about 10 servings per day.
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A small, quiet couple-run soba counter where a few words of Japanese and an easygoing attitude go a long way; it is cash-only and does not take reservations, so come early before the limited daily soba sells out.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. While the headline jūwari (100% buckwheat) soba contains no wheat in the noodle itself, the menu also includes nihachi (80/20 buckwheat-to-wheat) noodles, and the dipping/broth tsuyu is made with standard soy sauce and dashi, both of which typically contain wheat. There is no dedicated gluten-free preparation, no separate boiling water, and no documented allergen handling, so heavy cross-contact with wheat is expected in a soba kitchen.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The soba is served with a tsuyu/broth based on fish dashi (described as a robust, restrained-sweetness stock), and seasonal specialties include duck-broth soba. Soy sauce is used throughout. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: No official website found. Vegan/gluten/allergen accommodations are not documented on any fetched page; dietary statements above are inferred from the standard composition of soba, tsuyu, and dashi described in sources. Evening service is limited to weekends and holidays, and the hand-ground 100% buckwheat soba is capped at roughly 10 servings per day.

bio.background: Sobakiri Momoyozuki is a small, couple-run soba shop tucked into a quiet street within walking distance of Kintetsu Nara Station, recognizable by its wooden frame and large glass windows that open onto the kitchen. The owner shaped his craft under the influence of the renowned Osaka soba shop Sobakiri Bongu in the Taisho district.
bio.specialty: Stone-milled, hand-ground, hand-cut soba. The kitchen prepares both nihachi (80% buckwheat) and jūwari (100% buckwheat) noodles, with a hand-ground 100% buckwheat version limited to about ten servings a day. Buckwheat is sourced seasonally from regions such as Shimane (Matsue), Okayama (Hiruzen), Gunma, Hiroshima, Hokkaido and Fukui. Seasonal bukkake/tsukemen-style soba rotates roughly every two months from spring through autumn, with warm soba in the colder months, alongside side dishes and the soba-zenzai (buckwheat dumpling sweet red-bean soup) at lunch.
bio.philosophy: The shop encourages diners to taste the noodles first with just salt -- 'mazu wa oshio de' -- to appreciate the buckwheat's inherent aroma and character before reaching for the dipping broth, which is kept restrained in sweetness so as not to overpower the textured noodles.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name, Momoyozuki (百夜月), is taken from the name of a now-uninhabited settlement in Kumano City, Mie Prefecture.
The owner's soba-making was influenced by the well-known Sobakiri Bongu in the Taisho ward of Osaka.
Diners are guided to first try the noodles with salt to experience the buckwheat's pure flavor before using the broth.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A soba kitchen serving both nihachi (wheat-blended) and jūwari noodles cooked in shared water means heavy wheat cross-contact; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping broth/tsuyu uses standard soy sauce, which normally contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths are based on fish dashi, and seasonal dishes include duck-broth soba, so dishes are not vegan.

cuisine: 手打ちそば（石臼挽き戸隠そば・皿そば、鴨・天ぷら）
neighborhood: ならまち／京終（西木辻町）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西木辻町120-16
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.826981, 34.67408] for 奈良県奈良市西木辻町120-16; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 約15席（カウンター約5〜6席、4人掛けテーブル3卓ほど）
closed_days: 木曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, locally-loved soba counter off the tourist path; little to no English, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will go a long way.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The soba is made in a 二八 (2:8) blend, meaning roughly 20% wheat flour is mixed into the buckwheat, and the dipping broth and warm soup contain wheat-based soy sauce. Tempura items (天ざる, かき揚げ, あなご天) are wheat-battered and fried. There is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free preparation or fryer, so this is not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The soba dough itself contains wheat (not animal product), but the standard tsuyu/broth is fish-dashi based and the soup soba and duck dishes use animal stock; some sets come with egg and grated yam. A plain cold ざる order without the dashi tsuyu could be near-vegan, but there is no documented vegan broth, so vegan diners would need to confirm in person.

bio.background: 蕎麦切り 西本 is a small, owner-run soba shop tucked into the西木辻町 corner of Nara between the historic Naramachi district and Kyobate, set slightly off the main tourist streets so it keeps a calm, neighborhood feel. The kitchen mills its own buckwheat on a stone mill (石臼挽き) using Togakushi buckwheat from Nagano Prefecture.
bio.specialty: The house style is Izushi-influenced 皿そば (plate soba) served as five small plates per person with egg and grated mountain yam on the side, alongside a rotating lineup of roughly seven cold and ten warm soba. Crowd favorites include the かき揚げ天ざる (vegetable-fritter tempura with cold soba), 鴨 (duck) cooked shabu-shabu style in a mushroom broth, あなご天 (conger-eel tempura, two generous ~15cm pieces), and a spicy soba made with the owner's home-grown purple daikon.
bio.philosophy: Generous, unfussy portions of freshly stone-ground soba in a quiet room, finished with thick, buckwheat-rich そば湯 poured over the dipping broth; local sake and soba-shochu are kept on hand for a leisurely meal.
bio.origin: Nagano Prefecture Togakushi buckwheat, stone-ground in-house

anecdotes.text: The shop grows its own purple daikon and uses it for a 辛味大根 (spicy radish) soba, with extra spicy radish offered for just ¥100.
Portions run notably larger than a typical soba shop, and the meal ends with free そば湯 thickened with buckwheat flour for a satisfying finish.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba is a 二八 (2:8) blend containing wheat flour, and tempura is wheat-battered and fried, so wheat is pervasive in the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soba tsuyu and warm soup are made with wheat-containing Japanese soy sauce; no wheat-free tamari is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dipping broth and soups are fish-dashi based and some sets include egg and grated yam; duck dishes use animal stock.

caveats: Hours and 木曜定休 confirmed across Tabelog and two local food sites; the shop now runs both lunch and dinner (11:30-14:00, 17:00-21:00) though one older review mentions lunch only. Seat count given as ~15 (about 5-6 counter plus three 4-top tables). No official website found. Cash only. Vegan/GF assessments are inferred from documented ingredients (二八 soba, fish dashi, wheat soy sauce) rather than from any allergy statement by the shop, so confirm in person.

cuisine: Yoshoku (Western-style Japanese), specializing in omurice
neighborhood: Naramachi (Takamikado-cho), about a 10-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高御門町4-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市高御門町4-1 returned [135.8293, 34.677406], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Around 20 seats: roughly six two-top tables plus one large shared six-person table, with both chairs and sofa seating; no counter.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (lunch only; last order around 14:00-14:30). Closed Wednesdays.
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, casual lunch spot where pointing at the omurice and a friendly konnichiwa will get you by, though there is no English menu and weekend waits often top 30 minutes.
gf_detail: This is a Western-style omurice and demi-glace house; the signature dishes are built on wheat-based, slow-cooked demi-glace sauce, plus curry, hamburger, and cutlets, all of which typically contain wheat. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of celiac-safe preparation, so it is not suitable for gluten-free or celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on egg-rich omurice over chicken rice, with meat-based demi-glace, curry, hamburger and cutlets. Eggs, meat and likely fish/meat stock are pervasive, and no vegan or vegetarian options are documented, so this is not a vegan-friendly destination.
caveats: Lunch-only since around March 2019 (an older 2012 article describing evening service until 9PM is outdated). Walk-ins only, no reservations, and no on-site parking on a narrow Naramachi street. Hours sources vary slightly (Tabelog lists 11:00-14:30; some local guides list up to 15:00).

bio.background: Yuki-tei began in Tenri City as Bistro Yuki-tei before relocating to Nara's historic Naramachi district in July 2012, settling into a bright, glass-fronted space near the Sugioka calligraphy museum. The chef trained for about twelve years in hotel restaurant kitchens, learning classic Western technique before opening his own place.
bio.specialty: Fluffy-creamy omurice: a softly set omelet draped over lightly sweet chicken rice, finished with a house-made demi-glace (or ketchup, by choice), served as a set with soup and salad. The kitchen also turns out curry, hamburger steak, and cutlets on weekdays.
bio.philosophy: Everything possible is made in-house rather than from commercial products, including the slow-cooked demi-glace and the dressings, aiming for honest, approachable Western flavors that are rich but never overpowering.

anecdotes.text: The chef cooks the omelet swiftly from pan to plate so the outside stays fluffy while the inside remains glossy and creamy, spilling open over the rice when sliced.
Diners pick their sauce, demi-glace or ketchup, and the omurice is so beloved that on busy weekends the menu is pared down to omurice only, with lines that regularly run past 30 minutes.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes rely on wheat-based demi-glace, curry roux, breaded cutlets and hamburger, so shared cooking surfaces and wheat exposure are highly likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on eggs, meat and meat-based sauces; no separated vegan preparation is described.

cuisine: Handmade udon (specialty: kinchaku kitsune udon)
neighborhood: Naramachi (Hashimoto-cho), near Kintetsu Nara Station and Sarusawa Pond
gf_detail: This is an udon specialist; udon noodles are made from wheat, and the broth is a katsuobushi/kelp dashi typically combined with soy sauce. Not gluten-free and not safe for celiacs. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: The signature kinchaku kitsune udon and other dishes are served in a fish-based dashi (katsuobushi/bonito and kelp), so they are not vegan. No vegan options are documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市橋本町30-1
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to chō-level address (橋本町30); coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 25 seats
hours_raw: Mon 11:00-15:00; Tue/Wed/Thu closed; Fri 11:00-15:00; Sat 11:00-16:00; Sun 11:00-15:00 (lunch only)
closed_days: Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, very popular lunch-only udon counter where a few words of Japanese and patience for the queue go a long way; the famous pouch dish is easy to point at and enjoy even without much language.
caveats: Hours and seat count are from Tabelog and may change; cash-only and no reservations. Founding date and detailed owner biography are not documented in sources fetched.

bio.background: A small, independent udon shop tucked into the Mochiidono shopping arcade in Naramachi, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station and Sarusawa Pond. It is run by owner Isao Tsuboi and is known for long lines of both Japanese and international visitors.
bio.specialty: Its signature is the kinchaku kitsune udon: a large pouch of custom-made fried tofu (aburaage) tied at the top, floating in a light natural dashi. Diners cut open the pouch to release chewy udon noodles into the broth, which keeps the noodles hot. The shop has also developed a curry version and seasonal pouches decorated with Nara-themed stamps such as the Great Buddha, Ashura statue, Kasuga lanterns and deer.

anecdotes.text: The pouch dish is built around a clever idea: the udon stays sealed inside the fried-tofu purse in the broth, so it stays hotter and chewier than a normal bowl until the diner tears it open.

gf_cross_contamination.text: An udon specialist where the noodles are wheat-based and the dish is wheat-and-dashi-centric; there is no gluten-free preparation, so heavy cross-contact with wheat is inherent.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth is a fish-based dashi (bonito/katsuobushi and kelp), so dishes are not vegan and share the same broth.

cuisine: 十割そば（生粉打ち・霧下そば）／和食
neighborhood: 三条通沿い（下三条町）／JR奈良・近鉄奈良駅
gf_detail: The soba itself is jūwari (100% buckwheat, pounded with no wheat binder), so the noodles are wheat-free — unusual and promising. However, the dipping broth uses bonito dashi and almost certainly contains wheat-based soy sauce, side dishes include sushi and inari, and there is no dedicated gluten-free kitchen or published allergen/cross-contamination protocol. The same pot and water are used for cooking. Not confirmed celiac-safe; a sensitive diner must ask directly about the tsuyu/soy sauce and request noodles plain. Cash-only, Japanese-speaking owner couple, so phone ahead to discuss.
vegan_detail: The 100% buckwheat noodles and the 十五穀かゆ (fifteen-grain porridge) are plant-based in themselves, and 山菜いなり (mountain-vegetable inari) may be vegetarian, but the standard soba broth/tsuyu is made with bonito (fish) dashi, so ordered as served the soba is not vegan. No vegan menu or vegan dashi is documented. A vegan could potentially eat noodles plain plus the porridge if they confirm the broth and porridge seasoning with the owners — must ask.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市下三条町24-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市下三条町24 returned [135.823395, 34.681438], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 14席（4人掛けテーブル2卓、8人掛けテーブル1卓）
closed_days: 水曜・木曜（祝日の場合は営業、翌日休みになることあり）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, traditional couple-run soba shop where ordering and any allergy questions will go far more smoothly with some basic Japanese; it is cash-only, so bring yen and a few key phrases.

bio.background: A husband-and-wife team has quietly run this little soba house for more than twenty years, tucked into a calm side lane just off the bustle of Sanjo-dori, a short walk from the Nara stations. A small frog ornament marks the door, and the interior feels like an old farmhouse.
bio.specialty: Hand-pounded jūwari (100%) buckwheat soba made from 'kirishita soba' — sweet, fragrant buckwheat grown in the cool, mist-covered highlands — worked with no wheat binder at all. The signature mori soba is served drained on a thick wooden board rather than a bamboo basket, a presentation that depends on precise water-draining technique. Simple sides round it out: fifteen-grain rice porridge (十五穀かゆ), Nara-style persimmon-leaf sushi (柿の葉寿司), and mountain-vegetable inari, alongside a thoughtfully chosen lineup of local sake.
bio.philosophy: The menu is kept deliberately spare — essentially just mori and kama-age soba — because, as the owners put it, strongly flavored dishes would kill the taste of the noodles (味の強いものはそばの味を殺してしまう). The food is meant to let the buckwheat speak for itself.

anecdotes.text: The shop is named for frogs (kaeru) and is filled with hundreds of frog figurines — many gifted by regulars over the years and displayed in the table nooks.
Serving the soba drained on a thick wooden board, rather than a basket, is described as fine craftsmanship that only works if the water is drained just right.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen or allergen handling is documented; while the noodles are 100% buckwheat with no wheat binder, sides such as persimmon-leaf sushi and inari and the soy-based broth share the same small kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping broth (tsuyu) is a robust bonito-dashi Kansai-style sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned, so standard wheat-containing soy sauce should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The standard soba broth is made with bonito (fish) dashi, so noodles served with broth are not vegan; a plant-based diner would need to confirm a fish-free option.

positives.text: The noodles are jūwari — 100% buckwheat pounded with no wheat binder (つなぎなし) — which is unusual and means the noodle itself contains no wheat.

caveats: Owner couple is Japanese-speaking and the shop is cash-only with no published allergen protocol; gluten-free and vegan diners must phone ahead (0742-24-8345) or ask in person about the broth (wheat soy sauce, bonito dashi). No official website found. The 100%-buckwheat noodle is a genuine point of interest for gluten-avoiders but is not a verified celiac-safe environment.

cuisine: 手打ち釜揚げうどん (hand-made kamaage udon)
neighborhood: ならまち／近鉄奈良駅・JR奈良駅
gf_detail: This is a hand-made wheat-flour udon shop — the entire menu centers on wheat noodles (made from flour and salt water), so it is not safe for celiacs or anyone avoiding gluten. The dipping dashi, while primarily kombu/shiitake/bonito, accompanies wheat noodles, and no gluten-free preparation exists.
vegan_detail: The signature dashi is made with three types of bonito (katsuobushi) in addition to kombu and dried shiitake, so the broth is not vegan. Complimentary lunch sides include inari-zushi and chirashi-zushi. There is no documented vegan option.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市杉ケ町17-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from the full street address via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 32 seats total, including a 14-seat tatami (zashiki) area, plus tables and one large communal table.
hours_raw: 11:00-21:30 (closed Wednesdays). Some local sources list 11:00-22:00 and an additional closure on the 3rd Tuesday; noodles are boiled to order, so allow about a 10-minute wait.
closed_days: Wednesdays (some sources also note the 3rd Tuesday)
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, traditional family-run noodle shop where staff are unlikely to speak much English, so a few basic Japanese phrases (and pointing at the kamaage udon) will help; it is cash-only.
caveats: Cash only and no reservations. The menu is entirely wheat udon with fish-dashi, so it is not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners. Hours and closed days vary slightly between sources (11:00-21:30 vs 11:00-22:00; Wednesday plus possibly the 3rd Tuesday) — confirm before a special trip.

bio.origin: The founder trained at his family's kamaage-udon restaurant in Miyazaki Prefecture before opening in Nara about 45 years ago.
bio.background: Founded roughly four to four-and-a-half decades ago, Shigenoi began on a streetside spot and moved about 25 years ago to its current quiet alley near the station. The founder, now in his late seventies, still helps in the kitchen during the day, and his son Takehide took over as the second-generation owner several years ago. The shop has drawn families across two to four generations, plus fans who travel from afar.
bio.specialty: Hand-made kamaage udon: noodles of just flour and salt water, hand-kneaded for an hour, foot-pressed for two hours, then rested for four — cut fresh to order so each strand varies a little in thickness. They are served hot in their boiling water for dipping into a rich dashi of kombu, dried shiitake, and three kinds of bonito; diners finish by pouring the remaining broth over the noodles as a soup. A weekday lunch comes with a complimentary side such as onigiri, inari-zushi, or chirashi-zushi.
bio.philosophy: The family aims to preserve both the shop's taste and its old-world atmosphere for the next generation; as Takehide puts it, the dashi is there to bring out the flavor of the udon.

anecdotes.text: The founder learned the craft at his family's kamaage-udon shop in Miyazaki, then opened Shigenoi in Nara about 45 years ago; some 25 years ago he moved it from a streetside location into the current back-alley spot, and several years ago handed the shop to his son Takehide while still helping by day.

Each batch of noodles is kneaded by hand for an hour, trodden by foot for two hours, rested four hours, then hand-cut to order — the owner cheerfully notes this gives the strands their irregular thickness and length.
The shop's vintage interior, with antique lamps and old clocks, has welcomed loyal families for generations, and the noodles are boiled fresh to order so there is about a ten-minute wait.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop's core product is hand-made wheat udon kneaded and cut on-site, so flour is present throughout the kitchen and there is no gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping dashi is based on kombu, dried shiitake, and three types of bonito; as a traditional Japanese tsuyu it is expected to contain soy sauce (and thus wheat), and is in any case served with wheat noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi contains three kinds of bonito (fish), so the broth is not vegan, and lunch sides include fish/egg items such as inari-zushi and chirashi-zushi.

cuisine: 手打ちそば（ニ八・限定十割）の個人そば店
neighborhood: ならまち／近鉄奈良駅
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The standard soba here is ni-hachi (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat flour binder), and even the limited ten-wari (100% buckwheat) option is made and boiled in the same wheat-handling kitchen, so cross-contamination is unavoidable. The dipping sauce (Kanto-style tsuyu) and side dishes like tempura bits and karaage also contain wheat. There is no documented allergy handling or dedicated equipment. Celiac travelers should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The soba dipping sauce (tsuyu) is made with fish-based dashi (katsuo/kombu typical of Kanto-style tsuyu), the menu centers on items like sweet-shrimp karaage and tempura, and even plain mori soba is served with this fish-dashi sauce. No documented plant-based or vegan options. A plain order of soba with salt could in theory avoid animal products, but the noodles' wheat content and shared kitchen make this unsuitable for strict vegans seeking a reliable meal.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東寺林町20
geocode_note: Geocoded from GSI address search to 東寺林町20; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 22 seats; townhouse-style interior with table seating and tatami areas.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:30 (lunch/daytime). Some listings note an additional evening service 17:00-20:00; daytime-only listings are most consistent. Closed Tuesdays.
closed_days: Tuesdays (some sources also note Friday evenings closed if evening service runs)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional daytime soba shop where ordering is simple but staff likely speak limited English, so a few Japanese phrases or pointing at the menu will make ordering smoother.

bio.background: Kinose is a small, owner-run soba shop tucked into Naramachi, the old merchant quarter just south of Kintetsu Nara Station, set directly in front of the Naramachi Center in a traditional townhouse with both table and tatami seating and jazz playing quietly in the background.
bio.specialty: Hand-cut soba. The standard is a ni-hachi (80% buckwheat) noodle, with a prized limited-run ten-wari (100% buckwheat, no wheat binder) available for a small surcharge. The noodles are described as pale, thin, and refreshingly moist, served Kanto-style with a dipping tsuyu or simply with salt. Beyond mori soba there are grated-daikon soba, warm soba with tempura bits, soba kaiseki sets, and five-grain onigiri.
bio.philosophy: A commitment to quality buckwheat: 100% domestic buckwheat flour prepared with Nara's soft spring water, with the ten-wari made entirely without a wheat binder. The shop leans into a relaxed soba-bar style, pairing the noodles with seasonal Nara-region sake and small appetizers like sweet-shrimp karaage and chilled tofu.

anecdotes.text: The shop pairs its soba with a rotating selection of seasonal Japanese sake and shochu plus small plates, encouraging a leisurely soba-bar style of eating rather than a quick lunch.
The limited ten-wari (100% buckwheat) soba is offered alongside the regular ni-hachi for an additional charge, reflecting the owner's focus on pure-buckwheat craft.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The default soba is ni-hachi (contains 20% wheat flour binder); the kitchen handles and boils wheat-containing noodles, so even the ten-wari option carries cross-contamination risk.
Side dishes include wheat-based tempura bits and karaage, and the dipping sauce is a Kanto-style tsuyu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with a Kanto-style dipping tsuyu, which is soy-sauce based and thus contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Tsuyu and menu are built around fish dashi and seafood items (sweet-shrimp karaage, tempura); no documented vegan preparation.

caveats: Default noodle is ni-hachi (contains wheat); 100% buckwheat (ten-wari) is a limited-quantity option for a small surcharge and is made in a shared wheat-handling kitchen. Daytime soba shop; evening service is reported by some listings but not consistently. No documented allergy accommodation.

lead: ミアズ ブレッド 本店（MIA'S BREAD）
name_ja: ミアズ ブレッド 本店
cuisine: 自家製天然酵母パン・サンドイッチ・カフェ
neighborhood: 高畑町（破石町バス停すぐ・ならまちから東、近鉄奈良駅徒歩圏）
gf_detail: This is a wheat-based artisan bakery — bâtards, baguettes, muffins, bagels, focaccia, and pita are all made from wheat flour, and sandwiches are built on that bread. There is no gluten-free bread or menu, and the shop publishes no allergen or gluten-free information. Not suitable for celiacs or those avoiding gluten.
vegan_detail: Vegetarian options are genuine and a point of pride: the menu lists vegetable-forward sandwiches such as オールベジタブル (All Vegetable) and a cheese-muffin vegetable sandwich, plus cream-cheese-and-jam and peanut-butter items. The bagels are made without egg or butter. However, fully vegan eating is not guaranteed — many breads/sandwiches contain dairy (cheese, cream cheese, butter) or egg, and there is no published vegan certification or ingredient breakdown, so a strict vegan should confirm specific items with staff. Best described as having solid vegetarian options rather than dedicated vegan.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1215-5
geocode_note: Geocoded from official current address 奈良県奈良市高畑町1215-5 via GSI; result within Nara box. Note: the Tabelog listing still shows the former Naramachi address (勝南院町2); the shop relocated to Takabatake-chō (高畑町) in 2024 per the official site.
