seats_text: Small bakery with a takeout bread counter near the entrance and a café/eat-in space in the back of a renovated old house; exact seat count not published. Café use before 11:00 is by reservation only.
hours_raw: 木〜土 11:00〜16:00、日 8:00〜16:00（パンは10:00〜10:30頃に揃う）。11時までのカフェ利用は予約のみ。月・火・水 定休。営業カレンダーに準じ臨時営業・休業あり。
closed_days: 月・火・水（臨時営業・休業あり、公式カレンダー要確認）
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly neighborhood bakery-café where you can browse loaves at the counter and point to what you want; a little Japanese helps for the eat-in café, but ordering bread is easy and welcoming.
caveats: The Tabelog page reflects the old Naramachi location; the shop moved to 高畑町 in 2024. Days off and special openings vary — check the shop's online calendar before visiting. Bread is fully stocked only around 10:00-10:30. About a 25-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, or take the city loop bus to 破石町 and walk east.

bio.background: MIA'S BREAD began roughly four decades ago from a small home kitchen and became a full bakery in 1997. Owner-baker Miwa Morita, who came to bread-making from a background in graphic design after baking as a hobby since high school, has moved the shop through several Nara locations — near Heijō Palace, then Tsubai-i and Naramachi — before settling in 2024 into a renovated old house in the Takabatake district, which Morita calls the shop's fourth and final chapter. Morita has published numerous bread- and sandwich-making cookbooks and essays.
bio.specialty: Thirty to forty kinds of additive-free natural-yeast and yeast breads daily — bâtards, baguettes (caquette), muffins, scones, and egg-and-butter-free bagels — and a celebrated range of sandwiches: thick-cut toast sandwiches, soft non-toasted sandwiches, muffin and bagel sandwiches, pan-baked focaccia for sharing, and pita pockets. Seasonal vegetable-packed sandwiches and monthly sandwich boxes with a scone are signatures.
bio.philosophy: Morita aims for bread with 'a gentle flavor you never tire of, a taste that lets you see a landscape,' made only from quality ingredients without additives, balancing the depth of natural sourdough with the lightness of yeast.

anecdotes.text: Settling into the renovated Takabatake house in 2024, Morita described it as making the bread he loves here 'with the tension out of my shoulders' — framing it as the shop's fourth and final move.
The new shop occupies a retro building on the Haraishi main street that visitors compare to 'a little diner in the American countryside.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-flour artisan bakery where flour is handled and baked throughout the premises; gluten cross-contamination is unavoidable and no gluten-free items exist.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetarian sandwiches are made in the same kitchen as meat and dairy items, and many breads contain dairy or egg; bagels are noted as egg- and butter-free but no fully vegan line is documented.

positives.text: Genuine vegetarian sandwich choices on the menu, including an All Vegetable sandwich and a cheese-muffin vegetable sandwich, plus egg-and-butter-free bagels.

cuisine: Wagashi (Japanese sweets) — freshly pounded yomogi (mugwort) mochi
neighborhood: Mochiidono Center Street / steps from Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市橋本町29
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市橋本町29 returned [135.828613, 34.681683], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Counter/storefront stand for freshly pounded mochi; primarily takeaway with no real dining seating.
hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 (until sold out); closed Tuesdays
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A busy, welcoming street-side stand where you can simply point, pay in cash, and eat a warm mochi on the spot — no Japanese needed beyond a smile, though staff focus on the crowd-pleasing pounding.
gf_detail: The signature yomogi mochi is built on glutinous rice, mugwort, sweet red bean paste, and kinako (roasted soybean flour), so the core product contains no wheat by recipe. However, this is a small artisan stand with no published allergen program, no celiac handling, and shared equipment, so it is not verified celiac-safe — ask before assuming.
vegan_detail: The yomogi mochi ingredients that are documented (Saga Hiyoku glutinous rice, Hokkaido Tokachi red beans, domestic kinako/soybean flour, Ehime mugwort) are all plant-based, so the main item appears to contain no animal products. There is no official vegan or allergen statement, so a strict vegan should confirm in person.
caveats: Hours run until sold out, so the popular yomogi mochi can run out before closing. Cash only. Allergen details are not officially published; dietary claims above are inferred from documented ingredients, not from an allergen statement.

bio.origin: Founded in 1992 in central Nara by a man from the remote Kototsu district of Kamikitayama Village in Yoshino, who wanted to share his mountain hometown's traditional mochi with a wider audience.
bio.background: Nakatanidou is a small family-run wagashi shop on Mochiidono shopping street, just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station, that has become one of Nara's most beloved sights. Several times a day the staff perform a breathtaking high-speed mochi pounding right at the storefront, drawing crowds who watch the mallet strike up to twice per second.
bio.specialty: Freshly pounded yomogi mochi (mugwort rice cake) filled with sweet red bean paste and dusted in kinako, made from carefully chosen ingredients: Saga Hiyoku glutinous rice, Hokkaido Tokachi red beans, domestic kinako, and fresh mugwort sprouts from Imabari, Ehime.
bio.philosophy: The shop insists the lightning-fast pounding is not a show but genuinely necessary work: pounding the rice while it is still piping hot keeps it from cooling and stiffening, yielding mochi that is at once soft, smooth, and beautifully stretchy.

anecdotes.text: The high-speed pounding technique was handed down in the founder's hometown of Kamikitayama, where pounding the mochi extremely fast was a practical answer to the village's bitterly cold winters, keeping the rice hot enough to stay soft.
The mallet falls at roughly two strikes per second, fast enough that watching it has become a signature Nara spectacle, with the shopfront regularly lined with onlookers.

positives.text: Documented core ingredients of the yomogi mochi (glutinous rice, mugwort, red beans, kinako, sugar) are all plant-based and naturally wheat-free, making the signature item plausibly suitable for both vegan and wheat-avoiding diners if confirmed in person.

name_ja: 茶・アートカフェ うつぎ
cuisine: Japanese tea house / wagashi cafe (町家風の日本茶喫茶・甘味)
neighborhood: ならまち／京終・西木辻（猿沢池の南）
gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are made anywhere on the cafe's pages. The menu centers on brewed Nara teas served with seasonal wagashi (上生菓子), dessert plates, and baked goods. Wagashi can sometimes be naturally wheat-free (rice/bean based) but baked goods contain wheat, and no allergen handling is documented. A celiac visitor would need to ask in person about each item; nothing here is verified safe.
vegan_detail: No vegan labeling is documented. The core offering is tea (vegan by nature) paired with seasonal Japanese sweets; drinks include hojicha latte and chai (likely dairy), and dessert plates may include baked goods with eggs/dairy. Some wagashi are plant-based, but this is not verified per item. A vegan visitor should confirm the specific sweet and whether latte/chai use dairy milk.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西木辻町200-62 ナカタニビル1F
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the central Nara box for 奈良市西木辻町200-62.
seats_text: Seat count not published. Described as a compact shoes-off (靴を脱いであがる) wood-finished space; a small intimate tearoom.
hours_raw: 11:00–17:30 (L.O.). Open Monday and Thursday–Sunday; closed Tuesday and Wednesday. Tabelog lists hours as 11:00–18:00; the cafe's own page states 11:00–17:30 last order.
closed_days: Tuesday, Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, traditional shoes-off tearoom run by a tea practitioner; a foreign student is welcome but should expect a calm, Japanese-language setting where you choose and savor tea slowly.
caveats: Cash only. Hours differ slightly between listings (own page says 11:00–17:30 L.O., Tabelog shows to 18:00). Closed Tuesday and Wednesday, with extra closures posted on Instagram. Compact shoes-off space.

bio.background: Utsugi is a small, individually run tea-and-art cafe tucked into the quiet southern edge of Naramachi. You take your shoes off at the entrance and settle into an intimate, wood-finished room built for slowing down.
bio.origin: Nara — the cafe pours teas grown in Nara Prefecture (Yamato tea), including roji sencha, naturally-grown sencha and black tea, kamairi-cha (pan-fired tea), and hojicha.
bio.specialty: A tea service (about 1,000 yen) where you choose a Nara tea to match your mood, brewed in an iron kettle (tetsubin) for three to five infusions and paired with a seasonal fresh wagashi (jō-namagashi). There are also dessert plates and glass drinks such as hojicha latte, roasted chai, and ginger ale.
bio.philosophy: The host's idea is to choose your tea and tea vessel according to your body and mood and brew it carefully for yourself — 体調や気分に合わせてお茶を選び、茶器を選び、自分の為に丁寧に淹れる. The space doubles as an art cafe, inviting reading, drawing, and writing while you sip.

anecdotes.text: Beyond tea, the cafe offers hands-on experiences using Nara craft materials, including suminagashi (marbled ink painting) with local brushes, ink and paper, plus casual and formal tea-ceremony (otemae) sessions.

positives.text: The menu is built around brewed Nara teas, which are naturally gluten-free and vegan; pairing sweets can be discussed item by item with the host.

lead: 音楽と珈琲 ジョニー
name_ja: 音楽と珈琲 ジョニー
cuisine: レコードの流れる純喫茶・珈琲とチーズケーキ
gf_detail: This is a coffee shop whose food menu centers on cheesecake and toast — both wheat-based — so it is not a gluten-free destination and there is no indication of gluten-free preparation or celiac accommodation. Coffee itself is naturally gluten-free, but the kitchen is not set up for cross-contamination control.
vegan_detail: A small owner-run kissaten focused on coffee. Black coffee can be ordered without dairy, but the food offerings (cheesecake, toast) are not vegan and there is no documented vegan menu. A vegan would need to limit themselves to plain coffee and confirm directly.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市光明院町10-2 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市光明院町10-2 returned [135.829086, 34.679798], within the central Nara box and consistent with the Naramachi / Mochiidono-area location near Kintetsu Nara Station.
neighborhood: ならまち（餅飯殿センター街付近）／近鉄奈良駅徒歩圏
seats_text: Small second-floor space: about 6 counter seats plus a few table seats in the back; intimate, single-room kissaten.
closed_days: Thursdays (木曜定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, owner-run vinyl-records kissaten reached by narrow alley stairs — comfortable for a foreign student who can order simply in a little Japanese and respects the calm, no-fuss atmosphere.
caveats: Cash only, no reservations, and seating is very limited, so it can fill up; closed Thursdays. The food menu is small (mainly coffee, cheesecake, toast) and not suited to gluten-free or vegan diets.

bio.background: A hideaway coffee shop tucked on the second floor above the Mochiidono shopping street in the old Naramachi quarter, reached by a narrow passage and a steep flight of stairs that open onto a quiet, retro-styled room set apart from the bustle of the city.
bio.philosophy: Coffee and music, side by side: the owner runs the place single-handedly, brewing each cup with care while jazz plays from vinyl records, creating an unhurried space made for slowing down.
bio.specialty: Carefully brewed coffee paired with homemade cheesecake; toast is also on the short, coffee-centered menu.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe it as a hidden, secret-base-like coffee house where jazz drifts from records and the master quietly tends the counter.

cuisine: 石臼挽き北海道産そば粉の手打ち九割そば（個人店）
neighborhood: 水門町（東大寺・奈良公園そば）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house soba is "gai-ichi" (外一) / 九割そば: 90% buckwheat flour with 10% wheat flour used as a binder, so even the noodles contain wheat. The dipping broth (tsuyu) and tempura batter are also wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Unsuitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. As a traditional soba shop the tsuyu (dipping/serving broth) is made with fish-based dashi, so standard soba dishes are not vegan. Some components could in principle be plant-based — e.g. Yoshino kudzu sashimi tofu, Yamato yam tororo, and vegetable tempura (Nara vegetables) — but the broth and shared kitchen mean a fully vegan meal is not confirmed. A vegan diner would need to ask in advance; no documented vegan handling.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市水門町50
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.835876, 34.686699] for 奈良県奈良市水門町50; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 18 seats total: three 2-seat tables and three 4-seat tatami rooms.
hours_raw: 11:00–16:00 (open until soba sells out); evening service available by request. Closed Tuesdays and the second Wednesday of each month.
closed_days: Tuesdays and the second Wednesday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional walk-in soba house in a quiet residential lane near Todai-ji; a few words of Japanese and patience with a cash-only, no-reservations spot will go a long way for a foreign student.

bio.background: Kitahara occupies a traditional single-family house in the quiet Mizumon-cho lane just beside the grounds of Todai-ji and Nara Park. The shop took its current name and reopened on April 1, 2011, having previously operated under the name よし川 (Yoshikawa). A large Shigaraki tanuki statue at the entrance is its landmark, and windows look out onto flowers, birds and the greenery of Nara Park while you eat.
bio.specialty: Hand-cut 九割 (gai-ichi/外一) soba — 90% buckwheat to 10% wheat binder — made from Hokkaido buckwheat that is stone-milled fresh in-house every morning, served the traditional 'three fresh' way (freshly milled, freshly cut, freshly boiled). Signature plates include ebi-oroshi soba (shrimp tempura with grated daikon), tempura zaru soba with seasonal Nara vegetables, and Yamato-yam tororo soba; the thickened soba-yu (buckwheat broth) poured at the end is a favorite, alongside Yoshino-kudzu sashimi tofu.
bio.philosophy: A focus on freshly stone-milled buckwheat and Nara-grown vegetables, prizing the aroma and texture that comes only from grinding and cutting the soba each day.

anecdotes.text: The buckwheat seeds are ground every morning on a stone mill in the shop, and the soba is cut by hand and boiled to order, so each bowl reflects the day's fresh milling.

Housed in a converted single-family home near Todai-ji and marked by a big Shigaraki tanuki, with views of Nara Park's flowers and birds from the windows; the signature ebi-oroshi soba is praised for its fresh, smooth-yet-springy noodles with grated daikon and crisp shrimp and vegetable tempura.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The soba itself is 90% buckwheat / 10% wheat (gai-ichi), so the noodles contain wheat; tempura batter and a shared kitchen mean wheat is present throughout. Not suitable for gluten avoidance.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional soba tsuyu is soy-sauce based, which normally contains wheat; no wheat-free broth is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Soba broth (tsuyu/soba-yu) at a classic soba shop is made with fish-based dashi, and tempura share fryer/kitchen with seafood items, so cross-contact with animal products is likely.

positives.text: Buckwheat is stone-milled in-house each morning and the soba is hand-cut and boiled to order, so ingredients are fresh and the menu is simple and transparent.

caveats: 九割 soba contains wheat binder — not gluten-free. Tsuyu uses fish dashi — not vegan. Hours listed as 11:00–16:00 but close early when soba sells out; closed Tuesdays plus the 2nd Wednesday monthly (hence irregular). Cash only, walk-ins only. Seat count and former name (よし川) confirmed via Tabelog.

lead: 日本酒とおばんざい 新奈
name_ja: 日本酒とおばんざい 新奈
cuisine: Japanese sake bar serving home-style obanzai (small seasonal dishes) paired with regional sake, warm-sake focused
gf_detail: Not gluten-free in any meaningful sense for a celiac. This is a sake bar built around omakase obanzai (Japanese/Western/Chinese-influenced home cooking) where dishes routinely use soy sauce, miso and pasta, and sake itself is rice-based but the kitchen makes no gluten-free claims. No dedicated gluten-free menu or handling is documented. A celiac traveler should treat this as not safe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The menu is omakase obanzai that frequently includes fish, meat and seasonal Tanba game (jibie), and dishes typically rely on fish/bonito dashi. Some individual vegetable obanzai exist (e.g. beet potage, dried daikon, vegetable rolls), but there is no documented vegan menu and the proprietor decides the courses, so a vegan would need to discuss requirements in advance via Instagram DM; outcome is not guaranteed.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向北町6
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市東向北町6; within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: 東向北商店街 (Higashimuki-kita shopping street), immediately by Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: 8 counter seats only (L-shaped counter in a renovated townhouse); up to about 10 people accepted for reservations
hours_raw: 17:00-23:00 (Tabelog); one local guide lists 17:00-22:00. Evening-only operation. Irregular closures announced via Instagram.
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays, plus irregular closures (check Instagram before visiting)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 8-seat counter run by one proprietress with no printed menu and a budget around 6,000-8,000 yen, so a foreign student should expect Japanese-language conversation, cash payment, and an advance reservation (Instagram DM) to feel comfortable.

bio.background: Niina opened on August 4, 2023, in a restored traditional townhouse along the Higashimuki-kita shopping street, just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is a one-woman counter place: an L-shaped counter for only eight guests, lit by hanging lamps over warm wood, with a quiet, nostalgic Showa-era feel that glows like a hideaway after the surrounding shops close early for the night.
bio.specialty: Home-style obanzai served as a small omakase course alongside carefully chosen sake, with a particular love of warm sake (kanzake). Dishes are humble home cooking elevated with a little extra technique and span Japanese, Western and Chinese influences; seasonal game (jibie) from the Tanba area of Kyoto appears in the colder months. Sampled plates have included beet potage, octopus-and-pasta salad, dried daikon with perilla, cabbage rolls with yacon, and yellowtail-and-daikon rolls.
bio.philosophy: There is no printed sake list; the bottles lining the shelves are all available, and the proprietress talks with each guest about preferred flavors and serving temperatures before suggesting a few to choose from. The aim is gentle, attentive hospitality that pairs everyday home cooking with sake that suits the moment.

anecdotes.text: The proprietress holds a SAKE DIPLOMA and is a certified sake sommelier (kikisake-shi) with a special fondness for warm sake, and is known for soft, gentle hospitality that guides guests through her selection by first learning their taste.
Because it is evening-only and the rest of the Nara shopping street closes early, its lit doorway around 8pm reads like a hidden retreat tucked into the quiet arcade.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu is home-style obanzai blending Japanese, Western and Chinese cooking, which typically involves soy sauce and miso (wheat-containing) and dishes such as pasta salad; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Omakase courses regularly include fish, meat and seasonal game and generally rely on Japanese dashi, so vegan separation is not assured; requirements would need to be arranged in advance.

positives.text: With only eight counter seats and a single proprietress who chooses the courses and discusses each guest's preferences directly, there is room to talk through dietary needs in advance via Instagram, though no formal allergy program is documented.

caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources (Tabelog 17:00-23:00 vs one local guide 17:00-22:00); closed days (Mon/Tue) come from a local guide while Tabelog lists only "irregular closures via Instagram" — confirm via the Instagram before visiting. Owner's name, hometown and detailed personal story are not documented in the fetched sources.

cuisine: 純喫茶・自家焙煎コーヒー (retro kissaten / self-roasted coffee)
gf_detail: No gluten-free offerings documented. This is a traditional Japanese coffeehouse whose food menu centers on wheat-based items: an egg sandwich on bread, toast morning sets, Taiwanese castella cake, and homemade cakes/pudding. Nothing on the menu or in any source indicates gluten-free preparation or awareness, and there is no dedicated gluten-free handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan options documented. The signature food is an egg sandwich, and morning sets include toast with a boiled egg; cakes and pudding are dairy/egg based. Drinks are coffee-focused (hand-drip blends) and could be taken black, but milk-based and dessert items dominate and no plant-milk or vegan menu is mentioned. Not a vegan destination.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市花芝町6-2 プラザ花芝 101
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市花芝町6 returned [135.828766, 34.686535], within the central Nara box. Chō/banchi level; building-level pin not available.
neighborhood: きたまち・花芝商店街（近鉄奈良駅 北側）
seats_text: Counter seating for about 8 plus one table for 4 (roughly 12 seats); small intimate space.
hours_raw: 火～日 08:00-18:00（モーニングは11時まで）／月曜定休
closed_days: Monday
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, family-run neighborhood coffeehouse where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed attitude go a long way; English is unlikely, but pointing at the menu and ordering coffee with toast is easy and welcoming for a foreign student.
caveats: Food menu is wheat- and egg-heavy with no documented gluten-free or vegan accommodations; not suitable for celiac or strict vegan diners. Hours and closed day confirmed via Tabelog; seat count is approximate from a feature article.

bio.chef_name: Higo Yoko (current owner); Higo Tatsuya (second-generation master)
bio.background: Cafe CROCO carries a half-century of Nara coffee history. The original master opened the first shop near JR Kyobate in 1970, then moved it to the Yamato-Saidaiji area in 1973, where it became beloved as 'Kohii-kan Kuroko.' When that building aged, the shop relocated in 2017 to the Hanashiba shopping street on the north side of Kintetsu Nara Station, where it continues today.
bio.specialty: The Croco Blend - four bean varieties roasted dark, freshly ground and hand-dripped one cup at a time for deep bitterness balanced by bright acidity. The fluffy tamago (egg) sandwich with cucumber and a light hand on the mayonnaise is a signature, alongside Taiwanese castella sweetened with brown sugar and honey, plus homemade cakes and pudding.
bio.philosophy: Self-roasting was pioneered here around 1990 when the founder taught himself the craft - rare for the time - and that dedication to roasting and careful hand-drip brewing has been handed down through the generations and even spun off a separate roaster, TABI Coffee Roaster.

anecdotes.text: Antique stained-glass doors, vintage coffee mills, bean-filled jars behind the counter, and oversized custom ceramic cups made by a Kiyomizu potter give the room a timeless, retro character that draws students, office workers, and devoted regulars alike.

A legendary novelty item once existed: a 9,650-yen coffee served in an enormous cup holding about 30 servings, created to honor Nara's traditional Ocha-mori tea ceremony - reportedly ordered by only one group.
The shop's commitment to roasting was so formative that an employee went on to independently open TABI Coffee Roaster in 2022, which now roasts CROCO's beans.

cuisine: Soba (handmade buckwheat noodles), tempura
neighborhood: Naramachi (Nishinoya-cho, near the Naramachi Museum)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西新屋町18
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市西新屋町18, within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 24 seats; small non-smoking shop with table seating, no private rooms.
hours_raw: 11:00–14:00 (Tue–Sun); local sources note hours sometimes given as 11:30–14:00 until sold out.
closed_days: Mondays (some local listings also note an irregular Friday closure)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, lunch-only local soba shop where little to no English is likely, so a few Japanese words and pointing at the tempura zaru soba will go a long way.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The buckwheat noodles are paired with wheat-batter tempura, and the dipping sauce (tsuyu) is made with wheat-based soy sauce; even if a soba is 100% buckwheat there is heavy wheat handling in the same small kitchen. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Soba and tempura are served with a bonito/fish-dashi tsuyu (dipping broth), so even plain zaru soba is not vegan, and no plant-based broth option is documented.
caveats: Lunch only and closes when the day's soba sells out, so arrive early; closure days can be irregular (Monday always, sometimes Friday). The signature soba is described locally as 100% buckwheat (十割/そば粉100%), but a related sibling shop is described as 二八 (80/20) — confirm with staff if buckwheat ratio matters to you.

bio.background: A small, family-style soba shop tucked into the lanes of Naramachi near the Naramachi Museum, Ginsho has been serving the neighborhood for around three decades and is supported largely by local regulars.
bio.specialty: Handmade buckwheat soba — the house pride is its smooth throat-feel and aroma — with the most-ordered dish being tempura zaru soba (cold soba with a plate of freshly fried tempura).
bio.philosophy: Keep it freshly made and freshly fried: the shop's stated aim is that every bowl is uchitate (just-cut) soba and agetate (just-fried) tempura, with no gimmicks.

anecdotes.text: The shop has run in Naramachi for roughly 32 years on the strength of neighborhood regulars, priding itself on 100% buckwheat noodles with a distinctive throat-feel and aroma.
Its signature tempura zaru soba is always served just-cut and just-fried, drawing both locals and sightseers strolling Naramachi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura is fried in wheat batter and served alongside the soba, so a shared small kitchen handles wheat heavily.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is eaten with a tsuyu dipping sauce, which in a traditional shop like this is made with wheat-containing soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The tsuyu broth is fish/bonito-dashi based, so even vegetable items and plain soba are not vegan.

lead: 大和野菜とせいろ蒸し すみか
name_ja: 大和野菜とせいろ蒸し すみか
name_en: Yamato Yasai to Seiro Mushi Sumika
cuisine: 和食・居酒屋（大和野菜とせいろ蒸し、地酒）
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（小西町・三条通エリア）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. This is a Japanese izakaya where dishes are seasoned with soy sauce (which contains wheat) and typically use fish dashi; the menu also features Miwa sōmen, which is wheat noodles. There is no documented gluten-free menu or allergy protocol, so celiac and gluten-sensitive diners cannot eat safely here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-focused, but vegetable-forward. The kitchen centers on Yamato (Nara) vegetables and offers a vegetable seiro (bamboo-steamer) basket and vegetable appetizers such as bagna cauda with root vegetables and sautéed mushrooms. However, the restaurant primarily serves Yamato chicken and pork, and standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, likely fish dashi) are used, so most dishes are not vegan by default. A strict vegan would need to ask in advance about which vegetable dishes can be prepared without animal-derived stock or fish products; no dedicated vegan menu is documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市小西町2-1 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 奈良市小西町2 (chō-block level); within the central Nara box. Building is on the 2nd floor near the Sanjō-dōri / Konishi-chō area.
seats_text: Around 26 seats (counter and tables; sources vary, with Tabelog listing 30). Half-private rooms available; can accommodate groups of 20-26 for private events.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 17:00-23:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays 16:00-23:00 (L.O. 22:30). Open year-round (年中無休).
closed_days: None (open year-round, 年中無休)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed modern izakaya where a foreign student should be comfortable with a few words of Japanese and a friendly attitude; an English menu is not guaranteed, so pointing at dishes and basic phrases help.
caveats: Seat count differs between sources (Tabelog lists 30; the official page lists about 26). No documented allergen, gluten-free, or strict-vegan protocol, so dietary travelers should confirm directly. No founder/chef background story was found in available sources.

bio.background: A modern Japanese izakaya a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, in the Konishi-chō area near Sanjō-dōri. The dining room has a calm wood-and-white interior with counter and table seating and half-private nooks.
bio.specialty: The signature is seiro-mushi: ingredients steamed in stacked bamboo baskets so they keep their nutrients and natural flavor. Baskets come in vegetable, pork, and a three-variety combination, built around Yamato (Nara) vegetables and Yamato chicken. Other dishes include jako-Manganji (small fish with green peppers), shrimp-and-lotus-root ahijo, bagna cauda with root vegetables, and Miwa sōmen, paired with local Nara sake.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated focus is showcasing local Nara ingredients - Yamato vegetables, Yamato chicken, and regional sake - and cooking them gently by steaming to draw out each ingredient's flavor in a healthy way.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes Miwa sōmen (wheat noodles) and dishes seasoned with soy sauce; no gluten-free preparation or separation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese izakaya seasoning relies on soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen prepares Yamato chicken and pork alongside vegetable dishes; vegetable items likely use fish dashi or shared cooking, so vegan diners must ask in advance.

positives.text: Strong vegetable-forward focus with a dedicated vegetable seiro basket and several Yamato-vegetable appetizers, giving plant-based diners real (if not certified-vegan) choices.

lead: 大和薬食処 ならやま茶館
name_ja: 大和薬食処 ならやま茶館
cuisine: Yamato regional yakuzen (medicinal) cuisine centered on seasonal organic Yamato vegetables and tea porridge
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（ならまち・えびす小路）
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and not celiac-safe by default. The cuisine centers on chagayu (Yamato tea porridge) and Japanese home-style preparations that typically involve wheat-containing soy sauce and miso, and the kitchen uses natural seafood-based broth. The restaurant does take a constitution/preference check sheet and says it can tailor dishes to a guest's preferences and symptoms, so a gluten-sensitive diner could ask in advance — but no dedicated gluten-free handling, gluten-free soy sauce, or cross-contamination control is documented. Treat as "ask in advance," not safe for strict celiacs.
vegan_detail: Vegetable-forward but not vegan. The menu can accommodate guests who prefer no meat, however the kitchen explicitly still uses fish, eggs, and dairy, and the broth/dashi is made from natural ingredients from the sea (i.e., fish-based). So a no-meat (vegetarian-leaning) plate is possible on request, but a strict vegan meal is not documented. Vegans would need to ask whether fully plant-based dishes and a kombu-only broth can be prepared in advance; this is not confirmed.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南市町14-6
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市南市町14 within the Nara box.
seats_text: 15 seats (4 counter, 11 table) in a traditional machiya
hours_raw: Mon 11:30-13:45 (L.O. 13:30), 17:30-20:00 (L.O. food 19:00, drinks 19:15); Tue-Sun 11:30-13:45 (lunch), 17:30-19:00 (dinner). Dishes are prepared to order from reservations.
closed_days: Irregular holidays (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, reservation-based machiya where dishes are made to order, so book ahead and be ready to communicate your needs in Japanese (or with a translation app); the owner uses a preference/health check sheet that helps bridge the gap.
caveats: Reservations are essentially required (accepted from one month prior) because dishes are prepared to order; cash only. Hours are irregular and the kitchen closes early, especially for dinner. The "no meat" option still uses fish, eggs, and dairy, so it is not suitable for strict vegans, and the seafood-based dashi and likely wheat-containing seasonings mean it is not celiac-safe.

bio.background: Narayama Sakan is a small medicinal-cuisine (yakuzen) house tucked into a traditional machiya along Ebisu Alley (Ebisu Koji) in Naramachi, a few minutes' walk from central Nara. The kitchen relocated here to the historic merchant quarter after earlier operating in the Takano area, settling into the kind of old wooden townhouse setting that suits its nostalgic, home-cooking spirit.
bio.origin: Naramachi, Nara City (after an earlier location in Takano)
bio.specialty: Yamato-style medicinal home cooking built around seasonal organic Yamato vegetables and the local tradition of chagayu (tea porridge). Signature offerings include a seasonal medicinal-food course and an omakase plate that rotates with the seasons -- typically a protein such as mackerel or balsamic-style chicken, vegetable tempura, several small side dishes using different techniques, rice that can be served as grains or as tea porridge, and a dessert -- alongside herbal teas chosen for their documented wellness benefits.
bio.philosophy: The cooking is guided by the idea that everyday meals quietly adjust the body and mind. The kitchen leans on locally sourced seasonal organic vegetables and natural broths rather than chemical seasonings, aiming for gentle, easy-to-eat flavors that move away from the bitter image people often associate with medicinal food. Guests fill out a constitution/preference sheet so dishes can be tailored to their tastes and how they're feeling.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant accommodates solo diners and offers a children's plate by reservation, and can adjust toward a no-meat meal on request (though fish, eggs, and dairy are still used).
It moved from the Takano area into a machiya along Ebisu Alley in the Naramachi district, reflecting a turn toward the historic merchant quarter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Cuisine centers on tea porridge and Japanese home-style dishes that typically use wheat-containing soy sauce and miso; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Yamato home cooking and tea porridge sides are normally seasoned with standard (wheat-based) soy sauce; gluten-free soy sauce is not mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The broth/dashi is made from natural ingredients from the sea (fish-based), and the kitchen uses fish, eggs, and dairy even on no-meat plates, so dishes are not vegan by default.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guests complete a constitution/preference check sheet and the kitchen says it can prepare dishes tailored to a diner's preferences and symptoms, so dietary needs can be discussed when reserving.

positives.text: Heavily vegetable-forward, using locally sourced seasonal organic Yamato vegetables prepared without chemical seasonings, with the option to adjust toward a meat-free plate on request.

lead: 土佐料理とおばんざい 柚のす
name_ja: 土佐料理とおばんざい 柚のす
cuisine: Tosa (Kochi) regional cuisine and obanzai home-style dishes; izakaya with regional sake
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅・JR奈良駅 徒歩圏（やすらぎの道沿い）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市林小路町13-1 第2福田ビル3F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from chō-level address 奈良県奈良市林小路町13-1; result within Nara box.
seats_text: 20 seats total: a 7-seat counter where the day's obanzai are displayed, plus tatami seating in the back accommodating up to about 13 (2-, 4-, and 5-person tables). No private rooms.
hours_raw: 15:00-23:00 (Food L.O. 22:00, Drink L.O. 22:30). Closed Wednesdays.
closed_days: Wednesday (regular closing day changed to Wednesday from November 2025)
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, owner-run local izakaya where a little Japanese helps; the proprietress is friendly and chatty, so a foreign student comfortable with basic Japanese (or a translation app) will be well looked after.
gf_detail: This is a Tosa-style izakaya built around fish, dashi, and soy-based seasoning, with deep-fried items (croquettes, dumplings) and ponzu/soy on signature dishes like bonito tataki. No gluten-free menu, no celiac accommodation documented, and soy sauce here contains wheat. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A Kochi regional seafood/meat izakaya: straw-grilled bonito (katsuo) tataki, Shimanto eel, Tosa akaushi beef hamburger, and dashi-rolled egg are the core offerings. Dishes rely on fish dashi and bonito, so even the obanzai sides are generally not vegan. No vegan menu documented.

bio.background: Yunosu is a small Tosa-cuisine and obanzai izakaya tucked on the third floor of a mixed-use building along Yasuragi-no-michi, a short walk from central Nara's stations. It is run by a warm, Kochi-connected proprietress who brings the flavors and hospitality of her home prefecture to Nara, sourcing ingredients shipped directly from Kochi.
bio.specialty: The signature is straw-grilled bonito tataki (warayaki katsuo no tataki) served Tosa-style; the recommended shio-tataki pairs Tosa salt with yuzu juice to lift the fish. Other Kochi specialties include Shimanto eel served three ways (kabayaki, shirayaki, and salt-grilled), a Tosa akaushi beef hand-kneaded hamburger, one-bite Anbei-style gyoza, house-made bonito-flake croquettes with fluffy potato, and a dashi-rolled egg fragrant with aonori from the Shimanto River.
bio.philosophy: To share authentic Tosa home cooking and hospitality in a relaxed, unhurried setting, letting guests linger over Kochi ingredients and sake.

anecdotes.text: The drinks list leans hard into Kochi: regional sake such as Suigei and Miyoshigumi's Bishafu, alongside Reihoku shochu made from Kochi rice, plus a rotating selection of regional sake from around Japan.
The counter doubles as a display of the day's obanzai home-style dishes, with tatami seating in back, making it comfortable for everyone from solo diners to small groups.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes are seasoned with ponzu and soy sauce (e.g., bonito tataki), and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is fish- and dashi-centered (bonito tataki, bonito-flake croquettes, dashi egg), so animal-derived stock is used broadly across dishes including sides.

caveats: Older listings (gNavi, Nara gourmet guide) cite Tuesday as the closing day, but Tabelog and Hot Pepper both state the regular closing day changed to Wednesday from November 2025; Wednesday is used here as current. Seat count reported as 20 total across sources; the spec's "7-seat counter plus tatami" matches the layout (7 counter + tatami up to ~13). No official standalone website found; online reservations run through Gurunavi/Hot Pepper.

cuisine: 洋食
neighborhood: 高天町（近鉄奈良駅直結・近鉄高天ビル地下）
gf_detail: This is a Showa-era yōshoku (Japanese-Western) diner whose menu is built on wheat-heavy, gluten-laden dishes: チェンバーライス is finished with demi-glace sauce (wheat-roux based), and the lineup centers on breaded chicken cutlets (チキンカツ), hamburg steak, and dishes dressed in Worcestershire/demi-glace/ketchup sauces. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no allergen handling described. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-egg-focused Western diner. The signature dish is seasoned/garlic rice topped with egg and sausage under a meat-based demi-glace; other mains are chicken cutlet and hamburg steak. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented, and core sauces are meat/dashi-based. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高天町38-3 近鉄高天ビル B1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI at chō/banchi level (高天町38) to lat 34.684013, lng 135.825607 — within the central Nara box and consistent with the Kintetsu Nara Station underground building location.
seats_text: 25席（カウンター・テーブル席）
hours_raw: 11:30-14:00, 17:30-23:00（ランチ・ディナー）。日曜・祝日定休。（注: 一部情報源ではランチ開始を11:00と記載）
closed_days: 日曜日・祝日
cultural_comfort_note: A small, cash-only old-school diner where the menu and warm master are entirely in Japanese, so basic Japanese (or pointing at the famous Chamber Rice) makes the visit smoothest.
caveats: Lunch opening time differs slightly between sources (11:00 vs 11:30); hours shown use the Tabelog 11:30 start. Cash only — bring yen. Smoking is permitted throughout the room.

bio.background: A long-running family-style Western diner tucked into the underground arcade of the Takaten building, directly linked to Kintetsu Nara Station. The shop traces back to the postwar Showa era, originally opened as a restaurant tied to the Nara Chamber of Commerce; when that building was rebuilt roughly four decades ago, the diner relocated to its present basement spot, carrying its retro Showa atmosphere with it.
bio.specialty: The namesake チェンバーライス (Chamber Rice): seasoned garlic pilaf-style rice crowned with soft egg and sausage and blanketed in a rich house demi-glace that locals describe as a peppery, garlicky blend reminiscent of Worcestershire, demi-glace and ketchup found nowhere else. Diners can bump it up to a volume version by adding fried chicken, chicken cutlet, hamburg steak or extra egg. The kitchen also turns out classic yōshoku plates like chicken cutlet and hamburg steak.

anecdotes.text: The room keeps an unmistakable Showa-era mood, more like a snug old bar-café than a modern restaurant, with counter and table seating for about 25.
Regulars describe the master as a warm, kind-hearted host whose courtesy toward customers is felt at the table.

Said to date to the postwar Showa period and to have started as the Nara Chamber of Commerce restaurant before moving to its current underground location when that building was rebuilt about 40 years ago.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is wheat-centric: demi-glace sauce (wheat-roux based) tops the signature rice dish, and mains include breaded chicken cutlet and hamburg steak — high gluten exposure with no documented gluten-free handling.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes are built on meat, egg, sausage and meat-based demi-glace; no vegan or vegetarian preparations are documented.

name_ja: エニー ビー&ビー+コーヒー（ANY B&B+COFFEE）
cuisine: 自家焙煎スペシャルティコーヒー＆カフェ（朝食・ブランチ、グルテンフリー＆ヴィーガンの焼き菓子・スイーツ、自家製グラノーラ）
gf_detail: A specialty coffee cafe (not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen) that offers rotating gluten-free baked goods and sweets, often sourced from a local organic partner. Hot sandwiches and toast on the menu are wheat-based, so a shared/non-dedicated environment should be assumed. Confirm specific gluten-free items available on the day, as offerings rotate.
vegan_detail: Vegan-friendly options are available: plant-based (vegan) baked goods and sweets that rotate daily, plus house-made granola, and alternative (non-dairy) milks for coffee drinks. Not a fully vegan kitchen — egg/cheese sandwiches and dairy are also served — so ask which items are vegan on the day.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西城戸町23-4
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市西城戸町23-4; within the Nara bounding box. Located along Yasuragi-no-michi, about an 8-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara and JR Nara stations.
neighborhood: ならまち／西城戸町（やすらぎの道沿い）
seats_text: About 11 seats; small cafe on the ground floor with a B&B room upstairs (one group per day).
hours_raw: 月・火・金・土・日 08:00-17:00／水・木 定休（食べログ表記）。公式サイトでは平日7:00-17:00、週末8:00-17:00、木曜定休と案内。営業時間・定休日は媒体により表記が異なるため要確認。
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday (per Tabelog). Note: the official site lists Thursday only as the regular closed day, with weekday opening from 07:00 — hours/closures vary by source, so confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: The owners trained in Melbourne and run an English-language hospitality podcast, so English-speaking visitors will feel comfortable ordering and asking about menu items.
caveats: Hours and closed days differ between the official site and Tabelog (official lists Thursday-only closure with 07:00 weekday opening; Tabelog lists Wed/Thu closed, 08:00 open) — confirm directly. Older Nara guide pages mark earlier ANY locations (a Yasuragi-no-michi spot and a separate COFFEE BREW BAR) as 閉店/closed; the current Saijodo-cho cafe is operating (official site and recent listings confirm). Gluten-free and vegan items rotate and may be sourced from a partner bakery, so availability varies by day.

bio.origin: Nara, Japan (owners trained in Melbourne, Australia)
bio.background: ANY B&B + COFFEE is run by a husband-and-wife team, Yutaro and Chiharu, who developed their craft as a barista and roaster in Melbourne, Australia before returning to Nara. They built a small in-house roastery and cafe along Yasuragi-no-michi in central Nara, a short walk from both Kintetsu Nara and JR Nara stations, with a single B&B room upstairs where overnight guests wake to the smell of fresh coffee and a complimentary breakfast.
bio.specialty: House-roasted specialty coffee — filter and espresso drinks — built largely around African beans from origins such as Kenya and Ethiopia, served alongside all-day breakfast and brunch, house-made granola, hot sandwiches, and a daily-rotating selection of gluten-free and vegan baked goods.
bio.philosophy: The couple frame their work around storytelling and human connection — choosing beans with meaningful backstories and economic significance to producing countries, and leaning into sustainability with compostable coffee bags, glass-bottle reuse, and zero-waste practices.

anecdotes.text: The owners met and trained in Melbourne as barista and roaster, then brought that specialty-coffee culture back to Nara, building a cafe that doubles as a one-group-per-day bed and breakfast.

They focus on African-origin beans (Kenya, Ethiopia) chosen for their stories and value to the growing regions, and serve drinks in handmade ceramics, including handleless cups by a Melbourne-based Scottish ceramic artist.
Gluten-free and vegan baked goods rotate daily and are sourced in part from a local organic partner shop; non-dairy milk options are available for coffee.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen; wheat-based hot sandwiches and toast are prepared in the same small cafe, so cross-contact is possible for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy and egg/cheese items (e.g., egg-and-cheese sandwiches, cow's milk) are handled in the same space, so vegan items are made in a non-vegan environment.

positives.text: Offers a daily-rotating selection of gluten-free and vegan baked goods and sweets alongside house-made granola.
Provides non-dairy (alternative) milk options for coffee drinks.
Melbourne-trained, English-comfortable owners make it easy to ask about ingredients.

cuisine: 洋食・喫茶 (yoshoku / old-school kissaten) — chicken nanban with homemade tartar sauce, mix fry, tonkatsu
neighborhood: 御所市栄町 (Gose City, Sakaemachi) — near Gose Station, NOT central Nara
address_ja: 奈良県御所市栄町60-23
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to [lng 135.74028, lat 34.463333], which is in Gose City and well OUTSIDE the central Nara box (lat 34.64–34.74, lng 135.76–135.90), so lat/lng set null. The restaurant sits ~30km south of central Nara, about 477m / a few minutes from Gose Station — not near Kintetsu Nara Station as the target area stated.
seats_text: 26 seats: 6 counter, four 4-tops, two 2-tops (per Tabelog)
hours_raw: Sources conflict: Tabelog lists lunch only (Mon, Wed–Sun 11:30–14:30); the Gose City official page and Nara Gourmet list two services 12:00–15:00 and 18:30–21:00. Closed Tuesdays. No reservations; first-come, first-served.
closed_days: Tuesdays (火曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, old-fashioned local yoshoku/kissaten where ordering is in Japanese and there's no English menu, so come ready to point and use a few words of Japanese.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free in any form. The menu is breaded, deep-fried Japanese yoshoku — chicken nanban (flour/egg/panko coating), mix fry, tonkatsu, shrimp fry, hamburger steak — all wheat-heavy, plus soy-sauce-based sauces and a shared fryer. No celiac accommodation indicated.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The restaurant is built around meat and fried-seafood dishes; set meals come with miso soup (typically fish dashi) and rice. No vegan items or accommodation documented.
caveats: AREA MISMATCH: the target described this as a central-Nara (近鉄奈良駅周辺) eatery, but the only グリルヨシダ matching the description (tartar chicken nanban, mix fry, tonkatsu, kissaten atmosphere) is in Gose City (御所市栄町60-23), ~30km south of central Nara near Gose Station. It does not exist near Kintetsu Nara Station. Hours conflict between sources (lunch-only on Tabelog vs. lunch+dinner on official Gose City page). Phone reported as currently not answering on Tabelog; no reservations taken.

bio.background: A neighborhood yoshoku restaurant opened in 1982 (Showa 57), housed in a homey one-story building with orange Western roof tiles, white walls, and a green sign — the kind of old-fashioned kissaten-style place where regulars linger over manga and magazines. By the mid-2010s it had been running for over three decades, described by the city as quietly carrying on with patient, handmade work ('コツコツ地道に手作りでやっています').
bio.specialty: The signature is a big chicken nanban: a generous chicken breast fried light and crisp like a cutlet, finished with a not-too-heavy sweet-vinegar glaze and a thick blanket of creamy homemade tartar sauce, served as a set with miso soup, rice and a drink. Other favorites are the mix fry set and a demi-glace tonkatsu, plus a Western-style bento of shrimp fry, pork cutlet and hamburger steak.
bio.philosophy: Steady, handcrafted cooking made to order — dishes are cooked after you order, so they take a little time, and at lunch the place fills with locals.

anecdotes.text: The chicken nanban is credited to a chef from Miyazaki Prefecture (the birthplace of chicken nanban), and despite its imposing size it uses breast meat with light breading so it eats more easily than it looks.
The city of Gose lists it as a long-running local spot famous for its homemade-tartar chicken nanban and a Western-style bento of shrimp tempura, pork cutlet and hamburger steak.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu is deep-fried breaded items (chicken nanban, mix fry, tonkatsu, shrimp fry) sharing fryer oil with breaded wheat coatings — high gluten cross-contamination risk.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yoshoku sauces and the sweet-vinegar nanban glaze are soy-sauce based, which normally contains wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen centers on meat and fried seafood; set meals include miso soup (typically fish dashi). No vegan-safe preparation documented.

cuisine: 懐石・日本料理（kaiseki）
neighborhood: 紀寺町／京終（近鉄奈良駅・ならまち南）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市紀寺町968
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.834351, 34.675434] for 奈良県奈良市紀寺町968, within the Nara box. The restaurant relocated here in 2021 from its former Sanjo-cho location; the 紀寺/京終 area is just south of Naramachi, roughly 1.5 km from Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: Around 16 seats total — counter seating plus one private room (3–6 persons). Some sources cite about 12 seats; intimate small-scale dining either way.
hours_raw: Lunch 12:00 / Dinner 17:30 (reservation-only fixed-start omakase). Tue–Sun dinner 17:30, last entry 19:00; lunch Wed–Sun 12:00 (no lunch Tue, and no lunch on the 1st of the month).
closed_days: Mondays; the last day of each month; Tuesday lunch and the 1st of each month lunch are also closed. Other irregular closures possible.
cultural_comfort_note: The chef has cooked in New York and London and the restaurant has an English website and takes international reservations, so a foreign student would be comfortable — though this is a high-end formal omakase experience (around ¥25,000+), not a casual drop-in.
caveats: High-end reservation-only kaiseki; budget roughly ¥25,000–¥35,000 per person, so well over ¥30. Located in the 紀寺/京終 area south of Naramachi (relocated here in 2021), not in the core Naramachi lanes. Hours are the fixed seating start times for the omakase course, not all-day service. No standing vegan/vegetarian/GF menu is published; the chef's shojin background means plant-based or allergy needs may be accommodated only by arrangement when booking — confirm by phone in advance.
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program. This is a traditional kaiseki kitchen where soy sauce (wheat-based), dashi, miso and seasonal courses are central, so it is not celiac-safe by default. The chef's experience with shojin and bespoke omakase means dietary requests can sometimes be honored, but a celiac traveler must arrange this explicitly at booking and cannot assume cross-contamination control.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant; the standard omakase features Yamato beef, Yamato chicken and fish/dashi, so default courses are not vegan or vegetarian. However, chef Masato Nishihara was the opening head chef at Kajitsu in New York (a shōjin/Buddhist-vegetarian restaurant that earned two Michelin stars) and weaves shōjin elements into his cooking, so a fully plant-based or vegetarian course can potentially be requested in advance. Vegan diners should arrange this by phone when reserving rather than expecting it on the day.

bio.background: Chef Masato Nishihara began cooking straight out of high school at the celebrated Kitcho Arashiyama in Kyoto, where he trained for about ten years under Teiichi Yuki, learning not only the kitchen but also flower arranging, the tea ceremony and calligraphy. He went on to cook at Toma, a soba-kaiseki house in Karuizawa, and in 2009 was recruited to open Kajitsu in New York, a shōjin (Zen Buddhist vegetarian) restaurant that earned two Michelin stars within a year. He also gained experience in London before returning to Japan and settling in Nara, where he opened Tsukumo.
bio.specialty: Seasonal kaiseki omakase built around Nara's own ingredients — Yamato vegetables (including Uda kintoki burdock), Yamato beef and Yamato jidori chicken — with shōjin sensibilities and occasional bolder accents such as truffle. The menu changes on a monthly theme and is served as a single fixed course at lunch and dinner.
bio.philosophy: The name plays on a Japanese idea: take one (一) away from one hundred (百) and the character 白 (white) remains — read 'tsukumo,' it evokes 99 and the beauty of the not-quite-complete (未完の美). Nishihara is drawn to the beauty of the incomplete and the playful sense of possibility it leaves open, an aesthetic he carries into a city whose ancient temples and treasures he treats as living heritage.

anecdotes.text: Nishihara was headhunted in 2009 to become the opening chef of Kajitsu in New York, a Buddhist-vegetarian (shōjin) restaurant that won two Michelin stars within a year of opening.
He trained for roughly a decade at Kitcho Arashiyama in Kyoto under Teiichi Yuki, studying flower arranging, tea ceremony and calligraphy alongside cooking.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional kaiseki cooking here relies on standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, miso, dashi); no wheat-free soy sauce or gluten-free protocol is documented, so wheat exposure should be assumed unless arranged.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The standard omakase includes Yamato beef, Yamato chicken and fish/dashi, so dishes are not vegan or vegetarian by default and would need to be specially prepared on request.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Reservation-only fixed omakase with a small kitchen; the chef's shōjin background suggests plant-based or dietary courses can sometimes be arranged, but only if requested in advance when booking.

positives.text: Chef trained as the opening head chef of a two-Michelin-star shōjin (vegetarian) restaurant, so the kitchen genuinely understands plant-based Japanese cooking when given notice.

cuisine: 洋食 (Japanese-style Western cuisine) — owner-run yoshoku restaurant
gf_detail: This is a Japanese-style Western (yoshoku) restaurant where the core menu is built around wheat: mixed fried platters and chicken nanban use a flour/breadcrumb coating, the stewed hamburger steak and curries use wheat-based roux, and pasta (peperoncino) is wheat. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac protocol. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on meat and fish — wagyu roast beef, chicken nanban, mixed fried platters, stewed hamburger steak, shrimp/fried-chicken curries, and a fish bouillabaisse. Dishes also use egg (soft-boiled egg garnish). No vegan options are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市毘沙門町3-1
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to lng 135.832275, lat 34.676659 (奈良県奈良市毘沙門町3番地), within the central Nara box. Naramachi district near Gangoji Temple.
neighborhood: ならまち（毘沙門町）／京終駅・近鉄奈良駅周辺
seats_text: Seat count not documented; occupies a renovated traditional townhouse and the owner describes welcoming both small and large groups.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (daily, when open); Dinner 17:30-20:00 (Mon, Thu, Fri, Sat only). Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays. Hours/days can vary — check the shop's social media calendar.
closed_days: Tuesdays and Wednesdays (dinner served only Mon, Thu, Fri, Sat; some days may have no evening service — check social media)
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run townhouse restaurant in Naramachi where ordering and signage are in Japanese; a few basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will make ordering smooth, and cash is required.
caveats: Cash only; no parking. Dinner is served only Mon/Thu/Fri/Sat and the closed days and evening service can vary, so confirm via the shop's Instagram before visiting. The restaurant opened May 1, 2026, so information is still limited.

bio.background: Opened on May 1, 2026, this Western-style restaurant is the independent successor to 'cafe 春,' which ran for about eight and a half years in Nara's historic Naramachi district near the World Heritage Gangoji Temple. The space occupies a renovated traditional wooden townhouse, with red lattice work and walls evoking the area's old quarter, antique furniture, and high ceilings.
bio.specialty: Signature dishes carry over and expand on the cafe's beloved kettle-baked curry (available with shrimp or fried chicken), alongside hearty yoshoku staples like chicken nanban, mixed fried platters, and stewed hamburger steak. A standout newer dish is a roast beef peperoncino made with pesticide-free Hiroshima lemons and rare-cut wagyu, finished with a soft-boiled egg. Evening service adds appetizers and a fish bouillabaisse.
bio.philosophy: The owner wanted guests to be able to linger over a relaxed meal with a drink, which is why dinner service and a curated sake and wine list were added — emphasizing ingredients from the owner's hometown of Hiroshima together with Nara-grown produce, so that both small and large groups can dine casually.

anecdotes.text: The drinks list pairs Hiroshima sake (the pure-rice 'Kamerei') with Nara's 'Mimuro Sugi,' reflecting the owner's wish to bring a taste of their Hiroshima roots together with local Nara flavors.
The kettle-baked curry is a holdover from the previous 'cafe 春,' carried forward into the new restaurant so longtime regulars could keep enjoying it.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built around fried, breaded, and roux-based dishes (mixed fry, chicken nanban, hamburger, curry) plus wheat pasta, so heavy shared-wheat exposure is expected. No GF protocol documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen centers on meat, fish, and egg dishes; no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

cuisine: 町中華 / 中華料理・ラーメン・餃子 (local Chinese diner — ramen, gyoza, fried rice, rice bowls)
neighborhood: 杉ヶ町・JR奈良駅 (Sugigacho, near JR Nara Station)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市杉ヶ町27-4 山本ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from address 奈良県奈良市杉ヶ町27-4; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Counter seats plus tatami/raised (小上がり) seating; exact seat count not published.
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays (月曜・火曜)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a longtime neighborhood Chinese diner where menus and service are in Japanese, so a little spoken Japanese or a translation app will make ordering much smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: This is a Chinese diner built around ramen, gyoza, and stir-fries — wheat noodles, wheat-skin dumplings, and soy/oyster sauces are everywhere, and there is no documented gluten-free handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood Chinese menu (ramen, mapo tofu with pork, tenshinhan with egg, shark fin soup, stamina ramen). No documented vegan dishes; broths and sauces rely on meat/seafood. Not a vegan destination.
caveats: Hours and closed days vary slightly across listings (one local source lists Tuesday-only closure); the authoritative listing shows Monday and Tuesday closed. Seat count is not published. No dietary (vegan/GF) accommodation is documented.

bio.background: 大宮飯店 is a family-style Chinese diner that built its reputation over more than 40 years in the Shin-Omiya district of Nara, where it was a familiar neighborhood fixture (including a long-running local delivery service). It moved to its current spot near JR Nara Station — taking over the space of the former ramen shop 麺人ばろむ庵 — opening there in October 2022. The new room is described as bright, clean, and a touch more refined than the old shop, with counter seating and a raised tatami (小上がり) area.
bio.origin: Nara (Shin-Omiya, then relocated to Sugigacho near JR Nara Station)
bio.specialty: A sprawling menu of over 30 town-Chinese (町中華) dishes — gyoza, fried rice (chahan), and ramen at one end, and heartier or premium plates like shark fin soup at the other. Often-mentioned dishes include tenshinhan, mapo tofu rice bowl, chuka-don, stamina ramen, shrimp chili, and sesame balls for dessert.

anecdotes.text: After more than four decades in Shin-Omiya, the shop relocated into the former premises of the popular ramen shop 麺人ばろむ庵 near JR Nara Station, opening in its new home in October 2022.
Weekday lunch sets are famously cheap — reported around 600-700 yen — drawing in local regulars.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by wheat-based items (ramen noodles, gyoza wrappers) and soy/oyster sauces, so cross-contamination with gluten is effectively unavoidable; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Chinese restaurant, dishes use standard soy and brown sauces that typically contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen centers on pork, egg, and seafood dishes (mapo tofu with pork, tenshinhan with egg, shark fin soup), so shared woks and broths make vegan-safe preparation unlikely; no vegan options documented.

lead: 大和茶カフェ 茶楽茶
name_ja: 大和茶カフェ 茶樂茶
cuisine: Yamato-cha (locally-grown Nara tea) specialty café serving carefully brewed tea and Japanese sweets
neighborhood: Naramachi (Kōno-dō-chō), near Kōfuku-ji
gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are published anywhere. This is a tea café whose food is sweets — the signature purple-sweet-potato Mont Blanc is built on a sponge base and chocolate crumble (wheat), so the menu is not celiac-safe. The tea itself (Yamato kabusecha) is naturally gluten-free, but no dedicated GF handling is documented. A celiac would need to confirm directly and likely stick to tea only.
vegan_detail: The core offering is brewed Yamato tea, which is plant-based, and the tea is finished with an ochitashi (the steeped tea leaves served as a small side). However, the documented sweets (Mont Blanc with whipped cream, warabimochi) contain dairy, so they are not vegan. No vegan menu or substitutions are advertised. A vegan can enjoy the tea service but should ask about any food item.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市公納堂町6
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates [135.83287, 34.677406] for 奈良県奈良市公納堂町6, within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 6 counter seats only
hours_raw: 11:00-18:00; closed Monday and Friday
closed_days: Monday and Friday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny six-seat counter café focused on a quiet, multi-infusion tea experience; some Japanese helps and the calm, intimate setting rewards visitors comfortable observing local etiquette, though staff are welcoming to curious guests.

bio.background: Opened on 2 November 2020 in a quiet corner of Naramachi by an owner named Ishikawa, an avid international traveler who wanted to bring the unhurried richness of overseas tea culture home to Japan.
bio.specialty: Carefully brewed Yamato-cha, especially kabusecha (shaded tea) sourced directly from tea farmers in Nara's Tahara district, served as three successive infusions at different temperatures (the first drawing out umami, the second balancing umami and bitterness, the third a clean finish), and finished with ochitashi, the steeped leaves served as a small side. The most beloved sweet is the purple-sweet-potato Mont Blanc, layering a purple-sweet-potato sponge with hojicha warabimochi, whipped cream, sweet-potato paste, chocolate crumble and edible flowers.
bio.philosophy: To convey, in Japan, the abundance and slowness of tea time the owner experienced abroad, engaging all five senses through aroma, the mist of the kettle, and a space that blends Japanese and Western aesthetics.

anecdotes.text: The wide counter and seating look out over a small garden, and tea is poured from a flat Tokoname-ware teapot whose micropores are said to mellow the brew and encourage the leaves to unfurl.
The owner enjoys international travel and founded the cafe to share the richness of overseas tea-drinking time with Japan.

positives.text: The house specialty is brewed Yamato tea, which is naturally plant-based and gluten-free, so a tea-only order is the safest choice for restricted diets.

caveats: No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan handling is documented on any source; dietary guidance above is inferred from the published menu (sweets contain wheat and dairy) and the nature of the tea service. Confirm directly for any strict requirement.

lead: ゐざさ茶屋（ゐざさ中谷本舗 東大寺門前夢風ひろば店）
name_ja: ゐざさ茶屋（ゐざさ中谷本舗 東大寺門前夢風ひろば店）
name_en: Izasa Chaya (Izasa Nakatani Honpo, Todaiji Monzen Yumekaze Hiroba)
cuisine: 柿の葉ずし・奈良郷土料理 (kakinoha-zushi, Nara regional cuisine; dine-in tea house)
gf_detail: The house specialty, kakinoha-zushi (vinegared rice and fish wrapped in a persimmon leaf), is naturally wheat-free, and the persimmon-leaf sushi and bamboo-leaf "Izasa-zushi" are rice-and-fish based. However, the teahouse menu also features Miwa sōmen (wheat noodles) and tempura (wheat batter), and sushi rice/fish are typically seasoned with regular wheat-containing soy sauce. There is no documented gluten-free protocol or dedicated preparation, so a celiac traveler must ask carefully and cannot assume safety.
vegan_detail: This is a fish-sushi specialist. The signature kakinoha-zushi is made with mackerel, salmon and other fish, and set meals center on tempura, sōmen and steamed sushi. There is no documented vegan menu; dashi and fish are pervasive, so a vegan would be very limited and should ask in advance.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市春日野町16 東大寺門前夢風ひろば内（2Fお食事処）
geocode_note: Geocoded from 奈良県奈良市春日野町16 via GSI address search; first feature coordinates [135.839111, 34.684177], within the central Nara box. Matches the Tōdai-ji gate / Yumekaze Hiroba location.
seats_text: Seat count not published. Located on the 2nd floor of the Yumekaze Hiroba complex as a sit-down tea house ("お食事処") with views of Tōdai-ji's Nandaimon gate and the Great Buddha Hall roof.
closed_days: No fixed weekly closing day; the shop publishes specific irregular closed dates each month (e.g., several Mondays). Confirm the day of your visit on the official site.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed tourist-area tea house at the Tōdai-ji gate where pointing at the picture menu and a friendly konnichiwa will get you a meal, though detailed allergy questions may need translation help.

bio.background: Izasa is the dine-in tea house of Nakatani Honpo, a Nara persimmon-leaf-sushi house that began in 1921 (Taishō 10) as a rice merchant, Nakatani Shōten, in the mountain village of Kamikitayama in Okuyoshino. Selling rice in a village that thrived on forestry, the family also sold the local kakinoha-zushi; about fifty years ago the previous owners devised their bamboo-leaf-wrapped 'Izasa-zushi,' and the company reinvented itself as a maker of Nara's regional 'specialty sushi,' reaching its 100th anniversary in 2021. This branch sits at the gate of Tōdai-ji inside the Yumekaze Hiroba complex: a sushi shop on the first floor and the Izasa Chaya tea house on the second, where you can eat looking out over the Nandaimon gate and the great roof of the Daibutsuden.
bio.specialty: Kakinoha-zushi (vinegared rice topped with mackerel, salmon and other fish, each piece wrapped in a fragrant persimmon leaf) and the house bamboo-leaf-wrapped Izasa-zushi. The tea house also serves set meals with Miwa sōmen, tempura and steamed sushi.
bio.philosophy: The shop's motto is to let guests savor seasonal Nara flavors in comfort ('季節折々のおいしさを、くつろぎながら'), carrying the food culture of Okuyoshino from the ancient capital of Nara to the wider world in a spirit of 'honoring the old to learn the new' (温故知新).

anecdotes.text: The trade name 'Izasa' comes from a Kamikitayama legend of Izasa-ō, a giant wild boar with bamboo grass (sasa) growing from its back; it was bestowed by Kōshō Shimizu, a former chief abbot of Tōdai-ji, with the wish that the company act 'for the region' and 'for people' (the character ゐ deriving from 為, meaning 'for the sake of').
Nakatani Honpo, maker of the 'Izasa kakinoha-zushi' often seen at Shinkansen station kiosks, was founded in 1921 as a long-established rice shop in Okuyoshino.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No gluten-free soy sauce is documented; standard sushi/fish seasoning uses wheat-based soy sauce, and the menu also includes wheat sōmen and tempura.

positives.text: The core kakinoha-zushi is naturally a rice-and-fish dish wrapped in a persimmon leaf, so it contains no wheat in the wrapping or rice itself.

caveats: Weekly closing days are irregular (specific dates posted monthly, not a fixed weekday); confirm hours for your date. The 2F tea house keeps shorter hours (11:00-17:00, LO 16:00) than the 1F shop. No published seat count and no documented allergy/gluten-free or vegan protocol — ask in person if you have strict dietary needs.
neighborhood: 東大寺門前・夢風ひろば (Tōdai-ji gate, Nara Park)

name_ja: TEGAIMON CAFE（テガイモン カフェ）
cuisine: 町家カフェ / フレッシュジュース（夏はかき氷、冬は焼き芋）
neighborhood: きたまち（東大寺 転害門前）/ 近鉄奈良駅
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市手貝町1-1
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市手貝町1番地; within Nara box.
seats_text: Counter 8 seats + table 6 seats (14 total).
hours_raw: 10:00–18:00. Closed Wednesdays, and 2nd & 3rd Thursdays.
closed_days: Wednesdays; also closed on the 2nd and 3rd Thursdays of the month.
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, owner-run juice café where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way; the menu is simple (point-and-order juices, shaved ice, sweet potato), so a foreign student can manage comfortably even with limited Japanese.
vegan_detail: The fresh cold-pressed/hand-squeezed fruit juices (e.g. orange from 3 whole oranges, kiwi) and the winter roasted sweet potato are plant-based and suitable for vegans. However, several signature items are not vegan: the fruit punch includes ice cream and the popular shaved ice includes strawberry-milk/dairy toppings, and coffee may be served with milk. No dedicated vegan menu; choose the straight juices or yaki-imo to stay plant-based. Confirm toppings when ordering.
gf_detail: This is a juice and fresh-fruit café rather than a wheat-heavy kitchen, so naturally gluten-free choices exist: the fresh fruit juices, fruit punch (fruit/soda based), shaved ice, and roasted sweet potato contain no obvious wheat. However, there is no celiac/allergen program, no documented cross-contamination controls, and no gluten-free certification. Suitable for gluten-avoiders by menu choice; not verified safe for medically strict celiacs.
caveats: Hours are irregular (closed Wed plus 2nd/3rd Thu); seat count (14) and payment methods are from listing/review data and may change. No dedicated vegan/GF or allergen protocols are documented — dietary suitability is by ingredient/menu choice only. Verify current hours via Instagram before visiting.

bio.background: TEGAIMON CAFE sits in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district, directly across from Todai-ji's Tegaimon Gate, a National Treasure that has stood since the Nara period more than 1,300 years ago. The café occupies a former merchant's storefront that is over 100 years old, renovated into a retro-modern space with high ceilings and an airy, open feel. It opened in August 2017.
bio.origin: Nara (Kitamachi, in front of Tegaimon Gate)
bio.specialty: Cold-pressed and hand-squeezed fresh fruit juices — the signature orange juice is made from three whole oranges squeezed by hand — alongside a fruit punch piled with five or more fruits, seasonal shaved ice in summer, and slow-roasted sweet potato baked for about two hours in a handmade clay pot in late autumn and winter.
bio.philosophy: The shop frames itself around 'drinkable fruit' — letting visitors recharge with the vitamins of fresh-cut fruit while taking in centuries-old architecture across the street.

anecdotes.text: The orange juice is squeezed by hand from three or more whole oranges using a hand juicer, served fresh on the spot.
In the colder months the café serves roasted sweet potatoes in two varieties (Naruto Gold and Beni Haruka), each baked slowly for roughly two hours in a handmade clay pot.
Owner Chikako Nakamori is known for a welcoming smile and fills the space with seasonal decorations and artwork.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature items mix dairy with fruit (fruit punch includes ice cream; shaved ice includes strawberry-milk toppings), so vegan-suitable juices are prepared in a space where dairy is also handled; no separation protocol is documented.

positives.text: Menu centers on fresh-cut and hand-squeezed fruit juices and roasted sweet potato, which are naturally plant-based and free of wheat.

cuisine: Macrobiotic-based old-folk-house lunch with prominent vegan/vegetarian options (bean-and-vegetable vegan hamburger, vegan dan dan noodles, raw sweets); regular lunch sets may also include fish and meat.
vegan_detail: Not a fully vegan restaurant — the macrobiotic-based seasonal lunch can include fish and meat dishes. However, the owner offers a genuine dedicated plant-based lineup: a vegan hamburger and dan dan noodles made only from beans and fresh vegetables with no meat, plus raw-food sweets. Because the kitchen tailors each meal to the guest (preferences asked at booking), vegan diners should state their needs clearly when reserving (at least 2 days ahead).
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program. The raw sweets are explicitly made without wheat flour (also no eggs, dairy, or refined white sugar, prepared below 46C), so they can be a safe sweet option. The dan dan noodles are noodle-based (likely wheat) and other dishes/seasonings are not confirmed wheat-free, so a celiac diner must discuss needs directly when reserving rather than assume safety.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市丹生町
geocode_note: GSI cho-level geocode for 奈良県奈良市丹生町 returned [lng 135.963806, lat 34.698093], outside the central-Nara box (lng 135.76-135.90); 丹生町 is rural eastern Nara City, roughly 40 minutes by car/taxi from JR or Kintetsu Nara Station. Exact banchi not published (reservation-only private home/restaurant). Coordinates set null per out-of-box rule.
neighborhood: 奈良市丹生町 (rural eastern Nara City, far from the Kintetsu/JR Nara station core)
seats_text: Renovated old folk house accommodating up to about 20 guests
hours_raw: Completely reservation-only lunch (reservations required by 2 days before visit, via phone / email / website). Specific opening clock-hours are not published on the official site; service is arranged per reservation.
closed_days: By reservation only; no fixed published business days or holidays. Reserve at least 2 days in advance.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a remote, reservation-only private home-restaurant run by one owner who plans each meal individually, so you will need basic Japanese (or a Japanese-speaking helper) to book by phone/email two days ahead and to arrange the ~40-minute drive from central Nara.
caveats: Despite a brief listing placing it "near Kintetsu Nara," 厨や is actually in rural 丹生町, about 40 minutes by car from central Nara — there is no convenient public-transport access, so it does not function as a central-Nara walk-in eatery. It is completely reservation-only (book 2+ days ahead) with no fixed posted hours, and the regular lunch is not vegan by default, so dietary requests must be stated when booking.

bio.background: After culinary school, owner-chef Yuri Nishihata trained in Kobe and travelled through Southeast Asia to study its cuisine; motherhood awakened her to the importance of vegetables. She renovated an old folk house in Niu-cho, eastern Nara City, as her home and restaurant, opening Kuriya in April 2023.
bio.specialty: Macrobiotic-based seasonal lunch sets and a la carte; signature bean-and-vegetable vegan hamburger and vegan dan dan noodles; raw-food sweets made below 46C without eggs, dairy, wheat flour or refined sugar.
bio.philosophy: Macrobiotic, locally sourced cooking gentle on body and mind; fully reservation-only and tailored to each guest, asking preferences at booking.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained in Kobe and travelled Southeast Asia before turning to macrobiotic, vegetable-forward cuisine after having a child, renovating a kominka in Niu-cho and opening in April 2023.

Diners describe a secluded hideaway in nature about 40 minutes by car from central Nara, where vegan guests are accommodated with plant-based lunches such as the bean-and-vegetable vegan hamburger and dan dan noodles.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The regular macrobiotic lunch can include fish and meat dishes alongside the plant-based options, so a shared kitchen handles animal products; vegan guests should confirm preparation when reserving.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner is fully reservation-only and designs each meal per guest, asking about preferences at booking time, which makes it practical to communicate dietary needs in advance.

positives.text: Offers a dedicated plant-based lineup: a vegan hamburger and dan dan noodles made only from beans and fresh vegetables, with no meat.
Raw-food sweets are made without eggs, dairy, wheat flour, or refined white sugar and cooked below 46C, giving wheat- and dairy-avoiding diners a safe dessert option.

cuisine: Traditional Japanese confectionery (wagashi) shop with a second-floor tea room serving sweets and kakigori
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature satsuma-yaki bun is made of a wheat-flour-and-egg dough wrapped around red bean paste, and the tea room serves wheat-based wagashi alongside mochi and warabi-mochi items.
