There is no indication of gluten-free options or celiac accommodation, and cross-contact with wheat flour is unavoidable in a wagashi kitchen.
vegan_detail: Some items appear plant-based in principle (anko/red bean paste, kuzukiri, kuzumochi, warabi-mochi, matcha kakigori), but the signature satsuma-yaki contains egg in its dough, and no allergen or vegan information is published. A strict vegan would need to ask item-by-item; nothing is certified or advertised as vegan.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市中新屋町29
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.830673, 34.677631] for 奈良市中新屋町29; within the central Nara box (Naramachi, near Gangō-ji).
neighborhood: Naramachi (中新屋町), near Gangō-ji temple and Kōshin-san; approx. 10 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: Second-floor tea room has 18 seats; first floor is the confectionery sales counter.
hours_raw: Confectionery shop (1F): 9:00–18:00. Tea room (2F): 11:00–18:00 (L.O. 17:00). Tea room closed Thursdays and the 2nd & 4th Wednesdays (closed all Wednesdays and Thursdays December–March). Shop sales: irregular holidays.
closed_days: Confectionery shop: irregular holidays. Tea room: closed Thursdays and 2nd & 4th Wednesdays (all Wed & Thu December–March).
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming neighborhood sweet shop where a friendly konnichiwa and pointing at the picture menu will carry you through; little to no English is likely, but ordering tea and a sweet is simple and low-pressure.
caveats: Hours_week reflects the first-floor shop (9:00–18:00 daily, irregular holidays). The second-floor tea room keeps shorter hours (11:00–18:00, L.O. 17:00) and is closed Thursdays plus the 2nd & 4th Wednesdays, expanding to all Wednesdays and Thursdays from December through March. Founding year is given as Meiji 30 (1897) in some sources and Meiji era generally; treat as late-19th-century establishment.

bio.background: Kasuga-an is a long-established wagashi (Japanese confectionery) house in the historic Naramachi district, tucked near Gangō-ji temple and the Kōshin-san shrine. Founded in the Meiji era (sources cite Meiji 30 / 1897), it has spent well over a century making and selling its signature manjū, with a quiet second-floor tea room added so visitors can rest and taste sweets fresh from the kitchen.
bio.specialty: The house specialty is satsuma-yaki — a sweet-potato-shaped manjū. Smooth koshi-an (strained red bean paste) made from Hokkaido azuki and finished light and not overly sweet is wrapped in a dough kneaded from wheat flour and egg, then skewered and grilled to give it its characteristic browned, sweet-potato-like skin. The tea room also serves mochi, kuzukiri, kuzumochi, warabi-mochi, and seasonal kakigori such as Yamato matcha shaved ice with white dango and red beans, or a kuromitsu-kinako version with warabi-mochi, paired with matcha, coffee, or black tea.

anecdotes.text: The second-floor tea room overlooks the UNESCO World Heritage Gangō-ji temple, letting guests eat freshly grilled satsuma-yaki and seasonal sweets with a view over the old townscape of Naramachi.
The satsuma-yaki bun is shaped like a sweet potato, its skin grilled on a skewer to mimic the look of a roasted satsuma-imo, with a light Hokkaido-azuki koshi-an inside.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen works with wheat flour for the satsuma-yaki dough and other wagashi, so cross-contact with wheat is inherent; no gluten-free handling is mentioned anywhere.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature satsuma-yaki dough contains egg, so it is not vegan; other items like kuzumochi and matcha kakigori may be plant-based but are not labeled, and no separate-utensil or vegan handling is documented.

cuisine: 自家焙煎の喫茶店／ハンバーグ・洋食ランチ・ケーキ
neighborhood: もちいどの商店街（近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約7分）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町33
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町33 returned [135.828796, 34.681133], within the Nara box.
seats_text: テーブル50席（1階・2階あり）
closed_days: 無休（年中無休）
cultural_comfort_note: A nostalgic, locals-only retro coffee shop with no English menu or English-speaking staff, so a foreign student will be most comfortable pointing at the photo menu boards or going with a Japanese-speaking friend.
vegan_detail: This is a traditional Western-style kissaten built around meat and fried foods (hamburg steak, shrimp fry, cream croquettes, pork cutlet curry). Dishes rely on demi-glace, dairy, eggs and fish-based broths, and no vegan menu is documented.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The core menu is wheat-heavy — breaded/fried shrimp and cutlets, hamburg steak with demi-glace, curry roux, cakes and toast — and there is no documented gluten-free option or allergen handling.
caveats: Hours (7:00-22:30, no regular closed day) come from a local listing and a gourmet blog; confirm before a special-occasion visit. Seat count and cash-only/no-reservation policy are from the restaurant listing. There is a noted policy restricting entry for children under 5.

bio.background: 珈琲一族 (Coffee Ichizoku) is a long-running retro kissaten tucked into the Mochiidono shopping arcade, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The storefront is covered in hand-lettered menu boards, and the interior carries an old-Showa, almost cave-like calm across its two floors. It is the kind of all-day neighborhood spot that flows from morning service through lunch to evening drinks.
bio.specialty: Generous, affordable Western-style set meals — the signature being the hamburger-and-shrimp-fry teishoku, a coarsely-ground beef hamburg steak under plenty of demi-glace alongside a crisp shrimp fry with tartar sauce, served with rice, miso soup, macaroni salad and pickles. Cream croquettes, katsu curry, and cakes like Mont Blanc round out the lineup, with most sets around or under 1,000 yen.

anecdotes.text: The shop is beloved as a nostalgic, retro coffee house where hearty homemade Western lunch sets stay remarkably cheap, mostly under 1,000 yen.
Locals describe a bright exterior with a historic, cave-like interior atmosphere, and note it offers a wide variety of substantial Western-style lunches.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen routinely fries breaded items (shrimp fry, cutlets) and serves wheat-based hamburg steak, curry roux and cakes, so heavy gluten cross-contact should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A meat- and dairy-centric Western menu with no vegan items documented; shared cooking surfaces with meat and fried foods should be assumed.

lead: 焼き芋専門店 維新蔵 ならまち店
name_ja: 焼き芋専門店 維新蔵 ならまち店
cuisine: 焼き芋専門のカフェ／スイーツ・甘味
gf_detail: The signature item is a slow-aged, twice-roasted honey sweet potato (蜜芋焼き芋) — plain roasted sweet potato is naturally gluten-free, making it a safe, simple option for those avoiding gluten. However, this is a cafe with a broader sweets menu (e.g. candied sweet potato / 大学芋, cakes) where glazes, batters, or sauces may contain wheat, and there is no documented dedicated gluten-free protocol or allergen handling. Confirm individual items at the counter before ordering.
vegan_detail: The core product — a roasted honey sweet potato aged 100 days and described as additive-free with sugars developed naturally by the potato itself through a twice-roasting method — is plant-based with no dairy, egg, or animal ingredients, making it genuinely suitable for vegans. Other cafe sweets and drinks may contain dairy or egg (e.g. cheesecake, baked confections), so vegans should stick to the plain roasted sweet potato or confirm each item. No formal vegan labeling.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市下御門町4 eリュエル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 奈良県奈良市下御門町4-1; coordinates fall within the central Nara box. Located in the Shimomikado-cho area of Naramachi, within walking distance of Kintetsu Nara Station.
neighborhood: ならまち（下御門町／近鉄奈良駅 徒歩圏）
seats_text: 16 seats; small eat-in cafe space with terrace seating that welcomes dogs.
closed_days: Tuesdays (open on public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, walk-in sweet-potato cafe in historic Naramachi where you can simply point to the roasted sweet potato and pay by card or QR, so even with no Japanese it is an easy and welcoming stop for a foreign student.

bio.background: Ishingura is a roasted sweet potato (yakiimo) specialist whose Naramachi shop sits in the historic townhouse district near Gangoji Temple, in the old 'outer capital' area of the former Heijo-kyo. The Naramachi location was renovated and reopened on August 10, 2023, adding eat-in seating and seasonal menu items so visitors can enjoy the sweet potatoes on the spot in the atmospheric old-town setting.
bio.specialty: Their signature is the 濃蜜焼き芋『維新蔵の蜜芋』, a rich honey-like roasted sweet potato. The shop ages the sweet potatoes for 100 days and uses a proprietary twice-roasting method so the potato itself develops a variety of natural sugars, producing a creamy texture and a deep, honey-like sweetness rather than plain sweetness, with no additives. They also serve candied sweet potato (大学芋) and seasonal sweet-potato sweets.
bio.philosophy: The focus is on letting the sweet potato create its own natural, additive-free sweetness through long aging and careful roasting, drawing out a honey-like depth of flavor.

anecdotes.text: The Naramachi shop was renovated and reopened on August 10, 2023, introducing eat-in dining and seasonal offerings.
The sweet potatoes are aged for 100 days and twice-roasted so the potato develops its own natural sugars, giving a honey-like, additive-free sweetness.
The terrace seating is dog-friendly.

positives.text: The signature roasted honey sweet potato is described as additive-free, with its sweetness developed naturally by the potato through 100-day aging and a twice-roasting method — a simple, single-ingredient item that is plant-based and naturally gluten-free.

caveats: Plain roasted sweet potato is the safest item for both GF and vegan diners and is genuinely suitable, but this is a sweets cafe with other menu items (candied potato, cakes) that may contain wheat, dairy, or egg, and there is no documented allergen-handling or cross-contamination protocol — confirm specific items at the counter. Phone, hours, seat count, and payment options confirmed across the official site and listing pages.

cuisine: レトロ喫茶店／オムライス・洋食（家族・店主経営）
neighborhood: ならまち・下御門商店街（近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約10分）
gf_detail: A retro Western-style kissaten built around omurice (egg-wrapped ketchup rice), pork cutlet, and curry — all wheat- and flour-heavy dishes. Ketchup sauces and breaded cutlets, plus the typical kissaten kitchen, mean there is no gluten-free menu and no celiac-safe preparation. Not suitable for those who must strictly avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on egg (omurice), pork cutlet, and chicken/meat-based Western dishes; coffee and dairy-based drinks and coffee jelly round it out. No vegan or vegetarian items are documented, and the signature dishes rely on egg, butter, and meat. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市下御門町38
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the building address 奈良県奈良市下御門町38; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 34席（カウンター席およびペット可のテラス席あり）
closed_days: 火曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, longtime local kissaten where the menu and atmosphere are entirely Japanese — knowing how to order an omurice or cutlet set in basic Japanese will make the visit smooth, and cash is essential.
caveats: Cash only — no cards, e-money, or QR payment. Evening hours run late (to 23:00), and reviewers note the vintage interior can carry a slightly musty, old-bookshop scent. Closed Tuesdays.

bio.background: Takarajima is a longtime retro kissaten tucked along the Shimogomon shopping street in Naramachi, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It has been quietly serving the neighborhood for over twenty years, run by an older proprietor who keeps oldies and jazz playing through a calm, time-stopped interior decorated in classic Showa-era café style.
bio.specialty: Old-fashioned omurice — buttery ketchup chicken rice wrapped in a soft omelet — is the signature, most often ordered as a set with a small pork cutlet or curry. Lunch sets come with salad and a post-meal coffee or tea, and the house coffee and coffee jelly are local favorites.
bio.philosophy: An unhurried, nostalgic refuge: the owner curates the music and the slow pace as much as the food, the kind of neighborhood spot regulars return to for comfort rather than novelty.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers describe a nostalgic jazz-café ambiance with oldies flowing through a 'fallen-out-of-time' room, and praise the butter-scented, ketchup-laced old-style omurice as the dish to order.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes (omurice, breaded pork cutlet, curry) are flour- and wheat-based and prepared in a shared kissaten kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature items rely on egg, butter, and meat, with no vegan options noted; vegan diners cannot be reliably accommodated.

cuisine: Japanese / udon (和食・うどん)
neighborhood: Naramachi (Minami-ichi-cho)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南市町27
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市南市町27 returned [135.829971, 34.680401], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 30 seats across a two-story wooden townhouse; first floor has sunken kotatsu/hearth-style seating, second floor has tatami rooms (private banquet seating up to about 18).
hours_raw: Mon-Sat & holidays: 11:30-14:00, 17:00-22:00; Sunday closed.
closed_days: Sundays (open on holidays unless they form a consecutive holiday period)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, homey local spot where you remove your shoes for tatami/hearth seating; staff are warm but communication is mostly in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help.
vegan_detail: This is a fish-and-meat-forward Japanese eatery: udon broth, sashimi, tempura and chicken dishes all rely on fish dashi or animal ingredients. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented, and even the udon sets are not plant-based.
gf_detail: Udon is the house specialty and is made from wheat, and tempura batter and soy-based seasonings add further gluten. There is no gluten-free menu or documented accommodation, so this is not suitable for celiac diners.
caveats: No official website found; details drawn from Tabelog and local Nara food guides. No documented allergy or dietary accommodation information.

bio.background: Kyoya began as an udon specialist on Nishi-Kitsutsuji before relocating into a traditional wooden townhouse in Nara's atmospheric Naramachi quarter, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station near Sarusawa Pond. After the move it broadened from a pure udon shop into a full Japanese kitchen while keeping the smooth, springy noodles that built its name. By day it is a calm, casual lunch spot; in the evening it shifts into an izakaya mood.
bio.specialty: Hand-served udon (bukkake and kama-age styles) with smooth, throat-pleasing noodles, plus the changing Kyoya Gozen lunch set that pairs udon with seasonal fish tempura, small side dishes, rice and pickles. Evening favorites include fresh sashimi platters, grilled chicken, and eggplant dengaku.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe the converted townhouse as a hidden-gem retreat, with shoes-off sunken-hearth seating downstairs and quiet tatami rooms upstairs giving an old-fashioned, peaceful feel.
The kitchen carried over the silky, pleasing udon texture from its original udon-shop days even after expanding the menu in Naramachi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat udon is the central dish and tempura is fried on site, so wheat is pervasive in the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes such as bukkake udon, eggplant dengaku and grilled chicken use Japanese soy-based seasonings, which typically contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Udon broth and most sets rely on fish dashi, and sashimi and chicken are prepared in the same kitchen.

cuisine: Japanese home-style set meals (teishoku) centered on dashi-maki tamago (dashi-rolled omelet)
neighborhood: Naramachi (Higashishiodo-cho), near Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東城戸町7
geocode_note: Geocoded from GSI address search on 奈良県奈良市東城戸町7; first feature coordinates within Nara box.
seats_text: 12 seats total: 6 counter seats and 6 table seats (one 4-person table, one 2-person table)
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:15), open daily including holidays; closes when sold out. Hours have varied across sources (one local report lists 10:30-18:00, closed Wednesdays). Closures are irregular — check before visiting.
closed_days: Irregular closures (some reports note Wednesday). Check ahead before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny family-run spot in an old house with a one-order-per-person and no-reservations policy; a little Japanese and patience help, as this is a traditional local eatery rather than a tourist-oriented restaurant.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The signature dashi-maki tamago and set meals rely on Japanese dashi and seasonings that typically include wheat-based soy sauce, and rice is served alongside. No gluten-free protocols are documented, so a celiac diner would need to ask directly and exercise caution.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-suitable. The core offering is egg-based (dashi-maki tamago, egg sandwiches, omurice) and the dashi is made from bonito (katsuo) and kombu, so it contains fish. No vegan options are documented.

bio.background: Someya Shoten began about 70 years ago as a specialist workshop making thick-rolled egg for the sushi trade. Founder and master craftsman Masahiro Someya, now in his 80s, supplied gyoku (sushi-topping egg) and sweet datemaki egg rolls wholesale to fish markets and sushi restaurants. As the family workshop's members aged past 70 and questions of succession and physical strain set in, the family decided to preserve the craft in a new form, converting the ground floor of their three-story former workshop-residence into a small cafe in the summer of the previous year.
bio.specialty: Dashi-maki tamago (dashi-rolled omelet) teishoku set meals; also a thick egg sandwich and classic omurice. The signature omelet is built around four to five eggs and a generous amount of dashi for a soft, fluffy texture, cooked fresh to order.
bio.philosophy: The founder's eldest daughter Kiyoko, who now runs the cafe, describes the aim that customers remember their dashi-maki tamago so warmly they would want to taste it one more time. Ingredients are sourced locally: eggs, Koshihikari rice, Yamato (Nara) vegetables, and domestic bonito and kombu for the broth.

anecdotes.text: The founder said he wanted to make the decision to wind down the original factory while he was still physically able to handle disposing of the large machinery.
The cafe occupies the converted first floor of the family's former three-story workshop-residence in Naramachi, and the dashi-maki tamago is prepared fresh for each order.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Set meals and the dashi-based omelet use traditional Japanese seasonings; soy sauce in Japan is typically wheat-containing, and no gluten-free soy sauce is documented here.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers entirely on egg dishes and uses fish (bonito) and kombu dashi, so animal products are pervasive throughout the menu.

positives.text: Ingredients are simple and locally sourced: local eggs, Koshihikari rice, Nara vegetables, and domestic bonito and kombu broth.

caveats: Reported opening hours vary by source (Tabelog: 11:00-15:00 daily; a local news article: 10:30-18:00 closed Wednesdays; some blogs: 8:00-15:00). Closures are irregular and the shop closes when sold out. The surname 染谷 is romanized here as "Someya" (the common reading and the basis for the shop name); one source transliterated it "Somatani." No official website found.

cuisine: 甘味・茶屋（わらび餅・奈良茶飯）
neighborhood: ならまち（猿沢池畔・興福寺そば、近鉄奈良駅）
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The signature warabi mochi is made from bracken starch (warabiko), which is wheat-free at its base, but the shop is a traditional sweets house with no published allergen handling and no dedicated preparation for celiacs. Matcha and Japanese sweets are generally low-wheat, but the savory side (奈良茶飯 / nara chameshi sets, often accompanied by pickles and side dishes) and any soy-sauce-based seasonings are not verified gluten-free. A celiac diner must ask directly; cross-contact cannot be assumed safe.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Warabi mochi (bracken starch, sugar, kinako soy flour) can be plant-based, but the kinako/anko and matcha sweets may involve dairy in some variations (e.g., the milk warabi mochi version), and savory dishes such as 奈良茶飯 are not confirmed dashi-free. Vegan-leaning diners should ask which sweets are free of dairy; this is not a dedicated vegetarian/vegan destination.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市登大路町49
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市登大路町49 returned [135.830948, 34.681396], within the Nara box; matches the Sarusawa Pond / Kofukuji location described on the official site.
seats_text: 53 seats total across the 1st floor and basement; tatami rooms, table seating, and private rooms available.
hours_raw: 11:30-17:00 (L.O. 16:30); some listings note Wed/Thu opening at 11:00. Closed Mondays.
closed_days: Mondays (定休日 月曜)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, tourist-familiar traditional tea house by Sarusawa Pond where a friendly greeting and pointing at the warabi mochi or matcha set will get you served comfortably even with limited Japanese.

bio.background: Yanagi Chaya was founded in 1902 (Meiji 35) beside Sarusawa Pond, in the shadow of Kofukuji's five-story pagoda in central Nara. It was opened specifically to keep alive Nara's old tea-rice tradition: 'nara chameshi' (奈良茶飯), rice cooked with tea and roasted, crushed soybeans, a dish eaten in Nara since ancient times that had been fading from memory. The shop occupies a wooden building that has stood since the Meiji era, and visitors are seated in tatami rooms, at tables, or in private rooms looking out toward the pond.
bio.specialty: Warabi mochi (bracken-starch jelly served with matcha) is the flagship, available year-round; there is also a milk warabi mochi variation with red bean and chestnut, and seasonal kakigori (shaved ice with strawberry or Uji kinako). The founding signature is the nara chameshi set.
bio.philosophy: Preserving Nara's tea-rice culture so that it would not be forgotten, served in a historic wooden tea house overlooking Sarusawa Pond.

anecdotes.text: The founder opened the tea house in 1902 to ensure Nara's tea rice, made from tea and roasted crushed soybeans, would not disappear from the city's table.
The setting beside Sarusawa Pond is considered scenic enough to be worth the visit on its own, with views toward Kofukuji's pagoda.

positives.text: The signature warabi mochi is made from bracken starch (warabiko), a naturally wheat-free base, served with matcha.

caveats: Hours have a minor discrepancy: most sources list 11:30-17:00 (L.O. 16:30) daily except Monday, while the Tabelog listing notes Wed/Thu opening at 11:00 — reflected in hours_week. Closed Mondays. Payment is cash plus QR (PayPay); no credit cards or e-money. Address normalized to 奈良県奈良市登大路町49 (official site shows 登大路町; one translation rendered it as 'Todai-ji Town' which is incorrect). No GF or vegan program is documented; allergen details must be confirmed directly with the shop.

cuisine: 自家焙煎スペシャルティコーヒー・喫茶（自家製食パントースト）
neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市中新屋町18
geocode_note: GSI address search returned a single exact match for 奈良県奈良市中新屋町18 at [135.8302, 34.677711], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Small counter-and-table café; roughly 10 seats per the opening interview.
hours_raw: Official site: 7:00–17:00 (Naramachi main shop). Opening interview / local guides: 7:00–15:00 with irregular closures. Tabelog lists 12:00–17:00 closed Friday. Hours appear to vary, so confirm before visiting.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures); sources disagree (Tabelog notes Friday closed) — confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: The owner is a licensed English-speaking guide who has lived and taught abroad and has run weekend English-conversation café sessions, so a foreign student should feel very comfortable here.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The signature food is in-house-baked 食パン (wheat shokupan) toast, and there are no documented gluten-free items or celiac accommodations. Plain black coffee is naturally gluten-free, but the kitchen is bread-focused, so this is not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: No documented vegan menu. Black filter coffee and the water-diluted BLACK espresso are incidentally plant-based, but the WHITE drink uses steamed milk and the signature toast uses wheat bread with butter/honey toppings. A vegan can drink black coffee but has no documented vegan food option; confirm with staff.

bio.background: 天迦久珈琲 opened on March 1, 2021 in Nara's historic Naramachi district. The founder, Yui Kitaguchi, was 27 at opening and is a licensed English-speaking tour guide who had lived and taught English in English-speaking countries before returning to open the café.
bio.specialty: Self-roasted specialty coffee served three ways: WHITE (espresso with steamed milk), BLACK (espresso let down with natural spring water), and FILTER (hand drip), each around 550 yen; plus thick toast made from house-baked shokupan, topped with Nara-prefecture honey or peanut butter. Beans are roasted on a 1996 Old Probat machine.
bio.philosophy: The name 天迦久 (Amenokaku) comes from a deity in the Kojiki who overcomes hardship; the owner chose it hoping to surpass whatever difficulties lie ahead. She washes green beans before roasting for cleanliness and uses spring water (Gororo Water from Tenkawa village) for brewing, prizing the iron-cast Old Probat roaster for the deep sweetness and flavor it brings out.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a licensed English-interpreter guide who planned weekend English-conversation café sessions to help the local community welcome international visitors.
The shop logo was designed by Ouka, a calligraphy artist known for yokai-themed work and an official Nara tourism ambassador.
Beans are roasted on a 1996 Old Probat machine, prized because the iron casting brings out stronger sweetness and flavor than modern equipment.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The café bakes its own wheat shokupan in-house and serves it as toast, so flour and gluten are present on-site; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Milk is used for the WHITE espresso drink and butter/honey for the toast; no documented separation for vegan orders.

positives.text: The owner is a licensed English-speaking guide, so dietary questions can be asked directly in English.

caveats: Hours vary by source (official site 7:00–17:00; opening interview ~7:00–15:00; Tabelog 12:00–17:00 closed Friday) and closures are irregular, so confirm before visiting. A second branch on Sanjo-dori (上三条町) and a separate roastery also exist; coordinates given are for the Naramachi main shop.

lead: 奈良 オモテナシ食堂
name_ja: 奈良 オモテナシ食堂
cuisine: 創作和食・健康ごはん（手作りの体にやさしい定食、肉不使用の月替りベジプレート、奈良県産ヒノキ内装の個人店）
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約5分
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is advertised. This is a Japanese teishoku-style kitchen; menus include sixteen-grain rice, soup and various vegetable preparations that in Japan typically rely on soy sauce and dashi (both common gluten sources via wheat-based soy sauce and fish-stock seasonings). There is no documented dedicated gluten-free menu, no stated cross-contamination controls, and a shared home-style kitchen, so a celiac traveler should not assume safety and would need to ask directly about every dish, including the soy sauce and stock used.
vegan_detail: The signature からだ想いベジプレート (body-kind vegetable plate, ~1,650 yen) is a meat-free, monthly-changing plate of about eight vegetable dishes with roughly 350g of vegetables (e.g. lentil salad, samosa-style potato gyoza, simmered kombu, daikon-and-hijiki, marinades, a detox soup, sixteen-grain rice). It is explicitly meat-free, but there is no published confirmation that it is fully vegan: Japanese vegetable simmered dishes and soups very commonly use fish dashi (bonito/katsuo) and the gyoza/田楽 components may contain non-vegan seasonings. The rest of the menu features meat- and fish-based plates. A vegan diner should confirm dashi, fish products and egg/dairy in each component before ordering.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市角振新屋町6-3
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市角振新屋町6-3; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 12 seats: 5 counter, 7 at tables; terrace seating also noted.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays); Monday and Tuesday are lunch-only (no dinner service)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, calm individually-run teishoku spot where a little spoken Japanese will help you order and ask about ingredients, though pointing at the vegetable plate works in a pinch.
caveats: Meat-free vegetable plate is the draw, but vegan and gluten-free status is unconfirmed (likely fish dashi/wheat soy sauce in some dishes) — ask before ordering. Monday and Tuesday are lunch only and the regular closing day is irregular, so check before visiting.

bio.background: A small individually-run cafe-restaurant a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, reached by heading down the Konishi-dori arcade and crossing Sanjo-dori. It built its identity around home-style cooking you can eat with confidence every day.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is the からだ想いベジプレート (body-kind vegetable plate), a monthly-rotating, meat-free plate of about eight vegetable dishes with roughly 350g of vegetables served with sixteen-grain rice. The kitchen also serves meat- and fish-centered plates, healthy curries, and takeout bento.
bio.philosophy: Built on the idea of 'homemade, body-kind food you can eat with peace of mind every day,' emphasizing generous vegetables and nutritional balance.

anecdotes.text: The interior is finished with a generous amount of hinoki cypress grown in Nara Prefecture, giving the counter, table and terrace seating a bright, warm feel.

The vegetable plate changes monthly and has featured items such as lentil salad, samosa-style potato gyoza, simmered kombu, shredded daikon with hijiki, eggplant-and-tomato marinade, and a spring-cabbage and yuzu-pepper detox soup.

positives.text: Offers a clearly meat-free vegetable plate with around 350g of vegetables across roughly eight dishes, making it an easy choice for diners avoiding meat.

cuisine: French-style baked sweets shop
neighborhood: Naramachi (Mochiidono-cho, back alley off Mochiidono shopping street); approx. 5-8 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町27-1
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to chō-level point for 餅飯殿町27 within Nara box (lat 34.681343, lng 135.829208).
seats_text: About 5 counter seats; small eat-in space, primarily takeout.
hours_raw: 11:00-18:00, 不定休 (irregular closures)
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); check Instagram before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, casual counter sweets shop where you can simply point at the baked goods you want, making it very easy for a foreign student even with no Japanese.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The entire lineup is wheat-flour baked goods — canelés, financiers, madeleines, Breton galettes, scones, and butter sandwich cookies — so it is unsuitable for celiac or gluten-avoiding diners. No gluten-free items are documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The baked goods are built around cultured butter, eggs, and dairy (financiers with browned butter, butter-rich Breton galettes, butter sandwich cookies); no vegan items are documented.
caveats: Hours are 11:00-18:00 daily but closures are irregular (不定休); confirm via Instagram before a special trip. Eat-in space is just a few seats — mostly a takeout shop. Canelés are best eaten the day of purchase.

bio.background: OYATSUYA 323 (おやつや みつみ) opened on November 1, 2022, in a quiet back alley off the Mochiidono shopping street in Nara's old-town Naramachi district. It is a tiny one-shop sweets atelier run by a pastry chef who previously trained and worked at prestigious hotels, including the Imperial Hotel Osaka and a JW Marriott property, before setting out to open a small neighborhood bakery of their own.
bio.specialty: French-style baked confections: rum-and-vanilla canelés with a crisp shell and chewy center, almond browned-butter financiers made with cultured butter, thick rum-scented Breton galettes, bite-size 'scrap' scones (plain, chocolate, Earl Grey), and playful sea-bream-shaped butter sandwich cookies filled with red bean and butter.
bio.philosophy: A small, approachable neighborhood okashi shop that pairs serious hotel-trained pastry technique with a sense of humor, seen in pun-named items like the madeleine.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a pastry chef with a hotel background (Imperial Hotel Osaka, JW Marriott) who chose a narrow Naramachi side street to open their own small okashi shop.
Signature items include a large rum-and-vanilla canelé with a crisp exterior and chewy interior, and a sea-bream-shaped butter sandwich cookie filled with red bean and butter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Everything sold is wheat-flour baked goods made in the same small kitchen, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable throughout the shop.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Items are made with butter, eggs and dairy; no separation from animal products is documented.

name_ja: アフタヌーンティー ロシェ
cuisine: 築100年超の町家を改装したアフタヌーンティー専門店。パティシエが手がける奈良県産食材のセイボリー・スコーン・プティフールと、奈良の和紅茶やほうじ茶を楽しむ隠れ家カフェ
gf_detail: This is an afternoon tea specialist built around wheat-based pastries, scones, quiche/pies, cakes and petit fours, so it is not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten-free diets. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. A gluten-free traveler would have essentially nothing safe to eat here.
vegan_detail: The set menu centers on dairy, eggs and cured meat: butter-based scones and pastries, Mont Blanc, meringue, nougat glacé, prosciutto cake salé and anchovy/fish pie, plus quiche. Nothing in the documented menu is vegan and there is no stated vegan option, though some plant-forward items (carrot salad, Nara-vegetable elements) exist. A vegan should ask in advance; expect very limited or no suitable options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南市町14-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Nara box for 奈良市南市町14-1; located in the Naramachi district near Kintetsu Nara Station.
neighborhood: ならまち（南市町）／近鉄奈良駅
seats_text: 12 Western-style table seats plus 6 tatami seats (approx. 18 total); a second-floor gallery space ("雨月") hosts rotating exhibitions.
hours_raw: 月・火・水・金・土・日 12:00-17:00（料理L.O. 15:00）／木曜定休。平日12:00-15:30、土日祝は16:00頃までとする情報もあり。90分制。
closed_days: Thursdays, plus irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A foreign student would find a warm welcome to solo diners but should expect a primarily Japanese-language setting and reservations made by phone or Instagram DM, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) helps.
caveats: Two reputable Japanese articles spell the owner's name 岩崎能久 but romanize it differently (Iwasaki Takahisa vs. Iwasaki Yoshihisa); the reading is uncertain. Hours differ slightly between sources (one lists weekdays to 15:30, weekends to 16:00; Tabelog lists 12:00-17:00 with food L.O. 15:00); hours_week uses the Tabelog times. Seating (12+6) is reported by articles, not Tabelog. Minimum age is middle-school and up. There is also a lighter à la carte option (¥1,100-2,200) alongside the ¥3,400-4,500 afternoon tea sets.

bio.background: Owner-pastry chef Iwasaki trained at well-regarded patisseries in Tokyo, Fukuoka, Paris and Normandy before building a string of beloved Nara dessert spots, including the patisserie Bon Chic, Patisserie Karaku, the Taiwanese sweets shop Sarusawa Touka, and Cheesecake Rocks. He is also a certified tea advisor and personally curates the tea list.
bio.specialty: Three-tier afternoon tea built on Nara-prefecture ingredients: savories like prosciutto cake salé, anchovy fish pie and Nara-vegetable quiche; scones made with local hōjicha and Yamato tea with chocolate chips and local honey; and petit fours such as pumpkin-caramel tart, Mont Blanc, strawberry-rose-chocolate tart, champagne biscuit and matcha meringue.
bio.philosophy: A fusion of Japanese and Western aesthetics inside a century-old machiya, offering an accessible luxury: a full, professionally made afternoon tea from around ¥3,400, in a setting that warmly welcomes solo guests rather than requiring groups.

anecdotes.text: The cafe occupies a machiya over 100 years old in the Naramachi district; the building previously housed a cafe called 'Naramachi-mura,' and Iwasaki took it over through a connection with the former owner, preserving original wooden beams and pillars while adding William Morris-style wallpaper, European antiques and a preserved tatami tea room. The name 'Rocher' is French for 'rock,' a nod to his surname.

The tea program features Nara producers and a touch of history: award-winning 'Tsuki no Kaori' black tea from Tea Farm Inokura, premium Yamato hōjicha from Kenichi Shizen Noen, and a Naramachi masala chai blended with a long-established Kikuoka kanpo (herbal) apothecary, alongside German Ronnefeldt teas.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu is built on wheat (scones, pastries, cakes, quiche, pies, biscuits), so cross-contamination is inherent and the venue is not suitable for celiacs. No gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are prepared with shared dairy, eggs and meat (prosciutto, anchovy); no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

positives.text: Menu items and ingredients (Nara-vegetable quiche, carrot salad, prosciutto cake salé, anchovy fish pie, local-tea scones, named petit fours) are described in detail by published articles, helping diners identify what they are eating, though no formal allergen program is documented.

cuisine: 昭和レトロな個人経営の純喫茶（自家焙煎ブレンドコーヒー・モーニング）
neighborhood: きたまち（近鉄奈良駅北側・花芝商店街）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市花芝町6-2 プラザ花芝 101
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市花芝町6-2 returned [135.828766, 34.686535], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Counter seating with about 8 counter seats plus one table for 4 (roughly 12 seats); a small hideaway-style room.
hours_raw: 8:00-18:00 (morning service until 11:00); closed Mondays
closed_days: Mondays (occasional irregular afternoon openings)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny family-run retro coffee house where ordering in basic Japanese is easiest; staff are warm but English is not assured, so a few phrases or pointing at the menu will help.
gf_detail: This is a traditional kissaten focused on coffee; food is limited to wheat-based items like toast, egg sandwiches, and Taiwanese castella cake. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of celiac-safe handling, so it is not suitable for strict gluten-free needs.
vegan_detail: The signature foods (egg sandwich, toast with boiled egg, honey/brown-sugar castella) all contain egg and/or dairy, and morning sets pair drinks with egg dishes. No documented vegan options; black coffee is the only plant-based item, so it is not a vegan destination.
caveats: Coordinates geocoded to the chō (Hanashiba-cho 6) rather than the exact building. Seat count is from a descriptive listing (about 8 counter + 1 four-top), not an official figure. Owner names (Higo Tatsuya, Higo Yoko) are from a single Nara food-feature article.

bio.origin: Originally a coffee house called 珈琲館 煦露粉 (Kurokofu), founded by the first-generation master, that moved to the area near Kintetsu Saidaiji and the Nara Family complex in 1973.
bio.background: The shop earned a reputation for self-roasting its own coffee back in an era when that was rare. When the old building aged, the family relocated in December 2017 to the Hanashiba shopping street on the north (kitamachi) side of Kintetsu Nara Station and reopened as cafe CROCO. The second-generation master Higo Tatsuya, who started as floor staff in the 1970s, runs operations, while his daughter Yoko took over management and handles cooking and new menu development.
bio.specialty: The house 'Croco Blend' is built from four bean varieties roasted dark to balance deep bitterness with a clean acidity, hand-dripped to order one cup at a time. Food highlights include a fluffy egg sandwich with thin cucumber and light mayo (about 450 yen) and Yoko's own Taiwanese castella made with brown sugar and honey (about 300 yen). A morning service runs until 11:00 with toast and a boiled egg served alongside drinks.
bio.philosophy: The family's guiding idea is that the place and the interior may change, but the taste of the coffee and the warm atmosphere stay the same; Yoko keeps the previous shop's flavor while trying new menu ideas.

anecdotes.text: Since the 2017 move the beans have been roasted by TABI Coffee Roaster, a roastery opened by a former employee of the shop, keeping the coffee in the family of people who learned the craft there.

A giant commemorative cup holding the equivalent of about 30 regular servings, marked with the CROCO name, inspired a playful 9,650-yen coffee item nodding to the old large-bowl tea ceremony; only one group ever ordered it and most of the cups were given to regular customers when the previous shop closed.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu centers on wheat items (toast, egg sandwiches, castella cake) in a small kitchen, so cross-contact with wheat is effectively unavoidable; no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg and dairy are central to the food offerings (egg sandwich, boiled egg morning set, honey castella), with no separate vegan preparation noted.

cuisine: 自然菜食ヴィーガン・スローフード（無農薬玄米・自家農園の季節野菜）
vegan_detail: Fully plant-based (完全菜食). No meat, fish, eggs, or dairy in any dish, including desserts and dressings; fish dashi is not used. Vegetables are pesticide-free and largely grown at the kitchen's own Ikoma/Takayama farm (ナイヤビンギファーム). Signature plant-based dishes include soy-meat karaage seasoned with Hozanji miso, soymilk gratin, and a mochi-based "cheese" fondue. Lunch is a set of around ten seasonal small dishes over three-part-milled brown rice (生駒産3分づき玄米).
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program documented. The kitchen is plant-based but not gluten-free: signature items use wheat (soy-meat karaage is flour-dredged, soymilk gratin, house-baked breads and biscotti), and soy products/soy sauce are central, so wheat exposure and cross-contact are likely. The brown-rice base is naturally GF, but a celiac would need to confirm specifics in advance; reservation-only lunch may make accommodation possible if requested when booking. No public information confirms celiac-safe handling.
address_ja: 奈良県生駒市門前町12-9
geocode_note: GSI geocode returned [lng 135.690155, lat 34.686069]. Latitude is within range but longitude (135.690) is far below the Nara box lower bound (135.76), so coordinates set to null. This venue is in Ikoma City on the western edge of Nara Prefecture (Hozanji temple approach, reached by the Ikoma Cable Car), not central Nara.
neighborhood: 生駒・宝山寺参道（生駒市門前町）
seats_text: Listed at 6 reservable seats. The converted lodging has multiple private rooms (about seven small private rooms plus a medium and a large hall) with floor seating; dining is private-room based.
hours_raw: Lunch 12:00-13:30 (entry; LO ~15:30), Café 14:30-16:00 (LO 17:00); some sources also list dinner 18:00-19:30 by reservation. Hours and closures are irregular/seasonal.
closed_days: Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday (sources vary; closures can be irregular/seasonal)
cultural_comfort_note: A calm, reservation-only spot in a converted old temple lodging where staff and menus are mostly in Japanese, so a foreign student should book at least a day ahead (by phone or Instagram) and be ready to communicate in basic Japanese.
caveats: Located in Ikoma City (生駒市門前町), on the Hozanji temple approach reached via the Ikoma Cable Car — this is the western edge of Nara Prefecture, not central Nara, so it falls outside the central-Nara geocode box. Fully vegan but not gluten-free. Reservation-required, cash-only, private-room dining with irregular seasonal hours.

bio.background: Nyahbinghi occupies a former temple lodging (宿坊/旅館) on the stone-paved approach to Hozanji temple in the Ikoma hills, a few minutes' walk from Hozanji Station on the historic Ikoma Cable Car. The old building has been kept largely intact, its depth and tatami rooms preserved, giving it an unusual atmosphere that blends traditional Japanese architecture with a relaxed, faintly other-Asian feel. Diners sit on the floor in private rooms looking out over the Nara Basin, which turns into a sweep of city lights at night.
bio.philosophy: A natural-vegetarian, slow-food kitchen built on pesticide-free, self-grown produce. The cooking is entirely plant-based, using no meat, fish, eggs or dairy, with a focus on seasonal Ikoma vegetables and three-part-milled local brown rice; courses are paced slowly so guests can relax and refresh.
bio.specialty: A reservation-only lunch set of roughly ten seasonal small dishes; signature items include soy-meat karaage seasoned with Hozanji miso, a soymilk-based creamy gratin, a mochi-based plant 'cheese' fondue with vegetables and bread, and house-baked sweets.

anecdotes.text: The vegetables come from the kitchen's own pesticide-free farm in the Takayama/Ikoma area (referred to as the Nyahbinghi farm), part of why nearly everything on the plate is local and self-grown.
Guests describe the soy-meat karaage as convincing enough to pass for real chicken, served with the kitchen's own Hozanji-miso seasoning.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Not a gluten-free kitchen: fried soy-meat karaage is dredged in flour, and the menu includes soymilk gratin, house-baked breads and biscotti, so wheat is present and cross-contact is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cooking is soy- and miso-forward (soy meat, soymilk, Hozanji miso), and standard Japanese soy sauce/miso seasoning typically contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The entire kitchen is fully plant-based with no animal products used anywhere, so animal cross-contact risk is minimal.

positives.text: Everything served is 100% vegan, including desserts and dressings, with no meat, fish, eggs, dairy, or fish dashi.
Lunch is reservation-only and made to order from seasonal produce, so guests can raise dietary needs when booking.

cuisine: 手打ち十割そば（国産そば粉・名水使用、奈良の地酒、そばがき）
neighborhood: ならまち（猿沢池・近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約8分）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東寺林町20
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市東寺林町20 returned [135.830978, 34.679344], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 22 seats (table and tatami seating in a townhouse-style interior); some listings note 18.
hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Sun & holidays 11:00-14:30; closed Tuesdays. (An older local-guide listing also showed a 17:00-20:00 dinner service with Tuesday and Friday evening closed, but current Tabelog/Gnavi listings show lunch only.)
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed neighborhood soba shop where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you by, though staff likely speak limited English.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The dipping sauce (tsuyu) is made with soy sauce, which contains wheat, and even the 100% buckwheat (jūwari) soba is prepared in a kitchen handling wheat-based two-eight (nihachi) soba and tempura, so cross-contamination is highly likely. No celiac accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Soba broth is based on fish dashi (bonito/kombu), and most dishes involve fish, egg (e.g. soft-boiled egg soba), or tempura. No documented vegan options.
caveats: Hours vary between sources: current Tabelog and Gnavi listings show lunch service only (11:00-14:30, closed Tuesdays), while an older local guide listed an evening service. Confirm dinner availability before visiting in the evening. Seat count is listed as 22 on Tabelog and 18 elsewhere.

bio.background: A small, intimate hand-made soba shop tucked into the historic Naramachi district, directly in front of the Naramachi Center and a short stroll from Sarusawa Pond. It blends a traditional townhouse setting of table and tatami seating with a quietly modern touch, with jazz playing softly in the dining room.
bio.specialty: Hand-cut soba made from 100% domestic buckwheat flour and local spring water. The house standard is a refined nihachi (two-eight) soba described as pale, thin, and crisply refreshing, served with a Kanto-style broth; a pure 100% buckwheat (jūwari) version is available for a small supplement. Signature plates include soba topped with soft-boiled egg and tempura bits, grated-yam tororo soba, and soba set meals, and diners are invited to taste the noodles with a pinch of salt.
bio.philosophy: An emphasis on craftsmanship and quality ingredients, pairing carefully made soba with sake and shochu for an unhurried soba-shop drink before the meal.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits right in front of the Naramachi Center and plays jazz in the background, mixing table and tatami seating for a calm, refined atmosphere.
Noodles are made from 100% domestic buckwheat flour and natural spring water, with a pure 100% buckwheat option offered alongside the standard two-eight blend.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen prepares both 100% buckwheat soba and wheat-containing two-eight (nihachi) soba plus tempura, so even the jūwari soba is highly likely to share equipment and water with wheat products.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with a Kanto-style broth/dipping sauce based on soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is fish-dashi based and the menu includes egg and tempura dishes; no vegan-dedicated preparation is documented.

positives.text: Diners may eat the soba with a pinch of salt instead of sauce, which avoids the wheat-containing broth (though the noodles themselves are not certified gluten-free).

cuisine: オーガニック・ヴィーガン／フードセラピーカフェ（自然食、玄米菜食）
neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅・東向商店街エリア）
gf_detail: The kitchen states it uses no wheat in its dishes and describes itself as "添加物・グルテン不使用" (additive- and gluten-free). The staple is organic sprouted brown rice, which can also be eaten as house-made gluten-free brown rice noodles (玄米麺), served cold in spring/summer. This is a genuinely wheat-avoiding, grain-conscious kitchen, so it is strong for gluten-free diners. However, it is not explicitly described as a certified celiac-safe facility and no dedicated-equipment / cross-contamination protocol is documented, so a strict celiac should still confirm by phone (Japanese) before visiting.
vegan_detail: The cafe's menus are made without fish, meat, dairy, or wheat, with carefully selected grains and vegetables and minimal additives/pesticides — explicitly described as suitable for people practicing veganism. The signature tofu hamburger plate (豆腐ハンバーグプレート) comes with pre-meal shiso tea, the tofu patty with carrot puree, small side dishes, salad, sprouted brown rice, and miso soup, all plant-based. This is a genuinely vegan kitchen, not an omnivore place with a few options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町5-1 井上ビル2F-A・B
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI on chō-banchi level (東向南町5-1); result 34.682663N, 135.828918E falls within the central Nara box and matches the Higashimuki shopping arcade location ~2-3 min from Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: Seat count not published; a small 2nd-floor cafe in the Inoue Building with table seating and no private rooms.
hours_raw: 食べログ表記: 09:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00)。公式サイト受付時間: 10:00-18:00。営業時間は時期・予約状況により変動。
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A calm, owner-run natural-food cafe where a friendly greeting and pointing at the popular tofu hamburger plate will get you far, though menu explanations are mostly in Japanese so a translation app helps.
caveats: Posted hours differ between sources (Tabelog lists 09:00-21:30 with evening by reservation; the official site lists reception 10:00-18:00); confirm same-day, as the cafe also runs a wellness/salon floor (aroma, herbs, footbath, yoga) and may keep variable hours. Cash only.

bio.background: KURURU is a natural-food cafe tucked on the second floor of the Inoue Building along the Higashimuki shopping arcade near Kintetsu Nara Station, open since the mid-2000s. The owner developed the menu through independent research into therapeutic, plant-based eating, and the cafe bills itself as a food-therapy specialty house — the idea of 'eating fasting' (食べるファスティング), where simple, clean dishes nourish and gently cleanse at the same time.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is the tofu hamburger plate (豆腐ハンバーグプレート): a tofu patty with carrot puree served with pre-meal shiso tea, three small side dishes, salad, sprouted brown rice, and miso soup. The kitchen also makes its own gluten-free organic brown-rice noodles (玄米麺), served cold in the warmer months, plus handmade sweets like banana cake.
bio.philosophy: Cooking is kept simple to draw out the natural umami of the ingredients, prioritizing Nara-grown produce and carefully chosen seasonings while avoiding fish, meat, dairy, wheat, and unnecessary additives or pesticides. The owner frames mindful, lighter eating as a small contribution toward the SDGs.

anecdotes.text: The cafe shares its space with a wellness salon floor offering aromatherapy, herbs, footbaths, and yoga, reflecting its whole-body 'total therapy' approach to food and health.
Its house-made gluten-free brown-rice noodles are popular enough to be sold for delivery beyond the cafe itself.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner has independently researched and developed vegan, vegetarian, and allergen-conscious dishes, indicating awareness of dietary needs (no documented formal protocol).

positives.text: All menus are prepared without fish, meat, dairy, or wheat, using carefully selected grains and vegetables with minimal additives and pesticides.
The kitchen describes its food as additive- and gluten-free (添加物・グルテン不使用), built around organic sprouted brown rice and house-made brown-rice noodles.
The signature tofu hamburger plate is explicitly noted as suitable for people practicing veganism.

lead: 江戸川 ならまち店
name_ja: 江戸川 ならまち店
cuisine: Unagi (Kanto/Tokyo-style grilled eel), Japanese washoku set meals
vegan_detail: This is a specialist eel restaurant. The core of the menu is unagi (eel) served as unaju/unadon and hitsumabushi, all built on a fish-based kabayaki tare and rice. Set meals and side dishes rely on fish dashi and animal proteins. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian menu, and nothing here is suitable for a strict plant-based diet.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature kabayaki tare (sauce) used on every eel dish is a soy-sauce/mirin-based glaze, and Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat; rice bowls are served with this sauce. Side dishes and set components also use soy-based seasonings and dashi. No gluten-free menu, labeling, or dedicated preparation is documented, so this is not safe for celiacs.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市下御門町43
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.829269, 34.679035] for 奈良県奈良市下御門町43, within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: Naramachi (Shimomikado-cho), within walking distance of Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: 84 seats, including tatami and sunken-kotatsu (horigotatsu) seating across two floors, plus a converted warehouse annex (蔵乃間) for private use.
hours_raw: Mon-Fri 11:00-15:00, 17:00-21:00; Sat-Sun 11:00-21:00. Closed the 3rd Thursday of each month.
closed_days: Third Thursday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: A polished but welcoming eel restaurant where a foreign student can order comfortably by pointing at picture menus and saying a few words of Japanese; English support is not guaranteed, so a translation app helps.
caveats: Operated by the Kintetsu Restaurant International group (a multi-branch chain), not a family-run independent. Eel and soy-based sauce mean it is unsuitable for celiac, vegan, or vegetarian diners. Card and electronic payments are accepted.

bio.background: The restaurant occupies a roughly 150-year-old former merchant house in the heart of Naramachi. Built in the early Meiji era and later home to the Miyajima family, it operated for generations as a kimono and textile shop—its latticed itoya-goshi facade still signals that fiber-trade past. After the shop closed in the 1980s, the building was carefully converted into a dining house, keeping original details such as a wooden box-staircase (hakodansu) that is still in use, along with sunken-hearth seating and restored garden views.
bio.specialty: Tokyo (Kanto)-style eel: the fish is split along the back, steamed, then grilled so it turns soft, fluffy and lighter in fat. Signature dishes include unaju/unadon over rice with a long-aged kabayaki sauce, and hitsumabushi served so you can enjoy it three ways. Local Yamato ingredients also appear in set meals.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen follows the old eel-cook's creed, 'three years to skewer, eight years to fillet, a lifetime to grill,' and continually replenishes a founding tare said to carry decades of accumulated flavor.

anecdotes.text: The converted warehouse next door, called Kura-no-ma, looks out over a restored garden and is used as a quiet space for private gatherings.
The Edo-yaki method—back-splitting, an initial grill, steaming to render out fat, then a final grill—is what gives the eel its tender, fluffy texture.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The eel is finished with a soy-sauce/mirin-based kabayaki tare brushed onto every dish; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the glaze is a gluten source.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on eel and fish-based sauce and dashi throughout; no plant-based preparation is documented.

lead: ならまちとうふあん こんどう
name_ja: 奈良町豆腐庵 こんどう
cuisine: 豆腐料理（町家でいただく国産大豆100％の豆腐づくし）
neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅エリア）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西新屋町44
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.829605, 34.676605] for 奈良県奈良市西新屋町44; within Nara box.
seats_text: テーブル12席、個室座敷2卓8席（計20席）
closed_days: 火曜（一部案内では月・火）
cultural_comfort_note: A serene traditional townhouse where the staff likely speak limited English, so bring a translation app or a Japanese-speaking friend and consider booking ahead, especially for the weekend dinner course.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Tofu cuisine relies heavily on soy sauce and dipping sauces that almost always contain wheat, and dishes like agedashi tofu and hirousu may involve flour or shared frying oil. Nothing on the available pages confirms gluten-free handling, so a celiac diner would need to ask in detail and should not assume safety.
vegan_detail: Plant-forward but not certified vegan. The menu centers on tofu, yuba, oboro tofu, okara and soy milk from 100% domestic soybeans, which sounds ideal for vegans, but yudofu and other dishes are commonly served with bonito/fish dashi and soy-sauce-based dipping sauces. The published sources do not state that dashi is plant-based, so a strict vegan must confirm the broth and sauces before ordering.

bio.background: 奈良町豆腐庵こんどうは、天理市に本店を構える「近藤豆腐店」が営む豆腐料理の店。自家製造した豆腐の本来のおいしさを味わってもらうために、ならまちの一角にこの食事処を開いた。建物は築180年を超える町家で、登録有形文化財に指定されており、江戸期からのならまちの風情をそのまま残している。
bio.origin: 奈良県（ならまち／本店は天理市）
bio.specialty: 国産大豆100％の豆腐づくし。看板の『奈良町ランチ』は湯どうふ、おぼろ豆腐、生揚げ、おから、ひろうすなど豆腐料理が並び、食前にクリーミーな豆乳が供される。夜は予約制の『奈良町御膳』も。揚げだし豆腐や生湯葉の刺身なども人気。
bio.philosophy: 厳選した国産大豆と、ミネラル豊富な伊豆大島の『海精にがり』を用い、大豆本来の味を最大限に引き出すことを大切にしている。
roles: 豆腐製造・豆腐料理店

anecdotes.text: 築180年を超える登録有形文化財の町家は、河瀬直美監督の映画『沙羅双樹』（2003年）のロケ地として使われた。
豆腐は天理市の本店で自家製造され、ミネラル豊富な伊豆大島の『海精にがり』で仕上げられている。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tofu cuisine here is served with soy-sauce-based dipping sauces and seasonings, which in Japan typically contain wheat; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned on the available pages.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes such as yudofu are commonly served with fish (bonito) dashi and the sources do not confirm a plant-based broth, so the meal may not be vegan despite being tofu-centered.

positives.text: All tofu is made in-house from 100% carefully selected domestic soybeans and Izu Oshima nigari, so the core ingredient is whole-food and minimally processed.

caveats: Hours conflict slightly between sources: Tabelog lists closed Tuesdays with weekday lunch-only (Mon, Wed-Fri) and Sat/Sun lunch+dinner; a tourism summary lists Mon & Tue closed. hours_week follows Tabelog. Confirm current days/dinner availability before visiting. Official site (kondou-touhu.co.jp) returned HTTP 403 and could not be fetched.

lead: KURURU（ナチュラルライフ クルル）
name_ja: ナチュラルライフ クルル（KURURU）
cuisine: オーガニック・ヴィーガン（食事療法ベースの定食・カレー・玄米麺）
gf_detail: The kitchen avoids wheat across its menu and the rice is sourced from contract farms; its signature original "brown rice noodles" (玄米麺) are made gluten-free, served cold in spring and summer. Set meals are built on organic brown rice rather than wheat. Because miso soup, seasonings, and sauces typically involve fermented soy products, anyone with celiac disease should confirm that the specific seasonings (e.g. soy sauce, miso) used in their dishes are wheat-free before ordering, but the overall menu is intentionally wheat-avoiding and a strong choice for gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: Entirely plant-based. The kitchen excludes fish, meat, dairy, eggs and wheat from all dishes. The rotating menu offers three to four set meals built around organic brown rice — noodles, curry, and a tofu patty among them — each served with sides such as salad and miso soup. No fish dashi is used, making the set meals genuinely vegan rather than vegetarian.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町5-1 井上ビル2F-A・B
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the chō block 奈良市東向南町5-1; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: 東向南町・近鉄奈良駅すぐ（東向商店街脇）
seats_text: A small space on the 2nd floor of the Inoue Building; exact seat count not published. Intimate, café-sized seating shared with a wellness salon area.
hours_raw: Tabelog lists 09:00–21:30 (LO 21:00) with a note that evening hours vary and reservations are recommended; the official site lists café hours 10:00–18:00. As of 2025 the kitchen operates largely by reservation/appointment.
closed_days: 不定休（irregular closures）
cultural_comfort_note: The owner is warm and happy to explain every ingredient, but conversation and the booking-based service are mostly in Japanese, so a foreign student should arrive with a few key phrases or a translation app and ideally reserve ahead.
caveats: Hours and closures are irregular and the evening operates by reservation, so call ahead. Sources differ on operating hours (10:00–18:00 on the official site vs. a wider range elsewhere). Cash only. Portions are modest and prices run higher than typical Nara cafés (set meals roughly ¥1,980 and up). It shares its floor with a wellness salon offering aromatherapy, herbs, foot baths and yoga, so it is as much a healing space as a dining room.

bio.background: KURURU is a healing-focused organic café tucked on the second floor of the Inoue Building, just a few minutes on foot from Kintetsu Nara Station beside the Higashimuki shopping arcade. It bills itself as a food-therapy specialty kitchen, and the dining room sits alongside a small wellness salon offering aromatherapy, herbs, foot baths and yoga — the idea being to heal the body from the inside and out.
bio.origin: Central Nara, near the Higashimuki arcade and Kintetsu Nara Station.
bio.specialty: Rotating set meals built on organic brown rice from contract farms — including curry, a tofu patty, and the house original gluten-free brown rice noodles (genmai noodles), each plate rounded out with salad and miso soup. The brown rice noodles are served chilled in the warmer months.
bio.philosophy: Eating as therapy: the kitchen excludes fish, meat, dairy, eggs and wheat, choosing grains and vegetables that minimize additives and pesticides so that meals support circulation, gentle detox and overall well-being. The founder frames good food as energy that connects body, spirit and the local community.

anecdotes.text: Diners describe the owner walking them through every ingredient on the plate, naming each component so that every bite feels intentional.
The founder speaks of local resources and regional food creating a circulation of energy, tying the café's plant-based cooking to community and sustainability.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Set meals come with miso soup and soy-based seasonings; the page does not confirm whether the soy sauce/miso used is wheat-free, so celiac diners should verify the specific seasonings.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner reportedly names every ingredient in each dish for guests, indicating a high level of ingredient transparency.

positives.text: All menu items are vegan and the kitchen avoids fish, meat, dairy, eggs and wheat entirely, with grains and vegetables chosen to minimize additives and pesticides.
Offers an original gluten-free brown rice noodle made in-house, a rare genuinely wheat-free noodle option.

cuisine: Kominka (renovated townhouse) cafe serving Western-style comfort food; signature baked curry (yaki curry), pasta, and dessert plates
neighborhood: Naramachi (奈良町), Nara City — within walking distance of Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市毘沙門町3-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良市毘沙門町3 returned [135.832275, 34.676659], inside the Nara box; matches the Naramachi location reported by local sources.
seats_text: Around 20 seats at individual tables (no private rooms); intimate townhouse setting.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:30 (L.O.); some sources list 11:00-15:00 or until ingredients sell out
closed_days: Wednesdays (and Tuesdays per some sources / irregular Tuesday closures); may close early when ingredients sell out
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed local townhouse cafe where a foreign student can comfortably point at the signature baked curry on a picture menu, though staff communication is mainly in Japanese.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The core menu (baked curry with curry roux, pasta, gratin/doria, cream croquettes, breaded cutlets, dessert plates) is wheat-heavy, and no gluten-free accommodation is documented. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Signature dishes center on shrimp, beef/pork hamburger, roast beef, chicken nanban, and cheese; the tomato cream pasta sauce reportedly uses sakura-shrimp paste, bonito, and kelp. No vegan options are documented.
caveats: Tabelog currently shows this listing as 掲載保留 (listing on hold), so confirm the shop is open before visiting; however, multiple local food guides (most recent ~2023) describe it as operating with consistent hours. Hours and closed days vary slightly between sources (Wed closed is consistent; Tue closure reported as irregular by some). Phone 0742-23-1055.

bio.background: Cafe Haru is the third venture of the well-loved Western-style restaurant Yoshoku Haru, set inside a renovated traditional Naramachi townhouse with red lattice work and vintage furnishings that echo the area's old pleasure-district character. It opened in October 2017, a short two-to-three-minute walk from its perpetually busy original location, offering a calmer place to sit during a stroll through the historic district.
bio.specialty: Baked curry (yaki curry) — especially the shrimp baked curry, served sizzling in a hot iron pot with crispy rice, beef curry, melted mozzarella, a soft egg, and grilled prawns; also tomato cream pasta with tiger prawns, stewed Yamato-beef-and-pork hamburger, chicken nanban, and dessert plates.
bio.philosophy: Bringing a hometown specialty to Nara: the owner, who is from Kitakyushu where baked curry is a local signature, recreates that dish in a comfortable townhouse setting with generous, good-value portions.

anecdotes.text: The owner hails from Kitakyushu, where baked curry is one of the region's signature dishes, and brought that specialty to his Naramachi cafe.
Positioned as a less-crowded alternative just a couple of minutes from the always-busy original Yoshoku Haru, while sharing the same culinary identity.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The tomato cream pasta sauce is reported to combine sakura-shrimp paste, bonito, and kelp, and signature dishes feature shrimp, beef/pork, and cheese, so animal products are central to the kitchen.

lead: カフェ クロコ（cafe CROCO）
cuisine: レトロ純喫茶（喫茶店）— 自家ブレンド珈琲、玉子サンド
neighborhood: きたまち・花芝商店街（近鉄奈良駅北側）
gf_detail: A traditional Japanese kissaten with no gluten-free offerings. Signature items are toast, egg sandwiches, and Taiwanese castella — all wheat-based. There is no indication of gluten-free options or allergen accommodation, and the kitchen is not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: A coffee-focused kissaten. Black coffee, the house blend, and homemade ginger ale are plant-based, but the food menu centers on egg sandwiches, toast with boiled egg, and castella (egg/dairy). No documented vegan dishes; a vegan visitor could drink coffee but has essentially no food options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市花芝町6-2 プラザ花芝 101
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 奈良県奈良市花芝町6 — within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 8 counter seats plus one table for four.
hours_raw: 8:00-18:00（モーニングは11:00まで）、月曜定休
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A small, quiet local coffee shop with mostly Japanese regulars and little English, so basic Japanese or a translation app helps, but a solo student will find the counter seats welcoming.
caveats: Closed Mondays. A tiny 8-seat counter shop, so seating is limited at busy times. Morning service runs until 11:00.

bio.background: Cafe CROCO traces back to a coffee shop founded in the 1960s, which relocated in 1973 to the Nishi-Oji area near the Nara Family commercial complex and built a devoted following over the following decades. When the original building aged, the cafe moved again in 2017 to the quieter Hanashiba shopping street just north of Kintetsu Nara Station, chosen for its calm surroundings and easy station access so longtime regulars could keep coming.
bio.specialty: The deep-roasted house blend, built from four kinds of darkly roasted beans for a deep bitterness with a clean acidity, ground and brewed one cup at a time to order. Popular plates include the egg sandwich and the owner's original Taiwanese castella.
bio.philosophy: Consistency above all — 'always the same taste' — which is what keeps regulars saying it has to be here and nowhere else, while the shop still quietly adds new menu items.

anecdotes.text: The second-generation master, Higo Tatsuya, has worked the floor since the 1970s; his daughter Higo Yoko took over ownership during the 2017 move and now runs the shop alongside him.
A legendary special once existed: a giant cup holding the equivalent of about 30 ordinary servings, ordered just once by a group of businessmen to commemorate their visit.
Stepping through the classic door, you are met with old melodies on a retro radio, stained glass and an unhurried, nostalgic atmosphere built around the counter.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A traditional coffee shop whose menu is built on toast, egg sandwiches and castella; bread and wheat are handled throughout, so cross-contamination with gluten should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg and dairy are central to the food menu (egg sandwiches, boiled egg, castella), so shared surfaces and utensils make vegan cross-contact likely.

cuisine: Nara chagayu (tea rice porridge) with an all-you-can-eat buffet of 20+ Yamato-vegetable pickles, rice, miso soup and monaka
gf_detail: The core meal is rice-based: freshly cooked chagayu (rice simmered in tea), white rice, and a buffet of 20+ pickled Yamato vegetables, which is naturally a more rice-forward, low-wheat spread than most Japanese restaurants. However there is no gluten-free program, no labeling, and no dedicated handling. Many Japanese pickles are seasoned with soy sauce (which usually contains wheat), and the miso soup most likely uses standard miso/dashi. A celiac visitor cannot assume any specific dish is safe and would need to ask, in Japanese, which pickles avoid soy sauce/wheat. Treat as not verified celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: The format is vegetable-forward: rice, chagayu and 20+ pickled seasonal Yamato vegetables, so a largely plant-based plate is achievable. But the restaurant makes no vegan claim. Some pickles may be cured with bonito (katsuo) or other fish-derived seasonings, and the miso soup most likely uses fish dashi, so it is not a verified vegan venue. A vegan can probably build a satisfying plate from the pickle and rice buffet but should ask which pickles are free of fish/bonito and skip the miso soup.
address_ja: 〒630-8213 奈良県奈良市登大路町59
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市登大路町59 returned [135.836563, 34.684834], inside the Nara box; matches the Nara Park / Nara National Museum area described on the official access page.
neighborhood: Near Nara Park, by the Nara National Museum / Himuro Shrine on Noborioji-dori, about a 10-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station toward Todai-ji
seats_text: About 70 seats (roughly 50 table seats plus around 20 garden terrace seats)
hours_raw: 11:00～16:00（定休日 不定休）— Tabelog lists kitchen/serving to 15:30
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); no fixed weekly closed day published
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed buffet-style spot where you can serve yourself rice, chagayu and pickles, so a foreign student gets by fine with a friendly konnichiwa and gestures, though staff communication is mostly in Japanese and it is cash only.

bio.background: A small, independent cafe-restaurant set right by Nara Park, between the Nara National Museum and Himuro Shrine on Noborioji-dori, a roughly 10-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station toward Todai-ji. It celebrates one of Nara's oldest everyday foods, chagayu (rice slowly simmered in tea), pairing it with the region's deep pickling tradition.
bio.specialty: An all-you-can-eat spread built around freshly cooked chagayu and Nara-grown rice, served with a buffet of more than 20 carefully chosen pickles made from seasonal Yamato (Nara) vegetables, plus miso soup, roasted tea and house-made monaka, all refillable.
bio.philosophy: The place leans into Nara's identity as a city of pickles and tea porridge, letting guests graze slowly through many small vegetable pickles rather than ordering a single fixed plate.

anecdotes.text: The dining room is a bright, open space hung with photographs of Nara's temples, shrines and Buddha statues, and there is a garden terrace where you can eat in the green shade of trees said to be several hundred years old; it is also pet-friendly.

Local Nara guide Narakko describes it as a spot to enjoy Nara's local chagayu with a pickle buffet of 20-plus seasonal prefectural-vegetable pickles, freshly cooked chagayu and local rice, and refillable house-made sweets.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The meal centers on many small vegetable pickles; Japanese pickles are commonly seasoned with soy sauce, which usually contains wheat, and the menu provides no wheat/gluten labeling.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Some Nara-style pickles can be cured with bonito or other fish-derived seasonings, and the accompanying miso soup most likely uses fish dashi, so a strict vegan should confirm which pickles are fish-free and skip the soup.

positives.text: The format is buffet-style with rice, tea porridge and 20-plus vegetable pickles you serve yourself, so it is easy to build a vegetable-and-rice plate and to see what you are taking.

caveats: Cash only and no reservations for individuals (private bookings of 20-50 people are possible). Closures are irregular (不定休), so confirm the day before visiting. No vegan or gluten-free program is advertised; dietary suitability depends on which pickles avoid soy sauce/wheat and fish-based seasonings, so ask on arrival.

lead: 奈良町 あしびの郷
name_ja: 奈良町 あしびの郷
cuisine: Japanese set-meal restaurant and café run by Nara-pickle maker Ashibiya Honpo; pickle-focused teishoku, hand-pulled Yoshino-kuzu udon, and Yamato-tea sweets
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature udon is made with premium wheat flour, the set meals include grilled fish marinated in miso, and dishes rely on fish dashi and Nara-pickle seasonings that typically include wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented; not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Largely not vegan. Set meals center on Nara/Yamato pickled vegetables but are served with grilled fish and miso soup, and broths use fish dashi. While the pickle assortment itself is plant-based, the meals as composed contain fish and dashi; there is no documented vegan set, so a vegan would need to ask and likely receive only the vegetable/pickle portions.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市脇戸町29
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates [135.828552, 34.678051] for 奈良県奈良市脇戸町29; within the Nara box.
neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅）
seats_text: 30 seats
hours_raw: Lunch/café reception 11:30-16:30 (lunch service typically ends around 15:00 or when sold out); dining venue open to 22:00-23:00, reservations required after 17:00. 不定休 (irregular closures).
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional Nara pickle house with a calm machiya-style dining room; menus and service are mostly in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help a foreign student order comfortably.
caveats: Daytime lunch sets sell out; evening kaiseki dining requires reservations after 17:00. Hours per weekday are estimated from the daytime café/reception window 11:30-16:30 since the venue does not publish fixed per-day hours and closes irregularly.

bio.background: Ashibi-no-Sato is the dining and retail home of Ashibiya Honpo, a Nara-pickle (narazuke) maker whose family roots in the craft reach back to the Edo period. The business resumed and was formally re-established after the Second World War, became a company in 1970, and in March 1996 opened this venue on the site of its old number-one pickling storehouse in the heart of Naramachi, the historic merchant district that survived wartime bombing. A small chapel was later added in 2012 for concerts, parties, and weddings.
bio.philosophy: The place describes itself as 'a space of constant incompletion where one can feel green, light, water, and wind,' set within the preserved townscape of old Nara. Its name comes from the ashibi (Japanese andromeda) flower native to the region.
bio.specialty: The signature おつけもの御膳 (pickle set) showcases the house narazuke and Yamato pickled vegetables alongside Nara-grown rice, miso soup, and miso-marinated grilled fish; another house specialty is hand-pulled udon made with premium flour and rare Yoshino kuzu, plus Yamato-tea sweets. The shop is also known for unusual pickles found nowhere else, including a summer-only fresh-pickled gourd and a patented odorless garlic narazuke called 'Ninniku Shichisen.'

anecdotes.text: The dining house stands on the former site of Ashibiya Honpo's number-one pickling storehouse, repurposed in 1996 into a machiya-style space for meals, retail, and events.
Among its exclusive products is a patented garlic narazuke, 'Ninniku Shichisen,' said to retain garlic's nutrients while being completely free of the usual pungent smell.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen serves wheat-flour udon and miso-marinated fish in the same set meals, so cross-contact with wheat is likely; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Set meals rely on miso and pickle seasonings; narazuke and Japanese miso/soy seasonings typically contain wheat. No wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pickle and vegetable dishes are plated alongside grilled fish and fish-dashi soups in the standard sets, so vegan diners cannot assume separation.
