positives.text: The menu is built around pickled Yamato vegetables and Nara-grown rice, so vegetable-forward, lighter options are available even if not certified vegan or gluten-free.

lead: 蕎麦切り ももよ月
cuisine: Hand-made soba (buckwheat noodles); stone-ground and hand-ground 十割 (100% buckwheat) and 二八 (80% buckwheat) soba
neighborhood: Central Nara, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, on a quiet side street west of the Higashimuki shopping arcade
gf_detail: Not gluten-free and not safe for celiacs. While 十割 (100% buckwheat) soba contains no wheat in the noodle itself, the 二八 noodles are 20% wheat, and the dipping/pouring tsuyu and seasonal bukkake sauces use Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat). There is no dedicated gluten-free preparation, no allergen protocol documented, and heavy shared-kitchen wheat use. A celiac traveler should not rely on this restaurant.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Soba is served with tsuyu broth that is fish-dashi based (Japanese soba tsuyu is made from katsuobushi/bonito and kombu), and menu items include duck broth soba and scallop/seafood seasonal toppings. No vegan-specific or plant-based options are documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市中筋町38
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.827148, 34.685162] for 奈良県奈良市中筋町38; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 14 seats: one large wooden counter/table seating about 10, plus two tables for two.
hours_raw: Mon/Thu/Fri 11:30-14:30; Sat/Sun 11:30-14:30 and 17:30-19:30; evening service only on weekends and holidays. Closed Tuesday and Wednesday.
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, traditional husband-and-wife soba counter with no reservations and cash only — a little Japanese and patience for the queue and limited daily noodles will make the visit much smoother for a foreign student.
caveats: Cash only; reservations not accepted (queue on arrival). The hand-ground 100% buckwheat soba is limited to about 10 servings per day and sells out. Evening service runs only on weekends and holidays; closed Tuesday and Wednesday.

bio.background: A small soba specialist run as a husband-and-wife operation in central Nara, just a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station on a quiet side street near Nara Park. The kitchen is visible through large glass sliding doors, and the owner is known for chatting warmly with regulars. The proprietor drew culinary inspiration from the celebrated Osaka soba house Sobakiri Bongu in the Taisho district.
bio.specialty: Stone-ground, hand-made soba in three styles: 二八 (80% buckwheat), 十割 (100% buckwheat), and 手挽き hand-ground 100% buckwheat limited to roughly ten servings a day. Buckwheat is sourced seasonally from regions such as Shimane (Matsue), Okayama, and Gunma. A standout is the seasonal chilled bukkake soba, refreshed every two months from May through October with toppings like scallop and okra or roasted donko mushroom; the menu also includes duck-broth soba and a lunch-only soba-gaki zenzai sweet.
bio.philosophy: The chef encourages diners to first taste the soba with salt alone, so the character of the buckwheat grain comes through before the tsuyu is added. Serving vessels are carefully chosen, including Bizen ceramics and other artisan pieces.

anecdotes.text: The hand-ground 100% buckwheat soba has a deliberately rustic, uneven texture — a random granular feel and bumpy surface — that shows the noodles were ground and cut by hand.
Set on a narrow one-way lane near Nara Park, the area is one where you might cross paths with the park's free-roaming deer, regarded as messengers of the gods.
The seasonal chilled bukkake soba concept has been a fixture since 2015, with the topping rotating roughly every two months through the warmer half of the year.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-containing 二八 (80% buckwheat / 20% wheat) noodles are made and boiled in the same kitchen as the 十割 noodles, so cross-contact with wheat is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with tsuyu and seasonal bukkake sauces that use Japanese soy sauce, which normally contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Tsuyu broth is fish-dashi based and the menu includes duck broth and seafood toppings, so even the noodles are typically served with animal-derived broth.

positives.text: Pure 十割 (100% buckwheat) and hand-ground noodles are available, so the noodle itself can be wheat-free even though the accompanying broth is not.

cuisine: 老舗大衆食堂・定食（牛すい・鶏すいなど名物）
neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅周辺）
gf_detail: A traditional Japanese diner (taishu shokudo) whose menu is built around wheat- and dashi-heavy dishes: udon, sara-udon, katsudon, oyakodon, and tonkatsu. Soups and broths use fish dashi and Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat), and fried items share fryers. There is no gluten-free menu, no dedicated fryer, and no documented allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: An omnivore comfort-food diner centered on meat, egg, and pork dishes (gyusui/torisui meat-and-broth, katsudon, oyakodon, niku-udon). Broths rely on fish/meat dashi. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes, and no information that broths can be made without animal products. Not a vegan destination.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市鶴福院町32
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.832169, 34.679268] for 奈良県奈良市鶴福院町32番地, within the Nara box.
seats_text: 座席数の記載なし（小規模な大衆食堂）
hours_raw: 火・水・木・土・日：11:00-15:00（食材が無くなり次第終了）／月・金：定休日
closed_days: 月曜日・金曜日（祝日は変更する場合あり）
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and the casual diner sees many foreign visitors, so a foreign student can order comfortably without much Japanese.
caveats: Hours can end early once ingredients run out, and closed days (Mon/Fri) may shift around holidays — go for an early lunch to be safe. Sources show some inconsistency on hours; the most recent listings reflect lunch-only service. No allergen accommodation, so unsuitable for celiac or vegan diners.

bio.background: Hisagoya Shokudo is a long-running neighborhood diner founded in 1950 (Showa 25), tucked into Nara's historic Naramachi district on the slope of the old approach toward Gangoji and Kofukuji temples. Once tied to a soy-sauce shop, it has carried on for generations as a classic taishu shokudo — a humble, everyone-welcome eatery — and was refreshed with a clean, tidy interior in recent years while keeping its old-town warmth.
bio.specialty: The house is known for its regional comfort dishes: gyusui and torisui (savory beef and chicken broth bowls), niku-udon, katsudon, oyakodon, sara-udon, and Kansai specialties like kitsune-don and konoha-don. Bowls come hearty and homey, with the cutlet and egg dishes praised by visitors.
bio.philosophy: From its earliest days the kitchen has prized good water, drawing on Nara's tasty local groundwater for both cooking and drinking — a quiet point of pride for the family table it sets.

anecdotes.text: A diner who visited called it the very essence of a Japanese food hall, noting Kansai dishes like kitsune-don and konoha-don that surprise visitors from Tokyo, set in a building with a genuine old-city feel near Kofukuji.
Local listings highlight charming touches of an old-school diner, including the menu breadth and even Oronamin C among the drinks, alongside an English menu that draws foreign guests.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by wheat noodles (udon, sara-udon) and breaded/fried tonkatsu and katsudon, so shared cooking surfaces and fryers make cross-contact effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broth and donburi dishes are seasoned with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat, and rely on fish dashi.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built around beef, chicken, pork, and egg with fish/meat broths; no plant-only options are documented.

lead: ブタとエスプレッソと
name_ja: ブタとエスプレッソと
cuisine: Pork curry and tonkatsu (spice-forward braised pork belly curry)
neighborhood: Higashimuki shopping arcade, near Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: Not suitable for celiac diners. The menu centers on wheat-breaded tonkatsu (deep-fried pork cutlet), katsu curry and katsudon, and the curry roux is built on a long-simmered base; flour-thickened roux and breaded fried items make heavy gluten exposure and shared cooking surfaces near-certain. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: This is a pork specialist. The signature curry is built around roughly 250–280g of sous-vide braised pork belly, and the curry sauce itself is simmered with chicken stock (鶏ガラ) alongside fruits and vegetables, so even the sauce is not vegetarian. Sides include miso soup (typically fish dashi). There are no documented vegan or vegetarian dishes.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町2 パレスビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded to chō level (東向南町2) via GSI; building-level coordinates unavailable. Result falls within the central Nara box and matches the Higashimuki arcade location ~270m from Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: About 10 counter seats only; very small.
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); no fixed weekly day off documented.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter shop where a few words of Japanese and patience help, especially since the entrance is famously hard to find — you reach it by walking through the neighboring ramen shop to the seating at the back.
caveats: Payment is cashless only — no cash accepted. The entrance is genuinely hard to find: you enter through the adjacent Tenkaippin ramen shop and walk to the counter at the rear. Posted hours vary between sources, so check before going. Portions are very large (the signature dish totals roughly 800g of pork, rice and curry).

bio.background: A small counter eatery tucked into the Palace Building on the Higashimuki shopping arcade, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The shop has moved location more than once, and the owner has spoken of wanting to settle and stay put here for the long haul. It is famously hard to find: the way in runs through the neighboring ramen shop, so you walk through and head to the counter at the back.
bio.specialty: The flagship is the Super Pork Curry (超ブタカレー): a single massive slab of sous-vide braised pork belly — around 250 to 280g of Kazakaze sangen pork — laid over a generous bowl of rice, so tender it falls apart with a spoon. The curry sauce is its own quiet showpiece, slow-simmered with coffee and cacao, chicken stock, and a large amount of fruit and vegetables for a deep, fruity, only-mildly-spicy flavor. The shop also serves tonkatsu, katsu curry, katsudon (including a sauce katsudon made with hard-to-find Hermes sauce) and hamburg steak, with set meals offering free rice and curry refills.
bio.philosophy: An unpretentious, generous, pork-and-spice-obsessed kitchen — big portions, careful low-temperature cooking of the pork, and a curry built more on depth than on heat.

anecdotes.text: The Super Pork Curry built a following beyond Nara: it reportedly sold around 1,500 portions over two days when the shop took it to the Fuji Rock Festival in 2022.
Getting in is part of the legend — the entrance is so hidden that you have to walk through the ramen shop next door to reach the counter seating at the back.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen deep-fries wheat-breaded pork cutlets (tonkatsu, katsu curry, katsudon) in a small space, making cross-contact with gluten very likely; no dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The curry sauce itself is simmered with chicken stock (鶏ガラ) and the menu is entirely pork-centered, so there is no vegan-safe dish to begin with.

cuisine: 老舗洋食（家族経営の個人店） / Old-school Japanese-style Western (yōshoku)
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（花芝町・花芝商店街）
gf_detail: This is a classic yōshoku (Japanese-Western) diner whose core dishes are inherently wheat-heavy and not celiac-safe. The signature items — hamburg steak and cutlets — are bound and breaded with wheat, and the demiglace and white (gratin) sauces are thickened with wheat flour (roux). There is no documented gluten-free menu, no allergen information, and no indication of a dedicated fryer or any cross-contamination control. Not suitable for someone who must avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: A meat-centered Western diner: the menu is built around hamburg steak, beef cutlet (bifukatsu), chicken cheese cutlet, beef stew, and cheese gratin. Sauces are meat-based demiglace and dairy white sauce. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian option, and the small one-person kitchen offers no indication of plant-based accommodation.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市花芝町17
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned first feature coordinates [135.829056, 34.686321] for 奈良県奈良市花芝町17番地; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Roughly 6 table seats; an intimate, small space not suited to large groups.
hours_raw: 火～日 11:30-14:00（ランチ中心）。月曜定休。出典により夜営業（〜20:00）の記載もあり、夜の営業有無は要確認。
closed_days: 月曜日 (Mondays)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-only neighborhood diner run solo by an elderly chef with a gruff but warm manner — go relaxed, expect little to no English, and a few words of Japanese will go a long way.
caveats: Hours conflict between sources: Tabelog lists lunch-only 11:30-14:00 while one aggregator showed 11:30-20:00 — confirm evening service before visiting. Seat count (~6) and signature dishes are from a diner blog and aggregator listings, not an official site (none found). Cash only; no parking.

bio.background: Yoshikawa-tei is a long-standing family-run Western diner founded in 1981 in Nara's Hanashiba neighborhood, a short walk north of the city center through the covered shopping arcades. It is run single-handedly by its now-elderly owner-chef, who trained in his younger years at Kikusuirou (菊水楼), one of Nara's most venerable traditional restaurants, before opening his own intimate yōshoku kitchen.
bio.specialty: Old-school yōshoku: a thick-cut chicken thigh dressed in a thin batter with melting cheese and finished in demiglace (chicken cheese cutlet), alongside hamburg steak, beef cutlet (bifukatsu) under generous demiglace, beef stew, and a rich, piping-hot cheese gratin.

anecdotes.text: The chef trained at the historic Nara restaurant Kikusuirou before opening Yoshikawa-tei in 1981, and has kept the small Showa-era dining room going for more than four decades.
Regulars affectionately describe the owner as gruff and blunt in appearance, but find the warmth of this tiny, nostalgic room endearing rather than off-putting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are breaded cutlets and roux-thickened demiglace/white sauces, so wheat is pervasive across the menu and shared cooking surfaces; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat- and dairy-centered kitchen (hamburg, cutlets, beef stew, cheese gratin) run by a single chef; no plant-based preparation or separation is documented.

cuisine: 定食・家庭料理の食堂（旬の野菜を中心とした手作りごはん）
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（東笹鉾町・東大寺近く）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東笹鉾町34
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from address_ja 奈良県奈良市東笹鉾町34; first feature coordinates [135.83371, 34.690727], within the Nara box.
seats_text: Very small counter/table seating (around 3 seats); reservations and visits limited to groups of up to 3 people, larger groups cannot be accommodated.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 14:30); lunch-focused. Tabelog lists 11:00 open, L.O. 13:00, close 13:30. Closed Mondays and irregular days; hours announced on the official blog/Instagram.
closed_days: Mondays and irregular days (check official blog/Instagram before visiting)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, owner-run lunch spot with strict policies (email reservation in advance, groups of three or fewer, no children under 10, cash only), so some conversational Japanese and advance planning will make your visit much smoother.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu centers on Japanese set meals using soy sauce and dashi, with dishes like croquettes and hamburgers that contain wheat. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or celiac-safe handling is documented.
vegan_detail: Vegetable-forward seasonal cooking (one course features around 10 seasonal vegetable dishes), but the kitchen uses fish dashi/stock and the menu includes meat and fish mains, so it is not vegan by default. The owner asks that you communicate dietary needs in advance by email; whether a fully vegan meal is possible would need to be confirmed directly.

bio.background: ごはん 芽や is a very small, owner-run lunch eatery tucked into the quiet Higashisasahoko-cho area near Todai-ji in central Nara, behind an unassuming storefront with a large glass window.
bio.specialty: Handmade seasonal set lunches (teishoku) built around vegetables of the season, served as a two-course choice with two main dishes; recent examples include cod-roe and potato croquettes, chicken-burdock hamburger, and chicken roll in tomato cream sauce.
bio.philosophy: An emphasis on careful, handmade, heartfelt home cooking centered on seasonal Yamato/Nara vegetables, served in a deliberately intimate setting.

anecdotes.text: The shop keeps a deliberately personal, small-scale operation: reservations are requested by email the day before, parties are limited to three people, children under ten are not admitted, and customers are asked to mention any dietary concerns ahead of time.
Daily set lunches rotate with the seasons, with two mains offered at a fixed lunch price and small extra portions of rice available on request.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Japanese set-meal kitchen using soy sauce, dashi, and wheat-containing dishes (croquettes, hamburgers); no gluten-free protocol documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese seasonings including soy sauce are used; soy sauce typically contains wheat and no wheat-free alternative is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish dashi/stock is used in cooking and meat/fish mains are on the menu, so vegan and vegetarian dishes share a non-vegan kitchen.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner asks customers to communicate dietary concerns and allergies in advance when reserving by email.

positives.text: Cooking is centered on seasonal vegetables, with a course featuring around ten seasonal vegetable dishes.

caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources (Tabelog lists L.O. 13:00 / close 13:30; the official blog indicates 11:00-14:00, L.O. 14:30). Operation is lunch-only with irregular closures, so confirm via the blog/Instagram. Seat count is approximate (very small; group size capped at 3). Note: one fetched source segment referenced an Ikebukuro/Tokyo address that appears unrelated to this Nara shop and was disregarded; the verified address is 奈良市東笹鉾町34.

cuisine: チーズ料理カフェ／国産ナチュラルチーズ・手作りランチ・チーズケーキ
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodations are documented. The kitchen is cheese-and-baking focused: the lunch features quiche (wheat pastry), and the signature items are baked cheesecakes, so wheat is central to the menu. There is no evidence of gluten-free preparation, and the place is not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: This is a cheese-specialty café; dairy is the core of nearly every dish (cheese boards, cheesecakes, cheese-laden lunch). No vegan menu is documented. The lunch does include organic/pickled Nara vegetables as components, but the menu is built around dairy and is not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市光明院町13-2-1（奈良もちいどのセンター街）
geocode_note: Geocoded from Tabelog lot address 奈良県奈良市光明院町13-2-1 via GSI; within Nara box. Shop sits on もちいどのセンター街 near Kintetsu Nara Station.
neighborhood: もちいどのセンター街（近鉄奈良駅）
seats_text: About 8 seats (small café).
hours_raw: 11:00–20:00. Wednesday is takeout only. Closed Thursdays and alternate Tuesdays.
closed_days: Thursdays, plus alternate (every other) Tuesdays; Wednesdays are takeout only.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, friendly owner-run cheese café where a warm greeting in Japanese goes a long way; pointing at the day's cheese board or cheesecake works fine even with limited Japanese.
caveats: Hours are irregular: closed Thursdays and every other Tuesday, with Wednesdays takeout only. Only about 8 seats and roughly 10 lunch servings a day, so lunch can sell out. Closing time and exact lunch availability are best confirmed via the shop's Instagram before visiting.

bio.origin: Relocated from Tokyo to Nara, a city she grew to love during her university years.
bio.background: The owner, Miho, is a certified Cheese Professional who moved from Tokyo to open a café devoted to Japanese natural cheese. The shop first operated inside the Mochiidono 'Dream CUBE' challenge-shop space, then relocated and reopened a short walk away on もちいどのセンター街 in September 2024.
bio.specialty: Curated boards of domestic natural cheese from small Japanese makers (selections from prefectures such as Saga, Shiga, Saitama and beyond), handmade lunches built around quiche, soup and organic Nara vegetables, and Miho's signature two-layered cheesecakes in rotating seasonal flavors like pear-caramel and purple-sweet-potato-parmigiano.
bio.philosophy: Miho is fascinated by Japan's natural cheese world and the individual personalities of the makers she sources from. She is drawn to Nara as the historic home of 'so' (蘇), an ancient form of cheese, and designs her layered cheesecakes around the idea of two distinct characters combining into one harmony.

anecdotes.text: Miho was drawn to Nara partly because of its connection to 蘇 (so), an ancient Japanese cheese, and she frames her shop as celebrating individual small-batch Japanese cheesemakers whose lifestyles she deeply admires.
The café relocated about 50 meters from its original Dream CUBE space to a renovated storefront on もちいどのセンター街, and has expanded its lineup to include tea, jam and cup gelato.

positives.text: Lunch plates include organic Nara vegetables prepared as pickles/sides, so vegetable components are part of the menu (though dishes are still dairy-centric).

lead: イタリア食堂 パインストア
name_ja: イタリア食堂 パインストア
cuisine: Creative Italian rooted in Nara, with fermentation and smoking
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and no GF program is documented. This is an Italian-rooted kitchen using Japanese seasonings (miso, pickles, likely soy/fermented condiments) plus smoked and fermented preparations, so cross-contact and hidden wheat are realistic. Celiacs should not assume any dish is safe; you would need to ask directly, and confirmation cannot be relied upon here.
vegan_detail: Menu is meat- and fish-forward — signature plates include smoked salted mackerel, local pork ham, and cheese-based items. There are vegetable dishes featuring Yamato vegetables, but at least one mushroom dish uses a fermented mushroom dashi and others likely rely on animal-derived seasonings, so vegetable plates are not reliably vegan. No vegan offering is documented; ask in advance about a plant-based plate.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市橋本町32 もちいどの林ビル 2F東
geocode_note: Geocoded from full address (奈良県奈良市橋本町32) via GSI; result within central Nara box.
neighborhood: もちいどのセンター街（近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約3〜5分）
seats_text: 10 seats — 6 at the counter plus one table for four; small, intimate space
closed_days: 火曜日 (Tuesdays)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, chef-run counter spot where ordering and dietary questions will go most smoothly in basic Japanese, so come prepared or with a translation app.
caveats: Cash only. Very small (10 seats), so reserving ahead is wise even though walk-ins are accepted. Not a vegetarian/vegan or gluten-free destination — the menu centers on local pork, fish, and fermented/smoked plates.

bio.background: The chef trained in Italian cooking and spent time working in both Italian and Vietnamese kitchens before opening this small counter restaurant in Nara's Mochiidono shopping arcade. That mixed lineage shows up in the cooking, which takes an Italian foundation and reinterprets it through Nara's pantry.
bio.specialty: Fermentation- and smoke-driven small plates built on local ingredients. House favorites include an 'addictive' smoked salted mackerel (癖になる焼き塩鯖), ham made from locally raised Sato/Go pork (郷ポークのももハム), a mushroom stir-fry finished with a fermented mushroom dashi (十津川きのこ炒め), and a playful house take on smoked daikon pickle called 偽いぶりがっこ (a 'fake' iburigakko). Lunch has featured a Hainanese-style chicken rice.
bio.philosophy: To build on an Italian base while making the most of Nara's own produce and Japanese seasonings, aiming for a flavor that feels somehow nostalgic. As the kitchen puts it, Italian technique plus Nara ingredients and Japanese condiments creates 'a taste that feels familiar.'

anecdotes.text: The chef calls the cooking 'fermentation and smoking basin cuisine' (発酵と燻製の盆地料理), a nod to Nara's landlocked basin and to the smoked, fermented small plates that define the menu.
The room is deliberately tiny and personal — six counter seats and a single four-top across two floors, with carefully chosen dishware and quirky decorative touches.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around pork ham, smoked mackerel, and cheese, and even a vegetable dish (mushroom stir-fry) uses a fermented mushroom dashi, so vegetable plates are not reliably plant-based.

lead: チャイナダイニング 飛天
name_ja: チャイナダイニング飛天
cuisine: 中華料理・点心・飲茶（医食同源コンセプト／ビーガン・ベジ点心あり）
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（東向商店街）
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program. This is a Chinese restaurant where dim sum wrappers, dumplings, and noodle dishes (including the vegetable tanmen) are wheat-based, and most savory dishes use soy sauce, which in Japan virtually always contains wheat. There is no documented gluten-free menu or cross-contamination control, so a celiac would need to ask carefully in advance and should treat the kitchen as not celiac-safe. The vegan dishes are not automatically gluten-free.
vegan_detail: Genuinely vegan-friendly for a mainstream Chinese restaurant: there is a documented dedicated vegan section and a 7-course vegan tasting course (around 3,300 yen). Individual plant-based dishes include a vegetable salad with house carrot dressing, a vegetable tanmen in a vegetable-consommé (not fish-dashi) broth, a greens/mushroom/vermicelli soup, soy-meat with cashews, soy-meat black-vinegar "sweet and sour pork," and a greens-and-yuba stir-fry. The restaurant frames these around the Chinese 素菜 (Buddhist vegetable cookery) tradition and an 医食同源 ("food and medicine from the same source") concept. Because the bulk of the menu is meat- and seafood-based and standard Chinese seasonings can contain animal-derived stock or oyster/fish sauce, a strict vegan should confirm each non-course dish and request no animal stock; the labeled vegan course is the safest choice.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町26
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良市東向南町26, within the Nara box.
seats_text: Roughly 120 seats (about 70 table seats plus tatami seating ~50); upstairs tatami banquet rooms seat up to about 80. A large venue with private and curtain-partitioned spaces.
closed_days: No fixed regular closing day (irregular holidays); open daily with a mid-afternoon break.
cultural_comfort_note: English-speaking staff and English menus are available, and there is a clearly labeled vegan section, so a foreign student can order plant-based dishes here with relative ease.
caveats: Hours include a mid-afternoon break (closed roughly 15:00-17:00). Vegan items beyond the dedicated course should still be confirmed for hidden animal stock. Wheat is pervasive, so not suitable for strict celiacs without direct confirmation.

bio.background: A long-established Chinese restaurant tucked along the Higashimuki covered shopping arcade a few minutes' walk from the heart of central Nara. It sits a notch above the typical neighborhood Chinese place: the dining room is styled like a Chinese restaurant in an Asian resort, with interior furnishings imported from Bali, and it runs sister restaurants elsewhere in the city.
bio.origin: Nara, Japan (Higashimuki arcade near Kintetsu Nara Station)
bio.specialty: Hand-made dim sum and dumplings, oversized deep-fried sesame balls with a chewy mochi center, a black-vinegar 'sweet and sour pork' millefeuille made with local Yamato pork wrapped around mountain yam, and house-made almond tofu for dessert. A standout for travelers is the dedicated vegan course built on soy-meat, yuba, seasonal greens and mushrooms.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen leans on the Chinese idea of 医食同源 — that food and medicine share the same source — and on the temple-rooted 素菜 (vegetable cookery) tradition, with the aim that eating things that are both delicious and good for the body supports good health.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant explicitly draws on China's 素菜 Buddhist-vegetarian cooking heritage and an 医食同源 concept, presenting a full vegan course alongside its meat menu.
The dining room is themed as a Chinese restaurant in an Asian resort, decorated with interior pieces imported directly from Bali.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a dim sum and noodle-focused Chinese kitchen where wheat wrappers, dumplings and wheat noodles are central; no gluten-free protocol is documented, so cross-contact with wheat should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Many savory dishes are seasoned in the Chinese style with soy sauce; Japanese soy sauce almost always contains wheat, so soy-sauce-based dishes are not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegan dishes (soy-meat, yuba, greens) are prepared in a kitchen that primarily cooks meat and seafood; no separate-equipment or cross-contact policy is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Vegewel lists the restaurant as having both vegan and vegetarian menus and English-speaking staff plus English menus, which helps when discussing dietary needs, but no specific allergy-handling procedure is described.

positives.text: A clearly labeled dedicated vegan section and a 7-course vegan tasting menu give plant-based diners reliable, pre-vetted choices rather than ad-hoc substitutions.
The vegetable tanmen and the greens/mushroom/vermicelli soup are built on a vegetable consommé rather than fish dashi, making them suitable for vegans.

cuisine: Naturally-leavened (sourdough) bread sandwiches and craft cola cafe
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The entire concept is naturally-leavened sourdough and focaccia made from wheat and rye flour, so bread is the core of nearly everything served. There is no indication of gluten-free bread or menu items, and the shared bakery environment makes this unsuitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Mostly not vegan. The signature sandwiches feature meat (grilled chicken, ham), dairy (Havarti, ricotta cheese), and the breads/brownies are unlikely to be vegan. A vegan diner might manage a plain bread item, the craft cola, or the lemonade, but there is no documented vegan menu. Ask in advance (the owner speaks fluent English).
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町12 夢CUBE J号
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町12 via GSI; result (34.68055, 135.829178) falls within the central Nara box, in the Mochiidono shopping arcade near Kintetsu Nara Station.
neighborhood: Mochiidono Center-gai arcade (もちいどの夢CUBE), near Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: 6 counter seats
hours_raw: 10:00-16:00 (cafe); bread/goods sold until 16:00 or until sold out. Some listings note 8:00/8:30 openings for retail bread. Closed Thursdays.
closed_days: Thursdays (木曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: The owner is fluent in English and the cafe is popular with international visitors, so a foreign student can order comfortably in English.
caveats: Opened (pre-open) in September 2025, so the record is based on early reporting; hours vary slightly across listings (cafe service roughly 10:00-16:00, with bread retail starting earlier some days). Seat count (6 counter seats) and the owner being a foreign, English-fluent proprietor are reported but the owner's exact name and nationality are not formally documented.

bio.background: NEINA is a small, owner-run cafe tucked into the innermost unit of the Yume CUBE artisan incubator in Nara's historic Mochiidono shopping arcade, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It opened in autumn 2025. The proprietor is a fluent English speaker with a foreign background, and the shop quickly became a favorite of international visitors as well as locals. The interior is finished in warm Yoshino cypress, giving the six-seat counter a calm, unhurried feel.
bio.specialty: Naturally-leavened sourdough breads and focaccia, baked in-house in a large oven from wheat and rye flour fermented with wild yeast and lactic-acid cultures. About three kinds of bread are offered each day, turned into sandwiches such as a focaccia sandwich with ham, Havarti and roasted vegetables with pesto, a grilled chicken sandwich, and a fruit sandwich with lemon syrup, strawberries, apple and ricotta. These are paired with locally-made craft colas and craft beers.
bio.philosophy: Great bread starts with time, patience, and quality ingredients. NEINA uses a centuries-old natural fermentation technique and invites guests to slow down, savor the moment, and enjoy the simple pleasures of artisan bread.

anecdotes.text: The cafe leans into letting the ingredients speak for themselves, with hard-style breads served simply with olive oil and sea salt alongside the sandwiches.
Set in the back of the Yume CUBE artisan units in the Mochiidono arcade, the shop's English-speaking owner has made it a draw for overseas visitors.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a working sourdough bakery; wheat and rye flour are central to the entire operation, so airborne flour and shared surfaces make cross-contamination effectively unavoidable for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Breads and sandwiches share the same kitchen and counter as dairy and meat fillings; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner speaks fluent English, so allergy or ingredient questions can be discussed directly, though no formal allergen handling is documented.

positives.text: Bread is made in-house from wild-yeast and lactic-acid natural fermentation with carefully selected ingredients, and the English-speaking owner can answer ingredient questions.

cuisine: Traditional Japanese confectionery (wagashi)
neighborhood: Mochiidono Center Street (near Kintetsu Nara Station)
gf_detail: This is a traditional wagashi shop whose signature items — dorayaki-style "Saho no Kawase," chestnut pastries, manju, and baked confections — are made with wheat flour, and there are no gluten-free offerings or accommodations. Even the rice-based items (mochi, yokan, ohagi) are produced in a shared workshop with wheat-flour goods. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Some items are plant-based by tradition (mochi, yokan, ohagi, and azuki/mugwort sweets typically use only rice, beans, and sugar), but baked goods like dorayaki, castella-style items, and manju commonly contain egg and dairy. There are no labeled vegan products and the shop is not a vegan destination, so a vegan would need to ask item by item.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町12番地
geocode_note: Geocoded from official-site address 餅飯殿町12; Tabelog lists 餅飯殿町37-1, but both fall on the same Mochiidono arcade block. Coordinates are within the Nara box.
seats_text: A small takeaway confectionery shop with a workshop in the back; no dine-in seating is documented.
hours_raw: 9:00～18:30 (official site); Tabelog lists 09:00–18:00 daily. Closed Thursdays.
closed_days: Thursdays (closures may be irregular)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny family-run sweets counter where staff likely speak little English, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at the beautifully displayed confections will go a long way.

bio.origin: Nara, on the Mochiidono covered shopping arcade that leads toward the historic Naramachi district
bio.background: Yuzuki is a small, owner-run wagashi shop tucked into the middle of Nara's Mochiidono arcade. The proprietor trained for thirteen years in Takatsuki before opening this shop, and continues to make everything by hand in a workshop at the back of the store so the sweets are always fresh.
bio.specialty: The signature confection is 'Saho no Kawase,' a dorayaki-style sweet of yokan wrapped in a fragrant baked crust, named after a poem in the Man'yoshu. Other favorites include wakakusa-mochi (mugwort mochi heaped with bean paste) and kuri-i chestnut pastries, alongside seasonal sweets such as sakura-mochi in spring, kashiwa-mochi and warabi-mochi in summer, and freshly pounded mochi at New Year.
bio.philosophy: The shop's stated approach is to carefully select ingredients, let each material's natural quality shine, and make its sweets by long-standing traditional methods.

anecdotes.text: The owner spent thirteen years training in Takatsuki before setting up this shop on the Mochiidono arcade.
The flagship sweet 'Saho no Kawase' takes its name from a poem in the Man'yoshu, Japan's oldest poetry anthology.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-flour confections (dorayaki-style sweets, manju, baked goods) are made in the same back-of-shop workshop as rice- and bean-based sweets, so cross-contamination with wheat is likely.

caveats: Address differs between sources (official site 餅飯殿町12 vs Tabelog 餅飯殿町37-1); both are on the same Mochiidono arcade. Hours vary slightly by source (closing 18:00 vs 18:30). Closed Thursdays per the local gourmet listing, but the shop's closures appear irregular. Seat count and dine-in availability are not documented — this is essentially a takeaway sweets shop.

cuisine: Spanish
neighborhood: 餅飯殿町 (Mochiidono), near Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町27-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from full street address via GSI; result [135.829208, 34.681343] falls within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Seat count not published; sources describe it as a small, hidden-gem (隠れ家) space suited to both casual drinks and full meals.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:00 (L.O.), 17:00-21:00 (L.O.); closed Sunday and Monday
closed_days: Sunday and Monday
cultural_comfort_note: A small family-run neighborhood place where a few words of Japanese and a friendly attitude go a long way; bring cash, as cards are not accepted.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The kitchen centers on bread — house-baked natural-yeast bread, weekly sandwich lunches, and pan con tomate — so wheat is everywhere and there is no indication of gluten-free accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Veg-forward in spirit — the name huerto means "vegetable garden" and they cook with produce from their own kitchen garden — but it is not a vegetarian or vegan restaurant. Signature plates lean on jamón serrano, sheep's cheese, and meat, and dishes are not designed dairy- or animal-free. A vegan could likely ask for a vegetable-only plate, but should confirm in advance; default offerings are not vegan.
caveats: Cash only. Seat count not published. No official website found among fetched sources. Vegan/GF accommodation not documented — confirm directly.

bio.background: huerto is a family-run Spanish kitchen tucked beside the Mochiidono arcade in central Nara. The name is Spanish for vegetable garden, and that idea sits at the heart of the cooking: much of the produce comes from the owners' own kitchen garden, turned into honest, ingredient-led tapas and plates.
bio.specialty: Catalan-style pan con tomate (garlic-and-tomato-rubbed bread topped with sheep's cheese and 23-month-aged jamón serrano), house-cured and homemade charcuterie, paella, and a rotating weekly lunch built around sandwiches on the bread that the owner's wife bakes from natural yeast.
bio.philosophy: Cook simply and seasonally from the garden, and keep the room relaxed enough for either a quick glass of Spanish wine or a long, proper meal.
roles: baker (natural-yeast bread, by the owner's wife)

anecdotes.text: The house bread used for the weekly lunch sandwiches is a natural-yeast loaf baked by the owner's wife, whose own recipe anchors the midday menu.
A signature is pan con tomate finished with sheep's cheese and 23-month-aged jamon serrano, and the kitchen leans on vegetables grown in its own garden.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bread is baked in-house and is central to the menu (sandwiches, pan con tomate), so flour and wheat handling are pervasive throughout the kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes combine vegetables with cured ham (jamon serrano) and sheep's cheese on shared plates, so vegetable items are prepared alongside animal products.

positives.text: Much of the produce comes from the owners' own kitchen garden, so vegetable dishes are genuinely garden-forward.

lead: アカトンボコーヒー（aka tombo coffee）
cuisine: Cafe / Melbourne-style espresso and Australian meat pies
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. The signature items are wheat-pastry Aussie meat pies, and no gluten-free menu, dedicated handling, or allergy protocol is mentioned on the official site, the Nara tourism page, or Tabelog. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The cafe centers on espresso drinks (typically with dairy milk, e.g. flat whites) and Angus beef meat pies. Plant-milk substitution for coffee may be possible to ask about, but no vegan food options are confirmed. A vegan traveler would need to ask directly.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町15 Yビル2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町15 via GSI; within the Nara box.
neighborhood: 餅飯殿町 (Mochiidono arcade), near Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: 15 seats on the 2nd floor (3 counter, 6 table, 6 bench); an additional ~20-seat floor-seating self-study area on the 3rd floor.
hours_raw: Mon, Wed, Thu, Sat, Sun & holidays 07:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30); Tue 07:00-12:30 (L.O. 12:00); closed Friday (except holidays). On holidays, Tuesday and Friday run 07:00-19:00.
closed_days: Friday (except public holidays); Tuesday afternoons (open only 07:00-12:30). Irregular closures are also announced via social media.
cultural_comfort_note: A foreign student can feel completely at ease here: the Australian owner and his English-fluent Japanese wife run a daily English-conversation cafe with an English menu, free Wi-Fi, and power outlets.
caveats: Closed Fridays and Tuesday afternoons, with extra irregular closures posted on social media, so check before visiting. Cash only (PayPay also accepted), no credit cards or other electronic money. Up the stairs to the 2nd floor (Y Building).

bio.origin: Melbourne, Australia (run with his Nara-born wife Keiko)
bio.background: aka tombo coffee is a tiny handmade cafe tucked up on the second floor of a building in the Mochiidono shopping arcade, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It was created by Chris, a Melbourne native who fell in love with Nara, together with his wife Keiko. Inspired by the restoration of a traditional Japanese storehouse, Chris hand-built much of the interior himself, including a hand-plastered (shikkui) counter, custom shoji screens, and the tables. The couple wanted a place where visitors from around the world could meet and talk over good coffee.
bio.specialty: Melbourne-style espresso drinks (flat whites and other espresso-based coffees) paired with Australian-style Angus beef meat pies. The cafe also has fun with latte art such as '3D cat cappuccinos' at special events, and serves a morning/breakfast menu.
bio.philosophy: The cafe's guiding idea is 'east meets west' - bridging Japanese and Australian cultures. The name aka tombo means 'red dragonfly': in Japanese tradition the dragonfly is believed to carry ancestors and loved ones home during the Obon season, and the couple chose it to symbolize connecting two worlds. Keiko, who holds top English qualifications, also runs relaxed English-conversation sessions, making the cafe a meeting point for locals and travelers alike.

anecdotes.text: The name 'aka tombo' (red dragonfly) was chosen because, in Japanese tradition, dragonflies are thought to carry ancestors and loved ones home during Obon - mirroring the couple's wish to connect East and West.
Owner Chris, a Melbourne native, handcrafted much of the cafe himself - a hand-plastered counter, shoji screens, and tables - after being inspired by traditional Japanese storehouse restoration.
Keiko, Chris's wife, holds TOEIC 990 and Eiken Grade 1, and leads the morning English-conversation sessions that give the cafe its bilingual, welcoming character.

lead: トラットリア・ピアノ
name_ja: トラットリア・ピアノ
cuisine: Italian / Neapolitan wood-fired pizza and fresh pasta featuring Yamato vegetables and Nara brand meats
neighborhood: 橋本町 (Hashimoto-cho), off Sanjo-dori near the entrance to Nara Park, ~5 min from Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The kitchen is built around wheat: wood-fired Neapolitan pizza and fresh pasta are the core menu, so flour and gluten are everywhere and cross-contamination is unavoidable. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no GF accommodations are documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan place, but vegetable-forward. The restaurant is genuinely proud of Yamato traditional vegetables and offers a signature vegetable-heavy pizza ('Pizza Yamato'), so a plant-leaning diner may find something — but cheese, meat, and likely animal-based broths/dashi run through much of the menu. There is no documented vegan menu and no allergy/dietary labeling, so a vegan must ask directly about cheese, butter, eggs, and stock on each dish. Strict vegans should not assume any pizza or pasta is safe without confirming.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市橋本町15-1 1F・2F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市橋本町15; within the Nara bounding box.
seats_text: 46 seats (6 counter, 40 table), across 1F and 2F
hours_raw: Tue–Fri: 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00); Sat/Sun/holidays lunch runs to 15:30 (L.O. 15:00); Closed Mondays
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, tourist-friendly trattoria at the edge of Nara Park where pointing at the pizza or pasta menu works fine, though staff communication is mostly in Japanese so a few basic phrases or a translation app help.

bio.background: An established casual Italian trattoria at the edge of Nara Park, easy to spot by the stacked firewood, the piano-key motif, and the Italian flag out front. It sits in the Hashimoto-cho shopping-street area a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, making it a natural rest stop between Todai-ji, Kasuga Taisha, Kofuku-ji, and the Nara National Museum.
bio.specialty: Neapolitan-style pizza fired in a wood oven that reaches around 500C, giving a puffed, slightly charred cornicione edge with a thin, light center. The standouts are the locally minded pies like the original vegetable-piled 'Pizza Yamato', alongside Neapolitan classics such as Margherita and Quattro Formaggi, plus a rotating line of fresh pasta. Drinks lean into Italian, French, and Japanese wines and Italian beer.
bio.philosophy: Carries Italy's idea of cooking that honors regional ingredients and applies it to Nara: the kitchen sources lively produce directly from committed growers, including Yamato traditional vegetables, and pairs them with Nara brand meats like Yamato beef, Yamato pork, and Yamato jidori chicken.

anecdotes.text: The shop is recognizable from the street by its stacked firewood, piano-key decor, and Italian flag, signaling the wood-fired oven inside.
Beyond classic Margheritas and Quattro Formaggi, the kitchen builds an original 'Pizza Yamato' loaded with Nara vegetables, leaning fully into local produce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu is wood-fired pizza and fresh pasta, so wheat flour is central to the kitchen and airborne/surface flour cross-contamination is effectively unavoidable. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pizzas and pastas typically involve cheese, butter, and eggs, and meat dishes use Nara brand meats; shared oven and prep surfaces mean cross-contact with animal products is likely. No vegan menu is documented, so confirm each dish with staff.

positives.text: Strongly vegetable-forward for an Italian restaurant, with Yamato traditional vegetables featured prominently and a dedicated vegetable-heavy 'Pizza Yamato', so plant-leaning diners have real options to discuss.

caveats: Official website (piano.syncronicity.co.jp) returned a TLS certificate-name error and could not be fetched directly; identity, menu, and concept were confirmed via Tabelog, the Narakko local-info article, and the restaurant's gorp.jp official listing. No allergy, gluten-free, or vegan labeling was found on any source, so dietary claims are inferred from the cuisine and should be verified in person. Chef/owner name not documented.

lead: カフェ エトランジェ ナラッド（CAFE ETRANGER NARAD）
name_ja: カフェ エトランジェ・ナラッド
cuisine: Cafe / Italian / bar
neighborhood: Kamisanjo-cho, Sanjo-dori, near Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on Neapolitan pizza, pasta and bread-based dishes, so wheat is prevalent throughout the kitchen. There is no evidence of a dedicated fryer or cross-contamination controls. Celiac travelers should not assume any dish is safe and would need to ask staff directly.
vegan_detail: The kitchen builds dishes around Yamato (Nara) vegetables and offers plant-leaning items, notably a signature tofu hamburger served in a wooden box with ancient-grain rice and abundant local vegetables, which one listing describes as accommodating vegan, vegetarian and allergy needs and being additive-free. A vegetarian menu is noted as available. However the broader menu includes meat, fish, cheese and prosciutto, and many dishes likely use dashi or dairy, so vegan diners should confirm specific items and preparation with staff.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市上三条町23-4 奈良市観光センター ナラニクル
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; result [135.825439, 34.682251] falls within the Nara box and matches the NARANICLE tourist-center location on Sanjo-dori.
seats_text: Approximately 145 seats (about 115 indoor table seats plus 30 terrace seats).
hours_raw: Mon-Thu, Sun: 11:00-22:00; Fri, Sat, day before holidays: 11:00-23:00. Lunch 11:00-15:00, dinner from 17:00 (last orders around 21:30-22:00). Open year-round (no regular closing day).
closed_days: None (open year-round, 年中無休)
cultural_comfort_note: Located inside Nara's main tourist center on Sanjo-dori, this is a welcoming, casual spot used to international visitors, with picture-friendly dishes and card payment, so a foreign student can drop in comfortably with little Japanese.
caveats: Operated by a national restaurant company (Balnibarbi) rather than a solo owner, and it is a large 145-seat venue inside the city tourist center, so it reads more as a polished cafe-restaurant than a hidden local gem. Dietary accommodations beyond a vegetarian/tofu option are not formally documented and should be confirmed in person; no gluten-free or allergen-control program is published.

bio.background: Cafe Etranger Narad opened inside NARANICLE, the renovated Nara City Tourist Center on Sanjo-dori, which reopened in June 2017. It was conceived as a meeting place where travelers passing through Nara and local residents can mix, doubling as an information hub and occasionally hosting vegetable markets (marche).
bio.specialty: The signature Etranger Box is a wooden-box meal showcasing Yamato (Nara) vegetables and Japanese ingredients, anchored by a tofu hamburger with ancient-grain rice from Nara-grown rice, alongside items like red-miso gratin, caponata and salads. The kitchen is also known for Neapolitan pizza, colorful local-vegetable salads, matcha desserts, and pairings with wine and local Nara sake from the Toyosawa brewery.
bio.philosophy: To present the appeal of Nara on a single plate by celebrating local Yamato vegetables and regional producers, serving as a crossroads between visitors and the community.

anecdotes.text: The cafe sits within NARANICLE, the Nara City Tourist Center reborn in June 2017, and frames itself as a meeting point for travelers and locals, sometimes hosting vegetable markets on site.
Its signature wooden-box meal highlights Yamato vegetables and Nara-grown ancient-grain rice with a tofu hamburger, reflecting a farm-to-table focus on regional ingredients.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The menu is built around Neapolitan pizza, pasta and bread, so wheat is widespread in the kitchen and cross-contamination is likely; no gluten-free handling is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: One listing for the signature tofu hamburger box states it can accommodate vegan, vegetarian and allergy requests and is additive-free, suggesting some willingness to adapt, but this is not detailed for the full menu and should be confirmed directly.

positives.text: Cooking emphasizes local Yamato vegetables and offers a tofu-based hamburger box, giving vegetarian and plant-leaning diners a real anchor dish.

name_en: French o・mo・ya Naramachi (Furenchi Omoya Naramachi)
cuisine: Creative French in an Edo-period machiya, using Nara seasonal ingredients, served on Shigaraki ware with chopsticks
neighborhood: Naramachi (Kounodo-cho), near Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: No gluten-free program. The cuisine is wheat-heavy: courses include bread, French-style sauces, and breaded/floured preparations, and there is no celiac-safe handling described. The restaurant says it can remove specific allergens if requested well in advance when staff coordinate with the chef, but there is no dedicated gluten-free kitchen or cross-contamination control. Not suitable for celiacs; gluten-sensitive diners should call ahead.
vegan_detail: Not a vegetarian or vegan destination. Set courses are built around meat and fish (pork shoulder, wagyu, horse tartare, eel, sea bream, fresh fish) and French sauces that typically use butter, cream, and meat/fish stock. No vegan or vegetarian course is published. The restaurant will accommodate allergen removal if arranged in advance via the chef, so a meat-free request might be possible by prior phone arrangement, but nothing vegan-specific is documented. Vegans should not rely on this without advance confirmation.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市公納堂町11
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市公納堂町11, within the Nara bounding box.
seats_text: Approximately 60 seats on the ground floor of the machiya: one 6-person table, four 4-person tables, one 3-person table, and three 2-person tables.
hours_raw: Wed–Sun & holidays: Lunch 11:30–15:00 (L.O. 13:30), Dinner 17:30–21:30 (L.O. 19:30). Closed Mon & Tue. Additional closures around Golden Week, Obon, Christmas, and New Year.
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays; plus extra closures around Golden Week, Obon, Christmas, and New Year
cultural_comfort_note: A calm, refined sit-down course restaurant where reservations are expected and a little Japanese helps; a foreign student can manage with a polite greeting and a pre-booked course, but it is a special-occasion outing rather than a casual drop-in.
caveats: Reservation strongly recommended (the kitchen confirms bookings by phone). Dining is course-only, so it is pricier than casual spots, especially at dinner. Any allergen removal must be arranged in advance through the chef. A spring 2026 price increase applies. Despite the "Naramachi" name, the venue sits in Kounodo-cho within the old town.

bio.background: Set inside a roughly 150-year-old wooden townhouse from the late Edo period, this restaurant brings French cooking into a quintessentially Nara setting. The sukiya-style machiya keeps its old soul intact: a box staircase, a woven cedar ceiling, snow-viewing sliding windows, and panes of rare hand-drawn glass frame the meal. It is part of a small family of o・mo・ya and omo cafe dining spaces in the old town.
bio.origin: Naramachi, the historic merchant quarter of central Nara
bio.specialty: Creative French course menus built on seasonal Nara-area ingredients, plated on rustic Shigaraki pottery and eaten with chopsticks. Lunch offers tiered full courses (a lighter Petit course, a popular Regular course, and a celebratory Authentic course with the day's fresh fish), while dinner is a chef's tasting that has featured horse tartare, eel, scallop-and-shrimp soup, sea bream, and a wagyu roast.
bio.philosophy: A deliberate fusion of Japanese and Western sensibility — French technique expressed through local produce, native ceramics, and the rhythms of a traditional Nara house.

anecdotes.text: Diners eat refined French courses with chopsticks off rustic Shigaraki-ware plates, inside a sukiya-style townhouse preserved with box stairs, a woven cedar ceiling, snow-viewing windows, and rare hand-drawn glass.
Lunch is offered as three tiers — a Petit course for first-timers, the popular Regular course, and an Authentic course featuring the day's fresh fish for anniversaries and celebrations.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Courses include bread and French sauces, and there is no gluten-free menu or celiac-safe handling described, so cross-contact with wheat is likely.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menus center on meat and fish with French sauces typically using butter, cream and stock; no vegan or vegetarian course is published, so shared preparation with animal products is expected.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant indicates it can remove specific allergens on request when arranged in advance, with staff coordinating with the chef.

lead: 発酵薬膳料理 らふぁえろ
name_ja: 発酵薬膳料理 らふぁえろ
cuisine: Fermented yakuzen (medicinal) cuisine — vegetable-forward wellness cooking served as a set meal, plus cafe
neighborhood: Takabatake-cho, between Naramachi and Kasuga
gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. The cooking is built on fermented vegetables and a house "fermentation water," and the kitchen prides itself on using almost no conventional seasonings, drawing umami from the ingredients themselves — which is promising, but there is no published statement about wheat, soy sauce, or celiac-level cross-contamination control. A celiac traveler would need to ask directly about every component (especially any seasoning, miso, or sauce) before ordering.
vegan_detail: Strongly vegetable-forward, but NOT vegan by default. The signature 一汁三菜発酵膳 set (around 2,000 yen) is described as featuring grilled fish (焼き魚) as a main alongside many small vegetable dishes, and the menu makes no vegan/vegetarian distinction. The fermentation-water concept could lend itself to a plant-only meal, but you must phone ahead and ask whether a fully plant-based version (no fish, no fish dashi) can be prepared.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1055-1
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.836395, 34.679607] for 奈良県奈良市高畑町1055-1; within the Nara box.
seats_text: Exact seat count not published; small old-house cafe with tatami seating and a garden veranda, individual/semi-private room areas. Estimated under 30 seats.
hours_raw: Cafe 10:00–16:00 / Lunch 11:00（11:30）–16:00; closed Wednesday & Thursday
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, mostly-Japanese-speaking old-house cafe where a few polite Japanese phrases (and pointing at the set meal) will go a long way; phoning ahead in Japanese to ask about a fish-free version is wise.
caveats: Credit cards are not accepted, though electronic money is. The headline set meal includes grilled fish, so vegan/vegetarian and gluten-free diners must call ahead to confirm what can be adapted. The restaurant is run as part of a disability-employment support operation by a local company.

bio.background: Raffaello (らふぁえろ) occupies a roughly century-old Taisho-era machiya on a quiet street in Nara's Takabatake neighborhood, the old quarter between the Naramachi townscape and Kasuga Shrine. Stepping in feels like visiting a countryside grandmother's house: tatami rooms, a veranda overlooking a garden, and an unhurried pace meant for savoring each small dish. It is run as part of a local social enterprise that pairs the kitchen and cafe with a women's clothing atelier and upstairs woodworking, operating as a work-support workshop.
bio.specialty: The signature is the 一汁三菜発酵膳 (a one-soup, three-dish fermented set, around 2,000 yen): a large tray of colorful, locally grown vegetables — mountain-vegetable appetizers, a grilled main, and many small fermented side dishes.
bio.philosophy: Every dish is made with house 'fermentation water' produced from a culture they nickname the magic egg, ハッコーはる, fermented in a salt-free rice-bran bed. The kitchen holds that letting all the ingredients undergo enzyme fermentation draws out natural umami, so they use almost no added seasoning and serve very lightly seasoned food that lets each ingredient's own flavor come through — cooking they describe as gentle on body and mind, using pesticide-free vegetables sourced directly from contract farms.

anecdotes.text: The whole menu revolves around a 'fermentation water' made from a house culture nicknamed the magic egg (ハッコーはる), fermented in a salt-free nuka bed and used in every dish so that almost no conventional seasoning is needed.
Set in a roughly 100-year-old machiya in Takabatake with tatami seating and a garden veranda; the signature 一汁三菜発酵膳 lays out abundant local vegetables and a grilled main across a large tray.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature fermented set meal is described as including grilled fish as a main alongside the vegetable dishes, with no vegan/vegetarian distinction noted, so plant-based diners cannot assume the standard meal is fish-free.

positives.text: Cooking relies on fermented, pesticide-free vegetables and a house fermentation water, with the kitchen stating it uses almost no conventional seasoning and serves very lightly seasoned, vegetable-forward food.

cuisine: Japanese kaiseki / regional cuisine centered on Yamato vegetables, wild yam (jinenjo), Japanese-Chinese medicinal herbs, and Yamato beef
neighborhood: Takabatake-cho, near Nara Park (Sagiike pond), inside the Fufu Nara hotel
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a Japanese kaiseki kitchen that relies heavily on dashi and soy sauce (typically wheat-containing), with tempura and other wheat-based items on the menu, so it is not celiac-safe by default. The kitchen asks guests to declare allergies and dietary needs at the time of reservation, so a celiac would need to communicate requirements well in advance and confirm directly; cross-contamination cannot be assumed to be controlled.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. While the cooking showcases fresh Yamato vegetables, wild yam and Japanese herbs, the cuisine is built on fish dashi and also features sashimi and Yamato/domestic beef, so standard courses are not vegan. The hotel requests allergy and dietary preferences at reservation, so a plant-based meal might be arranged on advance request but is not a standing offering and must be confirmed directly.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1184-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市高畑町1184-1; within the Nara bounding box.
seats_text: 68 seats total (1F: 26 seats, 2F: 42 seats); private rooms available, with views over the Japanese garden and Sagiike pond
hours_raw: Lunch / cafe 11:30-15:00 (food L.O. 14:00, drinks L.O. 15:00). Dinner and breakfast service is reserved for hotel guests. Non-guests welcome for lunch.
closed_days: Open year-round except the New Year holiday period (closed December 28 - January 2)
cultural_comfort_note: As the restaurant of a luxury resort hotel, the setting is refined and reservations are recommended, but staff are used to non-Japanese visitors and lunch is approachable; a few polite Japanese phrases and advance notice of any dietary needs will go a long way.
caveats: Restaurant inside the Fufu Nara hotel. Non-hotel guests can visit for lunch/cafe only (dinner and breakfast are for staying guests). No documented vegan or gluten-free program; allergy and dietary needs must be communicated at reservation. Two phone numbers appear across listings (050-5597-8951 and the hotel line 0742-81-8236).

bio.background: Tekisui sits on the southern edge of Nara Park beside the tranquil Sagiike pond, occupying the dining space of the luxury resort hotel Fufu Nara, which opened in 2020. Guests dine in calm private rooms looking out over a Japanese garden. While dinner and breakfast are reserved for hotel guests, the lunch and cafe service welcomes visitors from outside the hotel.
bio.specialty: Body-friendly Japanese cuisine drawing on Nara's deep history as a cradle of medicinal knowledge: fresh Yamato vegetables, grated wild mountain yam (jinenjo tororo), aromatic Japanese and Chinese herbs, and Yamato beef. Documented dishes include the seasonal Tekisui set, a domestic beef loin cutlet with local tomatoes, and a gentle herb-spiced curry, alongside a handmade castella and house-made pudding.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated theme is 'karada ni yasashii Nihon ryori' (Japanese cuisine that is gentle on the body), pairing classical Japanese culinary technique with Nara's local character and its heritage as the birthplace of pharmaceutical knowledge, so that a meal is both flavorful and nourishing.

anecdotes.text: Every private dining room overlooks the hotel's Japanese garden and the adjacent Sagiike pond, framing the meal with a quiet view of greenery just south of Nara Park.
The cuisine leans on Nara's identity as a historic center of medicinal knowledge, weaving Japanese-Chinese herbs and wild yam into the dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The hotel asks guests to inform them of any allergies or food preferences at the time of reservation.

cuisine: Wagashi (traditional Japanese sweets) / daifuku mochi
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. A traditional wagashi shop whose baked confections (dorayaki-style "Saho no Kawase," monaka, manju) contain wheat flour, and the kitchen has no allergen separation. While some items such as mochi, daifuku, ohagi and warabi mochi are rice- or starch-based and naturally wheat-free, they are made in a shared workshop alongside wheat-based sweets, so cross-contamination is likely. Not suitable for celiacs; coeliacs should avoid.
vegan_detail: Possibly some options, but verify. Many classic wagashi here are plant-based in principle (mochi, anko/red-bean paste, mugwort wakakusa-mochi, kuri/chestnut sweets, ohagi, warabi mochi). However daifuku and dorayaki-style items can contain egg or dairy in the dough, and no vegan labelling exists. There is no documented vegan handling, so a vegan traveler must ask item by item.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町37-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町37-1 returned [135.82869, 34.680351], within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: Mochiidono arcade (Mochiidono-cho), about 3-4 minutes' walk south of Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: Primarily a takeout confectionery shop with a small counter/display area; no private rooms and no whole-shop reservations. Seating is minimal to none.
hours_raw: 9:00-18:00 (Tabelog); official/shop listings give 9:00-18:30. Open mid-arcade on Mochiidono Street.
closed_days: Sources differ: Tabelog lists 不定休 (irregular closures); a local guide lists Thursdays closed. Treat closing as irregular and confirm before a special trip. Note mame-daifuku (bean daifuku) is reportedly made only on Saturdays.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny family-run sweets shop where little English is likely, but pointing at the beautiful seasonal confections in the case and a friendly konnichiwa will get you served easily; bring cash.

bio.background: Yuzuki is a small, owner-run wagashi (traditional Japanese sweets) shop tucked midway along the historic Mochiidono covered arcade, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The proprietor trained for about 13 years in Takatsuki before opening here. The back of the shop is a working kashi-koba (confectionery workshop), so what fills the case each day is made fresh on the premises.
bio.specialty: The signature sweet is 'Saho no Kawase' (佐保の河瀬), a dorayaki-style confection of yokan wrapped in a fragrant baked skin, named after a verse in the ancient Man'yoshu poetry anthology. Other beloved items include wakakusa-mochi packed with mugwort, kuri-goromo (whole chestnut wrapped in chestnut paste), monaka, chestnut manju, ohagi, and daifuku. Bean daifuku (mame-daifuku) is made on Saturdays. The shop turns with the seasons: sakura-mochi in spring, kashiwa-mochi and warabi-mochi in summer, and freshly pounded mochi at New Year.
bio.philosophy: The shop's stated approach is to carefully select ingredients, let those ingredients speak for themselves, and make sweets in the old-fashioned traditional manner with restrained, gentle sweetness.

anecdotes.text: The flagship 'Saho no Kawase' takes its name from a place in the Man'yoshu, tying a humble arcade sweet to Nara's classical literary heritage.
The owner spent roughly 13 years training in Takatsuki before setting up the shop, and built a workshop into the back so the cases can be restocked fresh daily.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A traditional wagashi shop with a single on-site workshop that produces both wheat-based baked sweets and rice/starch-based mochi, so cross-contamination of naturally gluten-free items is likely. No allergen separation is documented.

positives.text: Many menu items are inherently plant-based and wheat-free in concept (mochi, red-bean anko, mugwort wakakusa-mochi, chestnut sweets, ohagi, warabi-mochi), giving a starting point to ask about, though shared-kitchen risk remains.

caveats: Two listings give slightly different hours (Tabelog 09:00-18:00 vs official/guide 09:00-18:30) and different closing info (Tabelog 不定休/irregular vs a local guide stating Thursdays); hours_week uses 18:30 with Thursday closed as the best-available estimate but closures are effectively irregular. No documented allergen handling; GF/vegan suitability is inferred, not confirmed by the shop. Cash only.

cuisine: Machiya café & bar — Hainanese chicken rice lunch, shaved ice, and local Nara sake
neighborhood: Naramachi (Nishiterabayashicho), about an 8-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西寺林町28-6
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Small: roughly 10 sofa seats during the daytime café, with about 8 counter seats for the evening bar.
hours_raw: 11:30-16:00, 17:00-22:00 (LO); closed Wednesdays (and irregular closures)
closed_days: Wednesdays, plus occasional irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run machiya café where Japanese will go furthest; the owner prefers no phone calls, so use the in-person counter or Instagram, and a few polite Japanese phrases will help.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu centers on rice dishes, fried items (shrimp fries, hamburger), Neapolitan pasta, and kinako-based desserts, and the chicken rice uses soy-based sauces; Japanese soy sauce typically contains wheat. No gluten-free or celiac protocols are documented, so this is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The signature dishes are chicken-based (Hainanese chicken rice, an "adult's children's lunch" with hamburger and shrimp), and even desserts like the kinako pudding are dairy/egg-style sweets. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented.

bio.background: A small café tucked into a traditional Nara machiya (townhouse) on the Nishiterabayashi shopping street in Naramachi, behind a natural-linen noren curtain. The same building runs a daytime café and an evening sake bar.
bio.specialty: By day the kitchen is known for its 海南鶏飯 (Hainanese chicken rice) — moist poached chicken breast with a scallion-and-ginger sauce, served with broth and adjustable with black soy and sweet chili — alongside year-round shaved ice (かき氷) and a 'black-molasses kinako pudding' made with roasted soybean flour and walnuts. A playful 'adult's children's lunch' piles together omelette-topped chicken rice, a hamburger, shrimp fries, Neapolitan pasta, salad and soup. By night the owner pours a curated selection of local Nara sake (奈良酒).

anecdotes.text: The chicken rice is a seasonal lunch item and service is suspended in winter, while shaved ice and pudding stay on the menu year-round.
The owner has asked food bloggers to remove the phone number and emphasizes a no-phone-call preference, reflecting how personal and small the operation is.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The chicken rice is served with soy-based sauces (black soy / sweet chili); standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, but no allergen detail is published.

caveats: Owner/chef name and personal background are not documented in public sources; hours are irregular with occasional unscheduled closures, and the Hainanese chicken rice is seasonal (paused in winter). The owner prefers not to take phone calls. No vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, or allergen accommodations are documented; the wheat/soy/dietary notes are inferred from typical Japanese ingredients, not stated by the venue.

lead: ならまち分校スイーツ部
name_ja: ならまち分校スイーツ部
cuisine: かき氷専門・古民家スイーツカフェ（クレープ・ガレット・ソフトクリームも）
gf_detail: This is a Western-style patisserie sweets cafe. Crepes, galettes, and the wafer/cookie toppings on the kakigori contain wheat, and shaved-ice syrups have not been documented as gluten-free. There is no gluten-free menu or accommodation noted. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The signature kakigori and soft-serve are built on dairy (cherry-milk syrup, matcha espuma/cream, soft serve) plus pistachio and other non-vegan components. No vegan options or plant-based substitutes are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南城戸町28-34
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 奈良県奈良市南城戸町28 at [135.827957, 34.676464], within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: ならまち（南城戸町・京終エリア）
seats_text: Small old-house cafe seating arranged like a Showa-era school classroom (student desks facing a blackboard); exact seat count not documented. Accommodates small groups.
closed_days: 火曜・水曜・木曜（Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday）
cultural_comfort_note: A charming nostalgic spot tucked in old-town Naramachi, but it is a tiny local Japanese cafe with no English menu or signage documented, so a foreign student is best off coming with a guide or a translation app and patience.
caveats: Hours vary by source: Tabelog lists Mon/Fri 12:00-16:00 and Sat/Sun 11:30-16:00, while local guides state 12:00-16:30; closing time may flex. Check Instagram before visiting as days/hours change and occasional evening "night ice" events run later. Dessert-only cafe; not a meal restaurant.

bio.background: Tucked down a quiet lane in old-town Naramachi, this dessert cafe is officially produced by patisserie trico, the well-loved Western-pastry shop based in nearby Kawai Town. The space is designed to recreate a rural Japanese branch-school classroom of the Showa era (1930s-60s): guests sit at wooden student desks facing a real blackboard you can write on, surrounded by children's calligraphy, exposed beams, and a vintage rotary telephone.
bio.origin: Naramachi, Nara City; the dessert craft comes from patisserie trico of Kawai Town.
bio.specialty: Towering, multi-layered kakigori (shaved ice) made with trico patisserie technique. The flagship is the 'Wakakusayama' ice, inspired by Nara's iconic three-tiered grassy mountain, layering pistachio and Uji matcha with hidden cherry-milk syrup and raspberry sauce, topped with a fawn-shaped wafer; newer creations such as the black-sesame-pistachio ice continue a numbered series. Crepes, galettes, and soft-serve ice cream round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: Blending Japanese and Western flavors in playful, Nara-themed desserts within a nostalgic classroom setting that invites lingering.

anecdotes.text: The kakigori are numbered, and from the 50th creation onward numbers are assigned in order of release, so a higher number means a more recent invention.
The blackboard at the front of the 'classroom' is free for guests to write on and is updated daily.
The signature Wakakusayama ice is themed on Nara's famous Mt. Wakakusa and crowned with a cute fawn-shaped decoration.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Desserts are built on dairy (milk syrups, matcha cream/espuma, soft serve) and the kitchen also makes crepes and galettes, so plant-based requests cannot be reliably accommodated.

cuisine: グルテンフリー米粉カフェ（米粉の食パン・シフォンケーキ・チーズケーキ、ランチに米粉パスタやキッシュ）
gf_detail: A dedicated gluten-free rice flour cafe. The owner developed a wheat allergy herself and built the entire menu around a 100% domestic rice flour blend (rice from Nara, Kyoto and Kyushu) - all bread, chiffon cakes, cheesecakes, caneles, plus lunch pasta and quiche are made with rice flour rather than wheat. Because the kitchen is built around rice flour for everything, this is one of the safer choices in central Nara for someone avoiding gluten. Still, no explicit celiac-grade cross-contamination protocol is documented, so a strict celiac should confirm details directly.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-focused cafe. Signature items use dairy and eggs (chiffon cake with whipped cream, baked and Basque cheesecakes, caneles; the lunch pasta uses a homemade rice-flour white sauce and cheese). No documented vegan menu, so vegans should ask in advance about what, if anything, can be prepared without animal products.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市下御門町20-19
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 奈良県奈良市下御門町20-19 to lng 135.828705, lat 34.67894 - within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: ならまち・下御門商店街（下御門町）
seats_text: Small cafe with table seating plus a small tatami area and a private back room seating up to about 6; exact total seat count not published.
closed_days: 月曜・火曜・水曜・日曜（営業は木・金・土のみ）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, calm owner-run cafe in a renovated old townhouse where a few words of Japanese and patience go a long way, though the gluten-free focus makes it especially welcoming for a foreign student avoiding wheat.
caveats: Open only Thursday, Friday and Saturday, 11:30-17:00 (last order 16:30); closed the rest of the week. Some lunch items (such as the rice-flour gratin/pasta) may need advance reservation. Seat count is not published and seating is limited.

bio.background: The cafe was opened in late November by Ms. Kawai, who developed a wheat allergy and went searching for foods she could eat safely. That search led her to gluten-free rice flour, and she went on to earn certification as a rice flour master (米粉マイスター) before opening her own cafe.
bio.origin: It sits in the Shimomikado shopping arcade just a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, in a renovated old townhouse that was once a shoe shop. Kawai kept original architectural touches - including the showcase's frosted decorative glass, reused as transom panels, and a wooden counter - giving the room a quiet Japanese-modern feel.
bio.specialty: Everything is built on a custom 100% domestic rice flour blend (rice from Nara, Kyoto and Kyushu), all handmade. The flagship is the chiffon cake, in flavors such as plain, wasanbon, Kaga hojicha, Sri Lankan Earl Grey, Uji matcha and Swiss chocolate, sweetened with mineral-rich kibi brown sugar and served with seasonal fruit and cream. Also notable are the baked and Basque cheesecakes, caneles and rice-flour bread, plus a lunch menu of rice-flour pasta and quiche.
bio.philosophy: Kawai wants the cafe to be unhurried: in her words, 'スピーディーさは無いが、ゆったりとした空間でほっこりとしてもらえるものを提供したい' - there is no rush here, but a relaxed space where guests can feel at ease. Her broader aim is to share 'health and happiness through food' and one day teach rice-flour baking classes.

anecdotes.text: The building previously appeared as a location in a film set in Nara, and the owner deliberately preserved the old shoe shop's fittings - such as the decorative frosted glass - when converting it into the cafe.
The interior is furnished with specialty coffee, custom-commissioned ceramics and items gathered from the owner's travels through some two dozen countries.
