staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner has a wheat allergy herself and built the cafe specifically around gluten-free rice flour, so staff understand wheat avoidance from personal experience.

positives.text: All bread, cakes and lunch dishes are made from a 100% domestic rice flour blend rather than wheat flour, making this a rare central-Nara cafe where the whole menu is gluten-free by design.
The owner became interested in gluten-free rice flour after developing a wheat allergy and earned rice flour master certification before opening, reflecting genuine expertise in wheat-free baking.

cuisine: 昭和の老舗喫茶・家庭的な大盛りカレーと珈琲
neighborhood: きたまち（南半田西町）
gf_detail: This is a single-dish curry-rice kissaten. Japanese curry is almost always thickened with a wheat-flour roux, so the only menu item is not gluten-free. There is no dedicated gluten-free preparation and no documented allergy accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The sole offering is beef curry rice — the curry is made from onions and beef chuck simmered for hours. There is no vegetarian or vegan option. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南半田西町4
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to chō-level (南半田西町); coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 11 seats: 3 counter seats plus three two-person tables. All non-smoking.
hours_raw: 11:00-14:00 (often sells out and closes early). Closed Sundays and the 1st and 3rd Wednesday of each month.
closed_days: Sundays; 1st and 3rd Wednesday of each month (also closes early when sold out)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, old-school local coffee shop with counter seating and cash only — basic spoken Japanese and a relaxed attitude will help, as there is no English menu and the format is a simple "curry only" affair.
caveats: Cash only, no reservations, just 11 seats, and frequently sells out before the 14:00 close — go early. The menu is literally curry rice only (small/medium/large). Closed Sundays and the 1st and 3rd Wednesdays. Not suitable for gluten-free, vegan, or vegetarian diners.

bio.background: Kissa Tagawa opened in 1966 in Nara's Kitamachi district and has been a beloved neighborhood fixture for over half a century. The shop's founder hailed from Tagawa City in Fukuoka Prefecture — the source of the shop's name — where he had run a bicycle shop before moving to Osaka to work as a bartender and in the coffee wholesale trade. The current owner, Okabe Ayako, is the second-generation proprietor, carrying on her father's shop. After the original premises (the ground floor of the aging Handa Apartment building) had badly deteriorated, the shop was given a full renovation and reopened on the same corner in March 2022, this time with an adjoining parking area.
bio.origin: Tagawa City, Fukuoka Prefecture (founder's hometown, source of the shop name)
bio.specialty: Enormous, mountainous portions of homestyle beef curry rice, served in small, medium and large sizes. The rice is piled so high that diners are offered extra curry sauce for free when the roux runs short.
bio.philosophy: An honest, unfussy 'curry only' shop: a hand-lettered note from the owner apologizes that they serve nothing but curry. The curry is made simply from onions and beef chuck simmered slowly for five to six hours, with no hidden seasonings or special spices — the owner describes portioning the rice purely 'by feel.' It is celebrated as the ultimate in gentle, homestyle Japanese curry.

anecdotes.text: The curry contains only onions and beef chuck, slow-simmered for five to six hours with no secret ingredients; the shop is famous for rice portions so towering that they sometimes finish the curry sauce before the rice, so extra sauce is offered free of charge.

Founded in 1966 by an owner from Tagawa City in Fukuoka — a former bicycle-shop keeper turned bartender and coffee wholesaler — the shop is now run by his daughter, second-generation owner Okabe Ayako, and reopened after a full renovation in 2022.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The only dish served is Japanese-style curry rice, which is conventionally thickened with a wheat-flour roux; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The single menu item is beef curry made with beef chuck and onions, so it is not vegan or vegetarian.

cuisine: Yoshoku — omurice (omelette rice) specialist
neighborhood: Kitamachi (きたまち), near Nara Women's University; Nabeyacho, central Nara
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市鍋屋町2-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市鍋屋町2-1 returned [135.829376, 34.687065], within the Nara box. Note: the place is in the きたまち (Kitamachi) area near Nara Women's University, NOT ならまち/三条通 as stated in the target brief — it relocated from Naramachi to Kitamachi in September 2022.
seats_text: Seven 2-person tables and two 4-person tables (22 seats); no counter seating; bright, casual room on the 2nd floor.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (Sat/Sun/holidays to 15:30); Dinner 17:00-21:00 on Wed, Sat, Sun only. Closed Thursdays. Order via ticket machine after being seated; roughly one-hour seating limit at busy times.
closed_days: Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: You order from a touchscreen ticket machine after sitting down, so little spoken Japanese is needed, but the menu and machine are in Japanese, so a translation app or a Japanese-speaking friend helps a foreign student order confidently.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The entire concept centers on soy-sauce-based omurice — the signature dishes use Igeta soy sauce (Inoue Honten), which is a wheat-brewed Japanese soy sauce, plus demi-glace, cream and other sauces. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and there is no information about a dedicated fryer or cross-contamination controls. Celiac travelers should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. This is an egg-and-chicken-rice omurice specialist — every signature dish is built on eggs (Ōmiya Farm eggs from Kashihara) over chicken rice, with soy-sauce, demi-glace, cream or mentaiko sauces. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.

bio.background: Sunbird is a one-man omurice specialist run by an owner-chef (surname Kamimura) originally from Kōchi, who built up experience running ethnic-cuisine restaurants and cafes in Nara before opening his own omelette-rice shop. He cooks alone, which can mean a wait at busy times. The shop opened in Naramachi in 2017 and relocated in September 2022 to a quieter second-floor space in the Kitamachi (きたまち) area near Nara Women's University, where it has built a loyal base of local regulars.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is the lemon butter soy sauce omurice — fluffy egg over soy-based chicken rice finished with a house teriyaki-style sauce made from lemon, butter and Edo-era Igeta soy sauce. Other favorites include the egg-yolk soy sauce omurice (topped with shredded nori, shichimi and green onion), demi-glace omurice, and cream-peperoncino and mentaiko-cream versions.
bio.philosophy: An emphasis on carefully sourced local Nara ingredients: specialty free-range eggs from Ōmiya Farm in Kashihara, Igeta soy sauce from the long-established Inoue Honten, and after the move a switch to fragrant, chewy Nara-grown Hitomebore rice.

anecdotes.text: Relocated from busy Naramachi to a calmer second-floor space in Kitamachi near Nara Women's University in September 2022; the owner says he likes the quieter neighborhood and the local residents who come in regularly.

After the move he switched the rice to the Hitomebore variety for its aroma and chewy texture, and has spoken about wanting to do pop-ups in his home prefecture of Kōchi and add side dishes and egg sandwiches.
The lemon butter soy sauce omurice is described by diners as a flavor you can't get anywhere else.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature omurice dishes are finished with a sauce made from Igeta soy sauce (Inoue Honten), a traditional wheat-brewed Japanese soy sauce, so the core menu contains wheat/gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All dishes are egg-based omurice over chicken rice; no plant-based options are documented.

caveats: The target brief locates this in ならまち/三条通 ~5-8 min from Kintetsu Nara, but the restaurant relocated to the Kitamachi (きたまち) area at 奈良市鍋屋町2-1 in September 2022; that is the current, verified location (Tabelog lists ~329m from Kintetsu Nara Station). Closed-day (Thursday) and dinner-day pattern come from a Nara gourmet article rather than the Tabelog page, which did not list closed days; treat hours as generally regular but confirm via Instagram before a dinner visit since evening service runs only Wed/Sat/Sun. No allergy, gluten-free or vegan handling information exists; this is an egg-and-soy-sauce omurice shop and is unsuitable for celiac or vegan diners.

lead: 日本料理 ひとしずく
name_ja: 日本料理 ひとしずく
cuisine: 日本料理・懐石（Japanese kaiseki）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a traditional Japanese kaiseki kitchen where dishes rely heavily on soy sauce (which contains wheat) and fish dashi; courses include items that are not celiac-safe. There is no published allergen handling, so this is not a safe choice for someone with celiac disease unless arranged directly in advance.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. The menu centers on seasonal fresh fish and Kuroge Wagyu beef, and Japanese cooking here uses fish-based dashi throughout, so the food is not vegan or vegetarian. No plant-based course is advertised; any vegan request would need to be discussed with the owner-chef ahead of time and is not guaranteed.
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from the full street address; coordinates fall within the central Nara bounding box (Takabatake-cho, Naramachi area).
neighborhood: 高畑町（ならまち界隈 / Takabatake-cho, Naramachi area）
seats_text: 7 counter seats only; no private rooms. Full buyout available for up to 7 guests.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–13:30 (L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:30– (L.O. 20:00, reservation only). 不定休 (irregular closures). Source: official site nara-hitoshizuku.com.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular / no fixed closing day)
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate seven-seat counter where the owner cooks everything himself, so come with a reservation and at least basic Japanese, since menus and conversation are likely Japanese-only and there is no published English support.
caveats: Hours differ slightly between listing sources; the official site (used here) gives lunch 11:30–13:30 and reservation-only dinner from 17:30. Closures are irregular, so confirm the date and reserve directly. Dinner runs around ¥20,000 per person.

bio.background: A solo owner-chef runs every part of Hitoshizuku, sourcing ingredients from across Japan and personally handling everything from purchasing to cooking. Guests sit at a single wood-toned counter and watch the dishes being prepared and served the moment they are ready.
bio.origin: Tucked into the quiet Takabatake-cho corner of Nara's Naramachi district, about a 14-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station.
bio.specialty: Seasonal Japanese kaiseki built around the day's fresh fish and Kuroge Wagyu beef, including a wagyu steak set; lunch courses are more approachable while dinner is a reservation-only multi-course experience tailored to budget and party size.
bio.philosophy: To let guests savor premium Japanese cuisine made from carefully selected ingredients in a warm, wood-grained space.

anecdotes.text: The counter seats just seven, with no private rooms, and can be reserved in full as a private buyout for a small group.

cuisine: Okonomiyaki / Akashi-yaki (Kansai-style griddle dishes)
neighborhood: Higashimuki Shopping Arcade (near Kintetsu-Nara Station)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町13
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市東向南町13, within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Around 44-48 seats combining table and tatami (zashiki) seating; sources vary slightly.
hours_raw: 11:00 - 21:00 (last order around 20:30; one source lists ~20:00 close)
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, popular local griddle spot where staff cook at your table; some basic Japanese or pointing at the menu helps, and cash is essential.
gf_detail: This is an okonomiyaki and akashi-yaki shop where the core dishes are wheat-flour batters and the dashi/sauce typically contain wheat. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Dishes are built around eggs, pork, squid, octopus, shrimp and fish-based dashi; akashi-yaki is dipped in dashi broth. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
caveats: Hours and seat counts vary slightly across sources; closing day is irregular, so confirm before visiting. Cash only.

bio.background: Okaru is a long-established okonomiyaki and akashi-yaki shop tucked into the Higashimuki shopping arcade a short walk from Kintetsu-Nara Station, and it is treated as something of a local soul food by Nara residents. Diners sit at table-side griddles where staff cook in front of them, and the retro Kansai interior is decorated with large fish rubbings (gyotaku), hinting that the proprietor is a keen angler.
bio.specialty: The signature draw is the akashi-yaki, fluffy egg-rich dumplings packed with generous octopus and served to dip in warm dashi; the okonomiyaki and modern-yaki (with a choice of soba or udon and pork, squid or minced meat) are equally beloved, alongside an extravagant deluxe Okaru-yaki loaded with beef, pork, prawns, squid, octopus, scallop, oyster and several eggs.

anecdotes.text: The shop gained extra fame after actor Tsuyoshi Domoto visited during a TV program, drawing in fans afterward.
A recent renovation kept beloved decor like the large fish rubbings on the walls.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Okonomiyaki and modern-yaki are wheat-flour batter dishes cooked on a shared teppan; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki sauce and dashi at Kansai griddle shops typically contain wheat; no allergen information is published for this shop.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Akashi-yaki is served in fish-based dashi and dishes rely on egg, pork, and seafood; no vegan preparation is documented.

lead: うどん そば 呑み処 三条坊
name_ja: うどん そば 呑み処 三条坊
cuisine: うどん・そば・居酒屋（大和牛・地元食材を使う麺処）
neighborhood: 三条通・JR奈良駅周辺（ビエラ奈良内）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市三条本町1-1 ビエラ奈良 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市三条本町1-1 returned [135.817139, 34.677574], within the central Nara box and consistent with the JR Nara Station / Viera Nara location.
seats_text: Around 29-30 seats (roughly 24 table seats plus a 5-seat counter; one semi-private space, up to 20 for group events).
hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30) daily; closed Dec 30 - Jan 1.
closed_days: No regular weekly closing day; closed for New Year period (Dec 30 - Jan 1).
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, walk-in udon and soba spot right by JR Nara Station with picture-friendly noodle dishes and card/QR payment, so a foreign student can manage with basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The core menu is wheat-based udon and soba, and broths/curries rely on dashi and standard sauces; there is no gluten-free menu, no allergen documentation, and no indication of a dedicated preparation area. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The menu lists a small ビーガン (vegan) section - vegan keema curry udon (about 1,180 yen, +50 yen to switch to soba), vegan soy-milk carbonara udon (about 980 yen), and vegan keema curry rice (about 1,180 yen) - but all three are marked 食材が入荷次第販売開始 ('to go on sale once ingredients are in stock'), i.e. not yet actually being served. There are also mountain-vegetable udon and vegetable tempura, but standard noodle broths use fish dashi and noodles are wheat. Call ahead (0742-23-1154) to confirm whether the vegan items are currently available before relying on them.
caveats: Seat count differs slightly between listings (about 29-30). The official site presents it as a standalone eatery inside the Viera Nara complex at JR Nara Station; an old narakotsu.co.jp link now just redirects to a HotPepper marketing page, so it is treated as an independent local eatery. Vegan dishes are listed but flagged as not yet on sale, so availability should be confirmed by phone.

bio.background: A relaxed udon, soba and small-plate drinking spot tucked into the Viera Nara complex right under JR Nara Station, opened in 2010. It pairs a calm, traditional Japanese interior fitting the old capital with hearty noodle dishes built around Nara's local Yamato beef (Yamato-gyu) and regional ingredients.
bio.specialty: Yamato beef dishes and noodle specialties: a 'Stamina Nara' udon developed as a noodle-shop take on Nara's stamina ramen, Yamato beef curry udon, Yamato beef rice bowl, and a signature large 'Daibutsu' udon, alongside tempura and a soba/udon lineup.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing Nara's local Yamato beef and regional produce in approachable, station-side udon and soba while doubling as a casual place for a drink.

anecdotes.text: Its 'Stamina Nara' udon is presented as a collaboration reworking Nara's well-known stamina ramen into udon form.
The menu features dishes built around Yamato beef, a Nara regional brand beef, including curry udon and a beef rice bowl.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on wheat udon and soba cooked in shared water, with no gluten-free menu or allergen labeling shown, so cross-contamination with wheat is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths and sauces use standard dashi and seasonings; no wheat-free soy sauce or gluten-free tsuyu is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan dishes are listed but marked as not yet on sale pending ingredients, and they would be prepared in a kitchen that handles fish dashi and meat, so cross-contact is likely; confirm by phone.

positives.text: The menu does include a small vegan section (keema curry udon/rice and soy-milk carbonara udon) plus mountain-vegetable udon and vegetable tempura, showing some plant-based intent.

cuisine: Vietnamese (ベトナム料理) — pho, bun, rice dishes
neighborhood: Higashimuki shopping arcade, near Kintetsu Nara Station (ならまち／近鉄奈良駅)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町19 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja (奈良県奈良市東向南町19) via GSI; within Nara box.
seats_text: 32 seats including a raised tatami/sunken-seating area, table seating, and counter seating
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, accessible Vietnamese spot popular with international visitors, with an illustrated/photo menu and affordable set lunches, so a foreign student should feel comfortable ordering here.
gf_detail: Pho and other dishes are built on rice noodles (naturally gluten-free), but the kitchen uses fish sauce and likely soy-based seasonings, and there is no indication of gluten-free protocols or a dedicated setup. A celiac diner would need to ask about each sauce and cross-contact; not a verified gluten-free destination.
vegan_detail: This is an omnivore Vietnamese restaurant centered on meat and fish: pho uses chicken or beef broth, fish sauce (nuoc mam) is a core seasoning, and most dishes feature chicken, beef, or pork. No vegan menu is documented. Some lighter herb/rice/spring-roll items may exist, but a strict vegan would need to confirm broth and fish sauce; not a reliable vegan destination.
caveats: Cuisine differs from the brief's "vegetable-centric, vegan-friendly small shop" framing: in practice this is a 32-seat omnivore Vietnamese restaurant relying on chicken/beef broth and fish sauce. Vegan and gluten-free status are best treated as ask-only. No official website found.

bio.background: Com Ngon is a Vietnamese restaurant tucked up a narrow staircase on the second floor of a building in the Higashimuki shopping arcade, a few minutes' walk from the center of Nara, with a soba shop occupying the floor below. It is described as a rare find for Nara, where Vietnamese cuisine is uncommon. Cooks brought from Vietnam prepare the food, giving it an authentic character; the name Com Ngon means 'delicious rice'.
bio.specialty: Signature dishes are the pho rice-noodle soups — Pho Ga (chicken) and Pho Bo (beef) in the Hanoi style, with a light, refreshing broth balanced by herbs, spices and fish sauce — alongside Hue-style spicy Bun Bo, Bun Cha (grilled and boiled pork over rice noodles), Banh Canh, and fresh and fried spring rolls.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims for authentic Vietnamese flavor while keeping dishes accessible to Japanese palates, including milder options for those who dislike spicy food.

anecdotes.text: The interior leans into Vietnamese atmosphere with bamboo tables, tin-can chopstick holders and ceramic coasters, and sunken kotatsu-style seats looking out over the shopping arcade; Vietnamese handicrafts and food ingredients are displayed and sold near the register.
On a weekday afternoon the room was nearly full and mostly international diners, reflecting strong inbound tourism to the area.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Vietnamese cooking relies on fish sauce (nuoc mam) as a core seasoning, and dipping sauces and marinades may also include soy-based or wheat-containing condiments; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pho and most dishes use chicken or beef broth and fish sauce, and meat dishes (chicken, beef, pork) dominate the menu; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

positives.text: Pho and many noodle dishes are based on rice noodles rather than wheat, which can suit a gluten-sensitive diner who confirms the sauces and broth in advance.

cuisine: Thai cuisine and kakigori (shaved ice), using local Nara ingredients
neighborhood: Takabatake / Naramachi area (near Kintetsu Nara Station, approx. 16 min walk)
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. This is a Thai restaurant whose signature dishes include Miwa-somen tom yum kung (wheat noodles) and curries; Thai cooking typically relies on soy/fish sauces, and no gluten-free or celiac protocols are documented. A celiac traveler should not assume safety here and would need to ask in detail; cross-contamination cannot be confirmed.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant. There is a vegetable yam (salad) made with produce from neighboring farms and many non-spicy dishes, but Thai cooking here generally uses fish sauce and the kakigori use dairy (yogurt cream, mimolette cheese, coconut milk). No vegan menu or labeling is documented, so a vegan diner would need to ask which items can be prepared without fish sauce, shrimp paste, or dairy.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1073-2
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市高畑町1073-2, within the Nara bounding box.
seats_text: 18 seats
hours_raw: 11:30-14:00, 14:30-20:30 (L.O. 20:00). Kakigori (shaved ice) is by advance reservation only. Tabelog also lists a simplified 11:00-19:00 range; the 11:30-14:00 / 14:30-20:30 split is from a detailed source.
closed_days: Mondays (if Monday is a public holiday, closed the following Tuesday instead)
cultural_comfort_note: A small chef-run Thai spot where ordering is comfortable with some basic Japanese or pointing at the menu, and staff are used to welcoming families and first-timers; the shaved ice typically needs an advance online booking.
caveats: The famous salt-and-cheese kakigori ("Kopech") and most shaved-ice flavors require advance reservation via the booking site, and the kakigori lineup rotates weekly. Hours sources differ slightly (Tabelog summary 11:00-19:00 vs. detailed 11:30-14:00 / 14:30-20:30); confirm before visiting.

bio.background: Run by an owner-chef with a wide-ranging culinary background spanning French cooking, bakeries, and other food-service work, who opened this small Thai restaurant out of a love for Southeast Asia, wanting to share with local diners the kinds of dishes that are everyday fare over there.
bio.specialty: Thai dishes built on local Nara ingredients - Miwa-somen tom yum kung, a green curry made with Yamato regional chicken, and a vegetable yam (Thai salad) using produce from neighboring farms - alongside a celebrated shaved-ice (kakigori) program. The standout is 'Kopech', an unconventional salt-based kakigori built on coconut, pepper and cheese (the name combines the three), finished with finely shredded mimolette and black pepper over coconut-milk syrup; mango kakigori made with Indian Kesar mangoes, yogurt cream and coconut-milk syrup is another favorite.
bio.philosophy: Reinterpreting Thai cooking through Nara's local produce, and treating shaved ice as a fast-rotating creative canvas - most kakigori flavors appear and then vanish from the lineup within about a week.

anecdotes.text: The kakigori menu rotates so quickly that most flavors disappear from the lineup roughly a week after they appear.
The 'Kopech' shaved ice is so distinctive - salty cheese crunch wrapped in creamy coconut sweetness with occasional bursts of pepper - that fans have given it their own nickname.
There are plenty of non-spicy dishes, so it is enjoyable for families with children and for older diners as well.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature dishes include Miwa-somen tom yum kung, which uses wheat somen noodles, indicating wheat is handled in the kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kakigori use dairy (yogurt cream, mimolette cheese) and Thai dishes typically use fish sauce, so vegan-safe preparation is not documented.

positives.text: A vegetable yam (Thai salad) is made with produce from neighboring farms, and many non-spicy dishes are available, so vegetable-forward and mild options exist.

lead: 御菓子司 鶴屋徳満 本店
name_ja: 御菓子司 鶴屋徳満 本店
cuisine: Traditional Japanese sweets (wagashi) and sweets parlor — house-made anko shaved ice (kakigori), anmitsu, and namagashi
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a traditional wagashi shop whose core products are wheat-based: castella, mikasa (dorayaki-style filled cakes), monaka wafers, and senbei all contain wheat flour. While the signature kakigori (kuzu/wasanbon syrup over shaved ice with house-made azuki bean paste) and pure-azuki items may be naturally low in wheat, the kitchen handles wheat-based confections extensively, so there is no dedicated gluten-free preparation and high cross-contact risk. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not advertised as vegan. Many items are plant-based by tradition (azuki bean paste, kuzu, agar/anmitsu, the signature aoniyoshi rakugan-style sweet made from kudzu starch and sugar), but the shop also makes egg-and-dairy confections such as castella and sponge-based mikasa, and does not label items for vegans. A vegan traveler could potentially eat plain anko-based sweets, the aoniyoshi, or fruit/agar anmitsu, but each item must be confirmed individually with staff for egg, dairy, and honey.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市下御門町29
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市下御門町29, within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Small eat-in space at the main shop for enjoying kakigori and sweets; exact seat count not published. The eat-in area is modest (the branch shop reportedly has more seating than the honten).
hours_raw: 9:00-18:00 (Thursday closed). A local food guide lists the eat-in service around 10:00-17:00; the shop's posted hours are 9:00-18:00.
closed_days: Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming old-town confectionery where pointing at the display case and a simple greeting will get you served, though menus and conversation are mainly in Japanese.
neighborhood: Shimomikado shopping arcade (Shimomikado-cho), at the northern edge of Naramachi, about a 10-minute walk south of Kintetsu-Nara Station
caveats: Founding year differs by source: the shop's history (via Shinise.tv and a regional magazine describing it as a ~130-year-old confectioner) points to a Meiji-era founding around 1898, while the company page lists Showa 31 (1956) — likely the year of corporate incorporation rather than the original founding. Eat-in hours from a food blog (10:00-17:00) differ slightly from the shop's posted retail hours (9:00-18:00); confirm kakigori availability on arrival, as shaved ice is seasonal (mainly warmer months).

bio.background: A long-established Nara confectioner founded in the Meiji era (around 1898), said to have started as an independent venture after splitting from the Osaka house of Tsuruya Hachiman. The main shop sits in the Shimomikado arcade at the northern edge of Naramachi, and after a renovation it continues to draw locals and visitors for both takeaway sweets and an in-shop sweets-parlor experience.
bio.specialty: The house signature is 'Aoniyoshi,' an elegant two-toned strip confection. Once made like rakugan from rice flour, it was reworked using kudzu starch, giving it its distinctive delicate texture. In summer the shop is loved for its kakigori: shaved ice with wasanbon-sugar syrup, black molasses, and house-made red bean paste from premium large azuki, finished with decorative leaf candies; a salted cherry-blossom 'sakura-gori' is also offered. Year-round the cases hold anmitsu, the imperial-style 'Kenjo Mikasa' filled cakes, monaka, yokan, senbei, and castella.
bio.philosophy: A confectioner rooted in Nara's classical imagery, naming and shaping sweets after the ancient capital's poetry and history.

anecdotes.text: The signature 'Aoniyoshi' is said to have been named when Prince Arisugawa visited Chugu-ji temple, and it is treated as a 'kenjo' confection presented to the imperial household.
A regional Nara magazine reported on the roughly 130-year-old confectioner's main-shop renewal, underscoring its standing as a Meiji-founded local institution.
To mark the 1300th anniversary of the Heijo-kyo capital, the shop created a five-colored confection, 'Goen Kyokusui no Utage,' reflecting its habit of designing sweets around Nara's heritage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop produces a wide range of wheat-based confections (castella, sponge-filled mikasa cakes, senbei, monaka wafers) in the same kitchen, so any naturally wheat-free item carries cross-contact risk; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg- and dairy-containing sweets such as castella and sponge-based mikasa are made alongside plant-based azuki, kuzu, and agar confections; no vegan-specific separation is documented.

positives.text: The signature kakigori is described as shaved ice with house-made red bean paste and wasanbon-sugar/molasses syrup, and the 'Aoniyoshi' is made from kudzu starch and sugar — these particular items are plant-based and wheat-free by tradition, though they should still be confirmed for cross-contact and ingredients on site.

lead: 東大寺二月堂南茶所 龍美堂
name_ja: 東大寺二月堂南茶所　龍美堂
cuisine: 寺門前の茶屋・甘味処（手作りわらび餅、自家製あんのおはぎ、行法味噌）
neighborhood: 東大寺二月堂南側（奈良公園内）
gf_detail: Signature items are naturally low-wheat in their base form — warabi mochi is made from bracken/warabi starch and dusted with kinako (soybean flour), and ohagi is glutinous rice wrapped in homemade red-bean paste. However, this is a traditional tea house with no documented gluten-free program, no allergen menu, and no celiac cross-contamination protocols. The kinako coating and shared utensils are concerns, and staff are unlikely to be equipped for strict celiac requirements. Treat as ask, not safe.
vegan_detail: The two signature sweets appear plant-based: warabi mochi (bracken starch, sugar, kinako) and ohagi (rice with adzuki-bean anko) typically contain no animal products. But the menu also includes parfaits and seasonal shaved ice with cream/dairy elements, and no item is labeled vegan. Ingredients are not documented in detail, so a vegan diner should confirm each item directly. Note kinako is soy.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市雑司町406-1
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 奈良県奈良市雑司町406; coordinates [135.838135, 34.686146] fall within the central Nara box and correspond to the Todaiji Nigatsudo area in Nara Park.
seats_text: 22 seats (official site); Tabelog lists 25.
hours_raw: 10:00～17:00（ラストオーダー 平日16:20／土日祝16:45）。※冬季変更あり。不定休。
closed_days: 不定休（irregular closures; winter hours vary）
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, traditional temple-side tea house where pointing at the warabi-mochi and ohagi display works fine, but there is no English menu or allergen support, so a foreign student with dietary needs should arrive with a guide or translated phrases.
caveats: Seat count differs by source (22 official vs 25 Tabelog). Hours are irregular with seasonal winter changes; confirm before visiting. No documented allergen, gluten-free, or vegan program — dietary suitability is inferred from typical recipes, not stated by the shop.

bio.background: 龍美堂 sits directly south of Todaiji's Nigatsudo Hall, famous for the Omizutori fire-and-water rites, and has long served as a resting spot for pilgrims. The shop pours tea boiled in an iron kettle said to be over 200 years old and offers a sweeping view over Nigatsudo and the Nara Basin. It also displays rare items connected to Nigatsudo and the Omizutori ceremony.
bio.origin: Nara — within Nara Park, beside Todaiji Nigatsudo.
bio.specialty: Handmade warabi mochi generously dusted with freshly-ground kinako (soybean flour), and large old-fashioned ohagi made with the shop's own homemade red-bean paste. The shop is also the licensed maker of 行法味噌 (Gyoho miso), sold as a souvenir; in summer it serves shaved ice with house anko.
bio.philosophy: The shop is the officially permitted maker of Gyoho miso, the secret-recipe miso said to have been used by the ascetic monks who shut themselves away during Nigatsudo's rites to build their spiritual strength — a practice the temple traces back over a thousand years to the priest Jitchu, disciple of Todaiji's founding abbot Roben.

anecdotes.text: 二月堂と奈良盆地を望む抜群のロケーションで、龍美堂自慢の甘味をご賞味下さい。
この行法は天平の昔、東大寺開山良辧僧正の高弟實忠和尚によって創められましてから今日まで千何百年の間一度も途絶えたことのないあらたかな秘法でございます。

positives.text: Signature warabi mochi is made from bracken starch and kinako rather than wheat flour, and ohagi is glutinous rice with red-bean paste — base recipes that typically contain no wheat and no animal products.

cuisine: Showa-retro independent kissaten (traditional Japanese coffee house) serving hand-roasted coffee, toast, and homestyle fare
neighborhood: Naramachi / Kintetsu-Nara Station area
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市元林院町34
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 奈良県奈良市元林院町34 to [135.829636, 34.681026], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Around 10 seats; a small, intimate counter-and-table space.
hours_raw: 08:30-21:00; closed Thursdays
closed_days: Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, owner-run old-school coffee house where the chatty proprietor speaks Japanese; basic Japanese or a translation app will make the visit far smoother and more enjoyable.
gf_detail: This is a traditional kissaten whose mainstays are coffee, toast, cake, and homestyle dishes — all wheat-heavy and prepared in a shared kitchen with no gluten-free accommodation documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan offering is documented. Black hand-roasted coffee is the safest plant-based option, but food items (toast, cake, pudding, homestyle cooking) typically contain dairy, eggs, or animal stock. Vegans would need to ask, and options will be very limited.
caveats: Cash only. The owner-cooked homestyle menu has shifted over time, so specific dishes may vary. Many locals come in the evening to drink alcohol with snacks; tourists more often come for coffee and cake.

bio.background: One of the oldest coffee houses in Nara — by the proprietor's own account effectively the oldest still standing, as the two that preceded it have closed. It sits on a narrow lane heading into the historic Naramachi quarter, in a quiet wood-toned space behind a white-and-black exterior.
bio.specialty: Hand-roasted coffee made by a roasting method handed down from the founder, alongside homestyle cooking and toast; in earlier years the shop was loved for its bittersweet homemade pudding and a notably spicy house curry.
bio.philosophy: The owner insists the place is a 'cafe' rather than a kissaten, partly because a cafe may serve alcohol — and indeed many regulars drop in to sip spirits cut with their own preferred ratio of water alongside small snacks.

anecdotes.text: The energetic proprietor greets guests together with the shop dog, Kuma, and her warm chatter is regarded as one of the real pleasures of a visit.
After her husband passed away, the menu drifted away from its old signature items toward the homestyle dishes she now cooks herself.
The coffee follows a roasting style preserved from the shop's first-generation owner.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A traditional coffee house centered on toast, cake, and homestyle dishes in a single shared kitchen; cross-contact with wheat is effectively unavoidable and no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: No vegan menu is documented; food items generally rely on dairy, eggs, or animal-based stock, so dedicated vegan preparation is not indicated.

positives.text: Plain black hand-roasted coffee is available and is the most reliably plant-based item on offer.

lead: 菓匠 千壽庵吉宗 奈良総本店
name_ja: 菓匠 千壽庵吉宗 奈良総本店
cuisine: 老舗和菓子（生わらび餅が名物、町家の本店、夏のわらび餅かき氷）
gf_detail: The house specialty is warabimochi, traditionally a wheat-free confection. The standard version blends sweet potato starch with domestic bracken (warabi) starch; the premium "pure" warabimochi (純本生わらび餅) uses 100% domestic bracken starch with only sugar and water. So the core sweet contains no wheat. However, it is dusted with kinako (roasted soybean flour) and the shop publishes no gluten-free or cross-contamination guarantees, and other wagashi sold here may contain wheat. Celiacs should ask directly before eating.
vegan_detail: The signature warabimochi is plant-based: bracken/sweet-potato starch, sugar, water, and kinako (soybean flour) topping, with no animal products in its core recipe. This is a confectionery shop, not a vegan-focused destination, and ingredient lists vary by item (some wagashi may use eggs or dairy). No formal vegan menu or cross-contact policy is documented, so vegans should confirm individual items.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市押上町39-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市押上町39-1 returned [135.834396, 34.688545], within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: 押上町（県庁東交差点北・東大寺戒壇院近く／奈良公園エリア）
seats_text: The shop is primarily a confectionery retail store; the attached 千壽茶寮 tea space offers limited seating for warabimochi and drinks on weekends/holidays. Exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 物販 10:00～18:00（元日のみ休業）。喫茶「千壽茶寮」は週末・祝日を中心に13:00～17:00頃、わらび餅とお飲みもの限定で営業（かき氷は本店では提供なし／なかまち店で季節提供）。
closed_days: 元日のみ休業（New Year's Day only）
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, walk-in-friendly old confectionery shop where you can point at the famous warabimochi and pay easily; a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way, but no special skills are needed to buy or enjoy a sweet here.

bio.background: Founded in 1936 (Showa 11) by Yamamoto Yoshio, who first learned the confectioner's craft through an uncle's candy business in the early Showa years. After the war the workshop made candies under the name Sanwa Seika, then evolved into traditional wagashi. The second generation, Yamamoto Kenji, grew the business, and since 1998 the third generation, Yamamoto Tetsuya, has led the shop, now in its ninth decade in Nara.
bio.specialty: Fresh warabimochi (生わらび餅) — a melt-in-the-mouth bracken-starch sweet dusted with kinako. The standard version blends domestic bracken starch with sweet-potato starch; the top-grade 純本生わらび餅 uses 100% domestic bracken starch with only sugar and water, hand-kneaded by veteran artisans. A historic candy called 古代瓦 (ancient roof tile) is named for the imagery of Todaiji and Nara's old architecture.
bio.philosophy: The shop aims to create sweets, spaces, and hospitality that make the heart quietly calm — what they call 'おだやかな幸せ' (calm happiness).

anecdotes.text: The shop's warabimochi was recognized with the Prime Minister's Award at the 21st National Confectionery Exposition, the result of extensive research into classical texts to recreate an additive-free, naturally soft sweet.

On weekends and holidays a small tea space, 千壽茶寮, opens in the afternoon serving the fresh warabimochi with a drink; in summer the warabimochi also appears in a fluffy Japanese-style shaved ice at the company's Nakamachi shop.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop is a general wagashi confectionery that also sells wheat-containing sweets; no gluten-free production line or cross-contamination policy is published, so shared utensils/surfaces are likely.

staff_allergy_handling.text: No published allergen or staff allergy-handling protocol; commentary on warabimochi allergens recommends contacting the maker directly to confirm ingredients.

positives.text: The premium warabimochi is made from only domestic bracken starch, sugar, and water — no additives — and the core warabimochi recipe contains no wheat, making it a relatively simple, plant-based sweet (note the kinako topping is soy).

caveats: Warabimochi is dusted with kinako (soybean flour), so it is not soy-free. While the warabimochi's core ingredients are wheat-free and plant-based, this is a confectionery shop with no published gluten-free or vegan certification or cross-contamination controls; other sweets sold here may contain wheat, egg, or dairy. The hours given are for retail; the sit-down tea service (千壽茶寮) only runs weekend/holiday afternoons and kakigori is served at the separate Nakamachi shop, not the main store. Confirm details before visiting as hours can vary.

name_ja: 純喫茶シンロク×オーシマドーナツ奈良店
name_en: Junkissa Shinroku x Oshima Donut Nara
cuisine: Showa-retro kissaten serving hand-made donuts, coffee and light cafe fare
neighborhood: Kitamachi / Hannya-ji area, north of Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: This is a donut-and-coffee kissaten. The signature items are hand-made fried donuts, which are wheat-based, and there is no indication of any gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation. Not suitable for celiac or strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The core offering is fried wheat donuts (typically made with egg, dairy and butter) and coffee. No vegan menu or plant-based options are documented, and the parfait/coffee drinks would normally use dairy. Not a vegan destination.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市般若寺町239-17
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 般若寺町239 (lat 34.69883, lng 135.836411), within the Nara bounding box. Located in Nara's Kitamachi district near Hannya-ji temple, north of Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: Around 12-13 seats: counter seats (4-5), one table for 4, and one to two tables for 2. Sources vary slightly (Tabelog lists 13 / 5 counter; the local feature lists 12 / 4 counter).
hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (L.O. 16:30), 年中無休 (open year-round, no regular closing day)
closed_days: None (open year-round, 年中無休)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, family-friendly neighborhood coffee shop where a friendly greeting and pointing at the donut display will get you by, though staff likely speak limited English.

bio.background: A small, calm retro coffee shop in Nara's Kitamachi district, just north of the central station area near Hannya-ji temple. It opened on November 24, 2025, in the space formerly occupied by 'Kissaten Mickey,' a cafe long loved by the local community, and was reborn as a collaboration between the Shinroku kissaten concept and Tokyo-based OSHIMA DONUTS. The interior keeps a nostalgic Showa-era calm while pairing it with a modern donut counter.
bio.origin: Nara (Kitamachi), built on the site of the former neighborhood cafe Kissaten Mickey
bio.specialty: Hand-made fluffy fried donuts (nine varieties, with the standard Oshima Donut a favorite) and hot coffee, plus a here-only original creation, the Shinjika ('sacred deer') Donut Parfait. Coffee is offered at a discount when bought as a donut set.
bio.philosophy: A place where 'nostalgic and new' are meant to live together in harmony, welcoming families, children and neighbors as a casual everyday gathering spot rather than a high-end establishment.

anecdotes.text: The shop took over the premises of the beloved old neighborhood cafe 'Kissaten Mickey,' reopening as 'Junkissa Shinroku x Oshima Donut Nara' on November 24, 2025, blending the old retro charm with hand-made donuts from a Tokyo donut maker.
An original menu item found nowhere else, the 'Shinjika Donut Parfait' (named for Nara's sacred deer), is served alongside the signature 290-yen fluffy fried donuts.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop fries wheat-based donuts on site; no gluten-free menu or dedicated equipment is documented, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

caveats: Very newly opened (November 24, 2025), so hours and offerings may still settle. Located in Kitamachi near Hannya-ji temple, roughly 1.5-2 km north of Kintetsu Nara Station rather than in the immediate station core, despite the Tabelog 近鉄奈良 area tag. Seat count and counter-seat figures differ slightly between sources (Tabelog 13 / 5 counter vs. local feature 12 / 4 counter). Coffee/donut prices noted: hot coffee 500 yen (400 as a set), donuts from 290 yen.

lead: 鶴屋徳満 奈良三条店
name_ja: 鶴屋徳満 奈良三条店
cuisine: 老舗和菓子店（三笠・上生菓子・かき氷・甘味）/ Traditional Japanese sweets (wagashi) shop
neighborhood: 三条通り周辺（近鉄奈良駅～JR奈良駅の間）/ Sanjo-dori area, central Nara
gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese-sweets maker. Its core products are wheat-based: the signature 三笠 (mikasa / dorayaki) is made with wheat flour, eggs and honey, and other baked confections similarly use flour. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no allergen labeling for celiacs, and no separate preparation. Not suitable for those requiring strict gluten-free.
vegan_detail: As a wagashi shop, some items are plant-based by tradition — e.g. red-bean-paste sweets and the kakigori topped with wasanbon syrup, kuromitsu (black sugar syrup) and kinako (soybean flour) over shaved ice and bean paste contain no animal products in their basic form. However, signature baked items like the 三笠/dorayaki and 上生菓子 contain eggs (and the dorayaki contains honey), and there is no vegan labeling or guarantee. A vegan should ask item-by-item; pure adzuki/kanten/kuromitsu/kinako sweets are the safer choices.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市角振新屋町4
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市角振新屋町4 returned [135.827881, 34.681591], inside the central Nara box.
seats_text: The Sanjo branch is primarily a takeout shop with only a small rest bench (about 2 seats) inside. The spacious, light-filled eat-in café with kakigori and amami sweets that is widely featured is actually the separate Main shop (本店) on the Mochiidono/Shimogakuencho arcade, not this Sanjo location.
hours_raw: 官公式サイト（三条店）: 10:00-18:00、定休日 月曜。食べログ表記: 09:00-18:00。/ Official site (Sanjo branch): 10:00-18:00, closed Mondays. Tabelog lists 09:00-18:00.
closed_days: 月曜（公式サイトによる三条店の定休日）/ Mondays per the official site. Note: Tabelog lists no fixed closed day with 09:00-18:00 hours, so confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, casual neighborhood sweets shop where you can point at what you want; staff are unlikely to speak much English, so a translation app or pointing is your best bet, but a confectionery purchase is low-pressure for a first-timer.
caveats: Address discrepancy resolved via official site: the target 三条店 is at 角振新屋町4 (a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station), distinct from the 本店 at 下御門町29. IMPORTANT: the "spacious eat-in with kuromitsu-kinako kakigori and amami sweets" described in the target brief corresponds to the Main shop (本店) café, NOT this Sanjo branch — the Sanjo branch is mainly takeout/retail with only a small bench. Hours and closed day differ between the official site (10:00-18:00, closed Mon) and Tabelog (09:00-18:00) — verify before visiting.

bio.background: Tsuruya Tokuman is one of Nara's long-established wagashi (traditional Japanese confectionery) houses, founded in 1898 (Meiji 31) after branching off from the older Tsuruya Hachiman lineage, with a confectionery tradition the house describes as spanning well over a century. The shop has a presence on the old Mochiidono / Sanjo arcade streets in the heart of Nara, between Kintetsu Nara and JR Nara stations.
bio.specialty: The signature product is the 三笠 (mikasa) — a generous dorayaki-style pancake about 13cm across, made with large premium adzuki beans and a moist, fluffy batter, with a celebrated 'kenjo mikasa' version. The house also makes 上生菓子 (seasonal fresh sweets) and, in summer, shaved ice (kakigori) — including a wasanbon-syrup 'an-gori' with house-made bean paste and a version dressed with kuromitsu (black sugar syrup) and kinako (roasted soybean flour).
bio.philosophy: The house emphasizes traditional methods and Nara's regional confectionery heritage, taking pride in long-standing craft; products such as the mikasa have reportedly been presented to the imperial household on multiple occasions.

anecdotes.text: The flagship mikasa is named in reference to the three-layered profile of Mt. Wakakusa, and is baked to stay fresh for about a week.

The Main shop's eat-in café was renovated during the pandemic while preserving vintage elements — repurposing old wooden storage boxes (banju) as counter decor and historic sliding doors as table tops, under a vaulted, window-lit ceiling, often watched over by an octogenarian proprietress.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-flour environment: baked goods such as dorayaki/mikasa are made with wheat flour on-site, so flour is present and cross-contact with any flour-free items cannot be ruled out.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Eggs and honey are used in the signature dorayaki/mikasa and the premises also handle bean-paste-only and kuromitsu/kinako sweets; shared utensils and surfaces mean cross-contact between egg-containing and plant-based items is plausible. No vegan separation is documented.

positives.text: Several items are inherently plant-based by tradition (adzuki bean paste, kanten, kuromitsu black-sugar syrup, kinako soybean flour over shaved ice), giving vegans some workable choices if they confirm ingredients per item.

cuisine: Traditional Nara teahouse and sweets shop (chagayu rice porridge, handmade mitarashi dango, warabi mochi, udon and noodle sets)
neighborhood: Nara Park / Todai-ji area, near Nigatsudo (Kintetsu Nara Station)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市雑司町407
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市雑司町407 returned [135.85025, 34.688213], within the Nara box and consistent with the Todai-ji/Nara Park location.
seats_text: Approximately 40 seats
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (Tabelog lists 11:00-14:30); during Omizutori (Mar 1-14) approx 12:00-18:00. Closes when sold out.
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual traditional teahouse popular with sightseers near Todai-ji; staff likely speak limited English, so a few basic Japanese phrases or pointing at the menu will help.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The core menu is wheat-based (udon, somen/nyumen, soba blends, mitarashi dango) and broths use bonito-and-kombu dashi; mitarashi sauce and tare are typically wheat-soy-sauce based. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature noodle and porridge sets use fish (bonito) dashi and include items like herring (nishin) and chicken donburi; sweets are plant-based but the kitchen is built around fish-stock broths. No vegan menu is documented.

bio.background: Azekuraya is a teahouse and sweets shop that traces its founding to the Edo period, tucked within Nara Park on the grounds near Todai-ji and Nigatsudo. Marked by a deer-motif noren curtain, it serves as a rest stop for visitors exploring the ancient capital.
bio.specialty: Yamato tea rice porridge (chagayu) sets, freshly grilled handmade mitarashi dango, homemade warabi mochi, and Rokusai (six-color) udon and noodle sets in a Kansai-style bonito-and-kombu broth.
bio.philosophy: The shop describes itself as offering seasonal handmade cooking served in simple vessels, a place to pause during sightseeing and take in the atmosphere of old Nara.

anecdotes.text: The shop is known for freshly grilled handmade mitarashi dango and self-made warabi mochi, with a chagayu (tea porridge) set presented as a gentle, traditional local Nara dish served with side dishes and pickles.
During the Omizutori festival (March 1-14) at Nigatsudo, the shop shifts to special afternoon-into-evening hours (approximately 12:00-18:00) rather than its usual daytime schedule.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is heavily wheat-based (udon, nyumen/somen, soba, dango) prepared in a shared kitchen, so cross-contact with gluten is effectively unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Mitarashi dango sauce and noodle tare are typically based on wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Noodle and porridge sets use bonito (fish) dashi broth, and the menu includes herring and chicken dishes, so plant-based sweets share a fish-stock kitchen.

caveats: Hours differ slightly between official site (11:00-15:00) and Tabelog (11:00-14:30); shop closes early when sold out and runs irregular closures. Seat count (40) and cash-only status from Tabelog. Founding is documented as "Edo period" but no precise year or family lineage was found on the pages fetched.

cuisine: とんかつ専門（大和ポーク・予約制の家族経営の定食店）
neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅／JR奈良駅）
gf_detail: This is a tonkatsu (breaded, deep-fried pork cutlet) specialist. The signature dish is coated in wheat-flour panko breadcrumbs and is not gluten-free. The accompanying vegetable soup and seasoned sides typically rely on standard dashi and seasonings, and the set rice is blended with barley (a gluten grain). There is no documented gluten-free menu or celiac-safe preparation, and no dedicated fryer. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A pork specialty restaurant built entirely around Yamato Pork cutlets, ginger-grilled pork, pork steak, and katsu curry. While the meal includes a notably generous amount of vegetables (a heaping plate of cabbage and a vegetable-rich soup), the soup contains imitation crab and the dishes are not prepared as vegan/vegetarian. No plant-based main is documented. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市勝南院町23
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.83017, 34.679035] for 奈良県奈良市勝南院町23; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 12 seats (two 2-person tables and two 4-person tables)
hours_raw: 11:15-14:30 (L.O. 13:30), 17:30-20:00 (L.O. 19:00). Closed Tuesdays, Fridays, the first Monday of the month, plus irregular closures. Reservation phone hours: 10:00-11:00, 15:00-17:00, 20:00-21:30.
closed_days: Tuesdays and Fridays, plus the first Monday of each month and occasional irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, completely reservation-only husband-and-wife shop where you should phone ahead in Japanese and arrive on time, so it is most comfortable for a student who can manage some basic Japanese or bring a Japanese-speaking friend.
caveats: Completely reservation-only (完全予約制); weekends often book out about a month ahead. Very small (12 seats). Irregular closures beyond the regular Tuesday/Friday days off, so confirm when reserving. The signature tonkatsu is served with seasoned salt rather than sauce.

bio.background: Chef Tsuyoshi Akeboshi trained at established Japanese restaurants and well-regarded hotels before opening this small shop in the historic Naramachi district in 2011. He runs it together with his wife as an intimate, reservation-only counter-and-tables eatery.
bio.specialty: Tonkatsu made from Yamato Pork (a premium Nara-prefecture brand pork prized for tender meat and sweet, oleic-acid-rich fat). The pork is coated in coarse panko for a light, crisp finish and fried gently in soybean oil at a low temperature (around 160C). It is served not with sauce but with Guerande sea salt from France blended with sweet tea, herbs, and the shop's own spices. Sets come with a heaping plate of cabbage, a vegetable-laden soup, a seasoned side, and rice blended with barley, Job's tears, and legumes; the meal opens with mekabu tea and closes with black oolong tea.
bio.philosophy: A health-minded, ingredient-first approach: the finest Yamato Pork cooked gently to highlight its natural sweetness, balanced by an abundance of vegetables and digestive teas before and after the meal.

anecdotes.text: The shop is so small and the cooking so deliberate that it operates entirely by reservation, with weekend seats often filling up about a month in advance.
Rather than the usual tonkatsu sauce, diners are given Guerande sea salt blended with sweet tea and spices to draw out the pork's flavor.
Even the cabbage and vegetable soup alone are filling, reflecting the deliberately health-conscious composition of the set meals.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The signature dish is wheat-panko-breaded deep-fried pork; the set rice is blended with barley, both gluten sources, so the meal is not gluten-free.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No allergen or soy-sauce ingredient information is documented; standard Japanese seasonings (typically wheat-containing soy sauce) should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a pork specialty restaurant; even the vegetable soup contains imitation crab and no vegan main is offered.

positives.text: The pork is fried in soybean oil at a low temperature, and meals come with a notably generous amount of vegetables and digestive teas.

cuisine: 古民家のオーガニック・ベジタリアン自然食（玄米菜食、無農薬野菜、ベジカレー、グルテン入り玄米カレー、オムライス）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. A signature dish is "gluten curry" made with seitan (wheat gluten, グルテン, made from flour to mimic chicken), and the menu includes other wheat items (scones, omurice, spaghetti, papad). Despite the word "gluten-free" appearing in some third-party listing snippets, the kitchen actively uses wheat gluten and is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Vegetarian establishment using no meat or fish. Many dishes are genuinely vegan (the bean/onion-based curries use no animal products, and dishes without egg, milk, or yogurt are available on request). However, it is not fully vegan: the spicy curry sauce can include yogurt (dairy) for sweetness/tang, and omurice/omelette dishes use eggs. Vegans should specifically ask for egg-, milk-, and yogurt-free preparations, which the kitchen accommodates.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市川之上突抜北方町18-3
geocode_note: GSI geocoder matched chō-level (川之上突抜町); coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: 京終／ならまち南端（川之上突抜町）
seats_text: 24 seats including tatami (zashiki) seating; all non-smoking, no private rooms.
hours_raw: 12:00-20:30 (Mon, Tue, Sat, Sun); some sources list 12:00-20:00. Closed Wed, Thu, Fri. May close early when dishes sell out.
closed_days: Wednesday, Thursday, Friday (sources vary slightly; one lists Wed-Thu closed). May also close early when sold out.
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu (and even a braille menu) is available and guide dogs are welcome, so a foreign student can order comfortably and ask about egg-, dairy-, or wheat-free options.
caveats: "Gluten-free" labels seen in some aggregator listings are misleading — the house specialty is a wheat-seitan ("gluten") curry, so the place is decidedly not for celiacs. Vegan diners must ask for egg/dairy/yogurt-free versions. Open hours and closed days vary between sources, and the cafe may shut early once dishes sell out; reservations are recommended for groups of 5-6 or more.

bio.background: あしゅーら occupies a roughly 100-year-old machiya (traditional townhouse) tucked into the quiet southern edge of Naramachi, near Kyobate. The old wooden interior is dressed with Indian and Asian crafts and a faintly temple-like, oriental atmosphere, giving the room the feel of a hideaway rather than a busy cafe.
bio.specialty: The signature plate is a brown-rice (genmai) vegetable curry. The Calcutta-style sauce gets its depth from pesticide-free onions caramelized slowly and beans blended smooth, so it tastes rich without any meat or fish. A popular variation adds 'gluten' (seitan made from wheat flour) that is crisped up to mimic chicken. Other regulars include tofu croquettes, omurice, soup spaghetti, papad, plus a tea menu with chai and fresh carrot juice and house-baked scones.
bio.philosophy: A meat- and fish-free kitchen built on organic, pesticide-free vegetables and brown rice. The mood is deliberately unhurried — guests summon the easygoing owner by ringing a bell or tapping a wooden fish block, and are even told they are welcome to doze off after eating.

anecdotes.text: Guests call the relaxed proprietor not by raising a hand but by ringing a small bell or striking a wooden fish block (mokugyo), and the cafe openly invites diners to take a nap after their meal.
The curry's signature 'gluten' is made from wheat flour and fried until crisp so that it tastes and bites like chicken despite being entirely plant-based.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat gluten (seitan) is a core menu ingredient prepared in the same kitchen, so cross-contact with gluten is unavoidable; the venue is not suitable for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Yogurt (dairy) is used in the spicy curry sauce and eggs are used in omurice/omelette dishes, so vegans must request egg-, milk-, and yogurt-free preparations.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff are described as friendly and happy to explain the food, and dishes made without egg, milk, or yogurt are available on request.

positives.text: Fully meat- and fish-free kitchen using organic, pesticide-free vegetables and brown rice, with many naturally vegan curry options.
English-language menu and barrier-free accommodations (braille menu, guide dogs welcome) make ordering and asking about ingredients easier for foreign visitors.

name_ja: cafe Oasis（カフェ オアシス）
cuisine: Wood-fired oven café — Italian/Western-influenced pizza, pasta, meat dishes and curry
neighborhood: ならまち / Konishi-cho, near Kintetsu Nara Station (Konishi Sakura-dori shopping arcade)
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The menu is built around wheat-based dishes — wood-fired pizza and pasta dominate — so this is not a celiac-safe destination and there is no indication of dedicated equipment or allergen handling.
vegan_detail: No vegan options are documented. Signature dishes center on beef, chicken, sausage, sea urchin (uni) cream, cheese pizzas and dairy, with no stated plant-based menu. A vegan traveler should not rely on this place without contacting the cafe directly.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市小西町1-8 axe unit 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from 奈良県奈良市小西町1-8 via GSI; first feature coordinates [135.827957, 34.68293], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 50 seats (can accommodate up to 80 for private events)
hours_raw: Wednesday-Sunday and holidays 11:30-21:00 (last food order 20:30); closed Monday and Tuesday. Modeled here as 11:30-20:30 service window per the kitchen last-order time.
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday (open on some holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, spacious wood-and-greenery café where a foreign student can comfortably order pizza or pasta with a few words of Japanese and a pointed menu, though staff English may be limited.
caveats: Hours sources conflict slightly: one local guide listed 11:00-23:30, but the official site and Tabelog agree on Wed-Sun 11:30-21:00 (last order 20:30), closed Mon/Tue — the latter is used. No vegan or gluten-free accommodations are documented anywhere; confirm directly if needed.

bio.background: A café tucked into the Konishi Sakura-dori shopping arcade just a couple of minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station. Despite sitting in the middle of town, the interior leans into a warm, resort-like feel with abundant wood and greenery, built around a wood-fired oven that sits in open view of diners.
bio.specialty: Wood-fired cooking: stone-oven pizzas and a range of pasta, plus heartier plates like a wood-roasted Joshu beef course with grilled vegetables, homemade-sausage pasta in an almond-basil-tomato-uni sauce, and zucchini pizza with spiced chicken and basil sauce. Lunch sets come with salad and dessert options.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen showcases live wood-fired cooking behind glass, aiming for a relaxed, welcoming spot that works from lunch through an evening drink.

anecdotes.text: The plates come from a chef trained at a popular Western (yoshoku/Italian-style) restaurant, which the cafe credits for its hearty, full-flavored cooking.
The wood-fired oven is the centerpiece of the room, with cooking visible through glass for a 'live' dining feel.

cuisine: Machiya café in a renovated century-old townhouse guesthouse, serving a homestyle Nara tea-rice set (chameshi-zen) and local sweets/coffee
gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. The café centers on rice-based dishes (hojicha-cooked rice, miso soup, seasonal small plates, Nara pickles), and a homestyle rice meal can be relatively low in obvious wheat, but miso, Nara pickles (often soy/koji-based), and any soy seasoning may contain wheat, and there is no stated celiac/cross-contamination protocol. Galettes and breads on the menu contain gluten. A celiac traveler must ask directly (info@nishimuratei.com / SNS) before relying on it.
vegan_detail: Vegetarian and vegan diners can be accommodated for the signature tea-rice set (chameshi-zen): hojicha-cooked rice, miso soup, seasonal vegetable small dishes, and Nara pickles, served without the optional egg ('okan no tamagoyaki' is a paid add-on). Accommodation is documented in travel coverage; because dashi and miso choices are not specified online, a strict vegan should confirm the broth and seasonings (no fish dashi/bonito) when ordering or reserving.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市花園町20
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 奈良県奈良市花園町20 to lng 135.829742, lat 34.674976 (within the central Nara box). Note: the property is at the southern end of Naramachi in Hanazono-chō (a few minutes from JR Kyōbate/Kyōsui station), not Kōnōdō-chō as listed in the target metadata; Tabelog and the official site both confirm 花園町20.
neighborhood: Naramachi (south end), Hanazono-chō — roughly 15 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, ~9 min from JR Kyōbate Station
seats_text: Small café area with seating for about 5 guests inside the machiya; also operates as a guesthouse (whole-house rental) and rentable tea room.
hours_raw: Café 11:00–17:00; closed Thursdays. (Lodging check-in 14:00–20:00, check-out by 10:00.) Hours may vary — check SNS.
closed_days: Thursdays (café); hours and openings can vary, so checking their SNS is advised
cultural_comfort_note: This is a quiet, intimate family-run machiya with just a few seats, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) helps; the owner is welcoming to travelers, but it has a homey "grandmother's house" feel rather than a tourist-restaurant setup.
caveats: Primarily a guesthouse with a small attached café open to walk-ins during café hours; only about 5 café seats and limited days, so it can fill up or close on short notice. The signature dish is a single homestyle rice set rather than a broad menu. Address is Hanazono-chō 20, slightly south of where the target metadata listed it.

bio.background: Nishimura-tei occupies a Nara machiya (traditional wooden townhouse) more than 100 years old that opened to the public in autumn 2019. The owner, Sugimoto Yuta, returned to his hometown after working in graphic design and food service, and renovated the family house — keeping original stairs, beams, and the gardens — into a guesthouse with a small café and rental space at the quiet southern edge of Naramachi.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is the 'everyday tea-rice set' (毎日の茶飯膳): rice cooked with local Nara hojicha roasted tea, miso soup, seasonal vegetable small dishes, and Nara pickles, with an optional homemade rolled egg ('okan no tamagoyaki') as an add-on. The café also serves coffee, tea, and sweets sourced from local Nara shops, plus seasonal items like a winter hibachi (charcoal-brazier) service with toasted handmade bread and mochi.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims for food you could eat every day without tiring of it — gentle on the body and quietly nostalgic, evoking the comfort of a grandmother's home. The whole place is built around an 'obāchan-chi' (grandmother's house) feeling of unhurried rest.

anecdotes.text: The building was the owner's own family townhouse, restored rather than rebuilt, so guests sit among the original stairs, beams, and two small gardens.
A 'book-trade coffee' idea lets visitors swap a book recommendation for a coffee, reflecting the relaxed, conversational spirit of the house.
Sitting between JR Nara and Kintetsu Nara stations at the south end of Naramachi, it's pitched as a calm rest stop for people walking the old town.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Travel coverage states that vegetarian and vegan guests can be accommodated for the tea-rice set, indicating the kitchen is willing to adjust, but no formal allergy/cross-contact protocol is published.

positives.text: The core meal is a simple, rice-and-vegetable homestyle set with miso soup and Nara pickles, and the egg is an optional add-on, making it easy to keep the plate plant-based on request.

lead: ロッカン ルーム（ROKKAN ROOM）
name_ja: ロッカン ルーム（ROKKAN ROOM）
cuisine: 喫茶・カフェバー（古民家カフェ）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature nyūmen is made with Miwa sōmen (wheat noodles), and the menu features curries and a custard pudding. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or celiac accommodations are documented; broths almost certainly contain wheat-based seasonings and the place is not set up for cross-contamination control.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. Some dishes appear plant-leaning (spiced fried-tofu nyūmen, chai-infused red-bean zenzai), but nyūmen broth is typically made with fish dashi and curries are not confirmed animal-free; the pudding uses Nara eggs. A flexible vegetarian might find an item by asking, but vegans should not assume any dish is safe without confirming dashi and curry ingredients.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市元林院町8
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市元林院町8, within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: ならまち（元林院町・近鉄奈良駅周辺）
seats_text: Seat count not published; intimate multi-room café spread across an old machiya including a tatami-floored former air-raid-shelter basement and a second-floor gallery room.
hours_raw: Tue-Sat 11:00-18:00; Sun 11:00-17:00; closed Mondays
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, art-filled café where a warm greeting in Japanese goes a long way; staff may not speak much English, so a translation app helps for asking about ingredients.
caveats: Seat count is not published and English-language menus/allergy info were not confirmed. Vegan and gluten-free travelers should verify dashi, curry, and broth ingredients directly before ordering.

bio.background: ROKKAN ROOM occupies a townhouse more than 140 years old in Motobayashiin-cho, once the heart of Nara's old flower district just south of Sarusawa Pond. The building was formerly an okiya (a residence for geisha), and owner Moe Oyagi actually lived in it before reimagining it as a café. She kept the layers of its past intact: the original earthen-floored entry, a storehouse (kura), repurposed lacquer tables from the okiya, and a wartime air-raid shelter in the basement now softened with tatami and cushions.
bio.specialty: Nyūmen made with Nara's local Miwa sōmen, served with a spiced fried-tofu pouch that releases curry when broken open; a chai-infused zenzai (red-bean soup) layering cinnamon, ginger and clove over strained sweet beans; and a custard pudding made with Nara-prefecture eggs.
bio.philosophy: Cooking around the themes of fermentation and spice using local Nara ingredients, herbs and medicinal touches, while preserving and celebrating the building's many historical layers through a deliberately eclectic old-meets-new design.

anecdotes.text: The basement, a former air-raid shelter, has low ceilings and bare concrete walls but is laid with tatami and plush cushions, with speakers playing hypersonic sound to help guests relax in a space that feels cut off from the modern world.
A second-floor gallery room displays works by knit artist Chihiro Hasunuma and features a round table that converts to a kotatsu in winter.
The chai-tea zenzai was born from one of the owner's inspirations: the aroma reads as chai, the first sip tastes like zenzai, and the finish returns to chai.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature nyūmen uses Miwa sōmen (wheat noodles) and the menu includes curries, so wheat is central to the kitchen; no gluten-free preparation or separation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Nyūmen broth is typically fish-based and the custard pudding uses eggs; no vegan menu or animal-free assurances were found.

cuisine: 自家焙煎コーヒースタンド（個人経営）
gf_detail: A specialty coffee stand, not a gluten-free destination. The only food offered is the owner's wife's homemade baked goods (canelé, biscotti), which are wheat-based. No gluten-free items or celiac handling are documented. Coffee itself is naturally gluten-free, but there is no dedicated GF preparation.
vegan_detail: This is primarily a coffee stand. Black coffee is plant-based, but milk-based drinks use dairy and no plant-milk option is documented. The accompanying baked goods (canelé, biscotti) typically contain eggs and dairy. No vegan menu or labeling exists; a vegan visitor would need to stick to black coffee and ask about any food.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市椿井町5
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 奈良県奈良市椿井町5; result [135.827026, 34.680511] falls within the central Nara bounding box.
neighborhood: ならまち / 椿井市場（近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約5分）
seats_text: Small counter-style coffee stand inside the retro Tsubai Market arcade; exact seat count not documented (very limited counter seating).
hours_raw: 食べログ: 平日 12:00～18:00 / 祝日 12:00～17:00。定休: 水曜・第1第3日曜ほか不定休。（別ソースのNarakkoは9:30～19:00、日祝～18:00と記載があり営業時間は変動あり）
closed_days: 水曜、第1・第3日曜、ほか不定休
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, friendly owner-run coffee stand in a retro market arcade; a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way, though ordering coffee is simple and approachable for a foreign student.

bio.background: TABI Coffee Roaster opened on July 18, 2017, tucked inside the retro Tsubai Market (椿井市場) arcade a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner spent roughly 11-13 years training as a roaster at the well-known Nara coffee house Kuroko before striking out on his own, and he runs the stand together with his wife, who hand-bakes the pastries.
bio.specialty: House-roasted single-origin and blend coffees, including the signature TABI Blend and a deeper-roast version, plus around eight rotating single origins. The shop is small-batch and hands-on: the owner hand-picks world-class green beans to remove defects, then roasts in small lots from medium to dark, checking temperature minute by minute. His wife's homemade canelé and biscotti accompany the coffee.
bio.philosophy: A focus on careful, small-batch hand-roasting using fully hand-picked premium beans, aiming to make fresh, approachable coffee that appeals to a wide range of ages.

anecdotes.text: The owner hand-picks each batch of world-class green beans to remove poor beans before roasting in small lots, measuring and adjusting the temperature every minute across medium to deep roasts.

The shop is hidden inside a retro market arcade, standing out for its simple glass-sliding-door aesthetic among the surrounding old vendors; the owner's wife bakes the canelé and biscotti served alongside the coffee.

positives.text: Black drip and single-origin coffees are the core offering and are naturally free of gluten and animal products.

caveats: Business hours differ between sources (Tabelog lists 12:00-18:00 weekdays / 12:00-17:00 holidays; a local guide lists 9:30-19:00), so hours are variable and best confirmed before visiting; closings are partly irregular. No seat count or any dietary/allergen handling information is documented.

cuisine: スープカレー・カフェ（薬膳スパイススープカレー）
neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅周辺）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南市町8-1 古古古屋 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良市南市町8-1 returned [135.830109, 34.679897], within the central Nara box and consistent with the Naramachi / Sarusawa Pond area described on the official site.
seats_text: 13 seats total: 5 counter seats and 8 table seats (two 2-tops and one 4-top).
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Wed, Fri, Sat, Sun 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30), 17:30-20:00 (L.O. 19:30); closed Thursday. May close early when sold out; occasional irregular closures announced on Instagram.
closed_days: Thursday (plus occasional irregular closures; may sell out early)
cultural_comfort_note: An English menu is available and the shop is used to welcoming foreign visitors, so a foreign student can order comfortably; payment is cash or PayPay (no credit cards or other e-money).
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a spice soup curry shop; curries are typically thickened/seasoned with mixed spices and may contain wheat-based ingredients, and there is no stated allergen control. A celiac traveler would need to ask directly and should not assume safety.
vegan_detail: The core menu is meat-based soup curry (chicken, braised pork) and honey is used throughout, so the shop is not vegan-oriented. Tabelog lists that some vegetarian-friendly handling is possible, but no dedicated vegan dish is documented and broths are meat/herb based. A vegan or vegetarian should confirm options in advance.

bio.background: A small owner-run shop in a renovated old townhouse (古古古屋) in Nara's historic Naramachi district, just south of Sarusawa Pond and about a 10-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The kitchen is led by a certified yakuzen (medicinal-herb) coordinator.
bio.origin: Set in the lanes of Naramachi, the shop draws a wide mix of locals, domestic tourists, and international visitors who linger over curry in the folk-house cafe setting.
bio.specialty: Yakuzen (medicinal-herb) spice soup curry served sizzling in a hot stone bowl, built from around 20 spices and seasonal medicinal vegetables with adjustable heat. Signatures include the tender chicken soup curry and the medicinal braised-pork soup curry. The shop also sells domestic pure honey from Itoh Yu Beekeeping and serves lassi, masala chai and honey drinks.
bio.philosophy: The guiding idea is 食べて元気になる — food that restores your energy — applying traditional medicinal-herb principles to a modern soup curry.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen is run by a yakuzen (medicinal-herb) coordinator who composes the curries from roughly 20 spices and seasonal medicinal vegetables, served in a hot stone bowl with customizable spice levels.
Beyond curry the shop sells domestic pure honey and pairs dishes with honey-based drinks, lassi and masala chai, reflecting its 'spice curry and honey' identity.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Honey is used in the shop's dishes (so much so that items are flagged as unsuitable for infants under 1 year), and the main curries are meat-based, so vegan/vegetarian cross-contact is likely.

staff_allergy_handling.text: A multilingual (English) menu is available, but no specific allergen-handling or cross-contamination policy is documented.

positives.text: An English menu is provided and the shop regularly serves international visitors, making it easier to ask about ingredients.

caveats: Name romanization: the shop styles itself "8nosu" and the official site uses ハチノス (Hachinosu), though the target listing supplied エイトノス (Eitonosu); both readings appear, so I used Hachinosu. Closed day confirmed as Thursday on both JP and EN Tabelog (one secondary source incorrectly said Friday). Hours may end early on sold-out days and there are occasional irregular closures, hence hours_status=irregular. Vegetarian/vegan and gluten-free handling are not documented in detail and should be confirmed directly.

lead: わんずはーと・かふぇ ならまち店
name_ja: わんずはーと・かふぇ ならまち店
