cuisine: 和カフェ・甘味（地元食材を使った創作和食とスイーツ）
neighborhood: 高畑町（奈良ホテル近く、ならまち南側）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1028-7
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市高畑町1028-7 returned [135.836197, 34.677956], within the Nara box (lat 34.64-34.74, lng 135.76-135.90).
seats_text: 26 seats per Tabelog (22 table seats plus 4 pet-friendly outdoor seats on clear days); a tourism listing cites 10 seats, so the room is small either way.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat & holidays 9:00-17:00 (food L.O. 16:00, drink L.O. 16:30); Sun 9:00-19:00/20:00 (food L.O. 18:00/19:00). Closed Wednesdays. Evening dining available by phone consultation.
closed_days: 水曜日 (Wednesdays)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locally-run Japanese cafe on a quiet backstreet where staff likely speak limited English, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will make ordering smoother.
vegan_detail: This is a homestyle Japanese cafe whose signature plates feature fried chicken, fried shrimp, fish, miso soup and egg-based items. No vegan menu is documented and dishes rely on standard Japanese seasonings and likely fish dashi, so it is not a vegan destination.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Signature items include fried shrimp and chicken (wheat batter) and a kudzu-udon pasta dish; an official allergen note for the lunch lists shrimp, wheat and eggs. Soy sauce and miso here are standard wheat-containing types, so it is not safe for celiacs.
caveats: Hours and seat count differ between sources: Tabelog lists 9:00-17:00 (Sun to 19:00/20:00) with 26 seats, while a Nara tourism listing cites an 11:30-14:30 lunch service with 10 seats. The previously listed official website (onesheartcafe-naramachi.com) now redirects to an unrelated foreign site and appears defunct; confirm hours by phone (0742-81-9850). Listed as currently operating.

bio.background: A small, independently run Japanese cafe tucked into a quiet lane near the Nara Hotel on the southern edge of the old Naramachi district. It pairs casual creative Japanese cooking with house-made sweets in a relaxed, homey setting, and welcomes guests with pet-friendly outdoor seating on fine days.
bio.specialty: The standout is the 'Wanzu Heart no Gohan' plate (around 1,400 yen): a main dish with several small seasonal sides, miso soup and pickles built around Nara-grown vegetables and locally produced Hinohikari rice. A playful 'grown-up's children's lunch' adds fried chicken and fried shrimp with house-made tartar sauce, and a distinctive Yoshino kudzu 'udon pasta' offers a slippery, chewy noodle in a crab-tomato cream sauce. Menus rotate roughly every two weeks with the seasons.
bio.philosophy: A focus on local Nara ingredients and seasonal home-style cooking, refreshed frequently so regulars find something new.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans on Nara's own produce and Hinohikari rice, and turns Yoshino arrowroot (kuzu) into a chewy noodle served as a crab-tomato cream 'udon pasta,' giving the menu a distinctly local Nara character.

positives.text: An official Nara tourism listing publishes an allergen note for the signature lunch, indicating the kitchen is aware of allergens (it lists shrimp, wheat and eggs).

lead: カフェ エトランジェ ナラッド
name_ja: カフェ エトランジェ・ナラッド
cuisine: Café / Italian / bar — Neapolitan pizza, fresh pasta, wine and local Nara sake, with dishes built around Yamato vegetables
neighborhood: Naramachi area, near Kintetsu Nara Station (inside the Nara City Tourism Center "NARANICLE" on Sanjo-dori)
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on Neapolitan pizza and fresh pasta (wheat), so this is not a celiac-safe destination. Anyone needing gluten-free would have to ask staff directly about what, if anything, can be accommodated.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant. It does cook with Yamato (Nara) vegetables and offers a signature wooden lunch box featuring a homemade tofu hamburger with ancient rice and plenty of local vegetables, so some plant-forward items exist — but most of the menu is omnivore (pizza, pasta, meat) and there is no documented vegan certification or guarantee that even the veg dishes are free of dairy, egg or fish-based stock. A strict vegan should confirm specific dishes with staff.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市上三条町23-4 奈良市観光センター「ナラニクル」内
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良市上三条町23-4 returned [135.825439, 34.682251], within the central Nara bounding box.
seats_text: Approximately 145 seats total (about 115 indoor table seats plus a 30-seat terrace); private spaces for up to 30, and full private booking up to about 80.
hours_raw: Mon-Thu & Sun: 11:00-22:00 (Lunch 11:00-15:00, Dinner 17:00-22:00 L.O. 21:30). Fri, Sat & day before holidays: 11:00-23:00 (Lunch 11:00-15:00, Dinner 17:00-23:00 L.O. 22:00). Open every day.
closed_days: None — open every day.
cultural_comfort_note: Set inside the city tourism center on the main Sanjo-dori shopping street, this is a large, casual, walk-in-friendly café used to tourists, so a foreign student can easily browse, point at the menu, and feel welcome.
caveats: Dietary accommodations (vegan, gluten-free, allergies) are not documented anywhere and should be confirmed directly with staff before relying on them. Coordinates are address-level (the building), accurate to the tourism center.

bio.background: Cafe Etranger Narad sits inside the Nara City Tourism Center, NARANICLE, right on the Sanjo-dori approach between the station and the old town. It was conceived as a place where travelers and local residents cross paths over food, set in a bright, high-ceilinged space with exposed concrete, a lively open kitchen and a terrace.
bio.specialty: A European menu with a Japanese sensibility built on Yamato vegetables and other Nara produce. The signature is the Etranger Box, several small dishes arranged in a wooden box — including a homemade tofu hamburger with ancient rice and Nara vegetables — alongside Neapolitan pizza, fresh salads, wine and local Nara sake. The soft-serve, served with a little deer-shaped cookie in vanilla, Yamato-tea, or mixed, is a popular takeout treat.
bio.philosophy: The cafe presents itself as a gathering place — a hub that doubles as a tourist information point and a small marketplace for local vegetables and souvenirs, weaving Nara's food culture into a relaxed all-day cafe-restaurant.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen leans on Nara's distinctive Yamato vegetables and serves them in a wooden lunch box alongside a homemade tofu hamburger made with ancient rice.
A signature treat is the Narad soft-serve topped with a deer-shaped cookie, offered in vanilla, Yamato green tea, and a mix.

positives.text: The kitchen regularly cooks with Yamato (Nara) vegetables and offers a tofu-and-ancient-rice option in its signature box, so some plant-forward choices exist, though they are not certified vegan.

cuisine: Patisserie and café — fluffy cakes, baked sweets, and coffee
neighborhood: Takahata-cho (near Nara Park / Kasuga Taisha, Kintetsu Nara Station area)
gf_detail: This is a cake and baked-goods shop. The signature "air cake," rolls, tarts, sponge cakes, cookies, and sablés are all built on wheat flour, so the menu is fundamentally not gluten-free. No gluten-free items or dedicated GF preparation are documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The shop's cakes are made with fresh eggs and milk (the owner specifically highlights using fresh eggs and milk for the delicate texture), plus dairy mousse and butter-based pastries. No vegan/plant-based items are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町738-2 ふれあい会館1F
geocode_note: GSI address search on 奈良県奈良市高畑町738-2 returned [135.840637, 34.677372], within the Nara box.
seats_text: Around 25 indoor café seats plus an outdoor terrace.
hours_raw: Shop sales 9:00–18:00; café seating 10:00–17:30 (last order 17:00). Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays (open if these fall on a national holiday).
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday (open if a national holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed neighborhood cake café where pointing at the display case works fine, though staff are unlikely to speak much English, so a few basic Japanese phrases help.
caveats: Cash only. Tucked away in quiet Takahata-cho, a roughly 20-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, away from the main tourist streets. Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays.

bio.background: Owner-chef Yoshiro Wakita trained as a chief patissier before opening his own shop in Takahata-cho, a quiet residential pocket at the foot of Kasuga Mountain where cake shops were scarce. The location took several years to ready, partly because deer wandering over from nearby Nara Park kept eating the greenery during the build.
bio.specialty: The signature 'air cake' — a coromboid, ultra-light dome of airy sponge wrapping a soft milk mousse, made in flavors such as plain, matcha, orange, and blueberry cheese. Other favorites include a Kotoka local-strawberry tart built on a cookie-style crust and seasonal fruit cakes.
bio.philosophy: Wakita aims for 'lightness' in everything, and wants the shop to be an 'air-like presence' that quietly stays close to people's hearts, using carefully chosen local Nara ingredients, fresh eggs, and milk.

anecdotes.text: Wakita says the air cake grew out of his love of dorayaki/mikasa (red-bean pancakes), which he reimagined in cake form: 'I took the dorayaki I loved and arranged it my own way into something cake-like,' aiming for a sweet as light as air, as if it could float away.
He developed the cake by building up an image step by step — starting from the name, then the fluffy texture and rounded shape — drawing on the mikasa pancake combined with mousse.
Readying the site took several years because deer from nearby Nara Park kept coming over and eating the foliage during construction.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-flour sponge, tart, and cookie items are made throughout the kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cakes are made with fresh eggs, milk, and dairy mousse, so cross-contact with animal products is inherent to the menu.

cuisine: Sukiyaki kappo featuring Yamato beef (modern reconstructed sukiyaki)
neighborhood: Naramachi (Motorin'in-cho, inside the Shika-Saru-Kitsune Building)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市元林院町22 鹿猿狐ビルヂング 1F
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市元林院町22; within Nara box.
seats_text: 26 seats, including one private room and two semi-private rooms
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-15:00, Dinner 17:00-21:00; closed Wednesdays (also 12/31, 1/1)
closed_days: Wednesdays (also closed Dec 31 and Jan 1)
cultural_comfort_note: English-language reservations are supported and the polished setting in a designed Naramachi complex makes it approachable, though it is a refined sit-down restaurant where booking ahead is expected.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly by default. Sukiyaki uses warishita (a soy-sauce-based broth), and Japanese soy sauce normally contains wheat; the menu also includes udon, which is wheat. No gluten-free accommodations or dedicated handling are documented, so a celiac diner would need to ask directly and should not assume safety.
vegan_detail: This is a beef sukiyaki specialist; the entire concept centers on Yamato beef, and courses use fish-based dashi and soy-based seasonings. There is no documented vegan menu or vegan accommodation.
caveats: Hours and seasonal menus can change without notice; courses are reservation-based. Official site lists course prices around 12,600-14,500 yen for dinner, so this is a higher-end destination rather than a casual stop.

bio.background: Kitsune sits on the ground floor of the Shika-Saru-Kitsune Building, a designed complex in the traditional streetscape of Naramachi's Motorin'in-cho. The building's name nods to its three tenants -- a deer (Nakagawa Masashichi Shoten), a monkey (a coffee shop), and a fox (kitsune), this restaurant. It is run under the direction of Shusaku Toriba, the chef behind the acclaimed Tokyo restaurant 'sio,' who set out to reimagine sukiyaki here.
bio.specialty: Sukiyaki built around Yamato beef, prized for its balance of lean meat and marbling. Signature offerings include sukiyaki courses, a sukiyaki donburi and uni-topped sukiyaki donburi, a chirashi-style lunch with Yamato beef over vinegared rice layered with uni and other toppings, and house desserts like a mitarashi pudding.
bio.philosophy: The team describes the cooking as a 'reconstruction' of sukiyaki -- the playful name expresses the wish to magically transform sukiyaki into something even more delicious. The attention to detail extends beyond the plate to the cutlery (sourced via Nakagawa Masashichi Shoten) and the interior design.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name and the building it lives in form a deliberate trio -- deer, monkey and fox -- tying the place to the surrounding Nara culture and its neighboring shops.
The team explains they chose the name Kitsune (fox) because they wanted to 'magically transform' sukiyaki into something even more delicious.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Sukiyaki is cooked with a soy-sauce-based warishita broth and the menu includes wheat-based udon, so gluten is integral to the core dishes.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sukiyaki here uses a soy-sauce-based seasoning; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built entirely around beef and uses fish-based dashi, so there is no vegan-safe preparation documented.

cuisine: Hand-made udon and cafe
neighborhood: Naramachi (Fukuchiin-cho), south of Nara Park; walkable from Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: A hand-made udon shop, and udon is a wheat-flour noodle, so the core dishes are not gluten-free. Broths and sides also rely on standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, etc.). No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. Menus center on udon in fish/meat-based broths (tori tempura, chicken-and-egg bukkake, offal miso udon, beef stew udon), so most dishes contain fish dashi, meat, or egg. A plain udon like kake or zaru udon might be adaptable, but the broth and dipping sauces typically use fish dashi, so vegans would need to ask directly. No documented vegan options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市福智院町1-1
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from the confirmed address 奈良県奈良市福智院町1-1; result (34.677, 135.834) is within the Nara box. The restaurant is in the Naramachi / Fukuchiin area south of Nara Park, not きたまち (north side) as stated in the target metadata.
seats_text: 20 seats: two 4-person tables, two 2-person tables, and 8 counter seats (one source lists 19 table seats).
closed_days: Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: A cozy, family-run shop where you take off your shoes to sit in a converted old house; little to no English is documented, so a few basic Japanese phrases (or pointing at the photo menu) help, but the atmosphere is welcoming and casual.
caveats: Target metadata placed this in きたまち (north of Nara Park); sources confirm the Sanuki training and ~100-year-old old-house setting, but the actual address (福智院町 / Fukuchiin-cho) is in Naramachi south of Nara Park. Hours include both lunch and dinner (one Tabelog fetch showed lunch only, but HotPepper, nara-gourmet, and hug-nara confirm an 18:00–20:00 dinner service). Seat count is reported as 19 (one source) or 20 incl. counter (another).

bio.background: Menkui is a small, family-run udon shop set inside a roughly 100-year-old townhouse renovated into a relaxed, home-like dining space where guests remove their shoes before sitting down. The owner trained in udon-making in Sanuki (Kagawa Prefecture), Japan's most famous udon region, and brought that craft to a quiet lane in the Naramachi area near Fukuchiin Temple, just south of Nara Park.
bio.origin: Sanuki (Kagawa Prefecture) udon tradition, brought to Naramachi, Nara.
bio.philosophy: A seasonal approach to the noodle itself: the flour blend is changed between summer and winter so the udon suits the season, with summer noodles made a touch softer for a smoother throat feel.
bio.specialty: Hand-made udon with a chewy, mochi-like texture and a smooth finish. Signature dishes include chicken-and-egg bukkake udon (tori oyako bukkake) with crispy juicy chicken tempura and a soft-boiled egg, the zaru udon and tori-ten set, offal-miso udon, and a beef-stew udon. A popular ~1,000-yen set pairs a monthly-changing udon with a side, ancient-grains rice (kodaimai), pickles, and dessert.

anecdotes.text: There is a glass-walled work area where you can watch the udon being made, and diners describe hearing a steady pounding sound from the noodle-kneading while they eat.
The interior is stocked with books and magazines so it doubles as a calm cafe space, and it is child-friendly with high chairs available.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hand-made wheat udon is the central product and is prepared on-site, so flour and gluten are pervasive throughout the kitchen; this is not a safe environment for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are built around fish/meat-based broths and toppings (tori tempura, chicken-and-egg, offal miso, beef stew), so shared broth and cooking surfaces make vegan cross-contact likely.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The shop's listing invites guests to contact them directly with any questions, but no specific allergy-handling procedures are documented.

cuisine: Sanuki-style udon (handmade noodles, tsukemen and curry udon)
neighborhood: Higashimuki shopping arcade (Higashimuki-minamicho), ~2 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町13-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市東向南町13-2 returned [135.828918, 34.682663], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 40 seats: 4 counter, a 17-seat central communal table, and side tables (~15). Tabelog lists 37.
closed_days: Irregular days off (不定休); may close early once the day's noodles sell out.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, locally loved udon counter where a little Japanese helps with ordering; menus and service are in Japanese, so a translation app is handy for a foreign student.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-noodle udon specialty shop — udon, tsukemen and tempura are all wheat-based, so it is not suitable for celiacs or anyone avoiding gluten. No gluten-free options or precautions are documented.
vegan_detail: Broths are built on fish/meat dashi (e.g. the signature curry udon uses domestic beef-tendon stock combined with the house dashi), and standard udon dashi and soy-based sauces are not vegan. No documented vegan dishes.
caveats: Seat count varies by source (Tabelog 37 vs. official-affiliated profile ~40). Hours are the same every day in published listings but closure days are irregular and the shop may close early when sold out, so confirm before a special trip.

bio.background: Owner-chef Masahiro Shimoda runs this small udon house rooted in the Sanuki (Kagawa) udon tradition, where he blends his own flour, works the dough carefully, and cuts fresh noodles daily in the workshop at the back of the shop.
bio.specialty: Sanuki-style handmade udon served many ways: zaru (cold) udon that shows off the wheat's natural sweetness, freshly fried chicken-and-chikuwa tempura, and a rich curry udon made from slow-cooked domestic beef-tendon stock with caramelized onions and the house broth; a chige hot-pot udon appears in winter.
bio.philosophy: The name 粋 (iki) stands for the shop's three commitments: quality of flavor, the freshness of made-to-order food, and warm, heartfelt service, with staff offering lively welcomes and farewells.

anecdotes.text: Though grounded in Kagawa's udon culture, the shop aims to serve udon equal to or even surpassing the home prefecture's standard, using a uniquely blended flour and daily fresh-cut noodles.
Noodles are made by the owner in a workshop at the back of the kitchen, with careful attention to temperature and water, and tempura is fried to order for maximum freshness.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core product is wheat udon made in-house and tempura fried in shared oil; no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Bukkake and dipping styles use soy-based sauces/broths, which conventionally contain wheat; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths rely on fish/meat dashi (the curry udon uses beef-tendon stock), so no separated vegan preparation is documented.

neighborhood: 今御門町（ならまち・猿沢池近く）
cuisine: 日本料理・懐石（季節のおまかせコース）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese kaiseki house with no documented gluten-free program. The cuisine relies on a katsuobushi-and-kombu dashi and seasonal courses that routinely include wheat-based elements such as Miwa sōmen noodles and soy-sauce-seasoned dishes, so it is not safe for celiacs. A diner with a strict gluten allergy would need to discuss it directly at the time of reservation, and even then accommodation is not confirmed.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The omakase courses are built around a fish-based dashi (Makurazaki katsuobushi and kombu) and feature animal proteins such as Yamato beef, so the standard menu is not vegan or vegetarian. As an omakase counter that changes monthly with seasonal Yamato vegetables and kudzu, the chef might be able to adapt dishes if asked well in advance at booking, but no vegan offering is documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市今御門町26-2 Noriビル今御門 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI on the chō-banchi address (今御門町26番地); result 34.680523, 135.830322 falls within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 12 seats total — counter seating plus a private room.
closed_days: Sundays
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, upscale counter where reservations are essential and the chef serves a set omakase course; a foreign student would have a smoother experience with some basic Japanese or a Japanese-speaking companion, and should communicate any dietary needs at booking.
caveats: Higher-end dining: expect roughly 10,000-15,000 yen at lunch and 20,000-30,000 yen at dinner. Only 12 seats and reservation-only, so book ahead. Not suitable for strict gluten-free or vegan diners without prior arrangement.

bio.background: Chef-owner Okada Naoki grew up in a farming family in Nara, so he knew the flavor of the region's produce from childhood. After culinary school he spent sixteen years training at the Osaka restaurant Shinsaibashi Masuda before returning home, opening his own small counter near Sarusawa Pond in September 2019.
bio.specialty: An eight-course seasonal omakase centered on a rotating hassun (assorted seasonal appetizer platter) that showcases Nara's bounty — Yamato beef, traditional Yamato vegetables, kudzu, and Miwa sōmen. The dashi is drawn Osaka-style from Makurazaki katsuobushi and kombu.
bio.philosophy: Okada wants guests to enjoy themselves through refined tableware, meticulously prepared dishes, and sincere hospitality, expressing the turning of the seasons through Nara's ingredients.

anecdotes.text: Okada studied the tea ceremony so that he can personally whisk and serve matcha to close each meal — a deliberate gesture of hospitality.
Raised in a Nara farming family, he draws on a lifelong familiarity with local Yamato ingredients, which he calls part of Nara's abundance.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Courses include wheat-based items such as Miwa sōmen noodles and soy-seasoned dishes, with no documented gluten-free protocol.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Cuisine uses Japanese soy sauce and a dashi base typical of kaiseki, both of which involve wheat and fish; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The dashi is made from katsuobushi (bonito) and kombu and the menu features Yamato beef, so vegan diners cannot rely on the standard courses.

cuisine: 懐石・割烹（大和野菜・季節食材のおまかせ／Japanese kaiseki-kappo, chef's omakase）
neighborhood: ならまち（不審ケ辻子町・奈良ホテル近く／近鉄奈良駅）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a traditional Japanese kaiseki house where dishes lean heavily on fish/kombu dashi and soy-sauce-based seasonings, and items such as tempura (e.g. grilled sweetfish tempura, persimmon-seed-batter kakiage) use wheat batter. There is no published allergen or gluten-free information, so a celiac diner would need to inquire directly in advance; it should not be assumed safe.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian option is documented. The omakase course is built around seasonal seafood (smoked sea bream, scallops, sweetfish) alongside Yamato vegetables, and dashi is fish/kombu based, so the menu is not vegan by default. As a single-chef omakase counter it is occasionally possible that dietary requests can be discussed when booking, but nothing is confirmed; vegans should ask before reserving and not expect a set vegan menu.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市不審ケ辻子町1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned the chō-level coordinate for 奈良市不審ケ辻子町1; within the Nara bounding box.
seats_text: After the December 2025 relocation to the renovated machiya near Nara Hotel, seating is intimate: a small counter (around 4 seats) where the chef works in view, plus a second-floor private room. Exact total seat count for the new premises is not officially published (the former location had 16 seats); treat the counter as roughly 4-5 seats.
closed_days: Sundays and the 1st & 3rd Mondays closed. Additionally lunch is not served on the 2nd & 4th Mondays and the 1st & 3rd Tuesdays (dinner only on those days). Note: Monday hours vary week to week, so confirm when booking.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a refined, reservation-only counter where the chef cooks in front of you and explains dishes calmly, so a little Japanese (or a Japanese-speaking friend) and an understanding of formal omakase etiquette will make the experience much smoother for a foreign student.
caveats: The linked Tabelog page still reflects the former 東城戸町 address; the restaurant relocated to a renovated Taisho-era machiya near Nara Hotel (不審ケ辻子町) and reopened on 6 December 2025. Lunch is a single simultaneous 12:00 start, arrival on time is required, and the special omakase course needs a minimum of 2 guests. Perfume is discouraged. Counter seats are limited and hard to secure. Verify the exact day's hours (especially Mondays/Tuesdays) when reserving.

bio.background: Chef Katsuya Matsumuro trained for around ten years at "Kawanami" in Shin-Omiya and spent considerable time at the long-established "Ajikicho" in Osaka before opening his own kappo in the Naramachi district. He ran the restaurant for about eight years in Higashishiodo-cho, then in December 2025 relocated it to a fully renovated Taisho-era former timber merchant's machiya near Nara Hotel.
bio.specialty: A reservation-only omakase built around seasonal ingredients and Yamato (Nara) vegetables, paired with local sake. Signature preparations have included smoked sea bream, grilled sweetfish tempura with sansho pepper, and Hokkaido scallop kakiage in a persimmon-seed batter.
bio.philosophy: The cooking is summed up by the phrase "美味真心" — drawing out the honest, true taste of each ingredient through sincere, careful work, letting seasonal produce speak for itself.

anecdotes.text: The chef works at a small counter in full view of guests and is known for being very calm and happy to answer questions about each dish as it is served.
After eight years in Higashishiodo-cho, the restaurant moved on 6 December 2025 into a renovated Taisho-era machiya that once belonged to a timber merchant, near Nara Hotel.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Japanese kaiseki kitchen the cooking relies on soy sauce and fish/kombu dashi, and fried dishes use wheat batter; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The omakase centers on seafood (sea bream, scallops, sweetfish) and fish-based dashi alongside Yamato vegetables, so dishes are not prepared vegan by default.

lead: 路地裏のおにぎり屋さん 一穂二穂
name_ja: 路地裏のおにぎり屋さん 一穂二穂
cuisine: おにぎり専門店 (handmade onigiri / rice balls)
neighborhood: 下御門商店街・ならまち (近鉄奈良駅)
gf_detail: The base is steamed white rice, which is naturally gluten-free, and a plain salt onigiri (¥180) or a salt-koji salmon onigiri is the closest to a safe choice. However, this is not a gluten-free establishment: many fillings are seasoned with soy sauce (which contains wheat) and other prepared ingredients, and there is no documented allergen control or dedicated preparation. A celiac traveler would need to ask carefully about each filling and accept cross-contact risk on shared surfaces.
vegan_detail: A small number of items could suit a vegan diner — most reliably the plain salt onigiri and a plum-and-kelp (梅昆布) onigiri — but the shop is not vegan-oriented. Many of the 13 onigiri varieties feature meat (ginger meat soboro, yuzu-miso meat soboro, a meat-wrapped onigiri stick), fish (salt-koji red salmon, egg) or broth-based seasonings, and fillings may be prepared with dashi. There is no documentation confirming which items are free of animal products, so a vegan should confirm ingredients directly.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市下御門町4 e・リュエル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from 奈良県奈良市下御門町4 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 7 eat-in seats including a relaxed indoor grass-floor area where you remove your shoes; takeout is the main focus.
hours_raw: Weekdays open 11:00, weekends/holidays open 10:30; closes when the day's rice runs out (お米が無くなり次第終了). Closing time varies; Tabelog lists service to around 20:00. Closed days are reported inconsistently across sources (Tabelog: Tue/Wed; local listing: Mon/Fri) — check the shop's Instagram for current days and temporary closures.
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday per Tabelog (a local listing instead states Monday and Friday); confirm via Instagram. The shop also closes early once the rice sells out.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run takeout counter tucked down an alley where ordering is simple — point at the onigiri you want — but staff likely speak limited English, so a few Japanese words and patience go a long way.
caveats: Closed days are reported inconsistently between sources, and the shop closes whenever the rice sells out, so hours are genuinely unpredictable — check the Instagram before going. Seat count (about 7) comes from one local listing and may vary.

bio.background: A small independent onigiri shop that opened in June 2023, tucked down an alley off the Shimomikado shopping arcade in Naramachi — look for the blue flag beside the roasted sweet-potato shop Ishinzo. The owner shapes every rice ball to order by hand.
bio.specialty: Hand-formed onigiri made from freshly cooked rice — a lineup of around 13 fillings priced roughly ¥180–300, from a simple salt onigiri to salt-koji red salmon, plum-and-kelp, ginger meat soboro and yuzu-miso meat soboro, plus a meat-wrapped onigiri stick (¥460) and a beef-and-vegetable broth on the side.
bio.philosophy: The shop is built on the idea of rice and filling lifting each other up. The owner is meticulous about sourcing — rice that genuinely impressed them, prized nori and salt — and ages selected fillings overnight, refining salt, fillings and broth through repeated tastings. The shaping itself is a craft: the aim is to fold in as much air as possible while pressing only just hard enough to keep the shape, so the onigiri loosens pleasantly in the mouth.

anecdotes.text: The eat-in space is unusually playful: an indoor grass-floor area where you slip off your shoes and enjoy your onigiri picnic-style, and you may bring your own drink.
The shop cooks a set amount of rice each day and simply closes once it sells out, so arriving early gives the best choice of fillings.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Several fillings (e.g. egg-yolk soy sauce, soboro) are seasoned with soy sauce, which in standard Japanese form contains wheat; broth-based and prepared fillings may also include gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meat and fish fillings are prepared and handled in the same small kitchen as any plant-based options, so cross-contact is likely; no separation is documented.

positives.text: Onigiri are shaped to order in front of you, so you can ask which fillings are involved before choosing.

lead: おむすび ばんしゅん
cuisine: おにぎり専門店（こだわり米のむすび、地元の朝ごはん・テイクアウト）
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺・下御門／もちいどの商店街（ならまち寄り）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市光明院町13
geocode_note: GSI address search returned the point for 奈良県奈良市光明院町13番地; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Open-front shop with about 6 counter seats for eat-in; mainly geared to takeout.
hours_raw: 11:00-17:00; closed Thursdays (some sources note irregular holidays).
closed_days: Thursday (sources also note occasional irregular holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: An open-front counter where you point at the onigiri you want and pay at the counter, with a friendly atmosphere welcoming to visitors, so a few words of Japanese and gestures are plenty.
gf_detail: Rice onigiri are naturally rice-based and salt-only versions exist, but the popular tea-rice and soy-sauce versions use soy sauce (contains wheat), and fillings include mackerel, salmon and fried chicken. There is no documented gluten-free or celiac handling, so this is not a celiac-safe destination; a salt onigiri may be the safest option if you ask.
vegan_detail: One source notes some vegan-friendly options among the roughly 10-12 daily onigiri (e.g., salt rice, pickled-vegetable / yuzu-miso and takana-style fillings), but many items use fish (mackerel, salmon), egg, or meat. Free miso soup for eat-in typically uses fish dashi. Confirm fillings before ordering if strictly vegan.

bio.background: A small onigiri specialty shop that opened in December 2023 on the Shimomikado/Mochiidono shopping street between Kintetsu Nara Station and Naramachi. It is an open-front, doorless counter shop: freshly made rice balls are displayed on one side and a handful of eat-in seats sit on the other, designed as a casual local breakfast-and-takeout spot for both neighbors and sightseers.
bio.specialty: Onigiri made from freshly milled Koshihikari rice. The signature is the Nara chameshi (tea-rice) onigiri, a local Nara tradition of rice cooked with soybeans in bancha tea, offered with salt or soy sauce. Around 10-12 varieties rotate daily, including yuzu-miso, takana, mackerel-and-ginger rice, and salt-koji grilled salmon, plus sides like rolled egg, fried chicken, and oden in winter.
bio.philosophy: A hands-on, fresh-and-simple approach: each onigiri comes with a single sheet of nori served separately so guests wrap it themselves for a crisp texture, a microwave is on hand to rewarm cooled rice, and eat-in guests get a complimentary bowl of miso soup.

anecdotes.text: Each rice ball is served with a separate sheet of roasted nori so customers wrap it themselves just before eating, keeping the seaweed crisp.
The shop drew a wave of new customers after being featured on the TV program 'Matsuko no Shiranai Sekai' (Matsuko's Unknown World) in spring 2024.
Takeout bags are designed so they can double as a small pocket trash bag, a small thoughtful touch noted by visitors.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Onigiri are offered in salt or soy-sauce versions and the signature tea rice uses soy sauce; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The lineup mixes vegetable/pickle fillings with fish (mackerel, salmon), egg and chicken items made in the same small open kitchen; no separation is documented.

positives.text: Plain salt onigiri made from freshly milled Koshihikari rice are available, and some vegan-friendly fillings are offered among the daily varieties.

caveats: Name confirmed as おむすび 番旬 (target spelling ばんしゅん is the reading). Seat count (~6 counter seats) and the Thursday closure come from review/local sources; Tabelog notes irregular holidays, so hours_status is irregular. Vegan options are reported but specific fillings rotate daily and should be confirmed in person. Official URL is the shop's Instagram (@omusubi_bansyun); no standalone website found.

lead: 大和野菜と串焼き やまと 近鉄奈良店
name_ja: 大和野菜と串焼き やまと 近鉄奈良店
name_en: Yamato Yasai to Kushiyaki Yamato Kintetsu Nara-ten
cuisine: 大和野菜・創作串焼き（大和野菜・大和肉鶏・大和ポーク・筒井れんこんなど地元食材、地酒20種超の居酒屋）
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町23 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to chō-level (東向南町23); coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 40 seats total (table and sunken kotatsu seating; private rooms for 7-14 guests; whole-venue reservations for 25-40).
closed_days: No regular closing day (open daily)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a Japanese-language local izakaya with no English website noted, so basic Japanese or a translation app helps, though staff are used to walk-in guests near the station.
gf_detail: An izakaya built around grilled skewers, hotpots, and local sake; sauces and dashi typically contain wheat-based soy sauce and miso, and no gluten-free accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on Yamato vegetables, and grilled vegetable plates (焼き野菜各種, from 660 yen) are a signature. However, the kitchen is meat- and dashi-forward (Yamato chicken, pork, Asuka hotpot with chicken broth and milk), and no fully vegan preparation is documented, so a vegan would need to ask carefully about seasoning and cooking surfaces.

bio.background: A Kintetsu Nara Station izakaya, a short walk from Exit 2, dedicated to the produce of the Yamato region. The kitchen builds its menu around locally grown Yamato vegetables and Nara-prefecture ingredients, served alongside more than twenty kinds of local sake.
bio.specialty: Assorted grilled Yamato vegetables and creative skewers, local Yamato chicken and pork, and Asuka-nabe, a regional Nara hotpot of chicken broth blended with milk, white miso, soy sauce, and sugar.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing seasonal Yamato vegetables and local Nara ingredients paired with regional sake.

anecdotes.text: Asuka-nabe, one of the house specialties, is a Nara regional dish combining chicken broth with milk, white miso, soy sauce, and sugar.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes such as Asuka-nabe are made with soy sauce and miso, which are typically wheat-based, indicating standard (gluten-containing) seasonings are used throughout.

positives.text: Assorted grilled Yamato vegetable plates are a documented signature item, giving plant-based diners at least some vegetable-forward choices.

caveats: Hours and seating confirmed via the official reservation site and Tabelog; no English-language information or documented allergy/dietary handling was found. Vegan and gluten-free travelers should confirm seasonings directly with staff.

cuisine: Café / kissaten — coffee, tea, and homemade cakes and cookies only
neighborhood: Takabatake-chō, beside the former Shiga Naoya residence, toward Nara Park and Kasuga Taisha
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The menu is limited to drinks and house-made cakes and cookies, which are wheat-based baked goods. There is no documented gluten-free option and no celiac accommodation.
vegan_detail: No documented vegan menu. The offering is drinks (coffee, café au lait, tea) plus homemade cakes and cookies, which typically contain dairy and eggs. Black coffee or plain tea may be the only safe choices; any vegan need would have to be asked about on the spot, and options are very unlikely.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1247
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.842651, 34.677849] for 奈良県奈良市高畑町1247; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Small indoor seating in the Western-style house plus an open-air garden terrace; exact seat count not documented. Limited capacity.
hours_raw: 14:00–18:00; open Friday, Saturday, Sunday and public holidays only. Closed Monday–Thursday.
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday, Thursday (open Fri–Sun and public holidays only)
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, family-run garden café in a historic home where Japanese is the main language and the gentle, contemplative atmosphere rewards visitors who arrive calmly and order simply.

bio.background: Takabatake Salon occupies a Taishō-era Western-style residence in Nara's Takabatake district, the Nakamura family home that the painter Nakamura Kazuo inherited from his father. He and his wife opened the café in the garden of the residence. The building stands directly beside the former home of the novelist Shiga Naoya, whose house in the early Shōwa era became famous as the 'Takabatake Salon,' a gathering place for writers and artists.
bio.specialty: Coffee, café au lait, and teas paired with homemade cakes and cookies; a beverage-and-cake set is offered at a small discount.
bio.philosophy: The owners opened the café out of a longing to revive the cultural spirit of the old Takabatake Salon — a place where conversation, art, and quiet contemplation could flourish again over a cup of coffee in a green garden.

anecdotes.text: The café revives the legacy of the historic 'Takabatake Salon,' the name given to Shiga Naoya's nearby residence where literary figures and artists gathered during the early Shōwa period; the owners drew direct inspiration from that era of cultural exchange.

Guests pass along a hushed, secluded path into a verdant walled garden where the coffee is said to taste exceptional — 'an ethereal, tranquil pathway leading to exceptional coffee enjoyed in a verdant garden.'
The Western-style building is a registered tangible cultural property, and the café serves afternoon tea on a terrace planted with seasonal flowers.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The menu consists of wheat-based homemade cakes and cookies baked on site, with no gluten-free items documented; cross-contamination with flour should be assumed in a small home kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cakes and cookies typically contain dairy and eggs; no vegan baking is documented, so plain coffee or tea are the only likely plant-based choices.

positives.text: Simple drink-and-dessert café where black coffee and plain tea are straightforward, low-risk orders for diners avoiding allergens in cooked dishes.

caveats: Open only Fri–Sun and public holidays, 14:00–18:00, with limited seating and no reservations. The menu is drinks and homemade wheat/dairy/egg desserts only — not a destination for gluten-free or vegan travelers, though plain coffee or tea work. Seat count not documented. Building is a registered tangible cultural property.

cuisine: 純喫茶・レトロ喫茶（ぬくもりのある個人喫茶店・文化サロン）
neighborhood: 高畑町（ならまち南部・新薬師寺方面／京終駅エリア）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1358-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.84462, 34.676289] for 奈良県奈良市高畑町1358-1; within Nara box.
gf_detail: A retro Japanese kissaten serving curry, cakes, and café drinks; no gluten-free menu, accommodation, or celiac handling is documented. Curry roux and cakes contain wheat, and there is no indication of dedicated equipment. Not suitable for celiac travelers.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options are documented. As a traditional café, the curry and cake sets are likely to contain animal products (dairy, eggs, possibly meat/fish dashi). A vegan would need to ask directly, and options are unlikely.
closed_days: 火曜日（Tuesdays）
seats_text: Seat count not published. A high-ceilinged café room plus a separate tea room available by reservation; a small independent space.
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, intimate local salon frequented mostly by older Japanese regulars; an English menu is unlikely, so basic Japanese or a translation app will help, but a friendly, calm atmosphere awaits.
caveats: No official website found; payment method not explicitly published but assumed cash-only as typical for this style of small kissaten — confirm on arrival. Seat count and credit-card acceptance are not documented.

bio.background: Tucked into a handsome earthen-walled building on the slope between Shin-Yakushiji Temple and Nara Park, Rokusaron is less a conventional café than a living salon. The high-ceilinged main room is washed in green tones and warm wood, with large windows, artwork, a courtyard garden, and a piano — a space regulars liken to a café hidden in a forest.
bio.specialty: A well-loved curry, cake sets, and seasonal sweets such as a sakura mont blanc, alongside classic café drinks.
bio.philosophy: The place doubles as a gathering hall: piano recitals, classical music talks, and an 'utagoe' sing-along café draw a lively, devoted community of mostly older patrons who fill it with conversation.

anecdotes.text: One visitor discovered it while walking down from Shin-Yakushiji toward Nara Park, mistook the earthen-walled facade for a grand private villa, and found inside a 'mysterious café with warmth' full of radiant, chatty elderly regulars — a Ghibli-like sense of wonder.
Beyond drinks and cake, the venue hosts piano recitals and classical lectures and runs as a singing café, functioning as a true salon rather than an ordinary coffee shop.

cuisine: カレー喫茶・純喫茶（家族経営のレトロな喫茶店。インド風さらさらカレーと手作りケーキが名物）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish is a Japanese/Indian-style curry rice; curry roux of this style is wheat-based, and the menu centers on curry plus home-baked cakes and焼き菓子 (flour-based baked goods). No gluten-free accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The signature curry contains beef brisket (牛バラ肉) and the curry is meat-based; the cakes and baked goods are made with eggs/dairy. No vegan dishes are documented. A vegetarian could possibly ask, but nothing is confirmed.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町840
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 奈良県奈良市高畑町840; coordinates within the Nara box.
neighborhood: 高畑町（ならまち東〜高畑エリア、奈良教育大近く）
seats_text: Seat count not published; sources describe it as a small, quiet renovated old townhouse (古長屋) with vintage tables — a compact owner-run space.
hours_raw: 11:30-19:30（食べログ表記）。定休日：月曜・火曜。出典により表記に揺れあり（ヒトサラでは11:00-20:00、日曜定休との記載もあり）。
closed_days: 月曜・火曜（Monday & Tuesday）
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, regulars-loved local kissaten where ordering in simple Japanese is smoothest, though the long-time owner's family has a French connection, so a warm welcome to foreigners is likely.
caveats: Hours and closed days differ across listings (Tabelog: Wed-Sun 11:30-19:30, closed Mon-Tue; another listing shows 11:00-20:00 closed Sun). Confirm by phone before visiting. The founding husband, Mr. Sakamoto, is reported to have passed away, with his French wife continuing the shop — confirm current operation locally.

bio.chef_name: 坂本節夫 (Setsuo Sakamoto) and his wife Miriam
bio.background: A long-running retro kissaten in Nara's quiet Takabatake district near Nara University of Education, open for around three decades. It occupies a renovated old row house (古長屋) hand-remodeled by the owners, with aged wooden tables, vintage hanging fishing lanterns, and famously no background music — just a calm, timeless room a minute's walk from the bus stop.
bio.origin: Nara, Japan (Takabatake / Naramachi-east area); the shop's name 'Miriamu' comes from the owner's French wife, Miriam.
bio.specialty: A spicy, Indian-style 'sarasara' (thin, free-flowing) curry rice built on eight spices, simmered about three hours and rested a day, with chunks of beef brisket. It comes with raisins and plain yogurt on the side — stir the yogurt in to soften the heat, or nibble raisins as a palate cleanser. Add +100 yen for a larger portion or +200 yen for a drink set. Miriam's home-baked cakes and rustic焼き菓子, including a popular dark chocolate cake, draw their own devoted following.
bio.philosophy: An unhurried refuge: no music, decades-old furnishings, and food made slowly by hand for regulars who have returned for years.

anecdotes.text: The cafe is named after the owner's French wife, Miriam, who bakes the cakes and baked goods served alongside the curry each day.
The curry is a thin Indian-style preparation built on eight spices, simmered about three hours and rested a full day, served with raisins and yogurt to adjust the heat.
The interior is a hand-renovated old row house with retro furnishings and deliberately no background music, giving the room a notably peaceful atmosphere.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu centers on wheat-based curry and home-baked flour cakes/pastries; no gluten-free handling is documented, so cross-contamination from flour should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature curry contains beef brisket and the baked goods use eggs/dairy; no separate vegan preparation is documented.

positives.text: Yogurt and raisins are served on the side so diners can adjust the curry's spice level themselves.

cuisine: 自家焙煎スペシャルティコーヒー・カフェ（スイーツ、サンドイッチ）
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅・ならまち
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市角振新屋町6-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市角振新屋町6-2 returned [135.827881, 34.681591], within the central Nara box. Consistent with official site's stated location (~34.68N, 135.83E).
seats_text: Approximately 20 seats across two floors, with counter seating and a terrace; the second floor is the main white-walled seating space.
hours_raw: 7:00-19:00 daily (official site lists 7:30-19:00 every day); open year-round, no regular closed days.
closed_days: None (open year-round / 無休)
cultural_comfort_note: A polished, modern specialty-coffee café with an English-language website and a takeout-friendly, walk-in counter format, so a foreign student should feel comfortable ordering here.
caveats: Dietary/allergen information is not published; gluten-free and vegan travelers should ask directly, as most sweets and sandwiches contain wheat and dairy. Hours differ slightly between sources (7:00 vs 7:30 open).
gf_detail: This is a coffee roastery café whose food is primarily wheat- and dairy-based baked goods (financiers, canelés, tarts, macarons, pies) and sandwiches. No gluten-free offerings or precautions are documented; not suitable for celiacs without direct confirmation.
vegan_detail: No vegan items are advertised. Coffee can be served black, but the signature drinks (lattes, frappés, vanilla brûlée) and sweets use dairy and eggs. Plant-milk availability is not documented; vegans should ask about milk substitutes and any dairy-free items.

bio.background: CHAMI is an independent specialty-coffee roastery café tucked into a stylish white-walled building a couple of minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station, on the edge of the historic Naramachi quarter. Coffee is roasted in-house, and a large espresso machine greets you at the entrance, where baristas prepare each cup one at a time. Seating spreads over two floors, with the main room upstairs framed by big street-facing windows that fill the minimalist space with light.
bio.specialty: House-roasted specialty coffee, including the original CHAMI blend and rotating single-origin Geishas, served alongside handcrafted French-style sweets such as financiers, canelés, citrus and Saint-Honoré-style tarts, and macarons. Signature drinks include the CHAMI latte and the CHAMI vanilla brûlée frappé.
bio.philosophy: The team frames coffee as a 'journey of discovery' and wants even non-coffee drinkers to enjoy the experience, encouraging guests to explore food-and-coffee pairings so that an everyday cup feels special.

anecdotes.text: A large espresso machine sits right at the entrance, where baristas brew each coffee individually, underscoring the shop's careful, one-cup-at-a-time approach.
Staff describe wanting to share 'the enjoyment of food pairing,' welcoming guests who don't normally drink coffee.
The cafe also sells its own original coffee tools and gift sets both in-store and online, presenting itself as a roaster for 'lovers and beginners worldwide.'

cuisine: 古民家カフェ・喫茶バー（スパイスカレー、三輪素麺、自家製スイーツ、茶系ドリンク）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature dishes are wheat-based: the curry "oage" dish is built on Miwa sōmen (wheat noodles) and the cafe also serves udon (wheat). No gluten-free menu, no dedicated prep, and no allergen accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Signature items are not reliably plant-based — the spiced curry/sōmen dish uses fried tofu (oage) but the broth and curry seasoning are not confirmed animal-free, the Nara egg pudding contains egg and dairy, and sweets/lattes use dairy. A vegan diner would need to ask directly about each item; nothing is verified as vegan.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市元林院町8
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned chō-level coordinates for 元林院町 (Motorinoin-chō); within the central Nara box. Address number 8 not resolved to building level.
neighborhood: ならまち（猿沢池南側・元林院町）
seats_text: Seat count not published. Small kominka with several distinct rooms — a former air-raid shelter tatami room, a ground-floor storehouse (kura) room, a second-floor knit-art room, and a rooftop area with a view of Kōfuku-ji's five-story pagoda.
hours_raw: 11:00–18:00（火〜土）、日曜 11:30–17:00、月曜定休。出典により日曜の開始を11:00とするものもあり。
closed_days: 月曜日（Mondays）。一部の情報では日曜も休みとする記載あり。
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, owner-run hideaway café where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed attitude go a long way; English menus or fluent English service are not documented, so bring a translation app for the curry and tea descriptions.
caveats: Seat count unpublished. Sunday opening time varies by source (11:00 vs 11:30). One listing notes the cafe may also close Sundays — confirm before a Sunday visit. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published; all dietary needs should be confirmed directly with the owner.

bio.background: ROKKAN ROOM occupies a 140-plus-year-old machiya in Naramachi's old Motorinoin entertainment district, just south of Sarusawa Pond. The building was once an okiya — a geisha house — and the owner, Moe Oyagi, converted what had been her own residence into the café. Layers of history remain throughout: an earthen-floor doma, a storehouse (kura), original lacquered tables from the okiya days, and, beneath and within the house, a preserved pre-war air-raid shelter that now serves as one of the seating rooms.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is a curry-filled fried-tofu sōmen — Nara's famous Miwa sōmen in a gentle broth topped with an oage pouch that, when split with chopsticks, releases spiced curry (around 1,350 yen). Other beloved items include a Nara-egg pudding (about 450 yen) that showcases local egg quality, and the owner's original chai-spiced oshiruko (chai red-bean soup, about 780 yen). A hōjicha latte float is a frequent recommendation.
bio.philosophy: Oyagi designed the space around intentional contradictions — low ceilings and bare concrete walls paired with tatami and plush cushions — to make guests feel as if they have slipped out of the modern world. She has spoken about preserving the air-raid shelter rather than hiding it, framing it as a way for visitors to encounter and reflect on the history of war.

anecdotes.text: The owner converted her own former residence, a former geisha house (okiya), into the café, keeping original okiya lacquer tables and the building's doma and storehouse intact.

A wartime air-raid shelter on the property has been turned into a tatami-floored seating room; the owner chose to keep it visible as a way for guests to learn about the war rather than treating it as something negative.
The curry-and-fried-tofu Miwa sōmen began as a seasonal item but became a year-round signature because of its popularity.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature dishes use Miwa sōmen and udon, both wheat noodles, prepared in a shared small-cafe kitchen. No gluten-free handling is described.

cuisine: Spanish cuisine / wine bar (vegetable-forward tapas, paella, house-baked bread)
neighborhood: Naramachi, in a back alley off Mochiidono shopping arcade
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町27-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良市餅飯殿町27-1 returned [135.829208, 34.681343], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 6 counter seats and 3 tables in a converted traditional townhouse.
hours_raw: Tue-Sat 11:30-14:00, 17:00-21:00; closed Sun & Mon. (Lunch L.O. ~13:00, dinner L.O. ~21:00)
closed_days: Sundays and Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny family-run alley bar with cash-only service and no documented English menu, so a few Japanese phrases (or a translation app) will smooth the visit; reservations are by phone only.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The kitchen centers on house-baked wheat bread (sandwiches, pan con tomate) and the dinner menu features paella alongside flour-based dishes, with no celiac or cross-contamination protocols mentioned. Not suitable for those requiring strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The owner grows the vegetables himself and produce features heavily (roasted-vegetable escabeche, salads, Spanish tortilla), so some vegetable dishes exist, but the menu is built around cured ham, sheep's cheese, anchovies, seafood paella and meat. There is no vegan menu and no statement that vegetable dishes avoid animal stock or dairy, so a vegan would need to ask and rely on a small number of plant-based plates.
caveats: Cash only. Reservations taken by phone only (0742-22-2771); recommended due to very small size (9 seats). The app target listed the name as "Welt" but the venue's actual name is "huerto" (Spanish for vegetable garden) — confirm matching.

bio.background: huerto is a small family-run Spanish bar tucked down a quiet alley off the Mochiidono shopping arcade in Naramachi, set inside a renovated traditional townhouse. Its name is the Spanish word for vegetable garden, and that idea is literal: the owner grows vegetables in his own garden and cooks with them, while his wife bakes the restaurant's bread.
bio.specialty: Vegetable-forward Spanish cooking and tapas: pan con tomate (bread rubbed with tomato and garlic, topped with sheep's cheese and cured ham), house-cured anchovies, roasted-vegetable escabeche, Spanish tortilla, and dinner dishes such as seafood and squid-ink paella, conger eel and clam stew, and Ezo venison steak. Lunch is a weekly-changing sandwich plate with soup and sides; dessert includes crema catalana with persimmon.
bio.philosophy: A garden-to-table approach using their own homegrown vegetables and house-made bread, in a space meant to suit both a casual glass of wine and a full sit-down meal.

anecdotes.text: The owner cultivates the vegetables used in the kitchen in his own garden, while his wife bakes the restaurant's naturally-leavened bread by hand.
Set in a converted historic townhouse with just six counter seats and three tables, the dining room is described as keeping a stylish but natural, warm feel.

positives.text: The owner grows the vegetables himself and produce is featured prominently across the menu, so vegetable-based dishes (roasted-vegetable escabeche, salads, tortilla) are reliably available as options.

cuisine: 居酒屋・おばんざい／地酒 (owner-run izakaya, seasonal Yamato vegetables and Amakusa seafood, Nara/Japanese jizake)
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. This is a Japanese izakaya built around fish, seasonal vegetables, and sake, with dishes such as soy-glazed cod milt that rely on regular (wheat-based) soy sauce, and fried items (e.g., fried horse mackerel, crab cream croquettes) that involve wheat batter/flour. Fish dashi and soy seasonings are pervasive. There is no documentation of gluten-free accommodation, a dedicated fryer, or wheat-free soy sauce, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Primarily a seafood-and-sake izakaya whose concept centers on fresh fish from the owner's family fishery in Amakusa. It does feature seasonal Yamato/Nara vegetables (e.g., snap peas and corn dressed in white miso), but vegetable dishes are typically seasoned with fish-based dashi and miso/soy, so they are generally not vegan. No vegan-specific menu or preparation is documented. A vegan could likely ask about a simple vegetable plate, but should not assume dashi-free cooking.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南市町2-3
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.829758, 34.680672] for 奈良県奈良市南市町2-3; within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: 南市町（近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約7分、餅飯殿センター街の東側の路地）
seats_text: Small space with an L-shaped counter plus two tables; exact seat count not published.
closed_days: Irregular holidays (不定休); no fixed closed day published.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, locally loved owner-run izakaya tucked in a back alley with cash-only payment and no published English support, so a foreign student should be comfortable with basic Japanese and izakaya etiquette and bring cash.
caveats: Opened recently (July 24, 2025) in the former "ひるねこ (Hiruneko)" coffee-and-bread space; hours come from the listing as 14:00-22:00 daily, but as a small new izakaya with irregular holidays, days/hours can change, so checking the owner's Instagram before visiting is wise. Cash only. Vegetarian/vegan and gluten-free travelers have limited options given the seafood/dashi/soy focus.

bio.background: After running the larger establishment うま杉 (Umasugi) in the Shin-Omiya area, the owner wanted to scale down and run a small, personal place, saying "the previous place was large, so I wanted to run something smaller." In summer 2025 he opened Nora in a narrow back alley east of Mochiidono shopping street, in the space formerly occupied by the coffee-and-bread shop Hiruneko.
bio.specialty: Fresh fish delivered almost daily from the owner's family fishing operation in Amakusa, Kumamoto (around six varieties at a time) — served as sashimi and izakaya plates — paired with seasonal Yamato/Nara vegetables and a tightly curated, personally chosen selection of Japanese jizake (local sake). Documented dishes include soy-glazed cod milt, snap peas and corn in white miso dressing, fried horse mackerel, and crab cream croquettes.
bio.philosophy: A small, intimate counter where the food follows the season and the catch, and the sake list reflects the owner's own taste rather than a broad commercial range.

anecdotes.text: The owner's family home runs a fishing business in Amakusa, Kumamoto, which supplies the restaurant with fresh fish — roughly six varieties arriving at a time.
He previously ran the larger restaurant うま杉 near Shin-Omiya and opened Nora because he wanted something smaller and more personal: "前が大きかったから小さな店をやりたかった."
Nora opened on July 24, 2025 in the former Hiruneko coffee-and-bread space, about a 7-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, on a street just east of Mochiidono Center-gai.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes deep-fried wheat-battered items such as fried horse mackerel and crab cream croquettes, indicating shared fryer/flour use; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Documented dishes such as soy-glazed cod milt rely on standard soy-based seasoning; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is centered on fish from the owner's family fishery and fish dashi; vegetable dishes (e.g., snap peas and corn in white miso) are typically seasoned with dashi/miso and prepared in a seafood-forward kitchen.

lead: 奈良の酒蔵全部呑み うまっしゅ
name_ja: 奈良の酒蔵全部呑み うまっしゅ
name_en: Nara no Sakagura Zenbu Nomi Umasshu
cuisine: 地酒居酒屋 (Nara jizake izakaya)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a sake-focused izakaya whose food is washoku otsumami — seasonal vegetables, fresh seafood, and Japanese black beef in kaiseki-style and creative Japanese preparations. Such cooking relies heavily on wheat-containing soy sauce and seasonings, and no dedicated gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination controls are documented. Celiac travelers should not consider this safe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. The kitchen does prepare dishes featuring seasonal local Yamato vegetables, but the menu is built around seafood and Japanese black beef, and otsumami at a Japanese izakaya almost always use fish dashi and wheat-based soy sauce. A vegetable dish or two may be possible if you ask, but there is no documented vegan menu and no stated dashi-free option.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向中町11 八宝マルハチビル1階
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市東向中町11 returned [135.828918, 34.683792], within the central Nara box; consistent with a location ~1 minute from Kintetsu Nara Station on the Higashimuki arcade.
neighborhood: 東向中町 (Higashimuki-Nakamachi), Higashimuki arcade, ~1 min from Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: 52 seats total: 16 counter seats, table seating (4-tops and larger tables), with private rooms accommodating groups of about 10-20.
hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Fri 15:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); Sat, Sun & holidays 14:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closed Tuesdays (open if Tuesday is a holiday, with the following day closed instead); also closed Dec 31 and Jan 1.
closed_days: Tuesdays (open on holiday Tuesdays, substitute closure the next day); Dec 31 and Jan 1.
cultural_comfort_note: A foreign student is welcome here as a casual neighborhood spot, but the menu and sake program are Japanese-oriented, so knowing a few words and being ready to point or ask will make ordering smoother.
caveats: Sake-centric izakaya; food is seafood/beef/vegetable washoku otsumami with no documented vegan or gluten-free accommodation. Confirm any dietary needs directly with staff.

bio.background: Umasshu is a casual local izakaya tucked into the Higashimuki arcade about a minute from Kintetsu Nara Station, built around a single big idea: to let you drink your way through the entire sake culture of Nara Prefecture under one roof.
bio.specialty: Local Nara sake (jizake) sourced from all 29 breweries across the prefecture, poured in 50ml, 100ml, and 130ml cups so you can either taste widely or settle in with a favorite; paired with hand-crafted washoku otsumami highlighting seasonal Yamato vegetables, fresh seafood, and Japanese black beef.
bio.philosophy: A place to drop by casually after work or gather with friends and enjoy yourselves together, with the prefecture's sake made approachable through small pours and a brewery-stamp-card journey.

anecdotes.text: The shop runs a sake stamp card that tracks your progress drinking through Nara's 29 breweries, with rewards that grow as you collect more stamps — turning a night of drinking into a tour of the whole prefecture.
Sake is served in three cup sizes (50ml, 100ml, 130ml) so guests can sample many labels in small measures or linger over a single favorite.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Food is creative Japanese / kaiseki-style otsumami built around seafood and beef, which in Japanese izakaya cooking standardly uses wheat-based soy sauce and seasonings; no gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu centers on seafood and Japanese black beef with seasonal vegetables; otsumami typically rely on fish dashi, and no vegan or dashi-free preparation is documented.

positives.text: Dishes feature seasonal local Yamato vegetables alongside seafood and beef, so some vegetable-forward small plates may be available if you ask.

cuisine: Kominka izakaya — owner-run, seasonal grilled dishes and spice-pairing otsumami, Nara & nationwide sake
gf_detail: No gluten-free program. This is a sake-and-otsumami izakaya whose dishes lean on dashi, soy sauce, and spice marinades; the masala oden uses dashi and the spice skewers use yogurt/parmesan sauces — nothing is documented as wheat-free. Soy sauce here will be standard wheat-containing shōyu. Not suitable for celiacs; no allergy or cross-contamination protocol is published.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-oriented place. The signature menu is built around chicken, lamb, hearts (ハツ), beef tendon and fish-dashi oden, and the spice sauces use yogurt and parmesan. There may be a few vegetable-based otsumami or grilled-vegetable plates, but most dishes use fish dashi or meat, so a vegan would need to ask and confirm what is available. No vegan menu is documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南市町16-2
geocode_note: GSI address search on 奈良県奈良市南市町16 returned [135.829422, 34.680107], within the Nara box and consistent with the Naramachi area ~10 min from Kintetsu Nara Station.
neighborhood: Naramachi (Minami-Ichi-chō), ~10 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: Roughly 18-19 seats across counter, table, and tatami (small-room) seating; sources vary between 18 and 19.
hours_raw: Wed–Sat 17:00–22:00; Sun 11:00–22:00; closed Mon & Tue (plus occasional irregular holidays). Note: some listings show different splits (e.g., dinner Mon–Sat 17:00–21:00, Sun/holiday 14:00–19:00, lunch 11:30–14:00) — the official site hours are used here.
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday (occasional additional irregular holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run kominka izakaya where ordering and chatting will mostly be in Japanese, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) helps, but the relaxed sake-and-snacks setting is welcoming.
caveats: Hours and seat counts differ between the official site and third-party listings; confirm by phone (0742-81-3932) or LINE before visiting. A ¥1,000 table charge applies to non-drinkers during izakaya hours.

bio.background: A renovated old townhouse (kominka) tucked into Nara's Naramachi quarter, about ten minutes on foot from Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner is a self-described izakaya lover who spent years researching spices — including study of spice technique in India — and folded that knowledge into a Japanese drinking-snack menu.
bio.specialty: Spice-forward otsumami built to pair with sake: spice skewers of chicken, hearts (ハツ) and lamb in a yogurt-and-parmesan sauce with citrus acidity; tandoori-style garam-masala chicken hearts; and a signature 'masala oden' that balances dashi with curry-leaf aromatics over tender beef tendon. Simple, ingredient-forward grilled plates round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: That traditional Japanese ingredients and izakaya technique can be elevated with thoughtful spice, and that spiced snacks can pair beautifully with Japanese sake.

anecdotes.text: The owner conducted years of spice research and studied spice technique in India, then built a sake-pairing otsumami menu around it — the masala oden, layering dashi with curry leaf, is described as 'a new frontier of flavor.'
The sake list reaches beyond Nara to selections from across Japan, alongside harder-to-find regional drafts like Sapporo's Sorachi.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes lean on dashi, soy-based seasonings and spice marinades; standard shōyu (wheat-containing) is expected and no wheat-free alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Spice sauces use yogurt and parmesan, and the oden uses fish dashi, so vegetable dishes are likely to share dairy/fish-based components.

cuisine: 純喫茶・洋食喫茶 (classic kissaten / Western-style coffee shop: omurice, curry, pasta, siphon coffee)
neighborhood: 大宮町 / 三条通り, near JR Nara & Kintetsu Nara Stations
gf_detail: This is a classic Western-style kissaten whose core menu is wheat-heavy and not suitable for celiacs. Signature dishes include omurice (ketchup rice with a demi-glace or white sauce — both typically thickened with wheat flour), curry, pasta/spaghetti, doria and gratin, plus morning toast, sandwiches and hot dogs. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated preparation, and no documented allergy handling. Not a gluten-free destination.
vegan_detail: A traditional omurice-and-coffee shop with no vegan offerings. Egg- and dairy-based dishes dominate (omurice, egg sandwiches, doria/gratin), and savory items use beef and demi-glace. No plant-based options are documented. Not suitable for vegans; a vegetarian could potentially find plain toast or coffee but nothing is confirmed vegan.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市大宮町2-3-4-105 東急ドエル奈良パークビレッジ1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō/banchi level (大宮町2-3-4) via GSI; coordinates fall within the Nara box.
seats_text: 28 seats: 6 counter seats, one 6-person table, four 4-person tables
hours_raw: Weekdays 7:00–18:30 (morning service 7:00–11:00, lunch 11:30–15:00); Sundays & holidays 7:00–17:00; last order ~30 min before close. Closed Wednesdays.
closed_days: Wednesdays (some sources also note the 3rd Tuesday of the month; Sunday/holiday hours are shorter, closing 17:00)
cultural_comfort_note: A cozy, regulars-filled neighborhood kissaten where the menu and service are in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or pointing at the omurice will help; warm and welcoming once you're seated.
caveats: Cash only. Hours shorten on Sundays/holidays (close 17:00) and the shop is closed Wednesdays (possibly also the 3rd Tuesday). No vegan or gluten-free provisions. No official website; details drawn from local Nara guides and the restaurant listing.

bio.background: A long-running classic kissaten near JR and Kintetsu Nara Stations, Rich has been pouring coffee and serving Western-style comfort food for around 40 years, occupying the ground floor of the Tokyu Dwell Nara Park Village building near the former Nara Girls' High School along Sanjo-dori.
bio.specialty: The house signature is a fluffy omurice — soft, half-set scrambled egg over ketchup rice with beef or mushrooms, finished with a choice of sweet demi-glace or white sauce. The other star is the coffee: a house blend of several beans brewed by siphon for a smooth, mellow, mild cup. Morning sets (egg sandwiches, toast, hot dogs) and lunch sets of curry, doria, gratin and pasta round out the menu, each lunch coming with salad, a mini dessert and a drink.
bio.philosophy: An unhurried, at-home (アットホーム) atmosphere that keeps decades of regulars coming back while staying easy for first-time visitors to drop into.

anecdotes.text: The coffee is a multi-bean house blend brewed in a siphon, described as smooth and mellow with no harshness.
Open from 7:00 in the morning with a dedicated morning service of egg sandwiches, toast and hot dogs, reflecting its role as an early-day neighborhood gathering spot.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes (omurice sauces, curry, doria, gratin, pasta, toast, sandwiches) are wheat-based; no gluten-free menu or separate preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is centered on egg, dairy and beef dishes; no vegan options or plant-based preparation are documented.

lead: 大和野菜と地酒 大和乃山賊 三条通り店
name_ja: 大和野菜と地酒 大和乃山賊 三条通り店
name_en: Yamato Yasai to Jizake Yamato no Sanzoku Sanjo-dori-ten
cuisine: 大和野菜・地酒居酒屋（郷土料理・釜飯）
neighborhood: 油阪町・三条通り／JR奈良駅東口 徒歩約2分
gf_detail: This is a Japanese izakaya built around fish-dashi cooking, soy-sauce seasoning, and grilled/fried small plates, with no documented gluten-free program. Soy sauce, miso, and frying are pervasive, and there is no published allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac diners; a coeliac traveler should not rely on this venue.
vegan_detail: The menu features Yamato (Nara) vegetables and home-style おばんざい small plates, so some plant-based vegetable dishes exist, but as a typical izakaya most dishes use fish dashi, and the signature kamameshi pot rice is made with seasonal fish. There is no dedicated vegan or vegetarian menu and no documented allergen/animal-product handling, so a vegan would need to ask carefully and options would be very limited.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市油阪町11-4 第一中村ビル3F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市油阪町11-4 returned [135.82103, 34.682484], within the central Nara box and consistent with the JR Nara Station east-side location. Note: the target listed the neighborhood as 油阪地方町, but the official site and listings place the building in 油阪町11-4.
seats_text: Official site states 60 total seats with all-private sunken-tatami (掘りごたつ) rooms for 2–20 (up to ~60 for the whole space); one listing cites 83 seats.
hours_raw: Official site: Mon–Fri 16:30–24:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays 16:00–24:00. Tabelog: Mon–Thu 16:30–23:00; Fri & day-before-holiday 16:30–00:00; Sat 16:00–00:00; Sun & holidays 16:00–23:00. Irregular closures.
closed_days: No fixed regular closing day; irregular closures (不定休).
cultural_comfort_note: This is a local private-room izakaya focused on Nara sake and dishes — a foreign student will be most comfortable with some Japanese, as no English-language menu or service is documented and reservations are handled in Japanese.
caveats: Two payment sources conflict: Tabelog lists cash only, while the official site lists VISA, MasterCard, and PayPay — confirm at the door. Seat count and exact daily closing times also differ between the official site and Tabelog. A separate per-person service/seating charge applies. The restaurant opened April 14, 2025.

bio.background: A private-room izakaya near the east exit of JR Nara Station that opened in April 2025, devoted to pairing locally grown Yamato (Nara) vegetables with the prefecture's sake. The space is designed in a calm wa-modern style with sunken-tatami private rooms suited to both small groups and larger gatherings.
bio.specialty: Seasonal kamameshi (clay-pot rice) featuring the catch of the season is the signature dish, served alongside home-style おばんざい small plates of Yamato vegetables and hearth-grilled fare.
bio.philosophy: The house theme is the marriage of food and sake — Nara-grown produce and regional dishes built to be enjoyed with a deep, owner-curated selection of around 30 local Nara sakes.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor personally curates a rotating lineup of roughly 30 local Nara sakes chosen to pair with the seasonal menu.
Local Nara news announced the shop's opening on April 14, 2025 near JR Nara Station.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Japanese izakaya serving grilled and simmered dishes, soy sauce (typically wheat-based) and miso are used throughout the menu; no gluten-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Most izakaya dishes here rely on fish dashi and the signature kamameshi uses seasonal fish, so vegetable plates may share fish-based stock; no animal-free preparation is documented.

positives.text: The menu centers on Yamato (Nara) vegetables and home-style おばんざい small plates, so some genuinely vegetable-forward dishes are available to ask about.

cuisine: Handmade hamburgers and sandwiches (independent burger shop with house-cured bacon, ham, and sausage)
neighborhood: Kitamachi, central Nara (near Kintetsu Nara Station / Naramachi area)
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The core product is a burger served on a wheat bun, so it is not celiac-safe. An allergen chart exists on the menu but its contents are not published online; gluten-free needs must be confirmed directly with the shop.
vegan_detail: A listing notes vegetarian options are available, but no vegan item is documented and the menu specifics are not published. The kitchen centers on house-cured pork (bacon, ham, sausage) and domestic beef patties, so a meat-free or fully vegan order should be confirmed with staff in advance.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向北町6
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市東向北町6 returned [135.82869, 34.684891], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 20 seats.
hours_raw: 9:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30); morning menu 9:00-11:00. Closed Wednesday and Thursday.
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, welcoming small burger counter a couple of minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will see you through, though detailed dietary questions are easier with a little Japanese.

bio.background: Sakura Burger is a small, owner-run hamburger shop in Nara's Kitamachi quarter, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. Its defining feature is house-cured charcuterie: the owner makes the bacon by rubbing spices directly into pork belly, aging it at low temperature, and slow-smoking it over cherry-wood chips using an additive-free dry-cure method. As bacon production outgrew the shop, the owner set up a dedicated smokehouse, Asuka Smokeworks, in Asuka Village, where the bacon, ham, sausage, and salami are now made.
bio.specialty: The signature is the namesake Sakura Burger, built on thick-cut house-smoked bacon; patties are 100% domestic beef (about 150g), grilled over lava stone and finished with the shop's own smoked soy sauce. Burgers come with potatoes, and there is a morning menu, a kids' menu, hot dogs, and takeout for eating in nearby Nara Park.
