bio.philosophy: A commitment to handmade, additive-free, dry-cured meats and seasonal Nara ingredients, with the owner emphasizing keeping up both quality and personal health to continue making burgers.

anecdotes.text: The shop's house bacon is dry-cured by rubbing spices into pork belly, low-temperature aged, then slow-smoked over cherry-wood chips with no additives.
When bacon-making outgrew the shop, the owner opened a separate smokehouse, Asuka Smokeworks, in Asuka Village to produce the bacon, ham, and sausage.
The signature burger uses a 150g 100% domestic-beef patty grilled on lava stone and dressed with house-made smoked soy sauce, and sometimes sells out.

staff_allergy_handling.text: An allergen information chart is included on the in-shop menu, but its contents are not published online.

positives.text: A vegetarian option is noted as available among the offerings.

caveats: Address is in Kitamachi (東向北町), the central Nara district just north of the main Naramachi area, near Kintetsu Nara Station. Vegetarian availability is noted on a listing but not detailed on the official menu; no vegan or gluten-free items are documented, so dietary needs should be confirmed with the shop. Seat count and payment methods are from the Tabelog listing.

cuisine: Ramen / tsukemen (chicken-based broth)
neighborhood: Mochiidono Center Street (Mochiidono shopping arcade), about 7 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市光明院町9
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.829086, 34.679798] for 奈良県奈良市光明院町9; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 11 seats (7 counter seats + 1 table of 4); all non-smoking
hours_raw: Mon/Thu/Fri 11:00-14:00, 18:00-20:30 (L.O. 20:15); Tue 11:00-14:30; Sat/Sun/Holidays 11:00-16:00. Closes early when ingredients run out.
closed_days: Wednesday (also no dinner service Tuesday); may close early when sold out
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter ramen shop with a ticket-style ordering flow and quick turnover; some basic Japanese helps, but pointing at the menu works fine for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The ramen and tsukemen use house-made wheat noodles, and ramen broths/tare typically contain wheat-based soy sauce, so this is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature dishes are built on chicken broth and topped with pork chashu; there are no documented vegan options.
caveats: Cash only and no reservations; small 11-seat counter, so expect a queue. Closes early once the day's broth/ingredients sell out. No dinner service on Tuesday and closed Wednesday.

bio.background: The shop is run by an owner-chef surnamed Kitao (the K in Menya K), who previously worked as a manager at the well-known Osaka ramen shop Ramen Jinsei JET before going independent. He opened Menya K in January 2020 in a renovated old townhouse on Nara's Mochiidono shopping arcade.
bio.specialty: Chicken-based ramen with a rich, golden chicken broth, house-made noodles, and pork shoulder chashu, alongside a chicken tsukemen (dipping noodles) with concentrated chicken dipping broth and a limited-quantity miso tsukemen. Noodles, broth, and chashu are all made in-house.
bio.philosophy: A focus on house-made everything — noodles, broth, and toppings — to express a deep but clean chicken umami.

anecdotes.text: The name Menya K comes from the owner's surname initial, and he built the shop after training and managing at the popular Osaka shop Ramen Jinsei JET.
Set in a renovated historic Nara townhouse on Mochiidono Center Street, the small counter shop draws locals, tourists, and overseas visitors alike.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shoyu (soy ramen) and tare are soy-sauce based, which in ramen is standard wheat-containing soy sauce.

anecdotes.text: Quiet, reserved proprietor with a creative streak shown in his hand-designed shop logo.

bio.background: A family-style pure kissaten in the Sugicho neighborhood near JR Nara Station since the Showa era (founded around 1968). A vintage facade lettered coffee and juice opens onto a classical interior that feels as if time stopped.
bio.specialty: Blend coffee in indigo cups, Kansai-style mixed juice, buttered toast, omelette/open-faced sandwiches, plus lunch curry, tempura soba and shokado bento.
bio.philosophy: A kissaten is a place to savor time itself, valuing atmosphere over flashy flavor.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by toast, sandwiches, soba and curry; wheat is present throughout the kitchen and no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg and dairy items (omelette sandwiches, buttered toast, milk coffee) are core; no vegan preparation documented.

cuisine: 純喫茶・喫茶店（昭和レトロな個人経営の老舗喫茶。モーニング・ランチ、オムライス、サイフォンコーヒー）
neighborhood: 奈良市大宮町（三条通り沿い、JR奈良駅から徒歩約5分）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市大宮町2-3
geocode_note: GSI address-search on 奈良県奈良市大宮町2-3 returned [135.814758, 34.682575], within the Nara box and consistent with the three-jō-dōri / JR Nara Station location.
seats_text: 28 seats: 6 counter seats, plus table seating (one 6-person table, four 4-person tables).
closed_days: Wednesdays (and the third Tuesday of the month, per a local guide).
cultural_comfort_note: A homey old-school neighborhood coffee shop run in Japanese with no posted English; a foreign student would be warmly received but should be ready to point at the menu or use a translation app.
gf_detail: This is a classic Japanese kissaten built around toast, sandwiches, hot dogs, spaghetti, curry and ketchup-rice omurice — wheat is everywhere (bread, pasta, likely roux in the curry) and there is no gluten-free accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on eggs, beef, demi-glace and dairy (omurice with scrambled egg and beef, egg sandwiches, boiled eggs, milk coffee). No vegan dishes are indicated and the kitchen does not cater to plant-based diets.
caveats: Hours can be irregular (closed Wednesdays plus reportedly the third Tuesday); confirm before a special trip. No parking. Cash only.

bio.background: リッチ is a long-running Showa-era kissaten on the ground floor of an apartment building along Nara's Sanjo-dori, an easy walk from JR Nara Station. It keeps the feel of an old-fashioned pure coffee house (junkissa) while serving a broad menu from early-morning sets through lunch and afternoon coffee.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is the omurice: fluffy scrambled egg over ketchup rice with onions, mushrooms and beef, finished with a sweet demi-glace (a white-sauce version is also offered). Siphon-brewed coffee, praised for its strong aroma, is a house point of pride, alongside morning sets, sandwiches, spaghetti and curry.

anecdotes.text: The morning sets (toast, half-sandwich, or hot dog) each come with a boiled egg, mini salad, fruit and coffee or tea, and are a popular, affordable way to start the day in central Nara.
Regulars and walk-in visitors alike are drawn by the homey atmosphere and the aromatic siphon coffee.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-heavy kissaten kitchen (bread, pasta, sandwiches, likely flour-thickened curry) handling shared cookware; no gluten-free protocol indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg, dairy and meat are central to nearly every dish; no plant-based separation indicated.

name_ja: koharu cafe（コハル カフェ）
cuisine: 古民家カフェ（Machiya café — lunch plates, beef curry, cheesecake, specialty coffee）
neighborhood: きたまち / Kitamachi (near Tōdai-ji Tegaimon, north of Kintetsu Nara Station)
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature menu centers on wheat-based baked goods (New York cheesecake, chiffon cake) and the lunch plates/curry are served with rice but accompanied by miso soup and other dishes that typically use wheat-containing seasonings. No celiac accommodation is documented, and as a solo-run kitchen there is high cross-contact risk. Not suitable for those needing gluten-free.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. The weekly one-plate lunch features seasonal Yamato vegetables (inari, rape blossoms, squash, mountain yam) which could be partly plant-based, but it comes with miso soup and the kitchen also serves a beef-tendon curry; dashi and dairy (cheesecake, milk in coffee) are used throughout. A vegan would need to ask the owner directly about the day's vegetable plate, with no guarantee.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東包永町61
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.832779, 34.691952] for 奈良県奈良市東包永町61; within the Nara box.
seats_text: About 13 seats (roughly 10 table seats and 3 counter seats); some sources describe it as around 15 seats total.
hours_raw: 11:00-17:00; closed Thursday and Friday
closed_days: Thursday, Friday
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, solo-run café tucked down narrow residential alleys with no apparent English menu, so a few words of Japanese (or a translation app) and patience finding the door will help; the owner asks "food or café?" on arrival.
caveats: Hidden down narrow alleyways in a residential pocket of Kitamachi and easy to miss; since one person runs the whole place, calling ahead to reserve is appreciated and service can be slow when busy. Card acceptance (AMEX noted) is listed but bring cash as a backup for a small place like this.

bio.background: koharu cafe occupies a roughly 100-year-old wooden machiya tucked down the narrow residential alleys of Nara's Kitamachi district, near the Tegaimon gate of Tōdai-ji and just north of the city's old town. The owner renovated the old house around a 'white wood' palette so that large windows fill the rooms with soft daylight, and runs the entire place single-handedly.
bio.specialty: Signature offerings are the baked cheesecakes — a simple New York style and a version made by melting white chocolate into the batter — alongside a beef curry simmered with beef tendon over two to three days, weekly one-plate lunches built around seasonal Yamato vegetables, and specialty coffee (including a French blend of Mandheling, Colombian and Mocha beans).
bio.philosophy: The owner's stated wish is simple: that guests who make the effort to find this hard-to-reach spot leave smiling and feeling it was worth the trip, as if they had just visited someone's home.

anecdotes.text: On arrival the owner asks each guest whether they have come 'for food or for café,' a small ritual that reflects how the space flexes between a quiet lunch spot and a relaxed coffee house.
The beef curry is slow-cooked with beef tendon and aromatic vegetables over two to three days before it reaches the table.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Solo-run kitchen producing wheat-based cakes (cheesecake, chiffon) and grain/wheat-seasoned lunch dishes in a small shared space; high gluten cross-contact risk and no documented gluten-free handling.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Beef-tendon curry and dairy desserts are prepared in the same one-person kitchen as the vegetable lunch plate; no separation for plant-based diets is documented.

positives.text: Weekly one-plate lunch is built around seasonal Yamato vegetables, so a plant-forward option may be available on a given day if you ask the owner.

lead: やちむんカフェ おことパーラー
name_ja: やちむんカフェ おことパーラー
cuisine: カフェ・かき氷（沖縄やちむんと独創的かき氷。本部産パッションフルーツ・アセロラ・黒糖・ふーちばー、島とうふ、古都華など）
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a kakigori (shaved ice) and cake cafe; kakigori syrups can be naturally wheat-free but the menu also features cakes, tarts, ちんすこう (Okinawan shortbread, made with flour) and other baked items that contain wheat, and there is no allergen labeling or dedicated handling described. A celiac would need to ask in person about each item and should not assume cross-contact control.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. Some kakigori use fruit, brown sugar and island-tofu elements that could be plant-based, but the cafe serves dairy cakes and custard/cream-topped shaved ice, and no vegan menu or dairy-free preparation is documented. A vegan would need to ask directly which fruit-based items can be made without dairy/cream.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市坊屋敷町42 石原ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI on the building address 奈良県奈良市坊屋敷町42; result lat 34.68676, lng 135.828033 falls within the central Nara box and matches the Kitamachi area north of Kintetsu Nara Station, near Nara Women's University.
seats_text: Seat count not documented; a small individually-run café on the 1st floor of the Ishihara Building in Kitamachi.
hours_raw: Business hours and closed days are not publicly listed on the cafe's official Instagram or aggregator pages; the cafe directs customers to check Instagram (@ocotoparlour) for current operating and closure information.
closed_days: Not publicly documented; varies — confirm via Instagram.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a small family-run Japanese café where the owner and a resident dog set a relaxed, personal mood — a little conversational Japanese helps and visitors with dog allergies should know a dog lives on the premises.
caveats: Cash only. Hours and closed days are not published and change seasonally — check the @ocotoparlour Instagram before visiting. A resident dog named Okoto lives in the café, so those with dog allergies or discomfort around dogs should take care. No documented gluten-free or vegan program; ask in person about individual items.

bio.background: A small, family-feeling café tucked into Nara's historic Kitamachi quarter, north of Kintetsu Nara Station and a short walk from Nara Women's University. The owner brings a slice of Okinawa to the old streets of Nara: gentle Ryukyuan music plays inside, and the shelves are lined with yachimun — Okinawan pottery with roughly 700 years of history. The owner travels to Okinawa to buy directly from about ten different kilns, so the bowls range from finely designed pieces to ones with loose, casual brushwork, and the shaved ice is served in these handmade yachimun bowls.
bio.origin: Nara (Kitamachi), with a strong Okinawan cultural theme
bio.specialty: Inventive kakigori built on Okinawan ingredients. A year-round signature pairs Motobu-grown passion fruit and acerola with brown sugar and custard syrup, finished with the herbal fragrance of fuchiba (Okinawan mugwort). Other creations include an Okoto Mango kakigori using local 古都華 strawberry and mango sauce over a green fuchiba layer and topped with chinsuko (Okinawan shortbread) for crunch and salt, plus an Island Tofu Tiramisu kakigori; cakes and fruit tarts round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: To let guests slow down on 'Okinawan time' in the middle of Nara — enjoying creative shaved ice in handmade pottery while soaking up Okinawan music, ingredients and craft.

anecdotes.text: The café is named after the owner's beloved dog, Okoto, who also serves as the shop's 'manager dog' and greets guests inside.
The yachimun on display are bought in person from around ten Okinawan kilns, and the kakigori is served in these very bowls.
The flagship shaved ice uses passion fruit and acerola grown in Motobu, Okinawa, balanced with brown sugar and the herbal scent of fuchiba (mugwort).

staff_allergy_handling.text: The cafe notes a dog allergy concern rather than a food-allergy program: a resident dog lives in the cafe, and they advise that people with dog allergies or who are uncomfortable around dogs should take care.

positives.text: Several kakigori are built on whole fruit, brown sugar and herbs (Motobu passion fruit, acerola, fuchiba mugwort, local strawberry, mango), which may suit some diners, though dairy cream/custard and wheat-based toppings like chinsuko are also used.

cuisine: カフェ・かき氷（モーニング／ランチ／空・雲をテーマにしたスイーツ）
gf_detail: This is not a gluten-free destination and is not suitable for celiacs. The menu is wheat-heavy: morning buffet centers on toast (gratin toast, French toast, plain toast), lunch features pasta (meat spaghetti, Napolitan) and copper-plate hot cakes, and there is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated prep area, or fryer. No GF accommodation is mentioned on any source.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly spot. The breakfast buffet is built around eggs (scrambled eggs), bacon, yogurt and dairy; lunch pasta and hot cakes use dairy and egg; signature sweets like the cloud pudding (雲の上のプリン) and cream soda use dairy, and the kakigori toppings include vanilla ice cream and cotton candy. No vegan menu or plant-based options are documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高天町46-1 変なホテル 奈良 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 奈良県奈良市高天町46番地, coordinates [135.826431, 34.683792], within the central Nara box. Matches the Henna Hotel Nara location near Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: 42 seats including 4 terrace seats (per official hotel site)
hours_raw: Morning 7:00-10:00 (L.O. 9:30); Lunch 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:30); Cafe 9:30-17:00 (L.O. 16:30). Tabelog lists Tuesday hours as 07:00-10:00 only.
closed_days: No regular closing day listed; Tuesday operates morning-only (7:00-10:00).
cultural_comfort_note: As the casual café inside an international hotel near Kintetsu Nara Station, a foreign student will find a relaxed, tourist-friendly setting where basic Japanese greetings and pointing at the picture menu work fine, though staff may have limited English.
caveats: Hours vary: Tuesday is morning-only (closes 10:00). Seat reservations are not accepted (only food can be pre-ordered), so seating is first-come. Located on the 1F of Henna Hotel Premier Nara, so it functions partly as a hotel breakfast venue. Cash-only status not confirmed; cards, e-money and PayPay are accepted.

bio.background: A weather-and-sky themed cafe and kakigori parlor near Kintetsu Nara Station. Step inside and the room is washed in soft pastel blue, designed to feel as though you are floating above the clouds or drifting through the sky. From early morning the place hums as a breakfast spot, then shifts through lunch into an afternoon dessert haven.
bio.specialty: Sky- and cloud-themed sweets are the draw: the 'pudding above the clouds' (雲の上のプリン) and the much-loved sky cream soda (空のクリームソーダ), alongside seasonal weather-named shaved ice. The three signature kakigori are styled after the forecast: Hare (sunny), Kumori (cloudy), and Ame (rainy), the rainy one layered with cider-flavored blue ice, Kyoho grape syrup, amber candy, vanilla ice cream, apple jelly and a tuft of cotton candy. Mornings bring a buffet with choose-your-own toast, fluffy scrambled eggs and bacon, a salad bar, soup, and a soft-drink bar.
bio.philosophy: The whole experience is organized around the playful idea of celebrating every kind of weather, turning sun, cloud and rain into something cute and delicious.

anecdotes.text: The interior is painted in pale pastel blue to evoke the sensation of being up among the clouds, matching a menu of weather-motif dishes.

A visiting kakigori reviewer described the 'Ame' (rain) shaved ice as a multi-layered creation topped with cotton candy, with a cider-flavored blue surface and Kyoho grape syrup beneath, served in about nine minutes.

cuisine: Hand-made buckwheat soba (jūwari / 100% buckwheat, no binder; buckwheat sourced from across Japan and stone-milled in-house daily)
gf_detail: The noodles themselves are jūwari (10-wari) — 100% buckwheat with no wheat binder (つなぎなし), which is unusual and means the soba itself is naturally wheat-free. However, this is NOT a celiac-safe or gluten-free establishment: the dipping broth (tsuyu) and most accompaniments rely on soy sauce, which contains wheat, and there is no indication of a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, separate boiling water, or cross-contamination controls. Soba shops in Japan routinely boil noodles in shared water and serve wheat-based sides. A wheat-sensitive diner could potentially eat the pure-buckwheat noodles but should ask directly about the broth and cross-contact; celiacs should treat this as not safe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. Traditional soba is served with a fish-dashi (bonito/kombu) based tsuyu, so the standard dishes are not vegan. Some items such as そばがきぜんざい (soba-cake sweet) and そばプリン (soba pudding) may be plant-leaning but composition is undocumented. A vegan would need to ask about dashi and any animal ingredients; assume the broth contains fish.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市押熊町2201-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the Nara bounding box (lat 34.718105, lng 135.765869). Location is in the Oshikuma district of northwest Nara along Narayama-ōdōri, a short distance from central Nara / Kintetsu Nara Station.
neighborhood: 押熊町 / ならやま大通り沿い（奈良市北西部, Oshikuma, along Narayama-ōdōri, northwest Nara）
seats_text: 18 seats (small owner-run shop)
hours_raw: 11:30-14:30, 17:30-20:30 (Wednesday lunch only 11:30-14:30; closed Thursday)
closed_days: Thursday (all day); Wednesday evening (lunch only on Wednesday)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional owner-run soba house where ordering is in Japanese and the atmosphere is quiet and local — knowing basic Japanese phrases and dining etiquette will make the visit comfortable.
caveats: Cash only. Small 18-seat shop, so it can fill up; portions are limited because soba is milled and made fresh daily and may sell out. Located in northwest Nara (Oshikuma) along Narayama-ōdōri rather than near central Kintetsu Nara Station — nearest access is via bus toward Gakuen-mae or Gakken Nara-Tomigaoka. Smoking was permitted indoors per listings; confirm current policy.

bio.background: Sobaya Kiminami is a small, independent soba house that opened in 2008 along Narayama-ōdōri in the Oshikuma district of northwest Nara. The owner first trained in soba-making at Norikura Kogen in Nagano, the heartland of Shinshu soba, then learned stone-milling techniques at a shop near Taima-dera temple in Nara (since closed) before opening this place.
bio.origin: Nara (with training in Nagano's Norikura Kogen)
bio.specialty: Jūwari (100% buckwheat) soba made with no binder. Buckwheat is carefully sourced from producers across Japan and stone-milled in-house each day, with the portion ground adjusted to the season, temperature and weather. Two signature noodles are offered: inaka-soba (rustic, dark, hull-inclusive) and seiro-soba (refined, with greenish tones). Soba-based sweets such as soba-gaki zenzai and soba pudding (sobapurin) round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: Because everything is hand-made in small daily batches, the shop openly notes that the flavor of the soba differs every time — a deliberate embrace of seasonal, made-that-day craft over consistency.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained at Norikura Kogen in Nagano, then studied self-milling at a soba shop near Taima-dera in Nara before opening Kiminami in 2008.
The shop sources buckwheat seasonally from across Japan — one documented batch came from Otoineppu Village in Hokkaido — and stone-mills only the day's portion fresh.
The interior pairs a triangular-roof atrium evoking Shinshu with traditional Japanese cabinetry alongside Western chandeliers and ceiling fans, giving the room a quietly retro character.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No documented gluten-free protocols. Although the noodles are 100% buckwheat with no wheat binder, soba shops typically boil noodles in shared water and serve wheat-containing sides and broth; no separate prep is indicated.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with a traditional tsuyu dipping broth, which is normally made with soy sauce containing wheat. No gluten-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Traditional soba tsuyu uses fish-based dashi; no vegan broth or animal-free preparation is documented.

positives.text: The noodles are explicitly jūwari (100% buckwheat) with no wheat binder (つなぎなし), so the soba itself is naturally free of wheat — unusual among soba shops.

cuisine: 天然酵母・全粒粉の自家製パン（個人経営ベーカリー、イートインあり）
neighborhood: 高畑町（奈良公園南側・近鉄奈良駅／春日大社方面）
gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery — its entire product line is bread made from domestic wheat and whole-grain wheat flour, so it is not suitable for celiacs or anyone avoiding gluten. No gluten-free options are documented.
vegan_detail: Some items appear plant-based (e.g. a soymilk scone, baguettes, rye and whole-grain loaves), but croissants and cream-filled breads contain dairy/egg, and the egg/butter content of other loaves is not documented. A vegan visitor should ask item by item. No verified vegan labeling exists.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1076-1 春藤第2ハイツ 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市高畑町1076 returned [135.835144, 34.678379], within the Nara box.
seats_text: 11 seats: 3 counter, 2 table, 6 terrace (eat-in area; no drinks served per one source)
hours_raw: 10:00-18:30 (per Tabelog, Tue-Sat)
closed_days: Monday and Sunday per Tabelog; note that some local blogs list Tuesday/Wednesday as closed days, so hours/closures may vary — confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood bakery with minimal English and cash-only payment, so basic Japanese or pointing at the breads will make ordering smoother.
caveats: Hours and closed days differ between sources (Tabelog: Tue-Sat 10:00-18:30, closed Mon/Sun; some blogs: closed Tue/Wed). Cash only. The eat-in space is small and reportedly does not serve drinks.

bio.background: Nomokemana is a tiny artisan bakery tucked into the Takabatake neighborhood on the southern edge of Nara Park, near the Fukuchiin-kita intersection. It opened in May 2009, slowly building breads from its own naturally cultivated yeast and domestic wheat. The shop has a warm, wood-lined interior and a small eat-in corner, and visitors have described a family atmosphere, on one occasion seeing the owner tending to an infant behind the counter.
bio.specialty: Slow-fermented breads made with house-cultivated natural yeast (levain) and domestic wheat, with whole-grain flour milled from pesticide-free, organically grown wheat blended into every loaf. Signatures include the tangy 'natural-yeast levain' hard bread made with wild yeast from Ikoma-grown grapes, baguettes, whole-grain loaves, rye bread, croissants, and a gently sweet soymilk scone.
bio.philosophy: Unhurried, honest bread: home-cultured yeast, domestic and organically grown grain, and whole-grain flour in everything, letting the wheat's own aroma and a subtle natural sweetness come through.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name 'Nomokemana' is the Japanese word for sloth, 'namakemono,' spelled backwards — a playful nod echoed in the shop's signage illustrations.
About 30 kinds of bread are baked, from a humble baguette to walnut and rye loaves, all carrying organically milled whole-grain flour.
A reviewer praised the soymilk scone for its simple, gentle sweetness and pleasant wheat aroma, in a calm wood-warmed interior.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-based bakery; all products are wheat bread, so there is pervasive gluten throughout and no celiac-safe options.

positives.text: Breads are made with domestic wheat and whole-grain flour milled from pesticide-free, organically grown grain, with house-cultivated natural yeast.

lead: ならまちパン工房 okage
name_ja: ならまちパン工房 okage
cuisine: Organic bakery
neighborhood: Sugigacho, near JR Nara Station east / Naramachi
gf_detail: This is a wheat bakery. Every product is made from domestic wheat flour, so nothing here is gluten-free and the entire space is full of flour. Not suitable for celiacs or anyone avoiding gluten.
vegan_detail: No vegan labeling is documented. Many signature items clearly contain dairy and eggs (cream pan, melon pan, rum-raisin cream, chocolate). Some lean items like the rustic stone-ground loaf or focaccia may be plant-based, but ingredients are not published — you would need to ask in person, and the kitchen handles dairy/egg throughout.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市杉ヶ町14-1 第1西田ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to chō-level (杉ヶ町14-1); coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Takeout-only bakery; no in-store seating.
hours_raw: 10:00～売り切れ次第終了（10:00 until sold out）
closed_days: Mondays, Thursdays, Fridays (and occasional maintenance closures announced on social media)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny neighborhood bakery where you point at what you want and pay at the counter — easy even with no Japanese, but bread sells out fast so go in the late morning.

bio.background: okage is a small, owner-run organic bakery that opened in May 2021 just east of JR Nara Station on the edge of the Naramachi district. The shop bakes everything by hand in a compact space and is known for selling out quickly, so loyal customers come early.
bio.specialty: House-baked breads made entirely with domestic Japanese wheat and organic yeast — signature items include the okage loaf, a rustic stone-ground rustique, multigrain melon pan, cream pan, focaccia, chocolate breads, and a rum-raisin cream.
bio.philosophy: Because bread is something you eat every single day, the bakery carefully chooses every ingredient — the yeast, the wheat, and everything else — by its origin and safety, aiming for "bread that makes both heart and body happy."

anecdotes.text: The shop's guiding line is "because it's something you eat every day" (毎日食べるものだから), the reason given for sourcing organic yeast and domestic wheat.
A local review singled out the hard-bread selection and called one caramel-and-nut creation "a blissful item" (至福の1品).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Entirely a wheat bakery using domestic wheat flour; flour is present throughout and no gluten-free products exist.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Breads are baked in a shared kitchen that handles dairy and eggs (cream pan, melon pan, rum-raisin cream), so cross-contact is likely.

positives.text: Owner emphasizes carefully selected, origin-conscious ingredients — domestic wheat and organic yeast — for everyday eating.

caveats: Hours are "until sold out" with no fixed close; the 17:00 end is an approximation — the shop closes whenever bread runs out, often earlier. Closed days (Mon/Thu/Fri) and occasional maintenance closures are announced on Instagram. No vegan/gluten-free or detailed allergy information is published; verify in person.

lead: ブーランジェリー リッシュ
name_ja: ブーランジェリー リッシュ
cuisine: ベーカリー（独立系パン屋）
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅・JR奈良駅周辺
gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery — its core products are bread, loaves, croissants, melon pan and sandwiches, all made from wheat. No gluten-free products, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation is documented anywhere. Not suitable for anyone with celiac disease or a gluten allergy.
vegan_detail: No vegan products or labeling are documented. The signature items are dairy- and egg-rich — coffee anpan and Yamato-tea condensed-milk anpan use whipped/sweetened cream, the caramel melon pan is butter-heavy, and there are custard danishes and fruit tarts. A plain baguette-style loaf might happen to be animal-product-free, but this is unconfirmed; vegans should ask directly about specific items.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市角振町5番地
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市角振町5; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Primarily a takeout bakery; seat count not published. A small coffee/eat-in option is mentioned (coffee discounted with a bread purchase) but no defined seating is documented.
hours_raw: 月～土 08:30-19:00、日曜休み（食べログ本店情報）
closed_days: 日曜日（Sundays）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual neighborhood bakery where you can browse the trays, point to what you want, and pay at the counter — a smile and a "konnichiwa" go a long way, and little Japanese is needed.
caveats: Hours/closed day reflect the central (本店) location; this is a small local chain with other branches that keep different schedules. Confirm same-day on the shop's Instagram before a special trip.

bio.origin: Nara City, near Kintetsu Nara Station
bio.background: A small French-style bakery tucked just off Nara's Sanjo-dori shopping street, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. Fresh bread comes out of the oven again and again through the day, so the trays are constantly being refilled. The team pairs trained bread artisans with pastry-chef talent, which lets the shop offer both savory rolls and refined sweets beyond what a typical bakery carries. It has since grown into a small local family of shops around the Nara area.
bio.philosophy: The shop wants choosing bread to feel exciting and joyful — for the look and the taste to satisfy together so customers leave thinking 'I want to eat that bread again.'
bio.specialty: The flagship 'Hojun' shokupan loaf, chewy with a natural wheat sweetness, has been a signature since the shop's founding. Other beloved items include the 'coffee anpan' (billed as an 'eating cafe latte,' filled with coffee and cream), the Yamato-tea condensed-milk anpan stamped with a cute Nara deer, a butter-rich caramel melon pan with homemade caramel inside, plus seasonal fruit danishes and tarts. Savory choices like mentaiko-potato croissants and vegetable-cheese focaccia make it a popular lunch stop.

anecdotes.text: The Yamato-tea condensed-milk anpan is finished with a branded image of a Nara deer, a nod to the city's famous residents.
Buy bread and a coffee drops to about 93 yen, and the adjacent Shibata parking lot offers a discount with validation — small touches aimed at making a quick visit easy.

gf_cross_contamination.text: An active wheat-flour bakery with flour airborne and on all surfaces; cross-contamination is unavoidable and there are no gluten-free products.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature breads and pastries use cream, butter, custard and red-bean fillings, so dairy and egg are pervasive in the product mix.

lead: コンフェクション アーティザナル ブレッド&スイーツ
name_ja: コンフェクション アーティザナル ブレッド&スイーツ
cuisine: アルチザン系パン＆焼き菓子・ケーキ（個人店）
neighborhood: ならまち・近鉄奈良方面（中院町）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市中院町3-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Takeout only — no in-store seating. Customers commonly enjoy items at nearby Sarusawa Pond or Nara Park benches.
hours_raw: 8:00-17:00 (some sources list to ~17:30). Closed Tuesday and Wednesday (one source lists Wednesday plus irregular closures).
closed_days: 火曜・水曜（不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed grab-and-go bakery counter where a smile and a few words of Japanese go a long way; the team has international experience and is used to serving foreign visitors, so pointing at what you want works fine.
vegan_detail: A wheat-based artisan bakery; the croissants are made with French fermented butter (dairy), and many breads and pastries contain butter, eggs, cream cheese, or honey. The croissants are noted as egg-free but still use butter, so there are no confirmed fully vegan items.
gf_detail: This is a flour-based bread and pastry bakery built around French and Japanese wheat (baguettes, shokupan, croissants, curry pan). It is not suitable for celiac or gluten-free diets, and no gluten-free products are mentioned.
caveats: Hours and closed days vary slightly between sources (8:00-17:00 vs ~17:30; closed Tue+Wed vs Wed plus irregular closures); confirm before visiting. Takeout only. Stock is fullest late morning, after about 11:00.

bio.background: Founded by entrepreneurs with international food experience who had previously run bakeries and done food consulting across France, Shanghai, and Taiwan before settling in Nara's historic Naramachi district. Head baker Hiroshige Honma is an internationally trained artisan who also owns the well-known Shanghai bakery 'mbd', and the shop is supervised by Yukiko Kondo, a Nara native.
bio.specialty: Artisan breads and pastries: the 'Hironetto' baguette of stone-ground French wheat blended with Japanese nishino-kaori flour and levain; three signature shokupan textures — mochiri (chewy, rice flour), sakkuri/crispy (whole grain), and shittori (moist, cream cheese); egg-free croissants made with French fermented butter; curry pan with a house spice blend; plus sweets like almond-cream amandine tarts.
bio.philosophy: Honma makes breads meant to be eaten as part of a meal, where the flavor of the wheat itself comes through, and he tailors offerings by listening to customers — observing that Japanese customers prize texture while international customers focus on taste. He also wants visitors to discover Nara's local ingredients: 奈良にはおいしい食材がたくさんあるので、それも知ってほしいですね.

anecdotes.text: The bakery weaves Nara's regional produce into its lineup — hojicha anpan made with roasted Yamato tea powder, ciabatta with Nara pickles and cream cheese, and a tart featuring Nara prefectural honey.
Head baker Hiroshige Homma adjusts his recipes to customer feedback, noting Japanese guests prioritize texture while international guests emphasize taste.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-flour bakery producing baguettes, shokupan, croissants and curry pan in a shared space; flour is pervasive and there are no gluten-free products.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Breads and pastries widely use butter, cream cheese, eggs and honey; croissants are egg-free but use French fermented butter (dairy), so vegan cross-contact is unavoidable.

lead: すき焼きと牛まぶし ももしき
name_ja: すき焼きと牛まぶし ももしき
cuisine: Sukiyaki and gyu-mabushi (Yamato wagyu beef cuisine)
neighborhood: Higashimuki-Nakamachi, near Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 〒630-8215 奈良県奈良市東向中町5-1 浅川ハーベストビル2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from chō-level address (東向中町5-1); coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Approximately 80 seats: tables for 2-8 and sunken kotatsu (horigotatsu) seating for groups up to 40, with semi-private partitioned configurations available.
hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00) daily; no regular closed day
closed_days: No regular closed day (occasional temporary closures, e.g., noted for Jul 7, 2025 and Jan 6, 2026)
cultural_comfort_note: A polished but welcoming wagyu restaurant a minute from Kintetsu Nara Station with online booking and English-friendly tourist traffic; a few words of Japanese and a reservation make a visit smooth for a foreign student.
vegan_detail: This is a Yamato wagyu beef specialist built around sukiyaki and beef rice bowls; the broth and core dishes are meat- and fish-based, and no vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. Not a vegan destination.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The signature sukiyaki broth is made with aged soy sauce (which contains wheat) plus shiitake and kombu, and gyu-mabushi is served over rice with soy-based seasonings. No gluten-free accommodations are documented, so celiac diners should avoid it.
caveats: Dietary categorization is based on documented menu (sukiyaki, gyu-mabushi, Yamato wagyu); no allergy, gluten-free, or vegan handling is described on the official site or listings. Seat count (~80) and group capacity confirm it is larger than a typical mom-and-pop, hence OMNI rather than MOM_AND_POP.

bio.origin: Nara, near Kintetsu Nara Station in the Higashimuki shopping arcade area
bio.background: A Yamato-beef sukiyaki house created to turn a trip to Nara into lasting memories, conceived around the idea of reimagining the dining of Japan's ancient capital — when the region was called Yamato — in a contemporary way.
bio.specialty: Premium Yamato beef sukiyaki using A4-ranked female cattle from a heritage Nara breed documented for centuries, simmered in a house-made broth of aged soy sauce, dried shiitake and kombu; and gyu-mabushi beef rice bowls featuring seared beef tataki, shigure-ni style, and grilled loin. Seasonal dishes pair the beef with local Nara produce such as Momotaro tomatoes.
bio.philosophy: To convey the charm of Nara through sukiyaki, expressing respect for the region by showcasing local Nara ingredients in cuisine that echoes ancient imperial dining traditions.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant sources what it calls 'Nara Star Ingredients' — local vegetables and seafood from regional producers — to complement its Yamato beef.
A sister location, Momoshiki Hanare, opened in July 2025, extending the same Yamato-beef sukiyaki concept nearby.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature sukiyaki broth is made with aged soy sauce, which contains wheat, so the core dishes are not safe for those avoiding gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is centered on wagyu beef with meat- and fish-based broths and seasonings; no vegan preparations are documented.

cuisine: 居酒屋・おでん・焼き鳥 (Izakaya specializing in oden and grilled chicken liver)
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺・餅飯殿商店街そば（奈良町）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市光明院町16 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市光明院町16 returned [135.828629, 34.679649], within the central Nara box. Location is just off Mochiidono shopping street in the Naramachi district, within walking distance of Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: Counter seating only. Tabelog lists 11 seats at a U-shaped counter; some local writeups describe roughly 20 around the U-counter. A small second-floor tatami room is available for groups by reservation.
hours_raw: Tabelog: Mon-Wed 16:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:00); Thu-Fri closed; Sat 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30) & 16:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:00); Sun 11:30-14:30 & 16:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:00). Local guides historically describe evening hours around 17:00-22:30 with irregular closures, so hours should be confirmed before visiting.
closed_days: Thursday and Friday (per Tabelog); also irregular ad-hoc closures noted by local guides.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, warm Showa-era counter bar where the staff chat with you and an English menu has been spotted, so a few words of Japanese and an open, friendly attitude will carry you a long way.
gf_detail: Not safe for celiacs. The menu centers on oden simmered in soy-sauce-and-fish-dashi broth, yakitori, and kushikatsu (deep-fried wheat-battered skewers), all of which involve wheat-based soy sauce and/or wheat batter. No gluten-free preparation, dedicated fryer, or allergen handling is documented.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. The oden broth and most dishes use fish/bonito dashi, and the house specialties are chicken liver and other meat/fish items. A few items like cold tofu (冷奴) or cucumber pickles exist, but they sit within a fish-dashi and meat-heavy kitchen with no vegan-dedicated handling.

bio.background: Sakedokoro Kura is a longtime neighborhood drinking spot tucked just off Naramachi's Mochiidono shopping street, set inside a converted old warehouse (kura). Inside, vintage signage and Showa-era decor wrap a U-shaped counter, where pots of oden simmer in front of guests and both regulars and first-timers settle in for a quiet drink.
bio.specialty: The signature draw is the oden: around ten varieties kept gently simmering at all times, sold piece by piece (roughly 100-150 yen each) with a praised egg and house mustard, so you order only what you want. The second star is the chicken liver (kimoyaki), slow-cooked rather than skewered, seasoned with a touch of sansho pepper and a sweet, concentrated richness that pairs with sake, shochu, or beer. Other plates include yakitori, kushikatsu, karaage, cold tofu, octopus in vinegar, and house-made mackerel (matsumae) sushi.
bio.philosophy: A welcoming counter where good, honestly priced food and easy conversation matter more than fuss; staff greet oden orders by asking whether you prefer pieces deeply soaked in broth or lighter.

anecdotes.text: When you order oden the staff ask 'よく染みたんがいい？淺いんがいい？' (Would you like it deeply soaked in the broth, or lighter?), letting you tune each piece to taste.
Beloved by locals for decades, the place keeps about ten kinds of oden simmering continuously at its U-shaped counter and is known for affordable, quality food in a nostalgic warehouse setting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kushikatsu and karaage are deep-fried in wheat batter/flour, and oden is simmered in a shared soy-sauce-and-dashi broth, so gluten is pervasive across the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Oden is cooked in a soy-sauce-seasoned dashi broth; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The oden broth and most dishes use fish/bonito dashi, and meat and fish items (chicken liver, mackerel sushi, octopus) share the same small counter kitchen.

caveats: Hours conflict between sources: Tabelog lists daytime/early-evening hours with Thursday-Friday closed, while local Nara food guides describe ~17:00-22:30 with irregular closures. Closures are irregular, so confirm by phone (listed as 0742-22-8771 on local guides, though one guide flags the number may be outdated) before visiting. Cash only; counter walk-in (no reservation taken for the counter, but the 2nd-floor tatami room requires reservation).

lead: 田舎料理 畔（ホトリ）
name_ja: 田舎料理 畔（ホトリ）
cuisine: Home-style countryside Japanese set lunches (teishoku) built around seasonal vegetables, mountain greens, and homegrown produce, served in a renovated old farmhouse
neighborhood: きたまち / Kawakami-cho, along the Saho River behind Todai-ji, within walking distance of Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The rotating lunch set centers on fried mains (e.g., fried horse mackerel, white fish, shrimp-and-shiitake croquettes) that use wheat batter/breading, plus miso soup and pickles that typically involve wheat-based miso and soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or allergen handling is documented. Celiac travelers should not consider this safe.
vegan_detail: Vegetable-forward but not vegetarian or vegan. The kitchen follows the "mago wa yasashii" principle, in which the "sa" stands for fish (魚), and the menu explicitly includes fish and meat dishes such as fried horse mackerel, seared tuna, pork mille-feuille, and braised hamburger. Side dishes and miso soup almost certainly use fish dashi. No vegan or vegetarian set is documented; a vegan would need to ask in advance and likely could not be accommodated.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市川上町92
geocode_note: Geocoded from full address via GSI; first feature coordinates [135.844284, 34.698227], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 20 seats across table, tatami, and terrace seating; small and intimate.
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (lunch only). Open Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat; closed Wed, Thu, Sun. Food quantities are limited, so advance inquiry/reservation is recommended.
closed_days: Wednesday, Thursday, Sunday
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny owner-run farmhouse lunch spot where almost everything is in Japanese and portions are limited, so calling ahead in Japanese to reserve and being comfortable with a simple set menu will make the visit much smoother.
caveats: Lunch only and reservation-strongly-recommended because the number of meals prepared each day is limited. Cards are not accepted (cash or PayPay only). Set menu only, with a few rotating main choices; little to no English. Not suitable for vegan, vegetarian, or gluten-free diets despite the vegetable focus.

bio.background: The owner spent about 35 years working as a childcare worker before opening this lunch spot to fulfill a long-held dream. She describes it modestly as a completely amateur venture devoted to vegetable-centered home cooking.
bio.origin: The restaurant occupies a renovated old farmhouse on the bank of the Saho River, behind Todai-ji in the quiet Kawakami-cho area; its name, Hotori, means 'the banks (of the river).' The space is filled with old farm tools and antique furniture, evoking the feeling of visiting a grandmother's house in the countryside.
bio.specialty: A single daily lunch set (with a smaller-portion option). It pairs a choice of rotating mains with five small vegetable side dishes, miso soup, pickles, and locally grown Hinohikari rice with free refills. Mains rotate among fried and braised dishes such as fried horse mackerel, white fish fries, shrimp-and-shiitake croquettes, seared tuna, pork mille-feuille, and braised hamburger.
bio.philosophy: Cooking follows the Japanese 'mago wa yasashii' principle of balanced eating across beans, seeds, seaweed, vegetables, fish, mushrooms, and root vegetables. The owner uses homegrown produce and seasonal mountain greens, and sun-dries ingredients like daikon and taro to concentrate their nutrition, aiming for simple, gentle flavors that are kind to the body.

anecdotes.text: Rice is the local Hinohikari variety grown with spring water from near the World Heritage Kasuga shrine area, and rice refills are free.
Vegetables such as daikon and taro are dried before cooking to enhance their nutritional value, reflecting the owner's focus on health and traditional preservation.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Set lunches regularly feature deep-fried mains (fried horse mackerel, white fish fries, croquettes) made with wheat batter/breading, with no indication of a separate gluten-free preparation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes include soy-marinated and braised items (e.g., soy-soaked tuna, braised hamburger) and miso soup, which in standard Japanese home cooking use wheat-containing soy sauce and miso.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen's 'mago wa yasashii' approach intentionally includes fish, and mains include fish, tuna, pork, and beef, with side dishes and miso soup likely made with fish dashi.

positives.text: Cooking is strongly vegetable- and produce-forward, using homegrown and seasonal Yamato-area vegetables and a balanced, health-minded menu philosophy.

cuisine: アジアン薬膳料理・古民家カフェ (Asian medicinal cuisine / old-townhouse café)
neighborhood: きたまち (Kitamachi, north of Kintetsu Nara Station)
gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are made and no wheat/allergen handling is documented. The cuisine draws on Thai, Vietnamese, Chinese and Korean home cooking with medicinal soups, Asian stews and curries — preparations that commonly involve soy sauce, miso or other wheat-containing seasonings. There is no dedicated gluten-free menu or stated cross-contamination control, so this is not a celiac-safe destination; a strictly gluten-free diner would need to call ahead (0742-27-1027) and ask in detail about each dish.
vegan_detail: This is a plant-forward 薬膳 (medicinal-food) café built around beans, vegetables and whole grains. The chickpea-and-root-vegetable curry lunch is explicitly described as vegetarian (ベジタリアン仕様), and herbal cakes with dates and nuts plus selected teas are offered. However, the rotating 季節の養生ランチ (seasonal wellness course) includes medicinal soups and Asian stews whose stocks/seasonings are not documented as animal-free, and "vegetarian" here does not guarantee strictly vegan (dairy/egg in desserts, possible fish-based seasonings in soups). Vegan diners should confirm dish-by-dish at the counter; the solo owner is happy to discuss ingredients.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町1232
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to lng 135.827469, lat 34.692081, within the central Nara box; consistent with the Hōren-chō / Kitamachi location north of Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: 15 seats total (11 counter, 4 table)
hours_raw: Thursday–Sunday 11:30–16:00 (lunch L.O. 14:30); closed Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A warm solo-run counter café where the owner enjoys chatting about Asian food and herbal medicine, but communication is primarily in Japanese, so a few key phrases or a translation app will help a foreign student feel at ease.
caveats: Cash only and open just four days a week (Thu–Sun, lunch only). It is a one-person operation, so service can be slow at busy times. No gluten-free or strict-vegan guarantees are documented — confirm ingredients directly if you have dietary restrictions.

bio.background: Rampu is run single-handedly by an owner who previously operated an Asian medicinal-cuisine establishment in Osaka before opening here in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district, in a relaxed space converted from a long-row townhouse roughly 100 years old.
bio.origin: Nara — Kitamachi, north of Kintetsu Nara Station
bio.specialty: Asian medicinal home cooking: a weekly-rotating seasonal wellness lunch (two vegetable dishes, a medicinal soup, an Asian stew, seasonal grain rice and a homemade dessert with tea), plus a chickpea-and-root-vegetable curry lunch, Hong Kong-style yin-yang herbal drinks (陰陽茶) and date-and-nut herbal cakes.
bio.philosophy: Built on the idea of 薬食同源 — that food and medicine share the same source — and on 漢方 (Chinese herbal) theory, drawing flavors from Thai, Vietnamese, Chinese and Korean home cooking with the aim of 'eating deliciously while keeping both mind and body well.'

anecdotes.text: The owner says: 'I'm always rushing around on my own, but chatting with customers across the counter about Asian food and herbal medicine is my daily joy.'
The café occupies a relaxed space inside a renovated long-row townhouse around 100 years old, tucked into the back lanes of Nara's Kitamachi quarter.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner runs the kitchen alone and welcomes conversation about Asian food and herbal medicine across the counter, so dietary questions can be asked directly, though no formal allergen-handling policy is published.

positives.text: The menu is centered on beans, vegetables and whole grains, with a chickpea-and-root-vegetable curry described as vegetarian and satisfying.

lead: コージー カフェ（Cozy Cafe）
cuisine: Home-style Japanese lunch (おうちごはん) with weekly-changing vegetable-forward set meals and homemade sweets
neighborhood: きたまち (Kitamachi), along Yasuragi-no-michi near Nara Women's University
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町1070-2 中嶋ビル 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI at chō level (法蓮町1070); coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: One 4-person table, four 2-person tables, and three counter seats (approx. 15 seats); intentionally intimate and small.
hours_raw: 11:30-18:00 (April–September); 11:30-17:00 (October–March). Closed Wednesday and Sunday, plus irregular closures. Lunch reservations available until 12:30 only.
closed_days: Wednesday and Sunday, plus occasional irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, warm home-style cafe where ordering is straightforward (one weekly set lunch) but staff likely speak mostly Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help a foreign student feel at ease.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. The home-style menu includes items such as hamburger and dressings, and Japanese home cooking typically uses wheat-based soy sauce and seasonings, so this is not a celiac-safe option.
vegan_detail: The lunch is vegetable-forward but not vegan: documented set meals include meat (mushroom-sauce hamburger), egg (Spanish-style omelet), tuna, and mayo-based dressings, and sweets are dairy/egg based. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented.
caveats: Lunch is a single weekly-changing set; dessert-only orders are not accepted during lunch service. Hours shorten in winter (Oct–Mar close at 17:00). Irregular closures occur, so calling ahead is wise given the small size.

bio.background: Cozy Cafe is a small individual cafe run by Cozy Planning, an architectural and interior design office based in Nara. It sits along Yasuragi-no-michi in the Kitamachi district, just past Nara Women's University and across the river, about an 8-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station's exit 5.
bio.specialty: The signature offering is the weekly-changing home-style lunch (週替わりおひるごはん) built around seasonal vegetables — for example a set with mushroom-sauce hamburger, Spanish-style omelet, tuna and greens with sesame mayo, pickled cucumber, house dressing salad, fifteen-grain rice, and carrot potage, with a free large rice portion. The cafe is also known for its rotating homemade sweets such as pumpkin pudding, blueberry crumble cheese, carrot cake, and seasonal tarts and parfaits.
bio.philosophy: Rather than special-occasion cooking, the cafe focuses on the kind of comforting 'home cooking' (おうちごはん) you would want to eat every day, paired with simple, rustic sweets. The tables, chairs, and warm Nordic-inspired interior were all handmade by the Cozy Planning team to create a genuinely cozy, lived-in space.

anecdotes.text: The interior furnishings — the tables and chairs — were all handmade by the Cozy Planning design office, and the room is decorated with dried flowers and Nordic-style décor to create a warm, intimate atmosphere.
The weekly home lunch rotates roughly every ten days and showcases seasonal vegetables, with a free large rice portion included.

positives.text: The lunch is built around plenty of seasonal vegetables, so vegetable-forward diners have substantial options even though dishes are not vegan or gluten-free.

lead: コハル カフェ（koharu cafe）
name_ja: コハル カフェ（koharu cafe）
cuisine: Renovated-townhouse café — homemade cheesecake, hand-drip coffee, and daily rotating bowl-style lunch plates
neighborhood: Kitamachi (きたまち), near Tōdai-ji Tegaimon
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Signature items are wheat-based: cheesecakes and lunch plates such as taco rice served with a tortilla. There is no indication of gluten-free options or any allergen-handling protocol, and miso soup and seasonings would carry cross-contamination risk. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The headline cheesecakes are dairy-based, and the rotating bowl lunches (taco rice, gapao rice, lu rou fan) are built around ground meat and cheese and come with miso soup (typically fish dashi). No documented plant-based menu.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東包永町61
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 奈良県奈良市東包永町61 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 13 seats total (roughly 10 table seats and 3 counter seats); sources vary between ~11 and 13.
hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (last orders around 16:40); lunch served roughly 11:00-14:00. Closed Thursdays and Fridays, plus Obon and New Year holidays and occasional irregular closures.
closed_days: Thursday and Friday (plus Obon, New Year, and occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, shoes-off townhouse café run single-handedly by one owner; menus and service are in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will make ordering smoother for a foreign student.
caveats: Hours and seat counts vary slightly between sources; lunch dishes rotate monthly. Payment: card (incl. AMEX) is accepted per listing, but bring cash as a backup at a small independent café. Closes two days a week (Thu/Fri) plus irregular holidays.

bio.background: Koharu cafe occupies a renovated old wooden townhouse tucked into a small lane in Nara's tranquil Kitamachi district, a short walk from Todai-ji's Tegaimon gate. Guests slip off their shoes at the entrance and settle into a calm, lovingly kept space of wooden floors, well-spaced tables, and a small counter, with antique music boxes playing softly and handmade crafts on display.
bio.specialty: The café is best known for its homemade New York-style cheesecake and a white-chocolate cheesecake, paired with hand-drip coffee brewed from Morihiko coffee beans. At midday it serves a homemade plate lunch that changes monthly, rotating through bowl-style dishes such as taco rice, gapao rice, and lu rou fan, served with miso soup.

anecdotes.text: The café is run single-handedly by its owner, who handles both the cooking and the service, giving the place a personal, homemade feel.
Its cheesecakes have drawn a loyal mix of regulars, from local university students to neighborhood seniors.

lead: アレ!!ル トレッフル（Allez!! Le Trèfle）
name_ja: アレ!!ル トレッフル
cuisine: Wine bar pouring Japanese (domestic) and French wines, with seasonal small plates built around Yamato vegetables and Nara sake lees
neighborhood: Kitamachi (Kitabukuromachi), north of Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市北袋町32-5
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from address 奈良県奈良市北袋町32-5; first feature coordinates [135.829117, 34.689808], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 12 seats, counter seating only
hours_raw: Mon/Tue/Thu 17:00-23:00; Fri/Sat/Sun & holidays 15:00-23:00 (last order 23:00). Closed Wednesdays plus occasional irregular closures. (Note: Tabelog's Japanese listing lists Mon-Tue/Thu-Fri 17:00 open and Sat-Sun 15:00 open; sources differ slightly on Friday opening time.)
closed_days: Wednesdays, plus occasional irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny 12-seat counter wine bar run by a sommelier; comfort with some Japanese and a relaxed bar conversation will help, though the welcoming atmosphere suits a curious solo visitor.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu of wine-pairing small plates is not designed for celiacs, and dishes such as braised beef-tongue in red wine and beef-stroganoff style preparations typically involve flour/sauces; anyone gluten-sensitive must ask directly about each dish.
vegan_detail: A wine bar with a mostly meat- and dashi-forward small-plate menu (gizzard confit, beef tongue, beef stroganoff). Some plant-based items appear, such as pickled/seasonal Yamato vegetables, but most dishes are not vegan and there is no documented vegan menu. A vegan could likely cobble together a few vegetable plates but should confirm preparation (sake lees, dashi, butter) with staff.

bio.origin: Nara (Kitamachi, north of Kintetsu Nara Station); opened May 30, 2014
bio.background: A small counter-only wine bar tucked into the Kitamachi neighborhood, run by a sommelier with more than twenty years of experience who became captivated by Japanese wine and set out to introduce it alongside French selections. The shop keeps 40 to 60 kinds of domestic Japanese wine on hand.
bio.specialty: Around thirty daily small plates conceived as wine cuisine, built around seasonal Yamato (Nara) vegetables and locally produced sake lees. Documented dishes include pickled vegetables, gizzard confit, squid-ink beef stroganoff, and beef tongue braised in red wine.
bio.philosophy: The name pairs the French Allez!! (go for it / cheer on) with Le Trèfle (clover, a symbol of happiness): the owner aims to cheer guests on and offer a comfortable, happy place to drink, helping people discover the deliciousness of Japanese and French wine through thoughtful food pairings.

anecdotes.text: The name combines the French Allez!! (an encouraging "go for it!") with Le Trèfle, a clover that stands for happiness, reflecting the owner's wish to cheer guests on over a glass.
The sommelier, with over twenty years of experience, became fascinated by Japanese wine and stocks 40 to 60 domestic varieties alongside curated French bottles.
Roughly thirty daily dishes are designed as wine cuisine, leaning on seasonal Yamato vegetables and Nara sake lees.

positives.text: Seasonal Yamato (Nara) vegetable plates and pickled vegetables appear on the menu, offering some plant-forward options to build from.

caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources: the burakita business profile and Tabelog English page give Mon/Tue/Thu 17:00-23:00 and Fri/Sat/Sun (and holidays) 15:00-23:00, while Tabelog's Japanese listing fetch returned Mon-Tue/Thu-Fri 17:00 open and Sat-Sun 15:00 open. hours_week follows the more detailed burakita breakdown (Fri opening at 15:00). The owner is described only as an experienced sommelier; the prompt's "sommelier couple" framing is not directly confirmed in fetched sources, so no spouse/partner is asserted. Closed Wednesdays with occasional irregular closures, so check the Facebook page before visiting.

lead: とおく＆らいぶcafé 葉音（ぱおん）
name_ja: とおく＆らいぶcafé 葉音（ぱおん）
cuisine: フェアトレード珈琲・ライブカフェ・軽食
neighborhood: きたまち（近鉄奈良駅北側・後藤町）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市後藤町12-2
geocode_note: GSI address search on 奈良県奈良市後藤町12-2 returned [135.831284, 34.688881], within the central Nara bounding box.
seats_text: Seat count not published; described as an intimate home-theater-style space inside a private residence, so seating is very limited.
hours_raw: 水〜日 11:30-19:00（ライブ開催時は最長21:00まで延長）／月・火 定休
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, home-based personal cafe where little English is likely; a friendly student comfortable using simple Japanese (or a translation app) will be warmly received, especially given the owner's welcoming, socially-minded spirit.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The cafe centers on fair-trade coffee, yuzu tea, and small natural-food/light snacks; whether any specific item is fully plant-based is not published, so vegans should ask on arrival.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented and there is no allergen information available. As a small home cafe serving coffee, tea, and light snacks, it is not a celiac-safe destination; anyone strictly avoiding gluten must ask directly.
caveats: Open only Wednesday through Sunday, 11:30-19:00 (later on live-event days). Cash only. Very small home-based space with limited seating; hours may flex around live performances. No published allergen, vegan, or gluten-free information — confirm directly if you have dietary restrictions.

bio.background: Paon is a small, personal cafe tucked into a private home in Nara's historic Kitamachi quarter, just north of Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner has turned a home-theater-style room into an intimate gathering space that doubles as a 'tooku' (faraway hideaway) and a live-music venue, hosting occasional concerts that can keep the doors open into the evening.
bio.specialty: Fair-trade coffee and Umajimura yuzu tea, served alongside small natural-food snacks and takeaway items.
bio.philosophy: The cafe is built around a clearly stated ethic of fair trade and social good: it sells fair-trade products and goods that support people with disabilities, framing itself as a place to enjoy nature, the environment, and the value of people over a cup of ethically sourced coffee.

anecdotes.text: Beyond drinks, Paon doubles as a tiny shop offering fair-trade goods, Tinarak weaving products, and items that support people with disabilities, so a visit also supports the makers behind them.
The room is set up like a home theater inside a residence and hosts occasional live performances, when the cafe stays open later than usual.

lead: 魚・創作料理 花しば
name_ja: 魚・創作料理 花しば
cuisine: 鮮魚・刺身・季節の鍋・炭火焼きの創作和食（魚介中心の小さな個人店）
neighborhood: きたまち（近鉄奈良駅周辺・東向北商店街）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市花芝町8-2
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市花芝町8 (chō-level), within the Nara bounding box.
seats_text: 26 seats total — counter, tables, and a tatami (zashiki) room.
hours_raw: Mon-Sat: 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 13:30), 18:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30). Sun & holidays: evening only / irregular, reservations accepted.
closed_days: No fixed closed day (不定休); Sunday/holiday lunch generally not served and Sunday evenings have irregular closures (reservations accepted).
cultural_comfort_note: This is a local, regular-packed fish izakaya with counter and tatami seating where basic spoken Japanese (and pointing at the day's catch) will go a long way; no English menu or English service is documented, and there is a small table charge (otōshi).
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly and not celiac-safe. This is a Japanese seafood izakaya built around sashimi, charcoal-grilling, and seasonal hot pots that rely on soy sauce and dashi; soy sauce contains wheat and there is no documented gluten-free handling. No information on dedicated equipment or cross-contamination control.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The entire concept is fresh fish — sashimi, grilled fish, and fish/shellfish hot pots — and broths are kombu/fish-based with dashi. No vegan dishes are documented and the menu is centered on seafood.

bio.background: The owner grew up in a Nara fish-selling family whose shop has more than 90 years of history, and he carries that lineage into the restaurant by going to the market himself to pick out carefully selected, in-season wild fish.
bio.specialty: Seared tuna (aburi maguro) using belly and tail of sushi-grade tuna, daily sashimi assortments cut the same day, and seasonal hot pots — yellowtail and oyster in winter, firefly squid in spring, and pike conger (hamo) from summer into autumn — often served with sake from the regions where that day's fish was caught.
bio.philosophy: Bring seasonal, wild-caught fish to the people of Nara and prepare it to maximize the natural flavor of the ingredients, whether as sashimi, over charcoal, or in a pot.

anecdotes.text: By day the counter fills with local office workers coming for the seared-raw-tuna lunch, and by night it is packed with neighborhood regulars — a small place that is reliably full.
The owner draws on his family's 90-plus-year-old fish shop and visits the market daily to source the day's catch.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and grilled/hot-pot dishes are served with soy sauce and dashi seasonings typical of a Japanese seafood izakaya; standard soy sauce contains wheat and no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is centered on fresh fish and shellfish, and hot pots use kombu/fish broth; cross-contact with fish and dashi is unavoidable for plant-based diners.

caveats: Tabelog lists credit cards (JCB/AMEX) as accepted, but the more detailed HotPepper Gourmet listing explicitly states credit cards, smart pay, and e-money are NOT accepted (cash only) and includes a 500-yen otōshi charge — treated as cash-only here. Hours/closed-day details vary slightly between listings; Sunday/holiday service is irregular and reservation-based. No official website found; phone 0742-26-6310. Geocode is chō-level (花芝町8), accurate to the block.

lead: 飲む柿の葉・食べる柿の葉 KAKIHA
name_ja: 飲む柿の葉・食べる柿の葉 KAKIHA
cuisine: Persimmon-leaf tea and persimmon-leaf sweets stand; organically grown persimmon-leaf powder drinks and desserts
gf_detail: The core offering — persimmon-leaf tea, persimmon-leaf powder, and powder-based drinks (KAKIHA tea, soda) — is naturally gluten-free. However the brand's product line also includes a wheat-based KAKIHA Udon, so the stand is not a dedicated gluten-free operation and no celiac cross-contamination controls are documented. Anyone with celiac disease should ask staff which items contain wheat before ordering.
vegan_detail: The signature persimmon-leaf tea, powder, and powder-based drinks are plant-based and made from pesticide-free, organically grown persimmon leaves. That said, the wider product line includes non-vegan items such as KAKIHA Milk Jam (dairy), and the rotating daily dessert/drink menu is not labeled vegan, so confirm individual sweets with staff. The plain teas and water/soda preparations are reliably plant-based.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市三条本町1-1 JR奈良駅 ビエラ奈良 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; result [135.817139, 34.677574] falls within the Nara box and corresponds to 三条本町1番 directly in front of JR Nara Station.
neighborhood: JR奈良駅前（三条本町・ビエラ奈良2F）
seats_text: No table seating; operates as a standing tea stand/retail counter (originally a 6-seat counter in Gojo, but the JR Nara location is currently retail with standing sampling only).
closed_days: 不定休（irregular closures）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny counter stand inside the station shopping complex where ordering is simple and inexpensive, though menus and signage are in Japanese, so a foreign student may want a few words of Japanese or a translation app to ask about ingredients.

bio.background: KAKIHA grew out of Kaki no Senmon, a persimmon specialist that began in Gojo City in 2014 with a small six-seat counter. After a television feature brought a rush of customers the original shop could not sustain, the team regrouped and reopened on December 28, 2019, just outside the JR Nara Station ticket gates in the Viera Nara complex.
bio.specialty: Drinks and sweets built around organically grown, pesticide-free persimmon leaves — persimmon-leaf tea (hot and iced), persimmon-leaf powder dissolved into water or soda, plum-syrup soda, persimmon-vinegar juice, and a rotating selection of persimmon-leaf desserts such as cake and milk jam.
bio.philosophy: The makers reclaimed abandoned orchards and developed their own farm in Tenri, the birthplace of the Tone-wase astringent persimmon, insisting on controlling everything from cultivation to the finished product rather than relying on contract farming. They discovered persimmon leaves are exceptionally rich in vitamin C and set out to share that nourishment in an everyday, approachable form.

anecdotes.text: Leaves are hand-harvested at their peak from roughly May through August, then carefully roasted and milled into a fine powder so the leaf's vitamin C, polyphenols, potassium, and fiber can be taken in whole.
The Gojo flagship's six-seat counter became so overwhelmed after TV coverage that it closed within about two years, leading to the relaunch as a stand at JR Nara Station.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free handling or cross-contamination policy is documented; the brand sells a wheat-based KAKIHA Udon alongside the naturally gluten-free teas and powders.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The product line mixes plant-based teas/powders with dairy items such as KAKIHA Milk Jam, and no vegan cross-contact controls are stated, so confirm individual sweets with staff.

positives.text: The signature persimmon leaves are organically grown without pesticides, and the plain teas, powders, and water/soda drinks are inherently plant-based and gluten-free.

caveats: The cuisine brief frames this as a vegan/gluten-free-friendly stand; in practice the core persimmon-leaf teas, powders, and soda drinks are plant-based and gluten-free, but the broader product line includes wheat-based udon and dairy milk jam, and the daily rotating menu is unlabeled, so this is best treated as offering vegan and GF options rather than a dedicated dietary destination. The JR Nara location is currently described as retail-with-sampling rather than a full café, and closures are irregular.

cuisine: 自家焙煎スペシャルティコーヒー・ロースタリーカフェ（スイーツ）
neighborhood: ならまち／近鉄奈良駅周辺（角振新屋町）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市角振新屋町6-2
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.827881, 34.681591] for 奈良県奈良市角振新屋町6番地; within Nara box.
seats_text: Two-floor café; sources vary (Tabelog lists 20 seats, a feature article lists 26 across two floors). Small intimate space.
hours_raw: 7:30-19:00 (official site, every day). Tabelog lists 7:00-19:00. Some feature articles cite café hours from 10:00. Café space is on the upper floor.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休 / no regular closing day）
cultural_comfort_note: The shop runs a full bilingual (English) website and is popular with international visitors, so a foreign student can order coffee and sweets comfortably here.
gf_detail: This is a coffee shop whose food offerings are baked sweets (tarts, cakes, choux/cream puffs) and assorted baked goods, all of which are wheat-based. The coffee itself is naturally gluten-free, but there is no documented gluten-free food menu and no celiac/cross-contamination handling described. Not a gluten-free destination.
vegan_detail: Black drip/pour-over coffee is plant-based, and the signature custard cream puff filling reportedly blends Hokkaido milk with coconut milk and almond milk (still contains dairy). Sweets are dairy/egg-based and the popular café latte uses milk. Plant-milk substitution is not documented. A vegan can drink black specialty coffee but should not expect vegan food; ask about plant-milk for lattes.

bio.background: CHAMI opened in November 2019 as a small in-house roasting specialty coffee shop a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, in the Tsunofuri-Shinyacho area on the edge of the historic Naramachi district. Coffee is roasted in-house and brewed one cup at a time at the counter, with around six single-origin beans and three blends available at a time.
bio.specialty: In-house roasted specialty coffee (single origins such as Geisha lots, plus house blends) brewed to order, paired with handmade seasonal tarts and cakes. The signature sweet is a cocoa choux custard cream puff whose custard jam was developed over roughly five years, blending Hokkaido milk, coconut milk and almond milk with about seven spices including cinnamon, star anise and cardamom for a sweet-bittersweet, gently spiced flavor. The café latte, known for a fluffy texture and gentle sweetness, is also a favorite.
bio.philosophy: The owner says she focuses, through careful selection, on crafting a cup that anyone can find delicious. The name CHAMI is explained as understanding the ingredients, adding a distinctive taste, and expressing them from a unique angle.

anecdotes.text: The coffee menu was developed with input from championship baristas — world latte-art champion Junichi Yamaguchi (山口淳一) and Japan Barista Championship winner Takayuki Ishitani (石谷貴之).
The signature cocoa cream puff's custard filling reportedly took over five years to perfect, combining Hokkaido milk, coconut milk and almond milk with seven spices.
Opened November 2019; the owner is identified as Takemoto Kiyoka, who emphasizes crafting a cup anyone finds delicious.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Food offerings are wheat-based baked sweets (tarts, cakes, choux pastry, assorted baked goods) prepared in-house; no gluten-free items or cross-contamination handling are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Sweets are dairy- and egg-based; even the spiced custard cream puff filling that uses coconut and almond milk also contains Hokkaido dairy milk.

positives.text: Black in-house-roasted specialty coffee (drip/pour-over) is naturally gluten-free and plant-based.

caveats: Hours and seat count differ slightly across sources: official site states 7:30-19:00 daily and Tabelog states 7:00-19:00 with 20 seats, while a feature article cites 26 seats over two floors and another cites café hours from 10:00. Used official-site hours as primary. Owner name (Takemoto Kiyoka) and barista contributors (Yamaguchi, Ishitani) come from feature articles, not the official site.

cuisine: Curry and kissaten (retro coffee house) — Indian-style beef curry, stews, and homemade French-style cakes
neighborhood: Takabatake (near Nara University of Education, walkable from Kintetsu Nara Station; toward Kasuga Taisha / Shin-Yakushiji)
gf_detail: No gluten-free offerings or accommodations are documented. The kitchen centers on curry served over rice plus homemade Western-style cakes and pastries (gateau chocolat and other baked goods), all of which are wheat-based. No celiac or cross-contamination handling is mentioned anywhere. Not suitable for strict gluten-free or celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The signature curry is built on slow-simmered beef brisket and is served with plain yogurt; menus also feature a creamy chicken stew and dairy-rich homemade cakes. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented. Ratatouille appears as a seasonal summer item but its preparation is not described as vegan.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町840
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市高畑町840; within the Nara box.
seats_text: Seat count not documented; described as a small, quiet retro space with aged wooden tables.
hours_raw: 11:30–19:30; closed Monday and Tuesday
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, calm family-run cafe where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way; the owner is French, so basic communication is easy, but it is a quiet local spot rather than a tourist-oriented place.
caveats: Seat count and any formal allergy policy are not documented. Card payment (incl. AMEX) is accepted, but bring cash as a backup since it is a small independent shop. There is no parking.

bio.background: A long-loved retro coffee house in Nara's quiet Takabatake neighborhood, near Nara University of Education and within reach of Kasuga Taisha and Shin-Yakushiji. The cafe was founded decades ago by a Japanese proprietor named Sakamoto, who built its reputation on a distinctive curry. After his passing, his French wife, Miriam — for whom the shop is named — has continued to run it, keeping its character intact inside a hand-renovated old wooden house.
bio.specialty: The signature is a fragrant Indian-style beef curry: chunks of beef brisket simmered with eight spices for about three hours and then rested a full day, served over rice with raisins and a side of plain yogurt that diners can stir in to soften the heat. A creamy chicken stew is also offered.
