Miriam bakes homemade Western-style cakes and pastries daily — including gateau chocolat — which draw their own devoted regulars, with seasonal touches like ratatouille in summer.
bio.philosophy: A deliberately tranquil room with vintage furnishings and pendant lamps and no background music, made for slow, unhurried meals.

anecdotes.text: The curry recipe calls for eight spices simmered for three hours and then left to rest a full day; raisins are offered alongside to act like a palate-cleansing accent and yogurt to mellow the spice.
The husband traditionally made the curry while his French wife baked the cakes and pastries; after his passing she carries the shop on, and her understated baked goods bring visitors in on their own.

positives.text: Yogurt is served on the side of the curry so diners can adjust the spice level to taste.

cuisine: 薬膳スパイススープカレー・カフェ（石焼鍋のスープカレー、ラッシー・チャイ、国産蜂蜜販売）
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The shop serves spice/soup curry with a 20-spice blend; while curry can sometimes be naturally low in wheat, there is no published information on flour-thickened roux, wheat in spice mixes, or cross-contamination, and no GF menu. Celiac travelers must ask in person and should not assume safety. Treat as ask-only.
vegan_detail: The signature soup curry is built on a chicken-bone broth (鶏ガラスープ), so the standard curry is not vegan or vegetarian. No vegan or vegetable-only curry option is documented on the official site or local listings. A vegan/vegetarian traveler would need to ask whether any meat-free preparation is possible; do not assume it is available.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南市町8-1 古古古屋 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.830109, 34.679897] for 奈良県奈良市南市町8-1, within the central Nara box. Location matches Naramachi, just south of the Mochiidono shopping arcade, within walking distance of Kintetsu Nara Station.
neighborhood: ならまち（南市町／もちいどの商店街南、近鉄奈良駅徒歩圏）
seats_text: 13席（カウンター5席、テーブル8席）
hours_raw: ランチ 11:30-15:00（L.O.14:30）／ディナー 17:30-20:00（L.O.19:30）。売り切れ次第終了。定休日は公式サイト・ならまち情報サイトでは金曜、Tabelogでは木曜と表記の相違あり。不定休あり（Instagramで告知）。
closed_days: 金曜定休（公式サイト基準）＋不定休。※Tabelogは木曜定休と表記しており要確認。
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 13-seat machiya curry shop that already welcomes a mix of locals, tourists and international visitors, so a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you a warm reception even with limited Japanese.
caveats: Conflicting closed-day data: the official site and the Naramachi info listing state Friday closed, while Tabelog lists Thursday closed; hours_week follows the official site (Friday). The shop also closes irregularly and sells out, announcing changes on Instagram (@8nosu_curry) — check before going. The soup curry uses a chicken-bone broth, so it is not vegetarian/vegan, and no GF or vegan menu is documented; both must be confirmed in person.

bio.background: 8nosu sits in a quiet alley of Naramachi, the old merchant quarter just south of the Mochiidono arcade, inside a renovated wooden townhouse (the Kokokokoya building). It is a small, owner-run spice and honey shop whose name, Hachinosu, means honeycomb in Japanese — a nod to the pure domestic honey it sells alongside the food.
bio.specialty: A medicinal-herb (yakuzen) soup curry served bubbling in a hot stone pot, built on a chicken-bone broth carrying the sweet, exotic aroma of around twenty spices, with adjustable heat. The shop also pours lassi, masala chai and honey drinks, and sells pure Japanese honey.
bio.philosophy: The shop sums itself up as a curry place where eating makes you healthy — seasonal medicinal herbs and spices meant to warm and energize you.
roles: owner-operated spice curry and honey shop

anecdotes.text: The owner is described as a medicinal-herb coordinator who infuses the soup curry with seasonal yakuzen herbs and about twenty spices, served piping hot in a stone pot with spice level you can adjust.
The honey sold here comes from the Itou Yuu apiary — pure domestic Japanese honey — tying directly to the shop's honeycomb name.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature soup curry is made with a chicken-bone broth, so it is not vegetarian or vegan; no meat-free version is documented.

cuisine: Old-fashioned Western-style (yōshoku) / European — hamburg steak, beef cutlet, curry rice
neighborhood: Mochiidono Center-gai shopping arcade, near Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a traditional Western-style diner whose core dishes — hamburg steak with demi-glace sauce, beef cutlet (katsu), and curry rice — are built on wheat. Demi-glace and curry roux are flour-based, and cutlets are breaded; there is no documented gluten-free menu or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The menu centers on meat dishes (hamburg steak, beef filet cutlet, steak) and the lunch sets include consommé and coffee; sauces are meat- and dairy-based demi-glace. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町31-2
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to chō-level (餅飯殿町); coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 44 seats (6 counter, 38 table) per Tabelog; a diner blog described a smaller, intimate layout of roughly four 4-person tables plus about five counter seats, consistent with a small family-run room.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:00, 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Closed Thursdays, with occasional irregular closures. Note: the shopping-arcade and lunch listings cite lunch running to 15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and dinner L.O. 20:40, so the lunch cutoff may extend later than the Tabelog figure.
closed_days: Thursdays, plus occasional irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, family-run neighborhood diner where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way; menus and ordering are simple, but English support is unlikely, so bring cash and a little patience.

bio.background: A long-standing, family-run Western-style restaurant tucked inside the Mochiidono shopping arcade, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The dining room is built in a cozy mountain-cabin (yamagoya) style with a retro, old-fashioned atmosphere that feels worlds away from the tourist bustle outside.
bio.origin: Nara, in the historic Mochiidono Center-gai arcade leading toward the old Naramachi district.
bio.specialty: House-made hamburg steak in an authentic demi-glace sauce, served as a set with salad, consommé, rice and coffee; also known for beef filet cutlet, steak, and homestyle curry rice.
bio.philosophy: Everything is made by hand — 'all-handmade, warm-hearted cooking' built around quality meat and seasonal ingredients, with wine and other drinks recommended as pairings.

anecdotes.text: Visitors note that the demi-glace sauce on the hamburg steak is surprisingly authentic and delicious for a spot in a tourist area, served over a roughly 150g patty in a tidy, welcoming room.
The place appears to be run by the family themselves, which gives it a warm, familiar feeling for solo diners and couples alike.
Described on the local arcade's own page as an old-fashioned European-Western eatery with a calming mountain-cabin interior, emphasizing fully handmade dishes and quality meat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes rely on flour-based demi-glace sauce and breaded cutlets, so wheat is pervasive across the kitchen; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is meat- and dairy-centric (hamburg steak, demi-glace, consommé, beef cutlet); no vegan options or separate preparation are documented.

caveats: Cash only (no cards). Closed Thursdays with occasional irregular closures, so confirm before a special trip. Lunch cutoff times differ slightly between listings (Tabelog shows lunch to 14:00; the arcade/lunch listings show lunch to 15:00, L.O. 14:30) — arrive earlier to be safe. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners; this is a meat-forward, wheat-heavy Western-style diner.

cuisine: 創作中華 (creative Chinese with Western/Japanese influences)
neighborhood: もちいどのセンター街（近鉄奈良駅前）
gf_detail: This is a Chinese restaurant where wheat and soy are central: dumplings, ramen, tantanmen, and most stir-fried/braised dishes rely on wheat-based wrappers, noodles, and soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A creative Chinese izakaya centered on meat and seafood (salt-pork fried rice, Taiwanese braised pork, saliva chicken, carpaccio, pufferfish milt). A few vegetable items exist (e.g., bamboo-shoot tempura, mapo tofu — though mapo tofu typically contains minced pork), but there is no documented vegan menu and dishes commonly use meat/fish and soy-based seasonings. A committed vegan would need to ask, with few safe options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町7
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町7 returned [135.829178, 34.680107], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Counter seating plus a raised tatami/zashiki area in the back (second floor has tatami seating for families/groups); exact seat count not documented. Small independent place.
hours_raw: Dinner from 18:00 until ingredients run out (Tabelog lists 18:00-21:30 Sun-Thu, 18:00-22:00 Fri-Sat); lunch service 11:30-14:00 offered on some days (commonly Wed/Thu/Sat/Sun per local listings). Hours and lunch days vary; check Instagram for daily updates.
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); some sources list Monday as a closed day. Confirm via Instagram.
cultural_comfort_note: A cozy local creative-Chinese spot run by one owner; little English is expected, so basic Japanese or a translation app will help you order, and the owner posts daily specials on Instagram.

bio.background: Heizo is a much-loved local creative-Chinese restaurant run by an owner who goes by 'Megutan,' who personally posts the day's specials and menu changes on social media. In April 2023 the place moved from Nara's Kitamachi (the Hanashiba shopping street) to a characterful old two-story building on Mochiidono Center Street, taking over a space that had previously housed a creative-oden shop called Yobareya.
bio.origin: Nara (Kitamachi originally, now Mochiidono Center Street)
bio.specialty: Creative Chinese that goes well beyond typical 'town Chinese': several regional styles of mapo tofu (Chengdu, Deyang, Chongqing) with selectable spice levels 1-5, chewy thick-skinned boiled dumplings, soupless tantanmen, plus Western-leaning plates like carpaccio and seasonal items such as bamboo-shoot tempura. A Sunday lunch-bar format offers small plates alongside seafood brought in from Shimane.
bio.philosophy: Rather than ordinary street-level Chinese, the kitchen blends Western and Japanese touches into Chinese cooking, with dishes changing by season and day.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name 'Heizo' comes from the owner's beloved dog.
The restaurant moved into a characterful aged two-story building with steep wooden stairs; the ground floor has counter and table seats and the upstairs has tatami seating for families and groups.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Chinese restaurant the menu features dumplings, ramen, tantanmen and soy-seasoned dishes, all of which typically contain wheat and wheat-based soy sauce.

caveats: Hours and closed days vary across sources: Tabelog lists dinner-only times (18:00-21:30 / Fri-Sat to 22:00) while local blogs describe lunch service 11:30-14:00 on certain days and a Monday closure. The shop runs irregular closures (不定休) and 'closes when ingredients run out,' so the per-day hours here are a best estimate from Tabelog plus blog reports — confirm via the owner's Instagram (@heizomeg). No dietary/allergy information is documented; GF and vegan assessments are inferred from the cuisine type, not from a stated policy.

cuisine: Machiya kissaten — coffee, tea, sweets and light meals
neighborhood: 元林院町・ならまち
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市元林院町8
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.829773, 34.681171] for 奈良県奈良市元林院町, within the Nara box.
seats_text: Seat count not published; multiple small themed rooms across the restored machiya, including a round table that becomes a kotatsu in winter.
hours_raw: Tue-Sat 11:00-18:00; Sun 11:00-17:00; closed Mondays. (Official site lists Sunday from 11:30/12:00 in places, so Sunday opening can vary.)
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, English-friendly-ish hideaway cafe popular with tourists; a few Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu will carry you through comfortably.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Signature savory dishes are nyūmen/sōmen (wheat) and curry, and sweets center on a baked egg pudding; there is no gluten-free menu and no information about avoiding wheat, so this is not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. Flagship items use eggs (Nara-egg pudding) and the nyūmen is served with curry; broths are likely to contain fish/meat dashi. No vegan menu is documented, so a vegan would need to ask what, if anything, is plant-based (some teas/chai-with-azuki may be options but milk/dairy is used).

bio.origin: Nara, in the former Motobayashiin-cho flower district just south of Sarusawa Pond
bio.background: ROKKAN ROOM occupies a townhouse over 140 years old that was once an okiya (a geisha lodging house) in Nara's old flower quarter. The owner, who actually lived in the building, restored it into a cafe while keeping its layers of history intact: an earthen-floored doma, a kura storehouse, original lacquered tables from the okiya, and even the remains of a pre-war air-raid shelter. Concrete bunker walls sit beside tatami and cushions, and a knit-art installation occupies the upper floor of the storehouse, so each small room feels like stepping out of the present day.
bio.specialty: Nara-egg pudding (about 450 yen) made with rich local eggs and beet sugar, served smooth and gently sweet in antique glassware; curry-topped nyūmen using Miwa sōmen (about 1,350 yen); and chai served with sweet red-bean soup (about 780 yen).
bio.philosophy: Unpretentious and nostalgic rather than polished, leaning on Nara-sourced ingredients and the building's own history to give visitors the feeling of a quiet time-trip away from the modern city.

anecdotes.text: The building still holds the remains of a WWII-era air-raid shelter alongside its kura storehouse and earthen doma floor, and the cafe reuses the original lacquered tables from its days as a geisha okiya.
A round table in one room converts into a kotatsu in winter, part of the deliberately mismatched mix of bunker concrete, tatami and plush cushions.
The signature pudding is made with rich Nara eggs and beet sugar for a naturally gentle sweetness and is served in antique glassware.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core savory dishes are wheat-based sōmen/nyūmen and curry, prepared in a small kitchen with no stated gluten separation; assume high cross-contamination risk.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu centers on egg pudding, dairy chai/latte and noodle dishes; no plant-based or allergen-free options are documented, so cross-contact with egg and dairy is likely.

caveats: Seat count is not published. Sunday opening time is inconsistent across sources (Tabelog 11:00, official site shows 11:30/12:00 in places), so confirm Sunday hours before visiting. Phone differs by source (Tabelog 0742-42-6675; official site 050-1112-7867). No vegan or gluten-free menu is documented; dietary suitability should be confirmed directly with the cafe.

cuisine: Medicinal-herb spice soup curry cafe
neighborhood: Naramachi, Nara City (about 8 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南市町8-1 古古古屋 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市南市町8-1 returned [135.830109, 34.679897], within the Nara box.
seats_text: 13 seats total: 5 counter seats plus table seating (two 2-tops and one 4-top).
hours_raw: Sources conflict. Tabelog: 11:30-15:00 and 17:30-20:00, closed Thursday. Official jimdo site: lunch 11:30-15:00 (LO 14:30), dinner 17:30-20:00 (LO 19:30), closed Fridays, additional closures via Instagram, closes when sold out. Narakko: weekdays 11:00-15:00 & 17:00-20:00, weekends/holidays 11:00-17:00, closed Tuesdays and 2nd & 4th Wednesdays.
closed_days: Closed day differs by source (official site states Fridays; Tabelog lists Thursday; another listing lists Tuesday plus 2nd & 4th Wednesday). Extra closures are announced on Instagram and the shop may close early when sold out — check before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, friendly Naramachi cafe used to tourists and international visitors; menu is build-your-own (base, soup, spice level, rice), so a few words of Japanese or pointing will go a long way.
vegan_detail: A vegetable-only curry exists (about ¥880-¥950), but the soup base is made with chicken-bone and dried-fish (niboshi) dashi, and the curries contain honey, so even the vegetable option is not vegan or strictly vegetarian. Not a vegan destination.
gf_detail: No gluten-free claim is published. It is a soup curry rather than a thick wheat-roux curry, but there is no documented information on whether the spice blend, soup, or sides are wheat-free, and no cross-contamination details. A celiac diner would need to ask directly and should not assume it is safe.
caveats: Opening hours and the weekly closed day differ significantly between the official site, Tabelog, and local listings; confirm via Instagram before visiting. The shop closes when sold out. Hours_week reflects a best-effort blend (closed Fri per official site) but is uncertain.

bio.background: 8nosu is a small spice-curry-and-honey shop tucked into a traditional wooden townhouse in Nara's Naramachi district, a short walk south of Sarusawa Pond and Kofuku-ji Temple. It is run by a certified yakuzen (medicinal-herb cooking) coordinator who built the menu around the idea of food as everyday wellness.
bio.specialty: Stone-bowl soup curry built from about 20 spices and seasonal medicinal-herb vegetables. Diners choose a base (vegetable-only, soft chicken, or slow-braised pork), a soup style (original, coconut, or shrimp), and a spice level. House drinks include lassi, masala chai, and a honey-lemon squash made with domestic lemons and pure honey from Nara's Uda area (Ito-yu Beekeeping), which is also sold by the jar.
bio.philosophy: Built on the motto 食べて元気になる - eat and become well - applying medicinal-herb principles so a casual curry lunch doubles as nourishment.

anecdotes.text: The curries are sweetened in part with honey, so the shop notes the dishes are not suitable for infants under one year old.
The honey served and sold is pure domestic honey from Ito-yu Beekeeping in the Uda area of Nara Prefecture.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The soup base for all curries, including the vegetable-only option, is made with chicken-bone and dried-fish (niboshi) dashi, so vegetable dishes share an animal-derived broth.
Curries contain honey.

positives.text: A vegetable-only medicinal-herb soup curry is offered as a standing menu choice (though it is not vegan due to the broth and honey).

cuisine: アンティーク町家カフェ（喫茶・和スイーツ・お茶）
vegan_detail: No vegan-specific menu is documented. As a Showa-retro tea room serving sweets and tea, some items (e.g. tea, certain wagashi) may incidentally be plant-based, but dairy, eggs, and other animal products are likely used in café sweets. Ask on arrival; there is no stated vegan offering.
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision is documented. The café focuses on sweets and tea, and typical café confections contain wheat flour. There is no indication of celiac-safe handling. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance without confirming directly.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市納院町8 2F
neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅周辺）
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI at the chō level (奈良市納院町8); coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 小規模。2階の喫茶室に座卓（ローテーブル）が約4卓。靴を脱いで上がる。
hours_raw: 金・土・日・祝 12:00-18:00（冬季は17:30頃まで、通常は11:00頃開店だが現在は12:00頃開店）。月～木は定休。金や祝は不定休の場合あり。
closed_days: 月・火・水・木（金・祝は不定休）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, owner-run hideaway down a narrow Naramachi alley where you remove your shoes and sit at low tables, so a little Japanese and comfort with traditional etiquette go a long way; little to no English is documented.
caveats: Weekend-only (Fri-Sun and holidays) with irregular closures, so confirm before visiting. Cash only. Up a steep stair to the 2F tea room above the antique shop; shoes off and floor-cushion/low-table seating. Phone listed as 080-5365-1323 (weekdays). Hours and open days have shifted over time and may differ from listings.

bio.background: Bitowa is a small antique-shop-and-tearoom tucked down a narrow back alley in Naramachi. The ground floor is a shop selling everyday Japanese antiques at honest prices: vintage tools, Showa- and Taisho-era tableware, obi sashes and Japanese ceramics. Climb the stairs (shoes off) and the second floor opens into the Bitowa tea room, a nostalgic Showa-retro space of low tables where you can linger over tea and sweets.
bio.specialty: Tea and seasonal sweets enjoyed in a Showa-retro tatami tea room above the antique shop; one visitor noted a summer set around 650 yen.
bio.philosophy: A place to slow down and spend leisurely, time-slipped time, surrounded by antiques that have accompanied everyday Japanese life.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a renovated machiya where much of the interior was reworked by the woman who runs it; visitors remove their shoes, browse antiques on the first floor, and climb to the second-floor cafe.
It is described as a hidden gem reached down a narrow alley, where you can experience a sense of having slipped back in time.

cuisine: Craft hamburgers (handmade, domestic-beef patties, house-smoked bacon)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is a burger shop: patties are served on wheat buns, and the house "smoked soy sauce" condiment is soy-sauce-based, which normally contains wheat. No gluten-free buns or celiac accommodations are documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The shop is built around 100% domestic Japanese beef patties and house-made smoked bacon, ham, and sausage produced at its own smokehouse. No vegan or vegetarian burger is documented on the menu.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向北町6
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市東向北町6 returned [135.82869, 34.684891], within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: Kitamachi / Higashimuki-kitamachi, just off the Higashimuki shopping arcade, about a 30-second to 2-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station Exit 1.
seats_text: 20 seats
hours_raw: 9:00–18:00 (L.O. 17:30); closed Wednesdays and Thursdays
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: This spot is well used to international visitors and is an easy, welcoming stop for a foreign student wanting a casual hands-on burger.
caveats: Wheat-based buns and a soy-sauce condiment mean this is not safe for celiacs, and the all-meat menu is not suitable for vegans/vegetarians. Closed Wednesdays and Thursdays. No dine-in reservations; a phone/mobile waiting-list system is used and takeout can be timed by phone. Limited seating (20), so expect a wait at peak times.

bio.background: Sakura Burger is a small owner-run craft burger shop tucked into Nara's Kitamachi neighborhood, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station and the Higashimuki shopping arcade. The shop is built around house-made smoked meats: the owner cures and smokes the signature bacon, ham, sausage, and salami in-house. As demand grew beyond what the small storefront could produce, the owner opened a dedicated smokehouse, Asuka Smokeworks (あすか燻製工房), in nearby Asuka Village to supply both the shop and other outlets.
bio.specialty: Handmade craft burgers built on 100% domestic Japanese beef patties (about 150g) grilled over a volcanic-stone net, paired with house-cured, cherry-wood-smoked bacon and a house-made smoked soy-sauce condiment. A popular order is the 'Sakura Mankai Burger' with house bacon, cheese, and avocado.
bio.philosophy: An additive-free, dry-cure approach to its smoked meats: spices are rubbed directly into pork belly, the meat is aged at low temperature, then slowly smoked over cherry wood chips, all done by hand.

anecdotes.text: Inside the shop hangs a world map on which visiting customers mark where they come from; so many international travelers have stopped in that the marks have nearly obscured the country borders.
The owner has spoken about updating the kitchen, replacing cooking equipment used for about 15 years to better prepare the meat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Burgers are served on wheat buns and the shop centers on bread-based sandwiches and burgers, so flour is present throughout the kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The shop uses a house-made smoked soy sauce as a burger condiment; standard soy sauce contains wheat, so it is not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built on beef patties and house-cured bacon, ham, and sausage smoked on-site, so the kitchen is a meat-forward environment with no documented vegan options.

cuisine: すき焼き（奈良食材を使った町家風のすき焼き割烹）
neighborhood: ならまち（元林院町）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市元林院町22 鹿猿狐ビルヂング 1F
geocode_note: First GSI feature for 奈良県奈良市元林院町22 returned [135.829636, 34.681026], inside the Nara box.
seats_text: 26 seats total, including one private room for 8 and two semi-private rooms seating 4 each.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (course L.O. 13:00, a la carte L.O. 14:00); Dinner 17:00-21:00 (course L.O. 19:00, a la carte L.O. 20:00). Closed Wednesdays (open if a national holiday); also closed 12/31 and 1/1.
closed_days: Wednesdays (open if Wednesday falls on a national holiday); closed Dec 31 and Jan 1.
cultural_comfort_note: A polished, design-forward sukiyaki restaurant that takes online and English-language reservations through international platforms, so a foreign student can book and dine comfortably—though it is a special-occasion price point.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. Sukiyaki is built on warishita (a sweetened soy-sauce broth) and standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, so the core dishes contain gluten. No dedicated gluten-free menu or celiac protocol is documented; a celiac traveler would need to ask directly and should not assume safety.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian. This is a beef sukiyaki specialist centered on Yamato beef (大和牛), with courses also featuring uni, ikura and other animal products. No plant-based course is documented.
caveats: Dietary specifics (allergen handling, gluten-free or vegan accommodation) are not documented on any fetched page; the GF/vegan assessments are inferred from the cuisine (wheat-based warishita, beef-centered courses). Address geocoded at chō-banchi level for the building, not a verified storefront pin.

bio.background: 㐂つね is the Nara sukiyaki restaurant created by the team behind sio, the Michelin-starred restaurant in Yoyogi-Uehara, Tokyo, with chef Toba Shusaku serving as executive chef. It opened on the ground floor of the Shika-Saru-Kitsune Building (鹿猿狐ビルヂング) in the historic Motobayashiin-cho quarter of Naramachi, a former geisha district. The building's name comes from the three businesses inside it: 鹿 (deer) for Nakagawa Masashichi Shoten, 猿 (monkey) for Sarutahiko Coffee, and 狐 (fox/kitsune) for this restaurant.
bio.specialty: Course-style sukiyaki built around Yamato beef (大和牛) prized for its balance of marbling and lean meat, paired with seasonal Nara-grown vegetables such as Asuka Ruby tomatoes dressed with persimmon vinegar. The lunch signature is a sukiyaki chirashi rice bowl crowned with uni, ikura, kazunoko and kamaboko, and courses close with local touches like Tsukigase hojicha tea and a house mitarashi pudding.
bio.philosophy: The name carries the concept directly: the team chose 'Kitsune' because they wanted to transform sukiyaki into something even more delicious, treating a familiar hot-pot dish as a refined, reconstructed (再構築) course experience while showcasing Nara produce. Attention to detail runs from the interior and tableware (sourced from Nakagawa Masashichi Shoten) down to the hand towels.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant sits inside the Shika-Saru-Kitsune Building, whose name is formed from the deer, monkey and fox of its three tenant businesses—the fox (狐/kitsune) being this restaurant itself.
The team states it chose the name Kitsune out of a desire to 'transform sukiyaki into something even more delicious,' presenting it as seasonal multi-course dining.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The cuisine is sukiyaki served in warishita, a sweetened soy-sauce broth; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat, so the dishes are not gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses are centered on Yamato beef and also use uni and ikura; no plant-based option is documented.

lead: リストランテ リンコントロ
name_ja: リストランテ リンコントロ
cuisine: イタリアン（町家・ジビエと大和野菜、シェフ自ら狩猟）
neighborhood: ならまち外れ（薬師堂町）／京終・近鉄奈良駅エリア
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is an Italian kitchen that makes its own pasta and bread in-house, so wheat is central to many courses. The menu is course-only and changes daily, so a celiac or gluten-avoiding diner would need to phone ahead and discuss carefully before booking; there is no published allergen or dedicated-GF information.
vegan_detail: This is a game-meat (ジビエ) and charcuterie-driven Italian restaurant — venison, wild boar and wild duck are the headline ingredients, much of it hunted or butchered in-house, and house-cured salami and prosciutto feature heavily. Yamato (Nara) vegetables do appear in the cooking, but there is no documented vegan or vegetarian course. A plant-based diner would need to call ahead to ask whether anything can be accommodated; it is not a vegan-friendly destination.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市薬師堂町9
geocode_note: GSI address search returned a single feature for 奈良県奈良市薬師堂町9 at [135.832169, 34.675636], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 15 seats (counter and tables) in a converted ~100-year-old machiya townhouse.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:30 (lunch seatings 11:30 or 12:30), 18:00-22:00 (dinner seatings 18:00 or 19:00). Closed Wednesdays.
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, reservation-only chef's-course restaurant where the owner-chef and his wife host personally, so a foreign student should be comfortable with a quiet, attentive Japanese dining setting and ideally book and confirm dietary needs in Japanese in advance.
caveats: Course-only format that changes with daily sourcing; advance reservation is essential and cancellations are charged in full. Children require a phone consultation. No vegan/vegetarian course or gluten-free program is published — call ahead for any dietary need.

bio.background: Chef-owner Masato Nishioka grew up in Nara and was drawn to restaurant life as a student. He trained at Italian restaurants in Osaka's Tosabori and in Tokyo's Nishi-Azabu and Jiyugaoka, opened his first place, Trattoria La Crocetta, at age 27, and several years later opened L'incontro in a roughly century-old machiya townhouse in Naramachi — a neighborhood tied to his own family roots, where his grandmother's house once stood.
bio.specialty: Game (ジビエ): venison from Tsuge in Nara and wild boar, plus wild duck that the chef himself hunts in season. He works nose-to-tail, using whole animals down to the bones, and cures his own salami and prosciutto. Signature plates include slow-roasted venison with a concentrated, refined flavor, terrines and charcuterie from heritage pork, and dishes built around Yamato (Nara) vegetables. Pasta, bread, anchovies and even limoncello are made in-house.
bio.philosophy: The name L'incontro means 'encounter' or 'serendipity' in Italian; Nishioka built the restaurant to foster genuine connections among guests, staff, producers and the local community, and personally visits the producers he sources from.

anecdotes.text: A licensed hunter, the chef goes out himself in season to take wild duck, and butchers and cures whole wild boar and deer into house-made salami and prosciutto.
He notes that while venison's classic season is winter, summer deer that have grazed on fresh grass have a more refined flavor he recommends to those new to game.
His wife Asami curates the cellar of roughly 150 Italian wines, with a pairing option available.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made pasta and bread are central to the kitchen, so wheat is handled throughout; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is built around game meat and house-cured charcuterie (salami, prosciutto), making meaningful separation from animal products unlikely.

positives.text: Owner-chef sources whole animals and produce directly from named producers and makes nearly everything in-house, so the kitchen has detailed knowledge of its ingredients and can discuss them when you call ahead.

lead: メインダイニングルーム 三笠（奈良ホテル）
name_ja: メインダイニングルーム「三笠」（奈良ホテル）
cuisine: クラシックフレンチ（French）
neighborhood: 高畑町（奈良ホテル本館内）／奈良公園・荒池近く
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program. Classic French cuisine relies on flour-thickened sauces, bread, and pastry, and the kitchen states all dishes are prepared in the same kitchen so cross-contamination is possible. The hotel does offer allergy accommodation if you notify them well in advance of your reservation, so a celiac or gluten-avoidant diner should call ahead (0742-24-3044) and discuss specific needs, but this is not a celiac-safe environment by default.
vegan_detail: The restaurant advertises ovo-lacto vegetarian course options (which include eggs and dairy, so not vegan) and builds menus around seasonal Yamato vegetables and local Nara produce. There is no documented fully vegan menu; French cooking here uses butter, cream, dairy and likely meat/fish stocks. Strict vegans should contact the restaurant in advance to ask what can be prepared, but should not assume vegan dishes are available off the standard menu.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1096 奈良ホテル 本館1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市高畑町1096 returned [135.83371, 34.680328], within the Nara box.
seats_text: Official Nara Hotel site lists 85 seats; Tabelog lists 130 seats. Used the official 85 figure.
closed_days: 無休（年中無休、ホテル営業に準ずる）
cultural_comfort_note: As the flagship dining room of a historic international hotel, the staff are used to overseas guests and can handle English, so a foreign student can dine here comfortably — though it is a formal, special-occasion-priced setting rather than a casual everyday spot.
caveats: The Tabelog URL supplied in the task (29000408) actually points to a closed ramen shop (元気屋); the correct page for this restaurant is 29000026. Seat count differs between the official site (85) and Tabelog (130). Lunch/dinner are mid-to-high priced; this is a formal hotel dining room, not a budget option.

bio.chef_name: 乾 洋介 (Yosuke Inui), head chef
bio.background: The main dining room of the Nara Hotel, which opened in 1909 in the Takabatake area beside Nara Park. The dining room has served guests since the hotel's founding and has hosted dignitaries and celebrities from Japan and abroad in a preserved Meiji–Taisho era setting.
bio.origin: Nara (奈良市高畑町), within the historic Nara Hotel
bio.specialty: Traditional French cuisine built on seasonal Yamato vegetables and local Nara ingredients, with recipes handed down through successive head chefs since the hotel opened.
bio.philosophy: The head chef describes the goal as French cuisine that brings smiles and emotion befitting the tradition and history of the Nara Hotel (伝統と歴史を培った奈良ホテルに相応しい笑顔と感動を与えるフランス料理).

anecdotes.text: The dining room has been part of the Nara Hotel since its 1909 founding and carries on traditional French cuisine inherited from successive head chefs, served in a preserved Meiji–Taisho-era room.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All dishes are prepared in the same kitchen, so cross-contamination is possible.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetarian offering is ovo-lacto (includes eggs and dairy) and shares the same kitchen as meat/fish dishes, so cross-contact is possible.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Allergy accommodation is available but requires advance notice when booking.

positives.text: Offers ovo-lacto vegetarian course options and menus featuring seasonal Yamato vegetables.

lead: 池利 三輪素麺茶屋 千寿亭
name_ja: 池利 三輪素麺茶屋 千寿亭
cuisine: Miwa somen (hand-stretched wheat noodles) — chilled somen, warm nyumen, and kaiseki-style set meals
gf_detail: This is a somen specialist, and somen is a wheat noodle, so the core menu is fundamentally wheat-based and not safe for celiac diners. Dipping sauces (tsuyu) are typically soy-sauce-and-dashi based, which also normally contain wheat. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergy protocol. Not a viable destination for gluten-free travelers.
vegan_detail: No documented vegan menu. The somen noodles themselves are plant-based (wheat, salt, oil), but the dipping sauce and nyumen broth are traditionally made with bonito/fish dashi, and set meals feature wagyu beef, Yamato chicken sukiyaki, and egg. A vegan would at best need to ask whether plain noodles can be served with a non-dashi sauce; this cannot be confirmed and should not be assumed. Tempura sides and seasonal vegetable dishes exist but their broths/batters are not documented as vegan.
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.840912, 34.533195] for 奈良県桜井市芝293. Latitude 34.533 is well south of the central-Nara box (34.64–34.74), as expected — the restaurant is in Sakurai City near JR Miwa Station and the Omiwa Shrine approach, roughly 20km south of central Nara. Coordinates set to null per central-Nara box rule; real location is ~135.8409, 34.5332.
seats_text: 44 seats including one tatami-mat room
neighborhood: Miwa / Shiba, Sakurai City (near JR Miwa Station and the Omiwa Shrine approach)
hours_raw: 11:00–16:00 (last order 15:00). Closed: 2nd and 4th Thursdays, all Fridays, plus year-end/New Year.
closed_days: All Fridays; also the 2nd and 4th Thursdays; closed for year-end and New Year holidays.
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming tourist-friendly somen teahouse on the Omiwa Shrine approach where a smile and a few basic Japanese words go a long way, though staff may have limited English so pointing at the picture menu helps.
caveats: Located in Sakurai City (Miwa), roughly 20km / ~30-40 min by train south of central Nara — not within central Nara despite the curated-area framing. Hours sources disagree slightly: the official site lists closures on the 2nd/4th Thursdays plus all Fridays, while Tabelog lists only Fridays; the official site (more conservative) was used. Phone reservations are accepted but not available on weekends/holidays, Golden Week, Jan 1–15, or July–August due to busy season.

bio.background: Senjutei is the direct-operated restaurant of Ikeri, a Miwa somen maker founded in 1850 (Kaei 3) in what is now Sakurai City, near the sacred Mount Miwa and the Omiwa Shrine. The surrounding land, watered by clear streams from Mount Miwa, has been prized for wheat cultivation and hand-stretched somen for centuries. The fourth-generation head opened Senjutei in 1980, wanting visitors to Miwa to taste authentic hand-stretched somen at the source rather than only buy it as a gift.
bio.specialty: Hand-stretched Miwa somen served in many forms: chilled somen with tempura, warm mushroom nyumen, multi-colored somen (plain, egg, and matcha), and seasonal kaiseki-style set meals such as the wagyu and colorful somen set, Yamato chicken sukiyaki lunch, and the Asuka kaiseki for groups.
bio.philosophy: Ikeri frames its work around three commitments — the pursuit of deliciousness, of technique, and of quality — preserving hand-stretching methods passed down through generations of the family while letting visitors experience them on the spot.
roles: Direct-operated teahouse of the Ikeri somen company

anecdotes.text: Senjutei was opened in 1980 by the fourth-generation head of the 1850-founded Ikeri somen house, who saw that there was no restaurant in Miwa specializing in somen and wanted travelers to enjoy the real taste of the noodles.
The French master chef Paul Bocuse is reported to have dined here, and the restaurant was listed among recommended spots in the 2017 Michelin Guide Nara special edition.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu is built on somen, a wheat noodle, and the kitchen handles wheat flour and wheat-based dipping sauces throughout, so cross-contamination with gluten is unavoidable. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Somen is eaten with tsuyu dipping sauce and nyumen is served in broth; these are traditionally soy-sauce-and-dashi based and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broths and dipping sauces are traditionally made with fish/bonito dashi, and set meals include wagyu beef, chicken, and egg, so vegan diners cannot assume any dish is animal-free without asking. No vegan handling is documented.

cuisine: Persimmon-leaf tea stand — caffeine-free persimmon leaf tea, persimmon-leaf-powder daily drinks and sweets
vegan_detail: The core drinks are plant-based and vegan-friendly: KAKIHA persimmon leaf tea (hot/iced) is caffeine-free and made only from pesticide-free persimmon leaves; KAKIHA soda is made with plum syrup; and there is a persimmon-vinegar drink. These contain no animal products. However, the food/sweets side is not vegan: the persimmon-leaf cake contains roasted walnuts and persimmon-leaf yokan and likely flour/eggs/dairy, and one product line is a persimmon-leaf milk jam (contains dairy). Daily desserts rotate, so a vegan should confirm the specific item of the day. Treat as a tea-focused stand with vegan drinks rather than a vegan eatery.
gf_detail: The signature persimmon leaf teas, soda and persimmon-vinegar drink contain no wheat, but the food menu does. The associated product line includes a persimmon-leaf udon (wheat noodles) and a cake that likely contains flour, and daily sweets rotate. There is no indication of celiac-level allergen control or a dedicated preparation area, so a gluten-sensitive visitor should stick to the plain teas and ask about each sweet. Not a celiac-safe destination.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市三条本町1-1 JR奈良駅ビル ビエラ奈良 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address 奈良県奈良市三条本町1番 via GSI; result (34.677574, 135.817139) is inside the central Nara box and matches the JR Nara Station / Viera Nara location.
neighborhood: 三条本町（JR奈良駅前 ビエラ奈良2F）
seats_text: A standing-style stand inside Viera Nara with compact standing space for roughly two people; essentially no seating.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures); operates on an irregular schedule
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, friendly counter stand inside the station building where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the day's drink will get you a cup of caffeine-free persimmon leaf tea with no fuss.
caveats: It is a standing tea stand, not a sit-down cafe, with space for only about two people. Hours and the daily drink/sweet menu rotate and the shop keeps irregular closures, so the specific item you want may not be available on a given day. Drinks are reliably plant-based; sweets vary and may contain wheat or dairy.

roles: Operated by Ishii & Co., Ltd. (Kaki no Senmon, the persimmon specialist)
bio.origin: Nara — farm in Tenri along the historic Yamanobe-no-michi
bio.background: KAKIHA grew out of Kaki no Senmon, a Nara company devoted entirely to the persimmon. Its current representative, Ishii Kazuhiro (b. 1969), studied agriculture and worked at a major confectionery maker before returning to the family business and creating award-winning persimmon products. The shop's driving figure is Ishii Nanako (b. 1970), a former childcare worker from Osaka who married into the family at 30 and moved to the Yoshino mountains, where she does everything from peeling persimmons to planning and sales. The first KAKIHA opened in 2014 as a tiny six-seat counter cafe in the old Shinmachi district of Gojo; after television coverage brought overwhelming crowds for its persimmon pudding, the little shop became unmanageable and closed within two years. Determined to keep sharing the leaf, the family reopened KAKIHA on 28 December 2019 as a stand inside Viera Nara at JR Nara Station.
bio.specialty: Caffeine-free persimmon leaf tea brewed from their own pesticide-free leaves, plus persimmon-leaf-powder drinks and sweets that change daily — including a persimmon leaf soda made with plum syrup and a persimmon-vinegar drink. The wider product line features ultra-fine (38-micron) persimmon leaf powder sticks, persimmon leaf cake with walnuts and yokan, and persimmon leaf udon.
bio.philosophy: Nanako sums it up with a play on the Japanese character for 'eat': 人を良くすると書いて食べる — 'to eat is written with the characters for making people well; what we eat is what builds our bodies.' Her conviction came from her own son's severe allergies, which improved dramatically when the family shifted to homemade, unprocessed food and good tea. The shop's stated aim is patient: 'even one customer a day is fine, even if it's slow — just convey the charm of the persimmon leaf.' Persimmon leaves were found to hold about 3,300mg of natural vitamin C per 100g, and the company reclaimed roughly 36,530 square meters of abandoned land near Tenri to grow them organically and pesticide-free so the tea is safe for children and expectant mothers.

anecdotes.text: The original 2014 Gojo cafe had just six counter seats; a TV feature on its persimmon pudding drew such crowds that the tiny operation could not cope and shut within two years before the family regrouped and reopened at JR Nara Station.

Ishii Nanako's push into persimmon-leaf nutrition was deeply personal — her son's allergies, which she described as severe, eased after the family emphasized homemade miso soup and quality tea over processed food.
The leaves come from a certified organic farm of about 36,530 square meters on reclaimed land near Tenri, beside one of the oldest roads in Japan, the Yamanobe-no-michi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop's wider product line includes a wheat-based persimmon leaf udon and cakes/sweets that likely contain flour, prepared in the same small operation, so cross-contact with wheat is plausible; only the plain teas and the soda/vinegar drinks are inherently wheat-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The food side includes a persimmon-leaf milk jam (dairy) and cake with walnuts/yokan, while the drinks are plant-based; in a tiny shared stand a strict vegan should confirm the day's item is dairy-free.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The founding family is unusually allergy-aware — the business is rooted in Ishii Nanako's experience managing her son's severe allergies through food, and the leaves are grown pesticide-free specifically to be safe for children and pregnant women.

positives.text: The signature persimmon leaf tea is caffeine-free and made solely from pesticide-free, organically grown persimmon leaves, making the core drinks naturally plant-based and free of common allergens like gluten and dairy.

name_ja: cafe zuccu（カフェ ズック）
cuisine: 町家古民家カフェ（自家製サンド・季節のパスタ・ハヤシライス・季節のケーキ／タルト、京都焙煎の珈琲）
neighborhood: 高畑町（春日大社・ならまち南東、近鉄奈良駅圏内）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町728
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市高畑町728 returned [135.840195, 34.676571], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 20 seats: 16 table seats (chairs and sofas) plus 4 counter seats.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri, Sat, Sun 08:00-18:00 (food L.O. 16:30, drinks L.O. 17:00); Wed 08:00-17:00 (food L.O. 15:30, drinks L.O. 16:00); morning service 8:00-10:00 until sold out. Closed Thursdays.
closed_days: Thursday (Wednesday closes earlier at 17:00)
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet owner-run townhouse café where roughly a third of guests come alone, so a solo foreign student will feel at ease, though menus and service are mostly in Japanese and the place is cash-only.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The core menu is built on wheat — toast, sandwiches, and pasta — and there is no mention of gluten-free preparation or allergy accommodation, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan offering is documented. Signature dishes feature eggs, bacon, chicken, and dairy (egg sandwiches, cakes, milk-based drinks), and soups/savory dishes likely use animal-based stock. No plant-based menu or vegan accommodation is mentioned.
caveats: Vegan and gluten-free suitability assessed as "no" based on absence of any documented options and a wheat-, egg-, and dairy-heavy café menu; no source explicitly addressed allergen handling. Confirm directly if dietary needs are strict.

bio.background: cafe zuccu occupies a machiya (traditional Nara townhouse) built more than 160 years ago in the late Edo period. The owner found the property online, fell for it instantly, and moved to the quiet Takabatake neighborhood southeast of Nara Park to open the café. Rather than over-renovating, they kept the original character intact, even leaving earthen walls deliberately unplastered.
bio.specialty: A menu that rotates with the seasons: morning toast and egg-sandwich sets, lunchtime seasonal pasta, sandwiches and a hayashi rice spiced subtly with lemongrass, Sichuan pepper and brown sugar, and an afternoon spread of six or seven rotating cakes and seasonal tarts. Coffee is brewed from beans roasted at Kyoto's Nakayama Coffee Roastery, giving an old-fashioned kissaten depth.
bio.philosophy: The owner, an antiques enthusiast, designed the space around 居心地のよさ (deep comfort) rather than just food, arranging seats so guests never feel watched and filling the rooms with vintage organs, sewing machines and books to browse. The hope is that visitors linger and think, 'I could stay here forever.'

anecdotes.text: The name 'zuccu' comes from an archaic Japanese word for shoes, expressing the owner's wish that people walk through Nara's old streets to reach the café and then settle in for a while.
Around 30% of guests come alone, and the seating is arranged so solo visitors can read or relax without feeling observed.
Morning sets are popular enough that they often sell out within about an hour of opening.

cuisine: Naramachi machiya teahouse serving Nara regional cuisine — tea porridge (chagayu) sets with Yamato vegetables, plus sweets
neighborhood: Naramachi (Nishinoya-cho), central Nara
vegan_detail: The signature meal is a tea-porridge (chagayu) set built around rice cooked in hojicha or azuki (red-bean) tea, accompanied by Yamato-vegetable side dishes, Nara pickles, and dessert (Yamato-tea warabimochi). An English-language guide reports the venue offers vegetarian options. However, the set includes Noppe soup, a regional dish that is commonly made with fish or chicken stock, and dashi/seasoning details for the porridge and sides are not documented, so a strictly vegan version is not confirmed. Vegan travelers must ask directly about the broth and seasonings.
gf_detail: The core dish is rice porridge, which is naturally gluten-free, and Yamato vegetables and pickles can be GF. But warabimochi and pickles can carry wheat-based soy sauce/seasoning, and Noppe soup and the tea-porridge seasoning are not documented as wheat-free. No gluten-free program or labeling is documented. A celiac diner would need to confirm soy sauce and broth ingredients in advance; this is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西新屋町43 奈良オリエント館内
geocode_note: GSI address search for 奈良県奈良市西新屋町43 returned coordinates within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 24 seats, including tatami-room seating with a garden view
hours_raw: Tue–Fri 11:30–14:00; Sat/Sun/holidays 11:00–14:30 (last order ~30 min before close); hours vary seasonally (sometimes weekdays 12:00–14:00). Closed Mondays (open if Monday is a national holiday).
closed_days: Mondays (open when Monday falls on a national holiday); hours can vary seasonally
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional tatami-room teahouse where a few words of Japanese help, though an English menu is available and staff are used to visitors exploring Naramachi.
caveats: Lunch-only and short daily hours (roughly 11:00/11:30 to 14:00/14:30); arrive early as the kitchen closes by mid-afternoon. Hours shift seasonally, so confirm before visiting. Cash only, no parking. Menu centers on tea porridge — limited choice for those wanting a large or meat-forward meal.

bio.background: Sabou Nonon is a tranquil teahouse set inside a historic Naramachi machiya — the former main residence of the Matsuyama family, one of the district's prominent merchant households, in a wooden townhouse described as a registered cultural property. Guests sit in tatami rooms looking out onto a garden, in a building that carries the quiet atmosphere of old Nara.
bio.specialty: Chagayu (tea rice porridge) sets: rice simmered in hojicha or azuki (red-bean) tea, which diners can season to taste with Yamato-tea powder and salt, served with Yamato-vegetable side dishes, Nara pickles, plum, butterbur, mustard greens, and the regional Noppe soup. Dessert features warabimochi with Yamato tea.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing chagayu as a Nara dish enjoyed locally for well over a thousand years, paired with seasonal Yamato vegetables and local tea, in a preserved merchant townhouse.

anecdotes.text: The teahouse occupies the former main residence of the Matsuyama family, one of Naramachi's most prominent merchant houses, a traditional machiya registered as a cultural property.

Tea porridge is presented as a local Nara dish enjoyed for more than 1,300 years, with sides of dried radish, Nara pickles, plum, butterbur, mustard greens and Noppe soup, finished with Yamato-tea warabimochi.

positives.text: An English menu is available, which can help travelers ask about ingredients.
The teahouse is reported to offer vegetarian options.

lead: 農園直送レストラン 古都華
name_ja: 農園直送レストラン 古都華
cuisine: 大和野菜・奈良食材の農園直送定食。野菜ビュッフェ付き「かご農膳」、大和ポーク・大和牛・大和肉鶏、奈良県産ヒノヒカリ使用。
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. This is a Japanese teishoku (set-meal) restaurant where wheat appears across the menu: breaded tonkatsu and fried prawns (panko), curry roux, hamburg steak, and standard soy sauce (which contains wheat) used in the morning tamago-kake-gohan and seasonings. No gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or celiac protocol is documented. A celiac traveler should not assume any dish is safe; rice (Nara-grown Hinohikari) and the vegetable buffet are the only naturally lower-risk elements, but cross-contact and soy-sauce dressings make this not celiac-safe without direct confirmation in Japanese.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant, but the signature lunch かご農膳 (kago-nōzen) comes with a fresh vegetable buffet (野菜バイキング) of seasonal, farm-direct Yamato produce sourced from inspected farms, plus Nara-grown Hinohikari rice. A vegan could potentially eat from the vegetable buffet and rice, but dressings, seasonings and most prepared dishes use fish dashi, soy sauce, egg and meat (Yamato pork tonkatsu/curry, Yamato beef hamburg loco-moco, fried prawns, katsuo tataki, tamago-kake-gohan). No labeled vegan menu exists; a plant-based diner must select carefully and ask about dressings/dashi. Verdict: limited plant-forward options, not a vegan destination.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市三条本町1-1 JR奈良駅ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 奈良市三条本町1番 (JR Nara Station area); within Nara box.
neighborhood: JR奈良駅構内1F「奈良のうまいものプラザ」(改札へ上がるエスカレーター横)
seats_text: カウンター5席、テーブル24席
hours_raw: 7:00～21:00（モーニング 7:00-10:30 LO / ランチ 11:00-14:30 LO / ディナー 17:00-20:30 LO）。各営業帯ともラストオーダーは閉店30分前。年末年始休業・時短営業あり。
closed_days: 無休（年末年始は休業。時短営業の場合あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, tourist-friendly counter-and-table spot right inside JR Nara Station where pointing at the photo menu and a few words of Japanese will get you through comfortably, though staff may not speak much English.
caveats: Operated as a prefectural antenna-shop restaurant by an industry association (一般社団法人 奈良のうまいもの会), so it is independent but institutionally run rather than a private family business. Menu and hours can shift seasonally; morning service is a Japanese breakfast (tamago-kake-gohan), lunch is the かご農膳 with vegetable buffet, and the place doubles as a casual pre-train drinking spot (昼呑み / local sake tasting set).

bio.background: Right beside the ticket gates on the first floor of JR Nara Station sits 古都華 (Kotoka), the in-house restaurant of the 奈良のうまいものプラザ — Nara Prefecture's antenna shop. It is run by the 80-member 奈良のうまいもの会 (Nara Fine Foods Association), led by chairman Kenichi Doudo, whose mission is to raise the profile of Nara's food and broadcast its appeal to visitors arriving and departing through the station.
bio.origin: Nara City — JR Nara Station, central Nara.
bio.specialty: The headline dish is the lunchtime かご農膳 (kago-nōzen), a set meal served with a help-yourself vegetable buffet (野菜バイキング) of seasonal, farm-direct produce, paired with a choice of mains such as Yamato Pork tonkatsu or curry, Yamato Beef hamburg loco-moco, fried prawns, or katsuo tataki. Mornings bring a tamago-kake-gohan breakfast made with fresh eggs and soy sauce from Gojo; every dish is built on Nara-grown Hinohikari rice. Takeaway bento versions of the kago-nōzen are also offered.
bio.philosophy: The association treats Nara as 'the roots of Japanese cuisine,' aiming to carry forward ancient food traditions (such as the dairy-based seasonings so and raku) while creating new Nara specialties. The 'farm-direct' (農園直送) name reflects sourcing produce straight from vetted farms that pass soil testing and quality checks.

anecdotes.text: The vegetable buffet draws on fresh seasonal produce delivered directly from superior farms that have passed strict checks including soil testing.

A local-sake tasting set (利き酒セット, 1,320 yen with two small dishes) is popular, and many travelers use the restaurant for a quick drink while waiting for their train — fitting, since Nara is regarded as a birthplace of Japanese refined sake.
Every dish uses Nara-grown Hinohikari rice, and the morning set features fresh eggs and soy sauce from Gojo City.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes panko-breaded and deep-fried items (Yamato pork tonkatsu, fried prawns) alongside curry and hamburg steak, all wheat-containing; no gluten-free segregation or dedicated fryer is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard soy sauce (which contains wheat) is used, including in the morning tamago-kake-gohan with Gojo-made soy sauce; no wheat-free tamari is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The vegetable buffet is plant-based produce, but the surrounding set meals center on meat, egg and fish (Yamato pork, Yamato beef, fried prawns, katsuo tataki, egg-over-rice); dressings and seasonings likely use dashi/soy and no vegan labeling is provided.

positives.text: A fresh vegetable buffet of seasonal farm-direct Yamato produce and Nara-grown Hinohikari rice gives plant-forward diners genuine options to assemble a vegetable-heavy plate.

cuisine: 大和郷土料理 (Yamato regional cuisine)
neighborhood: ならまち (Naramachi), 中新屋町
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a traditional Japanese kaiseki/regional restaurant where dishes rely on soy sauce, miso and seasonings that typically contain wheat, and the signature Asuka nabe and bento sets include items prepared with standard Japanese condiments. There is no stated dedicated gluten-free preparation, so a celiac diner would need to ask directly and likely cannot be assured of safety. Yoshino kuzu (arrowroot) used here is naturally gluten-free, but individual dishes are not certified GF.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant. The cuisine features Yamato vegetables and Yoshino kuzu, but the kitchen uses fish/bonito dashi broadly, the signature Asuka nabe is a chicken hot pot with milk, and "so" (蘇) is an ancient dairy product. Some vegetable-forward items may be adaptable, but most dishes are not vegan, so anyone seeking vegan food must inquire in advance.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市中新屋町15
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the building level (中新屋町15); coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 16 seats: 4 at the sunken-hearth (horigotatsu/irori) area and 12 chair seats; no counter. The official site notes maximum capacity around 24 but groups are currently limited.
hours_raw: Tabelog: Tue–Sun & holidays 11:30–14:00, 18:00–19:30; Thursday 11:30–14:00 (dinner closed); Monday closed. Official site lists lunch 11:30–15:00, dinner 18:00–21:00. Asuka nabe is reservation-only by 15:00 the previous business day.
closed_days: Mondays (open and closed the following day if Monday is a public holiday); Thursday evenings; year-end/New Year period.
cultural_comfort_note: A calm traditional machiya welcoming to international visitors; a few words of Japanese and a phone or online reservation (especially for the Asuka nabe) will make the visit smooth.
caveats: Hours differ between sources (Tabelog shows earlier last orders than the official site); confirm current times and reserve ahead, particularly for the Asuka nabe which must be booked by 15:00 the day before. Not a gluten-free or vegan destination.

bio.background: Harishin occupies a machiya (traditional town house) said to be roughly 250 years old, the former premises of a money-changer, on the western side of Naramachi near the World Heritage Gangō-ji temple and facing the ancient Kamitsumichi (上ツ道) road that once linked Fujiwara-kyō and Heijō-kyō.
bio.specialty: Signature dishes are the Kamitsumichi bento (かみつみち弁当), the Asuka nabe (飛鳥鍋, a milk-and-chicken hot pot rooted in Nara-period cuisine), and shokado-style bento built around Yamato vegetables, Yoshino kuzu, persimmon vinegar and the ancient dairy delicacy 'so' (蘇).
bio.philosophy: The owners frame the restaurant around the history of the Kamitsumichi pilgrimage road, where travelers were once sent off with boxed meals, and aim to offer a quiet, unhurried meal away from crowded tourist spots.

anecdotes.text: The dishes deliberately revive Nara-period and Asuka-era foods such as 'so' (an ancient cheese-like dairy food) and the Asuka nabe, tying the menu to the area's role along the old Kamitsumichi road.
The setting includes sunken-hearth (irori/horigotatsu) seating in the historic machiya, popular with families and overseas visitors.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Traditional Yamato regional cuisine here relies on standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, miso) that typically contain wheat; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu centers on fish/bonito dashi, a chicken-and-milk Asuka nabe, and the dairy delicacy 'so'; dishes are generally not vegan.

positives.text: Yoshino kuzu (arrowroot) featured here is naturally gluten-free, and the seasonal Yamato-vegetable focus means some plant-forward small dishes may be available on request.

cuisine: Okonomiyaki / teppanyaki / yakisoba (savory griddle pancakes and fried noodles)
neighborhood: Tarui-cho, near Sarusawa Pond, about a 5-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: This is an okonomiyaki, negiyaki and yakisoba house, all of which are built on a wheat-flour batter and wheat noodles, so it is not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten avoidance. Sauces (okonomiyaki sauce, yakisoba sauce) and soy sauce also typically contain wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation or separate cooking surfaces.
vegan_detail: The food relies heavily on egg, meat, seafood and fish-based ingredients. The signature 大仏焼 (Daibutsu-yaki) negiyaki includes squid, shrimp, beef, fish cake and egg, and okonomiyaki/yakisoba batters and sauces generally use egg and fish dashi. No documented vegan options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市樽井町9
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned a single feature for 奈良県奈良市樽井町9; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Around 53-55 seats across small raised tatami-style and table seating; can accommodate groups of 20-50 for private events.
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly, long-running neighborhood griddle house used to families and visitors alike; a little Japanese helps, but pointing at the picture menu and a smile will get you a warm welcome.
caveats: Wheat-batter and wheat-noodle cuisine with egg, meat and seafood throughout, so it is not appropriate for gluten-free or vegan diners. Closures are irregular, so it is worth checking before a special trip.

bio.background: Kameya has been griddling okonomiyaki on Tarui-cho since 1965, making it a half-century-old fixture in the old quarter of Nara just steps from Sarusawa Pond and a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. A big red lantern marks the door, and over the decades it has fed everyone from local families and office parties to the steady stream of travelers passing through the historic center.
bio.specialty: The house signature is the Daibutsu-yaki, a giant scallion pancake roughly 40cm across with the characters for 'Great Buddha' (大佛) drawn on top in mayonnaise. It is a thin-batter negiyaki rather than a thick okonomiyaki, packed with scallion, squid, shrimp, beef, seasoned konnyaku, fish cake and egg, and has been on the menu for over twenty years. Beyond it the kitchen runs a wide spread of okonomiyaki, negiyaki, teppanyaki, yakisoba and playful originals like an udon-based 'inaka' pancake and a curry noodle dish.

anecdotes.text: The Daibutsu-yaki is decorated with the characters for the Great Buddha piped in mayonnaise, a nod to Nara's most famous landmark, and despite its roughly 40cm size the thin batter makes it lighter and easier to finish than it looks.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core dishes are wheat-flour okonomiyaki/negiyaki and wheat yakisoba noodles cooked on a shared teppan griddle, so heavy gluten cross-contact is inherent.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Okonomiyaki and yakisoba sauces, as well as soy sauce typically used here, contain wheat; no wheat-free sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature negiyaki and general menu include egg, beef, squid, shrimp and fish cake, and batters generally use egg and fish dashi.

cuisine: 海鮮料理・和食・居酒屋（Seafood / Japanese / izakaya）
neighborhood: 三条本町（JR奈良駅すぐ）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市三条本町4-32 中室ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良市三条本町4-32 returned [135.819061, 34.680141], within the Nara box.
seats_text: About 22 seats: a counter where you can watch the cooking, tables, and a tatami room for groups.
hours_raw: Mon-Sat 11:00-14:00 (L.O. 13:30) and 17:00-21:30 (L.O. 21:00); Sunday closed. Also closed 1st and 3rd Thursdays.
closed_days: Sundays; also 1st and 3rd Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: This is a local seafood izakaya with no English signage or menu noted, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will help you order comfortably.
vegan_detail: A seafood-centered izakaya: sashimi, fish, tempura and braised fish dominate the menu, and dishes rely on fish dashi and soy sauce. No vegan or vegetarian offering is documented, and there is no indication staff can prepare plant-only meals.
gf_detail: No gluten-free provision is documented. Menus center on soy-sauce-marinated sashimi bowls, tempura (wheat batter) and braised fish, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. Not suitable for celiacs.
caveats: Hours vary slightly between local sources (one cites dinner to 22:30); Tabelog's L.O.-detailed hours were used. The 1st/3rd Thursday closures make the schedule irregular, so confirm before a midweek visit.

bio.background: A seafood izakaya tucked into the Nakamuro Building a one-minute walk from JR Nara Station's east side. In landlocked Nara, a fish-focused restaurant is something of a rarity, which is part of what makes this small place locally beloved.
bio.specialty: The kitchen builds its menu around fish brought in fresh each day: sashimi and seafood rice bowls, tempura sets, and braised fish such as simmered sea bream. A popular weekday lunch set pairs a rotating main with braised fish, sashimi, side dishes, rice, soup and pickles for around 1,000 yen.
bio.philosophy: Freshness first: the offering changes with each day's catch rather than a fixed list.

anecdotes.text: Though billed as an izakaya for its generous drink list, the wood-toned, calm interior feels closer to a refined Japanese restaurant, with a counter where you can watch the cooks at work, tables, and a tatami room for groups.
A Tuesday-only seafood bowl of yellowtail and salmon dressed in sesame sauce and topped with tobiko, served with stewed fish collar, tofu and clear broth, is a remembered favorite among regulars.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes include soy-sauce and sesame-dressed sashimi bowls; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built on fish and fish dashi, with sashimi, tempura and braised fish as core dishes; no plant-only preparations are documented.

cuisine: 中華料理
neighborhood: 東向商店街（近鉄奈良駅すぐ）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Chinese restaurant where wheat is everywhere: ramen, champon, fried rice seasoned with soy sauce, spring rolls, sweet-and-sour pork batter, and gravies thickened/flavored with wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu or accommodation is documented, and cross-contact in a shared wok kitchen is unavoidable. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood-forward Cantonese/town-Chinese menu (sweet-and-sour pork, crab omelet, shrimp chili, char siu, etc.). Chinese kitchens typically use chicken/pork stock and animal fats in stir-fries, and dishes are seasoned with soy and oyster-type sauces. Some vegetable stir-fries or plain fried rice might be adaptable on request, but nothing is documented as vegan and the default broths/seasonings are animal-based. Vegans should ask carefully and expect very limited safe options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町18
geocode_note: GSI address search returned the exact street address 東向南町18 at [135.828491, 34.682625], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Small ground-floor town-Chinese dining room (exact seat count not published); a second-floor banquet hall seats up to about 30 guests.
hours_raw: 11:00～21:00（L.O. 20:30）／木曜定休。地元情報では10:30開店との記載もあり。
closed_days: Thursdays（木曜日）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a casual, generations-old neighborhood Chinese spot where ordering is easy by pointing at set meals, but staff likely speak mainly Japanese, so a few basic Japanese phrases or a translation app will help.
caveats: Hours differ slightly across listings (Tabelog shows 11:00 open with L.O. 20:30; a local guide lists 10:30 open). Ground-floor seat count is not published. Allergen/dietary accommodation is undocumented; assumptions about adaptability are not confirmed by the restaurant.

bio.background: 上海楼 (Shanghairou) is a long-standing town Chinese restaurant tucked into the Higashimuki shopping arcade, just a couple of minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It has been cooking on this same spot since 1945, the year the war ended, which makes it one of central Nara's enduring neighborhood institutions, with loyal customers who have been coming back across generations.
bio.specialty: Honest, home-style Cantonese-leaning Chinese cooking served in a cheerful red-tabled dining room. Favorites include fried rice (炒飯), champon, sweet-and-sour pork (酢豚), crab omelet (かに玉), shrimp chili (エビチリ), spring rolls, mapo-tofu rice bowls and tenshinhan, with everything from single plates to affordable lunch sets and full banquet courses.
bio.philosophy: A dependable 'familiar neighborhood Chinese place' that pairs genuine kitchen skill with prices and warmth that keep regulars returning, rather than chasing media attention.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant has operated continuously since 1945 and is fondly remembered by multi-generational regulars, with a second-floor banquet hall that hosts gatherings of up to about 30 people.
Described by a local food guide as a quietly skilled spot that, despite little media coverage, serves orthodox yet genuinely authentic Chinese cuisine.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A traditional Chinese kitchen using a shared wok, ramen/champon noodles, soy-based sauces and wheat-thickened gravies; gluten cross-contact is effectively unavoidable and no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu relies on standard Chinese seasonings (soy sauce, oyster-type sauces); ordinary Japanese/Chinese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Stir-fries and rice dishes are cooked in shared woks alongside pork, seafood and egg dishes, so cross-contact with animal ingredients is likely; no vegan preparation is documented.

name_ja: シャトードール もちいどの店
cuisine: Bakery and wood-fired pizza cafe (Italian-leaning)
neighborhood: Mochiidono shopping arcade, central Nara (near Kintetsu Nara Station)
gf_detail: This is a bakery and pizzeria — its core products (bread, sandwiches, pastries, the wood-fired pizza dough) are all wheat-based. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated GF preparation area, and flour is handled throughout the open kitchen and bread counter. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: No documented vegan menu. Offerings center on bread, sandwiches, pastries (which typically use butter/egg/milk), wood-fired pizzas with cheese (Margherita, Quattro Formaggi), and meat/seafood items like ajillo and beef curry donuts. A plain tomato-sauce pizza or simple bread could potentially be made vegan, but this is not stated anywhere — a vegan diner would need to ask, with limited options likely.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町29-1
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned the building-level point for 奈良市餅飯殿町29-1; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Approximately 60 seats including terrace seating; spacious table area at the back behind the bread counter.
hours_raw: 8:00-20:00 (morning 8:00-11:00, lunch 11:00-14:00, then cafe until 20:00, last order 19:00)
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, walk-in bakery-cafe where you can point at bread at the counter or order pizza at a table, so a foreign student gets by easily with a smile and a few words of Japanese.
caveats: Part of a local Nara bakery brand (Chateau d'Or) with several branches; this Mochiidono location is its food-focused cafe format with a wood-fired pizza oven. Hours are split across morning/lunch/cafe dayparts and closures are irregular, so confirm the day you visit. Not a gluten-free or vegan destination.

bio.background: Chateau d'Or is a Nara bakery brand that has been baking for more than 50 years, with its flagship on Konishi-dori and further branches reaching the Higashimuki-kita arcade and beyond. The Mochiidono shopping-arcade location is its food-focused format, opened in the former Mitsuhashi building: a long space with a bread-sales counter at the front, an open kitchen with a wood-fired oven in the middle, and roomy table seating plus a bright terrace at the back.
bio.specialty: Wood-fired pizzas baked to order (Margherita, Quattro Formaggi finished with honey, and a light-crust Cicchincicelli with whitebait, mizuna, parmesan and tomato), alongside the bakery's beloved Daibutsu (Great Buddha) anpan stuffed with a generous 200g of red-bean paste, plus sandwiches, cream-filled pastries and beef curry donuts.
bio.philosophy: The bakery's long-standing motto is to make bread customers can eat with confidence, leaning on natural ingredients and a body-friendly approach so families and children can enjoy it; the company frames its work as service that quietly blends into everyday local life.

anecdotes.text: The shop runs across the whole day, from morning bread and coffee through a wood-fired pizza lunch to an afternoon cafe with wine, all a few minutes' walk from Sarusawa Pond and the Mochiidono arcade.
The Daibutsu anpan is packed with about 200g of red-bean filling, a nod to the city's Great Buddha.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Open bakery-and-pizzeria layout with a bread counter at the front and flour-based pizza dough worked in the central open kitchen, so airborne and surface flour contamination is unavoidable.

cuisine: Italian (creative, local Nara ingredients)
neighborhood: Naramachi (near Sarusawa Pond, ~7 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station)
address_ja: 〒630-8374 奈良県奈良市今御門町22
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市今御門町22; within Nara box.
seats_text: 32 seats across two floors; 1st floor mainly table seating, 2nd floor tatami seating (upper floor often reservation-based)
hours_raw: 11:30-14:00 (lunch, L.O. 13:30), 14:30-17:00 (cafe, L.O. 16:00), 18:00-21:00 (dinner, L.O. 20:00/20:30). Closed Tuesdays, with occasional irregular closures.
closed_days: Tuesdays (plus occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, family-friendly local Italian spot in a historic townhouse where a few words of Japanese and a friendly attitude will go a long way, though no special foreigner support is documented.
gf_detail: This is a pasta-forward Italian restaurant; wheat pasta is the core of the menu and there is no documentation of gluten-free dishes or celiac handling. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: The kitchen leans heavily on garden and local Nara vegetables (and a vegetable shaved-ice menu in cafe hours), but signature dishes use Yamato pork, fish, dairy (cream sauces with sake lees), and cheese. No vegan-labeled dishes are documented; a vegetarian could likely be accommodated for some vegetable plates if you ask, but assume animal products/dairy by default.

bio.background: Bambuno occupies a renovated townhouse more than a century old in the historic Naramachi district, a short stroll from Sarusawa Pond. It is a small, independently run creative Italian kitchen built around vegetables from its own garden and ingredients grown around Nara, served across two floors of table and tatami seating.
bio.specialty: The house signature is the 'Naramachi Pasta,' a cream sauce made with sake lees from Harushika Brewery's daiginjo, tossed with Yamato pork and local mushrooms and seasonal Nara vegetables that rotate daily. Another signature, 'Pasta Yamato,' leans on roasted local tea. During cafe hours the kitchen is known for inventive vegetable shaved ice (ベジ氷) such as sweet-potato-and-ruby-chocolate or tomato-orange-caramel.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen describes its food as 'Nara Italian' born from local Nara ingredients meeting on the plate, celebrating produce grown nearby and in its own garden.

anecdotes.text: The Naramachi Pasta was developed in collaboration with the nearby Harushika Brewery, using sake lees from its daiginjo in the cream sauce alongside Yamato pork and local vegetables.
In cafe hours the restaurant serves unusual vegetable-based shaved ice ('Vegi-gori'), turning Nara vegetables into desserts.

positives.text: Cooking centers on garden-grown and local Nara vegetables, so plant-forward vegetable dishes are part of the regular menu.

caveats: No owner/chef name is documented in the sources fetched. Dietary handling (cross-contamination, dedicated fryer, soy sauce, allergy procedures) is not documented anywhere; vegan/vegetarian accommodation is inferred only from the vegetable-heavy menu and should be confirmed directly. Hours are the same three service blocks every open day per the official site; exact last-order times vary slightly between sources (dinner L.O. 20:00 vs 20:30).

cuisine: Traditional sushi (sushi-ya); lunch also offers chirashi, tekka-don, and udon
neighborhood: 元林院町／ならまち (Motorin'in-cho, Naramachi)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市元林院町29
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市元林院町29, within the central Nara bounding box.
seats_text: Counter seating, sunken/tatami floor seating, and a second-floor banquet room; small private room seats about 4. Exact seat count not published.
