hours_raw: Dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); lunch around 12:00-14:00 on an irregular schedule. Closed Mondays. (Tabelog lists dinner 17:00-21:00; the Nara gourmet writeup lists 12:00-14:00 and 17:00-22:00.)
closed_days: Mondays (月曜日); lunch service is on an irregular schedule
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, traditional sushi counter run by a gentle proprietor; an English menu is not advertised, so basic Japanese or a translation app helps, but the warm hospitality makes it approachable.
gf_detail: This is a traditional sushi restaurant. Soy sauce (made with wheat), tempura/fried items, and wheat-based udon are standard, and sushi rice is seasoned with vinegar that may include additives; there is no indication of gluten-free accommodation or a dedicated preparation area. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: A sushi and sashimi specialist centered on raw fish, conger eel, salmon roe, egg, and fish-based dashi (e.g. in miso soup and udon broth). There are no documented vegan dishes; not a vegan-friendly destination.
caveats: Hours differ between sources: Tabelog lists dinner 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30) only, while a local gourmet writeup lists 12:00-14:00 and 17:00-22:00. Lunch is described as an irregular schedule, so confirm by phone (0742-22-2643) before visiting for lunch. Seat count is not published.

bio.background: A long-established sushi house that has operated for more than 70 years in the historic Naramachi district of central Nara, tucked into the old Motorin'in-cho lanes near Mochiidono shopping street, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The interior keeps a traditional feel with a clean white-wood counter, sunken floor and tatami seating, and a second-floor banquet room, with a tank displaying fresh fish such as horse mackerel and scabbardfish.
bio.specialty: Edomae-style nigiri and generously cut sashimi; a representative nigiri course runs through shrimp, tamago, yellowtail, salt-dressed squid, salmon roe, chu-toro, ponzu-dressed flounder, simmered conger eel, and torched scallop, finished with red miso soup. At lunch the kitchen also turns out chirashi bowls, tekka-don, and udon, with daily specials chalked on a whiteboard. A well-chosen sake list includes the local Kaze no Mori junmai daiginjo.
bio.philosophy: The proprietor is known for a calm, gentle manner and the floor staff for warm service, creating a refined but unintimidating grown-up atmosphere where regulars feel at ease.

anecdotes.text: The shop is described as having a calm, gentle proprietor and warm hospitality, giving it the air of a refined adult sushi house that nonetheless never feels inaccessible.
It maintains a family connection with the nearby restaurant Mangyoku.

cuisine: Old-style independent kissaten / yoshoku cafe — known for omurice (omelette rice), pasta, and pizza
neighborhood: Mochiidono Center Street, about a 3-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町24-3
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町24-3 returned [135.829193, 34.681015], within the Nara bounding box.
seats_text: Seat count not published. It is a small independent cafe within the Mochiidono arcade.
hours_raw: 11:00-20:00 (per Tabelog and the Mochiidono shopping-street listing)
closed_days: Irregular holidays (不定休) per the Mochiidono shopping-street listing; Tabelog lists no fixed closed day.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a cozy, locally loved Japanese cafe where a friendly greeting and basic Japanese will go a long way, as English menus or service are not guaranteed.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The core menu is built around wheat-heavy yoshoku dishes — pasta, pizza, and breaded/sauced items — and there is no indication of gluten-free options or allergen handling, so it is not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan options are documented. The menu centers on egg-and-meat dishes (omurice with chicken rice, hamburger bowls, cream pasta, seafood) and dairy desserts, so it is not suitable for vegans.

bio.background: Pinocchio is an old-style independent cafe in central Nara that relocated to the Mochiidono Center Street arcade from its former home on Sanjo-dori, a spot that had been popular enough to serve as a setting for anime and dramas. The current location is decorated with lamps, greenery, and a waterfall-themed wall, and diners can watch the flow of the shopping street as they eat.
bio.specialty: Although it is styled as a cafe, most customers come for the omurice: a traditional thin-egg omelette over chicken rice with onions and mushrooms, available in variations such as cheese and seafood. The menu also runs to hearty fare like curry doria with smoked chicken and a hot-spring egg, mentaiko (cod roe) cream pasta with shiso and seaweed, hamburger rice bowls, pizza, and desserts including rare cheesecake and chocolate cake.

anecdotes.text: Pinocchio moved into the Mochiidono arcade from Sanjo-dori, where its previous location had appeared as a setting in anime and dramas.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by wheat-based dishes (pasta, pizza, breaded yoshoku), with no documented gluten-free preparation or separation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes use eggs, meat, seafood, and dairy; no plant-based menu is documented.

caveats: This independent Nara Pinocchio is distinct from the unrelated "喫茶店ピノキオ" chain operated by Fujio Food System (cafe-pinokio.com) and the "グリル＆パフェ ピノキオ" / 富雄南 locations. Seat count and exact closed days are not published; hours and irregular-holiday note come from Tabelog and the Mochiidono shopping-street directory (last updated Nov 2021), so confirm current hours before visiting.

lead: cervo bianco（チェルボ ビアンコ）
name_ja: cervo bianco（チェルボ ビアンコ）
cuisine: Italian (stone-oven pizza and house-made gelato made with Nara-grown ingredients), in a renovated Edo-period townhouse
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The headline dishes are stone-oven pizza (wheat-based dough), and no gluten-free menu, gluten-free crust, or allergen handling is documented anywhere. A celiac traveler should treat this as unsafe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-oriented place. The signature pizzas are heavy on cheese (mozzarella, gorgonzola, camembert, grana) and the famous gelato is dairy-based using local farm milk. There may be a plain vegetable/tomato pizza if you ask for it without cheese, but nothing vegan is documented and no plant-based menu exists. Strict vegans should confirm directly and expect very limited options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高御門町7
neighborhood: ならまち（猿沢池近く）
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 奈良県奈良市高御門町7 to [135.8293, 34.677406], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 40 seats (counter and tables), plus a private tatami room that can hold a larger group.
hours_raw: 11:00-22:00 (17:00以降は予約のみの営業 / after 17:00 reservation-only). Note: the official site lists daytime as 11:00-16:00 with reservations required after 16:00; evening service runs by reservation. Closed irregularly (不定休).
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, tourist-friendly townhouse cafe in Naramachi where a basic greeting and pointing at the pizza or gelato menu will get you a warm welcome, though staff may have limited English.

bio.background: cervo bianco opened in June 2018 inside a renovated Edo-period machiya townhouse in the heart of Naramachi, near Sarusawa Pond. It is run by Ashibiya Honpo, a Nara pickle maker founded in the late Edo period, who wanted to breathe new life into the district's aging townhouses as longtime residents and shops disappeared. The dining room keeps the original exposed wooden beams and looks out onto a courtyard garden, and the central stone pizza oven sits where the house's old bathhouse once stood, with the chimney left in place as a memory of the building.
bio.specialty: Stone-oven pizzas built around Nara ingredients and house-made gelato. The signature pizza, 'Ashibi (馬酔木),' tops the dough with the family's Nara pickles and four cheeses (gorgonzola, camembert, mozzarella and grana); the oven's heat mellows the pickle's sharpness while keeping its umami. The gelato uses milk from nearby Uemura Farm, the local 'Kotohana' strawberry, and pesticide-free Yamato tea from Tsukigase.
bio.philosophy: Enjoy NARA Flavor: let visitors taste and feel Nara through restored townhouse architecture, locally grown produce, and the white-deer symbolism woven through the space. The name 'cervo bianco' means 'white deer' in Italian, a nod to the legend that a deity arrived in Nara riding a white deer, the origin of the city's famous deer.

anecdotes.text: The shop is operated by Ashibiya Honpo, a Nara pickle maker established in the late Edo period, as part of an effort to preserve and revive Naramachi's old townhouses.
Chef Hiroki Kawabata trained in Italian and French cuisine and spent time cooking in Canada, which informs his creative takes on traditional dishes.
The central stone oven was built on the footprint of the townhouse's former bathhouse, and the old chimney was kept as an architectural reminder of the building's past.

positives.text: Many pizzas and gelato flavors are built on identifiable single-origin Nara ingredients (local farm milk, named strawberry and tea varieties, house pickles), so a diner can ask clearly about what is in each dish.

caveats: No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published on the official site or in the sources reviewed; dietary details should be confirmed directly with the restaurant. Hours differ between listings (Tabelog shows 11:00-22:00 with reservation-only after 17:00; the official site shows daytime to 16:00 with reservations after) so the day-grid uses the broader Tabelog range. The under-30 (budget) question is unsure: the signature pizza alone is about 1,800 yen, so a full meal may exceed a tight budget.

cuisine: Tea house (Yamato tea, Taiwanese tea, lei cha / 擂茶) and fluffy shaved ice (kakigori) served year-round
neighborhood: 小西町・小西さくら通り商店街（コトモール1F）
gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are documented. The menu centers on tea drinks and shaved ice (kakigori), which can be naturally low in gluten, but signature items like the lei cha (擂茶) are made from a ground mix of "20 kinds of grains and legumes" that may include wheat/barley, and sweets such as parfaits and cakes typically contain wheat. There is no information on dedicated equipment or cross-contamination controls, so a celiac diner would need to ask in detail about each item.
vegan_detail: Plain Yamato/Taiwanese teas are vegan, but the popular shaved ice and latte items use milk (e.g., hojicha latte ice, strawberry milk ice) and parfaits/cakes likely contain dairy. No vegan labeling or plant-milk options are documented; a vegan diner should ask which syrups and toppings are dairy-free.

bio.background: Ochanoko is a small tea shop tucked into the glass-fronted entrance of the Kotomall building along the Konishi Sakura-dori shopping arcade, a two-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The bright, transparent storefront sells leaf tea while also serving drinks and desserts in-house. It centers on two tea traditions: locally roasted Yamato tea (大和茶) from Nara Prefecture and Taiwanese tea, with around twenty kinds of tea on offer.
bio.specialty: The signature offering is lei cha (擂茶), a savory-sweet drink and shaved-ice syrup made from about twenty kinds of grains and legumes ground into powder, giving a distinctive umami depth rather than plain sweetness. The shop is best known for its fluffy kakigori, served year-round rather than only in summer: the ice is cooled slightly before shaving so it comes off paper-thin and melts lightly on the tongue. Popular flavors include hojicha latte (made with Nara roasted Yamato tea and milk), lei cha, matcha, and a strawberry-milk version using freeze-dried Nara Asuka Ruby strawberries.
bio.philosophy: The shop blends traditional Japanese and Taiwanese tea culture with contemporary desserts, emphasizing local Nara ingredients and an airy, delicate shaved-ice texture.

anecdotes.text: The hojicha latte shaved ice, made with Nara-prefecture roasted Yamato tea and milk, was an opening signature item, and the lei cha (擂茶) versions made from twenty kinds of ground grains and legumes are among the most popular.

address_ja: 奈良県奈良市小西町35-2 コトモール1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from the building address 奈良県奈良市小西町35-2; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Seat count not documented; a small tea shop with limited in-store seating, primarily a takeout/tea-retail counter with some café service.
closed_days: Closed on certain third Wednesdays of the month (irregular monthly closures); confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, welcoming local tea-and-shaved-ice shop where a foreign student can comfortably order by pointing at the menu; a few words of Japanese help, but English support is not guaranteed.
caveats: Seat count and payment-method details are not documented; the shop has irregular monthly closures (some third Wednesdays), so confirm hours before visiting. No official website was found; information is drawn from listing and local guide pages.

lead: イタリアン キャラベル
name_ja: イタリアン キャラベル
cuisine: Italian café-bar; fresh handmade pasta, pizza, and Western dishes, set inside a vintage clothing and goods select shop
neighborhood: Mochiidono / Mochiidono Center Street arcade, Naramachi area (about 5 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町14番地 西村ビル2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町14番地 returned [135.829178, 34.68055], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Seat count not published; a small 2nd-floor café-bar space within a select shop, described as welcoming to solo diners.
hours_raw: Tue–Sun 11:30–15:30 lunch, 17:30–21:00 dinner (Tabelog). Official/arcade listing also describes lunch 11:30–15:00, café 15:00–18:00, dinner 18:30–21:30. Closed Mondays.
closed_days: Mondays (定休日). Year-end/New Year schedule varies.
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, casual arcade café-bar where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you by, though staff and menus are mainly in Japanese so a little effort or a translation app helps.
gf_detail: This is a fresh-pasta-focused Italian kitchen, so the core menu is built on wheat (handmade pasta, pizza). No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or celiac accommodation is documented. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Most dishes feature meat, bacon, cheese, or cream (carbonara, amatriciana, braised-beef hayashi rice, tiramisu). A plain tomato-based pasta or a salad might be adaptable if requested, but nothing is confirmed vegan, so vegans should ask in advance and expect very limited options.
caveats: Hours differ slightly between Tabelog (11:30–15:30 / 17:30–21:00) and the arcade/official listing (lunch–café–dinner spanning 11:30–21:30); confirm the current schedule when visiting. Seat count is unpublished. No vegan or gluten-free information is documented, so allergy-sensitive diners should confirm directly.

bio.background: Caravel is a tucked-away Italian café-bar on the second floor of a building along Mochiidono Center Street, the historic covered arcade that links central Nara to the old Naramachi district, a few minutes on foot from Kintetsu Nara Station. Unusually, it shares its home with a select shop of vintage clothing and curios, so the dining room is dressed in retro finds and collectibles that give it the feel of a stylish, lived-in hideaway rather than a standard trattoria.
bio.specialty: A fresh-pasta specialist built around the idea of 'casual but authentic Italian.' The signature is a thick, chewy handmade carbonara with generous chunks of bacon; the kitchen also turns out a spicy-tomato amatriciana, a garlicky peperoncino, Margherita pizza, a red-wine braised-beef hayashi rice, and a much-loved homemade tiramisu offered in full and half sizes, paired with a good range of wine and beer.
bio.philosophy: The house concept is 気軽に食べられる本格イタリアン — serving genuine, handmade Italian food in a relaxed, everyday setting that even solo diners can drop into comfortably.
roles: café-bar inside a vintage select shop

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is run as a fresh-pasta specialist with 'authentic pasta' as its stated concept, set within a select shop selling vintage clothing and accessories, and is described as a welcoming spot even for women dining alone.
Reviewers single out the carbonara of thick, chewy fresh pasta with large-cut bacon, alongside amatriciana, peperoncino, Margherita pizza, red-wine braised-beef hayashi rice, and homemade tiramisu.

cuisine: Kamaboko and satsuma-age (fish cake / deep-fried fish paste) specialty shop with freshly fried eat-in items
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. The shop specializes in kamaboko and satsuma-age (surimi fish-paste products), which are typically bound with wheat starch and other binders, and signature items such as the butter potato combine potato with fish paste. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation is documented. Celiac travelers should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Every product is built on fish paste (surimi) — kamaboko and satsuma-age are fish products by definition. Even the butter potato is fish paste mixed with potato and dairy butter. There are no plant-based options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町16
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the chō address 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町16; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: Mochiidono Center-gai shopping arcade, central Nara (about 5 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, near Kofuku-ji and Sarusawa Pond)
seats_text: Small eat-in counter space for enjoying freshly fried items; primarily a takeaway storefront, exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 9:00–19:30 (Tabelog); some sources list 9:00–18:00. Open daily with irregular closures (不定休).
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); some listings cite Thursdays.
cultural_comfort_note: An open-front, tourist-friendly counter shop in a busy arcade where you can point at the freshly fried fish cakes you want and pay by card or QR — a friendly smile and a few Japanese words are plenty.
caveats: Address number varies across listings (16 on Tabelog/narakko, 12 on the arcade directory); hours and closed day differ slightly between sources (9:00–19:30 vs 9:00–18:00; irregular vs Thursday). This is a fish-cake shop, not a sit-down restaurant.

bio.background: Uoman Honten has been making kamaboko and satsuma-age in Nara since 1901, a family trade now spanning well over 120 years. It sits in the Mochiidono Center-gai, the historic covered shopping arcade that links central Nara toward Naramachi, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station and the Kofuku-ji and Sarusawa Pond area.
bio.specialty: House-made satsuma-age (deep-fried fish cakes) in many varieties — burdock, onion, ginger, kikurage mushroom and more — plus playful original creations. The signature item is the Butter Potato: diced potato folded into fish paste with Hokkaido butter and fried, beloved as a hot snack to eat while strolling the arcade. Other inventions include bite-size NeRi Balls, the donut-shaped NeRing, and a centennial '100-Year Burger.'
bio.philosophy: Carefully selected ingredients and time-honored, hand-crafted methods carried down through the generations, paired with a willingness to keep inventing new shapes and flavors for a new audience.

anecdotes.text: The Butter Potato has become a small local celebrity, with the shop's fried specialties featured on national TV variety programs.
The open storefront sells items hot and freshly fried, designed for visitors to enjoy on the move through Naramachi, with a small eat-in space available.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Products are surimi-based fish cakes (kamaboko, satsuma-age) that are typically bound with wheat starch; everything is deep-fried in shared oil. No gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All items are fish-paste based; the signature butter potato also contains dairy butter. No plant-based items are offered.

cuisine: Handmade icing cookies and baked sweets paired with Chinese and Taiwanese tea
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, welcoming cookie-and-tea shop in a covered shopping arcade where you can browse and buy beautifully decorated sweets and curated Chinese teas; pointing and a smile work fine, though a few words of Japanese help.
gf_detail: This is a wheat-based bakery: icing cookies, baked cookies, and gateau au chocolat are made with wheat flour. No gluten-free products or celiac accommodation are documented, and cross-contamination from flour is inherent in a cookie kitchen. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Baked goods are made from scratch with quality ingredients and no unnecessary additives, but standard butter cookies, icing cookies, and chocolate cake typically contain butter, eggs, and milk; no vegan items are documented. The Chinese and Taiwanese teas themselves are plant-based. Vegans should ask, but expect very limited options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町35
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町35番地; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: 餅飯殿町・もちいどのセンター街 (Mochiidono Center-gai arcade), about a 5-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: No seating documented; the shop is primarily a retail/takeout confectionery and tea shop rather than a dine-in cafe. Confirm in person if you wish to sit.
hours_raw: 10:00-19:00; closed Thursdays (定休日 木曜)
closed_days: Thursday
caveats: Primarily a retail/takeout shop for cookies, baked sweets, and tea leaves rather than a sit-down eatery. Custom icing-cookie orders have at times been paused; products are also sold online via Minne and Creema. Wheat-, butter-, and egg-based baking, so not suitable for celiac or strict vegan diets.

bio.background: Kawano Kumiko originally set out to teach icing-cookie classes, but after struggling to reach students she pivoted to opening her own workshop and shop so she could share her designs more widely. After getting her start in the Mochiidono arcade's 夢CUBE (Dream CUBE) incubator program for new businesses, she graduated and reopened KUMI茶菓 as a standalone shop in the arcade on March 31, 2021.
bio.specialty: Hand-decorated icing cookies (a rare specialty in Japan), distinctively flavored baked cookies such as Nara-pickle (奈良漬け) and roasted-tea (ほうじ茶), a rich gateau au chocolat, and carefully selected Chinese and Taiwanese teas chosen by a certified tea master (茶藝師), along with teaware.
bio.philosophy: Kawano wants the shop to be a space that makes people feel 'how cute! how happy!' and a spot worth visiting when in Nara. Her sweets are made by hand with quality ingredients and no unnecessary additives, to the point of not even using baking powder.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits in the covered Mochiidono Center-gai arcade right next to a cat-goods shop, with a pastel storefront, and grew out of the arcade's Dream CUBE small-business incubator before becoming an independent store.
Beyond the signature icing cookies, Kawano bakes unusual local-flavor cookies like Nara-pickle and roasted green tea, reflecting a Nara sense of place.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The shop is a wheat-flour bakery producing cookies and cake, so airborne flour and shared baking surfaces make cross-contamination inherent; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Baked goods are made with quality ingredients and no unnecessary additives, but standard recipes for icing cookies and gateau au chocolat use butter, eggs, and milk; no dedicated vegan preparation is documented.

positives.text: Everything is handmade in small batches with quality ingredients and no unnecessary additives (not even baking powder), so the owner can describe exactly what is in each item if you ask.

cuisine: 定食・食堂（日替わり定食、家庭的和食）
neighborhood: きたまち（花芝商店街・近鉄奈良駅 北へ徒歩約3分）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市花芝町26
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市花芝町26 returned [135.829056, 34.686321], within the Nara box and consistent with the Kitamachi / Hanashiba arcade location a few minutes north of Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: Narrow interior with roughly 30 seats: two-person tables near the entrance, plus counter seating and larger tables toward the back.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (lunch only)
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, no-frills neighborhood lunch spot with a single daily set menu and no English — easiest if you go with someone who reads Japanese or are comfortable pointing and ordering "today's set."
vegan_detail: This is a home-style set-meal shop built around a single daily teishoku that typically includes tempura or fried mains, and the miso soup and dishes use standard Japanese fish-based dashi. There is no documented vegan option and only one menu choice per day, so it is not suitable for vegan diners.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature daily set centers on wheat-battered tempura and fried items, and meals come with miso soup and side dishes that use soy sauce and dashi. There is no gluten-free preparation and no menu flexibility, so it is unsafe for celiacs.

bio.background: Gohan no Ma opened in February 2011 in the Hanashiba shopping arcade in Nara's Kitamachi district, a few minutes' walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station. It runs on a deliberately simple model: open only for lunch, serving just a single daily-changing set meal. From the street the small building looks like an ordinary house when it is closed.
bio.specialty: A daily 'today's set meal' (around 750 yen) with a rotating main — examples include shrimp-and-vegetable tempura or tomato-stewed cabbage rolls — served with rice, a hearty miso soup, salad, and a side dish.
bio.philosophy: Keeping the format simple to one home-style daily set lets the kitchen offer generous, reasonably priced meals made with care.

anecdotes.text: The rice is sourced from Tahara in Nara Prefecture and is praised as genuinely delicious, and the miso soup is unusually substantial, packed with wakame, enoki, fried tofu and cucumber; the tempura oil is light and easy to eat.
Regulars describe it as 'mother's home cooking' (お母さんの家庭料理) — balanced, vegetable-rich lunch sets that feel homemade rather than restaurant-style.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen regularly prepares wheat-battered tempura and fried foods, so cross-contamination with gluten is likely throughout the cooking surfaces and oil.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard home-style Japanese sides and miso soup are served, which in Japan normally use wheat-containing soy sauce and miso; no gluten-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The daily miso soup and dishes use fish-based dashi, and the single daily set typically includes meat or seafood mains, so vegan diners cannot avoid animal ingredients here.

caveats: Sources conflict slightly on closed days: most listings (Ekiten, Yahoo Map text, the local blog) and the long-time format indicate closed Wednesday only with 11:00-15:00 lunch hours; one Tabelog summary suggested closed Wednesday and Friday. The majority and most detailed sources support Wednesday-only closure. No official website found; phone 0742-24-1539. Seat count (~30) and exact daily menu come from a 2012 local blog and may vary.

lead: かやく飯とお酒 かやく
name_ja: かやく飯とお酒 かやく
cuisine: 創作和食・定食
neighborhood: きたまち（北半田中町・近鉄奈良駅近く）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市北半田中町26
geocode_note: Geocoded from full address 奈良県奈良市北半田中町26 via GSI; result [135.832474, 34.68811] falls within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 22 seats inside a renovated traditional machiya townhouse; can accommodate up to about 20 for private events.
hours_raw: Per Tabelog: Wed & Sun 11:30-14:00 (lunch only); Thu/Fri/Sat 11:30-14:00 and 17:30-22:00; closed Mon & Tue. The official site describes lunch from 11:30 (kamadaki kayaku-meshi sets, advance request appreciated) and an evening service of sake and dishes in the old townhouse. Day-specific service varies, so confirm by phone/reservation.
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, locally-run townhouse spot where reservations are appreciated and little English is likely, so a foreign student should be ready to order in Japanese (or use a translation app) and book ahead.
vegan_detail: The signature kayaku-meshi rice is cooked in a kombu-and-bonito (katsuo) dashi, and the menu centers on fish (salted mackerel, straw-grilled bonito tataki) and chicken, so it is not vegan and there are no documented vegan dishes.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. As a Japanese izakaya/teishoku place, dishes rely on standard Japanese soy sauce (wheat) and dashi-based seasoning, so it is not suitable for celiacs. The rice itself is naturally gluten-free but is seasoned with dashi and likely soy.
caveats: Per-day hours vary (some days lunch-only); one source cites different closed days, so confirm by phone (0742-26-6870) and reserve ahead. Cash only.

bio.background: Kayaku occupies a renovated traditional machiya townhouse in Nara's kitamachi district near the prefectural government offices, with thick wooden beams and a storehouse-style entrance door added in later renovation. It opened around 2012 as part of a wave of independent eateries that grew in the neighborhood. By day it serves earthen-pot mixed rice; by night the old house becomes a place for sake and small dishes.
bio.specialty: The centerpiece is kayaku-meshi (mixed grain/vegetable rice) cooked in a clay pot with a kombu-and-bonito dashi and served in a wappa wooden box. The kitchen is meticulous about the rice, using selected 'enko' rice and milling it fresh each day. Dinner highlights include salted mackerel set meals, tender simmered chicken thigh, and straw-grilled bonito tataki, paired with sake.
bio.philosophy: A focus on carefully sourced rice, daily milling, and gentle, refined seasoning, served in an unhurried, relaxed townhouse setting.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is set in an over-100-year-old machiya whose intimate ~20-seat space is described as relaxed and unhurried without feeling cramped, with a storehouse-style door added during renovation.
The kayaku-meshi uses selected 'enko' rice that is refrigerated and freshly milled each day, then cooked in a clay pot with dashi made from kelp and bonito.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Menu centers on Japanese set meals and izakaya dishes (mackerel, chicken, bonito tataki) that conventionally use wheat-based Japanese soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature rice is cooked in bonito-and-kombu dashi, so even the rice is not vegan; no vegan menu is documented.

cuisine: 大和郷土料理・和食（大和肉鶏・大和牛・ヤマトポーク・奈良ジビエ・地酒）
neighborhood: 三条町（JR奈良駅周辺）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市三条町495-1 バナナビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 奈良県奈良市三条町495-1 to lat 34.681595, lng 135.821198 — within the Nara box.
seats_text: Official site states 60 total seats with private tatami rooms (7-32 guests) and a table private room for 8; Tabelog lists 65 seats.
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / 17:00-23:30 (L.O. 22:30) per official site; Tabelog lists 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 14:00) / 17:00-00:00 (L.O. 23:30). Open year-round except Dec 31 & Jan 1.
closed_days: 年中無休（12/31・1/1を除く）。臨時休業あり。
cultural_comfort_note: A lively local Japanese restaurant where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will go a long way; reservations and card/QR payment make it manageable for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The menu is built around soy-sauce-based dishes (sukiyaki, simmered dishes), wheat-based Miwa sōmen, and fried items, with no documented gluten-free accommodations. A celiac diner cannot eat safely here.
vegan_detail: Primarily a meat and fish restaurant (Yamato beef, Yamato chicken, Yamato pork, Nara game, daily fresh fish). Most dishes use fish dashi and are not vegan. There are some plant-forward items — house-made tofu (Hokkaido soybeans, natural nigari), Yamato vegetables, and a crispy daikon salad — but these are not confirmed dashi-free or animal-free, so a strict vegan should ask in advance.
caveats: Seat count (60 vs 65) and exact closing time (23:30 vs 00:00) differ slightly between the official site and other listings; hours_week reflects the official site. No official allergy, vegan, or gluten-free policy is published, so dietary claims should be confirmed directly with the restaurant.

bio.background: A modern Japanese restaurant a short walk from JR Nara Station that celebrates the food culture and history of the Yamato region. It centers its cooking on local branded ingredients — Yamato beef, Yamato chicken (a flavorful local breed with gamecock and Nagoya Cochin bloodlines), Yamato pork — and since 2017 has served venison and wild boar sourced from hunters in the Yoshino area.
bio.specialty: Yamato beef sukiyaki, wild-boar hot pot, handmade Yoshino ham, sashimi platters from daily-sourced fish, house-made tofu, and Nara local sake; signature set courses such as the Moon Course showcase Yamato beef and pork.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant frames its menu around Nara's culinary heritage, including the tofu culture said to have reached Japan via the Tang dynasty during the Nara period, and makes its own tofu from Hokkaido soybeans and natural nigari.

anecdotes.text: Since 2017 the restaurant has featured Nara game meat (venison and wild boar) sourced from hunters in the Yoshino region.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu relies heavily on soy sauce, wheat-based Miwa sōmen, and fried foods; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature dishes such as sukiyaki and simmered items use standard (wheat-based) soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is meat- and fish-focused (Yamato beef/chicken/pork, game, daily fresh fish); fish dashi is used broadly, so vegan dishes are not isolated from animal products.

positives.text: House-made tofu (Hokkaido soybeans, natural nigari) and Yamato vegetables are available as plant-forward options.

name_ja: cervo bianco（チェルボ ビアンコ）
cuisine: イタリアン（薪窯ピッツァ・自家製ジェラート、奈良県産食材）
neighborhood: ならまち（高御門町・近鉄奈良駅南）
gf_detail: This is a pizzeria where the signature item is wheat-flour oven pizza, plus pasta — not gluten-free and not safe for celiacs. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation was found on any source. The gelato is dairy-based and may be cross-contaminated in a kitchen full of flour.
vegan_detail: Menu centers on cheese pizzas (four-cheese, fresh cream, Yamato pork sausage) and dairy gelato made with local Uemura Dairy milk, so it is not vegan and not vegetarian-focused. No vegan or plant-based options were documented on the official site, Tabelog, or local food pages. A vegan diner would have essentially nothing safe here; confirm directly if visiting.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高御門町7
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.8293, 34.677406] for 奈良県奈良市高御門町7, within the Nara box.
seats_text: Roughly 40 seats: counter and table seating around a central wood/stone oven, plus a small tatami area (~10) and outdoor bench seating. Renovated Edo-period machiya with a courtyard garden (坪庭).
hours_raw: 11:00–16:00（16:00以降は予約のみ）。木曜はピッツァの提供休止。不定休。(Tabelog lists 11:00–22:00 with reservation-only after 17:00; official site states 11:00–16:00.)
closed_days: 不定休（irregular closures; some Thursdays pizza is not served）
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed cafe-style machiya where you can point at the pizza and gelato menu and get by with simple Japanese greetings; English support is not documented, so learning a few food words helps.
caveats: Daytime hours differ between sources (official site says 11:00–16:00; Tabelog lists 11:00–22:00 with reservation-only after 17:00) — confirm before a late visit, and note reservations are needed for the late slot. Closures are irregular. Not suitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

bio.background: cervo bianco opened in June 2018 inside a renovated Edo-period townhouse (machiya) in Nara's historic Naramachi district, a short walk south of Kintetsu Nara Station. The name is Italian for white deer — a nod to the legend that the deity of Kasuga Taisha arrived in Nara riding a white deer, the origin of the city's famous free-roaming deer. The shop was launched by a long-established local Nara-pickle maker as a way to revitalize Naramachi and preserve its aging townhouses by drawing in younger visitors.
bio.specialty: Oven-fired pizza and house-made gelato built entirely around Nara-prefecture ingredients. Signature pizzas include a four-cheese pie with Nara pickles and fresh cream, and a salsiccia with Yamato pork sausage and local tomatoes. Every gelato flavor uses Nara produce — rich milk gelato from Uemura Dairy, local figs, and seasonal fruit — made fresh each morning.
bio.philosophy: To let visitors 'Enjoy NARA Flavor' and to invigorate the Naramachi neighborhood by showcasing its charm and protecting its traditional houses.

anecdotes.text: The building dates to the Edo period, and diners can see a small interior courtyard garden (坪庭) from their seats, giving the modern Italian menu a quietly traditional setting.
The signature milk gelato is made with Uemura Dairy milk, prized for its 'natural sweetness.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core product is wheat-flour oven pizza made on-site, so the kitchen is flour-heavy; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on cheese pizzas and dairy gelato (local milk, cream); no plant-based items documented.

cuisine: 純喫茶・喫茶店 (retro kissaten)
neighborhood: 大宮町 / 新大宮 (Shin-Omiya, central-west Nara)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市大宮町4-275-5 森村第2ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良市大宮町4-275-5 returned [135.810242, 34.683746], within the Nara box.
seats_text: 12 seats (counter and tables)
hours_raw: Open from morning (morning service starting 7:00) until 17:00; closed Sundays and holidays. (One listing shows an 08:00 open; multiple local sources cite a 7:00 morning service, so confirm the exact opening time directly.)
closed_days: Sundays and public holidays
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, solo-run neighborhood coffee shop popular with office workers and some foreign visitors; a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way, and free WiFi and outlets at every seat make it easy to settle in.
vegan_detail: This is a traditional Japanese coffee shop centered on egg-and-dairy fare: homemade cheesecake (cream cheese, sour cream, whipped cream, eggs), French toast, hot sandwiches with ham and egg, toast, and beef-shank curry. No vegan menu is documented and core items rely on eggs, dairy, and meat.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature offerings are wheat-based or served on wheat bread (hot sandwiches, toast/morning sets, French toast, cheesecake) and curry is typically thickened with wheat roux. No gluten-free options or allergen handling are documented; not suitable for celiacs.
caveats: Exact opening time varies between sources (one listing shows 08:00, others cite a 7:00 morning service) — confirm directly. Solo-operated, so service may pause if the owner is busy.

bio.background: A small retro coffee shop on Omiya-dori, a few minutes' walk from Shin-Omiya Station, opened in May 2015. It is run single-handedly by the master, a devoted cat and motorcycle lover, who named the shop 黒猫 ('Black Cat') after his love of cats. The calm, slightly nostalgic 12-seat room is decorated with cat- and motorcycle-themed books and artwork, and through the windows regulars can watch the bikes parked outside.
bio.specialty: Homemade cheesecake made with cream cheese, sour cream, whipped cream and eggs, prized for a moist, smooth texture that melts on the tongue; hot sandwich sets with a choice of three fillings served with mild blend coffee, potato chips and a mini salad; a morning service from early in the day; and a beef-shank curry that the owner suggests brightening with a little garlic vinegar.
bio.philosophy: An easygoing neighborhood haven where the master shares his passions for cats and motorcycles; he is known to support cat-related causes, and the shop keeps space for bikers' helmets and a relaxed corner with magazines, free WiFi and outlets at every seat.

anecdotes.text: The owner, nicknamed Moriken, jokes that he is a 'super rain-bringer,' yet his opening day in Shin-Omiya dawned with perfect weather.

A motorcycle enthusiast, the master keeps biker magazines on the shelves, stores patrons' helmets, and arranged the shop so guests can see motorcycles parked outside through the glass; one regular credits him with sparking his own love of riding.
He named the shop 'Kuroneko' (Black Cat) out of his love for cats, and the room is filled with cat-themed artwork and books.

cuisine: Renovated machiya (townhouse) izakaya specializing in nabe (hot pot) with Nara local sake; signature sake-lees hot pot and straw-grilled Yamato beef, plus local Yamato chicken and pork
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. This is a sake-lees hot pot izakaya: the signature 酒粕玄人鍋 uses sake lees (a wheat-free byproduct but processed alongside grain) and chicken-bone broth, while the broader menu is built on soy-sauce seasoning and wheat-containing izakaya fare. No documented allergen handling or gluten-free menu, so it is not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The core of the menu is meat and seafood: Yamato chicken, Yamato pork, Yamato beef, shrimp, and oysters, and the signature hot pot broth is made from Yamato chicken bones. Vegetables (watercress, Chinese cabbage, etc.) appear only as hot-pot ingredients within meat-based broths. No vegan dishes are documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市今御門町34-1
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 奈良県奈良市今御門町34-1; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: Naramachi / Mochiidono area, just south of Sarusawa Pond near Sarutahiko Shrine; about a 10-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: 70 seats total — 6 tatami tables and 7 private rooms (no counter); private rooms accommodate 2 to 50 people
hours_raw: 11:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30; lunch L.O. 14:00) daily except Tuesday. Closed Tuesdays; when a Tuesday is a public holiday, the closure moves to the following Wednesday.
closed_days: Tuesdays (closure shifts to Wednesday when Tuesday is a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed townhouse hot-pot izakaya with private tatami rooms where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will get you through comfortably, though staff may not speak much English.

bio.background: Opened on 18 October 2020 by Yokoji Co., Ltd. (横路株式会社, founded 2009) as part of its sake-brewery hot-pot group, set in a renovated traditional townhouse (machiya) just south of Sarusawa Pond, beside Sarutahiko Shrine.
bio.specialty: The signature 酒粕玄人鍋 (sake-lees connoisseur hot pot) is built on a broth of Yamato local-chicken bones enriched with sake lees from Yuchonatsu Brewery's 'Kaze no Mori' label, brimming with Yamato pork, Yamato chicken, shrimp, fried tofu, watercress and seasonal vegetables; another standout is straw-grilled Yamato beef. The leftover hot-pot broth is finished into a carbonara-style pasta with fondue cheese.
bio.philosophy: A commitment to chisan-chisho (local production for local consumption), showcasing Nara ingredients and roughly 40 local Nara sakes curated by a dedicated sake specialist.

anecdotes.text: The cellar stores around 40 Nara-area sakes, with wine and champagne downstairs and shochu and whisky tucked into the attic room.
The unique sake-lees broth uses lees from the 'Kaze no Mori' brand, described as a one-of-a-kind soup you can taste only here.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Japanese izakaya, seasoning relies on soy sauce, which in Japan is normally wheat-based; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature hot-pot broth is made from Yamato chicken bones, so shared hot-pot service and broth are not vegan-compatible.

caveats: Seat count is reported as 70 on the official site and Tabelog, though one local listing states 85; 70 is used here as the figure agreeing across primary sources. Cards (VISA/Master/JCB/AMEX/Diners) are accepted per Tabelog; no allergen, vegan, or gluten-free information is published, so dietary claims could not be confirmed and are inferred from the documented meat/dashi/soy-based menu.

cuisine: Italian (イタリアン) with wine bar and café; owner-chef trained in Italy, sommelier-certified, extensive Italian wine list
neighborhood: もちいどの商店街エリア／ならまち（near Kintetsu Nara Station, ~6 min walk）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南市町8
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市南市町8 returned [135.830109, 34.679897], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 18 seats (front wine bar & café plus a back dining room; some private/table seating)
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:30 (L.O. 13:30), Dinner 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 20:00); wine bar/café service throughout. Irregular closing days (不定休).
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A warm but refined Italian spot where a few words of Japanese help; an Italian barista works the front bar, so basic communication is manageable, but the dinner courses run higher in price than a casual student meal.
gf_detail: No documented gluten-free program. The menu centers on Italian cooking with house-made pasta such as tortelloni, so most dishes contain wheat. No dedicated gluten-free preparation or fryer is documented; a strictly gluten-free or celiac diner would need to ask directly and should not assume safe options.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-oriented restaurant. The cuisine is meat-, fish-, and dairy-forward (game terrines, acqua pazza fish, butter-sauce pasta, cheese). No vegan menu is documented; a vegan diner would need to call ahead, and options would likely be very limited.
caveats: Closing days are irregular (不定休); hours_week assumes daily service but the shop may close on varying days, so confirm before visiting. Lunch and dinner L.O. times vary slightly across sources; dinner courses are higher-priced (~¥7,900+).

bio.background: Owner-chef Kohei Nakamura trained at a Tokyo restaurant before apprenticing in Italy, where he learned cooking across both mountainous central regions and the coastal north. He is also a certified sommelier and returns to Italy each year to research current flavors and trends so he can serve them in Nara.
bio.specialty: Authentic regional Italian cooking using carefully chosen seasonal ingredients: house-made tortelloni in sage butter, fresh fish acqua pazza, steamed squid, and winter game such as a young-wild-boar and foie gras terrine made with meat from Tsuge Village near the Nara-Mie border, marinated in Italian dried-grape spirit and served with red-wine gelee and walnuts. Around 200 Italian wines, including Piedmont Barolo and Tuscan Brunello, served in glassware matched to each pour.
bio.philosophy: To bring the true taste of Italy to Nara's old town - 'to provide the taste of the authentic place as much as possible.' The venue is split into a casual front wine bar and cafe and a more formal dining room in back, letting guests choose their experience.

anecdotes.text: The front bar functions as a wine bar and cafe with an Italian barista, Andrea Picchioni, manning the espresso counter, while the back room serves refined course meals on Limoges porcelain with Riedel glassware and Baccarat tumblers.
For winter dishes the chef sources game meat from Tsuge Village near the Nara-Mie prefectural border, reflecting a blend of authentic Italian technique and local Nara ingredients.

cuisine: Italian (traditional Italian cooking using Nara/Yamato local ingredients)
neighborhood: Naramachi, Imamikadomachi shopping street near Sarusawa Pond
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市今御門町26-2 Noriビル 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市今御門町26-2 returned [135.830322, 34.680523], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Compact: one 4-person table plus two 2-person tables (about 8 seats), with a small counter mentioned in some sources.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (some sources list 11:30-15:30); Dinner 17:30/18:00-20:30. Closed Mondays plus occasional irregular closures.
closed_days: Mondays, plus occasional irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny owner-run Italian place where reservations (by phone, in Japanese) fill up well in advance and it's cash only, so a little Japanese and some planning will make your visit much smoother.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The menu is built around handmade fresh pasta and house-baked breads (focaccia, walnut bread), so wheat is central to the experience and there is no indication of celiac-safe practices.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. This is a meat- and seafood-forward Italian restaurant (suckling goat, wild boar porchetta, duck, prawns), and courses are fixed/seasonal, so vegans would need to ask in advance whether the chef can accommodate; nothing specific is published.
caveats: Hours vary slightly across sources (lunch end 15:00 vs 15:30; dinner start 17:30 vs 18:00). Closures are irregular in addition to the regular Monday closing. Confirm current hours and reserve ahead.

bio.background: The owner-chef honed his craft as head chef at an established, well-known Italian restaurant in Osaka before opening his own place. Da Ciccio opened on February 23, 2023, on the second floor of the long-running Japanese restaurant Okada, tucked along the Imamikadomachi shopping street near Sarusawa Pond in Naramachi.
bio.specialty: Handmade fresh pasta and rustic Italian classics that change monthly: dishes like duck-and-porcini pasta, Nagasaki fan-prawn pasta in the Pici/Elba style, cuttlefish-ink pasta, roasted suckling goat with Nara vegetables in a lemon and green-peppercorn sauce, and wild boar (cinghiale) cooked porchetta-style. House-baked breads include potato focaccia and walnut bread.
bio.philosophy: Traditional Italian cooking as the base, with the essence of Nara woven in through local Yamato ingredients and seasonal produce.

anecdotes.text: The compact dining room means weekend lunches book out weeks ahead, and the chef prepares around five pasta options and five mains fresh each day for diners to choose from.
Local press welcomed it as a traditional-Italian newcomer to Naramachi's Imamikadomachi when it opened in February 2023.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu centers on handmade fresh pasta and house-baked breads, so wheat flour is heavily used throughout the kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cuisine is built around meat (suckling goat, wild boar, duck) and seafood (prawns, cuttlefish ink); courses are fixed and seasonal with no documented plant-based option.

lead: キナリ ピッツァ（Kinari Pizza）
cuisine: 薪窯ピッツァ（Wood-fired pizza using Yamato vegetables）
neighborhood: ならまち（西寺林町・もちいどの周辺）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The entire menu is built around wheat-flour pizza dough (made with Kumamoto-prefecture wheat flour, "南のめぐみ"), so it is unsuitable for celiacs or anyone avoiding gluten. No gluten-free crust or alternatives are documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-focused. The dough itself is plant-based (flour, water, yeast, salt — no egg/dairy), and the kitchen leans heavily on local vegetables, including a vegetable-loaded Pizza Ortolana Bianca with roughly 10 Nara-grown vegetables. However, nearly all named pizzas are topped with mozzarella/Italian cheeses (and some with Yamato pork), and there is no documented cheese-free or confirmed vegan option. A vegan diner would need to ask whether a plain or cheese-free vegetable pizza (marinara-style) can be made; this is not confirmed.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西寺林町5
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.829803, 34.679474] for 奈良県奈良市西寺林町5; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Open-air seating only: roughly 4 terrace seats near the kitchen truck plus about 5 covered table seats further back (Tabelog lists 30席, but local reporting describes only ~9 actual outdoor seats). Pet-friendly. Weather-dependent.
hours_raw: 11:00～15:00（L.O.）。生地が無くなり次第終了。急な臨時休業あり。定休日：火曜日・水曜日。
closed_days: 火曜日・水曜日（加えて急な臨時休業あり。生地が無くなり次第終了）
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, doorless open-air spot where a friendly greeting and pointing at the pizza menu displayed by the truck will carry you through; no reservations and casual ordering make it easy for a foreign student, though staff English is not guaranteed.
caveats: Tabelog lists 30席 and card payment, but local articles describe only about 9 open-air seats and emphasize the truck-based, weather-dependent setup; seat count is uncertain. Hours close early when the dough runs out, and irregular closures occur, so confirm before visiting. The owner's name 宮中 寿 reading is uncertain from sources. No explicit allergen/cross-contamination policy is documented.

bio.chef_name: 宮中 寿 (Miyanaka Hisashi — reading uncertain)
bio.background: Kinari Pizza is run by a former bread artisan who trained in France and Italy before opening this pizzeria in Nara's Naramachi district. Drawing on that baking background, the owner developed a long-fermented, low-salt, low-yeast dough that is gentler on the body than classic Neapolitan style.
bio.specialty: Wood-fired pizzas baked in an oven mounted inside a white food truck. Signatures include the vegetable-packed Pizza Ortolana Bianca (about 10 Nara-grown vegetables such as mizuna, chrysanthemum greens, Brussels sprouts and radish with semi-dried tomato and Italian cheeses), Margherita, Funghi with locally sourced mushrooms, Yamato Pork sausage and rapeseed-flower pizza, and a Kotoka-strawberry four-cheese dessert pizza with honey.
bio.philosophy: An open-air, doorless 'pizzeria where you eat under the sky' (空と食べるピッツェリア). The owner intentionally built no doors — 「ラフに遊びに来てほしいから扉は作らない」 — to keep the space casual and inviting, and centers the menu on seasonal Nara-prefecture vegetables and Yamato Pork from the Tahara district.

anecdotes.text: The wood-fired oven sits inside a white food truck parked on the Nishiterabayashi shopping street, with terrace and covered seats arranged around it so guests dine outdoors with the sky overhead.
The dough uses Kumamoto-prefecture wheat flour ('南のめぐみ') and a long fermentation, with less yeast and salt than authentic Naples-style for a lighter, body-friendly crust.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-flour pizza dough is the core of every dish and is baked in a shared wood-fired oven; there is no gluten-free option and flour is unavoidable throughout the kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pizzas are topped with mozzarella and Italian cheeses and prepared in a shared open kitchen; no dedicated dairy-free workflow is documented.

positives.text: The dough itself is plant-based (no egg or dairy) and the menu features an abundance of fresh, locally grown Nara vegetables, including a heavily vegetable-topped white pizza.

cuisine: Dessert cafe / patisserie (Viennese-style plated desserts in a renovated townhouse)
neighborhood: ならまち（南市町）
gf_detail: This is a Western pastry cafe whose menu is built around flour-based cakes, tortes, cookies, and biscuit layers. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no allergy program. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: The signature offerings are dairy- and egg-rich European desserts (caramel cream, whipped cream, chocolate biscuit, homemade ice cream) served with siphon coffee and pot tea taken with evaporated milk. No vegan options are documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南市町14-5
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市南市町14-5 returned [135.829559, 34.680405], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Small townhouse cafe with table seating at the front and a tatami-mat area at the back; exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 11:00-18:00; closed Wednesdays (plus occasional irregular closures)
closed_days: Wednesdays, plus occasional irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, quiet alley cafe run by a husband-and-wife team where a little Japanese and an unhurried, respectful manner go a long way; desserts are dine-in only, so plan to sit and enjoy rather than grab and go.

bio.background: Karamell opened in July 2015 in a renovated traditional townhouse tucked down a narrow alley in the Naramachi district. It is run by a husband-and-wife team: pastry chef Shinsuke Muraoka, who previously headed the pastry departments at Osaka's Rihga Royal Hotel and Kobe's ANA Crowne Plaza and taught as a pastry instructor, and his wife Mitsuyo, who handles the front of house and prepares siphon coffee and pot-service tea.
bio.specialty: Plated Viennese-style desserts. The signature is the Schonbrunn Torte, five layers of chocolate biscuit, caramel cream, and whipped cream evoking Vienna's Schonbrunn Palace, rotated through about six seasonal variations across the year. Each dessert is served as a composed plate with homemade ice cream or sherbet, daily-baked cookies, and seasonal fresh fruit.
bio.philosophy: Everything is served dine-in only, plated to order, so each dessert can be enjoyed in its best condition rather than taken away. The shop deliberately uses the German spelling Karamell and chose Nara's calm setting for an unhurried hideaway atmosphere.

anecdotes.text: The cafe sits down a quiet pedestrian alley near Ebisu Shrine off the Mochiidono shopping street, about a 7-8 minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, with a large glass window glowing invitingly from the renovated old house.
Seating is split between tables at the front and a rare tatami-mat area at the back, where guests can watch the kitchen through the counter.

caveats: No official website was found; details drawn from Tabelog, JR Central's Nara tourism site, and a sweets blog. Seat count and any allergy handling are not documented. As a Western pastry cafe, it is not gluten-free or vegan friendly. Hours include occasional irregular closures, so confirm before a special trip.

cuisine: 和菓子・甘味／お土産物店（喫茶・レストラン併設）
neighborhood: 高畑町（奈良公園周辺・ならまち寄り）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The signature sweet, 三笠 (mikasa, the Nara name for dorayaki), is made from wheat-flour batter, and other items such as 奈良漬 pickles and various wagashi are not celiac-safe. No allergen menu, dedicated equipment, or GF labeling appears on the official site or listings.
vegan_detail: No vegan information is published. Some items lean plant-based by tradition — the shop's exclusive くず餅 kuzu-mochi (plain, azuki, matcha) is made from kuzu/arrowroot starch and sugar — but eggs and dairy are common in dorayaki/mikasa, and the group restaurant's set meals are not described, so a vegan diner would need to ask about each item directly.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1071
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 奈良県奈良市高畑町1071 to [135.835144, 34.678379], within the central Nara box. Matches the spot's stated location near Nara Park, ~15 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: Exact seat count not published. A reservation-only group restaurant accommodates up to roughly 230–350 people (minimum 10 guests, reservations by the previous day), plus a casual cafe and a sake corner; first floor is wheelchair accessible.
hours_raw: Souvenir shop 9:30–16:30; group restaurant (reservation only) 10:00–21:00; sake bar 11:00–16:00. Tabelog listing shows 10:00–19:00 daily.
closed_days: No regular closing day documented; the official site notes the place operates year-round, with reduced sake-bar hours on Tuesdays. Shop, cafe, and reservation-only restaurant keep different hours, so confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: This is a large tourist-facing facility geared to bus groups, so casual drop-in for the cafe and souvenir floor is easy, but the main restaurant is reservation-only for groups of ten or more and not suited to a solo student.
caveats: This is primarily Nara's largest souvenir shop rather than a dedicated sweets cafe; the sit-down restaurant is reservation-only for groups of 10+, while individuals can use the casual cafe and buy wagashi to take away. Hours differ across the shop, cafe, restaurant, and sake corner, and the Tabelog listing's hours (10:00–19:00) differ from the official site's shop hours (9:30–16:30) — confirm directly. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published anywhere consulted.

bio.background: Nagomikan is billed as one of Nara's largest souvenir halls, stocking around 1,500–3,000 items centered on Nara specialties and Japanese goods, and pairing shopping with a rest-and-meal stop for visitors to the nearby temples and park.
bio.specialty: Nara-exclusive kuzu-mochi (plain, azuki, and matcha), Nara-pickle miso, and Nara's hallmark sweet 三笠 (mikasa) — the local name for dorayaki, said to be named after Mikasa-yama (Wakakusa-yama) for its mountain-like shape.
bio.philosophy: The house motto is 和みの心で和のおもてなし — offering Japanese-style hospitality in a spirit of calm and harmony.

anecdotes.text: In Nara, dorayaki is traditionally called 三笠 (mikasa), a name local sweet-makers attribute to its resemblance to Nara's symbolic Mikasa-yama mountain.

positives.text: The first floor is wheelchair accessible, with step-free paths from the parking area to the cafe and restrooms and one accessible toilet.

lead: 日本料理かがりや（篝屋）
name_ja: 日本料理かがりや（篝屋）
cuisine: 日本料理・会席（地産地消：大和牛・大和野菜など奈良食材）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese kaiseki restaurant built around dashi (fish/kombu broth) and Japanese seasonings, so wheat-based soy sauce and mirin are pervasive across courses. There is no documented gluten-free program and no dedicated preparation. A celiac traveler would need to contact the restaurant in advance to discuss what, if anything, can be safely modified; cross-contamination cannot be assumed to be controlled.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on Yamato beef, Yamato pork, Yamato chicken, fish, and fish/kombu dashi, so most dishes are not vegan. A "Seasonal Vegetable Set" (季節の野菜) is offered at lunch, but there is no published confirmation that it is free of fish dashi, bonito, or other animal products — Japanese vegetable kaiseki dishes are commonly seasoned with fish-based broth. A vegan or vegetarian diner should call ahead (0742-22-2577) to confirm whether a genuinely plant-based course can be arranged.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市油阪町1-58 ホテルアジール・奈良 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; first feature coordinates [135.82103, 34.682484], within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: 油阪町（JR奈良駅・近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約5分）
seats_text: Listed at 122 seats including private rooms and banquet space (banquets seat 20–80, up to 100 standing); some listings cite 64 seats for the main dining area. It is a hotel restaurant rather than a small counter eatery.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–14:00 (L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Breakfast buffet 6:30–9:20. Closed Tuesdays (changing to Wednesdays from September 2026). Note: lunch/dinner service may be suspended if no reservations are confirmed by noon two days prior.
closed_days: 火曜日（2026年9月より水曜日に変更予定）
cultural_comfort_note: As a hotel kaiseki restaurant it is set up for guests and accustomed to visitors, but service and menus run mainly in Japanese, so a few polite Japanese phrases (or asking the front desk to help relay dietary needs when booking) will smooth the experience.
caveats: Reservations are required and service can be suspended if no bookings are confirmed by noon two days ahead, so do not arrive without advance notice. Seat counts differ between listings (122 hotel-wide vs 64 main dining). The closed day shifts from Tuesday to Wednesday starting September 2026 — verify before visiting. No published vegan or gluten-free program; dietary needs must be arranged directly by phone in advance.

bio.background: Kagariya is the in-house Japanese restaurant of Hotel Azyl Nara, a five-minute walk from JR and Kintetsu Nara stations. Its name, written 篝屋, evokes the kagari-bi (watch-fire) keepers who once tended the bonfires that lit Japan's castles, and the restaurant takes that image as its aim: to be a warm, welcoming place that lights up a guest's stay. The kitchen builds its menu around Nara's own food culture and the craft of dashi, the umami broth recognized as part of Japan's intangible culinary heritage.
bio.origin: 奈良市油阪町（central Nara, inside Hotel Azyl Nara）
bio.specialty: Seasonal Nara kaiseki centered on local ingredients — rare Yamato beef (served as a signature ひつまぶし / hitsumabushi set and in sukiyaki courses), Yamato chicken teppanyaki, Yamato pork, Yoshino kudzu, and Yamato vegetables, paired with a broad selection of Nara prefecture sake. Course menus include the Kagariya Kaiseki, Yamato Kaiseki, and Heijo Kaiseki.
bio.philosophy: The head chef emphasizes drawing out delicate, layered umami through carefully made dashi, presenting the seasonal flavors of Nara with restraint and depth while honoring the region's food, sake, and hospitality traditions.

anecdotes.text: The name 篝屋 refers to the people who tended the kagari-bi (watch-fires) lighting Japan's castles; the restaurant adopts this to express its wish to be a warm, heartwarming place for guests.
A signature dish is the Yamato beef hitsumabushi set, presenting rare local Yamato beef three different ways.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Course menus are traditional Japanese kaiseki seasoned with soy sauce, mirin and other Japanese condiments, which in Japan are normally wheat-containing; no gluten-free seasoning alternatives are documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen prominently uses Yamato beef, pork, chicken and fish, and Japanese vegetable dishes are typically prepared with fish-based dashi, so plant dishes likely share animal-derived broth unless specifically requested.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site provides no allergen, vegan or gluten-free information and directs guests with dietary restrictions to contact the restaurant directly at 0742-22-2577 to discuss customization.

positives.text: A 'Seasonal Vegetable Set' (季節の野菜) lunch course exists, indicating the kitchen can build a vegetable-forward meal, though it is not confirmed free of fish dashi.

lead: 十割り蕎麦 つるや
name_ja: 十割り蕎麦 つるや
cuisine: 十割手打ち蕎麦（昼そば／夜は茶房バー）- Hand-cut 100% buckwheat soba
neighborhood: ならまち（今御門町）, central Nara
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市今御門町26-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市今御門町26-2 returned [135.830322, 34.680523], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Small sunken tatami counter (お座敷カウンター), roughly 6 seats.
hours_raw: 火～土 11:00-14:30, 18:00-23:00 / 日 11:00-14:30 / 月 定休。昼は十割そば、夜は茶房バーとして営業。各情報源で営業形態の記載に差あり（一部記事は昼そばのみ・日月休と記載）。
closed_days: Monday (定休日). Hours vary by source; some report lunch-only soba service.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, traditional six-seat counter run by a working geisha; a little Japanese and quiet respect for the setting go a long way, and a foreign student will feel welcomed at the casual daytime soba service.
gf_detail: Despite the name "十割" (100% buckwheat noodles, which contain no wheat in the noodle itself), this is not a celiac-safe venue: there is no documented dedicated gluten-free preparation, the dipping broth (tsuyu) is dashi/soy-based and the soboro and other dishes are not controlled for gluten. No GF/allergy protocols are documented. Travelers with celiac disease should treat this as not safe and ask directly.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The soba broths use fish dashi, and the signature plates feature chicken (chicken chāshū, cold chicken soba), eggs (soft-boiled egg, egg yolk) and chicken soboro rice. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.

bio.background: Tsuruya is run by Kikuno, an active geisha (芸妓), and is described as Nara's only tea house (お茶屋) operated by a working geisha. By day the small shop serves hand-cut 100% buckwheat soba; by night it becomes Ochaya Bar Tsuruya (お茶屋BARつるや), carrying on the city's roughly 130-year-old flower-district tea-house culture.
bio.specialty: Hand-cut 十割 (100% buckwheat) soba. Signatures include 'atsumori' warm mori soba (noodles warmed in hot water and served with a piping-hot dipping broth and raw egg, described as a Sakai-origin style rare in Nara) and a cold chicken soba finished with black pepper, with chicken chāshū, soft-boiled egg, scallion and seaweed plus chili oil. A daily rice special pairs chicken soboro and egg yolk with the noodles.
bio.philosophy: Tea houses traditionally followed 'ichigensan okotowari' (no first-time customers). By opening an affordable, casual soba counter, Kikuno aims to make the historic geisha-quarter culture approachable to ordinary locals and visitors, including foreigners.

anecdotes.text: Tsuruya is billed as Nara's only tea house run by a currently working geisha, opening a casual daytime soba counter to let anyone experience the traditional flower-district culture once closed to newcomers.
Its 'atsumori' hot mori soba is described as a Sakai-origin preparation available only here in Nara, blending the geisha district's long tradition with careful temperature-based noodle handling.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free handling is documented; the dipping broth is dashi/soy-based and other dishes (soboro rice, eggs, chicken) are not gluten-controlled.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Broths/tsuyu are dashi- and soy-based; standard Japanese soy sauce and noodle broths contain wheat, and no wheat-free alternative is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built around fish dashi broth, chicken and eggs; no vegan-separated preparation is documented.

positives.text: Noodles are 十割 (100% buckwheat), so the noodle itself contains no wheat flour, though the surrounding dishes and broth are not gluten-free.

caveats: Hours differ across sources: Tabelog lists Tue-Sat lunch 11:00-14:30 plus evening 18:00-23:00 (tea-house bar) and Sunday lunch, closed Monday; recent articles describe lunch-only soba service (closed Sun & Mon). Evening operation is the お茶屋BAR rather than the soba counter. Confirm current hours by phone (0742-22-5588) before visiting.

cuisine: おでん・おばんざい・地酒（カウンター中心の個人店）
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation. The kitchen centers on oden simmered in a Japanese dashi broth and daily obanzai (home-style side dishes), both of which typically rely on soy sauce and bonito/kombu dashi; soy sauce in Japan contains wheat. No dedicated gluten-free menu, no celiac protocol, and no allergen handling is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. Oden is simmered in fish-based (bonito/kombu) dashi and obanzai are seasonal home-style dishes that commonly use fish dashi and may include meat or seafood, so most items are not vegan. There may be a few vegetable-forward obanzai or plain vegetable oden items on any given day, but you would need to ask in person whether they were made without fish dashi. No documented vegan menu.
address_ja: 〒630-8214 奈良県奈良市東向北町21-1 松山ビル 1F-2
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市東向北町21; within Nara box.
neighborhood: 東向北商店街（近鉄奈良駅 北側）
seats_text: 24席（カウンター・テーブル・お座敷あり）
hours_raw: 17:00-22:00（L.O. 21:00）。昼は予約のみ。水曜定休、不定休あり。
closed_days: 水曜日（不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A small counter-focused local izakaya run by one owner; expect a warm but Japanese-speaking room, so a foreign student comfortable with simple Japanese (or a translation app) will get the most out of it.

bio.background: ごはん日和り opened on September 15, 2023 in the Higashimuki-kita shopping arcade, just a couple of minutes north of Kintetsu Nara Station, taking over a space that had previously housed a French regional-cuisine izakaya. It is a small, owner-run spot where a female proprietor cooks everything herself.
bio.specialty: Oden simmered in a light, delicate Japanese dashi (from around 200 yen per piece) and a rotating lineup of obanzai (home-style seasonal side dishes) using fresh seasonal vegetables, paired with a selection of local Nara and seasonal sake.
bio.philosophy: The owner frames the place around お家ごはん (home-style cooking) served with heartfelt hospitality, prepping each dish from scratch so the food tastes like home cooking.

anecdotes.text: The shop is known as an approachable, homey spot where solo women diners are common and families can settle into the tatami seating, making it an everyday neighborhood gathering place rather than a special-occasion restaurant.
It opened in the former premises of a French regional-cuisine izakaya that had closed in late 2022, reviving the corner of the Higashimuki-kita arcade as an oden-and-sake destination.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Oden is simmered in a communal Japanese dashi pot and obanzai are home-style dishes; soy sauce and dashi (which in Japan contain wheat and fish) are used throughout, so cross-contamination with gluten is effectively unavoidable. No gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The menu is built on oden and obanzai seasoned in the standard Japanese style with soy sauce and dashi; Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. No wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Oden broth is a fish-based (bonito/kombu) dashi shared across all oden items, and obanzai commonly use fish dashi, so even vegetable items are likely cooked with animal-derived stock. No vegan preparation is documented.

caveats: Hours are 17:00-22:00 with last order at 21:00; lunch is by reservation only. Closed Wednesdays with occasional irregular closures, so confirm before visiting. Payment supports QR-code payment per Tabelog; card/cash specifics not fully confirmed, so carry cash to be safe. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published by the restaurant; any dietary need should be confirmed in person, ideally in Japanese.

name_ja: 四季彩菜 一条 東向通店
cuisine: 居酒屋・和食（鮮魚・海鮮料理、奈良の地酒）
neighborhood: 東向商店街（近鉄奈良駅すぐ）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町15
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市東向南町15 returned [135.828918, 34.682663], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 200 seats total: counter seating, raised tatami/zashiki platform areas, private and semi-private rooms, plus a second-floor banquet hall for groups.
hours_raw: Official site: Mon-Fri 17:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00); Sat/Sun/Holidays 15:00-23:30 (L.O. 23:00). A listing also notes a lunch service 11:00-14:00. Open year-round (年中無休).
closed_days: 無休（年中無休）
cultural_comfort_note: A bustling, group-friendly izakaya in the Higashimuki arcade with an English menu and card/QR payments, so a foreign student can get by with a few basic phrases and pointing at the menu.
gf_detail: This is a seafood-focused izakaya whose dishes lean heavily on Japanese soy sauce (wheat-based), batters, and fish dashi, with no documented gluten-free menu or allergen handling. Not suitable for celiacs; even sashimi is typically served with wheat-containing soy sauce.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on fresh fish, sashimi, grilled seafood, chicken, and steak; vegetable dishes use fish dashi and are not vegan. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented, and the kitchen is built around seafood, so plant-based diners are not accommodated.
caveats: Full signage name is 四季彩菜 一条 東向通店; it belongs to a small local izakaya group, so it is not strictly independent. Hours differ across listings: the official site shows dinner-focused hours (Mon-Fri 17:00, weekends/holidays 15:00 open, closing 23:30), while another listing shows a daytime 11:00-14:00 lunch and 17:00-23:00 dinner. Confirm current lunch availability by phone (0742-22-6226).

bio.background: A lively seasonal-Japanese izakaya tucked along the covered Higashimuki shopping arcade just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station. Behind a discreet, small restaurant-style frontage it opens into counter seats, raised tatami areas, private rooms and a second-floor banquet hall, comfortable for couples, families and large groups alike.
bio.specialty: Daily-changing fresh seafood is the heart of the menu: sashimi platters, scallops baked in the shell, squid with sauce, sea cucumber in vinegar, and cod milt, rounded out with grilled chicken and steak. Local Nara sake such as Mimuro Sugi and Gohshin is poured, including a three-type tasting set.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen's stated commitment is to fresh ingredients sourced and prepared daily, served at reasonable prices so adults can drop in casually and comfortably.

anecdotes.text: A local blogger described the seafood-heavy spread as so generous you might forget you are drinking in landlocked Nara, and praised it as just right for adults wanting a relaxed, affordable evening.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a sashimi and seafood izakaya, dishes are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is centered on fish, shellfish, chicken and steak, and vegetable dishes typically use fish dashi, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

cuisine: 和食カフェ＆ダイニングバー（個人店）/ Japanese washoku cafe and dining bar
neighborhood: ならまち・元興寺周辺（西寺林町）/ 近鉄奈良駅
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西寺林町6-2
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from full address 奈良県奈良市西寺林町6-2; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 15 seats total (5 counter, 10 table); up to about 20 standing
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:00); Dinner 18:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Open Wednesday–Sunday and holidays; closed Monday and Tuesday.
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, locally run Japanese spot where a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, polite manner will go a long way, so a foreign student comfortable with a mostly-Japanese setting will enjoy it most.
gf_detail: This is a from-scratch washoku kitchen built around dashi stock and traditional seasonings, including locally sourced soy sauce and miso (which in Japan are made with wheat). There is no documented gluten-free menu or handling, so it is not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: Cooking starts from a freshly drawn dashi (typically fish-based) and the menus center on fish, chicken, and many vegetable side dishes. There are no documented vegan or vegetarian set options; the vegetable dishes are not confirmed free of fish dashi or animal products.
caveats: Hours and seat figures come from Tabelog and a local feature article; one search snippet mentioned later dinner hours, but the detailed Tabelog and Narakko sources both list dinner ending 22:00, which is used here. Vegan/GF status inferred from documented dashi-and-fish-based washoku style; no allergy accommodation is documented either way.

bio.background: Comfy sits at the entrance to Nara's old Naramachi quarter, on a quiet lane south of Sarusawa-ike pond, in a narrow machiya-style building shared with other small shops. It opened in December 2022 as a relaxed daytime cafe and evening dining bar where carefully made traditional Japanese cooking can be enjoyed casually.
bio.specialty: Lunch built around a luxury set of fish, chicken, and roughly ten small vegetable dishes; evening plates such as buri-daikon (yellowtail simmered with radish) and sashimi to go with sake and cocktails; rotating house-made seasonal desserts, including a summer Nara-style shaved ice.
bio.philosophy: The chef's guiding idea is that cooking means offering time and care -- 料理は手間暇を提供するもの -- honoring the fundamentals and doing the unseen preparation that lets each ingredient shine.

anecdotes.text: The dish lead is a woman who trained at culinary school and spent about ten years cooking at traditional Japanese restaurants before opening her own place, and she begins every dish by drawing dashi stock from scratch.

Ingredients are chosen with care from local Nara producers: seasonal vegetables from farms in Gojo, soy sauce and miso from Inoue Honten, milk from Uemura Farm, and eggs from Omiya Farm, with producers noted on the menu.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kitchen uses locally made soy sauce and miso from Inoue Honten as core seasonings; standard Japanese soy sauce and miso are brewed with wheat, so dishes should be assumed to contain gluten.
