vegan_cross_contact.text: All cooking starts from a freshly drawn dashi stock and the menu features fish and chicken alongside vegetables, so vegetable dishes cannot be assumed free of animal-based stock.

lead: 和食とお酒 やまと庵 近鉄奈良駅前店
name_ja: 和食とお酒 やまと庵 近鉄奈良駅前店
name_en: Washoku to Osake Yamatoan Kintetsu Nara Ekimae-ten
cuisine: 大和食材と奈良の地酒を出す和食・居酒屋。大和牛・大和肉鶏・吉野のジビエ（鹿・猪）、奈良の伝統野菜、手作り豆腐、釜飯など
gf_detail: This is a Japanese izakaya built around soy-sauce and dashi-based cooking, with wheat-based items such as Miwa sōmen on the menu. There is no documented gluten-free program, no dedicated fryer, and standard soy sauce (which contains wheat) is used throughout. Not suitable for celiacs without direct, careful confirmation with staff.
vegan_detail: The kitchen centers on Yamato beef, Yamato chicken, Yoshino game meat, and fish, and most dishes rely on fish-based dashi, so the menu is not vegan. There are genuine Nara traditional vegetable dishes and handmade tofu (100% Hokkaido soybeans, natural nigari), but these are typically seasoned with dashi/soy and are not prepared as a dedicated vegan offering. A vegetarian could find a few items by asking; strict vegans would struggle.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向中町4 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI at chō-level (奈良県奈良市東向中町4); coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅前（東向中町）
seats_text: 70 seats, including sunken kotatsu (horigotatsu) seating, tables, counter seats, and private tatami rooms for 8 to 30 guests
closed_days: No regular closing day; open daily (年中無休 per listings).
cultural_comfort_note: A lively, tourist-accessible izakaya right by the station where a few greetings in Japanese go a long way, though menus and pointing make ordering manageable for a foreign student.
caveats: Dinner hours differ slightly between sources (one lists last orders to 23:00, another to 23:30); the official-site hours (dinner to 23:00) are used here. Lunch is budget-friendly but dinner courses run around 4,500 yen and up, so the typical dinner spend is over 30 USD.

bio.background: Yamatoan sits a minute from Kintetsu Nara Station and celebrates Nara as a cradle of Japanese sake and as home to distinctive Yamato ingredients. The kitchen sources Yamato beef and Yamato chicken, wild venison and boar from the Yoshino mountains, and Nara's heirloom traditional vegetables, building a menu that pairs regional cooking with a deep list of local Nara sake.
bio.specialty: Signature dishes include Yamato chicken tsukune, braised Yamato pork, persimmon-leaf sushi (kakinoha-zushi), and a range of clay-pot rice (kamameshi) made with local ingredients. The house also makes its own tofu from 100% Hokkaido soybeans set with natural nigari.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant emphasizes showcasing the flavor, aroma, and texture of Nara's traditional vegetables grown in the region's rich climate, alongside Nara's heritage as the birthplace of sake.

anecdotes.text: The menu leans on Yoshino-area game such as wild deer and boar plus Yamato beef and chicken, and offers around eight kinds of clay-pot rice made with Nara ingredients.
Tofu is made in-house using 100% Hokkaido soybeans and natural nigari for a smooth, rich texture.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based dishes such as Miwa sōmen noodles are on the menu and standard soy sauce (containing wheat) is used in Japanese izakaya cooking, so cross-contamination is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional washoku izakaya, dishes are seasoned with soy sauce, which in Japan normally contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen prepares beef, chicken, game meat, and fish alongside vegetable dishes, and most cooking uses fish-based dashi, so vegetable and tofu dishes are not free of animal-product contact.

positives.text: Handmade tofu from 100% Hokkaido soybeans and Nara traditional vegetables are available, giving vegetarians a few plant-based options to ask about.

lead: 小川又兵衛商店 ならまち店
name_ja: 小川又兵衛商店 ならまち店
cuisine: 奈良の地酒を集めた酒場・座れる角打ち（地酒・ワイン・ビール）
neighborhood: ならまち（元興寺門前）／近鉄奈良駅
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市鵲町8番地
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市鵲町8 returned [135.832321, 34.677895], inside the Nara box (lat 34.64–34.74, lng 135.76–135.90).
seats_text: Small semi-open storefront terrace ("座れる角打ち" — a standing-style bar where you can also sit) facing Gangoji temple; exact seat count not published. Casual, a handful of outdoor terrace spots.
hours_raw: 平日 15:00–19:00 / 土日祝 10:00頃–19:00（正月三が日休み、ほぼ無休）。開店時間は変動あり。
closed_days: No regular weekly closing day (basically open year-round / 無休); closed the first three days of the New Year (正月三が日).
cultural_comfort_note: This is a relaxed, walk-in storefront drinking spot run by a friendly local sake shop, but staff communication is mostly in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help a foreign student order comfortably.
vegan_detail: This is primarily a drinks spot (local sake, wine, beer by the glass) rather than a food restaurant. Most sake is vegan-friendly by ingredient, but any snacks/肴 are not documented, so vegans should ask about what food, if any, is offered.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The signature drinks include beer (contains barley/wheat-related gluten) and sake is brewed from rice (generally gluten-free but processed in a setting that also serves beer). No gluten-free program is documented; celiac travelers should ask carefully and treat it as not celiac-safe.

bio.background: 明治初年創業の老舗酒屋。元興寺（世界遺産）の門前、ならまちにあり、店舗は奈良市の登録有形文化財（奈良市景観形成建物）に指定された百年家造りの町家。かつてお酒の桶を運ぶために敷かれた約60mのトロッコの線路が今も残る。
bio.specialty: 店先の半オープンスペースで、奈良の地酒を中心に、ワインやビールを一杯から楽しめる『座れる角打ち（ちょこっとカフェ）』。約70種の日本酒を扱い、大正期のレシピを復刻した『桜ビール（幕末のビール）』など個性的な一杯も。
bio.philosophy: 灘や伏見の大手に隠れがちな奈良県内およそ40の地酒蔵を応援するという思いから、奈良の地酒を主役に据える。コンセプトは『ぬるい・ゆるい・あやしい』。世界遺産・元興寺を眺めながら気軽に一杯を楽しめる、奈良町散策のオアシス的存在。

anecdotes.text: 看板犬の福ちゃんに会いに来る常連も多く、ならまち散策の休憩スポットとして親しまれている。
店内には酒桶を運んだ約60mのトロッコ用レールが当時のまま残されている。

positives.text: Sake, wine, and beer are served by the single glass, so visitors can choose drinks (such as rice-based sake) that suit their preferences.

caveats: Primarily a sake shop with a casual seated standing-bar (kakuuchi) for drinks rather than a meal restaurant. Cash only. Opening times are irregular (weekdays from ~15:00, weekends/holidays from ~10:00) and can vary. Seat count is not published. No documented food allergy or gluten-free/vegan program.

lead: とろけるハンバーグ 福よし ならまち店
name_ja: とろけるハンバーグ 福よし ならまち店
cuisine: 洋食・ハンバーグ (Western-style hamburg steak, tableside-grilled)
neighborhood: ならまち / 近鉄奈良駅
gf_detail: Notable for celiac/GF-aware travelers: the signature hamburg patty itself is made from coarsely-ground domestic beef and Kuroge (black) Wagyu only, with explicitly NO wheat, egg, or dairy binder (つなぎゼロ / 麦・卵・乳製品などのつなぎ不使用) — confirmed on the official site and shop pages. This is unusual and meaningful, since most hamburg steaks use breadcrumb/flour binder. HOWEVER this does not make a meal celiac-safe: set meals come with rice, miso soup (likely dashi + wheat-containing miso/soy), pickles, and a separate hamburg sauce (typically contains wheat-based soy sauce). There is no dedicated gluten-free kitchen, no stated dedicated fryer, and the menu's allergen page asks diners to inquire with staff for allergens not listed. A wheat-sensitive diner can likely eat the patty (salt/wasabi seasoning instead of sauce) but should confirm sauce, miso soup, and cross-contact directly with staff. Treat as "GF options with care," not dedicated.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The entire concept is a beef hamburg steak (国産牛・黒毛和牛) and skirt (harami) steak specialist; sets include miso soup made with fish/animal dashi, and the signature finale is garlic rice cooked in the beef drippings. No vegan or vegetarian main dishes are offered.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市脇戸町12-2
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned chō-level coordinates for 奈良市脇戸町12-2 (within the Nara box). Located in the "界 (Kai)" commercial complex in Naramachi, roughly 10 min walk / ~700m from Kintetsu-Nara Station.
seats_text: Approximately 34 seats (Tabelog lists 36 with counter 6 / tables 22 / garden-terrace 10; the official reservation page lists 34). Table seating, counter, and a dog-friendly terrace.
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / 17:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30). Open daily; closures irregular (不定休). Note: some listings show dinner until 22:00.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures; no fixed weekly closing day)
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly, casual sit-down spot where staff explain the cook-it-yourself tabletop grilling, so a few words of Japanese (or pointing at the picture menu) and a "konnichiwa" smile will carry you through; an English-translated listing exists but on-site service is mainly Japanese.
caveats: The patty's "no wheat/egg/dairy binder" claim applies to the hamburg meat itself, not to the full set (rice, miso soup, pickles, sauce) — confirm with staff before treating as gluten-free. Hours vary by listing (dinner close 21:00 vs 22:00) and closures are irregular, so check before visiting. Seat count differs slightly between sources (34 vs 36).

bio.background: Fukuyoshi began in 1998 with a single ambition: to recreate the sensation of eating chateaubriand — the most tender cut of premium beef — in the everyday form of a hamburg steak. The Naramachi branch is the brand's local home, set inside the 'Kai' complex a short walk from Kintetsu-Nara Station, with a townhouse-inspired modern interior nodding to Nara's Great Buddha and deer.
bio.specialty: The signature 'Torokeru (melting) Hamburg' is made from 100% domestic beef and Kuroge Wagyu, coarsely ground and bound with nothing at all — no wheat, egg, or dairy filler — so the meat's flavor is the whole point. It arrives nearly raw on a sizzling iron plate kept hot by a tabletop burner; diners grill each bite to taste over about three minutes, seasoning with the house hamburg sauce, wasabi, or simple salt. The meal's traditional finale is garlic rice fried tableside in the beef drippings. Aged harami (skirt) steak and egg-topped hamburg sets round out the menu, and the Nara location also features Yamato wagyu and Yamato vegetables.
bio.philosophy: 'Making Nara more lively through hamburgers' — letting honest, binder-free beef speak for itself through an interactive, theatrical cook-at-your-table experience.

anecdotes.text: The founder was inspired by chateaubriand and set out to capture that melt-in-the-mouth quality in a hamburg steak, which became the brand's defining 'torokeru' (melting) signature.
Patrons compare the rare, cook-it-yourself-on-a-hot-iron-plate style to the famous Shizuoka chain Sawayaka, finishing the meal with garlic rice cooked in the meat juices.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No dedicated gluten-free kitchen or stated dedicated fryer; set meals are served with rice, miso soup, pickles, and a separate hamburg sauce, so cross-contact and wheat-containing sides are likely even though the patty itself is binder-free.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house hamburg sauce and miso soup are not specified as wheat-free and most likely contain standard (wheat-based) soy sauce/miso; seasoning the meat with the provided salt or wasabi instead of the sauce is the safer option but should be confirmed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Beef-only menu with dashi-based miso soup and beef-dripping garlic rice; not vegan or vegetarian and no plant-based mains.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The menu instructs guests to ask staff about any allergens not listed, indicating staff will field allergy questions on request.

positives.text: The signature hamburg patty is explicitly made with no wheat, egg, or dairy binder — only domestic beef and black Wagyu — which is unusual for hamburg steak and a genuine advantage for wheat-avoiding diners (though the full set is not certified gluten-free).

name_ja: アンヌ・マリーカフェ
cuisine: British-style cafe / kissaten (scones, quiche, cakes, morning sets, afternoon tea)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The menu is built around wheat: handmade scones, quiche (tart pastry crust), cakes, chiffon cake, sandwiches, toast and English-style curry are all core offerings. No gluten-free menu, labeling, or accommodation is mentioned on the official site, Tabelog, or local guides. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is advertised. Offerings are dairy- and egg-heavy (clotted cream, cakes, quiche, ice cream, milk in scones). One noted detail: the quiche uses a tofu-based fluffy egg filling with no added sugar for a lighter feel, but it still contains eggs and a wheat pastry crust, so it is not vegan. A plant-based diner would need to ask in person; little here is reliably suitable.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向北町1-10
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to chō level (東向北町1番地); coordinates fall within the central Nara box near Kintetsu Nara Station.
neighborhood: 東向北町（東向商店街・近鉄奈良駅前）
seats_text: 34 seats
hours_raw: Tue-Fri 08:30-18:00 (L.O. 17:30); Sat/Sun/Holidays 09:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30); lunch 11:00-14:00; afternoon tea from 14:00 (last order 1 hour before closing). Closed Mondays.
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, antique-filled cafe a one-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station where solo guests are welcome; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will be plenty, but bring cash since cards are not accepted.
caveats: Cash only. The food is wheat- and dairy-centric (scones, quiche, cakes), so it is not suitable for gluten-free/celiac or strict vegan diners. Closed Mondays.

bio.background: A small, antique-styled British cafe tucked into the Higashimuki-kita shopping street, about a one-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station and close to Nara Park. Its flower-trimmed exterior and interior of antique furniture, books and small objects give it a quiet, homey English feel, with soft jazz playing inside. It is the kind of comfortable spot where even a solo guest can settle in.
bio.philosophy: A calm refuge welcoming guests of all kinds, including women dining alone, with handmade baking and generous portions meant to be lingered over and shared.
bio.specialty: Handmade scones (a daily plain version plus rotating seasonal flavors, served with clotted cream and blueberry jam) are the signature draw, alongside daily-changing quiche made from scratch. The afternoon-tea set, served from 2pm on a tiered cake stand, layers sandwiches, quiche, pickles, two kinds of cake, scones, marinated fruit and ice cream. Other favorites include nut chiffon cake, cheesecake, blueberry-yogurt parfait, omelette rice and an English-style curry, with morning toast sets earlier in the day and a wide tea and coffee selection.

anecdotes.text: Some customers travel from far away specifically for the scones.
The quiche is made from a handmade tart base using a tofu-based fluffy egg filling with no added sugar for a lighter result.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen bakes wheat scones, cakes, chiffon cake and quiche pastry on site, so flour is pervasive and cross-contact would be unavoidable for a gluten-free diner.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy and eggs are central across the menu (clotted cream, cakes, ice cream, egg-based quiche), so even items that seem plant-forward are prepared in a heavily dairy/egg kitchen.

lead: 奈良三条カレー 本店
name_ja: 奈良三条カレー 本店
cuisine: カレー専門店（和牛ビーフカレー）
neighborhood: 三条通り／近鉄・JR奈良駅 周辺（橋本町）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市橋本町28 春日三条通り館 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市橋本町28; coordinates [135.828613, 34.681683] fall within the Nara bounding box.
seats_text: 座席数は確認できず（三条通りの小規模なカレー店）
hours_raw: 11:00開店、無くなり次第クローズ
closed_days: 月曜定休＋不定休あり（売り切れ次第終了）
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual lunch-only counter curry spot where ordering is simple, but staff likely speak mainly Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will help.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature beef curry is built on a flour-based curry roux (カレールウ), so the sauce contains wheat and is unsafe for celiacs. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The shop centers on wagyu beef curry made from the affiliated steakhouse's beef tendon and trimmings, with a meat-and-roux base. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
caveats: Lunch-only and closes once the day's curry sells out, so arrive early. Closed Mondays with additional irregular closures. Hours beyond the 11:00 opening are not precisely published; the listed daily window is an estimate.

bio.background: 奈良の三条通りで黒毛和牛や活伊勢海老などを目の前で焼き上げる「鉄板ステーキ シェルブルー」の姉妹店として生まれたカレー店。ステーキ店で扱う上質な和牛のスジ肉や切り落とし肉をふんだんに使い、本格的なビーフカレーをリーズナブルな価格で楽しめるのが持ち味です。
bio.origin: 奈良市橋本町・三条通り
bio.specialty: 和牛のスジ肉・切り落としを使ったビーフカレー（和牛スジカレー）。姉妹店のステーキの旨みを活かした肉感たっぷりの一皿。
bio.philosophy: 肉を知り尽くした元帝国ホテルのシェフが何度も試作を重ねて作り上げたカレーで、ステーキ店ならではの肉のうまみを手頃に味わってもらうことを大切にしている。
roles: 元帝国ホテルシェフ（系列のシェルブルーが監修）

anecdotes.text: 同じ三条通にある鉄板ステーキ・シェルブルーの姉妹店で、人気のステーキ店で使う和牛のスジ肉や切り落とし肉をふんだんに使ったビーフカレーをリーズナブルに楽しめる。
肉を知り尽くした元帝国ホテルシェフが、何度も試作を重ね拘り抜いた絶品のカレーを提供している。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature beef curry uses a flour-based curry roux, which contains wheat; the dish is not suitable for gluten-free or celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built around wagyu beef curry using beef tendon and trimmings; no vegan or vegetarian preparations are documented.

cuisine: Beignet cafe (fried-to-order square doughnuts) and chicory coffee
gf_detail: This is a doughnut specialist. Beignets are made from fried wheat-flour dough (including rye/whole-wheat varieties) dusted with powdered sugar, so the core product is wheat-based and not suitable for celiac or gluten-free diners. No gluten-free items are documented.
vegan_detail: No vegan offerings are documented. Beignet dough and toppings (e.g. white chocolate, two-cheese, caramel and nuts) likely contain dairy and/or egg. Caffeine-free chicory coffee and homemade enzyme/ginger-syrup drinks may be plant-based but ingredients are not confirmed; a vegan diner would need to ask in person.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1002
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the central Nara box for 奈良県奈良市高畑町1002.
neighborhood: Takabatake-cho / Naramachi southeast (near Kasuga Taisha and the former Naoya Shiga residence)
seats_text: Around 10 seats: roughly 9 counter seats (window and interior counter) plus one table for two.
hours_raw: 12:00-18:00 (some sources list 11:00 opening); irregular holidays, check Instagram
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); the operating calendar is announced on Instagram.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, relaxed owner-run cafe where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu go a long way; English is not guaranteed and it is cash only, so bring coins and a smile.
caveats: Cash only, no reservations, and irregular days off mean it is worth checking the Instagram calendar before visiting. Seating is very limited (about 10). Hours are listed as 12:00-18:00 on the main listing but some sources note an 11:00 opening, so arrive after noon to be safe.

bio.background: A husband-and-wife team who spent about two years selling their beignets pop-up style around Nara — at Kumo-tei on Mount Ikoma and at spots like Kore Kara and Slow House Nara — before settling into a permanent home in 2021. They renovated the first floor of a vacant old house in the quiet Takabatake neighborhood, choosing to breathe new life into an existing building rather than build new.
bio.origin: Takabatake-cho, Nara (with roots in a traveling pop-up across the Nara area)
bio.specialty: Fried-to-order beignets — flat dough that puffs into a balloon-like, hollow square when fried, crisp outside and chewy-mochi inside, dusted generously with powdered sugar. Varieties include plain, rye whole-wheat, caramel and nuts, lemon and white chocolate, plus seasonal fruit towers. Paired with caffeine-free chicory coffee made from roasted chicory root and homemade syrup drinks.
bio.philosophy: A slow, unhurried neighborhood cafe in a restored old house, where snacks are fried hot to order and savored on the spot.

anecdotes.text: Beignets are best eaten hot by pinching the square corners with two fingers, enjoying the crisp shell first, then the chewy hollow center, and finally sweeping up the leftover powdered sugar from the plate.
Set in the Takabatake area near Kasuga Taisha, the shop is close enough to the deer that herds occasionally wander up to the storefront.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire product line is fried wheat-flour dough (plain, rye/whole-wheat, and flavored beignets), so the frying oil and surfaces handle gluten constantly; not suitable for gluten-free or celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Flavored beignets include dairy-containing toppings such as white chocolate and 'two-cheese'; dough ingredients (egg/butter/milk) are not documented, so vegan suitability is unconfirmed.

positives.text: Caffeine-free chicory coffee and homemade ginger-ale / enzyme-syrup drinks are offered, which may suit some non-dairy or caffeine-avoiding visitors (confirm ingredients in person).

cuisine: Small log-house tea house serving Yamato tea, onigiri, konnyaku dengaku, ohagi, and homemade light meals
neighborhood: Takahata-cho / Naramachi East (near Shiga Naoya's former residence)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1269-3
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the chō block (高畑町1269); coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Seat count not published; a very small log-house space with indoor seating plus a forest-view terrace.
hours_raw: Sat & Sun 10:30–17:00; closed weekdays (also opens on some weekdays during spring and autumn holiday seasons).
closed_days: Monday through Friday (open weekends and holidays; additional weekday openings during spring and autumn busy seasons)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny shop run by an elderly proprietress where little to no English is likely; a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, patient manner will go a long way.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment. Rice-based onigiri and konnyaku dengaku are naturally wheat-free in their base, but seasonings (miso on dengaku, any soy sauce) and the shared small kitchen are unverified, so a celiac would need to ask carefully and cannot assume safety.
vegan_detail: Many items are plant-forward — Yamato tea, plum juice, ohagi (sweet rice with red bean), barley-and-taro rice, and some onigiri fillings (red shiso, mixed vegetable). However, fillings, the dengaku miso, and possible dashi or non-vegan ingredients are not documented, so vegan suitability must be confirmed in person.
caveats: Hours are irregular: reliably weekends/holidays only, with extra weekday openings in spring and autumn. The space and menu are very small and seasonal. Dietary suitability (vegan/GF) is unconfirmed and should be asked about directly.

bio.background: Tucked into Nara's historic Takahata district, just a minute's walk from the former residence of novelist Shiga Naoya, this is a tiny wooden log-house tea shop run by a warm, elderly proprietress who prepares nearly everything by hand. It sits within a preserved historic landscape of Meiji- and Showa-era buildings, with a forest-view terrace and classical music drifting through the small interior alongside birdsong.
bio.specialty: Homemade light fare and local Yamato tea: onigiri (red shiso, mixed vegetable), konnyaku dengaku, seasonal ohagi (including a version with local Asuka Ruby strawberries), barley-and-taro rice, a tea-porridge set, zenzai, and homemade plum juice. The Yamato tea served is from Takenishi Farm, a producer with Edo-period roots.
bio.philosophy: An unhurried, hands-on hospitality — most dishes made fresh daily by the owner herself, offered as a peaceful retreat in a quieter corner of Nara.

anecdotes.text: Visitors describe an elderly proprietress greeting guests with gracious, personal service while preparing nearly all the food fresh by hand in the small log house.
The shop pours Yamato tea from Takenishi Farm, a tea producer whose roots reach back to the Edo period, and is praised for handmade touches like its plum juice and seasonal ohagi.

positives.text: Most dishes are described as homemade by the owner, and several menu items are simple and plant-forward (Yamato tea, plum juice, rice-based onigiri, konnyaku dengaku, ohagi).

lead: ジェラテリア フィオレ
name_ja: ジェラテリア フィオレ
cuisine: 古民家を改装した自家製ジェラート専門店（パスタ・ピザのランチも提供）
neighborhood: 高畑町・京終／ならまち南東
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The lunch menu is built around wheat: handmade pizza baked in a stone oven and pasta. Gelato is the main draw and many flavors (pistachio, chocolate, salted caramel, etc.) are likely safe in themselves, but there is no dedicated gluten-free handling, no documented allergen labeling, and shared equipment/scoops are likely. A celiac diner should treat the food menu as off-limits and ask carefully even about gelato.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The signature gelato is dairy-based, made with Hokkaido milk, and the lunch dishes (pizza with cheese/bacon, cream-style pastas) center on dairy and animal products. No plant-based gelato or vegan options are documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町464
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned 奈良県奈良市高畑町464番地 at [135.846817, 34.675102], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Counter 4, table seating (one 6-8 person, two 4-person, one 2-person) plus a terrace seat; roughly 20-25 seats.
hours_raw: 11:00～18:00（食事ラストオーダー15:00）。定休日：水曜＋不定休（Instagramで告知）。
closed_days: Wednesdays, plus irregular closures (announced on Instagram)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, unpretentious gelato café where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the gelato case go a long way; staff are described as warm and attentive, but no English menu is documented so come ready for a simple Japanese interaction.
caveats: Cards are not accepted (electronic money and QR-code payment are OK, so bring those or cash). Hours include irregular closures beyond the regular Wednesday, so check their Instagram before a special trip. Food (pasta/pizza) stops at last order around 15:00.

bio.background: Gelateria Fiore opened on October 13, 2019 in a renovated old house in Nara's quiet Takabatake district, just in front of historic Shin-Yakushi Temple. The building previously served as a curry restaurant popular with cyclists before being transformed into a gelato café. Perched on a rise, it looks out over the Yamanobe-no-michi trail and the Nara cityscape, filling a long-standing gap for a comfortable place to rest in this scenic but dining-sparse corner of town.
bio.specialty: Around ten house-made gelato flavors rotate at any time, from staples like pistachio, chocolate and roasted-tea (hojicha) to seasonal strawberry and cream cheese. The milk is from Hokkaido, strawberries are Nara's prized 'Kotoka' variety bought directly from farmers, and peaches are Wakayama's 'Arakawa no Momo.' A signature salted-caramel flavor uses the salt to quietly lift the sweetness. Lunch adds stone-oven pizza (such as a bacon margherita baked by the owner) and pasta like shrimp and spinach in basil sauce.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims to honor each ingredient's natural flavor for a rich but gentle, clean finish, and the room reflects the same care: antique furniture, dried flowers, and tableware chosen piece by piece, including a green pasta plate commissioned from a Hyogo artist and handmade wooden chopstick rests. The Italian name 'Fiore' means 'flower.'

anecdotes.text: The owner bakes the pizzas in an on-site stone oven, and serves gelato in antique glassware peaked into soft swirls with dried flowers set on the saucer.
The space was converted from a former curry restaurant that had been a hangout for cycling enthusiasts.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The lunch menu centers on wheat-based stone-oven pizza and pasta, and there is no documented gluten-free preparation or allergen labeling, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Gelato is dairy-based (Hokkaido milk) and pizza/pasta dishes use cheese and animal products; no plant-based or vegan options are documented.

cuisine: 自家製パティスリーを出す個人経営の喫茶・カフェ
neighborhood: ならまち・高畑エリア
gf_detail: This is a French patisserie cafe whose menu centers on wheat-based cakes, cheesecakes, eclairs, cookies and similar baked goods, all made with flour. There is no indication of any gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or celiac-aware preparation. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-sensitive diners.
vegan_detail: The cafe specializes in dairy- and egg-rich Western confections (cheesecakes, panna cotta, mousses, butter-based cakes and cookies) plus coffee and tea service. No vegan or plant-based options are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町600-1 入江泰吉記念奈良市写真美術館 1階
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 奈良県奈良市高畑町600-1 via GSI; first feature coordinates [135.84462, 34.676289], within the Nara box.
seats_text: About 40 seats, roughly ten four-person tables.
hours_raw: 9:30-17:00 (last order 16:30). Closed Mondays (open if Monday is a public holiday, closed the following day instead). Museum admission closes 16:30.
closed_days: Mondays (if Monday is a public holiday, open that day and closed the following weekday instead).
cultural_comfort_note: A calm museum cafe where ordering is simple and pointing at the cake display works; a little Japanese helps but is not essential.
caveats: Cash only (no cards or QR payments). Located on the first floor inside the Irie Taikichi Memorial Nara City Photography Museum; you can use the cafe without paying museum admission. Sources differ on reservations: the venue runs a reservation-only pastry school, but the cafe itself appears to be walk-in.

bio.background: A small handcrafted-pastry cafe and cake school run by pastry chef Tomoka Fujioka, tucked into the first floor of the Irie Taikichi Memorial Nara City Photography Museum in the quiet Takahata area near Naramachi. The bright, glass-walled room looks out onto the museum's garden, designed by architect Kisho Kurokawa, and is known for keeping an unusually peaceful, unhurried mood.
bio.specialty: Homemade daily cakes and tea sets, including a refreshing rare cheesecake made with Danish cream cheese and lemon sauce, a matcha sponge cake with sweet azuki, citrus cheesecake, panna cotta and assorted jellies. Every cake displayed in the cafe can also be learned as a recipe through the attached pastry school.
bio.philosophy: Thoughtful, made-by-hand French confections meant to be enjoyed slowly, paired with the idea of passing the techniques on to others through lessons.

anecdotes.text: During the 2020 pandemic, when in-person classes were not possible, Fujioka adapted by selling video recipes and ingredient kits so students could keep baking from home.
The cafe sits inside a photography museum dedicated to Taikichi Irie, famed for his images of Yamato landscapes and Buddhist sculpture, and you can sit and have cake without paying museum admission.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A flour-based patisserie producing cakes, cookies and pastries in a shared kitchen; high gluten cross-contact is inherent and no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is built on dairy and eggs (cheesecakes, cream, panna cotta, butter cakes); no plant-based items or vegan handling are documented.

lead: 奈良ゲストハウス 神奈寐
name_ja: 奈良ゲストハウス 神奈寐
cuisine: 町家ゲストハウス併設の小さなカフェ＆バー
gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published anywhere. This is a tiny renovated-house cafe and bar serving coffee, homemade sweets, light lunch fare and Japanese sake — nothing here is documented as gluten-free, and homemade sweets and lunch dishes typically contain wheat. A celiac traveler would need to ask directly, and there is no indication the small kitchen can avoid cross-contamination.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or vegan options are documented. The cafe serves house-blend coffee ("Kamunabi blend"), homemade sweets, simple lunch plates and a sake-tasting set. Whether any plant-based item exists is unknown; a vegan diner would have to ask in advance. Treat the menu as not reliably vegan.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市紀寺町1054
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned chō-level point for 紀寺町 (Kishidera-cho); within Nara box.
neighborhood: ならまち（町家エリア）／紀寺町
seats_text: Counter seating for about 4 plus window-side tables for roughly 6.
hours_raw: 11時頃～22:00（L.O.）。ゲストハウスのモーニング終了後に一般カフェ＆バーとして営業。
closed_days: No fixed closed days listed; as a small guesthouse cafe, opening can vary — confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: As a guesthouse cafe used to travelers, the hosts are welcoming to foreigners, though a few words of Japanese and a relaxed, quiet attitude will help you feel at home in this small old-house space.
caveats: Hours and opening days for the cafe/bar portion can be irregular since the space first serves guesthouse morning service before opening to the public; the published 11:00–22:00 window may not apply every day. No allergy, vegan, or gluten-free information is documented — call ahead for any dietary needs.

bio.background: Kamunabi opened in October 2016 inside a renovated old Nara townhouse of unknown age, just beside the historic Naramachi townhouse district in Kishidera-cho. The owner, Tawaraya Akira, and host Hayano Asahie kept the building's original storehouse and inner courtyard while quietly modernizing the plumbing and corridors with good materials. The entryway doubles as a small cafe and bar open to anyone, not just overnight guests.
bio.specialty: House-blend drip coffee called the 'Kamunabi blend,' homemade sweets, simple lunch plates, and an evening sake set that lets you taste three Japanese sakes (the 'Choi-Kamunabi set').
bio.philosophy: The place is built around blending nostalgia with the modern — described as feeling like a stylish, retro grandmother's house where a solo traveler can drop in and pass calm, unhurried hours without it ever feeling too quiet.

anecdotes.text: The name 'Kamunabi' is taken from an ancient word found in the Man'yoshu poetry anthology meaning a sacred natural space; the hosts coined the spelling by adding the kanji 寐 ('sleeping quarters') to evoke a place of rest within that sacred calm.
The cafe runs as a public space only after the guesthouse's morning service for staying guests has finished, so daytime visitors step into the same room where travelers begin their day.

lead: 奈良ホテル ティーラウンジ
name_ja: 奈良ホテル ティーラウンジ
cuisine: クラシックホテルのティーラウンジ（コーヒー・紅茶と自家製洋菓子・サンドウィッチ）
gf_detail: This is a hotel tea lounge whose menu centers on wheat-based baked goods — apple pie, an assortment of about seven house-made cakes, and sandwiches — so it is not suitable for anyone needing to avoid gluten. There is no published gluten-free offering and no documented allergen handling. A celiac or gluten-sensitive traveler should not rely on this venue; at most a plain coffee or tea could be ordered, but cross-contact in a shared pastry kitchen is unaddressed.
vegan_detail: The lounge is built around dairy-rich Western confectionery (cakes, apple pie, royal milk tea) and sandwiches, with German Ronnefeldt teas and house coffee. No vegan or plant-based items are documented, and cakes/milk tea contain dairy and eggs. Black coffee and plain (non-milk) teas would be the only incidental plant-based options; there is no vegan menu.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1096 奈良ホテル 本館1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市高畑町1096 returned [135.83371, 34.680328], within the Nara box and consistent with the Nara Hotel main building.
neighborhood: 高畑町・荒池／ならまち東（奈良ホテル本館）
seats_text: 43 seats in the main-building tea lounge with windows overlooking the hotel grounds.
hours_raw: 10:00–18:00 (L.O. 17:30); operates as a café until 18:00, after which the space becomes a bar. (Official site lists the lounge from 11:00; Tabelog lists 10:00–17:00. Hours have varied — confirm on arrival.)
closed_days: Open daily (no regular closing day listed)
cultural_comfort_note: As a long-running international hotel lounge, staff are used to foreign guests and walk-in tea service is relaxed, though prices run high for a student treat.

bio.background: The Tea Lounge sits on the first floor of the Nara Hotel's main building, a wooden Western-Japanese fusion landmark opened in 1909 (Meiji 42) and designed by Tatsuno Kingo, the architect behind Tokyo Station and the Bank of Japan headquarters. Long called 'the guesthouse of Kansai,' the hotel has welcomed figures such as Albert Einstein, Audrey Hepburn, and Helen Keller. The lounge offers tea and coffee beneath the building's historic woodwork, with garden and seasonal views through tall windows.
bio.specialty: House-made Western pastries are the draw — a traditional apple pie and a rotating selection of roughly seven cakes, including a signature 'karigane' tea cake using Nara-grown tea-infused sponge with passion-fruit cream, plus sandwiches. Drinks pair the hotel's own coffee with German Ronnefeldt teas (the lounge notes Japan's soft water suits this tea), and the Royal Milk Tea is a house favorite.
bio.philosophy: A place for a leisurely, unhurried tea time, pairing carefully chosen Japanese and Western sweets with premium tea in a serene, historic setting.

anecdotes.text: Einstein is said to have played the upright piano that still stands in the lobby's 'Sakura' room during his stay over a century ago.
Audrey Hepburn reportedly toured the hotel exclaiming 'Wonderful! Beautiful!' and posed for a family photo beneath the lobby's Shinto-inspired chandelier.
Etched glass at the Tea Lounge entrance carries water-and-fish motifs intended as fire-prevention charms for the wooden building.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by wheat baked goods (apple pie, assorted cakes, sandwiches); no gluten-free items or allergen statements are published.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cakes, apple pie and royal milk tea contain dairy and eggs; no plant-based menu is documented.

caveats: Reported hours differ across sources (official site lists the lounge from 11:00 and to 18:00 with the space turning into a bar after; Tabelog lists 10:00–17:00). hours_week uses the official 10:00–18:00 café window but confirm on arrival. No published allergen/dietary accommodation info was found; safety fields are inferred from the documented menu, not from a stated policy. A 10% service charge applies.

lead: 囲炉裏ダイニング たなか
name_ja: 囲炉裏ダイニング たなか
cuisine: Irori (hearth) grill and wagyu cuisine — Yamato beef, kuroge wagyu, charcoal-grilled meats, seafood, and Yamato highland vegetables and rice
neighborhood: Naramachi (Kasasagi-chō), opposite the east gate of Gangō-ji Temple, near Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a wagyu/grill restaurant whose courses center on grilled meats, seafood, dried fish and tofu, and Japanese grill cooking relies heavily on soy-sauce- and miso-based marinades and dipping sauces (which contain wheat) plus shared grilling surfaces. There is no mention of allergen handling, gluten-free menus, or a dedicated fryer. Not suitable for a celiac diner without direct confirmation, and even then unlikely.
vegan_detail: This is a meat-focused wagyu and irori-grill restaurant. All documented courses are built around Yamato/kuroge wagyu beef, local poultry, seafood and dried fish; while courses include organic vegetable salad, farm vegetables, tofu and a rice course, these are served alongside meat and almost certainly use fish dashi. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. Not a vegan destination.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市鵲町6-11 カーサ奈良町
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 奈良県奈良市鵲町6番地; result [135.832306, 34.677811] falls within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 40 seats; individual tables with built-in irori hearths, plus private rooms for up to 8 people.
hours_raw: Lunch 12:00–14:00 / Dinner 17:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Tuesdays. (Note: Tabelog lists lunch starting 11:30; the official site lists 12:00.)
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional sit-down wagyu course restaurant in Naramachi where some Japanese language ability or a translation app will help, as menus and service are oriented to a local clientele; reservations and dinner courses run on the pricier side.

bio.background: Tucked into the old streets of Naramachi just opposite the east gate of Gangō-ji Temple, this irori dining room sets each table around a built-in sunken hearth, so guests grill and dine in an intimate, classically Japanese setting.
bio.specialty: Creative wagyu cuisine built on Yamato beef and premium kuroge wagyu, charcoal-grilled over the hearth alongside seafood, dried fish, tofu, organic salad and farm vegetables, finished with a rice course and seasonal dessert. Signature multi-course menus range from the Irori course to a premium Miyabi course featuring top-grade wagyu and Chateaubriand, with lunch plates and a beef hitsumabushi rice bowl.

anecdotes.text: Each table is set around its own built-in irori hearth, and private rooms seat up to eight, giving the meal a quiet, traditional Naramachi atmosphere.
The kitchen frames its cooking as Nara wagyu creative cuisine served in an irori (sunken-hearth) dining setting in Naramachi, by the east gate of Gangō-ji.

caveats: Lunch start time differs between sources (Tabelog 11:30 vs official site 12:00); hours_week uses the official 12:00 start. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan/vegetarian information is published, so dietary suitability could not be confirmed beyond the general nature of the menu.

lead: ラサ ボジュン 奈良店
name_ja: ラサ ボジュン 奈良店
cuisine: Sri Lankan curry
neighborhood: Takabatake-chō, near Nara Park / Shin-Yakushiji, ~15 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and no celiac protocol is documented. Sri Lankan curries served over rice with papadam (lentil cracker) are often naturally wheat-free, but the menu also includes roti (typically wheat) and curries may use blended spice pastes and condiments of unconfirmed composition. There is no documented dedicated fryer or cross-contamination control. A celiac traveler would need to ask the owner directly about each dish.
vegan_detail: Genuinely strong plant-based options. The kitchen centers on multiple vegetable and bean (lentil/dhal) curries made with self-blended spices and coconut milk (not dairy), reflecting the largely Buddhist, vegetable-forward Sri Lankan home diet. A dedicated Vegetarian Set/plate is on the menu, and meat is a separate add-on rather than a base. However, the standard Bojun Plate as described includes a boiled egg and milk tea, and vegan-specific labeling/cross-contact control is not documented, so confirm no egg/dairy/ghee when ordering vegan.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1098-1 KOTOMATIビル104
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市高畑町1098-1 returned [135.832901, 34.678116], within the Nara box.
seats_text: 26 seats including terrace seating where pets are allowed
hours_raw: 11:00-15:00 / 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Some listings show continuous 11:00-22:00. Closed day reported inconsistently across sources (one lists Monday, one lists Tuesday with a question mark, one lists irregular).
closed_days: Irregular / unconfirmed (reported variously as Monday, Tuesday, or no fixed holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly owner-run spot where a foreign student can order comfortably with a few words and gestures; the Sri Lankan owner serves international visitors and the plate format makes pointing easy.
caveats: Closed day is inconsistent across sources (Monday vs Tuesday vs irregular); treat the weekly schedule as approximate and the holiday as unconfirmed. Lunch break between 15:00-17:00 appears on some listings but others show continuous service. Vegan diners should confirm no egg/dairy/ghee; celiac diners should confirm wheat content of roti and spice blends.

bio.background: Rasa Bojun opened in December 2019 in the quiet Takabatake neighborhood on the Nara Park / Shin-Yakushiji side of the city. It is run by a Sri Lankan owner-chef, Vijitha, who set out to bring genuine Sri Lankan home cooking to Nara. The name 'Rasa Bojun' means 'delicious feast' in Sinhala.
bio.specialty: The signature is the Bojun Plate: a spread of several vegetable and bean curries (carrot, okra, lentil, green bean and more) with saffron-tinted rice, papadam, two kinds of coconut sambol, and a choice of meat curry on the side (chicken, pork, beef, or mutton). Roti sets, double-curry sets, and a dedicated vegetarian set round out the menu, alongside Sri Lankan and international beers and milk tea.
bio.philosophy: Cooking reflects Sri Lanka's largely Buddhist, vegetable-forward home diet where curries appear at every meal. Spices are blended in-house and simmered with coconut milk for a mild, layered heat, and diners are encouraged to mix the components on their plate so each combination tastes a little different.

anecdotes.text: The owner-chef Vijitha came to Japan about a year before opening and wanted to share authentic Sri Lankan home flavors with local diners.
The eatery is described as a genuine Sri Lankan owner-operated place serving mild, easy-to-enjoy but authentic curries.

positives.text: Vegetarian set is explicitly offered on the menu, and the kitchen builds plates around multiple vegetable and bean curries cooked in coconut milk rather than dairy.
Coverage notes vegetarian accommodation is available and the cuisine is naturally vegetable-focused.

lead: リストランテ ボルゴ・コニシ
name_ja: リストランテ ボルゴ・コニシ
cuisine: Italian (modern "Yamato Italian" using local Nara produce)
neighborhood: Naramachi / Konishi-cho, near Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: This is a course-based Italian restaurant where pasta is a core element, so wheat is central to the menu and the kitchen is not gluten-free. No dedicated GF program is documented. A celiac traveler should not assume safety; because it is fully reservation-only and chef-driven, dietary needs would need to be discussed in advance, but no GF accommodation is confirmed.
vegan_detail: An omakase-style Italian course menu built around seasonal Nara/Yamato vegetables alongside meat, fish, and dairy. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. Vegetables feature prominently, and as a fully reservation-only chef's-table format, a plant-based course might be arranged on request in advance, but this is not confirmed and should be asked about when booking.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市小西町24 フラッツ小西2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 奈良県奈良市小西町24; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 8 counter seats only
hours_raw: Tuesday–Sunday: 11:00–15:00 (lunch, last order/seating ~13:00), 18:00–22:00 (dinner, last seating ~19:00). Closed Mondays (or the following weekday when Monday is a holiday). Fully reservation-only.
closed_days: Mondays (or the following weekday if Monday is a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, fully reservation-only chef's-counter restaurant where staff likely operate mainly in Japanese, so a foreign student should book ahead (the owners are used to fine-dining service) and ideally have a Japanese-speaking friend help arrange the reservation and any dietary requests.
caveats: Dinner runs roughly 10,000–15,000 yen and lunch around 8,000–10,000 yen per person, plus a 10% service charge, so this is a special-occasion price point rather than a casual meal. Fully reservation-only (no walk-ins) with just 8 counter seats.

bio.background: Chef Masaki Yamasaki came to cooking through a part-time job, then trained for eleven years at the pasta specialist 'Piatto.' In 2006 he opened his own casual pasta restaurant, 'Bekkan Piatto,' which in 2014 he reimagined as the more refined 'Ristorante Borgo Konishi.' A formative influence was studying under chef Junichiro Horie of 'Ristorante I Lunga,' which reshaped his approach to sourcing and technique. The restaurant is run together with his wife, Aiko Yamasaki, who earned her sommelier certification in 2010 and the rarer Sommelier Excellence qualification in 2015, and who pairs Italian wines to each course while handling the dining-room service.
bio.specialty: A seasonal course they call 'Yamato Italian' (大和イタリアン): modern Italian cooking that reinterprets the produce, ingredients, and food culture of Nara — the birthplace of much of Japan's food heritage — through Italian regional techniques.
bio.philosophy: The couple frame the restaurant not as a stage for the chef's self-expression but as a place where guests enjoy their meal and leave feeling restored and energized. Drawing on the European tradition of a trusted personal chef who knows each guest's tastes, they tailor the experience to the individual, and the chef even holds a medicinal-diet educator certification reflecting his focus on health and vitality.

anecdotes.text: The eight-seat counter, an intimate space designed by an architect, features a counter crafted by a lacquer artist and background music composed specifically for the restaurant, so that the room, the wine, and the food are meant to harmonize as a single experience.
The restaurant evolved over years from a casual pasta shop ('Bekkan Piatto,' opened 2006) into the current refined 'Ristorante Borgo Konishi' in 2014.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a pasta-rooted Italian course restaurant, so wheat is integral to the menu and the kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented and it should not be assumed celiac-safe.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses are built around seasonal Nara vegetables but also use meat, fish, and dairy; no vegan menu or separate preparation is documented.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant is fully reservation-only and chef-driven with only eight counter seats, so individual preferences are accommodated by design, but no specific allergy or dietary protocol is documented and any needs should be raised when booking.

positives.text: Strong emphasis on fresh, seasonal local Yamato/Nara vegetables and produce within the courses.

cuisine: 三輪そうめん・にゅうめん・柿の葉寿司 (Miwa sōmen, nyūmen, kakinoha-zushi)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The entire menu is built around Miwa sōmen — wheat-flour noodles served chilled (hiyashi), warm (nyūmen), or kettle-style (kamaage). The dipping/broth is made with soy sauce and mirin, and standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat. There are no gluten-free dishes and no celiac accommodations documented. Not suitable for those avoiding gluten.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The broth/dashi for the sōmen and nyūmen is not documented as plant-based and typically uses fish dashi (katsuo/iriko) in this style of cuisine; the soy sauce and mirin are confirmed but the stock base is unconfirmed. The house-made kakinoha-zushi (persimmon-leaf sushi) is traditionally mackerel or salmon, so it is not vegan. A vegan diner would need to ask directly whether any preparation can be made without fish dashi or fish; assume it cannot without confirmation.
address_ja: 奈良県桜井市三輪535
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 奈良県桜井市三輪535 returns lat 34.526627, lng 135.846573, which is in Miwa, Sakurai City (in front of Ōmiwa Shrine's second torii) — roughly 15 km south of central Nara and well outside the central-Nara bounding box (lat 34.64–34.74). Coordinates set to null because the venue is not in central Nara; the real location is by Ōmiwa Shrine in Sakurai.
neighborhood: 三輪（大神神社二の鳥居前）, Sakurai City
seats_text: No fixed seat count published; small venue created from the garden of a converted ~100-year-old former family residence, with heavy pine-wood tables and open terrace-style seating. Fills up during shrine-visiting seasons.
hours_raw: Weekdays 10:40–15:30; Saturday 10:30–16:00; Sunday 10:00–16:00; holidays 10:30–16:00; the 1st of the month 7:45–13:30. May close earlier depending on weather (天候等により、早く閉店する時もあります). Tabelog lists roughly 10:00–17:00 (winter to 16:30); the official site hours above are more precise.
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays (open if it falls on a holiday or the 1st of the month); occasional irregular/weather-related closures.
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional, rustic family-run sōmen shop where little to no English is likely; a foreign student should be comfortable with a simple Japanese-language menu, cash payment, and a relaxed point-and-order approach.
caveats: This venue is in Miwa, Sakurai City, directly beside the second torii gate of Ōmiwa Shrine — it is NOT in central Nara (about 15 km south, reachable via the JR Manyo Mahoroba/Sakurai Line to Miwa Station). Coordinates were intentionally left null for that reason. Cash only, no reservations, and hours shift by day of week and season, so confirm before visiting. Not suitable for gluten-free diners; vegans should ask about the broth.

bio.origin: Miwa, Sakurai — the cradle of hand-stretched sōmen
bio.background: Morisho stands just to the left of the second torii gate of Ōmiwa Shrine, the great shrine at the heart of Miwa, an area celebrated as the birthplace of hand-stretched sōmen. The shop occupies a roughly century-old former family home: the owners converted their own residence and turned what was once the garden into the dining space, opening around 1979. The imposing front gate is said to be tied to the famous 'Miwa teahouse' that appears in Chikamatsu Monzaemon's classic puppet play 'Meido no Hikyaku,' where the characters Chubei and Umegawa stayed the night.
bio.specialty: Authentic Miwa sōmen served three ways: chilled hiyashi in the warm months, warm nyūmen year-round, and kettle-drawn kamaage in the cooler season — eaten at heavy American-pine tables in the old garden setting. The broth is made with local Nara soy sauce and mirin, and the house-made kakinoha-zushi (persimmon-leaf sushi) is a signature side.
bio.philosophy: A spirit of omotenashi (heartfelt hospitality) rooted in regional ingredients and the centuries-old craft of Miwa sōmen, which an Edo-period guide praised as 'fine as thread, white as snow, never swelling when boiled.'

anecdotes.text: The dining area is the former garden of a ~100-year-old house the owners once lived in, converted into the shop around 1979; it overflows with visitors during New Year shrine pilgrimages.
Warm nyūmen is served with a charming chopstick rest fashioned from a camellia leaf, and the chilled sōmen is finished with the shop's own persimmon-leaf sushi.
The entrance gate is linked to the 'Miwa teahouse' that features in Chikamatsu Monzaemon's play 'Meido no Hikyaku.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is dedicated to wheat sōmen; flour-based noodles are the core of every dish, so a shared-wheat environment should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The broth is made with local Nara soy sauce and mirin; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The sōmen/nyūmen broth base is not documented as plant-based and this style typically uses fish dashi; the house-made persimmon-leaf sushi uses fish.

cuisine: Traditional Italian (with Nara ingredients)
neighborhood: Naramachi / Imamikadocho
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on house-made fresh pasta (wheat) and Italian courses, so it is not celiac-safe. A solo chef running an omakase format could potentially accommodate a request if asked well in advance, but nothing specific is published — you must inquire directly.
vegan_detail: No vegan offering is documented. This is a meat-and-seafood-forward traditional Italian kitchen (signature dishes include roast suckling kid goat and Yamato pork), and there is no published plant-based menu. Because it is an omakase/course format run by one chef, dietary requests might be handled if arranged in advance by reservation, but you must ask directly — nothing is confirmed.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市今御門町26-2 Noriビル2F
geocode_note: Geocoded from chō-level address (今御門町26-2) via GSI; within central Nara box.
seats_text: Around 10 seats — a small space with a counter and a few tables, run almost single-handedly by the owner-chef.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:30, Dinner 17:30-20:30. Closed Mondays. Note: in practice the schedule can be irregular, with some days reserved for private bookings — check the Instagram before visiting.
closed_days: Mondays (plus occasional irregular closures / private-booking days)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny owner-run course restaurant where reservations are essential and it is cash only, so a foreign student should book ahead (ideally via the Instagram or phone) and be ready to communicate mostly in Japanese.

bio.background: The owner-chef honed his craft at a well-known Italian restaurant in Osaka before going independent and opening this small place in Naramachi on February 23, 2023. He runs it almost single-handedly, cooking, serving, and hosting himself.
bio.origin: Naramachi, Imamikadocho — tucked on the second floor of a building just south of Sarusawa Pond, above a renowned kaiseki restaurant.
bio.specialty: Traditional Italian dishes given a quiet Nara accent: hand-rolled fresh pasta (such as clam and bamboo-shoot pasta), Yamato pork fillet with a cheese crust, and a roast of young suckling kid goat that he describes as something you cannot find anywhere else in the Kansai region. Lunch and dinner are served as omakase courses, finishing with touches like local strawberry gelato.
bio.philosophy: Cooking honest, traditional Italian food while folding in Nara's own produce — Yamato vegetables, local pork, regional fruit — so the plate tastes of where it is made.

anecdotes.text: He built his name as head chef at a famous Osaka Italian restaurant before striking out on his own with this intimate Naramachi room.
His roast of young kid goat is presented as a specialty found nowhere else in Kansai, served with local vegetables and a lemon-green-pepper sauce.
The restaurant opened February 23, 2023, with the concept of 'traditional Italian cuisine with a touch of Nara essence.'

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen is built around house-made fresh wheat pasta and Italian courses; no gluten-free handling is documented, so cross-contact with wheat should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu is meat- and seafood-forward (kid goat, Yamato pork, clam pasta) with no documented plant-based preparations.

positives.text: It is a small omakase-style place run by a single chef who takes reservations, so there is a realistic chance to discuss dietary needs in advance if you book ahead.

caveats: Chef surname (Sasaki / 佐々木) and the specific Osaka training (cited in one search summary as "Colosseo") were not directly confirmed on a fetched page; the fetched sources confirm only that he trained at a well-known Osaka Italian restaurant before going independent. Hours are nominally Lunch 11:30-15:30 / Dinner 17:30-20:30 closed Mondays, but multiple sources stress the schedule is irregular with private-booking days — confirm via Instagram. Dietary (GF/vegan) accommodation is unconfirmed and listed as "ask."

lead: とよのあかり すずの音
name_ja: とよのあかり すずの音
cuisine: Yamato-vegetable hot pot (kusanabe) and yakitori; family-run washoku sake house
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and not celiac-safe. The kitchen is wheat- and soy-heavy: dishes rely on soy sauce, the kusanabe comes with prawn crackers (ebisen) and a ramen/risotto/rice-porridge finish, and yakitori glazes typically contain wheat-based soy sauce. There is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination protocol. A traveler with celiac disease should not assume safety here; at most one could ask in advance about a plain salt-broth kusanabe with vegetables only.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian-focused. The signature kusanabe is built around Yamato vegetables but as served includes Daisen chicken, house-made tsukune (chicken meatballs) and thin fried tofu, with a choice between a rich house chicken broth and a salt broth. The restaurant is primarily a yakitori (grilled chicken) sake house, so most of the menu contains meat and fish/chicken-based stock. A vegan or vegetarian could ask whether a vegetable-only kusanabe with the salt broth can be prepared, but there is no documented vegan offering or dashi disclosure, so verify directly before visiting.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市椿井町36
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.828125, 34.681065] for 奈良県奈良市椿井町36, within the central Nara box. Location is in Naramachi area near Higashimuki shopping arcade and Kintetsu Nara Station.
neighborhood: Naramachi (Tsubaii-cho), near Higashimuki shopping arcade and Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: 32 seats
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:30 (L.O. 14:00; kusanabe L.O. 13:30); Dinner 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Hours subject to change; irregular holidays.
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); closures announced via Instagram/LINE
cultural_comfort_note: A cozy converted-townhouse sake house where the staff happily explain the day's vegetables; a few words of Japanese and a willingness to point at the kusanabe will go a long way, as English menus are not guaranteed.

bio.background: Tucked into a renovated old wooden townhouse just off the Higashimuki shopping arcade near Kintetsu Nara Station, Suzunone opened on June 20, 2020 as the second outpost of Toyono Akari, a small Nara sake house built around grilled chicken and Yamato vegetables. The setting trades on the quiet, lantern-lit charm of an old machiya home, an easy stroll from Naramachi's lanes.
bio.specialty: The signature dish is the kusanabe ('grass pot'), a dramatic hot pot piled some 30cm high with fresh, locally grown Yamato vegetables. Diners choose between a rich house-made chicken broth and a lighter salt broth, and the pot comes with Daisen chicken, house-made tsukune meatballs, thin fried tofu and prawn crackers, finished with a choice of ramen, chicken-soboro rice, cheese risotto or rice porridge. The kitchen is also known for its charcoal-grilled chicken (kurogayaki), a rare-cooked oyakodon, and tori namero (chicken tartare).
bio.philosophy: The cooking centers on Nara's branded Yamato vegetables, a group of heritage varieties grown in the region since before the war alongside a handful of specially cultivated 'kodawari' vegetables, letting the day's freshest local produce take the spotlight.

anecdotes.text: On a holiday visit, the staff took the time to explain in detail the specific Yamato vegetables used that day, such as freshly morning-picked Yamato-mana greens.
The house pours an original sake also named Toyono Akari, brewed for the restaurant by Nara's Yagi Sake Brewery, a maker with well over a century of history, to pair with the kusanabe.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen serves wheat-based items including a ramen finish to the kusanabe and prawn crackers, and yakitori, so shared surfaces and utensils are likely; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes rely on standard Japanese soy sauce and yakitori glazes, which contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature kusanabe vegetables are cooked with chicken, house-made chicken tsukune and a chicken-stock broth option, so even the vegetable dish involves animal products and shared broth.

positives.text: Staff actively explain the specific local vegetables used each day, suggesting they are open to talking through ingredients if asked about dietary needs.

caveats: Hours are modeled as the same daily lunch/dinner pattern (lunch 11:30-14:30, dinner 17:00-22:00) because the restaurant posts a single set of hours with irregular (不定休) closures announced on Instagram/LINE rather than fixed weekly days off; confirm the specific day before visiting. Seat count (32) and price band are from the listing. No reservation requirement is stated (reservations accepted, phone preferred). Dietary safety ratings are conservative: this is a chicken- and wheat-forward sake house with no documented allergen or vegan handling.

cuisine: Japanese / small-plate (kogyo-ri) — tempura, sashimi, shokado bento, kaiseki
neighborhood: Naramachi (near Sarusawa Pond / Kofukuji five-story pagoda, north of Gango-ji)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市鶴福院町3-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市鶴福院町3-2 returned [135.831757, 34.679047], within the Nara box; consistent with the stated location at the Naramachi entrance south of Sarusawa Pond.
seats_text: 5 counter seats plus 2 tables seating 4 (about 15 seats total)
hours_raw: Lunch 12:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Dinner 18:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Thursdays. (Some listings note dinner from 17:00.)
closed_days: Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny neighborhood spot run by locals with an older, mostly-Japanese clientele and little English; basic Japanese or a translation app will make ordering much smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The core menu is tempura (wheat batter), fried chicken, and fish/soy-based dishes, and Japanese cooking here relies on standard wheat-containing soy sauce and fish dashi. Not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: This is a fish- and meat-forward Japanese eatery (sashimi, tempura, Yamato beef, fried chicken, grilled fish). Dishes use fish dashi and likely wheat-based soy sauce, and no vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. Not suitable for vegans.
caveats: Dinner start time varies by listing (some show 17:00, the main listing shows 18:00); hours_week uses the 18:00 dinner start. Owner/chef name and any husband-wife structure are not explicitly documented in the sources fetched.

bio.background: The owner-chef trained for 17 years in restaurants across Osaka, Kyoto, and Kobe before returning to his native Nara to open this small seasonal-cuisine restaurant on September 17, 2009, in the historic Naramachi district within sight of Kofukuji's five-story pagoda.
bio.specialty: Lunch centers on a limited-quantity shokado bento box with shrimp-and-vegetable tempura, seasonal grilled fish, salad, side dishes, pickles, miso soup, and rice; other sets feature Yamato beef bite steak, Awaji chicken karaage, and sashimi. Dinner offers a Kanon kaiseki with tempura and mini sashimi plus reservation-based omakase courses, paired with a deep selection of local Nara sake.
bio.philosophy: He set out to build a place beloved by its guests and to contribute to Nara's history and culture through carefully prepared seasonal food and local sake.

anecdotes.text: Despite sitting just steps from major tourist sights, the place keeps a quiet, neighborhood feel with a mostly local, older clientele, reading like a daytime cafe at lunch and turning into a relaxed sake izakaya by night.
Opened on September 17, 2009 after the owner's 17 years cooking in Osaka, Kyoto, and Kobe.

lead: ならまち 鮨 はなこ
name_ja: ならまち 鮨 はなこ
cuisine: 鮨／寿司（おまかせ・江戸前スタイル）
neighborhood: ならまち（西新屋町・奈良町資料館隣）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西新屋町14-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市西新屋町14-2 returned [135.830124, 34.676643], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 1階ヒノキのL字カウンター7席のみ（2階サロン7席は貸切のみ）。一人客にも向く。
hours_raw: 昼 12:00-13:30(または14:00)／夜 18:00-21:00頃。完全予約制、売り切れ次第終了。定休日は月曜（資料・公式により火・水の一部も休みとの記載あり）。
closed_days: 月曜定休（公式サイトでは第1火曜・水曜や年末年始も休みとの記載あり、要確認）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny reservation-only sushi counter where the chef serves an omakase course in Japanese; comfortable for a foreign student who is at ease with a quiet, traditional counter experience and some basic Japanese.
gf_detail: Edomae sushi using soy sauce, vinegared rice and fish-based preparations; soy sauce contains wheat and there is no gluten-free accommodation documented. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: An omakase sushi restaurant built around fish, shellfish and seafood (tuna, scallop, uni, conger eel, etc.) with fish-based broths. No vegan offering is documented.

bio.origin: 大阪府出身（若い頃から奈良に親しみ、憧れの地としてならまちに開業）
bio.background: 整体師・スポーツインストラクターから飲食業に転身し、大阪の鮨調理学校（飲食人大学 大阪校）で学んだ後、系列店「千陽」で経験を積み、海外（シンガポール、オーストラリア等）でも鮨職人として活躍。令和4年（2022年8月）に姉の祥子さんとともにならまちの古民家を改装してこの店を開いた。
bio.specialty: 江戸前スタイルのおまかせ。昆布締め・酢締め・漬けなどの仕事を施したつまみと、10〜14貫の握り。シャリは岐阜の内堀赤酢と京都の千鳥酢（米と酒粕由来）をブレンドし、甘味のある穏やかな味わいに仕上げる。帆立、鮪、烏賊、雲丹、毛蟹の茶碗蒸し、のどぐろ、穴子などが供される。
bio.philosophy: 伝統に縛られすぎず魚の質を活かす、繊細で新たな鮨コースを目指す姿勢。地元・奈良の食材や日本酒との相性も大切にしている。

anecdotes.text: 店は元興寺の金堂跡地に建つ古民家（元は日本酒バー）を改装し、奈良町らしい木造の佇まいを残している。
店主は『奈良は若い頃から訪れて馴染みがあり、憧れの地でもあった』と語り、姉の祥子さんと二人三脚で営む。

gf_cross_contamination.text: Sushi service relies on soy sauce (which contains wheat) and vinegared rice; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Edomae sushi uses regular soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is an all-seafood omakase with fish-based broths; no vegan items or separate vegan preparation are documented.

caveats: Two-person sibling-run omakase counter; completely reservation-only and may close once ingredients sell out. Closed-day details vary between sources (Tabelog lists Monday; the official foodre.jp page also mentions 1st Tuesday/Wednesday) — confirm when booking. The chef is Hanako Matsumiya (per editorial sources); an unverified search snippet about a different male chef taking over in 2025 was disregarded as it conflicts with confirmed sources.

cuisine: 洋菓子・ケーキ＆喫茶（奈良産フルーツや酒粕を使った素朴なケーキ）
neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅エリア）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市中院町20
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市中院町20 returned [135.831223, 34.67802], within the Nara box.
seats_text: A tiny eat-in counter with roughly 4 seats; primarily a takeout shop.
hours_raw: Mon/Thu/Fri 10:30-20:00; Sat/Sun/Holidays 10:00-20:00; closed Tue & Wed. Closes early when sold out. Irregular closures announced on Instagram.
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday (plus occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny family-run townhouse cake shop where ordering is in Japanese and the few seats fill up fast, so a little basic Japanese or a translation app will make pointing at the case and ordering much smoother.
gf_detail: A traditional Western-style cake and pastry shop; the cakes, roll cakes, shortcakes, and cheesecake are flour-based, so this is not safe for celiac or gluten-free diners. No gluten-free items or allergy handling are documented.
vegan_detail: Classic Western confectionery built on dairy, eggs, butter, and cream (cheesecake, cream shortcakes, chocolate roll). No vegan options are documented and the menu is fundamentally non-vegan.
caveats: Hours and closed days vary between sources (some list closed Wednesdays only, Tabelog lists Tue & Wed); the shop closes early when items sell out, and occasional closures are posted to Instagram. Confirm before visiting. No official website found; an Instagram account is referenced for updates.

bio.background: A small, independently run cake shop tucked into a restored machiya (traditional townhouse) in Nara's historic Naramachi district, near the Nara Town Information Center. The retro wooden exterior blends quietly into the old streetscape, and the warm wood interior holds just a tiny counter where guests can sit with a cup of tea.
bio.specialty: The signature is a rich sake-lees cheesecake made with lees from the local Harushika sake brewery, which carries a gentle waft of sake aroma. Other beloved items include strawberry shortcake, a chocolate roll cake, and a strawberry charlotte, often made with Nara-grown fruit.
bio.philosophy: The shop focuses on carefully made, old-fashioned, nostalgic cakes rather than trendy designs, aiming for flavors that are simple yet never tiring.

anecdotes.text: The shop is well known around Naramachi and items can sell out before noon, with the sake-lees cheesecake being the most sought-after.
Set in a roughly four-tatami restored townhouse, the shop offers a small seat and complimentary tea so visitors can enjoy a cake on the spot.

cuisine: Tempura (天ぷら) — counter-style, freshly fried, lunch only
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅エリア（ならまち・下御門商店街）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市下御門町35
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.829269, 34.679035] for 奈良県奈良市下御門町35; within Nara box.
seats_text: 11 counter seats only; the chef fries each piece in front of guests.
hours_raw: 11:30–15:30 (some sources list to 16:00); closes when ingredients run out. Irregular closures (the shop publishes specific closed dates each month).
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); historically Monday was listed as closed, but the shop now publishes specific closed dates each month, so confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, intimate 11-seat counter where the chef fries and serves each piece in turn — there is little English, so a few Japanese phrases and a relaxed lunch pace will make the experience smooth for a foreign student.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan restaurant. The set meal is mostly vegetable tempura (eight or so seasonal vegetables) alongside seafood, but tempura batter and the accompanying tentsuyu/miso soup typically rely on fish (bonito) dashi, and items are fried in shared oil with seafood. Vegetable pieces exist but are not prepared as a vegan meal; ask directly if you avoid all animal products.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free and not celiac-safe. Tempura batter is wheat flour, the dipping sauce/seasonings commonly contain wheat-based soy sauce, and all items are fried in the same oil. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.

bio.background: A new tempura specialist that opened on 22 August 2025 along the Shimogomon shopping arcade in central Nara, beside the Shimomikado post office and an easy walk from Kintetsu Nara Station into the Naramachi quarter.
bio.specialty: A lunch-only tempura set (天ぷら御膳, around 2,640 yen) of roughly three seafood items and eight seasonal vegetables, served with rice, miso soup and pickles. The lineup rotates with the season — one documented day included chestnut, shrimp, sweetfish (or sea bream), string beans, squid, maitake, eggplant and sweet potato — and diners can add a featured fish or extra vegetable a la carte.
bio.philosophy: Each piece is fried to order and handed across the counter one at a time so it is eaten at its crispest — the appeal is the immediacy and rhythm of counter tempura rather than a large menu.

anecdotes.text: Opened 22 August 2025 in the Shimogomon arcade next to the Shimomikado post office, with 11 counter seats where the chef fries in front of guests.

Signature is the seasonal tempura set (about 2,640 yen) with several seafood and many vegetable pieces plus rice, soup and pickles, served one piece at a time and closing once the day's ingredients run out.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Tempura is wheat-flour batter deep-fried in shared oil with seafood and vegetables; no separate fryer or gluten-free option is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Tempura is typically served with tentsuyu/soy-based seasoning, which normally contains wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable tempura is fried in the same oil as shrimp, squid and fish, and the accompanying miso soup/dipping sauce typically use fish dashi.

caveats: Sister-restaurant relationship to つる由 (Tsuruyoshi) is suggested by the crane/turtle name pairing but was not confirmed in any single fetched source, so it is omitted from the bio. Chef's name is not documented. Hours and closed days vary; the shop publishes monthly closed dates and closes when ingredients sell out, so confirm by phone (0742-26-3800).

cuisine: コロッケ＆ワインバー・洋食
neighborhood: 角振町（近鉄奈良駅徒歩約3分、ひがしむき／三条通り近く）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市角振町5
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市角振町5 returned [135.827164, 34.681553], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Counter seating (Tabelog lists 7 counter seats; local articles describe a U-shaped/L-shaped counter of about 6 seats plus a standing area at the front for takeout).
closed_days: Thursdays (木曜定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, friendly counter croquette-and-wine bar where a smile and a little basic Japanese go a long way; you can also just grab a takeout croquette from the front if you'd rather keep it simple.
caveats: Wheat-heavy by nature: croquettes are coated in flour and panko breadcrumbs and deep-fried, and pasta dishes use wheat noodles, so this is not a celiac-safe or gluten-free destination. No documented vegan menu — croquettes typically contain beef/meat and the kitchen is meat-and-wine focused. Hours: Tabelog and local press differ slightly (Tabelog lists 16:00 start on weekdays; articles mention noon-to-evening continuous service), so confirm same-day via Instagram.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature dish is breaded, deep-fried croquettes (flour + panko coating) and the menu also includes wheat-flour pasta/fettuccine. No gluten-free options or dedicated handling are documented.
vegan_detail: No vegan offering is documented. The house specialty is beef-tendon croquettes and the food menu centers on meat and seafood (e.g., shrimp cream pasta), paired with wine, beer and craft gin. No plant-based menu is advertised.

bio.background: Croquette GranD is the second outpost of Wine Bar Grand, a wine bar near Kintetsu Shin-Omiya Station, opened by the same operator in October 2024. It sits on a quiet lane just off Konishi-dori and Sanjo-dori, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, with a casual front standing area for takeout and a small counter in back for sitting down.
bio.specialty: Made-to-order beef-tendon croquettes, spiced and richly flavored, fried to a crisp shell with a fluffy potato center and deliberately matched to wine; croquettes start around 150 yen each. The kitchen also serves house-made fettuccine pasta such as shrimp cream pasta, alongside wine, beer and craft gin (including Kurayoshi Distillery gin from Tottori).
bio.philosophy: A relaxed spot that bridges snack and meal — croquettes chosen to pair with wine, welcoming everyone from someone stopping for a quick glass to diners settling in for a proper bite.

anecdotes.text: The croquettes are billed as specially selected to go well with wine, and the beef-tendon croquette is the signature, described as spiced and full-flavored and bagged in paper for casual eating.
Opened October 11, 2024 as a second location of Shin-Omiya's Wine Bar Grand, a roughly 3-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station via Konishi-dori and across Sanjo-dori.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All croquettes are flour- and panko-breaded and deep-fried, so the fryer is used for wheat-coated foods; not suitable for gluten-free or celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature item is beef-tendon croquettes and the food menu centers on meat and seafood, so shared fryer/kitchen surfaces make plant-based dining impractical.

cuisine: グルテンフリー・ヴィーガン対応の自然派カフェ（米粉フォカッチャ、奈良周辺の季節野菜のサラダプレート）
neighborhood: ならまち南部・西紀寺町（京終駅から徒歩約9分）
gf_detail: グルテンフリーをコンセプトの中心に掲げ、米粉の自家製フォカッチャや米粉ケーキ、グルテンフリーの自家製ドレッシングを使用。小麦の代わりに米粉を用いた構成で、グルテンに敏感な人を明確に歓迎している。ただし専用調理スペースやセリアック向けの交差汚染対策が文書化されていないため、重度のセリアック病の場合は事前確認を推奨。
vegan_detail: ヴィーガン・ベジタリアンに明確に対応。看板メニューの季節のサラダプレートは10〜15種の地元有機野菜を使い植物性で構成可能。デザートには京都MINORIの植物性ジェラート（豆乳・ココナッツミルクベース）を使用。ただしハラール・コーシャ対応も掲げる店であり全メニューがヴィーガンというわけではないため、植物性希望の旨を伝えるのが確実。
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西紀寺町27
geocode_note: 国土地理院ジオコーダーで住所「奈良県奈良市西紀寺町27」の最初のフィーチャーを使用。奈良ボックス内。
seats_text: 約11〜12席（屋外カウンター2席、店内カウンター3席、2人掛けテーブル3卓、4人掛けテーブル1卓）
hours_raw: 11:00-18:00。火曜定休、水曜は隔週で休み。ペット同伴は13:30-18:00の指定席のみ可。
closed_days: 火曜、隔週水曜
cultural_comfort_note: 食の多様性を理念に掲げグルテンフリー・ヴィーガン・ハラール・コーシャに明確対応する店なので、外国人留学生でも食事制限を伝えやすく安心して訪れやすい。
caveats: 隔週水曜が休みのため訪問前に営業日の確認を推奨。重度のセリアック病やアレルギーがある場合、専用調理・交差汚染対策が公表されていないため事前に直接確認するのが安全。

bio.background: 江戸後期から明治の面影を残すならまち南部・西紀寺町の古い建物を改装したカフェ。外観はつる植物に覆われ、店内は無垢の木が基調。大きなトップライトから木漏れ日が差し込み、まるで森の中で食事をしているような空間で、英国Arcolleの椅子やニューヨークのOrganic Modernismの家具がゆったりと配されている。
bio.philosophy: 「誰もが自由に食を楽しめる」ことを理念に掲げる。オーナー自身が犬アレルギーを持ちながら、あえてアレルギーや宗教上の理由で食事制限がある人、さらにはペット連れの人も安心して過ごせる場として設計した。
bio.specialty: 看板は季節のサラダセット。奈良県産・南山城・自家菜園などの有機野菜10〜15種を二十四節気に合わせて彩りよく盛り付け、米粉の自家製フォカッチャとスープを添える。米粉のキャロットジンジャーケーキや、京都MINORIの植物性ジェラートをのせたあずきのデザートも人気。

anecdotes.text: オーナー自身が犬アレルギーを持ちながら、ペット連れも歓迎する店づくりをしている。ペット同伴は13:30〜18:00、奥の指定席でキャリーやカートに入れての利用が可能。
サラダに使う野菜は自家菜園や奈良・南山城産の有機野菜を中心に10〜15種類を使い、二十四節気に合わせて内容が変わる。

gf_cross_contamination.text: グルテンフリーをコンセプトに米粉を使用するが、専用調理スペースやセリアック向けの交差汚染対策は公表されていないため、重度のセリアック病の場合は事前確認が必要。
