vegan_cross_contact.text: 植物性デザートに豆乳・ココナッツミルクベースのジェラートを使用しており大豆を含む。大豆アレルギーがある場合は注意。

staff_allergy_handling.text: アレルギーや宗教上の理由による食事制限がある人が安心して食事できることを理念に掲げ、グルテンフリー・ベジタリアン・ヴィーガン・ハラール・コーシャに対応すると明記している。

positives.text: 米粉の自家製フォカッチャ・米粉ケーキ・グルテンフリーの自家製ドレッシングを用意し、グルテンフリーを店の中心に据えている。
奈良県産・南山城産・自家菜園の有機野菜を10〜15種使った植物性のサラダプレートが看板メニュー。

lead: 絵本とコーヒーのパビリオン
name_ja: 絵本とコーヒーのパビリオン
cuisine: Self-roasted coffee house in a renovated pre-war machiya/nagaya; picture-book cafe serving custard pudding, cheesecake, scones and a small lunch
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. This is a Japanese cafe whose menu centers on baked goods (cheesecake, scones), custard pudding and a curry-with-rice lunch — wheat is present in the scones and likely the cheesecake base, and curry roux typically contains wheat. There is no indication of celiac-aware preparation, so it is not suitable for someone requiring strict gluten avoidance. Ask in person if you only need to reduce gluten rather than eliminate it.
vegan_detail: No vegan items are documented. The signature foods are dairy-and-egg desserts (custard pudding from the owner's mother's recipe, cheesecake, scones) and a tomato-chicken curry lunch. Coffee can of course be had black, but there is no stated plant-milk option or vegan food. Treat this as a non-vegan cafe and confirm any dairy-free coffee option in person.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市今辻子町32-5
geocode_note: GSI address search returned the first feature for 奈良県奈良市今辻子町32 at [135.823074, 34.68317], within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: Naramachi area / Imatsujishi-cho, a quiet back alley west of Emperor Kaika's mausoleum, about a 6-7 minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: 15 seats
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, calm husband-and-wife cafe down a hidden alley where a friendly greeting goes a long way; the owners stock domestic and international picture books and the spot draws visitors of all ages, but a few words of Japanese will help with ordering.

bio.background: Tucked down a narrow back alley near Emperor Kaika's mausoleum in central Nara, this picture-book cafe was created by a husband-and-wife team — Masato, who roasts the coffee, and Chiharu, who curates the books — who spent years renovating a humble pre-war row house (nagaya) by hand, opening the doors in November 2009. The little building, with its red-tin exterior and green roof, was fitted out with the couple's own handmade tables, chairs and shelves, and the room is lined with picture books, little-press titles and poetry collections from Japan and abroad that guests are free to browse, read or buy.
bio.specialty: Self-roasted coffee offered in three house roasts the owner labels by color — 青 (medium), 赤 (medium-dark) and 黒 (dark) — served in antique cups, with the owner recommending one to match your taste. The signature food is a firm, old-fashioned custard pudding made from the master's mother's recipe: nostalgic, gently sweet rather than bitter, with a smooth caramel. Cheesecake, scones and a small lunch (such as a tomato-chicken curry, served around midday) round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: An invitation to slow down — to settle in with a picture book and a cup of carefully roasted coffee in a hidden hideaway, where the books are as much the point as the drinks.

anecdotes.text: The couple spent roughly three and a half years renovating the pre-war row house before opening in 2009, building the tables, floor and chairs themselves.
The custard pudding follows a recipe the owner's mother once made — a deliberately simple, not-too-sweet, retro style.
Coffee comes in three self-roasted levels the owner names by color (青/赤/黒), served in antique cups with a personal recommendation.

caveats: Coffee roast levels (青 medium / 赤 medium-dark / 黒 dark) and the lunch curry are documented in cafe-visit articles rather than the official site; prices and lunch availability may vary. No allergen or gluten/vegan handling information is published, so dietary travelers should confirm in person.

lead: イタリアン キャラベル（Caravel）
name_ja: イタリアン キャラベル
cuisine: Italian café-bar — fresh (handmade) pasta, pizza, Western-style dishes (hayashi rice), desserts, coffee and wine
neighborhood: Mochiidono Center-gai / Mochiidono-cho (near Kintetsu Nara Station)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町14番地 西村ビル2階
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町14番地 to [135.829178, 34.68055]; within Nara box.
seats_text: Seat count not published. The 2F space offers mixed seating — leather sofas, assorted chairs, and individual table seats — and is described as comfortable for solo diners as well as small groups.
hours_raw: Tue–Sun 11:30–15:30 lunch and 17:30–21:00 dinner (Tabelog); the shop's own listings describe lunch 11:30–15:00, café 15:00–18:00, dinner 18:30–21:30 (dinner last order around 21:00). Closed Mondays.
closed_days: Mondays (occasional substitute closures, e.g., Jan 13–15)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, friendly Italian café-bar where a relaxed manner and a little Japanese will go a long way; no English menu is documented, so pointing at dishes or showing photos helps.
gf_detail: This is a handmade-pasta and pizza house, so wheat is central to nearly the entire menu (pasta, pizza dough, breaded/Western items, desserts). No gluten-free menu, dedicated prep, or celiac accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The menu is built around cheese, cream and meat/seafood pasta (carbonara, shrimp-cream), pizza with mozzarella, and dessert like tiramisu/mascarpone. A vegetable-and-oil pasta or a margherita-style pizza (with cheese) might be adaptable, but anything plant-only would need to be confirmed directly with staff. Treat as ask.

bio.background: A hidden second-floor café-bar tucked above the historic Mochiidono shopping arcade, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. Caravel is the dining side of a small local outfit that also runs an adjoining select shop of vintage clothing, apparel and curios — so the dining room carries the same retro, collected-by-hand sensibility, full of cute bits and pieces and described as the kind of place a solo visitor can settle into comfortably.
bio.specialty: Handmade fresh pasta is the heart of the menu — carbonara, a peppery arugula pasta, and a shrimp-cream sauce among them — alongside pizza (margherita), Western-style plates such as hayashi rice, homemade tiramisu, and a wine list to linger over.
bio.philosophy: The guiding idea is approachable, authentic Italian you can drop in for anytime — proper fresh pasta in a casual, welcoming room rather than a formal restaurant.
roles: owner-operated café-bar tied to a sister select shop

anecdotes.text: Caravel and its sister vintage/select shop operate as one small family of shops, with the retail side built around the themes of 'healing and stimulation' and pieces meant to be kept and used for years — a sensibility that carries into the café's warm, collected interior.
It is regularly described as a stylish hidden gem on the second floor of the arcade, with leather sofas and mismatched seating that suit dates, small groups, or a quiet coffee alike.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen centers on handmade pasta and pizza dough, so flour is in heavy use throughout; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu leans on cheese, cream, meat and seafood (carbonara, shrimp-cream pasta, mozzarella pizza, mascarpone tiramisu); no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

caveats: Two slightly different hour sets are published: Tabelog lists Tue–Sun 11:30–15:30 and 17:30–21:00, while the shop's own/area listings describe lunch 11:30–15:00, a 15:00–18:00 café window, and dinner 18:30–21:30. Confirm same-day. Seat count and any English-language support are not documented. Dietary safety notes are inferred from the menu profile, not from a published allergen policy — confirm directly for celiac or strict-vegan needs.

cuisine: 洋食・ステーキ・粗挽きハンバーグ (Yoshoku / teppan hamburger steak & steak)
neighborhood: 高畑町（ならまち東側・奈良公園寄り） / Takabatake, near Naramachi and Nara Park
gf_detail: This is a teppan hamburger and steak house. Hamburger patties contain binders and the dishes are served with onion/red-wine sauces; no gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no documented celiac protocols. Not suitable for strict gluten-free or celiac diners.
vegan_detail: A meat-focused restaurant whose entire concept is coarse-ground beef/pork hamburger steak and rare beef steak. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1098-1 KOTOMACHIビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市高畑町1098-1 returned [135.832901, 34.678116], within the Nara box.
seats_text: About 25 seats: roughly 9 counter seats and several 2-person tables indoors, plus a pet-friendly terrace (terrace by phone reservation). Counter chairs are high.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:30 (L.O. 14:00) daily except Tuesday; Dinner 18:00–21:30 (L.O. 20:30) Wed–Sun & holidays. Monday is lunch only. Tuesday closed. Some irregular/event closures and year-end/New Year holidays.
closed_days: Tuesdays (plus Monday dinner); occasional irregular and event closures; year-end/New Year.
cultural_comfort_note: A small local teppan spot where ordering is smoother in Japanese, and because mains must be reserved in advance to secure stock, a foreign student should phone or message ahead (ideally with a Japanese-speaking friend).
caveats: Mains often require advance ordering/reservation to secure stock; the T-bone steak needs roughly 4 days' notice. Hours are irregular with event closures, so confirm before visiting. Daily covers are limited (around 50 meals).

bio.background: PE LONCHO opened in December 2019 in the Takabatake area at the eastern end of Naramachi-dori, closer to Nara Park. It is a small, independent teppan (iron-griddle) restaurant built around two signature plates: a thick coarse-ground hamburger steak and a thick-cut, rare beef steak, each served sizzling on a hot iron plate.
bio.specialty: Coarse-ground teppan hamburger steak and rare beef steak (around 250g portions). The hamburger blends Yamato beef, chuck roll and Kazaguruma pork in a secret ratio, finished with a sauce built on plenty of onions and red wine.

anecdotes.text: The signature hamburger recipe is said to have been handed down from a well-known Italian-cuisine chef in the Sangenjaya area of Tokyo.
The kitchen serves a limited number of meals per day (around 50), reflecting a small, hands-on operation.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hamburger steak is a core dish and typically contains wheat-based binders; sauces are onion/red-wine based with no stated gluten-free preparation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Entirely a beef/pork teppan concept (hamburger and steak); no vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented.

lead: 二代目 出雲そば だんだん
name_ja: 二代目 出雲そば だんだん
cuisine: 出雲そば・自家製粉手打ちそば（割子そば・釜揚げ）/ Izumo-style hand-cut soba, house-milled buckwheat
neighborhood: 新大宮〜近鉄奈良駅エリア（大宮町、三条通沿いの路地）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市大宮町2-2-34
geocode_note: GSI address-search matched 奈良県奈良市大宮町二丁目2番34号; coordinates within Nara box.
seats_text: Tabelog lists 15 seats (tables); a visitor blog describes a more intimate ~7 seats (4 counter + 3 tables) run by two staff. Small either way.
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, owner-run soba house where reservations are essential and walk-ins are often turned away, so a foreign student should book ahead (ideally with some Japanese) and be ready for a quiet, Japanese-language setting.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. Soba noodles here are made from buckwheat but Japanese soba typically uses wheat as a binding/cutting flour, and the dipping tsuyu is built on soy sauce (contains wheat) and fish dashi. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or celiac accommodation is documented. Celiac travelers should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The soba tsuyu and the seasonal omakase courses rely on fish/bonito dashi, and warigo soba is typically topped with bonito flakes. No vegan menu is documented; a vegan would need to ask in advance and options would be very limited. Treat as effectively non-vegan unless confirmed directly.
caveats: Seat count differs between sources (Tabelog 15 vs a visitor blog's ~7). Dietary details (gluten, dashi, soy sauce) are inferred from standard soba/tsuyu practice, not from an explicit allergen disclosure by the restaurant; confirm directly for any allergy or vegan need.

bio.background: Dandan first opened in 2008 to bring the flavors of Izumo (Shimane Prefecture) to Nara. In 2016 the founder's daughter, who had trained in French cuisine, took over as the second-generation owner, giving the shop its '二代目' (nidaime, 'second generation') name and broadening the menu beyond classic soba into creative, seasonal cooking.
bio.specialty: Izumo-style soba made from house-milled, hand-cut 100% buckwheat (jūwari), ground fresh and including the hull for the characteristic dark, rustic noodle. The signature warigo soba is served in stacked red lacquer tiers in the traditional Izumo style, topped with grated daikon, bonito flakes, dried wakame and green onion; kamaage (hot) soba and tempura are also offered. The evening is a seven-course omakase concluding with soba, drawing on seasonal ingredients from Shimane and around Japan, paired with Japanese sake and natural wine.
bio.philosophy: The name 'だんだん' means 'thank you' in the Izumo dialect, expressing gratitude to both the guests and the ingredients. The kitchen mills its own buckwheat each morning and dresses the room with seasonal ikebana, aiming for a calm, gracious experience rooted in regional Shimane tradition.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits on a quiet alley off Sanjo-dori near Omiya kindergarten, with antiques and a mix of counter and table seating creating a calm, sophisticated mood.
The proprietor is from Shimane and sources ingredients regionally; the soba is rough-textured 100% buckwheat served with a concentrated dipping sauce and generous sobayu (buckwheat broth) to finish.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba noodles and a soy-sauce/fish-dashi-based tsuyu are central here; no gluten-free preparation or dedicated equipment is documented, so cross-contamination with wheat should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The dipping sauce (tsuyu) for the soba is soy-sauce based, which in standard Japanese practice contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Warigo soba toppings include bonito flakes and the broth uses fish dashi, so dishes are not free of animal-derived ingredients.

positives.text: Buckwheat is milled in-house and noodles are hand-cut each morning, so the kitchen is hands-on and ingredient-focused, which can make it worth phoning ahead to discuss specific needs.

cuisine: South/North Indian spice curry and meals (ミールス)
neighborhood: きたまち / 花芝町 (near Kintetsu Nara Station north side)
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and no celiac protocol is documented. The meals plate centers on rice (jasmine/Nara rice) and many spice-based vegetable sides, which can lean naturally wheat-free, but fried items such as pakoda fritters and papad (lentil wafers) may contain or be cross-contacted by wheat, and the daily curries are made in a shared kitchen with no dedicated equipment noted. A celiac traveler must ask in person about each component; nothing specific is published.
vegan_detail: The fixed meals format pairs a choice of curries (which on most days include meat/fish options such as fish, mutton, chicken or pork curries) with a large spread of roughly eight vegetable sides — e.g. coconut-stir-fried carrots, mustard-simmered yam and taro, leafy-green and red-radish kachumbar salad, dal, achar pickles and chutneys. A vegetable curry is usually available so a largely plant-based meals plate can be assembled, but the kitchen has not published whether dals/sides use ghee, dairy or any fish/animal stock, and it is not a dedicated vegetarian/vegan kitchen. Vegans should confirm dairy (ghee/yogurt) and stock in person.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市花芝町30-3
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.829056, 34.686321] for 奈良県奈良市花芝町30-3; within Nara box.
seats_text: 14 seats (12 counter, 1 table of 4)
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday, Sunday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, popular counter shop with no reservations — write your name in the entry ledger and wait; a few words of Japanese and patience go a long way, and the hand-eating Indian style is welcomed.

bio.background: The owner-chef honed his craft at established Nara Indian and curry kitchens — including time as chef at an Indian cafe-restaurant in Tomigaoka and a monthly pop-up at a spot in Ayameike — and made a multi-week journey through India to deepen his feel for regional cooking before opening his own place in February 2021 in the Hanashiba-cho area just north of Kintetsu Nara Station.
bio.specialty: A daily-changing 'meals' (ミールス) plate: a choice of one to four curries that rotate each day — spanning South Indian chicken, Kerala-style seafood, vindaloo and vegetable curries — served around rice with about eight sides such as coconut-stir-fried carrots, mustard-simmered yam and taro, dal, kachumbar salad, achar pickles, chutneys, pakoda fritters and crisp lentil papad.
bio.philosophy: Indian food adapted to Nara's climate and local produce, plated like a jewel box but meant to be mixed and eaten by hand — the chef describes treating the plate as a palette and adjusting the flavors with your fingers as you go.

anecdotes.text: The chef frames authentic Indian dining as hands-on: '皿をパレットに見立て、指で調理しながら食べるのがインド流' — treat the plate as a palette and season as you eat with your fingers.
Local curry writers greeted the 2021 opening as 'a supernova born in Nara,' noting the chef's pedigree at earlier Nara kitchens.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The plate includes fried pakoda fritters and crisp lentil papad/appalam, which commonly involve wheat or shared frying oil; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Curries on most days include fish, mutton, chicken or pork options served from the same daily lineup as the vegetable curry; sides' use of ghee/dairy or any stock is not published.

positives.text: The fixed meals format includes a wide range of vegetable sides (coconut carrots, mustard yam and taro, dal, kachumbar salad, pickles, chutneys) so a largely plant-based plate can usually be assembled.

caveats: Chef's full name is not reliably confirmed (sources give conflicting nickname/name), so it is left null. Daily-rotating menu means specific curries vary; no published allergen or vegan/GF protocol, so dietary travelers must confirm in person. Hours: lunch Wed-Sat plus dinner only Fri-Sat; closed Mon/Tue/Sun.

cuisine: Temple café — Silk Road spice curry, Yamato-vegetable dishes, and a seasonal Japanese-sweets afternoon tea
neighborhood: きたまち / Kitamachi (near Kintetsu Nara Station, within the grounds of a renovated former Kofuku-ji sub-temple)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市登大路町63
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 奈良県奈良市登大路町63 to lat 34.685059, lng 135.835175 — within the Nara box. Note: the Tabelog/official listings place the café in Nodaiji-cho 63 (inside the renovated former 世尊院 / Shisui hotel grounds) rather than the 北袋町 address given in the target hint.
seats_text: 43 seats
closed_days: None listed (open daily)
cultural_comfort_note: Set inside an upscale hotel that draws international guests, so an English-speaking student should feel comfortable, though it is a refined, higher-priced afternoon-tea setting rather than a casual drop-in.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. Several menu items use fish or animal products — e.g. seared mackerel sushi (炙り鯖寿司), Yamato-beef nyumen, and chicken curry — and noodle/dashi dishes typically rely on fish stock. Some plant-forward items exist (a Yamato mustard-green smoothie, kuzu-dumpling kakigori anmitsu with Yamato soybean kinako, and plant-based elements in the seasonal afternoon tea), but no dish is documented as certified vegan. A vegan diner should ask in advance.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe for celiacs. The menu includes wheat-based noodle dishes (にゅうめん / sōmen) and sushi/curry that commonly involve soy sauce, and no gluten-free protocol or dedicated preparation is documented. Some naturally gluten-free items (rice-based sweets, kuzu, kinako) appear, but cross-contamination cannot be ruled out — ask staff directly.

roles: Hashimoto Maki — Chinese-medicine wellness instructor and plant therapist who supervised the seasonal afternoon-tea menu
bio.background: The café occupies Sesonji (世尊院), a former sub-temple of Kōfuku-ji built in the late Edo period and carefully restored, preserving the temple architecture and a Japanese garden of seasonal plants.
bio.specialty: Signature dishes include a red chicken curry built on an original house spice blend brightened with Yamato tachibana citrus, seared mackerel sushi, Yamato-beef nyumen, spiced lattes, and kuzu-dumpling kakigori anmitsu with Yamato soybean kinako.
bio.philosophy: The concept ties Nara's history as the gateway where Silk Road spices and medicinal herbs first entered Japan (the Shōsōin recorded some 60 spices by the 8th century) to a wellness-minded menu. Its seasonal afternoon tea follows the Eastern principle of yin-yang and the five elements (陰陽五行), rotating wood, fire, earth, metal, and water through the year.

anecdotes.text: A visiting food writer described the rare smoked-dried green plum (烏梅) used here as tasting like a smoky plum juice, and noted the soup featured bent noodle scraps (節) left from traditional hand-pulled noodle making.

positives.text: Menu features some naturally plant-based and rice/kuzu-based items such as a Yamato mustard-green smoothie and kuzu-dumpling kakigori anmitsu with Yamato soybean kinako.

caveats: The target hint listed the neighborhood as 北袋町; the verified Tabelog and official listings place the café at 登大路町63 inside the grounds of the restored former 世尊院 (now part of the Shisui / Luxury Collection hotel). It opened 29 August 2023, is open daily 11:00-17:00, and a 15% service charge applies. It sits within an upscale hotel complex, so it is not an independent mom-and-pop and meals run well over ¥3,000 (afternoon tea ~¥7,000). No gluten-free or vegan protocol is documented — verify directly for dietary needs.

cuisine: Patisserie & cafe (Basque cheesecake, tiramisu, seasonal Nara-fruit pastries, hand-drip coffee)
gf_detail: This is a patisserie whose core offerings are wheat-based: Basque cheesecake, tiramisu, montblanc, tarts, and lemon sablé cookies all contain flour. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated prep, and shared baking equipment. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A dessert cafe built around dairy- and egg-rich baked goods (cheesecake, cream-based tiramisu, butter pastries). No vegan items documented. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市水門町70-1-3-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市水門町70-1 returned [135.835876, 34.686699], within the Nara box and consistent with the Suimon-cho location beside Akordu on the west side of Todai-ji.
neighborhood: きたまち / 水門町 (Suimon-cho), west side of Todai-ji, near Nara Park; on the grounds of Akordu restaurant. ~15 min walk (755m) from Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: Compact interior with about 14 seats (three two-top tables plus roughly 8 counter seats); additional terrace seating (about 8 tables) that is pet-friendly.
hours_raw: 10:30～18:00（L.O.17:30）; 定休日 月曜日・不定休 (Tabelog lists 11:00–17:30, Tue–Sun)
closed_days: Mondays, plus irregular closures (不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A tourist-friendly cafe in a popular sightseeing area where many visitors are non-locals; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the cake case will get you a warm welcome, and note it is cashless only so bring a card or IC card.

bio.origin: Nara (on the grounds of Akordu, beside Todai-ji)
bio.background: This patisserie and cafe is the long-held dream of the owner-chef of Akordu, a celebrated Spanish restaurant that relocated to the east side of Nara Park near Todai-ji in 2016. After years of envisioning a casual sweets-and-coffee counterpart, the cafe opened in autumn 2023 (September 10, 2023) on the same wooded grounds. The name 'Abarotz' is Basque for the murmuring or rustling of trees, paired with 'kumori' (cloudy), evoking a calm, comforting place that is always there 'like Mount Mikasa.' The space is warm with wood detailing and a sunny yellow wall, a serene retreat from the sightseeing crowds.
bio.specialty: Signature sweets carried over from the restaurant and the chef's time in Spain: the Akordu Tiramisu, made without mascarpone for an unusually light texture (originally the finale of Akordu's tasting menu), and a Basque cheesecake of simple ingredients - cheese, cream and a touch of alcohol - with a deep cheese flavor. Also seasonal montblanc and tarts, lemon sablé cookies, and an original blend hand-drip coffee made with KOTO COFFEE ROASTERS of Gojo, a Japan Coffee Roasting Championship winner.
bio.philosophy: A strong commitment to Nara-grown produce. The chef notes that Nara, though underappreciated for it, offers abundant seasonal fruit - strawberries, figs, grapes, watermelon, pears, persimmons - bought directly from markets and farmers and built into the cakes. Wooden serving plates made by a local Nara artisan are used in the cafe and sold to guests.

anecdotes.text: The Akordu Tiramisu began life as the dessert that closed the restaurant's multi-course meal; here it is offered on its own, made without mascarpone for a notably light texture (660 yen dine-in, 648 yen takeout).
The Basque cheesecake reflects the chef's time in Spain's Basque Country and is sold by the slice (880 yen) or as a whole cake (3,240 yen).
The cafe also collaborates with local Nara craft brewers, offering items such as a MALKO craft beer tie-in with Nara Brewing.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu is flour-based baked goods (Basque cheesecake, tiramisu, montblanc, tarts, lemon sablé), so wheat is present throughout the kitchen; no gluten-free offering documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Desserts are dairy- and egg-based (cheese, cream, butter); no vegan items documented.

positives.text: Ingredient sourcing is transparent: cakes feature Nara-prefecture seasonal fruit bought directly from markets and farmers.

caveats: Hours differ across sources: the official site lists 10:30-18:00 (L.O. 17:30) while Tabelog lists 11:00-17:30; hours_week uses the official site times. Closed Mondays plus irregular closures, so confirm before visiting. Cashless only (no cash). Seat count (~14 indoor + terrace) is from a third-party visit report, not the official site.

name_ja: THE BURGER STAND MAVERICK（マーベリック）
cuisine: グラスフェッドビーフのクラフトハンバーガー（個人店）
neighborhood: 京終〜近鉄奈良駅エリア（奈良女子大学北側・北袋町）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市北袋町32
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市北袋町32 returned [135.829117, 34.689808], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Counter 6 seats + 2 tables (approx. 8 seats). At the previous Fukuchiin-cho location it was 10 seats; the relocated shop is a small counter-and-table space.
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) / 17:00-20:00 (L.O. 19:30); may close early when sold out
closed_days: Tuesdays and the 3rd Wednesday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: An easygoing American-style burger stand with an English menu, so a foreign student can order comfortably; just bring cash since cards are not accepted.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The core product is a burger on a natural-yeast wheat bun (baked in a stone oven), and there is no documented gluten-free bun or accommodation. The signature Luther Burger uses a wheat donut in place of the bun. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A grass-fed beef burger shop; the menu is built around beef patties, bacon, cheese and egg. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
caveats: The original Tabelog listing for the Fukuchiin-cho location (29011907) shows 掲載保留 because the shop relocated; the operating record is the Kitabukuro-cho location, which moved in early March 2025 and is currently open. Hours can vary and the shop may close early once it sells out.

bio.background: A small independent burger stand that began near Fukuchiin Temple in the southern part of Naramachi and in early 2025 relocated to a spot on the north side of Nara Women's University, a short walk from the heart of central Nara. The interior leans full Americana: counter seats and a couple of tables, oldies playing, and American-themed décor on the walls.
bio.origin: Naramachi (Fukuchiin-cho), then relocated to Kitabukuro-cho, Nara City
bio.specialty: Craft hamburgers built on patties made exclusively from grass-fed beef, served on natural-yeast buns slow-baked in a stone oven until the surface turns crisp. Standouts include the Cheese Burger, the Bacon Egg Cheese Burger, and the playful Luther Burger, which swaps the bun for a house-made glazed donut layered with bacon and cheese for a sweet-and-savory bite. Burgers come in a classic style with lettuce and tomato or a bread-and-meat-forward American style, and arrive with a side salad.
bio.philosophy: A commitment to lean, firm grass-fed beef and from-scratch buns, served in an unpretentious, all-American counter setting.

anecdotes.text: The shop's mascot, Maverick-kun, is said to have a hobby of collecting insects.
The Luther Burger sandwiches the patty between a self-made sugar-glazed donut with bacon and cheese, and reviewers describe its sweet-savory balance as addictive.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The Teriyaki Burger uses a Japanese soy-based sauce, which typically contains wheat.

lead: アンヌ・マリーカフェ（ANNE-MARIE CAFE）
name_ja: アンヌ・マリーカフェ
cuisine: 英国風カフェ・喫茶（手作りスコーン、シフォンケーキ、モーニング、キッシュ、オムライス、アフタヌーンティー）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The signature items — handmade scones, chiffon cake, and quiche — are all wheat-based, and the menu lists wheat (小麦) among its allergens alongside eggs and dairy. There is no gluten-free menu and no indication of a dedicated preparation area, so this is not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan friendly. The cafe is built around baked goods and dairy-rich items: scones served with clotted cream, chiffon cake, cakes, quiche, omelette rice, and morning toast. The kitchen flags eggs (卵), dairy (乳), and wheat (小麦) as allergens. No plant-based or vegan options are documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向北町1-10
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市東向北町1-10 returned [135.82869, 34.684891], within the Nara box and consistent with a location 1 minute from Kintetsu Nara Station on the Higashimuki shopping street.
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅前・東向北町（東向商店街）
seats_text: 34 seats (per Tabelog); a small single-room cafe.
hours_raw: Tue-Fri 08:30-18:00 (L.O. 17:30); Sat/Sun/Holidays 09:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30). Morning sets until 10:30, lunch 11:00-14:00, afternoon tea from 14:00. Closed Mondays.
closed_days: Mondays (some sources note occasional irregular closures; holidays follow weekend hours)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, welcoming British-style cafe popular with solo women and female students; a little basic Japanese (or pointing at the menu) is enough, and the calm atmosphere makes it easy for a foreign student to settle in.

bio.background: A long-running British-style cafe tucked just off the Higashimuki shopping arcade, one minute from Kintetsu Nara Station. The room is decorated entirely in English country style — antique furniture, books, and small ornaments — with soft music playing, and the entrance is dressed with flowers and cheerful little decorations.
bio.specialty: Handmade scones served with clotted cream and jam, nut and caramel chiffon cakes, and a multi-tiered afternoon tea set with sandwiches, quiche, pickles, cakes, scones, fruit marinade, and ice cream. Morning sets run until 10:30, and lunch brings quiche, omelette rice, and a British-style curry with salad and soup.
bio.philosophy: An unhurried retreat where guests, especially women on their own, are meant to linger — carefully chosen menus, a calm English-country setting, and soothing music encourage long, restful visits.

anecdotes.text: The scones have a following well beyond Nara — some guests travel from far away specifically to eat them.
The flagship afternoon tea set arrives on a two-tiered stand with sandwiches, quiche, pickles, two kinds of cake, scones with clotted cream and jam, fruit marinade, and ice cream.
The flower-trimmed English-style room draws a steady crowd of female university students.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-centric bakery cafe — scones, chiffon cakes, quiche, and toast are all made with wheat flour, so flour is present throughout the kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core menu relies on eggs and dairy (clotted cream, cakes, quiche, omelette rice), making plant-based eating impractical.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Listed allergens for the food are eggs, dairy, and wheat; no special allergy-handling or gluten-free/vegan accommodation is documented.

cuisine: Chinese home-style cooking from northeastern China (Dongbei); handmade boiled water dumplings (水餃子) and Shenyang-style dishes
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house specialty is boiled water dumplings (水餃子) and pan-grilled dumplings, all wrapped in wheat-flour dough — the core of the menu is wheat-based. Northeastern Chinese cooking also relies heavily on soy sauce (typically wheat-containing) and there is no indication of gluten-free wrappers, dedicated equipment, or celiac awareness. Not suitable for anyone avoiding gluten.
vegan_detail: Largely not vegan. This is a meat- and seafood-forward dumpling izakaya; most fillings contain pork, shrimp, or Chinese chive with pork, and broths/sauces likely use animal products. There are some vegetable-forward water dumplings on the menu — notably a napa/Chinese cabbage dumpling and a green-pepper dumpling described with pork, scallion and ginger — so a strictly plant-only filling is not confirmed for any single item. No vegan menu, no documented handling of plant-based requests; a vegan visitor would need to ask in detail and may find little they can eat.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高天町44-5 1階
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI at chō level (高天町44); within the Nara box. Address per official site and Tabelog is 高天町44-5; one secondary listing gave 44-2, but the building/block geocode is the same.
neighborhood: Kintetsu Nara Station area — a converted nagaya (longhouse) in a back alley near Higashimuki shopping street, on the edge of Naramachi
seats_text: 20 seats total — 4 counter seats and 16 table seats across 5 tables
hours_raw: Wed-Fri 17:00-22:00; Sat/Sun/holidays 11:00-14:00 and 17:00-22:00; closed Mon and Tue (if a holiday falls on a closed day, the following day is closed instead)
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays (holidays excepted; substitute closure the following day)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny owner-run spot where booking ahead by phone or Instagram DM is expected and payment is cash only, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) and some advance planning will make your visit much smoother.

bio.background: Suigyoza W is a small Chinese home-cooking izakaya tucked into a renovated nagaya (traditional longhouse) in a quiet alley a few minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is run by an owner from Shenyang in northeastern China, who brings the dumpling-and-home-cooking traditions of the Dongbei region to Nara. The same little complex is layered with the owner's world: the second floor holds a Chinese imported-goods shop, 友家中華物産, and the building next door operates as a one-group-a-day guesthouse called 肖舎 (syoya).
bio.specialty: Freshly made boiled water dumplings (水餃子) are the heart of the menu, served in small portions of three or six so guests can taste several kinds in one sitting — among them napa-cabbage, green-pepper-and-pork, plain pork, and shrimp-and-chive. The owner's special pan-grilled dumplings stuffed with whole shrimp are a signature, and northeastern dishes such as ladzuji (辣子鶏, spicy fried chicken with chillies) round out the table alongside Chinese drinks.
bio.philosophy: An emphasis on authentic, hand-made dumplings and honest northeastern Chinese home cooking in an intimate, hidden-away setting.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits in a converted longhouse shared with other small eateries, with a Chinese-goods shop upstairs and a tiny one-group guesthouse next door, giving it a hidden-gem, lived-in feel.
Water dumplings come in portions of three or six pieces so diners can sample multiple fillings in a single visit.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The menu is built around wheat-flour dumpling wrappers (boiled and pan-grilled), so flour is central to the kitchen and gluten exposure is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Northeastern Chinese home cooking and dumpling dipping typically use soy sauce, which usually contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable-forward dumplings such as napa cabbage and green pepper are made alongside pork and shrimp fillings on the same equipment; the green-pepper dumpling is itself described with pork, so plant-only items are not confirmed.

caveats: Confirmed real and currently operating via official site, Tabelog, and a local Nara food guide. Address per official site/Tabelog is 高天町44-5 (one secondary listing said 44-2); coordinates are chō-level for the 高天町44 block. Reservations are taken only by phone, SMS, or Instagram DM — not via reservation apps. No documented vegetarian/vegan menu or allergy accommodation; dietary assessments are inferred honestly from the dumpling-based, wheat- and meat-heavy menu rather than from explicit statements.

cuisine: Chicken / grilled chicken (yakiniku-style with Yamato Jidori local chicken)
neighborhood: Omiya-cho, near JR Nara Station (~300m)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市大宮町1-1-21
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市大宮町1-1-21 returned [135.820648, 34.683136], within the Nara bounding box.
seats_text: 32 seats total (counter, tables, and tatami/座敷); private rooms available for groups of roughly 10-20.
closed_days: Not specified in available listings; confirm directly with the restaurant.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual local grill house where you cook chicken at your own tabletop net; some basic Japanese or a translation app will help, as no English service is documented.
vegan_detail: This is a chicken-grilling restaurant built entirely around poultry; there is no documented vegan or vegetarian offering. Not suitable for vegan or vegetarian diners.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. Grilled chicken can be naturally GF, but marinades, dipping sauces (tare) and the shared grill typically involve wheat-based soy sauce and cross-contact, so this is not a celiac-safe destination. A gluten-sensitive diner would need to ask in detail (likely in Japanese).

bio.specialty: Yamato Jidori (大和肉鶏) chicken grilled at the table. Rather than serving skewered yakitori, the restaurant brings cuts of chicken that diners cook themselves on a tabletop net, yakiniku-style.

anecdotes.text: Set in a stand-alone house near JR Nara Station, the dining room has a chic interior with black-lacquered fixtures (黒塗りの建具). Yakitori is not served on skewers here; instead chicken is grilled at your seat on a net like yakiniku.
The kitchen showcases Yamato Jidori, a Nara-bred local chicken developed to revive the reputation of the old 'Yamato Kashiwa' breed, prized for firm texture, deep umami and well-balanced fat.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Chicken is grilled by diners on a shared tabletop net, and dishes use marinades and dipping sauces; standard Japanese tare and soy sauce contain wheat, so cross-contact and gluten exposure are likely. No GF protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built around chicken cooked on a communal grill; there is no documented vegan-safe preparation or separation.

caveats: Closed days are not listed publicly; confirm by phone (0742-23-5611). The "一軒家" (stand-alone house) and Yamato Jidori focus are documented, but there is no published owner/chef story. Diners grill their own chicken at the table.

cuisine: Indian spice curry / South Indian-style set meals (thali / meals)
neighborhood: Kitamachi (Hanashiba-cho, north of the Higashimuki-kita shopping street, ~5 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station)
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program is documented. The menu is built around rice (basmati/jasmine blend) and curries, which is naturally rice-based rather than wheat noodle-based, but side dishes such as pakora (fritters, typically chickpea flour but fried in shared oil) and the use of mixed spice blends mean cross-contact and hidden wheat cannot be ruled out. A celiac traveler would need to ask directly; nothing specific about gluten-free handling has been published.
vegan_detail: Not a vegetarian or vegan restaurant. The set meals include fish and mutton curries among the daily 1-4 curry choices, alongside vegetable-based curries and sides. Some individual vegetable curries, dal, pickles (achaar) and appalam may be plant-based, but ghee/dairy and shared cooking are likely, so a vegan diner should ask which of the day's items are animal-free. No documented dedicated vegan offering.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市花芝町30-3
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 奈良県奈良市花芝町３０番地; coordinates within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 14 seats (12 counter, 1 table of 4)
hours_raw: Tue-Thu 11:30-14:30; Fri-Sat 11:30-14:30 and 17:30-20:30 (evening L.O. around 19:30-20:30). Frequently sells out; early arrival recommended. Sources vary slightly on whether Tuesday or Wednesday is the first open day.
closed_days: Sunday and Monday (plus occasional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 14-seat owner-run counter shop where ordering and any allergy questions will most smoothly be handled in basic Japanese, though the food itself is approachable for newcomers.
caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources (Tabelog lists Wed-Thu lunch only while local guides list Tue-Thu); the shop closes when sold out, so arriving early is wise. Payment is cash or PayPay only (no credit cards). Menu rotates daily and includes fish and mutton, so it is not a vegetarian/vegan or gluten-free destination.

bio.background: Owner-chef Seishi Tamura trained at several Indian restaurants, including the long-established Tagore in Nara, before striking out on his own. To deepen his understanding of authentic Indian cooking he spent three weeks traveling through India, immersing himself in everyday food culture and the way people eat. He opened toi in the quiet Kitamachi area of Nara in February 2021.
bio.specialty: South Indian-style set meals (thali / 'meals') served on a single plate: a choice of one to four daily-changing curries — which can include vegetable, fish and mutton preparations — accompanied by roughly eight small sides such as pakora, achaar pickles and appalam crackers, over a basmati-and-jasmine rice blend. The most popular order lets you take all the curries and mix them together.
bio.philosophy: Tamura aims to cook Indian food 'as naturally as breathing,' blending techniques he absorbed in India with a Japanese sensibility and Nara's seasonal produce. He encourages diners to eat hands-on, mixing the elements on the plate with their fingers — treating the plate, in his words, like a palette you cook on while you eat.

anecdotes.text: Tamura describes the dining style with the line 'the plate becomes a palette; you cook while eating, using your fingers, Indian-style,' and the shop draws Indian regulars alongside Japanese diners, many eating traditionally by hand.
The concept is described as multi-faceted Indian cooking centered on spiced set meals that sync with Nara's climate and ingredients, set in a relaxed local-cafe-style space.

positives.text: The cooking is rice-based (basmati and jasmine blend) South Indian set meals rather than wheat-noodle dishes, and includes naturally plant-based components like dal, vegetable curries and pickles that can suit some diets when chosen carefully.

cuisine: Spice curry (Indian-style: butter chicken, butter prawn, seasonal specials)
neighborhood: Kitamachi (north of central Nara, west of Todai-ji)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西笹鉾町13
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良市西笹鉾町13; within central Nara box.
seats_text: About 13 seats: two 2-person tables, plus one 4-person and one 5-person table (sources vary slightly). Shoes off on entry; solo-operated.
hours_raw: Tue-Sat 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:00), lunch only. Closed Sun & Mon plus occasional irregular closures.
closed_days: Sundays and Mondays, plus occasional irregular days (check Instagram before visiting).
caveats: Credit cards are not accepted; electronic money and QR-code payments (such as d-Pay and au PAY) are accepted, so bring cash to be safe. First-come, first-served with no reservations and limited seats, so expect a possible wait. Lunch only. Confirm irregular closures on the shop's Instagram before going.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual solo-run shop where you remove your shoes; little to no English is documented, so basic Japanese or a translation app and a relaxed, polite manner will make ordering easiest.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and no celiac-safe accommodation is documented. Curries are Indian-style and served with basmati/jasmine rice (naturally wheat-free), but spice blends, sauces and sides are not verified gluten-free and no dedicated handling is mentioned. Celiacs should treat it as unverified and ask directly.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly as documented. The rotating curries are meat- and seafood-based (butter chicken, butter prawn, venison, mutton keema) and the butter-style sauces contain dairy. A lentil (daal) side accompanies plates, but it is part of a non-vegan meal and no standalone vegan curry is confirmed. Vegans cannot be reliably accommodated here.

bio.background: Tsuru Curry is the independent shop of chef Yuji Tsuruno, often described as the last apprentice of Prana, a beloved Nara Indian-curry restaurant that has since closed. He opened his own place in March 2022 inside a renovated traditional house more than 100 years old in the Kitamachi district, an old residential neighborhood west of Todai-ji that has been quietly gaining cafes and small eateries.
bio.specialty: Deeply layered spice curries served on aromatic long-grain rice. The signature is a rich butter chicken (made with Kishu chicken), alongside a butter prawn and rotating specials such as venison curry and a Saturday-only mutton keema; diners can often choose two curries on one plate, rounded out with daal, soup and vegetable sides.
bio.philosophy: Tsuruno chases depth through patient repetition: he slow-roasts over a kilogram of onions in a Dutch oven for about three hours to build a base, then spends another three hours marrying the spices into a single cohesive flavor. He has said his mentor cooked without conventional rules, and that the only way to approach that rich-yet-pure flavor is to faithfully follow his methods.

anecdotes.text: Tsuruno is described as the last disciple of the famed, now-closed Nara curry shop Prana, and built Tsuru Curry to carry that flavor forward.
He slow-cooks more than 1kg of onions in a Dutch oven for roughly three hours as the base for every curry, then spends another three hours blending the spices into one flavor.
In his own words: 'My mentor was someone who cooked without any set theory. To get close to his flavor, which is rich yet pure, all I can do is faithfully follow his methods.'

vegan_cross_contact.text: The curries are meat- and seafood-based and the butter-style sauces contain dairy; no separate vegan preparation is documented, so cross-contact with animal ingredients should be assumed.

lead: Cafe TANNE（カフェ タンネ）
name_ja: カフェ タンネ（Cafe TANNE）
cuisine: Owner-run machiya (townhouse) café serving handmade health-conscious sweets and light meals — Shinshu apple pie, buckwheat (soba) pancakes, sprouted brown rice bread hot sandwiches, and a set-style "Tanne Lunch."
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment. Some menu items lean naturally lower-gluten — buckwheat (soba) pancakes and sprouted brown rice bread — but the café is wheat-using overall (apple pie crust, pastries, pasta), buckwheat pancakes/galettes in Japan are commonly blended with wheat flour, and there is no documented dedicated prep, gluten-free flour sourcing, or cross-contamination control. A celiac diner would need to ask directly about each item; assume shared surfaces and possible wheat in batters.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. The kitchen uses animal products throughout — grilled fish and miso soup in the Japanese morning set, ice cream/whipped cream with pancakes, and butter/eggs/dairy in the apple pie and baked sweets. No documented vegan menu or vegan-specific items. A plant-based diner would need to ask what, if anything, can be prepared without animal products; options are likely very limited.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市下御門町36
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from address 奈良県奈良市下御門町36; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: ならまち・下御門町（近鉄奈良駅 徒歩圏）
seats_text: Approximately 16 seats — a small, intimate townhouse café.
hours_raw: 8:00–18:00 (morning service 8:00–10:30, lunch from 11:00). Closed Thursdays per the local shopping-street listing; some listings note irregular closures and year-round operation, so confirm before a special trip.
closed_days: Thursday (some sources list irregular closures / year-round open — verify ahead)
cultural_comfort_note: A calm, owner-run neighborhood café where a few words of Japanese and a friendly greeting go a long way; English support is not guaranteed, so a translation app helps for asking about ingredients.
caveats: Closed-day and exact closing-time information varies between listings (Thursday closure vs. irregular/year-round; closing 18:00 vs. 18:30) — confirm directly before visiting. Dietary suitability is based on documented menu descriptions, not a stated allergen policy.

bio.background: Cafe TANNE is a townhouse café on the Shimomikado shopping street, on the edge of Nara's historic Naramachi district. Its owner spent about 17 years living in Togakushi in Shinshu (Nagano) before returning to their Nara hometown to open the café. The name 'Tanne' means fir tree and carries the owner's affection for Togakushi, described as a second home.
bio.origin: Nara (owner's hometown), with strong ties to Togakushi/Shinshu, Nagano
bio.specialty: Handmade health-conscious sweets and light meals: a Shinshu apple pie made with Kogyoku (Jonathan) red apples, pancakes made with sprouted brown rice, hot sandwiches on sprouted brown rice bread, and a set-style 'Tanne Lunch.' The sweets menu runs to roughly fifteen items.
bio.philosophy: Gentle, body-friendly cooking built around seasonal ingredients — many sourced from Nagano — served in a warm, relaxed space aimed at adults who want a calm, nourishing meal or coffee break.

anecdotes.text: The owner lived in Togakushi, Shinshu for about 17 years before returning to their Nara hometown to open the café; 'Tanne' means fir tree and expresses their lasting affection for Togakushi as a second home.
Mornings offer a homestyle Japanese set with freshly cooked rice, vegetable miso soup, braised vegetables, grilled fish and pickled plum, alongside Western morning options.

positives.text: Some menu items are built on naturally lower-gluten bases such as buckwheat (soba) pancakes and sprouted brown rice bread, which may give flexible (non-celiac) gluten-avoiders something to work with — confirm ingredients with staff.

cuisine: 個人経営の町家風カフェ。ふわふわフレンチトースト、そば粉100%のガレット、月替りデコカレー（キーマ）、手作りケーキ・カヌレ
gf_detail: The signature galette is made with 100% buckwheat flour (no wheat), which is a naturally gluten-free base. However, this is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen: wheat-based French toast, cakes, and canelé are made in the same small space, so cross-contact is likely. There is no documented allergen handling or celiac-safe protocol, so a sensitive diner should ask directly and treat it as options-only rather than safe for celiac disease.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The core menu relies on eggs, Hokkaido milk, and butter (French toast soaked two days in egg mixture; homemade cheesecake and other dairy sweets), and lunch includes keema curry. No documented vegan dishes or plant-based options. A vegan would find very little to eat here.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1097-2 ならまち工房Ⅱ 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 奈良市高畑町1097 (chō/banchi level); within the Nara box.
neighborhood: ならまち・高畑町（近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約10分）
seats_text: Counter 3 seats plus 2 pair tables (7 total). Counter seats overlooking Mt. Wakakusa are the favorite spot.
hours_raw: 火～日 11:00～18:00（L.O. 17:30）／月曜定休（祝日の場合は翌日）。※一部情報では11:00～17:00 L.O.の記載あり。
closed_days: Mondays (the following day if Monday is a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, gently run owner-operated café where Japanese is the working language and ordering is cash-only, so a few words of Japanese (or a translation app) will make the visit smoother; the owner is known for being warm and approachable.
caveats: Cash only — no cards, e-money, or QR payments. Very small (7 seats) and can fill up; the deco curry rotates monthly. Hours are listed as 11:00–18:00 (L.O. 17:30) on the main listing but as ~11:00–17:00 on some sources, so confirm before a late-afternoon visit.

bio.background: coffret café opened on April 27, 2022, on the second floor of Naramachi Kobo II along Naramachi-dori, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is the realized dream of the owner, Imada-san, who turned a long-held passion for cooking — she had previously shared photos of her character bento (kyaraben) on social media — into a small café of her own.
bio.specialty: Fluffy French toast made with Hokkaido fresh milk and butter and soaked two days in an egg mixture with a hint of rum; a galette made from 100% buckwheat flour seasoned simply with coarse salt and pepper; a cute monthly-changing 'deco' keema curry; and all-homemade sweets including a Basque-style cheesecake, a retro canelé-shaped pudding, and paw-print-decorated canelés.
bio.philosophy: The name 'coffret' means treasure box. Imada-san says she wants it to be 'a café that makes your heart flutter with excitement, like opening a treasure box, every time you come,' and hopes it becomes 'a precious treasure box filled with everyone's favorites and memories.'

anecdotes.text: The name 'coffret' (treasure box): the owner's wish is to make it 'a café where, each time you visit, your heart races with excitement as if opening a treasure box.'
The counter seats look out toward Mt. Wakakusa and become a quiet hidden vantage point during the mountain's annual grass-burning festival (Wakakusa Yamayaki).
The owner is described as approachable and warm, and the small space (3 counter seats plus 2 pair tables) has a gentle, calm atmosphere.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The galette is 100% buckwheat, but wheat-based French toast, cakes, and canelé are prepared in the same small kitchen, so cross-contact with gluten is likely. No celiac-safe protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is built on eggs, milk, and butter (French toast, cheesecake, custard/canelé) plus keema curry, so animal products are pervasive throughout the kitchen.

positives.text: The signature galette uses 100% buckwheat flour with no wheat in the batter, offering a naturally gluten-free base for those who only need options (not strict celiac safety).

lead: ブライトン ティー ルーム（Brighton Tea Room）
name_ja: ブライトン ティー ルーム
cuisine: 英国式紅茶専門店・アフタヌーンティー／英国菓子のカフェ (British tea room and tea specialist serving traditional afternoon tea and British sweets)
neighborhood: ならまち・東城戸町（近鉄奈良駅 徒歩圏）
gf_detail: This is a British tea room whose menu centers on wheat-based baked goods — scones, Victoria sponge cake, chocolate pavlova, and finger sandwiches are all made with wheat flour or bread. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or allergen accommodation is documented on the official site or Tabelog. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A traditional British afternoon tea offering: scones, sponge cake, pavlova and sandwiches typically rely on dairy (milk, cream, butter), eggs, and meat/fish in sandwiches. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented on the official site or Tabelog. Travelers requiring vegan food should not expect accommodation here.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東城戸町35番地 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市東城戸町35 returned [135.827255, 34.679142], within the Nara box and consistent with the ならまち / Kintetsu Nara Station area.
seats_text: Exact seat count not published. Described as a spacious second-floor room with counter seating; suitable for solo diners and small groups. A 2-hour seating limit applies.
hours_raw: 11:00-18:30 (L.O. 18:00). 定休日: 月・火曜（祝日は営業）。(Official site; Tabelog lists 11:30-19:00 / L.O.18:30.)
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays (open on public holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: The owner spent over two decades living in Brighton, England, so English is understood here and the British tea-room setting feels familiar and welcoming to foreign visitors.
caveats: Afternoon tea set requires reservation by email at least 3 days in advance (name, date/time, party size, mobile number); same-day cancellations are charged in full. Walk-ins are welcome for the regular menu but seats cannot be reserved separately. No credit cards — cash or QR/e-money (PayPay, LINE Pay, Merpay, d-pay) only. Second-floor location; a 2-hour time limit applies. Note: official site and Tabelog list slightly different hours; the official site (11:00-18:30) is used here.

bio.background: Brighton Tea Room opened in the spring of 2015 on a quiet lane near Nara's historic Naramachi district. Its founder lived in Brighton, England for more than twenty years before returning to introduce authentic British tea culture to Japan, and continues to travel between Brighton and Nara. The room occupies the second floor of a retro building, lined with tea tins, cups and saucers; the tea leaves, china, pots and tableware are sourced directly from England.
bio.specialty: Traditional British afternoon tea served with pot tea — the set features fruit trifle, chocolate pavlova, Victoria sponge cake, two kinds of sandwiches, and scones. Individual British cakes such as carrot cake, scones, and masala chai are also served.
bio.philosophy: The owner believes that for British people tea is far more than a drink — it is a way to communicate, to show care, and to lift the spirits, describing the simple offer of 'Have a cuppa?' as a kind of magic phrase. That spirit of unhurried hospitality is at the heart of the room.

anecdotes.text: The founder lived in Brighton, England for over 22 years before opening the tea room, and shuttles between Brighton and Nara to bring authentic British sweets and tea to guests.
Tea leaves, cups, plates and pots are all imported directly from England to recreate a genuine British tea-room experience.
A larger second branch, Brighton Tea Room Cottage, was later opened in Sakurai, Nara for more spacious afternoon tea.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen produces wheat-based scones, sponge cakes and bread sandwiches; no separate gluten-free preparation is documented, so cross-contamination from flour is likely.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Baked goods are made with dairy and eggs and sandwiches contain meat/fish, so vegan cross-contact is inherent and no vegan options are documented.

cuisine: Bakery (artisan bread, croissants, hard-crust loaves, sandwiches)
neighborhood: Naramachi / Kitakoji-cho (near Jigenji Temple, off Yasuragi Road)
gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery — every product is made from domestic wheat and self-milled rye flour. There is no gluten-free option and it is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A butter-forward bakery (Yotsuba butter features heavily, plus egg tarts and dairy pastries), so most items are not vegan. Some hard-crust loaves like the campagne may be made from just flour, water, salt and rye, but vegan status of any specific loaf is not documented and would need to be confirmed in person.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市北小路町4-13
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良市北小路町4 returned [135.825714, 34.687668], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Primarily takeout; no indoor dining counter documented. A flowerbed bench in front of the shop lets customers eat their purchases on the spot.
hours_raw: Thursday–Sunday 09:30–15:00 (closes earlier when sold out / なくなり次第終了); closed Monday–Wednesday.
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday (and earlier closure once sold out)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny neighborhood bakery where you point at what you want, pay and go — a smile and a few words of Japanese are all you need, and arriving in the morning is wise since popular loaves sell out.
caveats: Open only Thursday to Sunday and closes early once bread sells out, so go in the morning. Largely takeout with no real seating beyond a bench outside. All products contain wheat — not suitable for celiacs or strict gluten-avoiders.

bio.background: panc is a small neighborhood bakery run by a husband-and-wife team, tucked beside Jigenji Temple along the quiet Yasuragi Road, a short walk north of central Nara. It opened in July 2017 and has become a quietly beloved local stop.
bio.specialty: Hard-crust loaves and viennoiserie: a signature campagne with a moist, chewy crumb made from domestic wheat and self-milled organic rye, a 100% Hokkaido 'Yumechikara' wheat loaf, butter-rich croissants, bagels, vegetable-packed sandwiches, cinnamon and cardamom rolls, egg tarts and lemon cakes. Around twenty kinds of bread are set out each day.
bio.philosophy: Simple, honest ingredients chosen with care — domestic wheat, organic rye milled in-house, and Yotsuba butter — letting the fragrance of the flour and a well-judged saltiness carry each loaf.

anecdotes.text: The little flowerbed in front of the shop doubles as a bench, so you can sit and eat what you just bought right there on the street.
Regulars are told to come in the morning, because the most popular loaves sell out and the shop closes early once the bread is gone.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Entirely a wheat-flour bakery (domestic wheat plus self-milled rye), so flour is everywhere and there is no gluten-free product or separation.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Heavy use of Yotsuba butter, plus egg tarts and dairy pastries, means animal products are present throughout the kitchen.

positives.text: Ingredient labels are simple and on display — one customer photographed a loaf's short, plain ingredient list.

cuisine: Bakery (hard-crust breads, filled breads, signature cream pan)
neighborhood: Horen-cho, Nara (near Ichijo High School, north of Shin-Omiya and Kintetsu Nara stations)
gf_detail: This is a wheat-flour bakery. Bread is the core product (hard-crust loaves, filled breads, bagels, sandwiches), so it is not suitable for gluten-free or celiac diners. No gluten-free items are documented.
vegan_detail: A traditional bakery whose best-known items contain dairy and egg (the signature cream pan "Kobako" is filled with cold custard cream; other items include curry bread and sandwiches). Plain hard-crust breads may be egg/dairy-free but this is not confirmed; a vegan diner would need to ask about specific loaves. No explicitly vegan items are documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町417-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 奈良県奈良市法蓮町417 via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Takeout only; no seating.
hours_raw: 8:00頃～売り切れ次第終了（営業時間・在庫はSNS等で告知）。定休日：水曜、火曜は不定休。
closed_days: Wednesdays; Tuesdays irregular
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny neighborhood takeout bakery where ordering is simple point-and-pay; a friendly greeting in Japanese is plenty, though English is unlikely and items sell out by early afternoon.
caveats: Takeout only with no seating. Bread is made fresh in limited quantity and often sells out by early afternoon (sometimes shortly after opening), so go early. Hours end "when sold out" rather than at a fixed time, and Tuesday closures are irregular, so check the shop's Instagram or blog before visiting. The signature cream pan "Kobako" has its cold cream piped in at purchase and is meant to be eaten right away.

bio.background: A small, independent neighborhood bakery tucked into a quiet residential block of Horen-cho in northern Nara, near Ichijo High School and a short walk from Shin-Omiya Station. The plain, unassuming storefront barely looks like a bakery from outside, but inside it is packed with around thirty kinds of bread laid out invitingly each day.
bio.specialty: Hard-crust, dough-focused breads alongside generously filled breads. The signature item is a cream pan called 'Kobako' (こはく。のこばこ): the cold custard cream is piped in fresh at the moment of purchase, and it is meant to be eaten right away while the cream is still cold. Other beloved items include a local Yamato-beef curry bread and a sweet-potato cream Danish.
bio.philosophy: The shop's own writing stresses making bread 'humbly and earnestly' (謙虚にそして真摯に) out of genuine love for the bread itself.

anecdotes.text: The bread is made in limited daily quantity and is famous for selling out fast, sometimes by just after midday, so regulars line up early.
The signature 'Kobako' cream pan has its cold cream added after you order, and is recommended to be eaten immediately while cold.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A working wheat-flour bakery; flour is used throughout, making cross-contamination unavoidable for gluten-free diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature and popular items contain dairy and egg (custard cream pan, curry bread, sandwiches); shared bakery equipment means egg/dairy contact is likely across products.

lead: cafe komorebi（カフェ こもれ陽）
name_ja: café komorebi（カフェ こもれ陽）
cuisine: グルテンフリー・ヴィーガン対応の町家ナチュラルカフェ（米粉フォカッチャ、二十四節気の季節野菜サラダ）
gf_detail: The cafe was designed by its owner specifically to accommodate gluten sensitivity, and gluten-free options run throughout the menu. The signature seasonal-vegetable salad set comes with a rice-flour focaccia made from Hinohikari rice flour (no wheat), and the house dressing is made with gluten-free soy sauce rather than regular (wheat-containing) soy sauce. This is one of the rare Nara spots where wheat-avoidance is a core design principle rather than an afterthought. Celiac travelers should still confirm shared-equipment handling directly, as no dedicated-fryer/dedicated-prep detail is documented.
vegan_detail: Vegan and vegetarian options are explicitly offered, with the kitchen also accommodating egg-free, dairy-free, and nut-free needs. The seasonal vegetable salad highlights 10-15 vegetables (some house-grown, others from Nara and southern Kyoto), and desserts include a plant-based gelato (soy-milk and coconut-milk base) from MINORI Workshop in Ide, Kyoto. Note the salad dressing contains honey, so strict vegans should request a substitution; confirm which specific dishes are fully plant-based when ordering.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西紀寺町27
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市西紀寺町27番地; within Nara box.
neighborhood: ならまち南部・西紀寺町（JR京終駅 徒歩約9分）
seats_text: Small townhouse layout: roughly 3 counter seats inside plus 2 counter seats to the side, three 2-person tables, and one 4-person table (about 9 seats at the counters/small tables, plus table seating). Compact and intimate.
hours_raw: 11:00-18:00; 定休日 火曜日・隔週水曜日
closed_days: Tuesdays and alternate (every other) Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming owner-run natural cafe built around dietary inclusivity, so a few words of Japanese plus pointing at the menu will get you a warm reception, even if full English service isn't guaranteed.
caveats: Address differs slightly from the briefing (actual: 西紀寺町 Nishikiteramachi, not 西木辻町); the alternate-Wednesday closures make the weekly schedule irregular, so check the Instagram before visiting. Pets are allowed only from 13:30 and only at back tables. The salad dressing contains honey.

bio.background: café komorebi sits in the quiet southern stretch of Naramachi, an old townhouse district, about a nine-minute walk from JR Kyobate Station. The owner built the cafe around a single idea: a place where everyone can freely enjoy a meal, regardless of allergies, dietary restrictions, or beliefs.
bio.specialty: The signature is a seasonal-vegetable salad set served with a chewy, faintly sweet focaccia made from Hinohikari rice flour and a soup. The plate carries 10-15 vegetables at a time, some grown in the cafe's own garden and others sourced from Nara and southern Kyoto, dressed in a house-made steamed-red-onion dressing. Desserts include a seasonal anmitsu built on a plant-based roasted-brown-rice gelato.
bio.philosophy: The menu follows the nijushi sekki, the twenty-four micro-seasons of the traditional calendar, with ingredients rotating to track nature's cycles. The cooking is designed from the ground up to be gluten-free, and to accommodate egg-free, dairy-free, nut-free, vegan, vegetarian, halal, and kosher diners.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's name means sunlight filtering through leaves, and the wooden, high-ceilinged room with skylights is arranged to recreate that dappled, forest-like light; the owner mixed in vintage Ercol furniture from the UK with driftwood and foraged botanicals.

Pets are welcomed only from 13:30 and only at the back tables, in carriers or strollers, a careful arrangement the owner set up to balance hospitality against their own dog allergy through strict cleaning and ventilation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The house salad dressing is made with gluten-free soy sauce rather than standard wheat-containing soy sauce.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The owner designed the cafe specifically to serve people with gluten sensitivity, egg allergies, dairy restrictions, and religious dietary needs, with the stated aim that everyone can eat freely.

positives.text: Gluten-free options run throughout the menu; the signature focaccia uses Hinohikari rice flour instead of wheat.
Vegan, vegetarian, nut-free, and dairy-free choices are available, and plant-based gelato is used in desserts.

lead: 喫茶と軽食 佐保（さほ）
cuisine: Showa-retro kissaten serving cheap home-style set meals, fried rice, spaghetti, pilaf, sandwiches and coffee, built around the owner's homegrown vegetables and fruit
gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese coffee shop serving wheat- and meat-heavy comfort food — sandwiches, spaghetti, pilaf, fried rice, tonkatsu and other set meals. No gluten-free menu, no allergen handling, and no dedicated fryer are documented. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Although the kitchen prides itself on the owner's homegrown vegetables and fruit, the menu centers on meat and egg dishes (tonkatsu, grilled-pork fried rice, sandwiches with boiled egg) and the savory dishes use standard Japanese broth/seasonings. No vegan dishes are documented and no plant-based accommodation is mentioned.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町462-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良市法蓮町462-1; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Small space: roughly one single-seat table plus about ten two-person tables (around 21 seats), with a living-room feel; parking available.
hours_raw: 8:00～17:00（モーニングは11:30まで、ランチは11:30から。売り切れ次第終了）日曜定休
closed_days: Sundays
neighborhood: Horen-cho, along Route 24 near Shin-Omiya Station (about 2 miles north of Kintetsu Nara Station), across from Ichijo High School
cultural_comfort_note: A homey neighborhood coffee shop full of regulars where little to no English is likely, so a few basic Japanese phrases and a relaxed, friendly attitude will go a long way.

bio.background: Saho has been quietly serving its corner of Nara for 42 years, tucked along Route 24 just east of Ichijo High School in the Horen-cho neighborhood. It is a true Showa-era kissaten frozen in time, with manga and magazines on the shelves and an atmosphere regulars describe as walking into a friend's living room.
bio.specialty: Generous, almost unbelievably cheap home cooking: morning sets, eight kinds of set meals such as tonkatsu and grilled-pork fried rice, plus pilaf, spaghetti and sandwiches — all anchored by produce the owner grows by hand.
bio.philosophy: The owner cultivates pesticide-free vegetables and fruit in the back garden, and that home harvest is the open secret behind prices that have barely budged in four decades.

anecdotes.text: The shop has run for 42 years, with set meals at just 600 to 700 yen and every drink 350 yen, kept affordable by the owner's own backyard pesticide-free vegetables and fruit.
It functions as a neighborhood living room: solo guests settle in to read manga while groups linger over conversation, and many regulars come daily or several times a week.

caveats: Address differs slightly across sources (Tabelog/Narakko list 法蓮町462-1, while an Instagram post lists 法蓮町457); 462-1 used for geocoding. Seat count is estimated from review descriptions (about one single + ten two-tops); no official seat figure published. Lunch ends when sold out, so afternoon availability is not guaranteed. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is documented anywhere.

cuisine: Independent neighborhood bakery
gf_detail: The bakery offers a small line of 100% rice flour (米粉) gluten-free bread alongside its main wheat range. However, this is a wheat-flour bakery at its core: nearly everything sold is made with Hokkaido wheat, and the rice flour bread is baked in the same kitchen. No information is published about a dedicated gluten-free production area or measures against flour cross-contamination, so a celiac traveler should treat it as a shared-flour environment and ask directly before buying.
vegan_detail: No vegan labeling is published. The breads are naturally leavened and additive-free, but signature items like the salted butter rolls and melon pan use dairy and likely egg, and the GF rice flour bread's ingredients are not documented. There may be some incidentally plant-based loaves, but a vegan visitor would need to ask which specific breads contain no butter, milk, or egg.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市四条大路4-1-77-1
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.792938, 34.681793] for 奈良市四条大路4丁目1番77号; within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: Shijo-Oji, Nara City (along Shijo-Oji Avenue facing the Suzakumon Gate of Heijo Palace Park; ~860m from Amagatsuji Station)
seats_text: Takeaway bakery; no eat-in seating documented.
hours_raw: Tue–Fri 8:30–19:00; Sat & public holidays 8:00–19:00; closes early when sold out. Closed Sunday and Monday.
closed_days: Sunday and Monday (and early close when sold out)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a local Japanese neighborhood bakery with little to no English signage, but ordering is simple point-and-pay at the counter, so basic Japanese phrases or gestures are enough.
caveats: Bakes in small batches and closes once sold out, so popular items (especially melon pan) may be gone later in the day; arrive earlier for the fullest selection.

bio.background: A small, independently run neighborhood bakery on Shijo-Oji Avenue in central Nara, with a storefront that looks out toward the reconstructed Suzakumon Gate of the World Heritage Heijo Palace site. The shop has built its reputation by word of mouth among locals.
bio.specialty: Melon pan is the signature draw, baked with a crisp cookie shell over a fluffy interior and rotated through flavors such as plain, Earl Grey, raspberry, cinnamon, and matcha; other favorites include salted butter rolls and curry pan. Roughly 30 kinds of bread are out at any time.
bio.philosophy: The maker's stated aim is simply to always deliver fluffy, freshly-baked bread, so loaves are produced in small batches throughout the day rather than mass-baked, and the dough is kept additive-free using 100% domestic Hokkaido wheat with no emulsifiers, yeast food, or dough improvers.

anecdotes.text: The melon pan became the shop's signature item largely through customer word-of-mouth, with several varieties kept available daily.
Alongside its wheat range, the bakery makes a 100% rice flour gluten-free bread, an unusual offering for a small neighborhood pan-ya.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is primarily a wheat-flour bakery; the gluten-free rice flour bread is baked in the same kitchen as wheat loaves, and no dedicated GF area or anti-cross-contamination measures are published. Treat as high cross-contamination risk for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature breads use butter and milk (e.g., salted butter rolls, melon pan); vegan status of individual loaves is not documented, so dairy/egg cross-contact should be assumed unless confirmed in store.

positives.text: Uses 100% domestic Hokkaido wheat with no synthetic additives (no emulsifiers, yeast food, or dough improvers).
Offers a 100% rice flour gluten-free bread option in addition to its standard range.

lead: 喫茶 優香里（ユーカリ）
cuisine: Showa-era retro kissaten (Japanese coffee shop): hand-drip coffee, homemade parfaits, morning sets
neighborhood: 富雄 (Tomio), western Nara City — Kintetsu Nara line, by Tomio Station west exit
gf_detail: This is a traditional Showa-era coffee shop whose food menu centers on toast, sandwiches, cakes and parfaits — all wheat-based. There is no gluten-free offering and no indication of celiac-safe handling. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: A classic kissaten focused on coffee, with morning sets (toast, eggs, salad), sandwiches, cakes, and dairy-based parfaits (homemade mocha ice cream, soft-serve). Dishes rely on eggs and dairy; no documented vegan options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市富雄元町2-3-27
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [lng 135.734207, lat 34.693916] for 奈良県奈良市富雄元町2-3-27. Latitude is within the Nara box (34.64–34.74) but longitude (135.734) falls just west of the box minimum (135.76), because the shop sits in Tomio in western Nara City. Coordinates set to null per the box rule; the geocoded point is accurate and located by Tomio Station west exit.
seats_text: 28 seats (12 counter, 16 table)
hours_raw: 7:00–17:00; morning service 7:00–11:00; closed Thursdays
closed_days: Thursdays
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, old-school neighborhood coffee shop where Japanese is the working language and English is unlikely, so come ready to point at the menu and enjoy the early-morning local atmosphere.
caveats: Cash only. Opens early (7:00) and closes mid-afternoon (17:00); closed Thursdays. Right by Tomio Station west exit; nearby parking is paid only.

bio.background: Opened in 1981, Kissa Yukari has poured coffee for the Tomio neighborhood of western Nara for more than 40 years. Run by the Hayashi family across generations, it keeps a Showa-era look alive while staying spotless — the walls have been repainted and the sofas reupholstered over the years, so the nostalgia comes without the wear.
bio.specialty: The signature is the Yukari Parfait (around 850 yen), built on the shop's own soft-serve, homemade mocha ice cream and coffee jelly. Coffee is brewed by hand drip one cup at a time, including the original Yukari Blend; early risers come for the morning sets (toast, eggs, salad) served until 11:00 at little more than the price of a drink.
bio.philosophy: Hand-drip, one cup at a time, with the same care since the shop opened in 1981.

anecdotes.text: The shop has run for over 40 years since 1981 and is tended by a Hayashi-family master described as approachable and chatty with regulars; its crowd mixes seniors, office workers, homemakers and students.
Coffee is hand-dripped one cup at a time to the same standard as at the founding, and the popular Yukari Parfait layers homemade mocha ice cream with coffee jelly.
