cuisine: Book café serving original South Indian / Sri Lankan-style spice curry in a 100-year-old machiya townhouse
neighborhood: きたまち (Kitamachi), 東笹鉾町
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東笹鉾町41
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市東笹鉾町41; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 14 seats (6 table seats plus an 8-seat tatami area).
hours_raw: Tue–Sat 11:30–17:30 (L.O. 17:00); closed Sun & Mon. (One local listing notes lunch service roughly 11:30–14:30.)
closed_days: Sunday and Monday
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, owner-run book café where ordering is simple (a short curry menu) but signage and conversation are mostly in Japanese, so a few basic phrases help.
gf_detail: The curries are South Indian / Sri Lankan-style and served with turmeric/saffron rice, so the rice plates may be naturally lower in wheat, but no gluten-free program is documented and spice blends, dal and side dishes have not been confirmed wheat-free. Anyone with celiac disease should ask directly before ordering.
vegan_detail: The "気まぐれカリィ" (whimsical curry) and several seasonal sides are vegetable- and legume-based (e.g., green-lentil keema, winter-melon dal, Bengal-style cabbage curry), and the owner's apple cake is made with rice oil instead of dairy. However, the signature chicken and keema curries contain meat, and no dish is confirmed strictly vegan (stocks/ghee not documented), so vegans should confirm which preparations are plant-based on the day.

bio.background: Mijinko Bunko opened in 2013 inside a roughly 100-year-old machiya townhouse in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district, a short walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner, Hitomi Shuji, came to the work by an unusual route: he had been a biology researcher before turning to his loves of books and architecture, studying at a building school and apprenticing under an architect, then renovating the old house himself into a café.
bio.specialty: Original spice curry drawing on South Indian and Sri Lankan traditions, blended from around 30 spices that the owner taught himself over many years. The regular line-up is a spicy chicken curry, a milder keema, and a medium-heat 'kimagure' (whimsical) curry that changes with his mood and the season; plates come with turmeric/saffron rice and seasonal vegetable and legume sides such as dal. His wife bakes the sweets, including a popular apple cake made with rice oil and fresh apple.
bio.philosophy: Books, more than 3,000 of them spanning children's literature, architecture, food, daily life, plants and microbes, are arranged at random rather than by category, so that 'wherever you sit, there's a discovery to be had,' and guests are welcome to linger over a single cup of coffee as if relaxing at home.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name nods to the owner's past as a biology researcher; 'mijinko' is the Japanese word for the water flea (Daphnia), and the books even reach into subjects like microbes.
The owner spent more than two decades teaching himself to blend roughly 30 spices for the curries.

positives.text: The apple cake is made with rice oil in place of dairy, so it is suitable for those avoiding milk and butter.
Several seasonal sides and the rotating 'whimsical' curry are built around vegetables and legumes such as lentils, dal and cabbage.

caveats: Tabelog and the official site list Tue–Sat 11:30–17:30 (L.O. 17:00); one local listing shows a narrower lunch window of about 11:30–14:30, so confirm closing time before a late visit. Curry prices were not consistently documented; the seasonal "Mijinko Plate" was cited at around 1,000 yen. Payment methods and reservation policy are not explicitly documented; phone 0742-24-8231.

lead: お台所＆サロン かたつむり
name_ja: お台所＆サロン かたつむり
cuisine: ギャラリーカフェ（戦前の古民家を活かした手作りランチと手作りスイーツ）
neighborhood: きたまち / 法蓮町
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a handmade home-cooking cafe serving a single daily lunch set; dishes such as miso-stewed daikon and braised pork, plus baked sweets, would typically involve wheat-based miso/soy seasonings and flour. Celiac travelers should not assume safety and would need to ask in advance.
vegan_detail: Vegetable-forward, health-conscious home cooking with some plant-based touches (the kitchen makes its own vegan mayonnaise, and dishes like vegetable namul and pumpkin salad appear), but it is not a vegan restaurant — the set lunch includes meat such as braised pork. A vegan meal is not guaranteed; ask ahead (best via Instagram or phone) about a plant-based option for the day.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町1269
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned a building-level match for 奈良県奈良市法蓮町1269, within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 12 seats — roughly five two-person tables on the first floor plus additional seating on the second floor of the converted house.
hours_raw: Sat–Sun 11:00–18:00 (lunch from 11:00). Open mainly on weekends; the schedule is irregular and can change for events, so confirm on Instagram before visiting.
closed_days: Weekdays (Monday–Friday); irregular weekend openings, confirm via Instagram
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate Japanese-language home cafe where you remove your shoes at the entrance; a foreign student comfortable with basic Japanese (or a translation app) will get the most out of it, and it helps to message ahead since hours are irregular.
caveats: Open only on weekends with an irregular, event-dependent schedule — always confirm the day's opening on the cafe's Instagram before going. Cash only, no parking, reached on foot through narrow streets about 15 minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station. The lunch is a single daily set, not an à la carte menu, so dietary needs should be raised when reserving.

bio.background: Set inside a renovated pre-war wooden townhouse in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district, this combined kitchen-and-salon cafe reopened in June 2023 under a new owner, taking over a space that had long been known by the same Katatsumuri ("snail") name as a gallery cafe. You leave your shoes at the door and settle in among art on the ground and upper floors of the old house.
bio.specialty: A single handmade daily lunch set of vegetable-forward home cooking — think dishes like miso-stewed daikon, braised pork, vegetable namul and pumpkin salad — with an optional dessert-and-drink add-on of seasonal baked sweets.
bio.philosophy: The owner, who came from a healthcare background, opened the cafe to rethink the connection between food and wellbeing, cooking with care for body and mind and even making house staples like vegan mayonnaise from scratch.

anecdotes.text: The owner reopened the long-standing 'snail' cafe in June 2023 after a career in healthcare, wanting to create a place where eating well supports both physical and mental health.
It keeps the unhurried, homey feel of an old private house, with guests removing their shoes and sitting at a handful of small tables across two floors.

positives.text: Kitchen makes its own vegan mayonnaise and leans toward vegetable-forward, health-conscious cooking.

cuisine: Tea house / café in a converted Edo-period temple building, serving spice-driven dishes (Yamato-citrus red chicken curry), Japanese sweets, and afternoon tea
neighborhood: 登大路町 (Noborioji-cho), near Kōfuku-ji and Nara Park, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市登大路町63
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市登大路町63 returned [135.835175, 34.685059], within the Nara box. Note: the target brief listed area as きたまち/北袋町, but every source (Tabelog, official site, tourism listings) gives the address as 登大路町63, in the Nara Park / Kōfuku-ji area. The building is the former Kōfuku-ji sub-temple 世尊院, consistent across sources.
seats_text: 43 seats
closed_days: None listed (open year-round)
cultural_comfort_note: A calm, polished tea-house café in a historic temple building where a few words of Japanese and basic café etiquette go a long way; staff are used to visitors but menus and conversation lean Japanese, and the afternoon-tea sets sit at a higher price point.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a café serving curry with rice, wheat-noodle nyumen (sōmen), Japanese sweets and afternoon-tea items; soy sauce, wheat and dashi are likely used throughout, and no dedicated-GF or cross-contamination information was found. Not suitable for celiacs without direct confirmation in advance.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian destination. Signature dishes are meat- and fish-based (red chicken curry, Yamato-beef nyumen, seared mackerel sushi), and most items likely use fish dashi, dairy or honey. Some sweets and tea may be plant-based but nothing is documented as vegan; ask staff directly before relying on any option.
caveats: The brief's area label (きたまち/北袋町) does not match the verified address; all sources place it at 登大路町63 near Nara Park / Kōfuku-ji. Prices are on the higher side (curry ~4,800 yen, afternoon tea ~4,400 yen). No allergy/vegan/GF accommodation is documented online.

bio.background: 茶寮 世世 occupies the former guest hall of Sesonin (世尊院), a sub-temple of Kōfuku-ji built more than 200 years ago in the late Edo period. The structure was renovated into a tea house that deliberately preserves the beauty of its temple architecture, opening as a café in 2023 with garden views that change with the seasons. The name 世世 derives from Sesonin and evokes the idea of tradition and culture being inherited and reborn from one generation to the next.
bio.origin: Nara, in the former Kōfuku-ji sub-temple Sesonin near Nara Park
bio.specialty: The signature 'Zeze no Spice' red chicken curry blends seven to eight spices in an original mix, evoking Nara's role as a terminus of the Silk Road, with Yamato citrus (大和橘) as a hidden flavor and a Nankō plum served alongside to soften the heat. The kitchen also offers a small afternoon-tea set with free-flow tea, a yin-yang five-elements afternoon tea supervised by a Chinese-medicine wellness instructor, and Japanese sweets such as purple-sweet-potato kuzu mochi and anmitsu made with Yamato soybeans.
bio.philosophy: To offer refined, contemplative moments that let guests taste and feel Nara's history, pairing Silk Road spice traditions with authentic Japanese cuisine and local Nara ingredients (Yamato tea, Tsukigase mint, Yamato honey, Yamato soybeans) in a restored temple setting.

anecdotes.text: The building was the guest hall of Sesonin, a Kōfuku-ji sub-temple built in the late Edo period over 200 years ago, renovated into a café while keeping its temple architecture intact.
The signature red chicken curry uses an original blend of seven to eight spices with Yamato citrus as a hidden flavor, served with a Nankō plum that softens and shifts the heat when eaten together.

lead: ならまちテラス食堂 蜜や
name_ja: ならまちテラス食堂 蜜や
cuisine: Japanese teishoku/shokudo
neighborhood: Naramachi (Higashiterabayashi-cho)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東寺林町5
geocode_note: GSI address search returned a single feature within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 22 seats total (16 terrace, 6 counter)
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00; closed Wednesday and Thursday (some items subject to daily availability)
closed_days: Wednesday, Thursday
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locally run lunch-only diner where Japanese will be the norm — a confident traveler can order by pointing at menu photos, but expect little to no English.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The kitchen uses Igeta (wheat-based) soy sauce in its house-made sauces, and the menu centers on breaded/fried dishes (fried chicken, tonkatsu/tonteki, hamburger) and noodle dishes, all of which involve wheat. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. This is a meat-forward teishoku diner built around Yamato pork, Yamato beef, chicken and shrimp; sauces use soy sauce and butter. No vegan menu or plant-based dishes are documented.
caveats: Lunch-only (11:30-15:00), closed Wed/Thu, cash only, no reservations. Some menu items are limited by daily availability. Owner/chef name and founding history are not documented in available sources.

bio.background: A cozy, cafe-style lunch diner tucked into the Naramachi district just south of Kintetsu Nara Station, with both indoor counter seats and a relaxed terrace. It has become one of central Nara's most beloved spots for a hearty set lunch, regularly topping local rankings of favorite teishoku eateries in the area.
bio.specialty: The signature plate is the Yamato pork tonteki set — thick yet tender pork steak in a house-made sauce of grated onion, garlic and butter blended with locally sourced Igeta soy sauce, served with rice, miso soup and a fresh-vegetable-and-potato salad. Other favorites include the house salt-koji fried chicken set with a sweet-vinegar glaze, chicken cutlet curry, and a Yamato beef hamburger with shrimp.
bio.philosophy: An emphasis on careful from-scratch cooking using local Nara ingredients — even the salad dressing is made in-house — paired with a warm, unhurried cafe atmosphere.

anecdotes.text: Known for generous portions — the fried chicken set comes with five or six pieces — the shop is repeatedly ranked the single most popular teishoku spot in Nara Prefecture in local rankings of diner favorites.
The tonteki uses Yamato pork in a butter-and-garlic sauce built on locally produced Igeta soy sauce, and even the salad dressing is house-made.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by breaded and deep-fried items (fried chicken, pork/chicken cutlets) cooked in shared oil, making cross-contamination with wheat highly likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: House-made sauces are built on Igeta soy sauce, a standard wheat-fermented Japanese soy sauce.

vegan_cross_contact.text: An animal-centric kitchen using pork, beef, chicken, shrimp, butter and soy sauce; shared cooking surfaces and oil make avoiding animal products impractical.

lead: NORR COFFEE ROASTERS（ノール コーヒー ロースターズ）
name_ja: ノール コーヒー ロースターズ
cuisine: Specialty coffee roastery and gallery cafe (self-roasted single-origin coffee, homemade-style baked sweets)
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering documented. The food menu centers on wheat-based baked goods such as financiers, pound cake, and Breton galettes, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners. No allergen handling or gluten-free items are mentioned on any source.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. As a coffee shop the black filter coffees and coffee tonic soda are plant-based, but milk-based drinks (cafe latte) and the baked sweets (financier, pound cake, galette) typically contain dairy and eggs. A vegan visitor could likely have a black coffee but should ask about everything else; no plant-milk options are confirmed.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市北半田中町16-2
geocode_note: Chō-level geocode via GSI for 北半田中町; falls within the central Nara box (lat 34.688, lng 135.832), about an 8-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station in the Kitamachi area.
neighborhood: きたまち（北半田中町）
seats_text: Small space: about 15 seats total — three single seats near the entrance, table seating on a mezzanine upstairs, and a semi-underground terrace facing a courtyard.
hours_raw: Sundays only, approx. 13:00-18:00 (last order around 16:45). The owner works on weekdays, so the shop opens only on Sundays; an "öppet" (Swedish for "open") sign is displayed when trading. Note: an older listing shows weekday 11:00-16:30 and Sunday 11:00-17:00 hours, which appears to be outdated. Confirm via the shop's social media before visiting.
closed_days: Monday through Saturday (open Sundays only)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, relaxed Nordic-themed coffee shop where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you by, though staff may not speak much English and the Sunday-only hours mean you should check before making the trip.
caveats: Opening hours differ between sources: recent visitor write-ups and the owner's own description say Sundays only (~13:00-18:00) because the proprietor has a weekday job, while an older directory listing shows weekday hours. Treat it as Sunday-only and verify the current schedule on the shop's Instagram (@norrcoffeeroasters) before going. Parking is not available.

bio.background: NORR Coffee Roasters is a small self-roasting specialty coffee shop tucked into the quiet Kitamachi residential area in northern central Nara, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner keeps a weekday job and opens the shop only on Sundays, hanging out an 'öppet' sign — Swedish for 'open' — when trading. The name NORR means 'north' in Swedish, a nod to the shop's location in the northern Kitamachi district and its Nordic-inflected sensibility.
bio.specialty: Self-roasted single-origin specialty coffees (four varieties available hot or iced, from origins such as Brazil, Honduras, Guatemala, Indonesia, El Salvador, Tanzania, Rwanda and more) plus a house 'NORR Blend' roasted to bring out natural sweetness. Also coffee tonic soda and frozen coffee, alongside homemade-style baked sweets such as financiers, pound cake and Breton galettes.
bio.philosophy: Uses 100% specialty-grade beans, roasting to emphasize each coffee's natural sweetness, and runs the space as both a roastery cafe and a rotating art gallery for a slow, leisurely atmosphere.

anecdotes.text: The space doubles as a gallery, with rotating artwork, three single seats by the entrance, a mezzanine with tables upstairs, and a semi-underground terrace facing a courtyard.
Limited-edition coffee packages are released to celebrate local Nara events such as the Todaiji water ceremony and the Wakakusayama grass-burning festival.

cuisine: 南インド系辛口スパイスカレー（カリープレート・ミールス）
gf_detail: Curries are served over rice rather than wheat, which is a starting point, but there is no documented gluten-free protocol. Spice blends, the ghee/oils, and house preparations have not been confirmed wheat-free, and this is a tiny shared-kitchen counter shop with no allergen handling described. A celiac diner would need to ask in detail and should not assume safety.
vegan_detail: The plate centers on meat and seafood (chicken, shrimp, keema/minced meat). There is a dal (lentil) curry and vegetable sides such as poriyal that are vegetable-based, but yogurt and ghee (clarified butter) are part of the cooking and the meals come with yogurt, so dishes are not reliably dairy-free or animal-free. A strict vegan would have very limited options and must confirm ingredients directly.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向北町22
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市東向北町22 returned [135.828949, 34.685017], within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: 東向北商店街（近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約3分）
seats_text: About 13 seats on the main floor and roughly 25 total including the second floor; intimate counter-style space shared with Café Wakakusa.
hours_raw: 火・金・祝前日 18:00-23:00／水・土 12:00-15:00, 18:00-23:00／日・祝 12:00-15:00／月・木 定休。ラストオーダーは閉店30分前。営業日は不定期に変動するためSNSの最新情報を確認。
closed_days: Mondays and Thursdays, plus additional irregular monthly closures
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, friendly counter shop whose owner deliberately aims for "glocal" appeal to both locals and international visitors, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at the curry plate will get you a warm welcome.
caveats: The kitchen is shared with Café Wakakusa and the owner is candid about being new to the trade, so days and hours shift month to month; check the shop's Instagram before going. Curries run genuinely hot, so order a lassi if you are sensitive to spice.

bio.background: Tarikaro first opened in central Nara in 2010 and built a cult following for its fiercely spicy South Indian-style curry before moving to Tokyo's Nishi-Ogikubo in 2021 and closing in January 2025. Akihiro Nozawa, a violinist and whistler who heads the music NPO 'World Ship' bringing orchestral performances to children across Southeast Asia, had been a devoted fan since the very beginning and kept visiting even after the Tokyo move. When the shop closed he pursued the chance to carry it on, was chosen over several other candidates for his commitment to the original vision, and brought it home to Nara's Higashimuki-kita shopping street in June 2025.
bio.specialty: A signature curry plate of four curries served side by side - dal (lentil), shrimp, keema, and a blazing chicken curry - arranged so the heat builds from one end to the other while the umami of good ingredients shines through. Accompanied by yogurt and best paired with a lassi if the spice runs too hot.
bio.philosophy: Nozawa describes inheriting the recipes as receiving 'art,' likening spice combinations to an orchestra in which the cook acts as conductor, deciding which flavors lead and which support. He aims to be 'glocal,' welcoming both Nara locals and travelers from abroad. The name '3.5' is his self-deprecating nod to still being half a cook, counting this as the fourth incarnation of the shop.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a professional violinist and whistler who runs an NPO delivering orchestral music to children abroad, and he had no prior restaurant experience - he calls himself '3.5' because he feels he is still only half a real cook.
He had loved Tarikaro since its 2010 opening and even followed it to Tokyo, so when it closed in 2025 he stepped up to revive it and won the succession over multiple other candidates.
The shop runs inside the established Café Wakakusa, whose owner Hiroyuki Nakanishi enthusiastically welcomed the collaboration, with a chimney installed to manage cooking aromas in the shared space.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Meals are served with yogurt and the curries use ghee (clarified butter), so dairy is present across the menu; meat and seafood curries are cooked in the same small shared kitchen.

positives.text: There is a vegetable-forward dal (lentil) curry and vegetable sides such as poriyal, giving vegetarians at least some options to build a plate around rice.

cuisine: Sake bar
gf_detail: No gluten-free program. Sake itself is rice-based and naturally wheat-free, so the core tasting flights are fine for someone avoiding wheat. However the food snacks are a different matter: cured/smoked items and seasonings commonly involve soy sauce (which contains wheat) and there is no allergen labeling or celiac handling here. Treat it as a drinking spot where the sake is the safe item and any food should be questioned individually. Not a celiac-safe kitchen.
vegan_detail: Primarily a sake bar; the food leans heavily on game meat (house-made venison jerky, wild boar smoked ham) and other animal products (smoked eggs, sweetfish). A few plant-based snacks exist — smoked tofu and pickled vegetables (漬物) — but these are likely seasoned with non-vegan dashi or soy-based marinades and are not advertised as vegan. The sake flights are the main vegan-friendly draw. Suitable for a vegan only as a place to drink with a token plant snack, not for a meal.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1071 なら和み館 1F
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市高畑町1071; within Nara box.
neighborhood: Takabatake (near Nara Hotel), Nara
seats_text: Standing-only (立ち飲み) counter format; no fixed seat count published
hours_raw: 11:00–16:00 (Tuesdays 11:00–15:00). Hours have varied over time (earlier listings showed 10:00–18:00 / 11:00–17:00); confirm before visiting.
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); Tuesdays shorter hours (11:00–15:00)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual standing sake bar inside a tourist-friendly souvenir hall near Nara Hotel where tasting sets are priced for walk-ins, so a foreign student can comfortably point at a flight and enjoy it with a few words of Japanese.
caveats: Hours and closures vary noticeably across sources and over time; the venue is part of the なら和み館 souvenir complex and its sake bar concept began in 2021 as a COVID-era pivot. Confirm current operation by phone (0742-21-7530) before a special trip.

bio.background: This standing sake bar sits on the ground floor of Nara Nagomi-kan, a souvenir hall facing the historic Nara Hotel in the Takabatake district. The hall once thrived on bus tours and school excursions, but when the pandemic erased that crowd in 2020, the director, Yamaji Junichi, shrank the gift-shop floor and reinvented the space as a place to drink. The bar opened on April 29, 2021, drawing on know-how from a sister project, a sake bar in Tohoku that had been running since 2013 as part of the region's earthquake-recovery efforts.
bio.specialty: Nara and Tohoku local sake tasting flights
bio.philosophy: The bar deliberately serves two crowds at once: an everyday neighborhood stop for locals, and a way for travelers to taste Nara's breweries and make a memory of the trip.

anecdotes.text: The bar is a COVID-era reinvention: with tour buses and school trips gone, Nagomi-kan downsized its souvenir section and opened a standing sake bar in its place in spring 2021.
The chef hunts the game himself, turning venison and wild boar into house-made jerky and smoked ham served as sake pairings.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Snacks include cured, smoked, and pickled items typical of a sake bar, which commonly use soy-sauce-based seasonings (soy sauce normally contains wheat); no gluten-free claims are made.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Food program centers on house-made venison and wild boar, so plant snacks like smoked tofu and pickles are prepared in a meat-handling environment.

positives.text: Sake is rice-based and naturally free of wheat and animal products, so the tasting flights are inherently safe for both wheat-avoidant and vegan drinkers.

lead: HALAL & VEGGIE Restaurant ポンテロッソ
name_ja: HALAL & VEGGIE Restaurant ポンテロッソ
cuisine: インド・ネパール料理（ハラル対応）/ Indian & Nepalese (Halal)
gf_detail: Not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen, but the restaurant is unusually accommodating: gluten-free rice can be substituted for naan, and gluten-free naan is available with advance notice. They also offer a "four poisons removed" (四毒抜き) preparation that eliminates wheat flour, vegetable oils, refined sugar and dairy from certain dishes. However, standard naan is wheat-based and listed allergens include wheat and cashew nuts, so cross-contact in a shared kitchen is likely. A celiac should request the GF rice option and the four-poisons-removed dishes, and confirm preparation directly. Best to call ahead (advance notice needed for GF naan).
vegan_detail: Genuine, well-documented vegan options rather than an afterthought. The kitchen offers a vegan dal bhat with seasonal vegetables and beans, vegan biryani, and a fully vegan naan made with Nara-grown flour and no eggs, milk, or baking powder. Halal and vegetarian/vegan customers are explicitly accommodated, and the "four poisons removed" approach can strip dairy from dishes. Meat curries (chicken, lamb, shrimp) and dairy naans exist on the same menu, so vegans should specify the vegan items clearly; non-vegan curries may use dairy/ghee.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市小西町2 ブティック一番館 2階
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja (奈良県奈良市小西町2) via GSI; first feature coordinates [135.827957, 34.68293], within the Nara box. Chō-level point for Konishi-cho.
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅エリア（小西さくら通り）
seats_text: Small second-floor space. Seat counts vary by source: roughly 20-24 total (counter plus tables). The room has high ceilings with natural-wood beams and seats around 20-24 guests.
hours_raw: 11:30-21:50 (closed Wednesday). Note: other listings cite 11:30-22:30 (L.O. 22:00) or lunch-only ranges; hours appear to vary.
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming, internationally-minded spot used to halal, vegan and vegetarian requests, so a foreign student should feel comfortable, though a few words of Japanese or pointing at the menu will smooth ordering and dietary requests.
caveats: Hours and seat counts differ across listings (some show lunch-only or 11:30-22:30); confirm current hours before visiting. Gluten-free naan needs advance notice. Listed allergens are wheat and cashew nuts.

bio.background: A second-floor Indian and Nepalese kitchen tucked above an import boutique just off Konishi Sakura-dori, a couple of minutes from the heart of central Nara. You climb the stairs into an airy room with high ceilings and curving natural-wood beams, where naan is baked fresh and torn by hand.
bio.philosophy: The owner cooks around the idea that 'in an age of 100-year lifespans, eat good-quality food' (人生100年時代は質の良い食事を), refusing commercial cooking oils and additives in favor of carefully selected ingredients. The kitchen even offers a 'four poisons removed' (四毒抜き) approach that drops wheat flour, vegetable oil, refined sugar and dairy from certain dishes.
bio.specialty: The signature dal bhat set is the dish to order, with diners describing it as light enough to eat every day 'without heartburn.' Other highlights include hand-made-from-the-skin soup momo dumplings, five kinds of basmati biryani, and twelve curries with heat adjustable across seven levels. Naan is made with Nara-grown flour, including a fully vegan version with no egg, milk or baking powder.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen avoids commercial oils and additives entirely, building the menu around quality ingredients for long-term health.
The soup momo dumplings are made by hand starting from the dough skins.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Standard naan is wheat-based and wheat is a listed allergen alongside cashew nuts, so cross-contact is likely in the shared kitchen; gluten-free rice substitution and gluten-free naan (with advance notice) are available as safer alternatives.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegan dishes (vegan dal bhat, vegan biryani, egg/milk-free vegan naan) are prepared in a kitchen that also makes dairy naans and meat curries, so vegans should clearly request the vegan items.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The restaurant explicitly accommodates halal, vegan and vegetarian diners and offers a 'four poisons removed' option excluding wheat, vegetable oil, refined sugar and dairy.

positives.text: Carefully selected ingredients with no commercial oils or additives used.
Gluten-free rice can replace naan, and a fully vegan naan made with Nara flour (no egg, milk or baking powder) is offered.

lead: Rasa Bojun 奈良店（ラサ ボジュン 奈良店）
name_ja: ラサ ボジュン 奈良店
cuisine: スリランカ料理・カレー（野菜中心のプレート、別皿で肉カレーも選べる家族的個人店）
neighborhood: 高畑町（ならまち東側／近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約11分）
gf_detail: スリランカカレーは米飯・野菜・豆・ココナッツミルクが中心で本質的に小麦を使わない皿が多く、ロティやパパダム以外は比較的グルテンを避けやすい。ただし専用調理体制やコンタミ対策の記載は確認できず、ロティ（小麦）も提供されるため、セリアックの方は必ず個別に相談を。
vegan_detail: スリランカの仏教文化を背景に、人参・オクラ・レンズ豆・各種豆などの野菜カレーが料理の土台で、肉は別皿の追加扱い。野菜カレーはココナッツミルクベースで植物性のものが多いが、プレートには茹で卵やミルクティーなど動物性も含まれるため、完全ビーガンには個別確認が必要。ベジタリアン対応は明確にあり。
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1098-1 KOTOMACHIビル104
geocode_note: GSI地番検索で高畑町1098番の座標を取得。奈良ボックス内（lat 34.64–34.74, lng 135.76–135.90）に収まる。
hours_raw: 11:00〜15:00、17:00〜22:00（L.O. 21:30）。月〜日・祝日・祝前日営業。タベログ表記では無休だが、地域情報サイトでは月曜および不定休の記載もあり。
closed_days: タベログ表記では無休。ただし一部の地域情報サイトでは月曜定休・不定休との記載があり、訪問前の確認推奨。
cultural_comfort_note: スリランカ出身シェフが営む家族的な個人店で、英語メニューや観光客対応は限定的なため、簡単な日本語かやさしいやり取りができると安心して注文できます。
caveats: 営業時間・定休日は媒体によって差があり（タベログは無休、地域サイトは月曜定休/不定休の記載）。完全ビーガン・セリアック対応の専用体制やコンタミ対策は明記されておらず、要個別確認。

bio.background: スリランカ出身のシェフ・ヴィジタ氏が、本格的なスリランカの家庭料理を日本の人に届けたいという思いで開いた個人店。2019年12月にオープンし、店名はシンハラ語で『おいしいごちそう』を意味する。
bio.specialty: 看板はワンプレートの『ボジュンプレート』。人参・オクラ・レンズ豆・各種豆など数種類の野菜カレーを自家調合スパイスとココナッツミルクで仕立て、サフランライス、パパダム、ココナッツの薬味などと一皿に盛り合わせる。日本人向けに鶏・豚・牛・マトンから選べる肉カレーを別皿で添えられる。ロティセットやダブルカレーセットなどもある。
bio.philosophy: 仏教徒が多いスリランカの食文化を背景に、一日三食を野菜カレー中心に組み立てる発想を大切にする。『同じ料理でも、各自が混ぜ合わせることでまったく違う味わいが生まれる』点こそスリランカカレーの醍醐味だと語る。

anecdotes.text: シェフのヴィジタ氏は記事掲載のおよそ一年前にスリランカから来日し、本格的なスリランカの家庭の味を日本に伝えることを目指している。
店名はシンハラ語で『おいしいごちそう』の意味。自家調合の香辛料とココナッツミルクを使った野菜カレーが料理の土台で、肉カレーは日本人向けに別皿で選べる構成。

positives.text: 料理の土台が野菜・豆・ココナッツミルクベースの数種類の野菜カレーで、ベジタリアン対応が明確にある。肉は別皿の追加扱いのため、野菜中心の食事を組み立てやすい。
自家調合スパイスとココナッツミルクを使ったマイルドな味付けで、辛さも比較的穏やかと紹介されている。

name_ja: 素味 スイーツ&コーヒー（Somi sweets&coffee）
cuisine: オーガニック・ヴィーガン/グルテンフリー焼き菓子＆スペシャルティコーヒー（テイクアウト）
gf_detail: Baked goods are described as basically vegan or gluten-free, with several items built on pesticide-free rice flour and almond powder (e.g. rice flour cookies, carrot cake). The shop emphasizes traceable organic ingredients. However, not every single item is guaranteed gluten-free — the bestselling rum raisin sandwich uses ancient spelt, which contains gluten. There is no documented dedicated gluten-free facility or cross-contamination protocol, so a strict celiac should confirm per item directly.
vegan_detail: Plant-based vegan sweets and specialty coffee, takeout only.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町1232
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市法蓮町1232 returned [135.827469, 34.692081], within the Nara box.
neighborhood: きたまち（奈良市法蓮町、東大寺/近鉄奈良駅エリア）
seats_text: Takeout only — no dine-in seating.
hours_raw: Current per official site: Saturday & Sunday 13:00–18:00 (may close early when items sell out; hours can vary for events). Historic listing: 11:00–18:00, irregular closures.
closed_days: Weekdays (Mon–Fri); currently open weekends only. Hours irregular — may close early when sold out.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly takeout sweets-and-coffee counter where a smile and a little Japanese go a long way, though the vegan/gluten-free concept and English website make it approachable for foreign students.
caveats: Tabelog lists the shop as 掲載保留 (listing on hold) because the platform could not confirm operating status, but the official website shows it is active with weekend hours, so it is treated as operating. Hours are irregular and weekend-only; the shop may sell out and close early. Confirm gluten-free status per item, as the bestselling rum raisin sandwich uses spelt (contains gluten).

bio.background: A tiny takeout-only sweets-and-coffee shop set in a renovated traditional townhouse in Nara's historic Kitamachi area, near Tōdai-ji and Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner trained at a Kyoto café, where they learned about vegan confectionery, coffee, and organic practices, then opened Somi to express a way of living through a shop scaled to their own life.
bio.specialty: Organic, plant-based baked goods and specialty coffee. The bestselling rum raisin sandwich layers cashew-cacao cream and rum raisins on ancient Shiga-grown spelt; the carrot cake uses pesticide-free rice flour and organic carrots with a lemon frosting; and the rice-flour cookies pair pesticide-free rice flour and organic almond powder with Nara organic green tea or domestic kinako and sesame.
bio.philosophy: The name Somi (素味) means the taste of simplicity — letting the natural flavor of carefully sourced, traceable, organic ingredients speak for itself. The owner aims to make sweets anyone can pick up, not treats for a special few.

anecdotes.text: The shop name 素味 (Somi) is meant to capture sosai no aji — the plain, honest flavor of the ingredients themselves and a simple kind of deliciousness.

The owner trained at a Kyoto café learning vegan sweets, coffee and organic practices before opening a shop sized to fit their own lifestyle, wanting to make sweets accessible to anyone rather than a special few.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No documented dedicated gluten-free facility or cross-contamination protocol; products are described as basically vegan or gluten-free rather than uniformly GF, and at least one bestseller (rum raisin sandwich) uses spelt, which contains gluten. Strict celiacs should confirm each item.

positives.text: Vegan sweets specialty shop using organic, traceable ingredients, with several items built on pesticide-free rice flour and almond powder rather than wheat.

cuisine: Monthly-rotating teishoku (set lunch), parfaits and house-roasted coffee; casual shokudo-cafe
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The monthly lunch sets are built around dishes such as tempura, chilled wheat noodles, fu (wheat gluten / seitan) and seasonal Japanese home cooking, and the menu uses standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, etc.) that contain wheat. There is no indication of a dedicated fryer or gluten-free preparation. Celiac travelers should not consider this safe.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan or vegetarian place. The monthly teishoku centers on meat and fish — e.g. pork Yanagawa-style hotpot, shrimp/mushroom chirashi sushi, mozuku vinegar — and most dishes use fish dashi. Vegetable side dishes (namul, seasonal Yamato/local produce) and the parfaits/coffee exist but are not prepared as guaranteed vegan, and dashi/honey/dairy are likely present. A vegan traveler would have very limited safe options and should ask directly.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市鳴川町19
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the building-level point for 奈良市鳴川町19; within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: Naramachi (ならまち), Narukawa-chō, in front of Tokuyū-ji temple; about 15 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, near JR Kyōbate Station
seats_text: One table for four plus roughly seven counter seats; intimate, single-room space
hours_raw: Lunch (set meals) 11:30–15:00; cafe 11:30–17:30 (L.O. around 17:00). Thursdays cafe only. Reported closed days vary slightly between sources (Tue/Wed, or Tue/Wed/Thu), with additional irregular closures announced on social media. Open on holidays.
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday (some listings also note Thursday as cafe-only or closed), plus irregular closures announced on Instagram
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run shokudo-cafe in old Naramachi where the menu changes monthly and is mostly posted in Japanese on Instagram, so a few words of Japanese (or pointing and a friendly konnichiwa) will go a long way.
caveats: Cash only. Very small (one table plus counter), so it can fill up — only the 11:30 lunch slot can be reserved. Hours and closed days are irregular and the lunch set rotates monthly; check the shop's Instagram before going. Note that as of 2025 some reports say regular teishoku service has scaled back toward a parfait-and-coffee focus, so the full set-meal lunch may not always be available.

bio.origin: Saitama Prefecture; moved to Nara in 2007 to study Buddhist art at Nara University
bio.background: Sakai grew up in Saitama with little exposure to temples until a middle-school trip to Kyoto, where the fierce, dynamic Buddhist statues at Toji (the Five Great Wisdom Kings) lit a lifelong fascination. He chose Nara University specifically for its cultural-heritage program and its closeness to countless Buddhist statues, then decided to make Nara his permanent home. He runs the guesthouse 古白 (Kohaku, started 2015), which offers Buddhist-statue talks and sukiyaki dinners, added a cafe and renamed it 古白 in 2018, and opened 七福食堂 in 2020 to reach a wider audience.
bio.specialty: A monthly-rotating teishoku (set lunch) of seasonal Japanese home cooking — past menus have featured pork Yanagawa-style hotpot with a soft-set egg, shrimp-and-mushroom chirashi sushi, tempura, miso-glazed fu, chilled noodles, mozuku vinegar and colorful namul — alongside seasonal parfaits (such as rum-raisin and Japanese-chestnut Mont Blanc, or caramel-nut and baked-apple) and house-roasted drip coffee.
bio.philosophy: Sakai talks about wanting to 'light the spark' that draws people into Nara's charm, treating a lodging, a cafe or a plate of food as an inviting entry point. He even compares a beautiful parfait to a Buddhist statue: both are tangible, attractive forms that can lead someone toward a deeper appreciation of Nara's culture.

anecdotes.text: The shop occupies a renovated former printing workshop, with a high ceiling, a glass frontage, antique tansu chests and old dishware that give the small room a calm, lived-in feel.
Sakai compares an enticing parfait to a Buddhist statue — a visible 'entry point' meant to spark curiosity in people (even high-schoolers) who might otherwise never engage with Nara's heritage.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Lunch sets regularly include wheat-based items (tempura, chilled wheat noodles, miso-glazed fu/seitan), so wheat is handled throughout the small kitchen.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use standard Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, miso glaze) which contain wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The menu is meat- and fish-centered (pork hotpot, shrimp chirashi, mozuku) and most dishes use fish dashi, so vegan separation is not maintained.

positives.text: Cooking emphasizes seasonal local and Yamato/Nara-area vegetables (e.g. produce from a farm in Ikoma), so plant-forward side dishes are part of the meal even though they are not certified vegan.

cuisine: Self-roast coffee kissaten / cafe (parfaits, craft soda) in a converted Naramachi printing-works building
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a coffee-and-dessert cafe; sweets such as parfaits and Mont Blanc, plus cookies and other baked components, are wheat-based, and no gluten-free menu or allergen handling is published. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The drinks lean naturally plant-friendly — self-roasted nel-drip coffee and house craft sodas (shiso, plum, ginger, lemon) are sold, and a black coffee or a craft soda without an ice-cream float would be incidentally vegan. However, the signature parfaits and desserts contain dairy and eggs, and there is no stated vegan offering, so a strict vegan should ask in person.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市鳴川町19
geocode_note: GSI address search returned the first feature for 奈良県奈良市鳴川町19 at [135.829361, 34.675777], within the central-Nara box.
neighborhood: Naramachi / Kyobate (south Naramachi), Nara City
seats_text: Small: roughly a 9-seat counter plus one table, in a converted printing-works building with high ceilings and vintage furniture.
hours_raw: Mon, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun 11:30-17:00; closed Tue and Wed. (Hours have varied over time and can shift; some listings note 11:30-17:30 for the cafe.)
closed_days: Tuesday and Wednesday (occasional irregular closures — check social media)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run cafe where the menu and conversation are in Japanese, so a few basic phrases or a translation app will make ordering coffee and a parfait much smoother.
caveats: The same Narukawa-cho 19 address has operated under linked names — the cafe 古白 (Kohaku), the food-serving 七福食堂 (Shichifuku Shokudo), and a nearby guesthouse also called Kohaku run by the same owner. Lunch/set-meal service was reported to have ended in 2025, leaving the cafe (self-roasted coffee, parfaits, craft soda) as the current operation. Confirm current hours and offerings via the shop's Instagram before visiting; payment is cash-based and seating is very limited.

bio.origin: Saitama; moved to Nara around 2010 to study
bio.background: Sakai Yuki came to Nara from Saitama to study Buddhist art and cultural properties at university, drawn in as a teenager by the fierce, many-armed Five Wisdom Kings he encountered on a school trip. He settled in the Naramachi neighborhood, opening a guesthouse in 2015 that grew to include a cafe, and later opening a small eatery-cafe in a converted former printing works a short walk away.
bio.specialty: Self-roasted, nel-drip coffee; seasonal parfaits (a recurring favorite pairs Japanese chestnut Mont Blanc with rum raisin); and a house craft cola made with roasted spices such as cinnamon, cola nut, vanilla, clove, lemongrass and cardamom, alongside fruit-and-herb craft sodas.
bio.philosophy: Sakai wants ordinary daily life to be a doorway into Nara's history and spirituality. He famously says he builds his parfaits 'thinking of them as Buddhist statues' — beautiful, approachable forms meant to draw a person gently inward, the way a statue points toward something unseen.

anecdotes.text: Sakai explains that he makes parfaits 'thinking of them as Buddhist statues' — outward beauty that invites the visitor toward something deeper, the same idea behind hosting casual Buddhist-art talks at his nearby guesthouse.

When food was served, the kitchen leaned on Nara-area produce (including vegetables from a farm in nearby Ikoma) and made its own mentsuyu and miso, with monthly-changing seasonal set meals before the operation shifted to a coffee-and-dessert cafe.
The room is a renovated former printing works with high ceilings, bare-bulb lighting and antique furniture; a working press still operates toward the back of the building.

positives.text: Self-roasted black coffee and house-made fruit-and-herb craft sodas (shiso, plum, ginger, lemon) are offered, which are incidentally suitable for many plant-based or wheat-avoiding visitors when ordered without an ice-cream float.

cuisine: Jibier (wild game) and Yamato-Japanese creative cuisine
neighborhood: Naramachi / Takabatake (near Kintetsu Nara Station)
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. This is a multi-course game-meat kaiseki/kappo experience built around Japanese seasonings (soy sauce, sake, miso) and dashi, which routinely contain wheat. There is no indication of celiac-safe preparation or cross-contamination control, so it should not be considered gluten-free safe. A determined diner could inquire directly when booking, but nothing is confirmed.
vegan_detail: This is a wild-game specialist whose entire concept centers on meat (deer, wild boar, bear, pheasant, rabbit and other game). No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented, and the fixed course is meat-based with fish dashi typical of Japanese cooking. Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1118
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.83255, 34.68111] for 奈良県奈良市高畑町1118; within the Nara bounding box. Located inside Setre Naramachi hotel near Sarusawa Pond.
seats_text: 6 counter seats only; limited to a few groups per night (max 6 guests), private booking available
hours_raw: Dinner only 19:00-22:00; all guests begin simultaneously. Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays.
closed_days: Tuesday, Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, reservation-only chef's counter where the host serves and explains each course personally, so some Japanese (or a Japanese-speaking companion) will make the experience far smoother, though the warm small-group setting is welcoming.
caveats: High-end fixed-course dinner (around 25,000 yen per person including drink pairing); advance reservation mandatory; dinner only with a single simultaneous start time. Not a drop-in spot and not budget-friendly. No vegan or gluten-free accommodation documented.

bio.background: Inspired by his father's love of cooking, Hiroshi Ida built his career in major Osaka hotels and clubs, serving as sous chef at Hotel Agora Osaka Moriguchi and Hotel Seagull Tenpozan, then five years as head chef at Garden City Club Osaka. In 2018 he became the inaugural chef of the dining room at Setre Naramachi, and later opened this dedicated game-cuisine counter on the shore of Sarusawa Pond.
bio.specialty: Wild game (jibier) courses built around Nara-sourced deer, wild boar, bear, pheasant, rabbit and other seasonal game, paired with Yamato vegetables and local Nara sake. Signature preparations include citrus-fed wild boar charcoal-grilled with a Yamato-greens puree, Yamato venison sukiyaki, and straw-fired game.
bio.philosophy: The concept is 'the more you know, the more delicious it becomes' (知ればもっと美味しくなる). Ida educates guests on why game is culled to protect farmland and how honoring that life by eating it well connects to a more sustainable future; he is even training as a hunter so he can one day serve game he has caught himself.

anecdotes.text: Game is sourced from Totsukawa Jibier Juku, a processing facility in Totsukawa Village in southern Nara, and Ida emphasizes that properly handled game has no gamey odor while being high in protein and low in calories.
The counter seats only six guests a night and all diners begin their course at the same time, with the chef preparing and presenting each dish directly in front of them.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Japanese game courses of this style rely on soy sauce, sake, miso and dashi seasonings that customarily contain wheat or fish; no gluten-free or allergen handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The restaurant is a meat-focused game specialist with no vegan menu, so animal products are inherent to every course.

name_ja: 和風薬膳料理 紅茶 京小づち
name_en: Washoku Yakuzen Ryori Benifucha Kyo Kozuchi
cuisine: 和風薬膳・創作料理（大和野菜・薬膳）— Japanese herbal-medicinal (yakuzen) creative cuisine using Yamato vegetables
neighborhood: ならまち / 下御門町（近鉄奈良駅）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市下御門町21
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 下御門町21; within Nara box.
seats_text: Official site lists 16 seats (Tabelog lists 24); a small intimate space along the Shimogomon arcade in Naramachi.
hours_raw: 11:30-19:00 (official site; reservations prioritized, closes when sold out). Tabelog lists 11:30-21:00. Closed Thursdays (open if Thursday is a holiday).
closed_days: Thursdays (open when Thursday falls on a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, warm herbal-cuisine spot where some Japanese helps; calling ahead to reserve and to discuss the herbal set menus will make a foreign student's visit much smoother.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. This is creative Japanese cuisine built on bonito-and-kombu dashi and Japanese seasonings, which in Japan virtually always means wheat-based soy sauce and likely other wheat-containing condiments. No gluten-free menu or allergen program is documented. Not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Although the kitchen is vegetable-forward (Yamato vegetables, herbs, ancient red rice), it is not vegan. The cooking base is bonito (fish) and kombu dashi, and the signature medicinal risotto/porridge uses heritage chicken stock. No dedicated vegan options are documented; a strict vegan should not assume suitability and would need to confirm directly.

bio.background: A small herbal-cuisine restaurant tucked just inside the Shimogomon arcade in Naramachi, central Nara, serving wellness-oriented Japanese 'yakuzen' (medicinal) cooking. The kitchen builds new Nara flavors from historic local ingredients, leaning on Yamato vegetables, accessible Chinese and Western herbs, and ancient foods tied to Nara's Heijo-kyo past.
bio.specialty: Signature dishes include the medicinal Naramachi bento (薬膳ならまち弁当) featuring ancient red rice (古代米) and 'so' (蘇), a reconstructed ancient dairy; and a medicinal broth risotto/porridge made with heritage chicken stock infused with bonito, kombu, dried shiitake, cinnamon, star anise and bay leaf, layered with vegetable broths. Menus note the health benefit of each of the roughly 40 ingredients used. The leaves of Yamato touki (大和当帰, Japanese angelica) appear as an accent in tomato sauce and, when fresh, as tempura.
bio.philosophy: Food that works gently from the inside out for bodies tired by stress and irregular living — 'historic ingredients creating new Nara flavors,' prioritizing immunity-supporting, seasonal local produce for health and beauty.

anecdotes.text: The medicinal Naramachi bento revives flavors of Nara's ancient capital, pairing古代米 (ancient red rice) with 蘇, a reconstructed dairy food eaten in the Nara period.
Yamato touki (Japanese angelica) leaves are folded into the cooking — woven into a tomato sauce as an accent and served as tempura when fresh leaves are in season.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The kitchen describes its method as carefully selected ingredients 'cooked in Japanese style with bonito and kombu dashi,' which in Japan customarily relies on wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free seasoning program is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The foundational broth is bonito (fish) and kombu, and the signature medicinal risotto/porridge uses heritage chicken stock, so animal products are integral to the core dishes.

positives.text: Strongly vegetable-forward and ingredient-transparent: menus list the health benefit of each of roughly 40 ingredients, and the kitchen emphasizes seasonal Yamato vegetables and herbs.

caveats: Hours and seat count differ between the official site (16 seats, to 19:00) and Tabelog (24 seats, to 21:00); official-site figures used. Closing time may be earlier when reserved menus sell out. No documented vegan or gluten-free program — dietary travelers must confirm directly. Owner/chef name not documented in sources reviewed.

cuisine: 食堂・居酒屋・定食（家族経営の地元店 / home-style set meals by day, izakaya by night）
neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南市町16番地
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.829422, 34.680107] for 奈良県奈良市南市町16番地; within the central Nara bounding box.
seats_text: Counter 11 seats plus one 6-person table (listed capacity ~13 counter+table; table seats up to 6).
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–15:00, Dinner 18:00–22:00 (Tabelog notes hours roughly 11:30–23:00). Closed Sat & Sun. Confirm closures by phone.
closed_days: Saturday and Sunday (also closed year-end/New Year; confirm other closures by phone)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-only family kitchen with counter seating where the proprietress cooks home-style meals; some basic Japanese and a relaxed, polite manner will make ordering much smoother for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The menu is built around Japanese home cooking — fried pork cutlet (tonkatsu, breaded in wheat), pasta dishes, miso soup, and dishes seasoned with regular soy sauce, all of which contain wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation or any awareness of celiac needs, so this is not safe for strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented.
caveats: Cash only — no cards, e-money, or QR payment. Closed weekends and year-end/New Year; exact closures are best confirmed by phone (0742-22-4043). Very small (counter-focused), so seating is limited at lunch.

bio.background: A small family-run eatery tucked in Naramachi, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station, marked by a red 'daily set lunch' banner at the entrance. It serves home-style set lunches by day and turns into a neighborhood izakaya in the evening, drawing loyal local regulars.
bio.specialty: The ¥800 daily set lunch (chitto choose fish or meat) — for example grilled atka mackerel or pork cutlet — served with sides such as hijiki pasta, sautéed komatsuna greens, pickles, and miso soup, finished with a complimentary coffee after the meal.
bio.philosophy: Warm, gentle home cooking meant to satisfy 'both the stomach and the heart,' served in a welcoming, family-style atmosphere.

anecdotes.text: The proprietress's home-style cooking is described as having a warm, gentle flavor that satisfies both stomach and heart, and regulars return specifically for her dishes; the day's lunch is signaled by a red set-lunch banner out front.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kitchen serves breaded fried pork cutlet and other wheat-based dishes in a tiny shared kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes use standard Japanese seasonings; regular soy sauce (which contains wheat) is the norm and no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Daily set includes fish or meat and miso soup typically made with fish dashi; vegan separation is not practiced.

lead: オステリア ユルリ
name_ja: オステリア ユルリ
cuisine: Italian osteria — charcoal-grilled meats and seafood, clay-pot rice, Yamato (local Nara) vegetables
gf_detail: Not gluten-free.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The kitchen centers on charcoal-grilled branded pork, premium seafood, and meat dishes; pasta uses house-made pancetta and the rice is cooked in shared cookware. Although local Yamato vegetables feature prominently, there is no documented vegan menu. Vegetarians may find vegetable-forward dishes but should ask in advance.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町13-1 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市東向南町13-1 returned [135.828918, 34.682663], within the Nara box.
neighborhood: ならまち / Higashimuki arcade, ~3 min from Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: 19 seats total (9 counter, 10 table); one source describes roughly 26 across five 2-person tables plus nine counter seats. Small second-floor space.
hours_raw: Lunch 12:00-14:30, Dinner 18:00-21:30 (last entry 20:00). Closed Wednesdays plus occasional irregular closures.
closed_days: Wednesdays, plus irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A small, reservation-only neighborhood osteria where staff ask you to choose your menu ahead of time, so a foreign student will be most comfortable with some basic Japanese or a translation app to handle the phone booking and pre-order.

bio.background: A casual, intimate Italian osteria tucked on the second floor of the Higashimuki shopping arcade in central Nara, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. In the spirit of an osteria — described locally as an 'Italian-style izakaya' — it favors warmth and approachability over formality, with a counter that opens onto the kitchen and an elegant, calm room.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilling is the heart of the kitchen: signature branded and pasture-raised meats such as Azumino free-range pork are slow-grilled over charcoal (the pork alone takes about 40 minutes) and served simply with salt to bring out the meat's natural umami. The other house point of pride is donabe gohan — rice cooked in an Iga-yaki clay pot using the kitchen's own reduced-pesticide rice. Seasonal local Nara 'Yamato' vegetables sourced directly from neighboring farms round out the plates, alongside daily-changing pastas and Italian sparkling wines like Franciacorta.
bio.philosophy: Cooking driven by carefully chosen seasonal ingredients — fresh vegetables bought directly from nearby Nara farms and select meats and seafood — finished over charcoal to coax out maximum flavor.

anecdotes.text: The slow charcoal preparation means the kitchen asks guests to decide their menu when booking; the pork is grilled about 40 minutes and arrives juicy with a pleasant firmness, served with salt.
Bread that accompanies the meal can be swapped out for the restaurant's clay-pot rice on request.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is pasta- and bread-based with a shared kitchen; no gluten-free protocol is documented, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Pastas use house-made pancetta and the kitchen centers on charcoal-grilled meats and seafood; no vegan preparation is documented.

positives.text: Staff ask guests to pre-select their menu when reserving, which gives an opportunity to discuss ingredients and substitutions in advance (e.g., swapping bread for clay-pot rice).

caveats: Chef's name and full origin story are not documented on accessible pages. The official site (http://www.yururi-italian.com/) failed to load over HTTPS due to a TLS certificate mismatch, so its content could not be fetched directly; details drawn from Tabelog, Gnavi, and a Nara dining blog. Seat count varies by source (19 on listings vs ~26 in one blog).

cuisine: 手延べ三輪そうめん・にゅうめん・会席膳（Hand-pulled Miwa somen, hot nyumen, and kaiseki set meals)
neighborhood: 三輪 (Miwa), 桜井市 Sakurai City
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The entire menu is built around sōmen and nyūmen, which are wheat noodles, and the dipping/broth tsuyu is a standard soy-sauce and dashi base. No gluten-free preparations or wheat-free options are documented. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Sōmen and nyūmen are served with a fish-dashi/soy tsuyu, and signature set meals feature tempura, wagyu, and other animal products. No vegan dishes or plant-based broth are documented.
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 奈良県桜井市芝293 to [lng 135.840912, lat 34.533195]. This is in Sakurai City near Miwa Myojin (Omiwa Shrine), well south of the central-Nara box (lat 34.64-34.74, lng 135.76-135.90), so coordinates are set null per the rule. The venue is genuinely located in the Miwa area of Sakurai, not central Nara.
seats_text: Approximately 44 seats including one tatami room.
hours_raw: 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 15:00). Closed Fridays, 2nd and 4th Thursdays, and year-end/New Year.
closed_days: Fridays, plus the 2nd and 4th Thursdays of the month; closed for year-end and New Year. (Note: Thursday closures are only on the 2nd and 4th weeks, so most Thursdays are open.)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, tourist-friendly noodle restaurant near Omiwa Shrine where you can point at the picture menu, but staff are unlikely to navigate gluten or vegan needs in English, so go for the experience rather than dietary accommodation.
caveats: Located in Sakurai City (Miwa area), a fair distance south of central Nara, not in the Nara city center. Reservations are taken by phone only and are not accepted on weekends, holidays, Golden Week, Jan 1-15, or in July-August. Every signature dish is wheat-noodle based, so unsuitable for gluten-free or vegan diners.

roles: Ikeri Co., Ltd. (operator / Miwa somen maker)
bio.background: Senjutei is the direct-run restaurant of Ikeri, a Miwa somen house founded in 1850 (Kaei 3) in the Miwa district of Sakurai, considered the birthplace of somen. The eatery opened in 1980, at a time when there were almost no restaurants dedicated to somen, so that visitors could taste the maker's hand-pulled noodles fresh, on the spot, near Omiwa Shrine.
bio.specialty: Hand-pulled Miwa somen served chilled, and hot nyumen; the popular signature is the chilled tri-color somen with tempura, alongside set meals such as the Senjutei lunch set, the wagyu colorful set, and an Asuka kaiseki course.
bio.philosophy: To serve high-quality hand-pulled somen at an approachable price as a maker-direct shop, letting diners enjoy the traditional flavor of Miwa while overlooking the area's ancient historical landscape.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant is operated by Ikeri, which has been making Miwa somen since 1850, and it opened in 1980 specifically to let people eat the maker's somen freshly prepared.
A signature offering is the colorful tri-color Miwa somen, served chilled with carefully selected tempura.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The kitchen centers on wheat sōmen/nyūmen noodles and also fries tempura, so airborne flour and shared boiling water make cross-contamination essentially certain; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Noodles are served with a standard Japanese tsuyu, which is soy-sauce based; soy sauce normally contains wheat, and no wheat-free tsuyu is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes use fish-based dashi tsuyu and the menu prominently includes tempura and wagyu, so vegan diners cannot rely on the broths or set meals.

cuisine: Ramen (handmade noodles; pork-bone and fish-dashi broths)
neighborhood: Babacho, between Kintetsu Nara and JR Nara stations, along Yasuragi-no-michi near Mochiidono
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市馬場町11
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 奈良県奈良市馬場町11 to [135.82576, 34.678917], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Counter-only seating (roughly 8-10 seats) with a small waiting area; non-smoking.
hours_raw: Mon, Wed-Fri & holiday eve/after: 10:50-13:50; Sat, Sun & holidays: 10:50-13:30; closed Tuesdays. Lunch only; occasional temporary closures.
closed_days: Tuesdays (regardless of holidays); occasional irregular closures
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, lunch-only family counter shop with no online presence in English and quick turnover at peak times, so basic Japanese and queue etiquette help a foreign student feel comfortable.
gf_detail: This is a ramen shop built around handmade wheat noodles, so it is not suitable for celiacs or anyone avoiding gluten. The broths are also seasoned with components typically containing wheat. No gluten-free options are indicated.
vegan_detail: Both signature bowls are animal-based: the pork broth uses pork bone and chicken with kombu and vegetables, and the fish broth is built on kombu and mackerel. There are no vegan options on the menu, and the noodles are paired with these meat/fish stocks.
caveats: Lunch only and prone to selling out or closing early; it can draw lines during its short midday window. Cash only, no reservations. Menu prices have risen over the years (broth bowls now around 880-1,050 yen).

bio.background: Fujimon Seimen opened at the end of July 2013 as a small, family-run lunch counter along Yasuragi-no-michi in central Nara. The owners committed to making their own noodles from the start, installing the noodle machinery before anything else, which is why the name carries the original meaning of a noodle workshop (seimen-jo). It is run as a two-person partnership between the noodle maker and the proprietress, who keep it deliberately small so the family can watch over every bowl.
bio.specialty: Two house broths over handmade noodles: a pork-bone-and-chicken broth rounded out with domestic vegetables and Dohnan kombu, served with a whipped, foamy surface; and a fish broth that leans on kombu and mackerel (rather than bonito) for a distinctive, udon-inspired character. Both come with chashu and seasoned egg, and noodles can be ordered thin or medium-thick.
bio.philosophy: No chemical seasonings and no added salt, relying on the strength of the stock ingredients and carefully selected, preservative-free flour. The seasoning is kept intentionally mild so that elderly customers and children can finish the whole bowl.

anecdotes.text: The shop name reflects the founders' decision to build around an in-house noodle factory, installing the noodle-making machine before opening so every bowl uses their own fresh noodles.
The fish broth deliberately uses mackerel instead of bonito to give the ramen its own identity, an idea the owners describe as inspired by udon.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu centers on handmade wheat noodles produced on-site, so a gluten-free diner cannot eat here safely.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Both broths are animal-based (pork bone and chicken, or kombu with mackerel), so there is no vegan bowl available.

positives.text: The kitchen avoids chemical seasonings and added salt, building flavor from pork, chicken, domestic vegetables, kombu and mackerel, and keeps seasoning mild enough for children and elderly diners.

lead: 粉もん屋 八 奈良餅飯殿店
name_ja: 粉もん屋 八 奈良餅飯殿店
cuisine: たこ焼き・粉もん（B級グルメ、屋台風スタンド）
gf_detail: This is a "konamon" (flour-foods) stand: takoyaki and okonomiyaki are made from a wheat-flour batter, so the entire menu is built on wheat. There is no gluten-free option and no indication of allergy handling. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
vegan_detail: Takoyaki contains octopus, egg and fish-stock-based batter, and is finished with bonito flakes, sauce, and mayonnaise; okonomiyaki similarly uses egg, meat/seafood and fish dashi. No vegan items are offered.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町42番地 OK広場内
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良市餅飯殿町42 returned [135.82869, 34.680351], within the central Nara box; matches the Mochiidono Center Street arcade location.
neighborhood: 餅飯殿センター街（もちいどの商店街）／近鉄奈良駅
seats_text: Roughly 8 outdoor terrace seats in the OK広場 plaza beside the stand; primarily a takeout/standing eatery.
hours_raw: 10:00-19:00（木曜定休、臨時休業あり）
closed_days: Thursday (臨時休業あり)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual order-at-the-counter takoyaki stand in the arcade where a smile and a few words of Japanese (or just pointing at the menu) are all you need, though staff are unlikely to speak much English.
caveats: Address on the arcade's official directory lists a nearby 餅飯殿町12 (夢CUBE 2F) location while Tabelog lists 餅飯殿町42 (OK広場); these may reflect a relocation or a second nearby spot within the same shopping street. Hours can vary with occasional temporary closures.

bio.background: Konamonya Hachi began as a single takoyaki stand in Nara and has grown over roughly 18 years into a brand of more than 40 shops across Nara, Osaka, Kyoto, Shiga, Saitama, Tokyo and Hokkaido, most of them in busy station and shopping-arcade locations. The original turf is here in central Nara, on the historic Mochiidono Center Street that links the modern town to old Naramachi. The Mochiidono stand sits in the OK広場 plaza, a prefab-style kiosk with a handful of outdoor tables where shoppers grab a tray of hot takoyaki to eat on the spot.
bio.specialty: Yamato-style takoyaki with a creamy molten center, served in five-piece portions with a choice of sauces (classic sauce, soy, salt-and-aonori, spicy, and a miso version topped with crispy fried-oil bits known as abura-kasu); the shop also griddles okonomiyaki and offers dote-nabe-style sides and drinks.
bio.philosophy: The brand describes itself as running on "energy and smiles," a customer-first stance backed up by community work such as handing out takoyaki at evacuation shelters after the 2011 Tohoku earthquake.

anecdotes.text: The shop won first place in Nara Prefecture's largest B-grade gourmet competition in 2011, cementing its reputation as a local takoyaki champion.
From one Nara stand the brand has expanded to over 40 locations across seven prefectures, but the Mochiidono arcade remains part of its central-Nara home turf.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Takoyaki and okonomiyaki batter is wheat flour cooked on shared griddles; the entire menu is flour-based, so there is no way to avoid gluten here.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy-sauce-flavored takoyaki is offered; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Batter and toppings include octopus, egg, fish-stock dashi, bonito flakes and mayonnaise; no vegan preparation is available.

cuisine: 居酒屋・家庭料理（店主おまかせ、地元密着の小規模個人店）
neighborhood: 餅飯殿町（もちいどのセンター街）／近鉄奈良駅
gf_detail: This is a traditional izakaya / home-style cooking spot with no indication of gluten-free accommodation. Documented dishes include karaage (wheat-flour-coated fried fish) and udon (wheat noodles), and izakaya cooking relies heavily on soy sauce and fish-dashi seasonings that typically contain wheat. There is no published information on dedicated equipment or cross-contamination control, so it cannot be considered safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: An izakaya whose menu centers on meat and fish dishes (signature chicken steak, white-fish karaage) and typical izakaya seasonings (soy sauce, fish dashi). No vegan or vegetarian options are documented. Not a vegan-friendly destination.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町40 財団ビル2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI address search on 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町40; first feature coordinates [135.82869, 34.680351], within the Nara bounding box.
seats_text: 13 seats total: 5 counter seats plus 2 tables seating 4.
hours_raw: 14:00–24:00 (営業は夕方頃から、というレビュー言及あり)。曜日別の詳細は未公表。
closed_days: No regular closing day documented; hours are loosely run by the owner so confirm by phone (0742-20-7211).
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run izakaya with karaoke and no published English support, so it suits a student comfortable speaking some Japanese and at ease chatting with locals at the counter.
caveats: A very small (13-seat) owner-run evening izakaya inside the Mochiidono arcade. Cooking is largely the owner's choice based on your budget (from around 2,500 yen), and karaoke is available. Hours are loosely kept, so calling ahead is wise. No vegan or gluten-free accommodation is documented.

bio.background: A small, locally rooted izakaya tucked on the second floor of a building in the historic Mochiidono shopping arcade, a few minutes' walk from the center of Nara. With just over a dozen seats at the counter and a couple of tables, it has the intimate feel of a neighborhood spot where the owner cooks for whoever comes in.
bio.specialty: The kitchen is known for its chicken dishes — the chicken steak is singled out as exceptional for its careful preparation and grilling technique — alongside fried white-fish dishes and udon. Rather than a fixed menu, the owner tends to put together dishes to suit each guest's budget.
bio.philosophy: A 'drink, sing, and eat' place: relaxed home-style cooking paired with karaoke, built around hospitality and the owner's personal touch rather than a formal concept.

anecdotes.text: Reviewers highlight that the chicken is praised specifically for the owner's attention to trimming, prep, and grilling technique, which makes the chicken steak stand out.
The arcade's own shop introduction describes it as a fun place where professional cooking is tailored to the customer's budget, combining dining with karaoke.

name_ja: PY Halal Kitchen（ピーワイ ハラル キッチン）
cuisine: Halal Asian / Indian curry
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The menu centers on naan, udon, laksa rice-vermicelli dishes and curries; biryani is rice-based but curries and many dishes use wheat-thickened sauces and naan is wheat. Nothing indicates a dedicated GF kitchen or allergen control, so a celiac traveler must ask in person and treat it as not celiac-safe by default.
vegan_detail: A vegetarian menu is offered and the kitchen is noted as "particular about vegetables," which suits vegetarians. As a halal kitchen there is no alcohol, but vegetarian here does not guarantee vegan — South Asian veg dishes commonly use ghee, butter (e.g., butter/cheese-based items) and dairy. Vegans should specify no dairy/ghee and confirm dish by dish.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市橋本町36-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市橋本町36-1 returned [135.828613, 34.681683], within the central Nara box. Location is in the Mochiidono shopping street area, ~3 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station.
neighborhood: 餅飯殿町／橋本町（もちいどのセンター街・近鉄奈良駅周辺）
seats_text: Approximately 70 seats; large space with a children's play room.
hours_raw: 11:00-20:00, closed Tuesdays
closed_days: Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, family-friendly halal restaurant used to international visitors and Muslim tourists, with a photo menu and a play room, so a foreign student can walk in comfortably and point to dishes.
caveats: Cash only. The Tabelog address (橋本町36-1) and the shopping-street promotion both place it in the Mochiidono/Kintetsu Nara Station area; sources differ on the listed phone number (080-3347-8181 vs 050-1231-1442) and on owner nationality detail. Vegetarian options are confirmed but no vegan- or gluten-free-specific program is documented; allergy/cross-contact handling is undocumented.

bio.background: PY Halal Kitchen sits in the Mochiidono shopping arcade a few minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station, run by a Muslim owner so that the whole menu is halal and no alcohol is served. The roomy, simply decorated space even has a children's play room, making it a relaxed stop for families and for Muslim travelers who want a worry-free meal while sightseeing in Nara.
bio.specialty: Tandoori chicken, cheese naan and lamb biryani are the house favorites, alongside a wide-ranging Asian lineup that runs from chicken laksa with coconut-milk broth and butter chicken with naan to spicy lamb udon and beef bhuna khichuri.
bio.philosophy: Keep everything halal and welcoming, so guests of any background can eat with confidence in a friendly, come-as-you-are setting.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen is run by a devout Muslim owner who keeps every dish halal and serves no alcohol, and the restaurant provides a prayer space and a play room for children.
Signature dishes promoted by the shop are tandoori chicken, cheese naan and lamb biryani, served in a casual large dining room steps from Kintetsu Nara Station.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free or allergen-control information is documented; wheat is pervasive via naan and udon, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetarian dishes are offered but South Asian veg cooking typically uses dairy/ghee and the kitchen also prepares meat dishes; no separation is documented, so vegans should confirm no dairy and ask about shared equipment.

positives.text: Entire menu is halal with no alcohol used, and a vegetarian menu is available.
The kitchen is described as particular about its vegetables, with vegetarian options listed.

lead: Shika san chocolat（シカサンショコラ）
name_ja: しかさんしょこら（Shika san chocolat）
cuisine: Gateau au chocolat / chocolate confectionery
gf_detail: The shop's signature and essentially sole product is a gateau au chocolat made deliberately without wheat flour or rice flour, so it is naturally gluten-free. The official site and a local news feature both describe it as グルテンフリー / made with no flour. This is not a certified celiac-dedicated facility and there is no published statement about cross-contamination controls, so highly gluten-sensitive visitors should still confirm in person, but the core product genuinely contains no gluten grains.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The gateau au chocolat is made with butter, fresh cream, and eggs (chocolate cake), so it contains dairy and egg. No plant-based version is documented.
address_ja: 〒630-8222 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町12 夢CUBE D室
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町12 via GSI; coordinates [135.829178, 34.68055] fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: No eat-in seating; takeout only (a small stall in the Yume CUBE entrepreneur-support facility on Mochiidono Center-gai).
hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 (Mon, Tue, Wed, Fri); 10:00-19:00 (Sat, Sun). Closed Thursdays. (Official site lists 10:00-19:00; Tabelog lists 10:00-18:00 weekdays / 10:00-19:00 weekends.)
closed_days: Thursdays
neighborhood: Mochiidono-cho / Mochiidono Center-gai (Naramachi area), about 10 min walk south of Kintetsu Nara Station
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, friendly takeout stall where staff happily offer free samples and explain the cake, so a few words of Japanese and pointing will carry you through a quick, low-pressure purchase.
caveats: Hours differ slightly between sources (official site shows 10:00-19:00 daily; Tabelog shows 10:00-18:00 weekdays and 10:00-19:00 weekends) — the weekday/weekend split from Tabelog is used here; confirm on the day. It is a takeout-only stall inside the Yume CUBE facility, not a sit-down cafe. Gluten-free is by recipe (no wheat or rice flour) but the shop publishes no dedicated-facility / cross-contamination statement, so very sensitive celiac visitors should ask.

bio.background: Shika san chocolat opened in late 2023 as a one-product specialty stand inside the Yume CUBE, the entrepreneur-support corner of Nara's historic Mochiidono Center-gai shopping arcade. Its owner runs it as a second venture alongside Milleturu, a women's tailoring atelier based in nearby Yamato-Koriyama, and spent more than a decade perfecting a single idea: a gateau au chocolat unlike any other.
bio.specialty: A sansho (Japanese mountain pepper) gateau au chocolat — a rich-but-clean-finishing, fluffy-yet-moist chocolate cake in which the chocolate slowly gives way to bright Japanese-pepper notes. It is made entirely without wheat flour or rice flour. Sold by the cut, half, and whole cake; a plain version without sansho is also offered for those who prefer it.
bio.philosophy: The maker set out to create 'a new standard no one has tasted before,' framing the chocolate-to-sansho transition as a journey ending in Nara, the terminus of the Silk Road. The flourless approach is central to the recipe rather than an afterthought.

anecdotes.text: The signature sansho gateau au chocolat was developed over roughly 15 years of trial and error, using powdered Japanese pepper, before the shop opened in 2023.
The owner also runs a women's tailoring business, Milleturu, in Yamato-Koriyama, and treats the chocolate shop as a parallel creative project.
Staff are known for offering free taste samples and explaining the cake to passers-by at the stall.

positives.text: The signature gateau au chocolat is made with no wheat flour and no rice flour, so the core product is naturally gluten-free by recipe.
A local news feature independently confirms the cake is made without wheat or rice flour and is naturally gluten-free.

cuisine: Yoshoku (traditional Japanese-Western home-style cooking)
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation indicated. This is a classic yoshoku (Western-style) kitchen built around demi-glace sauce (flour-thickened), breaded cutlets (bifkatsu), bread service, and hamburg steak — all wheat-heavy. No mention of gluten-free menu items, dedicated fryer, or celiac handling anywhere. Not suitable for celiacs.
