vegan_detail: A meat-focused yoshoku restaurant; the kitchen specializes in beef steaks, hamburg steak, beef and tongue stew, and duck confit. Sauces are demi-glace (meat-based). No vegan or vegetarian options are documented and the cuisine is fundamentally animal-based. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町31-2
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 135.828995, 34.680717 — within the central Nara box (餅飯殿町 / もちいどのセンター街, near Kintetsu Nara Station).
neighborhood: 餅飯殿町 / もちいどのセンター街 (Mochiidono Center-gai, near Kintetsu Nara Station)
seats_text: Approximately 44 seats: 6 counter seats and 38 table seats, including a private room that can hold around 20.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:00 (some sources list L.O. 14:30/close 15:00); Dinner 17:00–21:00 (L.O. 20:30–20:40). Closed Thursdays, with occasional irregular closures.
closed_days: Thursdays (occasional additional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A cozy, locally-run yoshoku diner where a little Japanese will help, as menus and service are geared toward Japanese regulars; point at dishes or ask a staff member if you're unsure.
caveats: Cash only — no cards. Closed Thursdays plus occasional irregular days, so confirm before a special trip. Lunch closing time varies between listings (14:00 to 15:00). Not suitable for vegan, vegetarian, or gluten-free diets.

bio.origin: Koriyama (郡山), before relocating to central Nara
bio.background: A long-established, family-run yoshoku restaurant that originally operated in Koriyama before moving to the Mochiidono shopping arcade near Kintetsu Nara Station. The dining room is built in a warm mountain-lodge style, giving it a calm, unhurried atmosphere where regulars settle in for a leisurely meal.
bio.specialty: Hand-cut filet steak grilled to a tender, juicy medium-rare; hamburg steak and beef/tongue stew rich with house-made demi-glace; beef cutlet (bifkatsu) topped with demi-glace; and duck confit offered as an alternative to the fish course. Lunches come with a rotating bread service and coffee.
bio.philosophy: Everything is made by hand, with care given even to the small accompaniments — down to mustard-dressed macaroni that comes with takeout orders — reflecting a commitment to warm, traditional, from-scratch Western home cooking using seasonal ingredients.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant moved from Koriyama to its current spot in the historic Mochiidono Center-gai arcade, trading a suburban location for the heart of old Nara.
Even small details show the kitchen's care — the meat is grilled to a tender, juicy medium-rare and bread is offered in three varieties at lunch, finished with coffee.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu centers on flour-based demi-glace sauce, breaded beef cutlet, hamburg steak, and bread service — pervasive wheat throughout the kitchen with no gluten-free handling noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A meat-focused kitchen (beef steaks, hamburg, beef/tongue stew, duck confit) with meat-based demi-glace; no vegan preparation or separation indicated.

cuisine: Japanese home-style izakaya (おばんざい / local sake)
gf_detail: This is a Japanese home-style izakaya whose dishes are built on Japanese soy sauce and carefully made dashi (fish/bonito-based broth), both of which typically contain wheat. Signature plates such as greens-and-fried-tofu stew and simmered Yamato pork rely on these seasonings. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no indication of celiac-safe handling. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance; a celiac traveler could not be confident here.
vegan_detail: The menu centers on Yamato pork and homemade dishes simmered in fish/bonito dashi, so most items are not vegan. A few vegetable- or tofu-based dishes exist (e.g., greens-and-fried-tofu stew), but these are almost certainly cooked with fish dashi and soy seasoning rather than prepared vegan. No vegan menu is documented; a vegan would need to ask carefully and options would be very limited.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市餅飯殿町41番地 OKビル2階
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; first feature returned [135.82869, 34.680351], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 20 seats (counter and tables); can seat up to about 25 for events
hours_raw: 17:00-22:00 (Tue-Sun and holidays); closed Mondays. (One review platform lists Wed-Sun open / Mon-Tue closed, but the official listing states closed Mondays only.)
closed_days: Mondays
neighborhood: 餅飯殿町 / もちいどのセンター街 (near Kintetsu Nara Station)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, locally loved husband-and-wife izakaya where ordering and conversation happen in Japanese, so a foreign student will be most comfortable with some basic Japanese or a translation app and an easygoing attitude.
caveats: Hours/closed days differ slightly between listings: Tabelog implies closed Monday-Tuesday, while the official Facebook-linked listing and HotPepper state open Tuesday-Sunday and holidays, closed Mondays only. The Mon-only closure (per the official source) is reflected here; confirm directly before visiting. Dashi and soy-based seasoning are used throughout, so this is not a GF or reliably vegan destination.

bio.background: Tucked into a small building along the Mochiidono center shopping street just off the busy lanes near Kintetsu Nara Station, this is an intimate little izakaya run by a husband-and-wife team. Stepping in feels less like entering a restaurant and more like being welcomed into the couple's own home, with regulars of all ages settling in along the counter.
bio.specialty: Local Nara sake is the heart of the place: the drink list is devoted exclusively to sake brewed within Nara Prefecture, and the owners help guests choose pours to match their taste. The kitchen turns out homey, carefully made dishes including a greens-and-fried-tofu stew, Kansai-style simmered Yamato pork and tofu, and Yamato pork chashu.
bio.philosophy: Everything is homemade, with attention paid to building good dashi, and the focus stays on showcasing Nara's own sake and local ingredients in a warm, unpretentious setting.

anecdotes.text: The couple keep the sake list limited to Nara-only breweries and guide guests toward bottles suited to their preferences, making the local-sake tasting a centerpiece of a visit.
The room is described as having a warm, at-home atmosphere drawing in neighborhood regulars across generations.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Home-style izakaya dishes built on soy sauce and dashi; no gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergy handling is documented, so cross-contamination cannot be ruled out.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese soy sauce and simmered/braised preparations (e.g., simmered Yamato pork dishes) are used; regular Japanese soy sauce contains wheat and no wheat-free tamari is advertised.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen emphasizes carefully made dashi (typically fish/bonito based) used across dishes, so even vegetable and tofu items are likely to contain animal-derived broth.

cuisine: Showa-era pure kissaten (traditional Japanese coffee house) — morning sets, honey toast, omelette rice, homemade juices and desserts
neighborhood: Takama-cho, a one-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station on the north side of Sanjo-dori / near Higashimuki shopping street
vegan_detail: This is a classic coffee house centered on toast, sandwiches, omelette rice, pasta, curry and dairy-based floats and parfaits, so there is no documented dedicated vegan menu. A black coffee or tea and possibly a fresh fruit juice (e.g. banana juice, which typically contains milk here) may be the only plant-leaning items, and even those are not confirmed dairy-free. A vegan should ask directly about each item before ordering.
gf_detail: The signature offerings are wheat-based — honey toast, hot dogs, sandwiches and pasta — and other dishes such as omelette rice and curry typically rely on wheat-containing sauces and broths. There is no documented gluten-free preparation or awareness, so this is not a safe choice for celiacs.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高天町48
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the building-level address 奈良県奈良市高天町48; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Spacious interior with sofa seating; total approximately 50 seats per the official listing.
closed_days: None (open year-round)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed retro coffee house where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way; menus are in Japanese and staff are unlikely to speak much English, but the simple morning-set format makes pointing and ordering easy.
caveats: Tabelog lists hours as "from 8:00" without a closing time, while the official listing and a local food site give 8:00-18:00; the 18:00 close is used here. One local write-up notes the shop was renovated after the previous owner retired, while the official page carries no history; the heritage details are reported from a single secondary source.

bio.background: Chambre is a long-standing pure kissaten just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station, in business since well within living memory of its present proprietor. After the previous owner retired, the shop was renovated while deliberately keeping its Showa-era retro mood — soft sofa seating, newspapers to linger over, and an unhurried atmosphere that invites visitors to rest before or after sightseeing near Todai-ji.
bio.specialty: Best known for its morning sets, including a honey toast morning and an egg-filled hot dog, alongside a fondly regarded house pudding and an omelette rice with generous chicken and egg that diners can have with ketchup or demi-glace. The menu rounds out with sandwiches, curry, pasta, pilaf, daily lunch specials, and old-school desserts like cream soda floats, parfaits and shaved ice.
bio.philosophy: Preserving the feel of a classic neighborhood coffee house — a calm, welcoming room where locals and travelers can take their time — while updating the space for a new generation.

anecdotes.text: The proprietor describes the cafe as having existed since their childhood; when the former owner retired, the shop was renovated while preserving its Showa-retro atmosphere.
The honey toast is toasted until fragrant and the omelette rice is small but filling, with a choice of ketchup or demi-glace sauce.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A wheat-forward coffee house (toast, hot dogs, sandwiches, pasta) with no documented gluten-free handling; cross-contamination from bread and flour-based dishes should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dairy and eggs are central to the menu (floats, parfaits, omelette rice, egg-filled hot dog), so shared equipment and dairy cross-contact are likely; no vegan-specific handling is documented.

cuisine: Kissaten and patisserie — macarons, fresh cakes, cream puffs, pudding, millefeuille, and coffee, with eat-in café space
gf_detail: This is a Western-pastry café where most items (cakes, millefeuille, cream puffs) are wheat-based and are made and handled alongside everything else, so there is no celiac-safe environment. The macarons are almond-flour meringues that contain no wheat in the batter, but they are produced and stored in the same kitchen and vending case as wheat pastries, so cross-contamination is likely. No gluten-free menu or dedicated handling is documented.
vegan_detail: A French-style patisserie and coffee shop. The sweets rely on butter, eggs, and dairy cream (macarons, shortcakes, pudding, cream puffs), so they are not vegan. No vegan or plant-based options are documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向中町24
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the chō-level point for 奈良県奈良市東向中町; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: 東向商店街 (Higashimuki shopping street), about 1 minute on foot from Kintetsu Nara Station Exit 2
seats_text: Seat count not published; a small café space at the back of the patisserie, self-serve style where customers order at the counter and bring items to their table.
closed_days: No regular closed day listed
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, self-serve sweets café where you order cakes and drinks at the counter and carry them to your table; English is unlikely, so point at the display case or use a translation app.
caveats: Cash only. Seating is limited and informal at the back of the patisserie. There is no documented allergy accommodation, so this is not suitable for anyone needing strict gluten-free or vegan handling.

bio.background: Tucked into the Higashimuki shopping arcade a minute from Kintetsu Nara Station, Ichouya is the café side of the small patisserie Kozue. Colorful macarons and seasonal shortcakes fill the front display case for takeaway, while a quiet retro café space at the back lets you sit and enjoy them on the spot.
bio.origin: Central Nara, in the Higashimuki arcade
bio.specialty: Macarons are the signature — the shop's flagship is the Chocolat Noir macaron, made with Hokkaido butter and almond powder and Belgian chocolate for a deep, toasty cocoa flavor. Seasonal fresh cakes, millefeuille, cream puffs, pudding, and coffee round out the menu.

anecdotes.text: In late 2020 the shop installed an unusual macaron vending machine on the arcade — a local curiosity that dispenses about 11 flavors (including Chocolat Noir, pistachio, strawberry, and matcha) in clear cylindrical cases, sold as 2-piece boxes for 500 yen or 3-piece boxes for 750 yen.
The Chocolat Noir macaron is named as the signature item, made with Hokkaido butter, almond powder, and Belgian chocolate.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A Western patisserie producing wheat-based cakes, millefeuille, and cream puffs in the same kitchen; macaron batter is almond-flour based but shares the kitchen and the vending case with wheat pastries, so cross-contamination is likely. No gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Sweets are made with butter, eggs, and dairy cream; no plant-based or vegan options are documented.

cuisine: Sushi / izakaya (sushi sakaba) — fresh and unusual fish, sashimi, chirashi, and small plates with sake and beer
neighborhood: JR Nara Station front — inside the Silkia Nara / Hotel Nikko Nara building, directly connected to JR Nara Station, near Sanjo-dori
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市三条本町8-1 シルキア奈良 2F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市三条本町8 (chō-block level) returned [135.818359, 34.681572], within the Nara box; matches JR Nara Station-front location of the Silkia Nara building.
seats_text: 27 seats — 17 at the counter, 10 at tables
closed_days: Mondays (occasional irregular closures noted)
cultural_comfort_note: A casual station-front sushi izakaya where pointing at the counter case or ordering a set lunch works well; a few words of Japanese go a long way, and being directly inside the JR Nara Station building makes it easy to find.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. As a sushi and izakaya, the kitchen relies on regular soy sauce (which contains wheat) for sushi, sashimi and dishes, and fried/battered items appear on the menu. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or cross-contamination controls are documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The menu centers on raw fish, seafood and egg, and broths/simmered dishes use fish dashi. No vegan or vegetarian dishes are documented; vegetable sides would still carry cross-contact and likely fish-based seasoning. Travelers seeking plant-based meals should look elsewhere.

bio.background: Tsuru is the second shop of Tsuru Sushi (鶴寿司), a sushi restaurant founded around 40 years ago in Tenri. This branch sits on the second floor of the Silkia Nara / Hotel Nikko Nara building, directly connected to JR Nara Station, where it serves both quick lunches and leisurely afternoon-and-evening drinking.
bio.specialty: Fresh and unusual fish: thick chirashi and seafood rice bowls, sashimi, plus izakaya plates such as beef-tendon stew (牛すじ煮込み), rock oysters (岩がき), and a mentaiko-and-cheese dashimaki omelet. A limited daily 'jewel box' (tamatebako) lunch heaps tuna, salmon, yellowtail, sea bream, squid, salmon roe, cucumber and egg over rice, served with tempura, chawanmushi and soup.
bio.philosophy: An everyday sushi sakaba meant to be enjoyed both as a fast lunch and as a relaxed spot for daytime or evening drinks over sashimi and small plates.

anecdotes.text: The Nara shop is the second location of Tsuru Sushi, a roughly 40-year-old sushi house from Tenri, opened in the JR Nara Station-front Silkia Nara building.
The interior is described as split roughly evenly between people eating quick lunches and people enjoying sashimi and appetizers with beer or sake, reflecting its daytime-drinking concept.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a sushi and izakaya, dishes are seasoned with standard soy sauce, which contains wheat; no wheat-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is fish- and seafood-centered, and simmered dishes and broths use fish dashi, so plant dishes are not free of animal-derived ingredients or cross-contact.

caveats: Two sources give slightly different hours (the Nara gourmet guide lists 11:30-14:00 and 16:00-22:30 with irregular closures; Tabelog lists Tue-Sat 11:30-14:00 and 16:30-22:00, Sun 16:30-21:00). hours_week follows Tabelog as the primary source. The official site (turu-nara.jp) was unreachable at verification time. No documented allergy, gluten-free, or vegan accommodations — dietary travelers should confirm directly.

cuisine: Hand-cut juwari (100% buckwheat) soba, stone-milled in-house from Ibaraki Hitachi-aki buckwheat
neighborhood: Shibatsuji-cho, north of Shin-Omiya Station (Kitamachi area, central Nara)
gf_detail: Despite serving juwari (100% buckwheat, tsunagi-free) soba, this is NOT a gluten-free or celiac-safe destination. The dipping broth (tsuyu) is made with wheat-based soy sauce, tempura and fried whitebait rice (on the lunch set) involve wheat batter and shared frying, and there is no dedicated gluten-free facility or documented allergy protocol. No gluten-free preparation is advertised.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The soba broth and accompanying dishes rely on fish-based dashi (and the menu features duck broth soba, tempura, and fried whitebait rice). No vegan options are documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市芝辻町4-1-4
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned the chō-level point for 奈良県奈良市芝辻町四丁目１番 at [135.811432, 34.687317], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Counter seating plus two large tables (roughly three tables); small intimate space, no private rooms. Exact seat count not documented.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 14:00), Dinner 17:15-20:00 (L.O. 20:00). Closes early when the soba sells out. Closed Tuesdays and Monday evenings.
closed_days: Closed Tuesdays and Monday evenings; occasional irregular closures. May close early when the day's soba runs out.
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, refined couple-run soba shop with a calm jazz-filled interior where solo diners are welcome, but menus and service are in Japanese, so a little preparation or a translation app helps a foreign student feel at ease.
caveats: 100% buckwheat soba is not the same as gluten-free: the broth uses wheat soy sauce and side dishes involve wheat, so this is unsafe for celiacs. Hours can end early if the soba sells out, and there are occasional irregular closures.

bio.background: そば切り 川名 is a small, couple-run soba house tucked into a beige building just north of Shin-Omiya Station in central Nara, near the Saho River. Open since the early 2000s, it has earned a quiet local following among Nara residents who consider it one of the city's best places for soba.
bio.specialty: Hand-cut juwari (100% buckwheat) soba ground in-house on a stone mill, served cold or warm. The owner builds his soba around the Hitachi-aki buckwheat variety from Ibaraki Prefecture, blending it with seasonal buckwheat through the year. Signature plates include spicy grated-daikon soba (karami daikon oroshi) and duck-broth soba (kamojiru soba), plus a lunch zen with tempura, soba tofu, salad, pickles and fried-whitebait rice. So-called soba-mae appetizers are offered for pairing with sake.
bio.philosophy: A commitment to pure tsunagi-free 100% buckwheat noodles that express the flavor of the soba itself, milled fresh in-house and matched to a Kanto-style dipping broth.

anecdotes.text: The polished counter and two large tables sit in a refined, understated room with jazz playing softly, making it comfortable enough for even a woman dining alone to stop in.
Locals describe it as the place to go for great soba in Nara, prized for the clean, refreshing texture of its stone-milled house-made 100% buckwheat noodles.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Although the soba is juwari (100% buckwheat with no wheat tsunagi), the dipping broth uses wheat-based soy sauce and the kitchen also prepares tempura and fried whitebait rice, so wheat is present and cross-contact is likely.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served Kanto-style with a soy-sauce-based dipping broth; standard Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu centers on fish-dashi-based soba broth, duck-broth soba, tempura and fried whitebait, so animal products are pervasive and vegan preparation is not indicated.

positives.text: Noodles are juwari (100% buckwheat, no wheat tsunagi binder), so the noodle itself contains no wheat.

cuisine: 老舗洋食 / Western-style (yoshoku) — wagyu hamburger, curry, hayashi rice, steak
neighborhood: 南京終町（ならまち南端・京終駅近く）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南京終町1-32-4
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned chō-level coordinates for 南京終町一丁目; within the Nara box.
seats_text: Tabelog lists 46 seats (counter and tables); the official site lists 48 seats including a private tatami room for 5-15 guests. Roughly 46-48 seats.
hours_raw: Tue-Sun 11:00-22:00 (Tabelog). Official site: Lunch 11:00-15:00, Dinner 17:00-21:00. Closed Mondays (open if a holiday; closed the following Tuesday).
closed_days: Mondays (open on public holidays, but closed the following Tuesday instead)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed local Western-style diner where pointing at the picture menu works fine, but staff likely speak little English, so a translation app or a Japanese-speaking friend will smooth ordering and any allergy questions.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a classic yoshoku restaurant built around wheat-heavy dishes — breaded beef cutlets, hamburger steak with demi-glace (a flour-based sauce), curry and hayashi roux (wheat-thickened), gratin, doria, sandwiches and okonomiyaki. No gluten-free menu or accommodations are documented, and cross-contamination with flour and breading is essentially unavoidable. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. The kitchen centers on Kagoshima/black wagyu beef — steaks, hamburger, beef curry and hayashi rice — alongside chicken and dairy-rich dishes (cheese doria, gratin). Sauces and soups rely on meat stock. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.

bio.background: Meirinkan opened in 1987, beginning life as a coffee shop (kissaten) before growing into the beloved Western-style restaurant it is today. It occupies a striking Meiji-era-style Western building with high ceilings that locals say feels like stepping into an old schoolhouse, giving the place a nostalgic, refined atmosphere a short walk from Kyobate Station on the southern edge of Naramachi.
bio.philosophy: Everything is made by hand: the kitchen states that frozen foods, retort (pre-packaged) products and chemical seasonings are not used at all, with each dish prepared carefully from scratch.
bio.specialty: Black wagyu is the star — a black wagyu loin steak cutlet lightly breaded and finished with a mild house curry-flavored sauce, a juicy black wagyu hamburger (choose tartare, white, or demi-glace sauce), and slow-simmered black wagyu curry whose meat is described as melting after long cooking. Hayashi rice made with onions and black wagyu, omurice, and a chicken doria baked with Danish cheese and Daisen milk round out a wide menu that runs from sandwiches to premium steaks.

anecdotes.text: The dining room sits inside a Meiji-era Western-style building with soaring ceilings that evoke an old school, lending the meal a sense of old-fashioned grandeur.
The half-and-half plate pairing hayashi rice with curry (or with the hamburger) is a popular way to taste two of the house's wagyu specialties at once.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by breaded cutlets, flour-based demi-glace and curry/hayashi roux, gratin and doria — wheat flour is used throughout the kitchen, making cross-contamination unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Beef and meat stock are central to nearly every dish (steaks, hamburger, beef curry, hayashi); no plant-based preparation is documented.

positives.text: The kitchen does not use frozen foods, retort products, or chemical seasonings — everything is made by hand from scratch.

caveats: Hours differ between sources: Tabelog shows a continuous 11:00-22:00, while the official site shows a split 11:00-15:00 lunch and 17:00-21:00 dinner; the split hours from the official site were used. Seat count is ~46 (Tabelog) to 48 (official). Closed Mondays, with an irregular holiday-shift rule. Under-3000-yen status is uncertain: lunch sets are affordable but steaks exceed 5,000 yen.

cuisine: Japanese-style Western (yoshoku) — omelette rice, mushroom-sauce hamburger steak, fried dishes
neighborhood: Sugigacho, near JR Nara Station
gf_detail: This is a Japanese-style Western (yoshoku) eatery built around breaded-and-fried cutlets (tonkatsu, shrimp fry, menchi katsu), hamburger steak, omelette rice and demi-glace/tomato/mushroom sauces — all of which typically contain wheat (flour, breadcrumbs, roux) and soy. No gluten-free menu, dedicated fryer, or celiac accommodation is documented. Not suitable for a gluten-free or celiac diner.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-fish-forward Western diner: signature dishes are hamburger steak, pork tonkatsu, shrimp and oyster fry, seasonal fish, and egg-based omelette rice. Sauces and sides rely on egg, dairy and meat/fish stock. No vegan or vegetarian options are documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市杉ヶ町12-4 第2西田ビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 奈良県奈良市杉ヶ町12; within the central Nara bounding box.
seats_text: About 28 seats (capacity up to 30), including uncommon-for-yoshoku flooring tatami seating that suits families.
hours_raw: 11:30-14:00, 18:00-21:00; closed Wednesdays. Omelette rice and chicken doria are offered on weekends only.
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A casual neighborhood Western-food diner where ordering is simple by pointing at set-lunch items, but staff likely speak little English, so a foreign student should come with a translation app or a Japanese friend.

bio.background: A small, reasonably priced family-style Western (yoshoku) diner tucked into the Sugigacho district a short walk from JR Nara Station. Unusually for a Western restaurant, it offers flooring-style tatami seating, which makes it comfortable for families and longer, relaxed meals.
bio.specialty: Mushroom-sauce hamburger steak and omelette rice are the signatures, alongside a rotating set of about five standard lunches and nine daily specials — including tonkatsu made with Yamato pork, shrimp fry, and chicken cutlet. Dinner adds small plates such as a potato-and-bacon galette and party courses. Omelette rice and chicken doria appear on weekends only.

anecdotes.text: The hamburger set is served with a choice of Japanese-style sauce or mushroom sauce and comes with sweet-potato chips on the side; shrimp fry is served with tartar sauce and the chicken cutlet with tomato sauce.
An overall reasonable price point and family-friendly flooring tatami seating, uncommon for a Western-style restaurant.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built around breaded fried cutlets, roux-based and demi-glace sauces, and omelette rice, all of which routinely contain wheat and share kitchen/frying equipment; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Egg, dairy, meat and fish are core to nearly every dish (hamburger, tonkatsu, omelette rice, fried seafood, seasonal fish); no vegan preparation or separation is documented.

caveats: No official website was found; details drawn from the Tabelog listing and a Nara local-gourmet blog. No chef name or founding story is documented. Allergy/cross-contamination handling is not addressed by any source, so GF and vegan diners should treat this as unsuitable. Card is accepted but electronic money is not.

cuisine: Spice curry (古民家 old-house style, made with dashi, salt and fermentation; no wheat flour, no chemical seasonings)
gf_detail: Unusually promising for a curry shop: the kitchen explicitly does not use wheat flour (小麦粉不使用) — most Japanese curry roux is thickened with wheat, so this avoids the single biggest gluten source in curry. However, it is not a dedicated gluten-free kitchen and is heavily dashi- and fermentation-based, so fermented seasonings (e.g. miso, sake lees/酒粕, and possibly wheat-containing soy sauce) may be present. No public information confirms gluten-free soy sauce or cross-contamination controls. A celiac should ask in person about soy sauce and fermented ingredients before ordering.
vegan_detail: The single daily plate ("本日のスパイスカレー") presents three rotating curries plus three sides on one plate, and the rotation usually includes meat or seafood (e.g. chicken-and-sake-lees curry, octopus/squid curry, chicken dashi biryani) and a bean/vegetable curry (豆カレー). The foundation is dashi (出汁), which in most Japanese kitchens is fish/bonito-based, so the plate as served is generally not vegan. A vegan might be able to eat the bean/vegetable component but should ask whether the dashi and sides are animal-free; full vegan service is not advertised.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町1232
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良市法蓮町1232; within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: きたまち (Kitamachi) — Horencho, north of Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: Small old-house seating: 2 counter seats, three 2-person low tables and two single low tables (roughly 9-10 seats total).
hours_raw: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); lunch only. Local sources note 11:00-15:00 opening on some days. 不定休 (irregular closures) — check Instagram for current dates.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays) — operating days announced on Instagram
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny family-run old-house curry spot with one daily set and irregular opening days — friendly and casual, but English is unlikely, so a little Japanese (or a translation app) and checking their Instagram for open days will make your visit smooth.
caveats: Lunch-only and closes by 15:00 (last order 14:30). Closures are irregular (不定休) — confirm the day is open via the shop's Instagram before walking the ~15 minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station. Menu is a single rotating plate; some sources list 11:00 opening, so arrive within the 11:30-15:00 window to be safe. Wheat-flour-free is a stated feature but the kitchen is not a dedicated allergen-free facility.

bio.background: Sarumame Curry opened on March 26, 2022 in a converted old wooden townhouse (古民家) in Nara's Kitamachi district, in the same long house that also holds the medicinal-cuisine eatery 藍布 (Rampu) and the sweets shop ちてはこ. Before settling into this permanent storefront, the shop built its following the slow way — cooking at events and through borrowed-kitchen (間借り) pop-ups, where it was already very popular.
bio.specialty: A single daily plate, the 本日のスパイスカレー (today's spice curry): three rotating curries, three side dishes and rice on one plate, with the freedom to mix and match. The rotation is wide-ranging — chicken-and-sake-lees curry, octopus-and-squid hot curry, bean curry, coconut curries, and a chicken dashi biryani have all appeared. A spiced soft-boiled egg is a popular add-on topping.
bio.philosophy: Their guiding idea is that curry should be 'おいしくて、うれしくて、ちからになる' — delicious, joyful, and nourishing. To get there they build flavor from 出汁・塩・発酵 (dashi, salt and fermentation) rather than shortcuts, deliberately using no chemical seasonings and no wheat flour, letting good stock and aromatic spice blends carry the dish so the plate stays interesting from first bite to last.

anecdotes.text: The shop earned its reputation as a roving event and pop-up curry maker before it ever had a fixed address, and was already a known name when the Kitamachi storefront opened.
It sits in a shared old-house terrace alongside the medicinal-cuisine spot 藍布 and sweets shop ちてはこ, a quiet cluster about a 15-minute walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station.

positives.text: The kitchen states it uses no wheat flour (小麦粉不使用) and no chemical seasonings — notable because conventional Japanese curry is thickened with wheat-based roux.
A bean/vegetable curry is regularly part of the rotating three-curry plate, giving plant-based diners at least one component to ask about.

cuisine: 日本料理・会席（カウンター懐石）
neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約4分）
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a traditional Japanese kaiseki restaurant where dashi and seasonings typically rely on soy sauce (which usually contains wheat), and dishes such as charcoal-grilled saba-zushi and seasonal platters are not designed to be gluten-free. A celiac diner could not assume safety here; any wheat avoidance would need to be discussed directly when booking the fully reservation-based course.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The chef sources nearly all vegetables locally from Nara, but the kaiseki course is built around fish (the chef personally procures fish in Osaka) and almost certainly fish-based dashi, so the standard course is not vegan. Because it is an omakase-style, fully reserved counter, plant-forward accommodation might be possible only by arrangement in advance — it is not offered as a set vegan menu.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市鍋屋町3
geocode_note: GSI address search returned the first feature for 奈良県奈良市鍋屋町３番地; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 15席（カウンター9席、テーブル6席）
closed_days: 月曜・日曜
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, fully-reservation-only counter where the chef serves an omakase course one dish at a time, so a foreign student should be comfortable with Japanese (or come with someone who is) and be punctual, as a reservation is cancelled if you cannot be reached five minutes past your time.

bio.background: 辻調理師専門学校を卒業後、奈良の老舗料亭「菊水楼」で研鑽を積み、京都祇園の割烹「祇園 西川」で西川正芳氏のもと次席（二番手）を務めた。2016年、33歳の時に奈良・ならまちに『而今』を開いた。
bio.specialty: 切ってから時間が経つほど味が落ちるという考えから仕込みを最小限にし、一番美味しいタイミングで一品ずつ供するカウンター懐石。八寸や炭火で仕上げる鯖寿司が看板。火床には三重県産の紀州備長炭を使う。
bio.philosophy: 「料理は出す瞬間が一番大事」という鮮度第一の信条。事前の作り置きを避け、目の前でお客の反応を見ながら最適なタイミングで仕上げることを重視する。多店舗展開はせず、一軒を深めて奈良の食材を究めることを選んでいる。

anecdotes.text: 店名『而今』は禅の言葉で「今この瞬間」を意味し、料理を最良のタイミングで供するという店の姿勢を表している。
野菜はほぼ奈良産でまかない、魚は店主自らが大阪の市場に足を運んで仕入れる。

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Japanese kaiseki kitchen, dishes are seasoned in the classic style, which typically includes soy sauce; soy sauce normally contains wheat, so dishes should be treated as containing gluten unless confirmed otherwise.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The course centers on fish (the chef personally buys fish in Osaka) and traditional dashi, so vegetable dishes share a fish-based kitchen and are not prepared as vegan.

positives.text: Nearly all vegetables are sourced locally from Nara, so produce is fresh and seasonal.

caveats: Chef name spelling: the target listed 清水庄治郎, but Tabelog and the chef interview consistently give 清水唱二郎 (Shimizu Shojiro); used the sourced spelling. No standalone official website was found (Instagram @nara_nikonn exists); official_url left blank. Fully reservation-based, course-only fine dining at roughly ¥20,000+, so under30 is no. GF/vegan details are inferred from the kaiseki/fish-dashi style, not from an explicit allergen statement, hence safety confidence is low and both dietary statuses are 'ask'.

cuisine: 個人経営の喫茶店（手作りスイーツ・スペシャルティコーヒー・紅茶、軽食セット）
neighborhood: きたまち（近鉄奈良駅北側）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市坊屋敷町36-2 今西ビル1F
geocode_note: GSI address-search returned [135.827118, 34.686672] for 奈良市坊屋敷町36; within Nara box.
seats_text: 座席数の記載は確認できず（小規模な個人経営の喫茶店）
closed_days: 日曜・第1・第2月曜
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional local kissaten where Japanese is the working language and an English menu is unlikely; pointing and a friendly konnichiwa go a long way, so it suits a student comfortable with a little Japanese.
vegan_detail: No vegan claims are documented. This is a coffee-and-cake kissaten; baked sweets like hotcakes and French toast use eggs and dairy, and there is no stated vegan menu. A vegan would need to ask item by item; assume nothing is vegan by default.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment. The signature okara (soy-pulp) cake notably uses okara in place of wheat flour, but other menu items (hotcakes, caramelized French toast) are wheat-based and there is no documented gluten-free protocol, so this is not celiac-safe. A celiac traveler should treat the kitchen as having heavy wheat cross-contact and verify the okara cake's full ingredients directly.
caveats: No dedicated Tabelog page or official website was found; identity, address, hours, and the okara-cake detail are confirmed via the cotrip travel guide and the GSI geocoder. Hours/closed days may vary; phone listed as 0742-26-5551. Cash-only and seat count not explicitly stated and should be confirmed on arrival.

bio.background: Kissa Baobab is a small, independently run café tucked into the Kitamachi area just north of Kintetsu Nara Station, in the ground floor of the Imamishi Building on Boyashiki-cho. It is the kind of warm, unhurried neighborhood kissaten where guests are encouraged to settle in and pass time at their own pace amid simple wooden tables.
bio.specialty: Handmade sweets paired with specialty coffee or fragrant tea: a signature okara cake that swaps wheat flour for soy pulp, a heart-shaped plain hotcake served as a set with deep-roasted coffee, and caramelized French toast.
bio.philosophy: A relaxed, homey café focused on gentle-flavored handmade sweets and carefully brewed coffee and tea, offering a quiet, generous slice of time.

anecdotes.text: An okara (soy-pulp) cake is offered as part of an afternoon snack set with coffee or tea, a wheat-flour-free take on a homemade cake that has become one of the café's small signatures.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based items such as hotcakes and caramelized French toast are core menu items, so a shared kitchen with heavy wheat handling should be assumed; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Baked sweets here typically rely on eggs and dairy and no vegan or plant-based preparation is documented, so cross-contact with animal products should be assumed.

positives.text: The signature okara cake is made with soy pulp in place of wheat flour, making it a notably lighter, wheat-free sweet option to ask about (confirm full ingredients on site).

lead: 珈琲風雅（プロヴァンス）
name_ja: 珈琲風雅 ぷろばんす
cuisine: Japanese kissaten (retro coffee house)
gf_detail: This is a Showa-era coffee house whose food menu is built around wheat: homemade bread, French toast, sandwiches and spaghetti. There is no gluten-free menu, no mention of celiac accommodation, and the kitchen is not set up for gluten avoidance. Not suitable for those who must avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: A traditional coffee shop with no vegan offerings documented. Food is dairy- and egg-centric (French toast, cakes, whipped cream, milk-based coffee drinks, bread). Black coffee is the only reliably plant-based item, and there is no documentation of plant milk or vegan food. Vegans should ask, but expect very limited options.
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市柏木町541-1 returned [135.799698, 34.674328], within the Nara box. Location is in Kashiwagi-cho, west-central Nara city off Route 24, between Shin-Omiya Station and the Heijo Palace area — not strictly kitamachi but central Nara.
seats_text: Roughly 60 seats across counter and tables (sources cite 50–60), with banquet capacity to ~70 standing.
hours_raw: Official site lists daily 8:30–17:00 (last order 16:30) with irregular closures. Other listings cite 8:00–18:00 or 8:00–20:00; hours appear to vary, so confirm before visiting. Morning service in the early hours; lunch sets from around noon.
closed_days: Irregular closures (no fixed weekly holiday).
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, old-school neighborhood coffee house where service is in Japanese; a few polite words and pointing at the menu will carry you through comfortably.
neighborhood: Kashiwagi-cho, west-central Nara (off Route 24, near Shin-Omiya)
caveats: Reported hours differ across sources (8:30–17:00 on the official site vs. 8:00–18:00 / 8:00–20:00 elsewhere) and closures are irregular — confirm the day before visiting. Cash assumed as no card/electronic/QR payment is accepted per listings.

bio.background: A retro Showa-style coffee house in Nara city that frames itself around the long history of the ancient capital, pairing serious coffee with a hand-made Japanese garden you can gaze at while you linger.
bio.specialty: Coffee brewed by an unusual 'light siphon' method that uses a halogen lamp instead of the traditional alcohol or gas flame for steady heat and a mellow cup; the menu runs to roughly 40 coffees, including house blends called Rokkasen (六歌仙) and single-origins called Junika-sen (十二珈撰). Food highlights are French toast and sandwiches on house-baked bread, lunch sets with spaghetti and salad, and homemade cakes and coffee jelly.
bio.philosophy: The shop describes itself as a place to taste the 1,300 years of the ancient capital, prizing a timeless, unhurried authenticity over passing trends.

anecdotes.text: The house brews coffee with a 'light siphon' that swaps the usual flame for a halogen lamp, giving more stable heat and a rounder flavor.
A hand-built Japanese garden, which the shop calls among the finest in the area, can be admired from the seats while you take your time.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is wheat-heavy (house-baked bread, French toast, sandwiches, spaghetti); no gluten-free handling is documented, so cross-contamination should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Food and drinks lean on dairy and eggs (French toast, cakes, whipped cream, milk-based coffees); no plant-based preparation is documented.

positives.text: Plain black coffee from a serious roaster-style program (around 40 varieties) is available and is naturally free of gluten and animal products.

cuisine: Plant-based gluten-free vegan sweets (cookies, canelé, baked goods, amazake)
gf_detail: Dedicated gluten-free shop: all products are made without wheat flour, using rice flour (Nara Hinohikari variety) instead. Note the kitchen also handles almonds and soybeans, and the official site states the facility handles specified allergens, so it is not a fully allergen-isolated operation — celiac travelers should confirm directly.
vegan_detail: Fully vegan. All sweets are 100% plant-based with no eggs, no dairy, and no refined white sugar. Even the canelé (traditionally made with eggs, milk and butter) is made entirely from plant ingredients.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西大寺東町2-1-4
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良市西大寺東町2-1-4, within the Nara bounding box, near Kintetsu Yamato-Saidaiji Station.
neighborhood: Saidaiji Higashi-machi, near Kintetsu Yamato-Saidaiji Station North Exit
seats_text: Takeaway/retail sweets shop; no seating information published (primarily for buying baked goods to take away, with a pink door as its landmark).
hours_raw: Wed–Sat 11:00–17:30; closed Sun, Mon, Tue.
closed_days: Sunday, Monday, Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny independent sweets shop where a friendly greeting in Japanese goes a long way; products are clearly labeled gluten-free and vegan, so even with limited Japanese you can browse and buy comfortably.
caveats: This is a retail/takeaway sweets shop rather than a sit-down eatery, so it suits picking up gluten-free vegan treats rather than a meal. While fully gluten-free and vegan, the kitchen handles almonds and soybeans and the facility processes specified allergens, so it is not an allergen-isolated environment for severe celiac or nut allergies.

bio.background: laccola is a small, independent gluten-free and vegan sweets shop that opened in July 2022, one minute on foot from the north exit of Kintetsu Yamato-Saidaiji Station on the western side of central Nara. It is marked by a distinctive pink door.
bio.specialty: Plant-based baked goods made without wheat, eggs, dairy or refined white sugar. The best-selling item is the salt-vanilla cookie made with Nara-grown Hinohikari rice flour, alongside a signature vegan canelé (baked in traditional copper molds with a crisp exterior and smooth interior) in flavors such as plain, citrus peel, cinnamon, dark chocolate and Yamato tea. Other offerings include pistachio & cranberry and lemon-mint cookies, cookies using Yamato angelica root, and a line of sugar-free flavored amazake.
bio.philosophy: The shop wants sweets that everyone can genuinely enjoy as delicious together, treating plant-based ingredients as a positive choice for flavor rather than a restrictive substitute. The name 'ラッコラ' is a coined word inspired by 'dakko' (a hug), expressing the wish that its sweets give people a warm, embraced feeling. They use locally and carefully sourced ingredients (Nara rice flour, Tanegashima sugar, Hokkaido soybeans) and no artificial flavors, colors or preservatives.

anecdotes.text: The shop opened on July 29, 2022, a one-minute walk from the Yamato-Saidaiji Station north exit, founded on the wish to make gluten-free vegan sweets that anyone can enjoy.
Their vegan canelé recreates a classically egg-, milk- and butter-based pastry entirely from plant ingredients, baked in copper molds and frozen on the day of baking to preserve texture.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Entirely wheat-free shop using rice flour, but the official site states the manufacturing facility handles foods containing specified allergens, so it is gluten-free by recipe rather than a certified allergen-isolated kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All products are fully vegan (no eggs, dairy or animal products), so there is no animal-ingredient line to cross-contaminate with.

positives.text: 100% gluten-free and 100% vegan shop: every item is made without wheat, eggs, dairy or refined white sugar, so the entire menu is safe for both vegan and gluten-avoiding diners.
Uses Nara-grown Hinohikari rice flour in place of wheat and avoids artificial flavors, colorings and preservatives.
Products typically contain almonds and soybeans, which is disclosed on the shop's listings.

cuisine: Old-house organic cafe — body-friendly lunch sets built around brown/black rice and seasonal Yamato vegetables, plus homemade low-sugar sweets, in a converted Meiji-era rice warehouse
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is advertised. The signature lunch is rice-based (brown, black, and enzyme-fermented rice) which is naturally wheat-free, but the kitchen also serves tarts, chiffon cake, and other wheat-flour sweets, and Japanese seasonings such as soy sauce typically contain wheat. There is no stated allergen handling or dedicated preparation, so a celiac diner would need to call ahead and confirm each dish. Not celiac-safe by default.
vegan_detail: The cuisine is vegetable-forward and "body-friendly," with abundant root and seasonal vegetables, but it is an omnivore cafe: lunch sets include a meat or fish protein and soups likely use fish dashi, while sweets use homemade ice cream and dairy. There is no documented fully vegan set. A vegetarian or vegan diner should phone ahead to ask what can be prepared without animal products.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市椿井町45
geocode_note: GSI address search returned a single feature at [135.828125, 34.681065], within the central Nara box; matches the Tsubakii-cho / Naramachi location described by sources.
neighborhood: Naramachi (Tsubakii-cho, near Tsubakii Elementary School, west side of Mochiidono shopping arcade); about 5 minutes' walk south from Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: Approximately 20 seats across a ground floor, a spacious second-floor tatami area, a loft with sunken kotatsu seating, and open terrace seating
hours_raw: Lunch & cafe 11:00–19:00; evening service is irregular. Tabelog lists 11:00–22:00. Closed Tuesdays.
closed_days: Tuesdays (evening service hours are irregular)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a quiet, family-run Japanese cafe where you will likely need basic Japanese to order and ask about ingredients, so bring a translation app and call ahead if you have dietary needs.
caveats: Two sources give different closing times (official site 19:00 vs Tabelog 22:00) and the evening service is described as irregular, so confirm hours before a late visit. Vegan and gluten-free needs are not formally accommodated and require a phone call in advance.

bio.background: Niko-chan-do opened in 2006 inside a roughly 160-year-old former rice warehouse built in the Meiji era, in the Naramachi district a few minutes' walk south of Kintetsu Nara Station. The owner restored the warehouse into a one-family cafe, keeping the high ceilings and adding a second-floor tatami room, a loft with a sunken kotatsu, a gallery, a studio, and a terrace. A large elephant painted on the tin roof has become the building's landmark.
bio.specialty: The signature 'nico lunch' is a body-friendly set built around brown rice, black rice, and enzyme-fermented rice with abundant root and seasonal vegetables, a meat or fish side, and soup. The kitchen is also known for homemade, low-sugar sweets — ice cream, tarts, and chiffon cake — and an afternoon tea set, with coffee roasted from beans sourced via a Nara/Ikoma roaster.
bio.philosophy: The cafe centers on vegetable-forward, 'body-friendly' cooking that values the natural taste of quality ingredients (素材の味を大切にした), using restrained sweetness in its desserts so the ingredients themselves come through.

anecdotes.text: The cafe occupies a converted Meiji-era rice warehouse whose retro interior — high ceilings, a loft, and a terrace — is meant to be a relaxing space where visitors won't want to leave.
It is family-friendly, providing nursing and diaper-changing facilities and asking families with young children to reserve by phone.

positives.text: Lunch sets are built around brown, black, and enzyme-fermented rice with plentiful seasonal and root vegetables, so a naturally rice-and-vegetable-based meal is the house style.
All sweets are homemade in-house with reduced sugar, emphasizing the natural taste of the ingredients.

cuisine: 懐石・割烹（季節の野菜を中心とした大和料理。名物は酒粕の天ぷら・豆腐の味噌漬け、奈良名物の茶粥）
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. Signature dish is sake-lees tempura (酒粕の天ぷら — wheat batter), and summer menus feature sōmen (wheat) noodles. Cooking is built on fish/kombu dashi and soy-based seasonings. No documented gluten-free protocol, dedicated fryer, or wheat-free menu. Celiac travelers should treat it as unsafe.
vegan_detail: The kitchen is vegetable-centric and the official site explicitly advertises vegetarian guidance, so plant-forward meals are possible with advance reservation, but the default kaiseki uses fish dashi and includes fish/egg dishes; vegans must call ahead to confirm.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市下御門町38 御門ビル2階
geocode_note: GSI address search on 奈良県奈良市下御門町38 returned [135.829269, 34.679035], within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: ならまち（もちいどの・下御門商店街2階／近鉄奈良駅 徒歩約8分）
seats_text: 20 seats — counter 6, tables 14 (two 4-person tables and one shared 6-person table). Private use possible for groups up to 20.
closed_days: Thursdays (with occasional irregular closures / 不定休)
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, traditional family-run kaiseki spot where the proprietor cooks and serves personally; phone ahead in basic Japanese to reserve and state your menu choice, and be comfortable in a calm, mostly Japanese-speaking setting.
caveats: Owner/chef name not documented on any fetched source. Vegetarian/vegan accommodation advertised but not detailed — arrange by advance phone reservation. Thursday closure regular but occasional irregular closures apply. Cash-only (no cards/e-money/QR).

bio.background: A small, independent kaiseki house in Nara's Naramachi district run by its proprietor for more than 30 years, on the second floor of a building along the Shimo-mikado arcade a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station.
bio.specialty: Vegetable-forward seasonal kaiseki at an approachable price. Two signature dishes are sake-lees tempura and miso-marinated tofu. It is also one of the few places in Nara still serving the local specialty chagayu (tea-steeped rice porridge), offered as a set and as a swap for plain rice at breakfast.
bio.philosophy: Letting Nara's seasonal vegetables and local Yamato specialties shine; menus rotate with the seasons. The owner recently added a weekend breakfast in response to demand from nearby guesthouses.

anecdotes.text: A diner praised the smooth feel of the tea porridge and the careful knife work in the thoughtfully cut vegetables.
The breakfast was introduced in response to requests from nearby guesthouse guests wanting an early seasonal Nara meal.
Described as a calm, hidden upstairs space with no background music and traditional decor including armor displays and fresh flowers, with windows over Naramachi.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature dish is sake-lees tempura (wheat batter) and summer menus include somen (wheat noodles); cooking relies on soy-based seasonings and fish/kombu dashi. No gluten-free protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Standard kaiseki includes fish dishes (salted grilled mackerel) and dashi/egg; the kitchen is not vegan by default, so vegans must request plant-only preparation in advance.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The official site advertises guidance for vegetarian guests; reservations by phone with menu choice stated in advance are recommended, allowing accommodation to be discussed ahead.

positives.text: Vegetable-centric seasonal cooking and explicit willingness to provide vegetarian guidance, with advance phone reservation, make plant-forward meals feasible.

cuisine: 居酒屋・海鮮・炉ばた
neighborhood: 西大寺本町（大和西大寺駅周辺）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西大寺本町2-24
geocode_note: Geocode resolved to 奈良市西大寺本町2-24, lat 34.695053 / lng 135.781158 — inside the Nara box. NOTE: this is the Yamato-Saidaiji area in western Nara, roughly 2 min from Yamato-Saidaiji Station, NOT 近鉄奈良駅周辺 or ならまち as the target brief stated. The stated area is incorrect.
seats_text: 78 seats total: counter 8, tables (4-6), plus tatami/sunken-hearth (掘りごたつ) banquet rooms seating up to 50.
hours_raw: 火～土・祝・祝前: 16:30-23:00（料理L.O.22:30 / ドリンクL.O.22:30）。日・月 定休。（公式サイト・ホットペッパー）。食べログには昼 11:30-14:00 の記載もあるが、公式とホットペッパーは夜のみ営業を掲載。
closed_days: 日曜・月曜（Sunday and Monday）
cultural_comfort_note: This is a local, Japanese-language seafood izakaya with no indication of English support, so a foreign student should be comfortable with basic Japanese or come with a Japanese-speaking friend.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. As a seafood izakaya, dishes rely heavily on Japanese soy sauce (contains wheat), fish dashi, and fried/grilled items in a shared kitchen; sashimi is served with wheat-based soy sauce. Not suitable for celiacs; no dedicated GF measures found.
vegan_detail: This is a seafood-centered izakaya whose whole concept is fresh fish bought daily by the ex-fishmonger owner. Menus revolve around sashimi, grilled fish, and hotpot; fish dashi and bonito stock are pervasive. No vegan offerings are documented.
caveats: Area mismatch: the brief listed 近鉄奈良駅周辺/ならまち, but the restaurant is actually in 西大寺本町 near Yamato-Saidaiji Station, western Nara — not central Nara/Naramachi. Also, although categorized MOM_AND_POP as a genuinely independent, locally loved owner-run seafood izakaya, it is larger than the typical <30-seat criterion (78 seats with banquet rooms). Hours: official site and HotPepper list dinner-only (16:30-23:00); an older Tabelog listing also shows a lunch slot (11:30-14:00) that the official channels do not confirm.

bio.background: 魚源 is a robatayaki seafood izakaya run by an owner who used to work as a fishmonger. Every morning he goes to the Nara market himself to hand-pick fresh, lively fish and shellfish, which he serves at honest, value-driven prices.
bio.specialty: The daily sashimi platter (お造り盛り合わせ), determined by that morning's catch, is the signature; other highlights include seasonal grilled fish such as wild sweetfish and live flounder, hotpot dishes, and a chef's-choice omakase course. The place also stocks dozens of regional sake and shochu from around Japan.
bio.philosophy: Captured by the shop's self-deprecating tagline, '字はまずいが、魚はうまい' (my handwriting is bad, but the fish is delicious) — the focus is entirely on letting the freshness and flavor of carefully sourced fish speak for itself.

anecdotes.text: The owner's former life as a fishmonger is the heart of the place — he personally goes to the Nara market each morning to choose the day's live fish and shellfish.
The shop's playful motto, '字はまずいが、魚はうまい' (the handwriting is poor, but the fish is excellent), sums up its no-frills, fish-first attitude.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Seafood izakaya relying on wheat-based soy sauce, fish dashi, and shared grilling/frying surfaces; no GF measures documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Sashimi and grilled-fish dishes are served with standard Japanese soy sauce, which contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Fish and shellfish are the core of the entire menu; fish dashi is pervasive, so cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

lead: くるみの木 一条店
name_ja: くるみの木 一条店
cuisine: Town-house cafe serving seasonal home-style (obanzai) lunch sets with Nara-grown vegetables, rice, miso soup; cakes and tea; attached goods/gift shop and gallery
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and not celiac-safe. The signature obanzai lunch is built around mixed-grain rice, miso soup and assorted vegetable side dishes seasoned with regular (wheat-based) soy sauce; the homemade hamburger uses binders and the dessert menu is cake- and tart-heavy. No dedicated gluten-free preparation, fryer, or allergen protocol is documented. Travelers with celiac disease should not rely on this venue.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The menu emphasizes home-style cooking with fish/bonito dashi in the miso soup and sides, and the lunch typically includes meat (homemade hamburger steak); cakes and cheesecakes use dairy and eggs. While there are vegetable-forward side dishes, full vegan meals are not offered and many seemingly plant-based dishes use fish dashi. Vegans would need to ask carefully and would have very limited options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町567-1
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 奈良県奈良市法蓮町567; within Nara box.
neighborhood: きたまち・法蓮町（一条通り、近鉄奈良駅北側／新大宮駅徒歩約11分）
seats_text: 44 seats, all tables
hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (L.O. food 15:00, drinks 16:30). Closed Wednesdays and the third Tuesday.
closed_days: Wednesdays and the third Tuesday of each month
cultural_comfort_note: A beloved, well-established local cafe where ordering and signage are mostly in Japanese, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps, but the relaxed atmosphere makes it welcoming to a foreign student.
caveats: The seasonal lunch is very popular and can sell out or involve a wait; reservations (taken from one month ahead by phone or in person) are strongly recommended, though walk-ins are possible. It is a bit of a walk from the nearest station (about 11 minutes from Shin-Omiya). Note the irregular closures: every Wednesday plus the third Tuesday.

bio.background: Founded more than 40 years ago by space coordinator and designer Ishimura Yukiko, Kurumi no Ki grew from a small cafe into one of the area's pioneering cafe-and-goods-shop spaces. It still occupies its original building north of Nara, with vintage wooden tables and chairs once used in a church chapel, surrounded by seasonal gardens and joined by a cake workshop, gift shop and gallery.
bio.specialty: A daily-changing seasonal lunch built around Nara-grown vegetables: an assortment of gentle home-style (obanzai) side dishes with mixed-grain rice, miso soup and a small dessert. A signature is the light homemade hamburger blended with hijiki, Chinese yam, carrot and tofu. The on-site workshop also turns out cakes such as lemon rare cheesecake and pineapple Victoria cake, plus homemade jam, ginger ale and apple juice.
bio.philosophy: The shop expresses a quiet philosophy of mindful, enriched everyday living, with carefully chosen tableware and goods meant to bring more care and pleasure to daily life. The kitchen follows the same idea, cooking with seasonal ingredients chosen as close to their peak as possible for food that is gentle on the body.

anecdotes.text: The lunch menu rotates every week or two to follow vegetables at their seasonal best, from new burdock and eggplant to okra, winter melon and peppers.
The dining room's wooden tables and chairs were salvaged from a church chapel, and the original building has been carefully maintained since the cafe opened over four decades ago.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The home-style side dishes and miso soup are seasoned in the traditional Japanese manner, which uses regular wheat-based soy sauce and miso; no gluten-free seasoning is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The miso soup and many vegetable sides rely on fish/bonito dashi, and the lunch typically includes a meat hamburger, so plant-based diners cannot assume vegetable dishes are animal-free.

positives.text: The kitchen cooks daily-changing meals around fresh Nara-grown seasonal vegetables, so vegetable-forward side dishes are a genuine focus of the lunch.

cuisine: Couple-run dining café serving handmade, vegetable-rich healthy meals made without chemical seasonings
neighborhood: Horenden-cho, along Yasuragi-no-michi, about 8 min walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. The menu centers on pasta, curry, omurice, fried cutlets (tonkatsu/chicken katsu), hamburger and rice-bowl sets; homemade miso soup and sauces typically rely on wheat-based soy sauce/miso. Nothing on the official site or other sources indicates dedicated gluten-free preparation or celiac-safe handling, so it is not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The kitchen is vegetable-forward and avoids chemical seasonings, following a マゴワヤサシイ (beans, seeds, seaweed, vegetables, fish, mushrooms, potatoes) balance, but the signature 卑弥呼ごはん and other set meals include meat (pork/chicken cutlets) and fish, and miso soup uses fish/sake-lees base. No certified vegan menu is documented; a vegan diner would need to ask in advance about plant-only options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町1086 岡専マロンビル1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI on chō-level address 奈良県奈良市法蓮町1086; result [135.8255, 34.690544] falls within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 17 seats; table seating with a kids' play corner
hours_raw: 10:00–20:00 (L.O. 19:30); open until 22:00 with reservation. Closed Mondays and Tuesdays. (Official site. Note: one listing shows 11:00–18:00, Wed–Sun.)
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, family-friendly local café run by an older couple; a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu will go a long way, as English support is not advertised.
caveats: Hours differ between sources: the official site lists 10:00–20:00 daily (Wed–Sun) while one aggregator shows 11:00–18:00. The hours_week reflects the official site. Vegan/GF suitability is not documented and should be confirmed directly with the owners.

bio.background: café 卑弥呼 sits along Yasuragi-no-michi, the Path of Tranquility, about eight minutes on foot from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is run by a kind older couple who keep the pace gentle and the mood cozy (ほっこりした気分になれる), with a small kids' play corner that makes it easy for families to linger.
bio.specialty: The standout is the daily 卑弥呼ごはん rice set, which pairs a main such as pork and chicken cutlets (oil-poached chicken style) with four small side dishes, homemade miso soup made with sake lees from the local Toyosawa Brewery, and plenty of vegetables. Other favorites include the homemade hamburger lunch, pasta, curry, and omurice.
bio.philosophy: Everything is made by hand without chemical seasonings, with abundant vegetables for nutritious meals (化学調味料不使用、すべて手作りで野菜多めの健康的なご飯), guided by the マゴワヤサシイ balance of beans, seeds, seaweed, vegetables, fish, mushrooms and potatoes. The kitchen leans on local Nara ingredients, including specially cultivated Hinohikari rice.

anecdotes.text: The miso soup is made using sake lees from the local Toyosawa Brewery, tying the meal to Nara's own producers.
The café name 卑弥呼 comes from the owner's personal connection to a Himiko Hakata doll and fortune-telling, paired with the Nara setting.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu includes fried cutlets, pasta, curry and hamburger sets, indicating heavy wheat use and shared kitchen handling; no gluten-free measures are documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Homemade sauces, dressings and miso soup are standard Japanese preparations that typically use wheat-containing soy sauce/miso; no gluten-free substitution is noted.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Main dishes include pork, chicken and fish, and miso soup uses a fish/sake-lees base, so plant-only meals are not separately prepared or guaranteed.

positives.text: All food is handmade without chemical seasonings, with sauces and dressings prepared in-house rather than from commercial products.

name_ja: アストラルレイコーヒー
cuisine: Specialty coffee stand / self-roasted coffee, lattes, house-made sweets and bread
neighborhood: きたまち／法蓮町・一条通り (Hōren-chō, along Ichijō-dōri, north of Kintetsu Nara Station)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町349-1 コーポラス一条 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded to chō-level address (法蓮町349-1) via GSI AddressSearch; within Nara box.
seats_text: Small stand seating; listed as 8 seats (3 standing counter, 2 chairs, plus shared/variable tables). Local articles describe an open layout of around 18 spots with Wi-Fi and charging outlets at every seat.
hours_raw: 11:00–20:00; closed Thursdays and 1st & 3rd Wednesdays
closed_days: Thursdays; also 1st and 3rd Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed neighborhood coffee stand run by two friendly owners; a few words of Japanese and a smile go a long way, and the casual order-at-the-counter style makes it easy for a foreign student to drop in.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program documented. The food menu centers on house-made bread, toast, and baked sweets (financiers, apple pie, cheesecake, tiramisu), all of which are wheat-based. Coffee and lattes are naturally gluten-free drinks, but there is no information on dedicated handling or cross-contamination control, so this is not a celiac-safe destination.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Black drip coffee is plant-based, and lattes can be made with milk; there is no confirmed plant-milk option, so ask on the day. The baked goods (financiers, pies, cheesecake) typically contain dairy and eggs. Best approach is to stick to black coffee or ask about plant milk.

bio.background: Astral Ray Coffee is a small self-roasting coffee stand opened on June 20, 2022 by two women: one a former high school teacher, the other a former preschool/childcare worker. They set out to create a 'machi no ibasho' (neighborhood gathering place) along Ichijō-dōri in the Hōren-chō area, north of central Nara.
bio.origin: Hōren-chō, Nara City (きたまち area, along Ichijō-dōri)
bio.specialty: Self-roasted specialty coffee with five or more bean varieties always available, ground and prepared to each customer's preference, plus a wide range of lattes. Their signature 'Astral Blend' anchors the menu, alongside house-made sweets such as financiers and apple pie and specially developed breads and toasts. Beans are sold for home brewing and branded tumblers are available; everything is offered for takeout.
bio.philosophy: A space where people can 'be themselves' (自分らしくいられる). The name combines Astral (stars) and Ray (light): the owners feel each person shines on their own, and when those individual lights connect they form constellations, expanding what is possible together.

anecdotes.text: The two founders left careers in education and childcare to open the stand, designing it as a welcoming spot for everyone from coffee aficionados to total beginners, with a sliding-door entrance roomy enough for strollers and Wi-Fi and power outlets at every seat.
Signature offerings noted by local press include the Astral Blend (around 550 yen), house-made financiers, and toast starting around 750 yen.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made breads, toasts, and baked sweets are prepared on-site in a small space, so wheat is handled throughout; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

positives.text: Black drip and single-origin coffees are naturally free of gluten and animal products; the owners customize grind and brew to each customer, so individual requests are welcome.

caveats: Hours of 11:00–20:00 are consistent across Tabelog and local press; one older search snippet listed 11:00–18:00, so confirm closing time on the day. Seat count is reported as 8 on the listing but local articles describe roughly 18 spots in an open layout. Phone number is not publicly listed; Instagram is the primary contact/official channel. Vegan plant-milk availability not confirmed.

cuisine: Self-roasted coffee specialty shop / kissaten
neighborhood: Kitamachi (Horen-cho), north of Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: This is a traditional coffee shop, not a gluten-free destination. Food offerings are light items such as pizza toast and cookies, which contain wheat. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. The coffee itself is naturally gluten-free, but no celiac-safe assurances exist.
vegan_detail: A coffee-focused kissaten with no documented vegan menu. Black coffee is naturally plant-based, but milk-based drinks and the light food (pizza toast, cookies) are not vegan. Plant milk availability is undocumented, so a vegan visitor would need to ask. Drink black coffee to be safe.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町972-1
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 法蓮町972, within the Nara bounding box; high confidence.
seats_text: Small kissaten with counter and table seating; exact seat count not documented, but a compact owner-run space.
hours_raw: 9:00-19:00, closed Wednesdays
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, traditional Japanese coffee house run by one owner; a little Japanese helps and a calm, respectful manner is appreciated, but pointing at the menu works fine for ordering coffee.
caveats: Cash only and no reservations. Primarily a coffee destination with only light snacks (toast, cookies); not a meal spot. Not suitable for strict celiac or fully vegan diets without asking.

bio.background: Coffee Mamekura is a small, owner-run coffee house in Nara's quiet Kitamachi district just north of Kintetsu Nara Station. The proprietor left a corporate career to devote himself to coffee, opening this calm kissaten where everything centers on the bean.
bio.origin: A former salaryman turned roaster who built a personal coffee shop around hand-roasting and meticulous bean selection.
bio.specialty: Self-roasted single-origin and blended coffees brewed one cup at a time to order. House blends include the Mamekura Blend (a balanced, approachable cup with good body and acidity) and the lighter Shin-Mamekura Blend, alongside single origins like Mandheling Sinar and the fruity, cassis-like Tanzania Tari AA. Beans are also sold to take home and are popular as gifts. Light bites such as pizza toast and cookies accompany the coffee.
bio.philosophy: Every batch is hand-picked bean by bean to remove imperfect beans, then roasted to suit each bean's character, and finally brewed individually after the order is placed so each cup expresses its own depth and personality.

anecdotes.text: The owner hand-picks defective beans out of his carefully chosen lots one by one, then matches the roast to each bean before brewing each cup fresh to order, drawing out its individual character and depth.
A second cup of blend coffee is offered at half price, an invitation to settle in and linger over the morning.

positives.text: Black coffee is the focus and is naturally gluten-free and plant-based, making a simple cup a safe choice for both diets.

lead: やさいパンのみせ まちのカフェVIVO
name_ja: やさいパンのみせ まちのカフェVIVO
name_en: Yasai Pan no Mise Machi no Cafe VIVO
cuisine: 野菜パンのベーカリーカフェ（サンドイッチ・焼きたてパン・ランチ）
neighborhood: きたまち・法蓮町／新大宮駅周辺
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町433-1 グローリー新大宮 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 法蓮町433-1; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 約20席（テイクアウトも可）
hours_raw: 月～金 10:30～17:00（売り切れ次第終了の場合あり）／土・日・祝休
closed_days: 土曜・日曜・祝日
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood cafe with a Japanese-only menu and staff; basic Japanese or pointing helps, but the relaxed, community-minded atmosphere is welcoming and the budget is well under 1,000 yen.
gf_detail: This is a bakery whose entire concept is wheat bread (vegetable-kneaded loaves, rolls, sandwiches), so it is not suitable for celiacs or anyone avoiding gluten. No gluten-free options are documented.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan venue. While many items use vegetables, the menu includes omelets, curry, sandwiches and cakes that likely contain egg, dairy and possibly fish/meat. Some vegetable bread may be plant-based, but no vegan labeling is documented, so a vegan would need to ask item by item.

bio.background: 新大宮駅近く、国道24号沿いの一条高校交差点そばにある、野菜をたっぷり練り込んだパンとサンドイッチの小さなベーカリーカフェ。子育て支援や女性・障がいのある人の就労支援に取り組むNPO法人Msねっとが運営し、同じ場所にある『のんびりベーカリー』とともに、地域の人が集う交流の場になっている。2024年4月に開店10周年を迎えた。
bio.specialty: にんじん・ほうれん草・ごまなどを練り込んだ色とりどりの野菜パンや『野菜まるパン』、焼きたてパン、新鮮な野菜サンドイッチ。近隣の自家焙煎店から仕入れたコーヒーも手頃な価格で楽しめる。
bio.philosophy: 一人ひとりの意思・人格・能力を尊重し、障がいのある人が地域の一員として自立して働き続けられるよう支援するという理念のもとで運営されている。

anecdotes.text: 運営はNPO法人Msねっとによる就労継続支援事業で、同じ建物の『のんびりベーカリー』と合わせて地域の交流スペースになっている。VIVOは2024年4月に開店10周年を迎えた。
新大宮駅から徒歩約8分、国道24号沿いで一条高校の交差点を挟んだ向かい、吉野家の近くにある。コーヒーは近隣の喫茶店から厳選し手頃な価格で提供している。

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a working bakery producing wheat bread on-site; flour is everywhere and cross-contamination is unavoidable for gluten-sensitive diners.

positives.text: Vegetables are kneaded directly into the breads (carrot, spinach, sesame), and the menu emphasizes body-friendly, vegetable-forward ingredients.

caveats: Hours and seating (20 seats, cash only) are from the operator's NPO site and Tabelog; menu details vary seasonally. Vegan/allergen suitability is not documented and should be confirmed in person.

cuisine: レトロ純喫茶・軽食（モーニング、サンドイッチ、焼き豚炒飯、スパゲティ、定食、コーヒー）
neighborhood: きたまち・法蓮町（佐保川沿い、国道24号沿い、一条高校東側、新大宮駅北徒歩約11分）
gf_detail: This is a traditional Japanese kissaten serving wheat-based dishes (sandwiches, spaghetti, breaded pork cutlet) and standard set meals. There is no gluten-free menu, no documented allergen handling, and no dedicated fryer. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: An omnivore café whose set meals feature meat (grilled pork, pork cutlet) and whose dishes rely on standard Japanese cooking with fish dashi, butter, and eggs. The owner grows pesticide-free vegetables and fruit out back, but there is no vegan menu and no documentation of plant-only dishes prepared without animal products. Vegans should not assume any dish is safe without asking.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町462-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市法蓮町462-1 returned [135.812088, 34.692677], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Seat count not documented; a small neighborhood kissaten with table seating, manga and magazines, described as a living-room-like space for regulars.
hours_raw: 8:00～17:00（モーニングサービスは11:30まで、ランチは11:30から、売り切れ次第終了）／日曜定休
closed_days: 日曜日（Sunday）
cultural_comfort_note: A genuine local retro coffee shop where almost everything is in Japanese and the clientele are neighborhood regulars, so a little Japanese and a relaxed, polite manner will make you feel welcome.
caveats: Cash-only is inferred from it being a small long-running local kissaten and is not explicitly confirmed; payment methods were not listed. Seat count is not documented. Confirm by phone (0742-34-1370) before visiting, as it closes when items sell out.

bio.background: A beloved retro coffee-and-light-meals shop that has run for more than 40 years along Route 24 in Nara's Horen district, just east of Ichijo High School. It works like a neighborhood living room, with regulars dropping by daily or several times a week to read manga over coffee or chat with friends.
bio.specialty: Famous for astonishingly low, never-raised prices: a 400-yen morning service, eight kinds of set meals at 600-700 yen, and every drink at 350 yen. A typical favorite is the daily set of grilled-pork fried rice with coffee. Sandwiches, spaghetti, pilaf and breaded pork cutlet round out the comforting, home-style menu.
bio.philosophy: The owner grows pesticide-free vegetables and fruit in the back garden, and that home-grown produce is the secret behind the famously affordable prices, which have been held steady even as costs rise elsewhere. Surplus organic vegetables are also sold cheaply to customers.

anecdotes.text: The shop has kept its original prices for decades, refusing to raise them despite inflation, sustained by the owner's home-grown pesticide-free vegetables and fruit.
Regulars treat it as a second living room, coming alone to read the shop's manga or in lively groups to talk, in a bright orange-toned retro space.

positives.text: Vegetables and fruit are grown by the owner without pesticides in the shop's own back garden.

cuisine: Handmade soba and unagi lunch house
neighborhood: Takahata-cho (east Naramachi / Gango-ji / Kyobate Station area)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町966-3
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市高畑町966-3 returned [135.836212, 34.677387], within the central Nara bounding box.
seats_text: Approximately 8 counter seats only, with a partition separating diners from the kitchen.
hours_raw: 11:30–15:00 (lunch only). Closed Wednesdays and Thursdays.
closed_days: Wednesday and Thursday (水曜日・木曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, calm counter-only soba house where some spoken Japanese helps; the menu is simple and ordering is straightforward, but English support is unlikely.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. Although soba is buckwheat, the broth, tempura batter, and likely the noodle blend involve wheat, and the dashi is fish-based; there is no documented gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergen handling. Celiac and gluten-sensitive diners should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The kitchen is built around fish dashi, eel (unagi), and tempura; the soba broth is described as a light fish/kelp-style stock and there are no documented plant-based dishes. No vegan options are advertised.

bio.background: A small, quiet soba house that opened in 2021 in Takahata-cho, the residential quarter just south and east of Nara Park near Gango-ji and the old Naramachi district. It is a tucked-away neighborhood spot serving only at lunch, with roughly eight counter seats and a partition that screens the kitchen so diners can eat undisturbed.
bio.specialty: Two dishes stand out: the tempura soba, served with about ten seasonal vegetables (shrimp, broccoli, shiso, okra, sweet potato and more) alongside three salts — yuzu salt, matcha salt and seaweed salt — and the unagi seiro rice (eel over rice) paired with soba. The cold soba options include zaru soba and a karami-daikon (spicy grated radish) soba; warm bowls include nishin (herring) soba and tanuki soba.
