anecdotes.text: The soba is cut thin with a satisfying firmness (細めでコシがありました), and the dipping broth is unusually light and clear — almost like a pale clear stock rather than a heavy tsuyu (蕎麦粉を混ぜないタイプで、さらっとして白湯に近かった).
Diners describe a clean, serene atmosphere, with a partition that keeps the meal feeling private and unhurried (清潔感があって、落ち着いた雰囲気).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba broth, tempura batter and the soba itself involve wheat; fish dashi is used throughout. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen centers on fish dashi, eel and tempura, so animal products and fish stock are pervasive across the menu.

caveats: Seat count (~8) and hours (11:30–15:00, lunch only, closed Wed/Thu) come from a review blog and Tabelog; closing time of 15:00 is from the blog rather than an official source. No official website was found. Dietary safety details are inferred from documented dishes, not from a stated allergen policy.

cuisine: Lakeside teahouse serving soba, udon, and traditional sweets (kissaten / amami-dokoro)
neighborhood: Sarusawa Pond (Saruzawa-ike), near Kofukuji Temple and Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core menu is wheat-based soba and udon noodles, and broths use fish dashi typically made with wheat-containing soy sauce. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergy accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. Signature dishes feature beef (niku udon, oroshi niku soba) and the broths are based on fish dashi. While some sweets like kuzu mochi and warabi mochi may be plant-based, there are no confirmed vegan savory options and no menu labeling. A strict vegan would have very little to safely order.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市登大路町猿沢49
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates within the central Nara box, at the Sarusawa Pond / Tobihino-cho area as expected.
seats_text: Small teahouse; reviews mention roughly four small tables plus window/counter seating overlooking the pond. No official seat count published.
hours_raw: 10:00-18:00, open daily with irregular closures (不定休)
closed_days: Irregular closures (no fixed closed day)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, very affordable old-fashioned teahouse where you can point at simple noodle and sweet dishes, but bring cash and don't expect English menus or allergy support, so it's easiest with a guide or some basic Japanese.
caveats: Cash only; no reservations; irregular closing days so it may be shut without notice. Wheat- and fish-dashi-based menu means it is unsuitable for celiacs and strict vegans. Seating is limited and can fill up during peak sightseeing times.

bio.background: Kinugake Chaya is a long-running lakeside teahouse said to have operated for over a hundred years on the shore of Sarusawa Pond, at the foot of the stone staircase that leads up toward Kofukuji Temple. It appears in old postcards of Nara and carries on the tradition of teahouses that once refreshed pilgrims and sightseers visiting the temple district.
bio.specialty: Simple, hearty noodle dishes such as warm beef udon with a soft-cooked egg and grated-radish beef soba, alongside classic Japanese sweets like kuzu mochi, warabi mochi, zenzai, anmitsu, and summer kakigori shaved ice. It also sells deer crackers for feeding the local deer.

anecdotes.text: The shop is built to extend out toward the water, with large windows that frame the surface of Sarusawa Pond and the five-story pagoda of Kofukuji; on bright days reflected light from the pond ripples across the coffered ceiling inside.
When a visitor asked, the proprietor confirmed the teahouse has been in business for more than a century, and the same writer noted it appears in historical postcards of old Nara.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The menu is built around wheat soba and udon noodles prepared in a shared kitchen, so gluten contact is unavoidable.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Noodle broths and sauces rely on standard Japanese soy sauce and fish dashi, which normally contain wheat; no wheat-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature dishes use beef and fish-based dashi, and noodles are cooked and served in the same kitchen as meat and fish items.

cuisine: ヴィーガン手打ち蕎麦・プラントベース
neighborhood: 中院町・ならまち（近鉄奈良駅徒歩約10分）
gf_detail: Despite a "gluten-free compatible" tag on one aggregator listing, this cannot be treated as celiac-safe. Soba noodles in Japan are very commonly cut with wheat flour (二八 / nihachi blend) and no fetched source confirms the noodles here are 十割 (100% buckwheat). The shop also serves vegetable tempura, which is wheat-battered, in a small shared kitchen. A gluten-free or celiac diner must ask directly whether the soba is 100% buckwheat and how the tempura is handled; assume not safe until confirmed in person.
vegan_detail: Fully plant-based. The kitchen is a vegan operation run by the same owners as the all-vegan Vegan Cafe Ramuna. The signature soba set (zaru or kake soba with a small side dish, pickles, and vegetable tempura, around 1,650 yen tax included) is vegan; the broth is plant-based rather than fish dashi. Other items described include soy-meat cutlet rice bowl and vegetable tempura rice bowl. Vegans can eat freely off the menu.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市中院町27-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市中院町27 (lng 135.831497, lat 34.67865), within the central Nara box. Matches the Naramachi / Nakain-cho location.
seats_text: Small townhouse space. Tabelog lists 16 seats (4 counter, 12 table); a local news writeup described it as roughly 3 counter seats and 4 tables. Either way it is a tiny weekend-only room.
hours_raw: 土日のみ 12:00～15:00（月～金 休み）。2025年5月3日オープン。
closed_days: Monday through Friday (open Saturday and Sunday only)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny weekend-only owner-run soba room in a Naramachi townhouse; expect Japanese to be the main language, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will help, though the all-vegan menu makes ordering simple.
caveats: Open weekends only (Sat/Sun lunch, 12:00-15:00); the same owners run Vegan Cafe Ramuna on weekdays. Cash only. Seat count differs slightly between sources. "Gluten-free" tags should be verified in person, as soba and tempura typically involve wheat.

bio.background: Vegan Soba Ramusa opened on May 3, 2025 in a quiet townhouse on a side street off Naramachi-dori in the Nakain-cho neighborhood, a short walk from central Nara. It is the weekend incarnation of a single owner who runs the all-vegan Vegan Cafe Ramuna in the Takabatake area on weekdays; on Saturdays and Sundays that same owner becomes a soba maker, opening this small lunch-only room.
bio.specialty: Hand-cut buckwheat soba made with Hokkaido buckwheat flour, served in a vegan set: chilled zaru or hot kake soba with a small side dish, pickles, and vegetable tempura (around 1,650 yen). Other plant-based plates such as a soy-meat cutlet rice bowl and a vegetable tempura rice bowl round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: A commitment to hand-cut soba (手打蕎麦) and fully plant-based cooking, carrying the vegan ethos of the owner's weekday cafe into a focused weekend soba practice.

anecdotes.text: The owner splits the week in two: weekday vegan cafe cook in Takabatake, weekend soba artisan in Naramachi, opening Ramusa only for Saturday and Sunday lunch.
The soba is made with Hokkaido-grown buckwheat flour and served as a set with vegetable tempura, pickles, and a small side.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Soba in Japan is commonly cut with wheat flour, and no source confirms the noodles here are 100% buckwheat; vegetable tempura on the set is wheat-battered. Treat as not celiac-safe unless confirmed in person.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The kitchen is fully vegan (sister to all-vegan Vegan Cafe Ramuna), so animal-product cross-contact is minimal.

positives.text: Entirely plant-based menu run by experienced vegan-cafe owners, so vegans can order anything without modification.
Hand-cut soba broth is plant-based rather than fish dashi, unusual for a Japanese soba shop.

cuisine: Self-roasted specialty coffee and homemade sweets in a renovated machiya townhouse
neighborhood: Kitamachi / Imajikecho (near Todai-ji Tengaimon Gate)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市今在家町40
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市今在家町40 returned [135.834732, 34.694286], within the Nara box and consistent with the location near Todai-ji's Tengaimon Gate.
seats_text: 8 seats, fully non-smoking
hours_raw: Thu–Sun 11:00–17:00; closed Mon, Tue, Wed (per Tabelog). A secondary write-up listed Fri–Sun 13:00–17:00, so day/time can vary — confirm before visiting.
closed_days: Monday, Tuesday, Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 8-seat owner-run machiya café where Japanese will carry you furthest; it is calm and welcoming, but bring cash and don't expect an English menu or English-speaking staff.
gf_detail: No gluten-free information is published. The signature offerings include a New York-style cheesecake and other homemade baked sweets, which are wheat-based, and there is no documented allergen handling, so this is not a celiac-safe destination. A gluten-free traveler would need to ask in person (in Japanese) and treat anything baked as containing wheat.
vegan_detail: No vegan information is published. The focus is hand-roasted coffee plus homemade cakes such as a New York cheesecake (dairy/egg), so default options are not vegan. Black coffee is the only obviously plant-based item; anything else would need to be confirmed in person.

bio.background: Tengaibou occupies a roughly 90-year-old machiya townhouse near Todai-ji's Tengaimon Gate that was originally a Chinese herbal-medicine pharmacy. The current owner bought and renovated the old building and revived its historic name, 転害坊, turning it into a quiet single-owner coffee house.
bio.origin: Imajikecho, in Nara's Kitamachi district just north of the Todai-ji precinct.
bio.specialty: Coffee from beans roasted in-house on the same day, drip-brewed to order, paired with a homemade Meyer lemon New York-style cheesecake designed to bring out the coffee's flavor. The shop also runs a hands-on session where visitors can roast green beans into their own coffee.
bio.philosophy: Sweets and drinks made with natural ingredients, served in a calm, unhurried space.

anecdotes.text: Although it sits close to busy Nara Station and the Nandaimon area, the interior is a surprisingly quiet, peaceful room that is uncrowded on weekdays, making it a good place to spend time alone with a book.

The owner inherited the historic shop name 転害坊 from the building's past life as a Chinese-medicine pharmacy and renovated the roughly 90-year-old townhouse to serve day-roasted coffee and a Meyer lemon NY cheesecake.

positives.text: Coffee is roasted in-house and brewed to order, and sweets are made by hand with natural ingredients, so the owner can describe exactly what is in each item if asked in person.

caveats: Identity, address (奈良県奈良市今在家町40), 8 seats, cash-only, and the Mon/Tue/Wed closures are confirmed via Tabelog. Hours differ between sources (Tabelog: Thu–Sun 11:00–17:00; one café roundup: Fri–Sun 13:00–17:00), so treat hours as irregular and confirm before going. No dedicated official website or Instagram was confirmed. No gluten-free or vegan/allergen handling is documented; baked sweets are wheat- and dairy-based.

cuisine: 和菓子・団子・かき氷 (traditional Japanese sweets, dango, shaved ice)
neighborhood: 東向中町・東向商店街 (Higashimuki arcade, near Kintetsu Nara Station)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. As a traditional wagashi shop, signature items include wheat-flour confections such as どら焼/三笠 (dorayaki/mikasa) and dango, and mitarashi dango sauce is typically made with wheat-based soy sauce. No gluten-free menu, labeling, or allergen handling is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is advertised. Some items (e.g., azuki-based wagashi, kuzukiri, warabi mochi) may be plant-based, but the popular kakigori includes milk ("大和茶ミルク金時") and some sweets may use egg or dairy. No vegan labeling or ingredient disclosure was found, so a vegan visitor would need to ask item by item.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向中町24-1
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.828827, 34.683727] for 奈良県奈良市東向中町24-1; within Nara box, consistent with the Higashimuki arcade near Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: Eat-in seating area at the back of the shop with tables (about 18 seats); self-service green tea available.
hours_raw: 10:00-18:00 daily (Tabelog lists 10:00-17:00; official site lists 10:00-18:00). Kakigori served until around 17:00. Kakigori season roughly late April through September.
closed_days: Open year-round (no fixed closed day stated; official site notes 年中無休).
cultural_comfort_note: A friendly old neighborhood sweets shop where pointing at the case and a simple greeting work fine, though menu and signage are in Japanese and English is limited.
caveats: Hours differ slightly between listings (Tabelog 10:00-17:00 vs official site 10:00-18:00); kakigori is seasonal (roughly late April-September) and the kuzukiri shaved ice is limited to about 20 servings per day. The official site notes sales are currently limited to select products, so some seasonal items may be unavailable.

bio.origin: Nara (Higashimuki arcade, near Kintetsu Nara Station)
bio.background: A long-established wagashi (Japanese confectionery) shop founded in 1890 (Meiji 23) that has carried on Nara's traditional sweets for over 130 years, tucked just inside the Higashimuki covered shopping street a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station.
bio.specialty: Freshly made mitarashi dango sold by the skewer; classic wagashi such as dorayaki/mikasa, daifuku, kuzukiri and warabi mochi; and a beloved summer shaved ice (kakigori) menu featuring 大和茶ミルク金時 (Yamato-tea-and-milk with sweet red beans) and 純生いちご (fresh strawberry), with a limited-quantity くずきり氷 (kuzukiri shaved ice).
bio.philosophy: The shop frames its work as heartfelt, time-honored craftsmanship, treating each piece of wagashi as a small art form connected to Nara's history.

anecdotes.text: The kakigori is built with premium azuki red bean paste accented by wasanbon-grade sugar and Yamato matcha, with warabi mochi layered inside the ice; it is served in Akahada-ware bowls painted in the traditional Nara-e style, with complimentary self-service hot tea in the eat-in corner at the back.
The kuzukiri shaved ice was suspended during the pandemic and later returned, and is offered in very limited daily quantities.

gf_cross_contamination.text: A traditional wagashi shop where wheat-flour confections (dorayaki/mikasa, dango) are made and handled in the same kitchen; no gluten-free preparation or allergen separation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Mitarashi dango is glazed with a sweet-soy sauce that is typically made from wheat-containing Japanese soy sauce; no allergen disclosure is provided.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The signature kakigori includes dairy milk (Yamato-tea milk variety), so shaved-ice equipment and toppings are not dairy-free; no separation for vegan guests is documented.

cuisine: かき氷・甘味／しゃぶしゃぶ・すき焼き（老舗旅館内）
neighborhood: 東向中町・東向商店街（近鉄奈良駅至近、天平旅館内）
gf_detail: No gluten-free program. The shop is known for kakigori (shaved ice) and for shabu-shabu/sukiyaki. Kakigori toppings such as kinako (roasted soybean powder), custard sauce, and cookie pieces, plus hot-pot dipping sauces, mean wheat and gluten are present and there is no celiac-safe handling. Not suitable for those needing strict gluten-free.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-oriented place. The kakigori is built on dairy (milk syrup, milk foam, custard), and the core menu is meat-based shabu-shabu and sukiyaki. No documented vegan options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向中町9 天平旅館 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI on address 奈良県奈良市東向中町9; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Seat count not published; small dining room inside Tenpyo Ryokan, kakigori-only customers welcomed alongside hot-pot diners.
hours_raw: 11:00-21:30 (Tabelog). Kakigori service is seasonal, typically running through about November.
closed_days: No fixed regular closing day listed; kakigori is seasonal, so the shaved-ice menu is limited outside the warmer months (typically available through ~November).
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional ryokan dining room where a little spoken Japanese helps, though pointing at the kakigori menu and a friendly konnichiwa will carry you through ordering shaved ice.
caveats: Kakigori is seasonal and the menu rotates, so specific flavors vary by visit and shaved ice may not be offered year-round. The venue is primarily a shabu-shabu/sukiyaki restaurant inside Tenpyo Ryokan that also welcomes shaved-ice-only guests.

bio.background: らつく occupies the first floor of Tenpyo Ryokan, a long-established Nara inn that has welcomed travelers for decades just off the Higashimuki shopping arcade, steps from Kintetsu Nara Station. While it operates as a shabu-shabu and sukiyaki restaurant, it has built a devoted following for its kakigori (shaved ice), and the staff happily seat guests who come only for dessert.
bio.origin: Nara, inside Tenpyo Ryokan on Higashimuki-nakamachi
bio.specialty: Fluffy, finely shaved kakigori with house-made milk syrup and milk foam; signature flavors include black-honey kinako (黒みつきな粉エスプーマ), matcha, and seasonal specials. The restaurant also serves shabu-shabu and lunchtime sukiyaki.
bio.philosophy: Careful, seasonal craftsmanship: the ice is shaved fine enough to scoop cleanly without shattering, and syrups are layered for balanced, not overly sugary, sweetness.

anecdotes.text: A 2021 visitor noted the kakigori takes about eight minutes to prepare, with the ice so finely shaved it scoops cleanly; the November special that year was a 'VERY Berry Custard Oreo' of strawberry, milk, custard, blueberries and Oreo pieces.
The shop signals on its exterior signage that kakigori-only customers are welcome, even though it is primarily a hot-pot restaurant.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Kakigori toppings include kinako, custard sauce and cookie (Oreo) pieces, and the kitchen also runs shabu-shabu/sukiyaki with wheat-containing sauces; no gluten-free protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Shaved ice is dairy-based (milk syrup, milk foam, custard); no dairy-free or vegan preparation is documented.

lead: 糀料理とビオスチームのお店 えん
name_ja: 糀料理とビオスチームのお店 えん
name_en: Koji Ryori to Bios Steam no Omise En
cuisine: Koji and fermented-food health cafe — Yamato vegetables, house-made fermented seasonings (salt koji, sweet koji, amazake, sake lees), koji plates and sweets in a renovated traditional house
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. Cooking centers on house-fermented seasonings; while koji and amazake are rice-based, the kitchen uses standard fermented seasonings (likely including wheat-containing soy sauce/miso), and scones are wheat-based. No gluten-free certification or dedicated handling is documented. A celiac would need to phone ahead in Japanese and confirm each dish; treat as not reliably safe.
vegan_detail: Vegetable-forward and uses many plant-based fermented preparations (salt-koji tofu, koji-miki pickles, miki dressing, abundant seasonal vegetables and fruit), but it is not a vegan restaurant — koji plates also feature koji-marinated fish and chicken, and miki/amazake items are common. A vegan should ask which plate components are animal-free; expect partial rather than full vegan menus.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市白毫寺町228
geocode_note: GSI geocoded 奈良県奈良市白毫寺町228 to [135.849564, 34.673923], within the central Nara box (Byakugoji-cho, southeast of Kintetsu Nara, between Shin-Yakushiji and Byakugoji temples).
neighborhood: 白毫寺町（高畑・ならまち/京終エリア、近鉄奈良駅から南東）
seats_text: Four tables seating 2–6 people each, plus a private tatami room; shoes removed at entry (small, intimate old-house setting)
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30–14:00; Cafe 14:00–17:00; Dinner from 17:00 by reservation only. Closed Sundays and Mondays.
closed_days: Sunday and Monday
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet old-house cafe where you remove your shoes and order from a rotating Japanese-only menu, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps, but the calm vegetable-forward plates make it welcoming for a curious student.
caveats: Daytime lunch and cafe hours run 11:30–17:00 (lunch service ends around 14:00); dinner is by reservation only from 17:00. Plates rotate every two months. Phoning ahead is wise on weekends as dishes can sell out. Located about a 12-minute walk from the Takabatake-cho bus stop.

bio.background: En opened in November 2020 inside an 80-year-old renovated traditional house tucked between Shin-Yakushiji and Byakugoji temples in the Takahata area, succeeding an earlier koji-focused spot that had stood here called Hana. Guests slip off their shoes and settle into a calm tatami space built around the quiet rhythms of fermentation.
bio.specialty: Seasonal koji plates that change every two months — such as the koji-and-miki plate and koji curry plate — built entirely on house-made fermented seasonings, alongside koji sweets like amazake scones, sake-lees scones, red-bean amazake tofu pudding (douhua), and a seasonal lemon-amazake lassi.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen replaces ordinary seasonings with fermented ones (sweet koji, salt koji, amazake, sake lees) so that every dish delivers fermentation bacteria and enzymes, aiming for food that gently supports gut health and, as the shop puts it, lets body and heart rejoice.

anecdotes.text: The plates are served with seasonal flowers for a sense of healing, and the menu is rebuilt every two months around what is in season.

The signature plates lean on house ferments — salt-koji tofu, koji-marinated fish and chicken, koji-miki pickles, and a dressing made from miki, a traditional fermented Amami Islands drink of rice, sweet potato and sugar.

positives.text: Plates are built around house-made fermented seasonings and incorporate abundant seasonal vegetables and fruit, so plant-forward components are central to the menu.

lead: 大和茶大福専門店 GRANCHA（グランチャ）
name_ja: 大和茶大福専門店 GRANCHA（グランチャ）
cuisine: Yamato tea sweets / kanmidokoro
neighborhood: 東向中町（東向商店街、近鉄奈良駅すぐ南）
address_ja: 〒630-8215 奈良県奈良市東向中町22
geocode_note: GSI geocoded address_ja to [135.828506, 34.683777], inside the central Nara box.
seats_text: Small eat-in space (STAND GRANCHA / tearoom), roughly 10 seats mixing tables and standing counter; primarily a takeout daifuku shop.
hours_raw: 大福販売 10:00-19:00 / STAND GRANCHA イートイン 10:00-18:00（茶寮は時期により12:00頃-17:30 L.O.）。年中無休（12/31・1/1のみ休み）。
closed_days: Open year-round; closed only Dec 31 and Jan 1 (the in-store tearoom/eat-in space may keep shorter or occasionally varied hours).
cultural_comfort_note: A casual sweets counter on the busy Higashimuki shopping street where you can point at the daifuku you want and pay by card or QR, so a few basic Japanese words are plenty for a foreign student.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core product is daifuku (mochi from glutinous rice, which is naturally wheat-free), but the menu also includes Mont Blanc and other cakes, shaved ice with toppings, and likely flour-based items, and there is no allergen labeling or celiac protocol documented. The shop publishes no gluten information, so it is not safe to treat as celiac-friendly without asking in person.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. Signature items use dairy heavily — matcha cream-filled daifuku, milk shaved ice, soft serve, and puddings all contain milk/cream. Plain anko (red bean) mochi may be plant-based in itself, but no vegan menu or labeling exists and cross-contact with dairy is likely in this small kitchen, so confirm directly if avoiding animal products.
caveats: Allergen/dietary details are not published; the eat-in/tearoom hours can differ from the daifuku-counter hours and shift seasonally, so verify in person for cafe seating. Daifuku are handmade and additive-free per the shop's description but no formal allergen testing is documented.

bio.background: GRANCHA is a small independent Yamato-tea daifuku specialty shop that opened in 2019 on the Higashimuki shopping arcade just south of Kintetsu Nara Station. It was created to spread the appeal of Yamato tea, the green tea grown on Nara's Yamato Plateau and rooted in over 1,200 years of Japanese tea history.
bio.specialty: Its signature is the 口福餅 (kofuku-mochi) daifuku, offered in graded matcha intensities — from the light 薄衣 and beginner-friendly 芽吹 through 新緑, 花萌黄 and the intensely concentrated 夏盛, plus a 冬芝 hojicha version. Each mochi is built in layers of matcha cream, red bean paste, matcha mochi and dusted matcha. Alongside the daifuku are matcha and hojicha shaved ice, Mont Blanc and soft serve, and matcha/hojicha puddings.
bio.philosophy: The shop frames its sweets as 'kofuku-mochi' — confections meant to bring families a smile — and centers everything on showcasing Yamato tea's fragrant aroma and the sweetness that sits beneath its astringency.

anecdotes.text: The mochi are handmade in the shop's own workshop and made additive-free, with daifuku offered in graded tea concentrations including one billed as possibly Japan's most concentrated matcha daifuku.
The shop presents itself as celebrating Yamato tea as the root of Japanese tea and aims to make that heritage known to more people through its daifuku.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature daifuku and desserts use matcha cream, milk shaved ice and dairy puddings, so dairy is present throughout the small kitchen and cross-contact with plant-based items is likely.

positives.text: Mochi are handmade in-house and described as additive-free, and the daifuku base (glutinous rice and red bean) is naturally wheat-free, though no allergen testing is documented.

lead: お庭 shaveice miracolo（ミラコロ）
cuisine: かき氷（創作かき氷専門店 / artisanal shaved ice）
vegan_detail: A shaved ice specialty shop. Many menus center on fresh seasonal fruit and tea (matcha, hojicha) syrups, but signature items lean heavily on dairy — milk, cream, fromage (cheese) cream, condensed milk and yogurt jelly (e.g. mango-almond-milk, kiwi fromage, milk tea). No documented vegan menu or plant-milk options. A traveler avoiding all animal products would need to ask whether any fruit-only, dairy-free preparation is possible. No vegan claims are documented.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and makes no GF claims. The core product (fruit/tea syrups over ice) is not inherently wheat-based, but there is no documented allergen control, no dedicated equipment, and toppings/cookie or biscuit garnishes and some house syrups/jellies may contain or contact gluten. A celiac visitor must ask about each menu item's components; cross-contact cannot be assumed safe.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町974
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市高畑町９７４番地, within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: 高畑町（ならまち南東、近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約16〜20分）
seats_text: Small indoor seating in a renovated residential house — shoes off, seated on tatami. Exact seat count not documented; capacity is limited.
hours_raw: 水・木 10:00〜16:00（L.O. 15:30）。基本は水・木のみ営業。4月〜11月の期間限定営業。臨時で週末等に開けることもあり、営業日はSNS（Instagram/Threads）で要確認。
closed_days: 月・火・金・土・日（基本）。営業は原則 水・木のみで、4月〜11月の季節営業。臨時営業日あり。
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run house cafe where you remove your shoes and sit on tatami; menus and ordering are in Japanese, so a little Japanese or a translation app helps, and check the shop's social media for that week's open days before going.

bio.background: miracolo is a one-person shaved ice shop that opened on April 11, 2019 in a renovated old house in the quiet Takabatake neighborhood at the southeast edge of Naramachi, near Nara Park and Gangoji. The owner describes it as the realization of a long-held dream to run a kakigori shop. After a pause, the shop resumed in 2023 to the delight of longtime fans, and now runs as a seasonal place open roughly April through November, generally only two days a week.
bio.specialty: Airy, fine-textured shaved ice with an exceptionally smooth melt-in-the-mouth quality, dressed with carefully sourced ingredients: local Koto-ka strawberries from Iwai Farm and tea from Kino-en, plus constantly rotating seasonal fruit creations such as the Mango-Almond-Milk with 100% Miyazaki mango and a Kiwi Fromage layering three kiwi varieties (green, golden, and the rare Ruby Red) over yogurt jelly and fromage cream.
bio.philosophy: The owner wanted to make a relaxed, no-reservation spot 'where people can casually stop by when they feel like having shaved ice,' welcoming walk-ins and changing the menu with the seasons.

anecdotes.text: You take your shoes off and eat seated on tatami inside the renovated house, giving the shop a homey, sit-in-someone's-living-room feel.
Its Ruby Red kiwi, a variety developed over more than 20 years, is only on the market about 4-5 weeks a year, so it appears on the menu for a very short window.
The shop has drawn devoted fans from across the prefecture and beyond, with some customers traveling from northeastern Japan to visit.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Signature shaved ices rely on dairy and cheese-based components such as milk, cream, fromage cream, condensed milk and yogurt jelly, so a shared workspace likely involves cross-contact with animal products.

positives.text: Many menus are built on fresh seasonal fruit and tea syrups, so fruit-forward options exist for those who can confirm a dairy-free preparation.

caveats: Open days are highly irregular and seasonal (roughly April-November, base days Wednesday/Thursday only); always confirm the current week's hours on the shop's Instagram/Threads before visiting. Phone number and exact seat count are not documented. No vegan or gluten-free claims are made by the shop; dietary suitability must be confirmed in person.

lead: 京終駅舎カフェ ハテノミドリ
name_ja: 京終駅舎カフェ ハテノミドリ
cuisine: Cafe inside a restored historic railway station — drip coffee, weekly soup lunch, sweets, seasonal shaved ice, and a few crafts
neighborhood: 南京終町（JR京終駅舎内、ならまち南端）
gf_detail: No gluten-free program. This is a casual community cafe whose food centers on toast (wheat bread), cakes/cheesecake, and bread-based light meals, with no indication of gluten-free items or celiac handling. A celiac traveler should not rely on it.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan establishment. The menu features dairy- and egg-heavy items (cheesecake, milk-based shaved ice such as strawberry milk and ginger milk, cakes) plus toast. Some plant-based drinks like hand-drip coffee, Yamato tea, and house ginger ale exist, but no documented vegan food menu; a vegan would need to ask about specific items.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南京終町211 JR京終駅構内
geocode_note: Geocoded at chō (town) level via GSI for 奈良県奈良市南京終町211; result 34.66449, 135.822388 falls within the central Nara box and matches the JR Kyobate Station location.
seats_text: Seat count not published; a small cafe occupying the former station office space inside the compact restored Kyobate Station building.
hours_raw: Weekdays 11:30–14:00, 14:30–16:00; Sat/Sun & holidays 11:00–16:00. Hours can vary; check SNS.
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, locally-run community cafe where a little basic Japanese helps; staff are unlikely to operate in English, but pointing at the menu and a friendly attitude work fine.

bio.origin: Nara (Kyobate district, southern edge of Naramachi)
bio.background: The cafe opened in February 2019 inside the JR Kyobate Station building, an unmanned wooden station that was restored to its original 1898 appearance. It was created by a group of local young people organized as an NPO who wanted to bring the long-quiet station back to life and make it a base for neighborhood vibrancy.
bio.specialty: Carefully hand-dripped coffee using beans from nearby roasters, a weekly-changing soup lunch and sweets, locally made cheesecake, Yamato tea grown in Nara, house-made ginger ale, and summer shaved ice with house syrups such as a chocolate-mint made by simmering fresh mint leaves.
bio.philosophy: Conceived as 'a space where people gather and come together' — offering unhurried, orthodox hospitality rather than chasing trends. The name 'Hatenomidori' plays on the 終 (bate) in Kyobate and expresses the wish that, like a single drop of water nurturing greenery, the cafe helps an endless vista of green grow in the neighborhood.

anecdotes.text: A white piano anchors the bright former station-office interior, and guests can watch trains pass by while they eat and drink; the cafe also hosts regular music events and markets.
The station building was restored to look as it did when it first opened in 1898, and the volunteer NPO behind the cafe runs it as part of efforts to revitalize the once-unmanned station.

caveats: Hours are described slightly differently across sources (one lists 11:00–15:00 with Mon/Wed closures; the more detailed and recent listings show a weekday lunch break and a Wednesday-only closure). Treat hours as irregular and confirm via the cafe's SNS. Seat count is not published. The official site (kyobate.org) currently returns an expired SSL certificate.

name_ja: かき氷＆ウイスキー Gentry（ジェントリー）
cuisine: Jazz bar and kakigori (shaved ice) specialist, famous for flambéed "yaki-gōri" baked shaved ice; also serves whisky, wine and cocktails
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature shaved ice is layered with components such as lemon jelly, azuki red beans, condensed milk and matcha meringue, with no documented gluten-free menu or allergen handling. Toppings and accompanying sweets are not verified as wheat-free, and there is no information about cross-contamination control. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The famous shaved ice relies on dairy (condensed milk, white chocolate, custard, milk-tea cheese foam, puddings) and the matcha-meringue version is brûléed and finished with flambéed Cointreau. No plant-based versions are documented. As a whisky/cocktail bar, drinks are the other focus. Not a vegan destination.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市小西町8-1 ラフィーヌ東洋 3F A
geocode_note: Geocoded to 奈良市小西町8-1; coordinates fall within the central Nara box. This is the current (post-relocation) address near Kintetsu Nara Station (about 146 m / 3-minute walk).
neighborhood: 小西町 / Konishi-chō, central Nara near Kintetsu Nara Station (formerly 下御門町・下御門商店街 before the 2024-2025 relocation)
seats_text: Seat count not disclosed; a small intimate jazz-bar space on the 3rd floor (elevator access). Counter centered on the kakigori machine plus limited seating.
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, owner-run jazz bar where a few words of Japanese and pointing at the photo menu will get you the famous flambéed shaved ice; the master is known for being friendly and polite, so a beginner can manage comfortably.
caveats: The original Tabelog page for the 下御門町 location (29003578) now shows 掲載保留 because the shop closed there in April 2024 and reopened on 23 October 2025 at a new address in 小西町 (Konishi-chō), closer to Kintetsu Nara Station. Hours and the Wednesday closing are from the current listing and may shift seasonally; the shaved-ice operates daytime with a ticket/queue system on busy days. Confirm current details via Instagram before visiting.

bio.background: Gentry first opened in April 1984 as an old-school jazz bar in central Nara, spinning jazz on vinyl and hosting occasional live performances. Over four decades it became beloved less for its records than for an unlikely signature: an elaborate flambéed shaved ice. After closing its long-time home in the Shimomikado shopping street in April 2024 to the sadness of many regulars, it returned in October 2025 a few steps from Kintetsu Nara Station, now billed as Kakigori & Whisky Gentry, still built around its Swan ice shaver and jazz-bar mood.
bio.specialty: The house specialty is yaki-gōri, baked shaved ice: layers of azuki beans, lemon jelly and condensed milk under a matcha meringue that the owner blowtorches to a caramelized crust, then douses with flambéed Cointreau so the mountain of ice briefly catches fire, a nod to Nara's Wakakusa-yama grass-burning festival. Seasonal creations rotate through pistachio and white chocolate, pumpkin with pumpkin pudding, strawberry, and custard brûlée. By night it pours whisky, wine and cocktails.

anecdotes.text: The signature baked shaved ice is set alight tableside with flambéed orange liqueur, an edible homage to the Wakakusa-yama mountain-burning that lights up the Nara skyline each winter.
Founded in April 1984, the shop closed its original location in April 2024 and reopened on 23 October 2025 nearer the station; the owner is remembered by visitors for a bright, courteous manner.

lead: 寧楽料理 こくとぅーら
name_ja: 寧楽料理 こくとぅーら
cuisine: Creative Japanese-Western fusion izakaya using Nara ingredients; specialty cold-smoked dishes
gf_detail: This is a creative izakaya whose dishes lean heavily on soy sauce, miso and dashi, plus cured meats, smoked items and pasta — all typical sources of wheat and gluten. No gluten-free menu, dedicated preparation, or celiac accommodation is documented anywhere. The kitchen is run single-handedly in a 9-seat counter space, so dedicated allergen control is unlikely. Not safe for celiacs; a gluten-sensitive diner would need to confirm directly in Japanese with the owner.
vegan_detail: The menu is built around meat, fish and seafood — Yamato pork, Yamato chicken, Yamato beef, homemade prosciutto and cured meats, smoked fish, squid, oysters and scallops — and seasoning relies on dashi (fish stock), soy sauce and miso. There is no documented vegan or vegetarian menu. A plant-based diner would have very limited options and should ask the owner directly.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市福智院町17-1
geocode_note: Geocoded to 奈良市福智院町17 via GSI; coordinates [135.834183, 34.677353] fall within the central-Nara box. The restaurant relocated here (Naramachi area, near the Harushika sake brewery) from its former 三条町 location in January 2024.
seats_text: 9 seats at a single wooden-slab counter (some sources list 8); run entirely by the owner
hours_raw: Weekdays (Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri) 17:00-22:00; Saturdays, Sundays and holidays 15:00-22:00. Last order: food 21:00, drinks 21:30. Closed Wednesdays.
closed_days: Wednesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 9-seat counter run solo by the owner in a renovated townhouse; basic Japanese helps a great deal as English service should not be assumed, and the intimate setting rewards diners comfortable with a quiet, local atmosphere.
caveats: The original Tabelog page for the 三条町 address (29010978) is now marked 移転 (relocated); the restaurant moved to 福智院町17-1 in the Naramachi area in January 2024, and the active Tabelog listing is 29014084. Seat count is given as 9 by most sources and 8 by one. Owner's given name reading (夏主) is reported as "Natsushi / Nassh."

bio.chef_name: 廣岡夏主 (Hirooka Natsushi, who goes by the nickname "Nassh")
bio.background: The owner ran a creative izakaya near JR Nara Station for roughly six years before relocating in January 2024 to a quietly renovated machiya (traditional townhouse) in the Naramachi district, near the Harushika sake brewery. He runs the entire counter single-handedly.
bio.specialty: Cold-smoking (冷燻): the homemade smoked assortment — which can include prosciutto, quail egg, oyster and scallop — is the most-ordered dish. Other signatures pair Nara produce with European technique, such as cold peach pasta with Yamato beef prosciutto, young-corn fritters with white-miso aioli, and a 'wa-hijo' (Japanese-style ajillo) of firefly squid and rape blossoms.
bio.philosophy: He builds dishes on a French, Italian and Spanish base and fuses them with Japanese seasonings — dashi, soy sauce and miso — describing his aim as expanding 'new possibilities in food,' an idea he links to the cultural mixing of the Silk Road that historically reached Nara.

anecdotes.text: The kitchen makes a point of using Nara-region ingredients — Yamato pork, Yamato chicken and Yamato beef — alongside local sake and hand-pulled Miwa somen noodles from the Yamamoto producer.
An earlier black-rice curry of the owner's took second place in a 2010 curry competition.
The current space is a renovated townhouse with a single wooden-slab counter, sitting near the Harushika brewery and a well-known soba restaurant.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The cooking is explicitly built on dashi, soy sauce and miso as its Japanese seasoning base, all of which typically contain or accompany wheat.

cuisine: Showa-era neighborhood Chinese (machi-chuka)
neighborhood: 紀寺町・京終エリア (Kiji-cho / Kyobate area, southern Naramachi)
gf_detail: This is a traditional Chinese eatery built around wheat-heavy dishes — gyoza wrappers, ramen noodles, and soy sauce (which contains wheat) are central to the menu. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation, dedicated equipment, or allergen accommodation. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-egg focused machi-chuka: signature gyoza are filled with pork, set meals include sweet-and-sour pork, fried shrimp and omurice, and broths/seasonings are animal-based. No documented vegan options. Not a vegan destination.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市紀寺町885-3
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates for 紀寺町885; within the Nara bounding box.
seats_text: Small intimate interior with a counter and two round tables; exact seat count not published. Clearly a tiny family-run room.
hours_raw: 11:00–19:00 (Tabelog); one local source lists 11:00–18:30. Irregular holidays (不定休).
closed_days: No fixed closing day (不定休 / irregular holidays). Best to call ahead.
cultural_comfort_note: A genuine old-school neighborhood Chinese spot run by a local family with no English menu — go with some basic Japanese (or a translation app) and a relaxed, friendly attitude.
caveats: Cash only; irregular holidays so confirm before a special trip. Smoking is permitted inside. Listed hours differ slightly between sources (closes 18:30–19:00).

bio.background: Wakou Hanten opened in 1977 in a quiet residential pocket of Kiji-cho in southern Naramachi and has been a family-run neighborhood Chinese restaurant ever since — closing in on 50 years of service. The shop's name comes from the owner's eldest son's given name (和廣), read in its Sino-Japanese on'yomi.
bio.specialty: The house signature is gyoza: ten crispy, lacy-edged dumplings filled with cabbage, hakusai and minced pork. Beyond that it's a classic machi-chuka spread — ramen, fried rice, and around eleven set meals, plus Western-style comfort plates like omurice and fried-shrimp sets.
bio.philosophy: Honest, unchanging neighborhood value — the gyoza have famously cost the same since the day the restaurant opened in 1977.

anecdotes.text: A plate of ten gyoza still costs 300 yen, a price the family has reportedly held since opening in 1977.
The red-toned interior with its counter and pair of round tables keeps a warm Showa-era atmosphere, and the menu reaches beyond Chinese standards into retro Western dishes like omurice.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is everywhere here — gyoza wrappers, ramen noodles and wheat-containing soy sauce — and a tiny shared kitchen means heavy cross-contact. Not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: A traditional Chinese eatery; soy-sauce-based seasoning is standard and ordinary Japanese soy sauce contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu centers on pork, egg and seafood dishes cooked in a single small kitchen; animal cross-contact is unavoidable.

cuisine: 和食・おばんざい居酒屋（Japanese izakaya / home-style small plates）
neighborhood: ならまち（元林院町）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市元林院町9
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市元林院町9 returned [135.830154, 34.681137], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 約11席（カウンター7席＋4人掛けテーブル3卓相当の小規模店）
closed_days: 月曜日
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, quiet evening-only izakaya in a historic townhouse with little to no English support and counter-focused seating, so basic Japanese and an easygoing attitude will make a foreign student's visit much smoother.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. This is a Japanese izakaya whose dishes lean heavily on dashi and soy-sauce-based seasoning (e.g., dashi-rolled egg, braised octopus, simmered dishes), and fried items like karaage use wheat flour. Soy sauce here is standard (wheat-containing) and there is no indication of a dedicated fryer or any celiac protocol, so it is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan-friendly destination. The menu is built around fish, seafood, meat and fish/kombu dashi (braised octopus, steamed chicken, dashi-rolled egg, beef-and-potato stew). A few vegetable small plates exist (roasted eggplant, root-vegetable sunomono) but these are almost certainly seasoned with fish dashi or soy sauce, and no plant-based or veganized preparation is documented. Strict vegans should not rely on this place.

bio.background: Mangyoku occupies one of Naramachi's oldest surviving machiya, a wooden townhouse said to date to 1742. The building was formerly a geisha house known as 萬玉楼 (Mangyokuro) in the Motorinin-cho district, an area that still carries the quiet air of Nara's old entertainment quarter. Today it has been converted into a small evening izakaya; the interior has been substantially reworked, keeping period touches like a mizuya cupboard and tansu chests while no longer obviously reading as its former teahouse self.
bio.origin: ならまち（元林院町）, central Nara
bio.specialty: Carefully made home-style Japanese small plates and izakaya fare: soft-braised octopus (蛸の柔らか煮), a fragrant dashi-rolled omelet, ジャコと青唐辛子の炒り煮 (small fish with green chili), root-vegetable sunomono, steamed chicken, and seasonal simmered dishes, paired with sake, wine and whisky.
bio.philosophy: A calm, grown-up spot prized for its unhurried mood and meticulously prepared appetizers, where the quality of the otsumami signals the care of the kitchen.

anecdotes.text: The entrance is so discreet that visitors unfamiliar with it can walk right past without realizing a restaurant is there.
The 1742 townhouse retains antique furnishings such as a water cupboard (mizuya) and chests that give the room a strong sense of history.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dishes rely on standard Japanese seasoning (soy sauce, simmered/braised preparations and a dashi-rolled egg), which typically means wheat-containing soy sauce; no gluten-free soy sauce is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Vegetable plates such as the dashi-rolled egg and root-vegetable sunomono are prepared with fish/kombu dashi and soy-based seasoning, so vegetable items are not reliably plant-based.

caveats: Cash only, evening-only (18:00–23:00), closed Mondays, and very small (~11 seats), so seating is limited. No official website was found; details are drawn from a Tabelog listing and local Nara food blogs. Not a vegan, vegetarian, or gluten-free destination — dashi, soy sauce and seafood/meat are central.

cuisine: Kaiseki / kaiseki ryori (traditional multi-course Japanese cuisine) at a historic ryotei founded in 1891, served in a registered tangible cultural property building near Kasuga Taisha's first torii gate.
neighborhood: Nara Park / in front of the first torii gate of Kasuga Taisha (about a 10-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1130
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市高畑町1130 returned [135.83371, 34.680328], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 24 seats across 4 private rooms.
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-15:00 (last entry 13:00) / Dinner 17:00-20:30 (last entry 18:00). Closed Tuesdays and Wednesdays.
closed_days: Tuesday, Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: This is a formal, high-end reservation-only ryotei where the multi-course meal runs roughly 16,500-21,000 yen per person, so a foreign student should book ahead, expect a refined Japanese-language setting, and notify any dietary needs at least three business days in advance.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment. As a traditional kaiseki ryotei, dishes rely heavily on fish/kombu dashi and standard Japanese soy sauce (which contains wheat), so it is not celiac-safe by default. The restaurant does ask guests to report allergies and disliked ingredients at least three business days in advance, so gluten avoidance may be discussed when booking, but no dedicated gluten-free preparation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not vegan or vegetarian by default. Kaiseki courses here are built around seasonal fish, sea bream, and fish/kombu dashi. A signature dish is goma-dofu (sesame tofu), but courses broadly include animal ingredients. The restaurant invites guests to notify allergies and food preferences three business days in advance, so vegetarian/vegan adaptations could potentially be requested when reserving, but no fully plant-based menu is documented.

bio.origin: Nara, in front of the first torii gate of Kasuga Taisha
bio.background: Founded in 1891, Kikusuiro is one of Nara's most storied ryotei, set among World Heritage surroundings just before the first torii gate of Kasuga Taisha. Its dignified main gate was relocated from Ennjoji temple and dates to the Edo period; built from materials given by the temple, the structure is designated a registered tangible cultural property. For more than 130 years the house has welcomed distinguished guests and literary figures.
bio.specialty: Seasonal kaiseki with no fixed menu, composed each day by the head chef around the best ingredients of the moment; refined goma-dofu (sesame tofu) and whole sea bream as celebratory dishes are signatures.
bio.philosophy: To offer taste and hospitality polished by tradition, selecting seasonal ingredients at their peak and attending to every detail of setting, aroma, and presentation so guests enjoy an extraordinary experience with all five senses, while passing the craft to the next generation.

anecdotes.text: The formal main gate was moved from Ennjoji temple and is an Edo-period structure designated as a registered tangible cultural property.
Founded in 1891, the house has been visited by prominent figures and writers and is regarded as one of Nara's leading restaurants.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional kaiseki ryotei the cooking is built on fish/kombu dashi and standard Japanese seasonings; wheat-based soy sauce is typical and no wheat-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Courses center on seasonal fish and fish dashi, so plant-based dishes are not the default and cross-contact with animal ingredients is expected.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guests are asked to report food allergies and disliked ingredients at least three business days before their reservation.

positives.text: The kitchen requests advance notice of allergies and food preferences, indicating a willingness to discuss dietary needs when booking ahead.

caveats: High-end, complete-reservation-only kaiseki ryotei; meals run roughly 16,500-21,000 yen per person plus a 10% service charge. Dietary accommodations are possible only with at least three business days' advance notice and are not guaranteed. Not a celiac-safe or vegan destination.

cuisine: Handmade cakes, bread, and small cafe (patisserie / boulangerie cafe)
neighborhood: きたまち (Kitamachi), Minami-Handanaka-cho
gf_detail: This is a cake and bread shop where wheat flour is the core ingredient in essentially everything (cakes, baked goods, breads, chiffon cakes). No gluten-free offerings or allergy accommodation are mentioned on any source. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoiding diners.
vegan_detail: A patisserie specializing in cakes, macarons, and baked goods that rely on eggs, butter, and dairy. No vegan, dairy-free, or egg-free items are mentioned. Not suitable for vegan diners.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南半田中町13-1
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to [135.832138, 34.687664]; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Small cafe with limited reserved seating; primarily a takeaway cake and bread shop. Exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 11:00–17:30 (January–February: 10:00–16:00). Irregular closures (不定休); updates posted on social media. Cakes and bread often sell out by midday.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular days off; one local listing notes frequent closures on Mon/Tue/Sun — check social media for the current month)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny solo-run neighborhood cake shop where you point at the day's cakes and breads in the case and pay in cash; little to no English, but ordering pastries requires almost no conversation.
caveats: Wheat-, egg-, and dairy-based bakery with no gluten-free or vegan options — not for celiac or vegan travelers. Cash only. Irregular closing days, and cakes/breads frequently sell out by lunchtime, so go early. Whole/decorated cakes need advance reservation (about 3 days).

bio.background: LA LIBERTE is a small handmade cake and bread cafe run by a single owner who grew up in Nara's Kitamachi district. A former kindergarten teacher, she moved into culinary instruction, working as a cooking-school teacher and leading pastry classes before opening her own little shop in the neighborhood she calls home, which she describes as an honor.
bio.specialty: Seasonal cakes and decorated 'Smiley' and flower cakes, macarons, tea chiffon cake, plus a daily rotating selection of breads and baked goods; she also makes a reservation-only confiture (jam).
bio.philosophy: Everything is made one piece at a time, by hand and with heart, with the wish that every customer who visits leaves happy. She likens the craft of cake-making to the classical music she loves, saying she was drawn ever deeper into it.

anecdotes.text: The owner describes becoming captivated by cake-making, comparing it to her love of classical music: 'ケーキ作りは、大好きなクラッシック音楽にどこか似ているように思え、どんどんはまって' (cake-making felt somehow like the classical music I love, and I was drawn in deeper and deeper).
She makes each item 'ひとつひとつ丁寧に心をこめて' (one by one, carefully and with heart), hoping that everyone who comes to the little pink shop becomes happy.
Alongside the shop she teaches sweets classes, including Wilton-method decorating and recently revived parent-and-child lessons, sharing her instructor background with the community.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a wheat-flour bakery (cakes, breads, baked goods, chiffon cakes); flour is pervasive and there is no gluten-free preparation, so high cross-contamination is inherent.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Products are built on eggs, butter, and dairy (cakes, macarons, chiffon); no vegan or dairy-free line is offered, so vegan separation is not relevant here.

lead: ヤマトモダン JR奈良駅前店
name_ja: ヤマトモダン JR奈良駅前店
cuisine: 和ダイニング＆カフェ・洋食（大和野菜・ヤマトポーク、米粉パンケーキ）
neighborhood: 三条町（JR奈良駅前、徒歩約4分）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市三条町485 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市三条町485 returned [135.822571, 34.682156], within the central Nara box, near JR Nara Station. Confirmed.
seats_text: 45 seats total, including counter and table seating plus one private room for 6-12 people.
closed_days: Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, modern townhouse-style cafe and dining spot that accepts cards and major mobile payments, so a foreign student can order comfortably with a few words of Japanese and pointing at the menu.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. The kitchen is built around Japanese and Western dishes that rely on wheat-based seasonings (soy sauce, teriyaki sauce) and breaded/fried items like Nara-style chicken nanban. The rice-flour pancakes (米粉パンケーキ) are made with rice flour rather than wheat flour, which appeals to people reducing wheat, but there is no documented dedicated gluten-free handling or cross-contamination control, so it is not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Primarily an omnivore meat-and-vegetable restaurant. Signature dishes feature Yamato pork (ヤマトポーク) spare ribs and chicken, and most Japanese savory dishes use fish dashi, so the bulk of the menu is not vegan. Local Yamato vegetables (大和野菜) appear in some dishes, but no confirmed fully vegan menu items are documented; a vegan diner would need to ask directly about specific vegetable plates.

bio.background: A modern Japanese dining bar and cafe set in a traditional townhouse-style space just steps from JR Nara Station. It is part of a small Nara restaurant group built around やまと庵, which also runs sister venues including やまと庵本店 and 大和野菜と串焼きやまと, all sharing a focus on Nara's regional ingredients.
bio.specialty: Dishes built on Nara-grown ingredients: tender, sweetly marbled Yamato pork (ヤマトポーク) served as teriyaki-glazed spare ribs, Nara-style chicken nanban, and local Yamato vegetables. By day it works as a cafe, with rice-flour pancakes (米粉パンケーキ) that have a scone-like texture served with maple syrup, fresh cream, and fruit; in the evening it shifts into a dining bar with local sake and small plates.
bio.philosophy: The parent group treats Yamato vegetables as Nara heritage produce prized for flavor, aroma, and texture, and emphasizes honoring the work of local producers by cooking in a way that brings out the ingredients' natural taste rather than masking it.
roles: operator: 株式会社MARIN'S (やまと庵 group)

anecdotes.text: The cafe's rice-flour pancakes are described as having a scone-like texture that pairs with maple syrup and fruit-topped fresh cream, a daytime signature alongside the savory Nara dishes.
The group sources from local farmers and even Yoshino hunters and frames its cooking as preserving Nara food culture, cherishing carefully grown Yamato vegetables.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Signature savory dishes such as the Yamato pork spare ribs are prepared with a teriyaki/soy-based sauce, which in Japan is typically wheat-containing soy sauce; no wheat-free seasoning is documented.

positives.text: The daytime rice-flour pancakes (米粉パンケーキ) are made with rice flour instead of wheat flour, which may interest diners cutting back on wheat, though no celiac-level allergy handling is documented.

caveats: The descriptor "三条本町" reflects the station-area name; the registered chō and geocoded address is 三条町485. The nara-gourmet.com page partly describes a sister branch (奈良スタンド ヤマトモダン at 角振町), so its hours/dishes were not used for this JR-ekimae location. GF/vegan safety details are inferred from menu descriptions, not from an allergy-handling statement by the restaurant.

cuisine: カフェ・ダイニングバー（韓国料理・夜カフェ）
neighborhood: 三条町（JR・近鉄奈良駅間、三条通エリア）
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The menu centers on Korean dishes that are inherently wheat-heavy: yangnyeom chicken has a fried wheat-flour batter, tteokbokki and seafood pancakes (haemul pajeon) use wheat, and Korean sauces typically contain wheat-based soy sauce and gochujang. No dedicated fryer or allergy handling is documented, so this is not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is advertised. The kitchen is meat- and seafood-forward (Korean fried chicken, seafood pancakes), and even sides like tteokbokki commonly use fish/anchovy-based broth and dairy (their tomato-cream tteokbokki). A vegan would need to ask in person, but options appear minimal and cross-contact with meat/seafood is likely.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市三条町495-4 バナナビル2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the chō-block coordinate for 奈良市三条町495; within the central Nara box. Building (バナナビル 2F) sits on Sanjo-dori, about a 2-minute walk from JR Nara Station.
seats_text: Official site lists 27 seats (Tabelog lists 23); small with a semi-private back room for groups of 6+.
hours_raw: Tue–Fri (and weekdays before holidays): 11:30–14:30 (lunch, L.O. 14:00), 17:00–00:00 (food L.O. 23:00, drink L.O. 23:30). Sat & holidays: 11:30–00:00 continuous. Closed Sun & Mon. Irregular closures occur — check Instagram.
closed_days: 日曜・月曜（不定休あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, youthful Korean cafe-bar run by young owners where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you through, though detailed allergy questions may be hard without some Japanese.
caveats: Seat count differs between sources (27 official vs 23 Tabelog). Hours are subject to irregular closures, so confirm via their Instagram before visiting. Card and QR payments accepted, but no dietary or allergy accommodation is documented.

bio.background: SomechoA is a small Korean-style day cafe and night bar tucked along Sanjo-dori between JR and Kintetsu Nara stations. It is run by three siblings, and the name itself reflects that: in Korean 'Sam' means 'three' and 'choA' means 'like/love,' so the place is built around the trio and the things they love.
bio.origin: Korea-inspired (Korean cafe and izakaya culture), in central Nara
bio.specialty: Korean fried chicken (yangnyeom) with a crispy batter and house-made yangnyeom sauce, creamy tomato tteokbokki, seafood pancakes, and night-cafe desserts including handmade gateau chocolate and New York cheesecake sold from the entrance showcase. A signature playful item is the Nara-themed 'deer pudding,' a local twist on Korea's animal-shaped puddings.
bio.philosophy: A relaxed hideaway that doubles as a lunch and night cafe and an evening drinking spot, filling the room with the siblings' favorite Korean food, sweets, and drinks.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name comes from Korean: 'Sam' (three) plus 'choA' (like/love), referring to the three siblings who run it and pack it with their favorite things.
They make a Nara-exclusive deer-shaped pudding, a regional riff on the trendy animal-shaped Korean puddings.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Core menu items use wheat: yangnyeom fried chicken has a fried wheat batter, and seafood pancakes (pajeon) and tteokbokki are wheat-based, sharing a kitchen with no documented separation.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Korean sauces such as the house yangnyeom sauce typically rely on wheat-containing soy sauce and gochujang; no gluten-free sauces are indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Kitchen is meat- and seafood-forward (Korean fried chicken, seafood pancakes) and desserts use dairy/eggs, so vegan cross-contact is likely and unmanaged.

cuisine: 老舗の名曲喫茶（コーヒー専門・軽食）/ Classic music kissaten — coffee specialist with light fare
neighborhood: 東向北町（近鉄奈良駅すぐ、東向商店街北）
vegan_detail: A traditional coffee specialist. Black coffee, non-caffeine teas and juices are plant-based, but the food offerings are limited to items like buttered toast and rare cheesecake, which contain dairy. There is no dedicated vegan menu and no documented vegan accommodation.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The food menu centers on wheat-based items (toast) and there is no gluten-free offering or allergen handling documented. Coffee itself is naturally gluten-free, but this is a wheat-forward kissaten, not a celiac-safe destination.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向北町8-4 大学堂ビル2F
geocode_note: Geocoded to chō-banchi level (東向北町8) via GSI; within Nara box. The shop is on the 2nd floor of the Daigakudo Building above a used bookstore.
seats_text: 14 seats (4 counter, 10 table)
hours_raw: Tue–Sun 10:30–18:30; closed Monday (per Tabelog). Note: other listings cite 11:00–18:00 and some weekday 10:00–19:00, and one source lists Sun & Mon closed — hours and closed days vary by source, so confirm on arrival.
closed_days: Monday (定休日). Note: some sources also list Sunday as closed — verify locally.
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, old-school Japanese coffee house run by an elegant proprietress; little to no English is expected, but pointing at the menu and a calm demeanor will see you warmly through a coffee and a slice of cheesecake.
caveats: Cash only. Hours and closed days differ between sources, so it is safest to visit midday Tuesday–Saturday. Up the stairs on the 2nd floor above a used bookshop; no parking.

bio.background: Opened in 1974 on the second floor of a building above a used bookshop, just a couple of minutes from Kintetsu Nara Station in the northern reach of the Higashimuki shopping arcade. The name El Mundo is Spanish for 'the world,' reflecting the owner's wish to create a worldly, cultured refuge within a modest corner of Nara. It is a rare survivor of the old kissaten tradition in a station-front area where such places have nearly vanished.
bio.specialty: A coffee specialist offering more than twenty preparations — blends, single-origin straights, and arranged coffees — anchored by the house El Mundo Blend (mild) and a fragrant charcoal-roasted coffee. The kitchen keeps it simple with buttered toast and a beloved rare cheesecake, plus non-caffeine teas and juices.
bio.philosophy: An invitation to briefly forget everyday life and to slow down over coffee and music. The room is a curated European reverie — retro furniture, stained-glass lamps, wall paintings, and European antique cups and saucers, with classical music and opera spinning from an analog record player chosen to the owner's own taste.

anecdotes.text: The proprietress, an elegant 'madam' deeply versed in coffee culture, personally bought many of the paintings and stained-glass lamps overseas; around ten artworks hang on the walls beneath lamps suspended from the ceiling, and she selects the opera and classical records she herself loves.
The space is built around an analog record player and European antique cups and saucers, designed so guests can slowly enjoy coffee and music away from the everyday.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based toast is a core menu item in a small kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Food offerings are dairy-based (buttered toast, rare cheesecake); no plant-based food preparation is documented.

positives.text: Black coffee, non-caffeine teas and juices are naturally plant-based and gluten-free beverage options.

cuisine: Owner-run café — handmade crêpes (sweet and savory) and pasta lunches
neighborhood: Higashimuki-Kita shopping street, near Kintetsu-Nara Station
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The café's signature crêpes are made from 100% domestic wheat flour (a strong/weak flour blend), and the lunch menu also features pasta — both are wheat-based. No gluten-free preparations or dedicated handling are documented, so this is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Mostly not vegan. The crêpe batter is made with milk, eggs and butter, and there is fruit/salad and avocado-based crêpe content, but the core sweet and savory crêpes contain dairy and eggs. Some fruit- or vegetable-forward items (e.g. avocado & green salad crêpe, fruit crêpes) may be adaptable, but a fully vegan meal is not advertised — ask in person about egg/dairy-free options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向北町22
geocode_note: GSI address search returned exact match for 奈良県奈良市東向北町22; coordinates within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 26 seats across two floors — counter seating on the first floor (near the owner), sofa seating upstairs.
hours_raw: Mon-Tue 08:00-19:00; Wed 09:00-17:00; Thu closed; Fri-Sun 08:00-18:00 (official site notes hours can be irregular/不定)
closed_days: Thursday (note: one source lists Tuesday as closed; the café also states hours can be irregular)
cultural_comfort_note: The owner speaks English, there is an English menu, and the café offers free Wi-Fi and power outlets, so a foreign student can order comfortably and linger.
caveats: Sources disagree on payment: the English listing states cards, e-money and QR payments are accepted, while a Japanese aggregator says no cards — bring cash to be safe. Closed day is listed as Thursday on some sources and Tuesday on another, and the owner notes hours can be irregular, so confirm before a special trip.

bio.background: A small, owner-run coffee shop tucked into the middle of Nara's Higashimuki-Kita covered shopping street, a short walk from Kintetsu-Nara Station. You will spot it by its distinctive young-grass-green ('wakakusa') storefront. The café spreads over two floors: a counter on the ground floor where guests can chat with the owner, and a relaxed sofa space upstairs.
bio.specialty: Handmade crêpes are the centerpiece — the batter uses only 100% domestic wheat flour (a blend of strong and soft flours), milk, eggs, unsalted butter and granulated sugar, with no additives, giving a chewy texture. There are more than ten savory crêpes and more than ten sweet crêpes, plus pasta-and-salad lunch sets and a popular fruit-piled sweet crêpe.
bio.philosophy: An emphasis on simple, additive-free, body-friendly cooking and an unhurried, conversational neighborhood-café atmosphere.

anecdotes.text: The crêpes are made from a 100% domestic wheat-flour blend with milk, eggs, unsalted butter and sugar only — no additives — for a deliberately chewy texture, with 10-plus savory and 10-plus sweet varieties.
The shop is set in the middle of the Higashimuki-Kita shopping street with a young-grass-green storefront, with counter seating downstairs and sofa seating upstairs.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is central to the kitchen — crêpe batter is 100% domestic wheat flour and lunches include pasta — so flour is handled throughout; no gluten-free preparation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Crêpe batter contains milk, eggs and butter, so dairy and egg are used across the core menu.

positives.text: An English menu and English-speaking owner make it easy to ask about ingredients before ordering.

cuisine: 懐石・割烹（日本料理）／Kaiseki & kappou Japanese cuisine
neighborhood: 不審ケ辻子町（ならまち北側、近鉄奈良駅と奈良ホテルの間）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市不審ケ辻子町1
geocode_note: Geocoded the CURRENT (relocated) address 奈良市不審ケ辻子町1 via GSI; result 34.679386, 135.832718 falls within the central-Nara box. The restaurant relocated here on 2025-12-06 from its former 東城戸町16-1 (Mochiidono) location given in the target brief.
seats_text: At the current (post-relocation) machiya: first-floor counter seating plus two tables, and an 8-person private tatami room upstairs that welcomes families. Reported layout is roughly counter (approx. 4) + 2 tables (approx. 6) downstairs; exact count not officially published. (The former location was listed at 16 seats.)
hours_raw: 昼 12:00-14:30（一斉スタート）／夜 18:00-22:00（L.O. 19:00）。定休日：日曜、第1・第3月曜（さらに第2・第4月曜と第1・第3火曜は昼のみ休み）。完全予約制。
closed_days: 日曜定休。第1・第3月曜は終日休み。月曜の一部と火曜の一部は昼のみ休業（隔週で変動）。営業日でも完全予約制のため要事前予約。
cultural_comfort_note: This is an intimate, reservation-only kaiseki counter where the chef serves an omakase course — comfortable for a curious student who can manage some basic Japanese or book through the online reservation system, but it is a special-occasion splurge rather than a casual drop-in.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free establishment. This is a traditional kaiseki/kappou kitchen built on fish dashi and Japanese seasonings, and standard soy sauce contains wheat; no gluten-free protocol is documented. A celiac diner should not assume safety. Because it is a fully reservation-based omakase, you could in principle phone ahead (0742-93-6222) to ask whether the chef can adapt, but no GF accommodation has been confirmed.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The cooking centers on seasonal Yamato vegetables but within a Japanese-cuisine framework that relies on fish/bonito dashi, and the house signature is an egg dish (thick low-temperature tamagoyaki), so courses are not plant-based. As a fully reservation-only omakase, a vegetarian/vegan request might be discussed in advance, but no plant-based menu is documented.
caveats: The target brief lists the former 東城戸町 (Mochiidono) address; the restaurant relocated to 不審ケ辻子町1 on 2025-12-06, and the linked Tabelog page is the archived pre-relocation listing (marked 移転). All logistics here reflect the CURRENT location. The chef's personal name is reported in secondary coverage but not on the official site, so it is given at medium confidence. Exact current seat count is not officially published.

bio.chef_name: 松室 克哉（Matsumuro Katsuya, reported）
bio.background: The owner-chef trained at respected Japanese restaurants — including Kawanami in Nara and a kappou house in Osaka — before opening his own counter in central Nara.
bio.specialty: An omakase kaiseki/kappou menu built on seasonal Yamato vegetables and premium local ingredients. The house signature is a thick tamagoyaki egg cake cooked slowly at low temperature for about three hours, giving an almost dessert-like, custardy texture that guests often order a second helping of.
bio.philosophy: 美味真心 — sincere hospitality as the source of true flavor: an unhurried, set-course experience where the chef quietly guides diners through the seasons.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant occupies a roughly century-old former lumber-merchant's machiya from the late Taisho era, fully renovated over about a year into a calm counter-and-tatami space; in December 2025 the kitchen moved to a townhouse on the lane between Kintetsu Nara Station and Naramachi.
Diners describe a meal that feels like a delightfully swift, thoroughly enjoyable 90 minutes, with the chef's relaxed, approachable manner and no pressure to drink.
The signature thick tamagoyaki is slow-baked at low temperature for about three hours and is a frequent reorder among guests.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a traditional Japanese kaiseki/kappou kitchen, dishes rely on standard Japanese seasonings; ordinary soy sauce contains wheat and no gluten-free protocol is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cooking is built on fish/bonito dashi and features an egg-based signature dish, so dishes are not plant-based and there is no documented vegan preparation.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Service is fully reservation-only omakase, which in principle allows guests to phone (0742-93-6222) to discuss dietary needs ahead of time, but no specific allergy-handling policy is published.

cuisine: Japanese tea café and farmhouse restaurant
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. The set lunch centers on hojicha rice and vegetables, but it includes homemade miso soup (miso typically contains barley/wheat-derived koji) and a tofu hamburger likely containing breadcrumbs/binder, plus seasoning that may include soy sauce — none verified as wheat-free. Not celiac-safe without prior arrangement; anyone needing strict GF must phone ahead when reserving.
vegan_detail: The cooking is strongly vegetable-forward (farm-grown organic vegetables, hojicha rice, homemade miso from their own soybeans), which reads vegetarian-friendly, but the reservation set is a fixed homemade lunch and individual dishes are not labeled vegan. The miso soup base (dashi) is not documented as plant-based and the menu has changed seasonally; vegans should call ahead to confirm whether a dashi-free, animal-free set can be prepared.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市中之庄町458
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 奈良県奈良市中之庄町458 returns [135.917404, 34.655792]. The longitude (135.9174) falls OUTSIDE the central Nara box (135.76–135.90); the latitude is in range. This is consistent with the venue's true location in the rural Tahara (田原) district, roughly 20 minutes by car east of Kintetsu Nara Station — NOT the Takabatake/Naramachi area stated in the target brief. Coordinates are accurate to the address but the place is not a central Nara eatery.
neighborhood: Tahara district (Nakanosho-cho), eastern rural Nara City — about 20 min by car from Kintetsu Nara Station, near the Takayama Drive Way; not central Nara/Takabatake
seats_text: Seat count not published; an intimate converted-cowshed farmhouse café with table seating overlooking the tea fields. Reservation lunches are described as taking small groups (roughly 4–15 people).
hours_raw: Café: Sat–Sun 11:00–17:00 (some sources list 10:00–17:00). Lunch: by full advance reservation (phone, generally about 2 days ahead), served from 11:30. Closed seasonally during tea-harvest preparation (around mid-May to mid-June, reopening about July).
closed_days: Weekdays as a walk-in café (weekday lunch is reservation-only); irregular closures (不定休); closed seasonally during tea harvest prep (~mid-May to mid-June)
cultural_comfort_note: A welcoming family farm café that hosts both Japanese and international visitors, but it is deep in the countryside, reached by car, and run in Japanese, so a foreign student should reserve by phone with some Japanese help and not expect English menus.
caveats: The target brief places this in 高畑 (central Nara / Naramachi east side); that is incorrect. The venue is in the rural Tahara district (中之庄町), about 20 minutes by car east of Kintetsu Nara Station, and its geocoded longitude falls outside the central Nara box. It is a real, currently-operating tea farm café, but it does NOT qualify as a central Nara eatery. Hours are irregular and the lunch is fully reservation-based; confirm by phone before visiting.

bio.background: Takenishi Noen is an organic Yamato tea farm whose family has grown tea in the Tahara hills of eastern Nara since the Edo period. It is now run by the fifth generation, with husband Nagahito tending the fields; Takako, who came from Osaka and a career in fashion before marrying into the tea-farming family, fell in love with Yamato tea and opened the Yuchaan café about 13 years ago in a converted cowshed beside the tea gardens.
bio.origin: Tahara district, eastern Nara City (Yamato tea country)
bio.specialty: Earthenware-pot rice cooked with the farm's own hojicha and homegrown rice, served with a seasonal spread of their organic vegetables (sesame-dressed eggplant, potato croquettes, marinated four-angle beans with tomato, ginger tofu hamburger) and homemade miso soup made from their own soybeans, finished with farm tea and hojicha jelly. Premium single-origin teas include 'Kizuna no Issen' from a tea plant said to be roughly 190 years old.
bio.philosophy: The farm switched to organic, pesticide-free cultivation in 1995, guided by a wish to protect the health of the people who drink the tea, the people who grow it, and the surrounding community, and to make Yamato tea approachable while passing tea and farming culture to the next generation.

anecdotes.text: The café occupies a cowshed the family remodeled about 13 years ago; spread by word of mouth, it has become a country 'station' where new connections form, drawing both domestic and overseas visitors, and the owners host an autumn harvest festival that brings people to the once-quiet region.
Every dish in the reservation lunch is made by hand by Takako, prepared carefully one at a time, paired with tea brewed by a certified Japanese-tea instructor.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The set lunch includes homemade miso soup made from the farm's own soybeans and other seasoned vegetable dishes; miso and standard seasonings are typical wheat/barley sources and none are documented as gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A ginger-sauce tofu hamburger appears on the documented lunch and dishes are not labeled vegan; the dashi/stock base for the miso soup is not specified as plant-based.

positives.text: Cooking is built around the farm's own organic, pesticide-free vegetables, homegrown rice and homegrown-soybean miso, all hand-prepared to order, which makes accommodating dietary needs more feasible if arranged when reserving.

cuisine: ベーカリー（パン・サンドイッチ・惣菜パン・菓子パン、米粉パンが充実）
gf_detail: A wide range of rice-flour breads (米粉パン) is a signature here — savory rolls like 米粉アボカドチキンサンド and 米粉塩サバ, plus sweet ones like 米粉あんバター and 米粉チョコクリーム — prized for a mochi-like chewy texture. However, this is a conventional bakery that also bakes large amounts of wheat bread, sandwiches and pastries in the same kitchen, and no source claims gluten-free production, dedicated equipment, or celiac safety. Rice-flour items here are a texture/variety choice, not allergen-controlled, so they are not safe for celiac diners. No GF claim found on any fetched source.
vegan_detail: No vegan labeling found. Most breads use dairy and eggs (e.g. chocolate cream with fresh milk, butter croissants, anbutter), and many savory items contain meat or fish (smoked chicken, grilled mackerel, sausage). Plain breads such as French bread or some rice-flour rolls may happen to be plant-based, but ingredients are not documented, so a vegan diner would need to ask item by item.
