No dedicated vegan options confirmed.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市鍋屋町51
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市鍋屋町51; within the Nara box.
neighborhood: 鍋屋町（近鉄奈良駅から約340m、奈良女子大学の北側）
seats_text: No seating — takeaway only; breads are displayed on shelves and bought to go.
hours_raw: 7:50–18:00（売り切れ次第終了）。土・日・祝休み。（タベログ表記では平日7:50–17:00とする情報もあり）
closed_days: 土曜・日曜・祝日（Saturdays, Sundays, holidays）
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed, point-and-pay neighborhood bakery where you simply pick breads from the shelf, so it is very easy for a foreign student even with little Japanese — just arrive in the morning for the best selection.
caveats: Hours vary slightly between sources (Tabelog lists weekday 7:50–17:00; the local feature lists 7:50–18:00, closing when sold out). It closes when bread sells out, and variety thins out by afternoon. Closed weekends and holidays. No seating. Rice-flour breads are a specialty but are NOT gluten-free/celiac-safe (shared wheat kitchen).

bio.background: A small neighborhood bakery tucked into the Nabeya-cho streets just north of Nara Women's University, a short walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. Its mornings belong to a steady mix of female students, nearby office and government workers, and longtime locals who stop in for the day's bread. The previously casual storefront was given a more stylish makeover, but the rhythm is unchanged: shelves fill up early and the selection thins as the day goes on, often selling out before closing.
bio.specialty: Best known for its unusually wide range of rice-flour (米粉) breads, prized for a soft, mochi-like chewiness. Savory standouts include 米粉アボカドチキンサンド (rice-flour roll with avocado, smoked chicken and mustard) and 米粉塩サバ (with grilled salted mackerel), alongside sweet versions like 米粉あんバター and a 米粉チョコクリーム filled with fresh-milk chocolate cream and topped with almond-cookie crumble. Beyond the rice-flour line, the shop turns out around twenty kinds of sandwiches plus rock-salt-and-French-butter croissants, cinnamon rolls, Amaou-strawberry and white-chocolate bagels, and apple pie with domestic apples.
bio.philosophy: An approach noted in local coverage is drawing flavor ideas from restaurant dishes rather than typical bakery fare, pairing them with the distinctive rice-flour dough for both texture and a lighter, nutrition-conscious appeal.

anecdotes.text: Local coverage highlights that the rice-flour breads are marketed not just for their mochi-like chewiness but as offering '美容＆栄養も満点' — strong on beauty and nutrition too.
Its regulars are described as '女子大生、官公庁の人たち、地元民' (women's-university students, government workers, and locals), who file in through the morning before popular items sell out.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a conventional bakery producing large quantities of wheat bread, sandwiches and pastries; rice-flour breads share the same kitchen and equipment, so cross-contamination with wheat is effectively certain. Not celiac-safe.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Breads and fillings frequently contain dairy, eggs, meat and fish; no vegan-specific handling or labeling is documented, so plant-based items cannot be assumed free of animal-product contact.

positives.text: Offers an unusually broad selection of rice-flour (米粉) breads, both savory and sweet, for those simply seeking wheat-reduced variety (not allergen control).

cuisine: 創作和食・居酒屋（手延べ素麺・にゅうめん、奈良野菜の創作料理、奈良産米のおむすび、奈良クラフトビール・地酒）
gf_detail: No gluten-free program found on any page. The menu centers on hand-stretched sōmen and udon (both wheat noodles), wheat-based rice-ball and izakaya dishes, and Japanese craft beer (barley/wheat). Creative Japanese and izakaya cooking here relies heavily on soy sauce and dashi, which typically contain wheat. There is no indication of dedicated gluten-free preparation, so this is not a safe choice for celiac diners. A celiac traveler would need to confirm specific dishes directly with the owner and accept cross-contamination risk.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. The kitchen emphasizes Nara vegetables (Yamato yasai), local rice onigiri, corn fritters and seasonal vegetable side dishes, so plant-forward items do exist, but most savory dishes in a Japanese izakaya use fish dashi, bonito or meat (e.g. the menu lists pepper stuffed with meat, and an Asuka milk-miso soup). A vegetarian or vegan diner could likely be accommodated for some vegetable and rice dishes by asking the owner in advance, but nothing is confirmed dashi-free or animal-free.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市林小路町23-1 いそかわビル2F
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良市林小路町23-1, within the central Nara bounding box.
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺・三条通り／ひがしむき（林小路町）
seats_text: 35 seats (8 counter, 28 tables), accommodates groups up to about 35
hours_raw: Mon-Thu 8:30-11:00 (LO 10:30), 11:30-16:00, 17:00-22:00 (LO 21:00); Fri-Sat 8:30-11:00 (LO 10:30), 11:30-16:00, 17:00-23:00 (LO 22:00); Sun 8:30-11:00, 11:30-16:00, 17:00-21:00 (dinner reservation only). Morning service (8:30-11:00) is offered seasonally (spring/autumn). Closed irregularly.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run izakaya focused on local Nara food where some Japanese helps; the owner works in Nara tourism and is used to visitors, but a foreign student should be ready to order in basic Japanese and confirm any dietary needs directly.
caveats: Hours are extensive but the morning slot is seasonal and Sunday dinner is reservation-only; closures are irregular, so check ahead. No documented gluten-free or vegan program. Payment is card/e-money/QR friendly, but cash is required for orders under 1,300 yen.

bio.background: Raden is a small second-floor eatery just off the busy Sanjo-dori and Higashimuki shopping streets a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is run by a proprietor who is also a registered Nara City tourism ambassador and wears many hats around town as a tour guide, regional PR videographer and planner. That civic, show-Nara-to-the-world spirit runs through the whole place: ingredients are sourced from named local producers, including rice from mountain villages such as Soni and hand-stretched noodles from the Yoshino/Kawakami area.
bio.specialty: Hand-stretched somen and nyumen from the Yoshino area, plump 'zabuton udon', and onigiri made from Nara-grown rice, alongside Nara-vegetable creative dishes like corn fritters and a milk-and-miso Asuka soup. By night it leans into a curated lineup of Japanese craft beer and local sake.
bio.philosophy: Promote and deepen appreciation for Nara through its regional ingredients and culinary heritage, putting local farmers' rice, vegetables and noodles at the center of the table.

anecdotes.text: The shop changes character through the day: a morning and lunch spot serving Nara rice balls and noodle sets, then an evening bar billed as 'the last drink' with the owner's hand-picked craft beers and local sake chosen to suit each guest.

The owner is a Nara City tourism ambassador who actively sources from local producers such as Soni village rice and Kawakami/Yoshino-area hand-stretched noodles as part of a mission to promote the region through food.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat-based hand-stretched somen, nyumen and udon are core menu items, so shared cooking water and surfaces make cross-contamination likely for gluten-sensitive diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: No allergen or gluten-free information is published; as a Japanese izakaya the seasonings almost certainly include standard wheat-containing soy sauce, but this is not confirmed on any page.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu items include meat (pepper stuffed with meat) and a milk-and-miso Asuka soup, and most savory dishes likely use fish dashi, so vegan diners cannot assume vegetable dishes are animal-free without asking.

positives.text: Vegetable-forward Nara dishes (Yamato vegetables, corn fritters, seasonal sides) and rice onigiri are available, giving plant-leaning diners some options to discuss with the owner.

lead: エル・ムンド（珈琲屋 エルムンド）
name_ja: 珈琲屋 エル・ムンド
cuisine: 純喫茶・名曲喫茶 (classic kissaten / classical-music coffee house)
neighborhood: 東向北商店街 (Higashimuki Kita arcade)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向北町8-4 大学堂ビル2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to chō-block level (東向北町8); coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 14 seats (4 counter, 10 table)
closed_days: Mondays
cultural_comfort_note: A quiet, adult-oriented classical-music coffee house run by a Japanese proprietress; a calm spot to sit with a coffee, though expect a Japanese-speaking, hushed atmosphere rather than English service.
vegan_detail: This is a coffee specialty shop, not a meal restaurant. Coffee can be ordered black, and there are non-caffeine teas and juices. Food offerings are limited to toast and a cheesecake, which are not vegan. No vegan menu is documented; ask about milk-free options.
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination. Food is limited to toast (wheat) and cheesecake. Beverages like black coffee, tea and juice contain no gluten, but there is no dedicated gluten-free handling and no celiac safety information.
caveats: Reported hours vary slightly across sources (11:00-18:00, 10:30-18:30, and 10:00-18:30); confirm before a long trip. Located up a steep staircase on the 2nd floor above a used bookstore (marked by a Nipper-dog figurine), so it is not step-free. Payment is most likely cash only (typical for this style of old kissaten); confirm card acceptance.

bio.background: El Mundo (Spanish for "the world") opened in 1974 on the second floor above a used bookstore in the Higashimuki Kita arcade, a couple of minutes north of Kintetsu Nara Station. You reach it by climbing a steep staircase marked at the bottom by a Nipper-dog figurine. Decades on, longtime visitors describe it as essentially unchanged.
bio.specialty: More than 20 coffees, from house blends to single-origin and arranged drinks, served alongside a small selection of non-caffeine tea, juice, toast, and a cheesecake. The house El Mundo Blend is a signature.
bio.philosophy: A nostalgic, adult retreat where you can step away from daily life. The proprietress curates classical records from a collection said to run into the thousands, playing requests on an analog player and choosing pieces even for vague wishes like "something by Mozart" or "a violin concerto."

anecdotes.text: The room is filled with retro furnishings, stained-glass lamps, wall paintings, and European antique cups and saucers, with classical masterpieces flowing from an analog turntable.

A visitor who first came as a student returned decades later and found the place unchanged, with shelves crammed full of records and a counter set with beautiful antique cups; the owner will pick a record even from a vague request.

positives.text: Coffee can be ordered black and there are non-caffeine teas and juices, which suit a traveler avoiding wheat or animal products as a drink-only stop.

cuisine: Small independent one-house cafe known for homemade gratin and chiffon cakes
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. The menu centers on wheat-based dishes: gratin served over pasta or inside a baguette, sandwiches, and chiffon cakes (made with wheat flour). No gluten-free options or accommodations are documented, and this is not a celiac-safe destination.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. Signature items use dairy and animal products: gratin includes cheese, cream/bechamel, salmon, chicken or hamburger, while chiffon cakes use eggs and dairy. A plant-based eater would have very limited options and should ask in advance; no vegan menu is documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市本子守町13-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from address via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: Motokomorichō, central Nara — along Yasuragi-no-michi, next to Isagawa (Isikawa) Shrine, about a 4-5 minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
seats_text: Seat count not published. Two-story house with a ground-floor takeout/retail counter and a second-floor dining area; described as a small, calm space.
hours_raw: 10:00-19:00; closed Wednesdays (open if Wednesday is a public holiday)
closed_days: Wednesdays (open when a Wednesday falls on a public holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed neighborhood cafe where a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will carry you through; bring cash and don't expect English support.
caveats: Cash only and reservations are not accepted. Closed Wednesdays. Not suitable for celiac or strict vegan diets — the core menu is wheat-, egg- and dairy-based.

bio.background: SAVAS CAFE sits in a crisp white one-house building along Yasuragi-no-michi, right beside Isagawa Shrine and just a few minutes' walk from the bustle of Kintetsu Nara Station. The cafe spreads over two floors: a takeout and retail counter on the ground floor, and an airy upstairs dining room with high ceilings, white walls, cheerful red accents on the window frames and furniture, and big windows looking out over the street. A ceiling fan keeps the room comfortable even in summer.
bio.specialty: The kitchen is built around two homemade specialties: gratin and chiffon cake. The gratin comes either baked in an ovenproof dish over pasta or tucked inside a baguette, with fillings that rotate through combinations like salmon and corn, spinach and chicken, or hamburger and broccoli, typically served as a set with salad and a drink. The chiffon cakes are the other star — light, finely textured and only gently sweet, offered in around five rotating varieties such as matcha, cocoa-banana, plain, apple rum-raisin and seasonal flavors, sold by the slice or as whole cakes. A roasted-tea jelly parfait made with Yamato tea and fruit-and-custard parfaits round out the dessert menu.
bio.philosophy: The place is run with a clear emphasis on comfort and relaxation — staff will even suggest the best seat for the temperature that day, a small touch that captures the cafe's attentive, unhurried spirit.

anecdotes.text: Staff proactively recommend the most comfortable seat depending on the temperature, reflecting the cafe's relaxed, guest-first attitude.
The chiffon cakes draw on a deep repertoire — described as rotating through well over a hundred recipes across the year, including seasonal flavors.

lead: みやけ 旧鴻池邸表屋
name_ja: みやけ 旧鴻池邸表屋
cuisine: Wagashi (Japanese sweets) and dessert café — house-made anko parfaits, anmitsu, zenzai, and seasonal kakigori (shaved ice)
cultural_comfort_note: A foreign student can comfortably order picture-and-price sweets here, but staff are unlikely to discuss allergens in English, so bring a translation aid if you have dietary needs.
gf_detail: This is a traditional wagashi and sweets café with no gluten-free program. Anmitsu, kuzu ice, anko, and kakigori toppings vary, and many Japanese sweets and syrups can contain or contact wheat (e.g. wheat-based mochi/dango/wafers). No dedicated equipment or celiac handling is documented, so it is not safe for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The core product is anko (sweet red bean paste) made from high-quality beans and sugar, and items like zenzai, anmitsu, and kuzu (arrowroot) ice are plant-based at their base. However, parfaits and several kakigori use dairy (milk, cream) and toppings may include non-vegan elements, and no vegan labeling exists. A vegan would need to ask and select carefully; some bean-paste and arrowroot items may be suitable.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市鳥見町1-5-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode for 奈良県奈良市鳥見町1-5-1 returned [135.728836, 34.692162]. Longitude 135.7288 falls west of the central-Nara box (135.76–135.90), consistent with the shop's location near Tomio in western Nara, so coordinates set to null per the box rule.
seats_text: Tabelog lists 50 seats; the café is spacious with tatami seating and garden views (reports of 100+ tatami places at peak).
hours_raw: 10:00–17:30 (L.O. 17:00); closed Sundays. Opening time may vary slightly by season.
closed_days: Sundays
neighborhood: Torimi-cho, near Tomio Station (Kintetsu Nara Line), western Nara
caveats: Located in western Nara near Tomio Station, roughly 8 minutes' walk — somewhat outside the central Nara/Naramachi tourist core. Very popular; waits can reportedly reach up to two hours at peak times. No reservations. Closed Sundays.

bio.background: みやけ 旧鴻池邸表屋 is the flagship café and sweets shop of Miyake, an anko (red bean paste) maker founded in 1908. The café occupies a remarkable building: the former townhouse of the wealthy Konoike merchant family, originally an enormous structure (roughly 65m wide by 36m deep) in Osaka's Imahashi merchant district. When the historic residence faced demolition in 1979, Miyake's second-generation president, Miyake Kazuma, rescued it and relocated it to Torimi-cho in Nara, where it reopened as a café and shop preserving the Edo-period merchant aesthetic with a central courtyard garden.
bio.specialty: House-made anko desserts: parfaits, anmitsu, zenzai, and seasonal kakigori (shaved ice). Signature kakigori include a two-variety strawberry ice using local Tsukuda Farm berries, a kinako-and-black-molasses ice over the company's premium anko, and a two-tone matcha-and-milk ice. Nara's specialty kuzu (arrowroot) appears in a jiggly frozen kuzu ice candy.
bio.philosophy: The maker's guiding principle is starting with high-quality beans (良質の豆を使用すること), with experienced craftspeople simmering beans individually to control flavor, color, and texture at every stage — and preserving a piece of merchant heritage by saving the Konoike building.

anecdotes.text: The café building is the former Konoike family townhouse, relocated from Osaka to Nara in 1979 to save it from demolition; it preserves the front storefront (omoteya) and rear residence separated by a courtyard.
President Miyake Kazuma said he wanted to 'somehow retain Semba's symbol,' motivating the rescue and relocation of the historic residence.
Because the parent company runs a bean-paste factory, the café's anko-based desserts highlight premium house-made red bean paste.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Several parfaits and kakigori use milk and cream alongside the anko, so dairy and bean-paste items are prepared in the same dessert kitchen; no vegan-specific separation is documented.

positives.text: House-made anko (red bean paste), kuzu (arrowroot) ice, and zenzai are plant-based at their base, offering naturally dairy-free options to choose from.

lead: きなこだんご たまうさぎ 本店
name_ja: きなこだんご たまうさぎ 本店
cuisine: 個人経営の和菓子・きなこだんご・甘味処
neighborhood: 尼ヶ辻
gf_detail: The shop's single product is kinako dango — dumplings made from in-house-milled Hinohikari rice flour (naturally gluten-free) and coated in kinako, which is roasted soybean powder (also gluten-free). However, the dumplings are dipped in a proprietary honey/syrup glaze whose full ingredients are not documented, and there is no celiac protocol, no allergen labeling, and no English allergy info at this tiny counter shop. The base ingredients are promising, but a celiac traveler would need to confirm the syrup and any shared utensils in person — treat as ask, not confirmed safe.
vegan_detail: The only item sold is kinako dango: rice-flour dumplings coated in roasted soybean (kinako) powder. There is no fish dashi, meat, or egg involved, so it is plant-based in spirit. The one caveat is the sweet glaze: some sources describe it as a "secret syrup" inherited from the founder's mentor shop, while one describes it as a "honey-based glaze." If the glaze contains honey, strict vegans would want to confirm. Otherwise the product is suitable for most plant-based eaters.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市尼辻西町6-4 エスポアール尼ヶ辻 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 尼辻西町6-4; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 3 seats for eating purchased dango on-site; primarily a takeaway counter shop.
hours_raw: 水〜日 9:00〜18:00（売り切れ次第閉店）／月・火 定休
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays (open on public holidays); also closes early when sold out
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, cash-only neighborhood dango counter run by the owner with no English — easiest if you go with a Japanese-speaking friend or guide, point at the single product, and have small change ready.
caveats: Cash only. Sells essentially one item (kinako dango) and closes early once sold out, so go earlier in the day. No preservatives — the dango hardens by the next day, so eat it the same day. Closed days reported inconsistently across sources (Monday and Tuesday per the most detailed listing; one source says Tuesday only). Per-piece price has risen over the years (reported at 85, 100, and most recently 130 yen).

bio.background: Tamausagi is a one-man neighborhood sweets shop opened in April 2002 in the quiet Amagatsuji district, a couple of minutes from Amagatsuji Station. The founder spent some 17 years apprenticing at Dango-sho, a long-established dango house in nearby Kashihara that traces its roots to the 1860s, before striking out on his own. He deliberately set up shop away from his old workplace so as not to compete with the mentor who trained him, taking with him a single guiding instruction: never compromise on taste.
bio.origin: Nara (Amagatsuji), with training rooted in Kashihara
bio.specialty: Kinako dango — the shop sells essentially nothing else. Soft, chewy little dumplings made from Hinohikari rice flour milled in-house, steamed and pounded by hand, glazed with an inherited sweet syrup, and then generously dusted with domestic roasted-soybean (kinako) powder. Each skewer carries several small dumplings.
bio.philosophy: Singular focus and freshness: one product, made entirely by hand on the premises so customers can watch, with no preservatives — the dango is meant to be eaten the day it is made. The owner judges the firmness of the dough by touch and eye rather than to a fixed recipe.

anecdotes.text: The owner trained for around 17 years at the historic Dango-sho in Kashihara before opening his own shop in 2002, and chose a different town specifically to avoid competing with his former master.
Despite expert training, the shop met initial resistance from customers, but over the years built such a following that people now travel in from Wakayama and Hyogo prefectures for the dango.
A large dango sculpture stands in front of the shop as its landmark, and locals say almost no one in Nara is unaware of the place.

positives.text: The single product is built on naturally gluten-free, plant-leaning ingredients: in-house-milled Hinohikari rice flour dumplings coated in kinako (roasted soybean powder), with no fish dashi, meat, or egg involved.
Everything is handmade on-site daily with no preservatives, so ingredients are simple and the dango is sold same-day fresh.

cuisine: 庭園喫茶・カフェ（自家製パン・ケーキ、サイフォン珈琲）
neighborhood: 柏木町（西ノ京・尼ヶ辻方面、中心部からやや西）
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering is documented. This is a café centered on handmade breads, cakes, French toast, and sandwiches — all wheat-based. No gluten-free menu, no dedicated handling, and no allergen information is published. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu or plant-based options are documented. The café's focus is handmade bread, cakes, and coffee; baked goods typically contain dairy and eggs, and there is no published vegan information. A vegan diner would need to ask directly, with low expectations. Black coffee from the café's siphon brews would be the safest plant-based choice.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市柏木町541-1
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 柏木町541; coordinates fall within the Nara box.
seats_text: 60 seats (can accommodate up to roughly 70; private events for 25-60). Note: this exceeds the typical small mom-and-pop size, but it is an independent owner-run garden café.
hours_raw: 8:30-17:00 (L.O. 16:30), open daily; 不定休 (irregular closures)
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures, no fixed closed day)
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed local garden café where ordering coffee and cake is easy, but staff likely speak limited English and there is no English menu, so basic Japanese or pointing helps.
caveats: Cash only — no cards, e-money, or QR payments. Hours are irregular (不定休), so it may close unexpectedly; consider calling ahead (0742-34-6155). Wheat-heavy menu makes it unsuitable for celiac or strict vegan diners.

bio.background: Provence (ぷろばんす) is an owner-run garden café in the Kashiwagi-cho area on the western side of Nara, set up to overlook a Japanese garden regarded as one of the finest in the neighborhood.
bio.specialty: Coffee brewed with an unusual halogen-lamp siphon method — using a halogen lamp instead of an alcohol or gas flame for steadier heat and a mellow, smooth cup — paired with handmade breads, cakes, and a well-liked French toast. The shop offers more than twenty carefully selected coffee beans, including house selections named 十二珈撰 and 組蔵, and sells its handmade bread and cakes to take home.
bio.philosophy: The café frames itself around blending Japanese aesthetic sensibility and a calm, garden-viewing atmosphere with quality coffee, drawing on Nara's long cultural history.

anecdotes.text: All of the bread, cakes, and dishes are handmade by the owner, and the handmade bread and cakes are also sold in the shop.
The café uses a distinctive light/halogen siphon brewing method for steadier heating and a mellower flavor, offering over twenty kinds of selected coffee beans.

cuisine: Nara local sake bar — paid tasting and standing-bar service of over 120 regional sake from Nara Prefecture breweries, by the glass
neighborhood: Nishiterabayashi-cho, off the Mochiidono / Higashimuki shopping arcades, about a 5–8 minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: This is a sake tasting bar, not a kitchen. Sake (junmai/ginjo) is brewed from rice, water and koji and is generally gluten-free, so the core offering of by-the-glass tastings is broadly suitable. However, this is not a celiac-focused venue and there is no published allergen handling. Accompanying small plates include items such as hishio (a koji-based fermented seasoning), aged sake-lees cream cheese and Nara pickles (narazuke) — narazuke and some seasonings are cured in sake lees / may involve soy or barley-derived components, so a sensitive celiac should ask about or avoid the food side and confirm before consuming.
vegan_detail: Drinks-focused venue. Most sake is vegan, though sake filtration/processing is not disclosed here. Food is limited to a few snacks: Nara pickles (narazuke) and koji-based hishio are plant-based, but the aged sake-lees cream cheese is dairy. There is no dedicated vegan menu — vegans can taste sake and choose pickle-type snacks but should ask about each item.
address_ja: 〒630-8372 奈良県奈良市西寺林町22
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned [135.829391, 34.67968] for 奈良県奈良市西寺林町22; within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Standing-bar format with an L-shaped counter seating roughly 6–7 people; no table seating.
hours_raw: 11:00–20:00, closed Thursdays (open if Thursday falls on a national holiday); occasional temporary closures announced on the official site.
closed_days: Thursday (open if a national holiday)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly counter where staff happily help you pick sake; a few words of Japanese and a willingness to point at the fridge go a long way, and inexpensive 50cc pours make it easy to try without commitment.
caveats: Cash only; standing-bar style with no seating to linger long. Primarily a beverage (sake) tasting and retail venue rather than a meal restaurant — food is limited to small snacks. Some sources list the brewery count as 27, 29 or 30; the shop's own current materials say over 120 varieties from Nara Prefecture breweries. A neighborhood guide cites 27 breweries / 120+ varieties.

bio.background: The shop opened in April 2012 in a white-walled traditional townhouse just off the Mochiidono arcade, on a site that had previously been the sake shop Sakagura Sasaya. It occupies a historic merchant quarter near Todai-ji, Kofuku-ji and Gangoji. The proprietor, Shinsuke Yamamoto, is a former researcher and director at Nara's prefectural industrial technology center with a background in fermentation chemistry — expertise he drew on, including work connected to reviving the ancient monk-brewed Bodaisen sake.
bio.specialty: Paid by-the-glass tasting of over 120 regional sake from breweries across Nara Prefecture, poured in small 50cc glasses from around 150–200 yen, with comparison sets (pure-rice, ginjo, dry) and the option to buy a bottle of anything you like. Snacks lean on Nara's food heritage — hishio (a revived ancient koji seasoning), sake-lees cream cheese and Nara pickles.
bio.philosophy: Celebrates Nara's claim as a birthplace of clear refined sake, giving visitors an approachable, low-commitment way to explore the prefecture's breweries (such as Harushika and Hatsugasumi) and discover a favorite with staff guidance.

anecdotes.text: The owner is a former director of Nara's prefectural industrial technology center with a fermentation-chemistry background, connected to the revival of the ancient monk-brewed Bodaisen sake.
Set in a historic white-walled townhouse off the Mochiidono shopping street, the compact space has high ceilings and a bright, refined feel.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Snacks include hishio (a koji/soy-type fermented seasoning) and Nara pickles cured in sake lees; these can involve wheat/barley or soy-derived components and are not confirmed gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Among the snacks, sake-lees cream cheese is dairy; pickles and hishio are plant-based but no vegan handling is documented.

positives.text: Sake is poured by the glass and bottles are visible in the counter fridge, so you can see exactly what you are served and ask staff about each one.

cuisine: 焼き鳥・鶏料理 (yakitori / chicken dishes)
neighborhood: ならまち（西寺林町・西寺林商店街の路地裏）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西寺林町11-5
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市西寺林町11-5 returned [135.829803, 34.679474], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: L字カウンター約7席のみ（諸説あり、約10席と記す情報源もある狭小店）
hours_raw: 食べログ表記: 14:00-22:00（月曜定休）。一部の地元ガイドは夕方17:00頃〜23:00頃と記載しており、営業時間の情報は出典により食い違う。
closed_days: 月曜日 (Monday)
cultural_comfort_note: This is a tiny, smoky, regulars-driven counter run solo by a quiet master with no reservations and cash only, so some spoken Japanese and a willingness to squeeze in next to locals will make a foreign student far more comfortable.
gf_detail: A traditional yakitori counter where skewers, tare, fried karaage and chicken dishes rely on wheat-based soy sauce, tare and batter, and there is no indication of gluten-free accommodation or a dedicated fryer. Not suitable for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: A chicken specialist (yakitori, karaage, chicken hot pot/porridge) where essentially every dish centers on poultry; there are no documented vegan options.
caveats: Hours differ across sources (Tabelog lists 14:00-22:00; local food blogs describe an evening-only ~17:00-23:00 service), so treat opening time as uncertain. No reservations and frequent waits mean arriving early is wise; cash only; very cramped and smoky.

bio.background: おんどり is a long-running yakitori and chicken-dish counter founded in 1982 (Showa 57) tucked into an alley off the Nishi-Terabayashi shopping street in the Naramachi quarter, a roughly ten-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. For more than forty years it has been run single-handedly by its master, drawing a loyal mix of local regulars to a tiny L-shaped counter under a glowing red lantern.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled chicken skewers from about 150 yen apiece, including the master's recommended local-chicken yakitori, negima (chicken and scallion), and kokoro (chicken heart) praised for its juicy, precisely grilled texture; a garlic-wrapped grilled chicken-skin dish seasoned simply with salt and pepper; a kushikatsu assortment; delicately crumbling tsukune; karaage; and chicken porridge (zosui).
bio.philosophy: A no-frills craftsman's bar: the quiet, gruff master focuses entirely on the grill, and the kitchen stopped serving raw items in line with regulatory guidance, prioritizing careful, hands-on cooking over comfort or polish.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe the counter as gyugyu-zume (packed tight); when newcomers arrive, seated guests instinctively shift closer together to make room.

The master comes across as muaiso (gruff and taciturn), which patrons read as devotion to his craft rather than coldness; the chicken heart was singled out as juicy and tender with excellent heat control.
Set on a narrow alley in the Nishi-Terabayashi shopping district, the aged red-lantern storefront is considered one of Nara's most 'deep' (atmospherically old-school) yakitori spots.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is built on charcoal-grilled chicken with wheat-based tare/soy seasoning and fried karaage, with no indication of gluten-free handling; high cross-contamination risk for celiac diners.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori and chicken dishes use standard Japanese soy sauce and tare, which contain wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A chicken specialist where all cooking surfaces (charcoal grill, fryer) and dishes are animal-based; no vegan-safe preparation.

cuisine: Yoshoku (Japanese-style Western comfort food) — hamburger steak, fried shrimp, tonkatsu, set lunches
neighborhood: Hōren Sahoyama, Nara City (near Konoike Sports Park, ~2km from Kintetsu Nara Station)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮佐保山3丁目2-26
geocode_note: Geocoded from full address via GSI; coordinates [135.82814, 34.700737] fall within the Nara box. Note: target metadata listed the Higashimuki shopping street / Kintetsu Nara Station area, but the restaurant is actually in the Hōren Sahoyama district, roughly 2km east of Kintetsu Nara Station (about a 30-minute walk), near Konoike Sports Park.
seats_text: No official seat count published. One detailed visit account describes roughly ten four-person tables, two two-person tables, plus additional seating in the back and a small counter (~5 seats); sofa-style seating in a spacious family-restaurant layout.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30); Fri, Sat, Sun & holidays: 11:00-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) and 17:00-20:30 (L.O. 20:00); closed Wednesday. (One aggregator lists a broader 10:30-22:30 window; the split-hours schedule above is the more detailed listing.)
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: This is a local, family-frequented neighborhood diner where almost no English is likely; a few basic Japanese phrases (or pointing at the picture menu and food samples) will go a long way, and it is cash-only so bring yen.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a yoshoku diner built around breaded and fried dishes (fried shrimp, tonkatsu), hamburger steak with demi-glace, and wheat-based sides like udon and sandwiches — wheat is central and shared fryers/breading make it unsuitable for celiac diners. No gluten-free accommodation is documented.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The menu is meat- and seafood-centric (hamburger steak, fried shrimp, ham, tonkatsu) and Japanese Western-style cooking here relies on meat, dairy, egg breading, and fish/meat-based sauces and dashi. No vegan options are documented.

bio.background: A long-loved Showa-era Western-style diner in Nara's Hōren Sahoyama district that has been serving for over fifty years. With its old drive-in exterior and wax food samples in the window, the dining room is a nostalgic Showa-era family-restaurant — sofa seating, generous tables, and a steady crowd of local families and regulars (including taxi and truck drivers stopping for an honest, familiar plate).
bio.specialty: The B Lunch is the house favorite: open-butterflied fried shrimp, a mini hamburger steak in a homemade demi-glace sauce, ham, and salad. There's also a Special B Lunch served on a sizzling iron plate with a larger hamburger plus potato and broccoli, a soft beef-forward handmade hamburger garnished old-school with pineapple, and a large headed prawn fry. Beyond Western plates the kitchen also turns out rice bowls, udon, seasonal specials, sandwiches, sundaes, and a Showa-style children's lunch.
bio.philosophy: Honest, nostalgic, home-style Western comfort cooking at neighborhood prices.

anecdotes.text: Regulars describe it as a time-slip into the Showa era — the kind of place that feels like the prototype of the modern family restaurant, with wax food samples at the entrance and a warm, retro dining hall feel.
On a Sunday around 11:30am the dining room was roughly 80% full, mostly families plus a few solo regulars, and food took about 30 minutes given the volume of orders.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Heavily breaded and deep-fried items (fried shrimp, tonkatsu) are core to the menu, so shared-fryer wheat cross-contamination should be assumed.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Cooking is meat-, seafood-, dairy-, and egg-based throughout; cross-contact with animal products is unavoidable.

caveats: Target metadata's stated area (Higashimuki shopping street / right by Kintetsu Nara Station) is inaccurate — the restaurant is in the Hōren Sahoyama district, ~2km (about 30 min walk) from Kintetsu Nara Station; a taxi or bus is recommended. Seat count is not officially published (estimated from a visitor account). Hours differ slightly between listings: the detailed Tabelog schedule shows lunch-only on Mon/Tue/Thu with added dinner on Fri-Sun/holidays, while one aggregator shows a broad 10:30-22:30 window. Owner/family names are not documented. No vegan or gluten-free accommodation found; not suitable for celiac or vegan diners.

cuisine: Western-style rice dishes (omurice, doria, risotto) / yoshoku dining bar
neighborhood: Takabatake, central Nara (Kyobate / Kintetsu Nara area, toward Naramachi)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町276-1 マックス高畑 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; coordinates [135.841034, 34.672119] fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 17 seats: four 4-person tables, four small 2-person tables, and about 5 counter seats; welcoming to solo diners.
hours_raw: 11:00-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); closed Tuesdays. Opened January 15, 2026.
closed_days: Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: A small, calm neighborhood spot with a Japanese-only menu and likely little English, so a foreign student may want a translation app or a Japanese-speaking friend to navigate the rice-dish menu, though cards and QR payments are accepted.
gf_detail: No gluten-free positioning. The menu centers on cheese-heavy rice dishes (omurice, doria, risotto) typically made with wheat-based roux, demi-glace and Western sauces; no celiac accommodation is documented. Not suitable for strict gluten-free diners.
vegan_detail: No vegan offering is documented. Dishes are dairy- and egg-heavy (omurice uses egg; doria and risotto feature cheese), so the menu is unsuitable for vegans as listed. A vegan would need to ask whether any dish can be adapted, but nothing specific is published.
caveats: No official website found; details drawn from the restaurant listing and a local lunch blog. No allergen, gluten-free, or vegan information is published, so dietary travelers should inquire directly. Portions are described as modest.

bio.background: Emiru is a small, independent Western-style eatery in the quiet Takabatake neighborhood of Nara, south of the university and a short distance from the historic Naramachi district. It opened in January 2026, bringing a distinctive concept to the area: yoshoku (Japanese-style Western food) built entirely around rice dishes.
bio.specialty: The kitchen specializes in rice at the center of the plate: omurice in several daily varieties, gratin-style doria, and risotto. Standouts diners mention include a creamy gorgonzola risotto and a four-cheese doria, with affordable lunch sets pairing a main with soup and salad.
bio.philosophy: The room is kept chic and calm, and the counter seating makes it an easy, comfortable place for solo diners as well as small groups.

anecdotes.text: A local Nara food writer sampled the gorgonzola cheese risotto and described it as creamy with real depth and a pleasantly pungent edge, while noting the portions run on the modest side.

cuisine: Yoshoku (Japanese-style Western cuisine)
neighborhood: 新大宮・奈良駅エリア
gf_detail: This is a traditional yoshoku (Western-style) eatery built around demi-glace sauce, hamburger steak, fried chicken, croquettes, gratin and doria — all of which are wheat-based or wheat-thickened. Demi-glace and the breaded fried items contain flour, and gratin/doria contain flour-based bechamel. No gluten-free menu or celiac accommodation is documented. Not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-seafood-forward Western diner. Signature items (hamburger steak, fried chicken, sausage, shrimp fry, crab cream croquette, chicken/shrimp gratin) all contain animal products. Demi-glace and bechamel are typically meat/dairy based. No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented; at most a traveler could ask, but there is no indication of suitable options.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市芝辻町2-11-15 パレス新大宮 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded from street address via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 20 seats with counter and table seating; solo diners welcome.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Fri, Sat, Sun 11:30-14:00 (L.O. 14:00), 17:00-22:00 (L.O. 21:30); closed Wednesday
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly neighborhood Western diner where ordering in basic Japanese will go smoothly; English support is not documented, so pointing at the menu and knowing a few food words helps.
caveats: No gluten-free or vegan/vegetarian accommodations are documented; the menu is heavily wheat-, meat-, and seafood-based, so this place is not suitable for celiac or plant-based diners.

bio.background: The owner-chef, Kunihiro Konoki, has worked in food service since his student days, carrying a long-held dream of cooking everything from scratch and hearing customers say "that was delicious." He brought that dream to life with this small Western-style diner in the Shin-Omiya district of Nara.
bio.specialty: Old-school yoshoku built around a house-made demi-glace sauce — the hamburger steak and omelette rice (omurice) are the signatures, alongside fried chicken, sausage, shrimp fry, crab cream croquette, and gratin and doria dishes. The kitchen also leans into seasonal produce, with touches like young-corn tempura.
bio.philosophy: Kon keeps the spirit of a nostalgic, traditional Western eatery while making it an approachable, comfortable space for Western food across the generations, with an emphasis on homemade cooking and a sauce made in-house.

anecdotes.text: A calm room of counter and table seating where it is easy to come in even on your own, and where regulars naturally gather — the kind of place with a warm, lived-in neighborhood atmosphere.
The chef has cooked in food service since his student days, holding onto the dream of serving food made from scratch and hearing diners say it was delicious.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The menu centers on flour-heavy yoshoku — demi-glace sauce, breaded and fried items (fried chicken, shrimp fry, crab cream croquette), and flour-thickened gratin/doria — so gluten is pervasive across the kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Core dishes are meat- and seafood-based (hamburger steak, fried chicken, sausage, shrimp fry, crab cream croquette, gratin), so plant-based diners have no documented suitable options.

lead: 植村牧場カフェレストランいちづ
name_ja: 植村牧場カフェレストランいちづ
cuisine: Western-style cafe restaurant (yoshoku) attached to a working dairy farm
neighborhood: Hannyaji / Kitamachi area, near Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市般若寺町168
geocode_note: GSI geocoder returned exact coordinates for 奈良県奈良市般若寺町168; within Nara box.
seats_text: Small farm-side cafe restaurant with an open terrace; exact seat count not documented.
hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (14:00-17:00 cafe service only; lunch last order around 14:00); closed Wednesdays. Note: some listings show 11:00-16:00.
closed_days: Wednesday
cultural_comfort_note: A warm, rural family-style spot beside a working farm where staff are unlikely to speak much English, so bring a translation app and point at the simple lunch menu.
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature dishes are Western yoshoku plates such as hamburger lunch, cream croquette, and mixed fry, which use wheat flour and breading; there is no documented gluten-free menu or celiac handling.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. The restaurant is built around the farm's fresh dairy (milk, soft-serve) and serves meat-based yoshoku such as hamburger and fried dishes; no vegan menu is documented.
caveats: Hours vary slightly between listings (11:00-16:00 vs 11:00-17:00 with cafe-only service in the late afternoon, lunch last order around 14:00); confirm before a late visit. Cash only. Reachable by Nara Kotsu bus to the Hannyaji stop, then about a 5-minute walk.

bio.background: The cafe restaurant sits inside Uemura Farm, founded in 1883 (Meiji 16) and described as the oldest dairy farm in Nara Prefecture, directly across from Hannya-ji Temple. Today the farm is led by Reiko Kurose and still keeps roughly 30 dairy cows along with sheep and goats in original Meiji-era wooden barns. The restaurant pours the farm's freshly drawn milk into its dishes, and large windows look out over the animals and toward Hannya-ji.
bio.specialty: Western-style lunch plates by an experienced chef, including hamburger lunch, cream croquette lunch and mixed-fry lunch (around 1,400 yen), plus the farm's own fresh milk and soft-serve ice cream.
bio.philosophy: The farm has worked since its founding to deliver honest fresh milk to everyone, hand-making each product rather than modernizing, and famously employs about a dozen people with intellectual disabilities who live and work as part of the farm family, some for over 30 years.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant's name, Ichizu, means devoting oneself single-mindedly to dairy farming, and was chosen by Nara-born, Cannes-honored film director Naomi Kawase; the sign was brushed by calligrapher Shishu, known for the NHK historical drama Ryomaden.

Uemura Farm employs about 12 people with intellectual disabilities who are treated like family, sharing meals and living space, with management describing themselves as perhaps Japan's most old-fashioned farm because they still hand-craft everything.

lead: そば切り よしむら
name_ja: 蕎麦切り よしむら
cuisine: Hand-cut soba (handmade buckwheat noodles)
neighborhood: Nishinokyo / Amagatsuji
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市五条町9-37
geocode_note: Geocoded from full address via GSI; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Approximately 24 seats: around 5-6 counter seats plus four tables seating 4-5 each.
hours_raw: 11:00-16:30 (lunch only; may sell out before closing). Closed Thursdays and the 2nd Wednesday of the month.
closed_days: Thursdays and the 2nd Wednesday of each month (may open on holidays with adjusted closures).
cultural_comfort_note: A small, traditional lunch-only soba shop where ordering is simple but staff likely speak limited English, so a few Japanese phrases or a translation app will help.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free safe. While the cold soba is made from 100% buckwheat (juwari), the hot soba uses a 10% wheat binder, the dipping sauce/broth is standard soba tsuyu (soy-sauce based, contains wheat), and kakiage/tempura items are wheat-battered and fried. No dedicated gluten-free preparation; celiac travelers should avoid.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The soba tsuyu (dipping/broth) is a standard preparation that uses fish-based dashi (bonito/kombu), and the menu features duck-broth soba and tempura. No documented plant-based dashi or vegan options.
caveats: Lunch only and cash only; the shop is small and popular, so waits are common at peak lunch and noodles can sell out before the 16:30 close. The 100% buckwheat noodles apply to cold soba only — hot soba contains a 10% wheat binder. Dashi/tsuyu ingredients are inferred from standard soba-shop practice and the duck-broth/tempura menu rather than an explicit ingredient disclosure.

bio.background: A small, family-run handmade soba shop tucked into the Nishinokyo district of Nara, set among the World Heritage temples and a roughly three-to-five-minute walk between Toshodai-ji and Yakushi-ji. The shop mills carefully selected domestic buckwheat in-house to hand-cut its noodles.
bio.specialty: Finely cut hand-made 100% buckwheat (juwari) cold soba, known for an unusually thin, refined strand that still holds firm bite without crumbling. Popular orders include zaru (plain), tororo (grated mountain yam), karami-daikon (spicy radish), kakiage zaru, and a duck-broth soba, alongside a lunch set with sesame tofu and pressed sushi.
bio.philosophy: A focus on in-house-milled domestic buckwheat and clean, balanced, aromatic noodles rather than aggressive flavor.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits between the World Heritage temples Toshodai-ji and Yakushi-ji, making it a natural lunch stop for visitors touring the Nishinokyo temples; its thin, refined 100% buckwheat noodles are described as having a clean, aromatic balance.
The cold soba is noted as extremely thin yet firm without a crumbly texture, and the meal finishes with soba-yu (the hot water the noodles were cooked in).

gf_cross_contamination.text: Hot soba uses a 10% wheat binder, and wheat-battered kakiage/tempura is prepared and fried in the same small kitchen, creating clear cross-contamination risk even for the 100%-buckwheat cold soba.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soba is served with standard soba tsuyu, a soy-sauce-based dipping sauce/broth that contains wheat.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu includes duck-broth soba and tempura, and the tsuyu is a standard fish-dashi preparation, so plant-based dishes would share equipment and stocks with animal products.

cuisine: 煮干しラーメン・つけ麺（自家製麺）
neighborhood: 近鉄奈良駅周辺（油阪町）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市油阪町460
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from address 奈良県奈良市油阪町460; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Small shop with roughly 5 counter seats plus 3 tables; exact total varies by source (counter + 3 tables).
closed_days: 日曜夜（Sunday evening closed; otherwise open daily, evening service ends when sold out）
cultural_comfort_note: A casual, cash-only counter ramen shop where a smile and a few Japanese words go a long way; menus and ordering are in Japanese, so a foreign student should be comfortable pointing and using basic phrases.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The house-made noodles are made from a blend of Hokkaido wheat flours (with added tapioca starch), and the broth is a fish-niboshi plus chicken paitan double soup typically seasoned with soy-based tare. There is no celiac-safe option here.
vegan_detail: Not vegan. The entire concept is built on niboshi (dried sardine) and chicken paitan broth, with pork chashu and egg toppings. There are no documented plant-based bowls.
caveats: Seat count is approximate (sources mention counter seating plus 3 tables). Evening service can end early once the day's broth/noodles sell out.

bio.background: A specialist niboshi (dried-sardine) ramen shop in central Nara that opened in October 2016. The owner trained at a Japanese restaurant in Osaka before striking out to build a menu devoted entirely to the deep umami of dried fish, occupying a spot near the Takamatsu intersection that previously housed other small eateries.
bio.specialty: Niboshi ramen served across a spectrum from light (assari) to ultra-rich (chо̄nо̄kо̄), plus tsukemen dipping noodles. The signature bowl layers a double broth of sardine, mackerel and horse-mackerel niboshi from around Japan with a chicken paitan base, topped with pork chashu, a soft-boiled egg, menma, scallion and naruto, finished with fish powder over nori.
bio.philosophy: Everything centers on the dried fish the shop is named for: the kitchen sources several varieties of niboshi to build its broth and makes its own chewy thick noodles from a blend of two Hokkaido wheat flours with tapioca starch. Diners can dial in the soup style and noodle thickness to taste.

anecdotes.text: The shop's house-made noodles use a blend of two Hokkaido wheat flours with added tapioca starch for an extra-chewy, springy texture.
The broth is a double soup combining several Japanese niboshi varieties (large wing sardine, mackerel, horse mackerel) with chicken paitan for an intense, layered umami.

gf_cross_contamination.text: House-made noodles are wheat-based (Hokkaido wheat flour blend), so all bowls contain gluten and are prepared in a wheat-heavy kitchen.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Broth is built on dried sardine and chicken paitan; bowls are topped with pork chashu and egg, so no plant-based preparation is possible.

cuisine: 創作中華・四川中華（大和野菜・大和ポーク・大和牛・地元豆腐を使った奈良ならではの中華）
neighborhood: 東向南町・東向商店街（近鉄奈良駅すぐ）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町1 AMIビル21 1F
geocode_note: GSI returned 403 on the building-level query; resolved at chō level (東向南町1) to lng 135.828918, lat 34.682663, well within the central-Nara box and consistent with the Higashimuki shopping street location near Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: 17 seats total — an L-shaped counter (about 5) plus three tables (about 12).
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:00/14:10); Dinner 18:00–22:00 (L.O. 21:00). Closed Tuesdays (plus occasional irregular closures).
closed_days: Tuesdays (occasional additional irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run Chinese spot down a side alley with no English menu, so a few words of Japanese and pointing at dishes will help; a friendly greeting goes a long way in a place this small.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free friendly. This is a Chinese kitchen built around gyoza (wheat wrappers), wheat-based soy sauce, and likely flour-thickened/wok dishes; there is no dedicated gluten-free handling and cross-contact is unavoidable. Not safe for celiac diners.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. The kitchen centers on Yamato pork, Yamato beef and seafood; signature dishes (gyoza, mapo tofu, vinegar chicken) are meat/fish-based. Even the locally made tofu dishes are cooked in meat- or fish-derived sauces. The gyoza being garlic-free is a flavor choice, not a vegan one. Vegans would need to ask, and options would be very limited at best.

bio.background: A small, hidden-gem Chinese restaurant tucked down a narrow alley off the Higashimuki shopping street, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is run almost single-handedly by its owner-chef, who has relocated the shop at least once over the years.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled gyoza filled with Yamato pork, Chinese cabbage and cabbage (kept juicy with added tofu) and made without garlic; and the house mapo tofu, made with locally grown soybean tofu and seasoned with kombu dashi and two kinds of fragrant Japanese pepper for a savory, brightly numbing heat. Vinegar chicken and a seafood-and-vegetable chanpon are also local favorites.
bio.philosophy: The chef prizes freshness over speed, cutting vegetables to order and avoiding chemical seasonings, building a 'light, gentle and colorful' style of Chinese cooking that leans on Nara-grown ingredients — Yamato pork, Yamato beef, Yamato vegetables and local tofu — to create a distinctly Nara take on Chinese food.

anecdotes.text: The standard menu is constantly refreshed with new dishes, so regulars find something different to try almost every visit.
It is known among locals as 'the Chinese place where mapo tofu lovers gather,' pairing especially well with aged Nara sake.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Gyoza and other dishes are made with wheat wrappers and almost certainly wheat-based soy sauce in a shared single-cook kitchen; no gluten-free protocol is documented, so cross-contact should be assumed.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: As a Chinese restaurant, dishes are seasoned with standard (wheat-containing) soy sauce; no wheat-free soy sauce is mentioned.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Even tofu and vegetable dishes are cooked alongside and in sauces derived from pork, beef and seafood; vegan separation is not offered.

positives.text: The signature gyoza are made without garlic, which can help diners avoiding garlic specifically (note: this is not related to gluten or vegan needs).

caveats: One-person owner-run kitchen with irregular closures beyond the regular Tuesday holiday, so hours can vary; dinner can be busy and reservations are recommended (a one-drink-minimum applies in the evening) though not strictly required. Building name appears as both AMIビル21 and AM1ビル across sources.

cuisine: 鶏白湯ラーメン・つけ麺・餃子 (chicken paitan ramen, tsukemen, gyoza)
neighborhood: 東向商店街 / 東向南町 (Higashimuki arcade, near Kintetsu Nara Station)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free in any meaningful way. The core menu is wheat ramen and tsukemen — the shop makes its own noodles from domestic wheat flour (and mineral-rich whole-grain wheat flour for the tsukemen), serves gyoza (wheat wrappers), and uses chicken/vegetable/kombu broth typically finished with standard seasonings. No celiac-safe options and no dedicated handling reported.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans. This is a chicken-paitan (tori paitan) specialist — broths are simmered from chicken bones, whole chicken and Yamato chicken, and the signature dishes include char siu, soft-boiled egg, shrimp and gyoza. Even the "soymilk" ramen (飛鳥豆乳ラーメン) is built on a chicken paitan base. No vegan menu documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東向南町26
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市東向南町26 returned [135.828491, 34.682625], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 40 seats (6 counter, 34 table); some sources describe table-heavy seating with IH cookers at the tables for finishing leftover broth as rice porridge.
hours_raw: 11:00-16:00 (L.O. 16:00), 17:30-20:00 (L.O. 20:00). Note: some listings show continuous service to ~21:30-22:00; hours vary by source/season.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures); some sources list 無休 (open year-round) and others note Monday closures — confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A casual ramen shop right in the tourist-heavy Higashimuki arcade that sees lots of international visitors, so a friendly greeting and pointing at the menu will get you a comfortable, no-pressure meal.
caveats: Hours and closed days differ noticeably across listings (continuous vs split service; 不定休 vs 無休 vs Monday-closed), so check the shop's social media before going. Part of the local Hiten (飛天) restaurant group, which also runs the Chinese restaurants 飛天 / 飛天散華 nearby.

bio.background: A chicken-paitan ramen and tsukemen shop tucked into the Higashimuki covered arcade, a two-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is run as part of a small local restaurant group (which also operates the nearby Chinese kitchens Hiten and Hiten Sange), and the Nara branch is descended from an earlier ramen shop on the site once known as 'Nara Ramen Aoniyoshi,' rebranded around the end of 2011.
bio.specialty: The kitchen builds everything on a milky tori paitan broth simmered for hours from chicken bones, whole chicken and Yamato local chicken with vegetables and kombu. The flagship is the chicken-paitan tsukemen, with house-made noodles from domestic wheat (a mineral-rich whole-grain flour for the dipping noodles) and rice from Nara-grown organic Hinohikari. Tourist-friendly specials round out the menu, including the lavish Daibutsu (Great Buddha) ramen topped with shrimp tempura, fried mochi, char siu and egg, and the lighter Asuka soy-milk paitan ramen.
bio.philosophy: An emphasis on a long-simmered, made-from-scratch chicken paitan base and self-made noodles, paired with local Nara rice.
bio.origin: Nara (Higashimuki arcade, near Kintetsu Nara Station)

anecdotes.text: Each table is fitted with an IH burner so diners can finish the meal by turning the leftover dipping broth, rice and a raw egg into a savory zosui rice porridge or risotto at the table — many regulars say the rice finish is the best part.
The original site once operated as 'Nara Ramen Aoniyoshi' and was known for its Daibutsu (Great Buddha) ramen, which still survives on the current menu after the rebrand to Genkishin.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat is central and unavoidable here: house-made wheat ramen/tsukemen noodles, wheat gyoza wrappers, and shared cookware make this unsafe for celiacs.

vegan_cross_contact.text: All broths are chicken-based (tori paitan from chicken bones, whole chicken and Yamato chicken); toppings include char siu, egg and shrimp. No vegan preparation reported.

cuisine: 麻婆豆腐ラーメン専門店（個人店・町中華系）／ Mapo tofu ramen specialist
neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅エリア）／ Naramachi
gf_detail: This is a ramen shop whose entire concept is a wheat-noodle dish. The signature mapo tofu ramen uses standard medium-thick wheat noodles, and the mapo sauce relies on doubanjiang and fermented soybeans (typically wheat-containing) plus a chicken-bone broth. There is no documented gluten-free menu, no dedicated equipment, and no allergen handling information. Not suitable for celiacs or anyone needing strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The core and signature dish is mapo tofu ramen built on a chicken-bone broth with minced meat and wheat noodles. No vegan or vegetarian version is documented. Tofu is present but the dish is meat- and animal-broth based, so it is not vegan or vegetarian.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南城戸町28-34
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市南城戸町28-34 returned [135.827957, 34.676464], within the central Nara box. Note: the official site lists the address as 南城戸町 with postal code 630-8341.
seats_text: 15 seats; small counter/table space in a residential back alley
closed_days: Tuesdays（火曜定休）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, lively local ramen counter where a basic Japanese greeting and pointing at the signature bowl will get you a long way; the young owners are open-minded and welcoming, but expect Japanese-only ordering and a short wait at busy times.
caveats: Cash only and no reservations; expect a line at peak times. The official site address (南城戸町, 630-8341) and the Tabelog listing both place it in the Naramachi back streets about a 10-13 minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. Spice/numbing levels for the mapo can reportedly be adjusted, but allergen and dietary handling is undocumented.

bio.chef_name: 西 奈槻 (Nishi Natsuki) & 奥野 亮太郎 (Okuno Ryotaro)
bio.origin: Nara, Japan; founded by Kinki University students
bio.background: Two friends and Kinki University students (Faculty of Agriculture, Department of Aquaculture) started the shop during the 2020 pandemic. Nishi, a former aspiring drummer who felt adrift after giving up that dream, spent the COVID lockdown visiting local Nara business owners to explore his future. On his third visit, the owner of a Naramachi halal ramen shop offered to hand over the space; Nishi immediately called Okuno, a friend known for his cooking and obsession with mapo tofu, who agreed on the spot. They first opened as 'jinniyah / 奈KAMA' in October 2020, then rebranded to 'すするか、すすらんか。' on June 6, 2021.
bio.specialty: Their flagship is the mapo tofu ramen called '革命物語' (Kakumei Monogatari) — a custom mapo tofu developed specifically for ramen, layering the heat of doubanjiang and fermented soybeans with the richness of a chicken-bone broth over medium-thick smooth noodles. It was described as an unprecedented bowl for Nara Prefecture at the time.
bio.philosophy: The name means roughly 'Shall we slurp, or shan't we?' — a play on the founder's mantra '結局やるか、やらんかやで' ('In the end it comes down to whether you do it or not'). They see the shop as more than a restaurant: a way to show young people in Japan that they have choices ('日本の若者に選択肢を'), running initiatives like inviting young people to submit recipes and hosting one-day managers to spark conversations about life paths.

anecdotes.text: The shop took shape through a public consulting session with Nakagawa Atsushi, chairman of Nakagawa Masashichi Shoten, which helped the founders crystallize their mission of giving young people more choices.
The 奈KAMA name combined Nishi's character '奈' with Okuno's nickname 'Kamakiri' before the pair rebranded to the current playful name.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Wheat ramen noodles are the foundation of every bowl and are boiled in shared water; the mapo sauce uses doubanjiang and fermented-soybean pastes that typically contain wheat. No gluten-free preparation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The mapo tofu base relies on doubanjiang and fermented soybeans, which are conventional wheat-containing fermented seasonings; no wheat-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Bowls are built on a chicken-bone broth with minced meat, so all preparation surfaces and broth contact animal products; no vegan option is documented.

cuisine: ヴィーガン台湾バオ（割包・台湾バーガー）専門店
neighborhood: ならまち（近鉄奈良駅徒歩圏）
gf_detail: This is a bao (steamed bun) specialist and the buns are made from organic WHEAT flour, so the core product is not gluten-free and the shop is not celiac-safe. Donuts are also wheat-based. No gluten-free menu or wheat-free options are documented. A celiac traveler should not rely on this shop.
vegan_detail: Fully vegan / 100% plant-based — the storefront is marked "100% PLANT-BASED" and the shop is described across multiple sources as a 100％ヴィーガン bao specialist. Around 11 varieties of vegan bao (Taiwanese-style burgers, e.g. plant-meat with BBQ sauce and ample vegetables), plus fries sets, vegan donuts, smoothies, matcha drinks, and ice cream. Uses additive-free seasonings and rice oil. No animal ingredients used.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市中新屋町17-2
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市中新屋町17-2 returned [135.8302, 34.677711], within the central Nara box and consistent with the Naramachi district location described in sources.
seats_text: 14 seats total (12 table seats + 2 counter/single seats); small shop with additional outdoor bench seating for casual visits.
hours_raw: Wed–Sun 10:00–16:00 (last food order 15:30); closed Mondays and Tuesdays. One local listing notes 11:00–16:30; the Tabelog listing states 10:00–16:00 (L.O. 15:30).
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual, takeout-friendly counter shop with a clearly visible "100% PLANT-BASED" sign and picture-friendly bao, so a foreign student can order comfortably with basic Japanese greetings and pointing, though staff English is not documented.
caveats: A relocated shop (moved from Osaka's Nipponbashi to Naramachi on March 15, 2026), so it is newly opened in Nara as of 2026. Reported opening/closing hours vary slightly between sources (10:00–16:00 vs 11:00–16:30). No parking. Vegan claims are well documented; gluten/cross-contamination and staff allergy-handling specifics are not documented, so allergy-sensitive diners should confirm in person.

bio.background: LUU's BAO is a 100% plant-based bao specialist that began in Osaka's Nipponbashi (Nihonbashi/Ebisucho) area and relocated to the historic Naramachi district of Nara on March 15, 2026, settling into a quiet machiya streetscape between Amenokaku Coffee and across from Kasugaan. The storefront announces '100% PLANT-BASED,' and the shop set out to prove that a fully vegan menu can still be hearty and satisfying.
bio.specialty: Vegan 'bao' — the steamed split bun known as 割包 (guabao), often called a Taiwanese burger. About 11 varieties are offered, including a plant-meat burger with barbecue sauce piled with vegetables, available with a fries set. Sweets include freshly fried vegan donuts, smoothies, matcha drinks, and ice cream.
bio.philosophy: Wholesome, body-conscious plant-based eating: the dough is made from organic wheat and steamed in bamboo baskets like a traditional pork bun, fillings are entirely plant-derived, and the kitchen uses additive-free seasonings and rice oil while aiming for filling, satisfying portions without animal products.

anecdotes.text: The shop relocated from Osaka's Nipponbashi to Naramachi and opened in Nara on March 15, 2026, choosing a calm machiya building that suits the area's traditional architecture, with an outdoor bench for casual stops.
It is described as a 'hidden celebrity' sweets spot, with freshly fried donuts and ice cream alongside its roughly 11 bao varieties.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire core product line is wheat-based (steamed bao buns and donuts made from organic wheat flour), so the kitchen is a wheat-heavy environment and is not suitable for celiac diners.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The shop is described as 100% plant-based across all sources, so animal-product cross-contact risk is inherently low; no specific cross-contact procedures are documented.

positives.text: Marked '100% PLANT-BASED' on the storefront and consistently described as a fully vegan bao specialist, so all menu items are plant-based.
Uses additive-free seasonings and rice oil with organic wheat and plant-based fillings.

lead: Vegan Ramen by Playpen Friends（ヴィーガンラーメン専門店）
name_ja: Vegan Ramen by Playpen Friends（ヴィーガンラーメン専門店）
cuisine: ヴィーガンラーメン専門
gf_detail: This is a ramen shop; the noodles are wheat-based, so the core dishes are not gluten-free and the kitchen is not celiac-safe. One reviewer mentioned a "gluten free" option, but this is unconfirmed by the shop and there is no documented dedicated gluten-free preparation. A celiac traveler should not rely on it; ask directly before ordering.
vegan_detail: A vegan-ramen-focused shop with around six clearly labeled vegan ramen styles (vegan miso, soy sauce, tomato, vegan "tonkotsu"-style). The vegan miso uses a domestic miso base with vegetables and spices stir-fried in plant oil, topped with broccoli, corn, fried tofu, soy meat, several seaweeds, wood-ear mushroom, green onion and chili threads. Note that the same kitchen also serves genuine pork-bone (tonkotsu) and chicken paitan ramen, so this is a vegan-friendly shop rather than a 100% vegan kitchen; strict vegans should confirm separate handling.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市登大路町59-12 2F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the cho-level point for 登大路町59 (奈良国立博物館向かい、しづか隣); within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: 登大路町（奈良公園・奈良国立博物館前、近鉄奈良駅すぐ）
seats_text: Seat count not documented; has window counter seats overlooking Nara Park (deer visible from the window).
hours_raw: 11:00-17:00 (closed Tuesdays). One local listing lists 10:00-17:00 — confirm on arrival.
closed_days: Tuesdays (火曜定休)
cultural_comfort_note: Very foreigner-friendly with clearly labeled vegan options and a heavily international clientele, so a foreign student can order comfortably with little Japanese.

bio.origin: Second outpost of a vegan ramen concept that started in Osaka's Nishi Ward (Kita-Horie area), where it built a loyal following before expanding to Nara.
bio.background: Opened on July 29, 2024 in Noborioji-cho, on the second floor of a building between the Shizuka park branch and Mamejika Shokudo, directly across from the Nara National Museum and a step from Nara Park. The space previously housed a Japanese-tea cafe, Chahua.
bio.specialty: Plant-based ramen built around a vegan miso bowl: a domestic-miso base with vegetables and spices stir-fried in plant oil, generously topped with broccoli, corn, fried tofu, soy meat, several kinds of seaweed, wood-ear mushroom, green onion and chili threads. Other vegan styles include soy-sauce, tomato and a vegan tonkotsu. The shop also cooks non-vegan pork-bone and chicken-paitan bowls.
bio.philosophy: Bring approachable, clearly labeled plant-based ramen to one of Japan's most-visited tourist areas so international and vegan diners can eat ramen overlooking the deer of Nara Park.

anecdotes.text: Window counter seats look straight out over Nara Park, so diners can watch the famous deer while eating; reviewers repeatedly note the generous, colorful assortment of toppings and friendly staff.
Sits between the Shizuka park branch and Mamejika Shokudo, opposite the Nara National Museum, in a spot that previously held the Japanese-tea cafe Chahua.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Ramen noodles are wheat-based and the menu is wheat- and broth-heavy; no dedicated gluten-free handling is documented, so the kitchen should be treated as not celiac-safe.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Soy-sauce and miso ramen styles are offered; standard Japanese soy sauce and miso contain wheat and no wheat-free version is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The same kitchen cooks genuine pork-bone and chicken-paitan ramen alongside the vegan bowls, so shared-equipment cross-contact is possible; strict vegans should confirm handling.

positives.text: Around six clearly labeled vegan ramen options make it easy for plant-based diners to order without ambiguity.
Vegan miso bowl is built on a domestic miso base with vegetables stir-fried in plant oil and plant-based toppings (fried tofu, soy meat, seaweed, vegetables).

caveats: Hours conflict slightly between sources (11:00-17:00 vs one local listing 10:00-17:00); Tuesday is the confirmed closed day. Phone number is not published. Seat count is undocumented. It is a vegan-focused shop but not a 100% vegan kitchen, as it also serves pork and chicken ramen.

cuisine: Japanese tourist dining hall (shokudō) and souvenir shop — set meals, bento, tonkatsu, curry, udon, kamameshi, plus a Buddhist-vegetarian (shōjin) set
neighborhood: Wakakusayama foothills, Nara Park area (Zōshichō), east of Kintetsu Nara Station near Kasuga Taisha
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation is documented. The menu is built on wheat-heavy dishes (tonkatsu, katsu curry with roux, hamburg, udon) and Japanese set meals that rely on wheat-based soy sauce and dashi. There is no indication of gluten-free preparation, separate fryers, or allergy handling, so this is not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: The official menu lists a Buddhist-vegetarian (精進料理 / shōjin) set at roughly 1,650–2,350 yen, which is plant-forward by tradition, but its vegan status is not confirmed — shōjin sets in Japan commonly include items prepared with non-vegan dashi or other animal-derived seasonings, and no allergen/vegan information is published. A traveler must confirm directly. The rest of the menu (tonkatsu, hamburg, curry, kamameshi, teppanyaki) is omnivore. The black-soybean soy-milk vegan soft serve mentioned in some listings could not be verified on the official site or any source and should be confirmed on arrival.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市雑司町933（若草山麓）
geocode_note: Geocoded at the chō (district) level for 奈良市雑司町 via GSI; the building-level number 933 did not resolve, so coordinates point to the Zōshichō area at the foot of Wakakusayama. Within the Nara bounding box.
seats_text: Not published. The official site describes a 1F shop with table dining and a 2F dining area with table and tatami seating; capacity is large (group/tour-bus oriented).
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures, no fixed weekly day off)
cultural_comfort_note: A large, tourist-facing dining hall and souvenir shop in Nara Park where ordering is straightforward by pointing at set-meal photos; basic greetings are enough, though detailed dietary questions may need patience or a translation app.
caveats: Hours are seasonal and closures are irregular (不定休) — confirm before visiting. The official address is 雑司町 at the foot of Wakakusayama (not 春日野町 as some listings state). The black-soybean soy-milk vegan soft serve and a dedicated "tea house" menu described in the target brief could not be verified on the official site or other sources, so treat those as unconfirmed.

bio.background: Mikasa Kanko Kaikan sits at the foot of Wakakusayama in the heart of Nara Park, a short walk east of the city center toward Kasuga Taisha. It is a long-running tourist hall that pairs a ground-floor souvenir shop with sit-down dining spread across two floors — table seating downstairs and a mix of tables and tatami-mat rooms upstairs — making it a common stop for school groups and sightseeing travelers exploring the deer park.
bio.specialty: Hearty Nara-style set meals and bento: the Sōhei (warrior-monk) bento, tonkatsu and Nanto set meals, kamameshi, teppanyaki sets, katsu curry and hamburg plates, alongside a Buddhist-vegetarian (shōjin) set. Bundles of deer crackers (shika-senbei) are sold out front for feeding the park's free-roaming deer.

anecdotes.text: The hall is named after Mikasayama (the older name for Wakakusayama, the grassy hill it sits beneath), the same mountain that lent its name to Nara's famous Mikasa sweet — a dorayaki-style confection whose shape evokes the rounded mountain.
It functions as a one-stop tourist rest house — souvenirs and packaged Nara sweets downstairs, full meals upstairs, and deer crackers for sale at the entrance for visitors feeding the park deer.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Menu is dominated by deep-fried and wheat-based dishes (tonkatsu, katsu curry, hamburg, udon) served in a shared high-volume kitchen; no gluten-free handling is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Standard Japanese set meals and bento are served, which in Japan typically use wheat-based soy sauce; no wheat-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: A Buddhist-vegetarian (shōjin) set is offered, but it is prepared in the same kitchen as meat and fish dishes and its dashi/seasoning ingredients are not disclosed, so vegan suitability and cross-contact cannot be confirmed.

positives.text: A dedicated Buddhist-vegetarian (shōjin) set meal is available on the regular menu, giving plant-based diners a starting point to ask about.

lead: 春日大社 カフェ・ショップ鹿音
name_ja: 春日大社 カフェ・ショップ鹿音
cuisine: カフェ／甘味・軽食
cultural_comfort_note: A relaxed museum cafe inside a major shrine's National Treasure Hall, used to tourists and easy to order at by pointing, though staff English may be limited so a translation app helps.
gf_detail: No gluten-free accommodation found. The menu centers on wheat-based items: Miwa sōmen / nyūmen and ramen are wheat noodles, pancakes and cookies contain wheat flour, and there is no dedicated fryer or allergen handling documented. Not celiac-safe.
vegan_detail: No confirmed vegan items. The documented soft serve flavors (matcha, matcha-milk, chocolate, mango-milk, etc.) are all dairy-based, and meal sets use rice/sōmen typically with fish dashi. Despite a claim that a vegan soft serve is offered, no source I checked lists a ヴィーガン/植物性 (plant-based) item, so a plant-based eater would need to ask directly.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市春日野町160 春日大社国宝殿1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市春日野町160 returned [135.844284, 34.682671], within the central Nara box and consistent with the Kasuga Taisha grounds in Nara Park.
neighborhood: 春日野町（春日大社境内・奈良公園内）
seats_text: Approximately 40 seats (per listing); interior features a counter table made from an old Kasuga cedar.
hours_raw: 10:30–17:00 (L.O. 16:30); rurubu lists 10:00–17:00 (L.O. 16:30). Follows the Kasuga Taisha National Treasure Hall schedule.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures, following the Kasuga Taisha National Treasure Hall closure schedule; confirm before visiting)
caveats: A claim that this cafe serves a vegan soft serve could not be verified from any source; documented soft serves are dairy-based. Hours/closures track the adjacent National Treasure Hall and are irregular, so confirm before visiting. Two listings give slightly different opening times (10:00 vs 10:30).

bio.origin: Opened in autumn 2015 alongside the renovated National Treasure Hall (Kokuhoden) of Kasuga Taisha, this cafe and shop sits on the first floor of the museum building within the shrine's sacred grounds in Nara Park.
bio.background: The space is built to carry the feeling of Kasuga's primeval forest indoors: its counter is fashioned from a Kasuga cedar that grew on the mountain and was felled by a lightning strike in the Showa era, and other wooden fittings are made from nagi, a tree designated a natural monument. A display of an 800-year-old cedar stump anchors the room.
