bio.specialty: Soft serve made with Nara-grown matcha is the signature, offered in five flavors, alongside sweets like black-honey-and-kinako pancakes with warabi mochi and the Shiki Gozen seasonal set pairing Nara rice bowls with Miwa somen.
bio.philosophy: To let visitors to the shrine taste Nara's local produce and feel the atmosphere of the sacred Kasuga mountain forest within the cafe itself.

anecdotes.text: The cafe's counter table is cut from a cedar that once stood on Mount Kasuga and was struck by lightning during the Showa period, having been stored at the old treasure house before being repurposed here.
Original goods and confections are themed on cats found depicted in the scabbards of national-treasure swords held by the shrine.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Soft serve flavors documented are dairy-based (matcha-milk, chocolate-milk, mango-milk), so plant-based options are not confirmed and any would share equipment with dairy.

cuisine: Onigiri (rice ball) lunch café — handmade rice balls with ~15 fillings, served with pork soup (tonjiru), rolled egg, and small sides
gf_detail: Not a gluten-free destination and no GF accommodation is documented. The base food is rice (onigiri), and a plain-salt onigiri (こだわりの塩) exists, but the lunch sets come with a rolled egg, a hearty pork soup (tonjiru) that almost certainly contains wheat-based miso/seasoning, and fillings such as seared cheese-mentaiko, tuna mayo, and roast beef that may use soy sauce or other wheat-containing seasonings. There is no mention of dedicated GF handling or cross-contamination control. A celiac diner would need to ask in detail and likely cannot eat the standard set safely.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-oriented. The signature lunches center on fish, meat, and dairy fillings (tuna mayo, mentaiko, seared cheese, prosciutto with cream cheese, roast beef) and include a rolled egg and a pork-based soup. A plain-salt or kombu onigiri could in principle be vegan if ordered à la carte, but the set meals are not, fish dashi is likely in the soup, and no vegan menu is documented. A vegan would need to ask whether individual non-animal onigiri can be ordered separately.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市恋の窪1-6-1-1
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to 奈良県奈良市恋の窪一丁目6番1号; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Seat count not published; described as a small welcoming café with casual table seating.
neighborhood: 恋の窪 (Koinokubo), northwest of Kintetsu Nara Station
closed_days: Irregular holidays (不定休); no fixed weekly closing day published. Check ahead before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly local lunch café where a smile and a little basic Japanese go a long way; the menu is simple (pick your rice-ball fillings), so a foreign student can manage comfortably even with limited Japanese.
caveats: Owner's personal background and philosophy are not documented in available sources; the official site (aonocafe.com) was unreachable at verification time. Seat count is unpublished. Hours shown daily but the café keeps irregular holidays, so confirm before visiting. Card, e-money, and QR payments are accepted.

bio.background: A photogenic onigiri café that opened on March 25, 2024 in the Koinokubo area northwest of central Nara. The shop is built around handmade rice balls and a relaxed, welcoming lunch atmosphere.
bio.specialty: Onigiri lunch sets: diners choose three rice balls from around fifteen original fillings. The playful menu splits into an 'Ao' (blue) line of classics like tuna-mayo and mentaiko, and a 'Shiro' (white) line of creative choices such as seared cheese-mentaiko, house-made roast beef, kombu, and prosciutto with cream cheese. Each set comes with a generous, vegetable-packed pork soup, a rolled egg, and small sides, with takeout also available.
bio.philosophy: The shop's stated draw is offering unique rice-ball varieties you can't find elsewhere, served generously and affordably for a satisfying everyday lunch.

anecdotes.text: Opened on March 25, 2024 and quickly drew attention as a 'photogenic onigiri shop,' with a full lunch set built around three chosen rice balls and a hearty pork soup offered around the 1,000-yen mark.
Visitors describe a warm, busy little café with the rice-ball sets praised as filling yet never monotonous thanks to the vegetable-forward pork soup.

positives.text: The core dish is rice-based onigiri, and a simple salt onigiri (こだわりの塩) and kombu onigiri are on the filling list, so plain rice-ball options exist for diners avoiding meat or fish if ordered carefully.

name_ja: 珈琲風雅 ぷろばんす
cuisine: 昭和レトロな純喫茶・自家焙煎/光サイフォン珈琲（個人経営の庭園喫茶）
neighborhood: 西ノ京・柏木町方面（奈良市西部、国道24号沿い付近）
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市柏木町541-1
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良市柏木町541-1 returned [135.799698, 34.674328], within the Nara box. Note: the target brief listed this as a きたまち/新大宮 (Kitamachi/Shin-Omiya) shop, but the confirmed address is 柏木町 in western Nara near Nishinokyo, off Route 24 — not Kitamachi. Same establishment, corrected area.
seats_text: 総席数60席（カウンター・テーブル）。貸切は25～60名対応。
closed_days: 不定休（定休日なし、不定休日あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A traditional family-run Showa-era coffee house where staff are unlikely to speak much English, but a friendly greeting and pointing at the picture menu will get you a warm welcome and a relaxing garden seat.
gf_detail: This is a coffee house whose food centers on wheat: house-baked bread, French toast, sandwiches, and a pasta lunch set, plus homemade cakes. No gluten-free menu, dedicated equipment, or allergy handling is documented. Not suitable for celiacs. Black coffee itself is naturally gluten-free, but the food is not.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. Offerings are coffee-shop fare — morning toast/sandwich sets, French toast, a pasta-based 風雅ランチセット with salad and homemade bread, and cakes — which typically contain dairy and egg. The wide drink list (about 40 coffees) means a plain black coffee is fine, but a vegan would need to ask about any food and milk-free options.

bio.background: A long-standing Showa-retro 純喫茶 (pure coffee house) tucked just off Route 24 in western Nara, near Nishinokyo. The shop is individually run and frames itself within Nara's 1,300-year heritage, blending Japanese aesthetics with serious coffee culture.
bio.specialty: Self-roasted coffee brewed with an unusual 光サイフォン ("light siphon") method that uses halogen lamps instead of an alcohol or gas flame for a stable, mellow extraction. The signature is the house blend 玄武 (Genbu) — described as a gentle bitterness emerging within the acidity — one of about 40 coffees spanning six blends (六歌仙/ろっかせん) and twelve straight single-origins (十二珈撰). Morning toast and sandwich sets, French toast, the 風雅ランチセット (pasta, salad, daily side and homemade bread), plus house-baked breads and cakes round out the menu.
bio.philosophy: The owners emphasize slow, contemplative time: guests are invited to sit with a coffee and gaze at a handcrafted Japanese garden, reputed to be the finest in the neighborhood, and to take the gentle homemade flavors home as well.

anecdotes.text: Coffee is extracted by a halogen-lamp 光サイフォン rather than a conventional flame, which the shop says gives stable heating and a mellow, rounded taste.
The interior depth opens onto a handcrafted Japanese garden visible from the seats, locally reputed as 'この付近では一番' (the best in the area).
A morning set can be added to a drink for a small surcharge — toast morning set +130 yen, sandwich morning plate +160 yen — making it a popular leisurely breakfast spot.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Bread, French toast, sandwiches, pasta and cakes are all wheat-based and house-baked on site, so flour is handled throughout the kitchen; no gluten-free accommodation is documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Menu centers on dairy/egg items (cakes, French toast, milk drinks); no documented vegan separation or plant-milk option.

positives.text: Black coffee and the extensive single-origin/blend coffee list are naturally free of gluten and animal products.

caveats: The target brief named this as a Kitamachi/Shin-Omiya shop, but the verified address is 奈良市柏木町541-1 in western Nara near Nishinokyo (off Route 24) — same establishment, corrected location. Hours differ slightly by source (Tabelog/official: 8:30-17:00 L.O.16:30; nara-gourmet: 8:00-18:00); used the official/Tabelog times. No vegan or gluten-free accommodation documented; this is a classic kissaten, not a dietary destination. Parking available.

cuisine: ベーカリー（国産小麦・地元食材の食事パン、食パン、ハード系）
neighborhood: きたまち（法蓮町）／近鉄奈良駅から徒歩約8〜10分
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町1026-2
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates within the central Nara box for the full street address.
seats_text: Compact takeaway-focused bakery of a few tsubo; no dedicated seating count documented.
closed_days: Monday and Tuesday
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny neighborhood bakery where you point at what you want and pay at the counter, so a foreign student can shop comfortably with little or no Japanese, though staff are unlikely to speak English.
gf_detail: This is a wheat bakery whose entire product line is built on domestic wheat flour (plus rice and other grains), so it is not suitable for celiacs or anyone avoiding gluten. No gluten-free products are documented.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is advertised. Documented items include shokupan, melon pan with almonds, anko-and-butter, and curry pan, several of which clearly contain dairy/egg. Some simple hard-crust breads may be free of animal products, but ingredients are not published, so a vegan diner would need to ask item by item.
caveats: Hours and closed days are consistent across Tabelog and the bakery's own description (Wed–Sun 8:00–16:00, closed Mon/Tue). Payment methods and exact seating were not confirmed; the official site could not be fetched directly (HTTP 403) so its details come via cited Nara local-media coverage.

bio.background: A small bakery in Nara's quiet Kitamachi (Horen-cho) district, a short walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station along the Saho River. It was founded by the Murakami family to be a neighborhood bread shop rooted in their home city of Nara.
bio.specialty: The signature 'Hibi no Shokupan' (daily milk-style loaf) made from a proprietary blend of domestic wheat and Nara rice, with a soft, chewy crumb that is faintly sweet eaten plain and crisp-edged when toasted. Other beloved items include 'Yamato-cha to Azuki' (organic Nara green tea and sweet adzuki swirl), a baked-not-fried vegetable curry pan, the almond-and-sugar 'Sunrise' melon pan, and an anko-and-butter roll.
bio.philosophy: To make gentle, everyday bread that children and elderly customers alike can enjoy, kept at approachable prices and convenient hours, using domestic wheat and local Nara ingredients such as Yamato tea and Nara-grown rice so the bakery stays tied to its hometown.

anecdotes.text: The owner is described talking about bread with sparkling eyes, wanting the shop to walk alongside the community's daily life, which is the spirit behind the name 'Hibi' (everyday).
The curry pan is filled with house-made curry of onion and carrot and is baked rather than deep-fried so it appeals to all ages.

gf_cross_contamination.text: This is a flour-based bakery where every product is made with domestic wheat, so there is pervasive wheat/gluten throughout the kitchen and no gluten-free options exist.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Documented breads include dairy and egg items (milk-style shokupan, almond melon pan, anko-and-butter), so animal ingredients and shared surfaces are common; no vegan-specific handling is described.

positives.text: The bakery emphasizes domestic wheat and local Nara ingredients (Nara rice, Yamato tea), so ingredient sourcing is transparent and locally focused.

cuisine: Handmade cake & bread cafe (daily-changing cakes, tarts, baked sweets, breads)
neighborhood: きたまち (Kitamachi), 南半田中町
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市南半田中町13-1
geocode_note: Geocoded from address 奈良県奈良市南半田中町13-1 via GSI; result lat 34.687664, lng 135.832138 falls within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Small cafe with a handful of in-store seats; exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: 11:00-17:30 (January-February: 10:00-16:00). Cakes (5-7 kinds), baked sweets (~3 kinds) and breads (~4 kinds) change daily and often sell out by midday.
closed_days: Mondays and Tuesdays, plus some Sundays; irregular closures (不定休) announced on social media. Note: Tabelog lists daily 11:00-17:30, but the official/press listing specifies Mon/Tue and select Sundays closed.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny neighborhood sweets shop run by a local owner; ordering cakes and bread is easy by pointing, but service is in Japanese and a few basic phrases help.
vegan_detail: A French-style cake and bread cafe whose products are built on butter, eggs, milk and cream; no vegan offering is documented. Not suitable for vegans.
gf_detail: A wheat-flour bakery: cakes, tarts, baked sweets and breads are all wheat-based, with shared prep surfaces and equipment. Not celiac-safe and no gluten-free menu is documented.

bio.background: The owner grew up in Nara's Kitamachi neighborhood and returned to open her shop in the very area she knew as a child. She first worked as a kindergarten teacher before moving into teaching at cooking schools and running her own confectionery classes, eventually opening Cake & Bread Cafe La Liberte.
bio.specialty: Handmade cakes that change daily (five to seven kinds), tarts, baked sweets and breads, served alongside black tea, flavored teas and herbal teas; many items sell out by lunchtime.
bio.philosophy: She likens making cakes to classical music, a craft she fell ever deeper in love with, and pours daily 'care and sincerity' into handmade sweets with the simple aim of sending every customer home smiling.

anecdotes.text: Beyond selling cakes and bread and running the cafe, she also teaches cake, pastry and bread lessons, carrying her earlier life as a teacher into the shop.
Cakes, baked sweets and breads are made in small daily batches and frequently sell out by midday, so the lineup is never quite the same twice.

gf_cross_contamination.text: All products are flour-based cakes, tarts and breads made in one small shared kitchen, so flour and gluten cross-contact is unavoidable.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Sweets and breads are made with butter, eggs and dairy; no vegan or allergen-free line is documented.

caveats: Tabelog shows daily 11:00-17:30 hours, while the official site and local press indicate Monday/Tuesday plus some Sunday closures and seasonal Jan-Feb hours of 10:00-16:00; hours_week reflects the official Mon/Tue closure. Seat count is not published. Two different phone numbers appear (Tabelog 050-1063-5291; official 0742-93-6698).

lead: ユーズ・キッチン（U's kitchen）
cuisine: Italian / fresh-pasta specialist
neighborhood: Kitamachi (near Higashimuki-kita shopping street), about a 5-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: This is a fresh-pasta specialist where essentially every dish is wheat-based (tagliatelle, linguine, pappardelle, spaghetti, penne). There is no documented gluten-free menu, no gluten-free pasta, and no celiac accommodation. Not suitable for those who must avoid gluten.
vegan_detail: An Italian pasta house built around cream sauces, meat sauce, bacon, baby scallop, smoked chicken and shrimp dishes. A plain tomato-sauce pasta or basil pesto (ジェノベーゼ) may be the only meatless option, but no dish is confirmed dairy-free or vegan, and there is no documented vegan menu. Vegans would need to call ahead and likely find very little.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市花芝町7番地 プラザ花芝
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI; first feature returned [135.828766, 34.686535], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Around 25 seats; small intimate room with a few tables and counter seating (one local source counts roughly 12, so the room is genuinely small either way).
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-14:30, Dinner 17:00-20:30 (last entry); closed Sundays; dinner closed on the third Monday.
closed_days: Sundays; dinner service also closed on the third Monday of the month. Hours vary slightly by source, so confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A small, locally loved neighborhood pasta room where menus and service are in Japanese, so a few words of Japanese or a translation app will make ordering smoother for a foreign student.
caveats: Sources disagree on a couple of details: a local listing describes thin-crust Roman-style pizza, but the restaurant's own site states plainly that as a pasta specialist they serve pasta only, so pizza availability is uncertain. Exact hours and seat count also vary between sources.

bio.background: A snug pasta room tucked into Nara's old Kitamachi quarter, a short walk north from the Kintetsu station amid the nostalgic streetscape near the Higashimuki shopping arcade. It has been a quiet local favorite for years rather than a tourist stop.
bio.specialty: Fresh house-made pasta is the heart of the menu, built around three carefully chosen shapes: tagliatelle (an egg-rich ribbon a bit like kishimen), linguine with a firm bite and faint sweetness, and wide pappardelle. The kitchen turns out a long list of sauces, and the baby-scallop cream pasta is a standout regulars come back for.
bio.philosophy: The owner keeps the focus deliberately narrow, stating that this is a pasta specialist that serves pasta and nothing else, letting the noodles and sauces carry the meal.

anecdotes.text: A longtime regular writes that they stumbled on this pasta specialist years ago and it became their go-to, praising the chewy tagliatelle in baby-scallop cream sauce.
The restaurant's own site sums up its identity bluntly: as a pasta specialty shop, there is nothing on the menu but pasta.

gf_cross_contamination.text: The entire menu is built on house-made wheat pasta, so flour is central to the kitchen; there is no gluten-free preparation or separate workflow documented.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Sauces lean heavily on cream, meat sauce, bacon, scallop, chicken and shrimp, so meat- and dairy-free cooking is not a focus and shared pots are likely.

lead: 奈良きたまち食堂 IL SORRISO DELLA FAMIGLIA
name_ja: 奈良きたまち食堂 イル・ソッリーゾ・デッラ・ファミーリア
name_en: Nara Kitamachi Shokudo Il Sorriso Della Famiglia
cuisine: イタリアン・洋食（日替わりワンプレートランチ、家庭的な創作料理）
neighborhood: きたまち／近鉄奈良駅
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市鍋屋町2-1 シティーホームズ女子大前 203
geocode_note: Chō-level geocode of 奈良市鍋屋町2-1 via GSI; first feature within Nara box. Pinpoints the 鍋屋町 block where the building (2F, unit 203) is located.
seats_text: 14席（カウンター6席＋テーブル2卓）。オーナーと1名のスタッフで切り盛りする小さな店。
closed_days: 不定休（訪問前にSNS等で営業状況の確認を推奨）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, student-friendly owner-run canteen near Nara Women's University where ordering and chatting will go most smoothly in Japanese, though the relaxed family atmosphere is welcoming to all.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program. This is an Italian/Western canteen built around pasta, fried items (フリット), and rice plates, so wheat is pervasive and there is no celiac-safe handling. The chef is known for adjusting dishes to customer requests, so you could ask, but cross-contamination cannot be assured in a kitchen this small.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan/vegetarian destination — the menu centers on meat and fish mains (fried chicken breast, grilled pork, deep-fried horse mackerel). The owner does customize dishes and asks diners about dislikes, so a vegetable-forward plate might be possible by request, but no documented vegan offering exists. House-made non-alcoholic sangria is one plant-based item noted.

bio.background: A small, home-style Italian and Western canteen tucked on the second floor of a building in Nara's historic Kitamachi district, near Nara Women's University. Run by an owner who goes by Sadayan with one part-time helper, it sits along the route east from Kintetsu Nara Station through the Higashimuki and Hanashiba shopping streets, marked by a wooden sign painted with black cats.
bio.specialty: Daily-changing one-plate lunch sets (around 850-900 yen) with a rotating main such as fried chicken breast, grilled pork shoulder, or deep-fried horse mackerel, served with a generous portion of rice, assorted sides, and fruit; pasta dishes and a teppan Neapolitan are also signatures, alongside house-made non-alcoholic sangria.
bio.philosophy: The Italian name means 'the family's smile,' reflecting the owner's stated wish to meet many customers' happy, satisfied faces each day. The kitchen embraces flexibility, adjusting sauces and ingredients to individual tastes and inviting requests.

anecdotes.text: The shop keeps the spirit of a relaxed 'home drinking' spot where you can come alone for a single drink or gather with friends and family, and it is known for keeping cats around, adding to its casual, family feel.
A diner came away with the strong impression that the chef genuinely loves cooking, noting the kitchen will tweak dishes to suit the customer.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Staff ask diners about dislikes and will adjust sauces and ingredients to suit individual tastes, and the menu can be customized on request.

positives.text: The owner welcomes customer requests and can accommodate dietary preferences or specific dish requests.

caveats: Hours and closures are irregular (不定休); confirm via the owner's social media before visiting. Cash only. The dietary accommodations noted are informal chef flexibility, not a celiac-safe or certified-vegan program. Coordinates are a chō-level geocode of the 鍋屋町2-1 block, not the exact 2F unit.

cuisine: クラフトハンバーガー＆スペインのタパス（バーガーバル）
neighborhood: きたまち／近鉄奈良駅
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市花芝町17 第二丸谷ビル 1F
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市花芝町17 returned [135.829056, 34.686321], within the central Nara box. Some blog sources list the building as 花芝町16-1; Tabelog and the geocode use 花芝町17.
hours_raw: 火〜日・祝 11:30-15:00, 18:00-20:00（ランチは15:00まで、ディナーは17:00または18:00開始の記載あり）。売り切れ次第終了。月曜定休。
closed_days: 月曜（Monday）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny 18-seat chef-run burger and tapas bar where a few Japanese greetings and pointing at the menu will carry you through comfortably, though staff may not speak much English.
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The core menu is craft hamburgers on house-made brioche/wheat buns, and there is no documented gluten-free option or celiac handling. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not vegan-friendly. The concept centers on hand-chopped beef patties, house-made bacon and smoked brisket, plus cheese; Spanish tapas also lean meat/seafood. No documented vegan or vegetarian dishes.
caveats: Lunch and dinner start times vary slightly across sources (dinner listed as 17:00 or 18:00; one source lists Sat/Sun continuous 11:30-20:00). Closes early when sold out. Address building number differs between Tabelog (花芝町17) and some blogs (花芝町16-1).

bio.background: ボカロスは、奈良市内でスペイン料理店『Gentry（ジェントリー）』を営んでいた店主が、2021年3月に同店を閉め、2021年4月にきたまち・花芝町で独立開業したクラフトバーガー＆タパスバルです。元スペイン料理シェフの経歴を生かし、ハンバーガーにスペインの小皿料理を組み合わせた独自のスタイルを打ち出しています。
bio.specialty: 看板はカルニータスバーガー。豚肩肉をオレンジとスパイスで低温コンフィ風にじっくり火入れし、ほろほろと柔らかく仕上げた一品で、丼スタイルでも提供されます。ほかにベーコンチーズバーガー、ダブルチーズバーガー、BBQチーズバーガーなど。バンズは自家製ブリオッシュ、パティは手切り（ハンドチョップ）のビーフ、ベーコンやスモークブリスケット、各種ソースまで店で手作りしています。自家製コーラやジンジャーエールも提供。
bio.philosophy: バンズを毎朝店で焼き、ベーコン・ブリスケット・ソースまで自家製にこだわる、手作り主体のクラフト志向。夜はハンバーガーに加えてスペインのタパスやワイン・カクテルが楽しめ、ランチとは異なるバル的な雰囲気になります。
bio.origin: 店名『ボカロス』は、スペイン語で『ボカディージョ（サンドイッチ）』のボカと、米料理を意味する『アロス（arroz）』を組み合わせた造語です。

anecdotes.text: 店名『ボカロス（BOCARROZ）』はスペイン語のボカディージョ（サンドイッチ）の『ボカ』と、米料理を意味する『アロス（arroz）』を掛け合わせた造語。
店主は元スペイン料理店『Gentry』のオーナーシェフで、2021年3月に同店を閉店、同年4月にボカロスを開業した。
看板のカルニータスバーガーは豚肩肉をオレンジとスパイスで低温コンフィ風に火入れし、丼でも提供される。

cuisine: Tunisian / North African & Arab (halal)
neighborhood: きたまち（法蓮町）／近鉄奈良駅
gf_detail: No dedicated gluten-free program. The signature dish is couscous, which is wheat-based semolina pasta and unsafe for celiacs. However, some naturally gluten-free items exist: falafel (chickpea), shakshuka (a tomato/vegetable stew), and rice-based kebsa/biryani. Cross-contamination is not addressed in any source, and harissa/spice blends and sauces have unverified ingredients. A gluten-free diner would need to ask the owner directly about each dish.
vegan_detail: The restaurant explicitly offers some vegan-appropriate and vegetarian dishes. Documented plant-based options include falafel (chickpea, ~¥1,300) and shakshuka (Tunisian vegetable/tomato stew, ~¥1,200), and the kitchen emphasizes fresh vegetables (cabbage, chickpeas, dates, radish, zucchini, onion, carrot). It is not a fully vegan restaurant — couscous and kebsa dishes are prepared with lamb or chicken. Confirm egg-free preparation of shakshuka, which can sometimes include egg, directly with the owner.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市法蓮町1071-2
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the building-level address 法蓮町1071-2; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Roughly 10 seats across four tatami rooms of varying sizes, decorated with Middle Eastern rugs and cushions.
hours_raw: 11:30–20:00 (some sources list split hours of 11:30–15:00 / 17:00–20:30). Closed Fridays; some sources also note Mondays and irregular closures.
closed_days: Friday (some sources also note Monday; irregular closures possible — check Instagram before visiting)
cultural_comfort_note: A small owner-run place where the Tunisian proprietor is used to international guests and Muslim diners, so a friendly greeting and a few words about your dietary needs will be warmly received.
caveats: Hours and closed days vary between sources (single 11:30–20:00 span vs. split lunch/dinner; Friday vs. Friday+Monday closures), and closures can be irregular — check the shop's Instagram before going. Cash only. The menu is entirely halal but it is an omnivore restaurant, not a dedicated vegan or gluten-free destination.

bio.background: SAHHA is a Tunisian and North African restaurant tucked inside a renovated traditional Japanese house (kominka) in the Kitamachi district of Nara, near Nara Women's University. It opened in August 2021 and is one of very few places in Nara — and rare even nationwide in Japan — serving authentic Tunisian home cooking. The owner, originally from Tunisia, runs the place largely on his own, and family cooking is central to the experience.
bio.specialty: Couscous is the signature: the traditional couscous (around ¥1,400–¥1,800) is built on the world's smallest pasta soaking up a tomato-based broth simmered with root vegetables and lamb, finished with a hint of raisin sweetness. Other beloved dishes include kebsa (an Arab-style spiced long-grain rice biryani with lamb or chicken, described as milder than its Indian counterpart), shakshuka (a Tunisian tomato-and-vegetable stew), and falafel made from chickpeas. Homemade harissa chili paste, baklava, and Arab coffee round out the table.
bio.philosophy: Every dish on the menu is halal, so Muslim travelers can eat freely, and the kitchen leans heavily on fresh vegetables — cabbage, chickpeas, dates, radish, zucchini, onion and carrot — giving vegetarians and vegans some honest options too.

anecdotes.text: The traditional couscous is said to taste richer the more you chew it, the tiny pasta grains soaking up a lamb-and-root-vegetable broth with a faint sweetness of raisin.
The dining space is a restored kominka with several tatami rooms, dressed in Middle Eastern rugs and cushions so guests sit cross-legged in a North African mood inside an old Nara house.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The entire menu is prepared to halal standards, which means strict attention to what goes into each dish, and the owner runs the kitchen personally — making it practical to ask about ingredients directly.

positives.text: Documented vegetable-forward and chickpea-based options exist, including falafel and shakshuka, alongside an emphasis on fresh produce.
Naturally gluten-free choices such as falafel, shakshuka and rice-based kebsa are on the menu, though cross-contamination is not documented.

lead: KAYA cafe ならまち店（カヤカフェ）
cuisine: 豆腐スイーツ専門カフェ（手作り豆腐ティラミス、パフェ、パンケーキ、オムライス）
gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are documented. The signature tofu tiramisu and other sweets (pancakes, parfaits) typically contain wheat-based components (sponge/biscuit, flour), and savory items like omelette rice are not GF. There is no published allergen information or dedicated gluten-free preparation, so a celiac traveler must ask directly and should not assume safety.
vegan_detail: Although the desserts are tofu-based and marketed as healthy, they are not described as vegan. The tiramisu is "creamy" in a mascarpone style and the menu includes items like strawberry milk and omelette rice, indicating dairy and egg use. No vegan labeling is published, so vegans must confirm ingredients in person.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市鶴福院町25
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市鶴福院町25, within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: ならまち（鶴福院町）／近鉄奈良駅徒歩約10分
seats_text: 13 seats (5 counter, 8 table)
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly local sweets café where a foreign student can comfortably order with a few basic Japanese words and pointing at the photo menu, though staff may have limited English.
caveats: Phone number and exact reservation policy are not published; hours are subject to irregular closures, so confirm before visiting. No allergen or dietary documentation is available.

bio.background: A specialty tofu-sweets café in the historic Naramachi neighborhood, about a 10-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station and minutes from Sarusawa Pond. The Naramachi location opened in early 2023 (pre-opening in late January, with a February 1, 2023 start), joining the brand's presence that also includes shops associated with Kobe and Osaka's Nakazakicho area.
bio.specialty: Handmade tofu tiramisu served in a small wooden box, offered in flavors such as cocoa, matcha, hojicha, black sesame, mango, and a seasonal fresh-strawberry version ('Ichigo kaoru tofu tiramisu'). The menu also features parfaits, pancakes, an 'Angel Strawberry Milk' drink, and savory omelette rice.
bio.philosophy: The café centers on 'guilt-free,' health-conscious sweets made with tofu that are described as creamy and indulgent without tasting of tofu.

anecdotes.text: The tofu tiramisu is presented in a stylish wooden box, making it both photogenic and healthy, which has made it especially popular.
Local coverage framed the shop around saying goodbye to guilt by eating seasonal tiramisu made from tofu.

lead: 奈良きたまち食堂 イル・ソッリーゾ・デッラ・ファミーリア
name_ja: 奈良きたまち食堂 イル・ソッリーゾ・デッラ・ファミーリア
name_en: Nara Kitamachi Shokudo Il Sorriso della Famiglia
cuisine: Casual Italian and Japanese-style home cooking (yoshoku), tapas-style small plates
neighborhood: Kitamachi (Nabeyacho), about a 5-minute walk north of Kintetsu Nara Station
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering or accommodation is documented. The menu centers on pasta, Napolitan, fried dishes, and bread-based Italian fare, all wheat-based, with no dedicated gluten-free preparation noted. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No vegan or vegetarian menu is documented. This is an omnivore-focused casual eatery whose signature dishes feature meat, egg, and fish (Teppan Napolitan with raw egg and sausage, rotisserie proteins, chicken-skin escabeche). A vegetable-forward antipasto platter exists but is not confirmed animal-free. Vegans would need to ask directly, and options are likely very limited.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市鍋屋町2-1 シティーホームズ女子大前 203
geocode_note: Geocoded to chō-level (Nabeyacho 2-1) via GSI; within the Nara central box.
seats_text: 14 seats: counter seating plus two tables in a bright dining room.
closed_days: 不定休 (irregular closures)
cultural_comfort_note: A small, friendly local spot where a foreign student will be welcomed, but menus and conversation are likely Japanese-only, so a translation app helps; reservations are taken by phone or Instagram DM.

bio.background: A cozy neighborhood eatery tucked on the second floor of a small building in Nara's Kitamachi quarter, a short walk north of the city center near Nara Women's University. Its Italian name, Il Sorriso della Famiglia, means "the family's smile," and the place lives up to it as a relaxed, hidden hangout where casual Italian cooking meets Japanese home-style dishes at everyday prices.
bio.specialty: The signature Teppan Napolitan arrives sizzling on a hot iron plate with tomato sauce, sausage, and a raw egg stirred through. Other favorites include a small-plate antipasto platter (miso-glazed fried eggplant, chicken-skin escabeche, pickled daikon, mushroom peperoncino) and a customizable Famiglia bento with rotisserie proteins and sides.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen describes itself as an approachable eatery serving Italian food and a range of genres at accessible prices, built for everything from a casual lunch with a drink to an easygoing dinner rather than formal fine dining.

anecdotes.text: Happy-hour pricing and inexpensive lunch sets (a daily special with two drinks around 1,000 yen) make it an easy, welcoming stop for locals and students alike.

caveats: Hours reconciled from Tabelog (detailed split lunch/dinner and weekend hours, 不定休); one local guide listed "closed Mondays," but Tabelog's irregular-closure listing was treated as more current. No official website found; reservations via phone (090-5364-7567) or Instagram DM. No documented vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free, or allergy-handling information.

lead: 大和自家焙煎 桜座珈琲
name_ja: 大和自家焙煎 桜座珈琲
cuisine: Self-roasting specialty coffee shop and café (beans roasted daily in-house, brewed cup by cup; also sells beans by weight)
gf_detail: This is a coffee shop, not a kitchen geared to dietary needs. Black coffee itself is naturally gluten-free, but the food offered (hot dogs) is wheat-based bread, and no gluten-free food or celiac-safe handling is documented. Treat it as a place for a drink rather than a safe gluten-free meal.
vegan_detail: Black coffee is the core offering and is plant-based, but no plant milk or vegan menu is documented; food items such as the hot dog are not vegan. Suitable only if you stick to black coffee; confirm milk options on site.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市恋の窪2-1-8 徳丸ビル103
geocode_note: GSI geocoded to 奈良県奈良市恋の窪二丁目1番8号 at lat 34.67524, lng 135.807816 — within the central Nara box.
neighborhood: 恋の窪／新大宮エリア（佐保川桜並木近く）
seats_text: Small: roughly 7-8 seats (counter plus a four-person and a two-person table per reviews); a few seats only.
hours_raw: 10:00–19:00. Closed Mondays (also closed during Obon, year-end/New Year, and some holidays).
closed_days: Mondays (plus Obon, year-end/New Year, and some holidays)
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run neighborhood roastery where Japanese is the working language; a friendly point-and-smile spot for a coffee, but do not expect English menus or service.
caveats: Owner's given-name reading is uncertain: the official site lists the roaster as 白井 祥雅 (Shirai), while a local magazine profile romanized the first name differently and gave his age as 69 — the surname Shirai is consistent. Seat count is approximate, drawn from customer reviews rather than an official figure.

bio.background: The shop is run by Mr. Shirai, who took up coffee roasting after retiring and became absorbed in its depth and complexity, eventually opening this self-roasting coffee shop in the quiet Koi-no-kubo residential district of Nara.
bio.specialty: Self-roasted specialty coffee, with a signature 'Sakura Blend' that is balanced with fruity notes and named for the Saho River cherry blossoms nearby; beans are roasted daily in-house on a 1kg roaster and sold by weight, and food includes a hot dog.
bio.philosophy: To help each customer find the coffee truly suited to their taste and to enjoy fresh, aromatic specialty coffee, while creating a relaxed gathering place for the neighborhood. Green beans are checked before roasting and hand-picked afterward for quality.

anecdotes.text: First-time visitors are offered a complimentary tasting, and buying 200g or more of beans comes with a free cup of coffee — small touches reflecting the shop's wish to be welcoming.
The shop sits near the cherry-tree-lined banks of the Saho River, a peaceful setting that inspired its 'Sakura' blend.

positives.text: Black self-roasted coffee is the focus, brewed cup by cup, so a plain cup of coffee is naturally free of gluten and animal products.

cuisine: Edo-style unagi (charcoal-grilled steamed eel); eel specialist
neighborhood: Takabatake / in front of Kasuga Taisha first torii gate, within the Kikusuiro property
gf_detail: Not gluten-free. The signature unagi is grilled and finished with a tare made from wheat-based soy sauce (Tenno Shoyu) and mirin, so the core dish contains gluten. No dedicated gluten-free menu, preparation, or accommodation is documented. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: Not suitable for vegans or vegetarians. This is a dedicated freshwater-eel (unagi) specialist; the entire menu centers on grilled live eel. No plant-based dishes are documented.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1130 菊水楼
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市高畑町1130 returned [135.83371, 34.680328], within the Nara bounding box and consistent with the Kasuga Taisha first-torii / Kikusuiro location.
seats_text: 58 seats total (1st floor 28, 2nd floor 30)
hours_raw: Lunch 11:00-14:45 (last entry), Dinner 17:00-19:00 (last entry); last order around 18:30. Closed Tuesdays. Year-end/New Year closures and a modified New Year's menu apply.
closed_days: Tuesdays (plus year-end/New Year period)
cultural_comfort_note: An upscale eel restaurant inside a historic ryotei property where reservations and basic Japanese dining etiquette help; a foreign student can manage with a polite "konnichiwa," a reservation, and a translation app, but it is a special-occasion splurge rather than a casual drop-in.
caveats: Dinner runs roughly 6,000-8,000 yen and lunch around 3,000-4,000 yen per person; reservations are recommended/required. Eel-only menu with wheat-based tare, so unsuitable for vegans, vegetarians, and celiacs.

bio.background: Unakiku is a dedicated eel restaurant born from Kikusuiro, a celebrated ryotei founded in 1891 (Meiji 24) and now a registered tangible cultural property, set directly in front of the first torii gate of Kasuga Taisha shrine. For more than 130 years the house has welcomed distinguished guests, and Unakiku carries that hospitality into a focused eel specialty.
bio.specialty: Edo-style unagi: live eels are filleted to order, white-grilled, thoroughly steamed, then charcoal-grilled with the house tare. Signature dishes include the classic unagi-don and the ainose-don, which pairs plain white-grilled eel (shirayaki) with tare-glazed eel in one bowl. The tare blends pure additive-free Tenno Shoyu soy sauce (brewed with Fuji spring water) and traditional hon-mirin, and the rice is exclusively Niigata Uonuma Koshihikari grown for the restaurant by a partner farmer.
bio.philosophy: The restaurant frames eating eel as part of a pilgrimage ritual, building on the local custom of enjoying unagi after visiting Kasuga Taisha, and aims to connect people through eel cuisine.

anecdotes.text: Unakiku grew out of the long-standing local custom of eating eel after worshipping at Kasuga Taisha, positioning itself in front of the shrine's first torii gate as part of Nara's food-culture pilgrimage experience.
Its parent house, Kikusuiro, has served eel cuisine for over 130 years and is a registered tangible cultural property dating to 1891.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: The signature tare is made by blending pure Tenno Shoyu soy sauce with hon-mirin; standard Japanese soy sauce is brewed with wheat, so the eel glaze contains gluten.

vegan_cross_contact.text: This is a dedicated eel restaurant; effectively every dish is built around grilled freshwater eel, so there is no plant-based mainline and shared grill/tare make it unsuitable for vegans.

lead: オーセンティックテーブル タバーン（AUTHENTIC TABLE TAVERN）
name_ja: オーセンティックテーブル タバーン
cuisine: Yoshoku / casual French (Western)
neighborhood: 下三条町 (Shimosanjo-cho), on Sanjo-dori near Kintetsu Nara & JR Nara stations
gf_detail: No gluten-free program is documented. This is a casual French/yoshoku restaurant whose menu includes sauces, homemade ham, soup curry and charcoal-grilled dishes; flour-based sauces and wheat are common in this cuisine, so it is not celiac-safe without direct confirmation. A celiac diner must ask the kitchen about every dish.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. The kitchen does source seasonal Yamato vegetables from Nara and grills vegetables such as broccoli, but the menu centers on charcoal-grilled meats, fresh fish, homemade Yamato-pork ham and dairy-based French preparations. There may be vegetable side dishes, but no confirmed plant-only mains; a vegan diner should call ahead and ask.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市下三条町24
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市下三条町24, within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 30 seats total (2 counter, 28 table); a private room for up to 8 is available at no extra charge.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Thu, Sun: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) & 17:30-21:00 (L.O. 20:30); Fri, Sat & day before public holiday: 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:30) & 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 21:30). Closed Wednesday (subject to change).
closed_days: Wednesday (subject to change)
cultural_comfort_note: A multilingual (English) menu is available and the staff are used to guests, so a foreign student can order comfortably; web and phone reservations are accepted.
caveats: Lunch runs roughly 1,000-2,000 yen per person while dinner is course-style around 4,000-5,000+ yen, so a budget-conscious student is best off visiting at lunch. Credit cards and cash are accepted, but electronic money and QR-code payments are not.

bio.background: A casual French dining room set on Sanjo-dori, the historic street running between Nara Station and Kasuga Taisha Shrine, in a warm interior of natural-wood tones meant for relaxed special occasions.
bio.specialty: Charcoal-grilled cooking is the heart of the kitchen, built around carefully chosen ingredients from Nara and Hokkaido. Signature plates include charcoal-grilled steak and vegetables, fresh charcoal-grilled fish, a house specialty soup curry, and homemade ham made from Yamato pork, all paired with an extensive sommelier-curated wine list.
bio.philosophy: The kitchen aims to honor the natural flavor of its ingredients, sourcing seasonal Yamato vegetables and premium produce and using special sauces and spices only as refined accents rather than to mask the food.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant hosts wine-pairing evenings and occasional live guitar performances, doubling as a relaxed evening venue.

positives.text: Sources seasonal Yamato vegetables from Nara and offers charcoal-grilled vegetable dishes such as broccoli, so some plant-forward options exist on the menu.

cuisine: Tofu sweets and dessert cafe (甘味カフェ) — tofu-based tiramisu and parfaits
neighborhood: Tsurufukuin-cho, Naramachi (about a 10-minute walk from Kintetsu Nara Station; 5 minutes from Sarusawa Pond)
gf_detail: No gluten-free claims are made anywhere. The signature tiramisu involves layered cake/cocoa components and the cafe also serves pancakes and omurice, so wheat is present and there is no celiac-safe handling documented. Not suitable for strict gluten avoidance.
vegan_detail: The cafe is built around tofu as a healthy base, which can read as plant-friendly, but the signature "tiramisu" is a dairy-style cream dessert and drinks like "Angel Strawberry Milk" and "Heaven's Milk" (condensed milk) clearly contain dairy. No item is documented as vegan and the tofu desserts are not confirmed dairy-free, so a vegan diner would need to ask directly about each item.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市鶴福院町25
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI to the chō address 奈良県奈良市鶴福院町25; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: About 13 seats (roughly 5 counter and 8 table); small cafe, sources also describe it as "approximately 12 seats"
hours_raw: 11:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:00). Irregular holidays (不定休). During the early 2023 soft-opening period operation was weekends-only; after the February 2023 grand opening sources list daily operation, though some listings still note primarily weekend operation — confirm same-day via Instagram.
closed_days: Irregular holidays (不定休) — no fixed closed day
cultural_comfort_note: A small, casual, photogenic dessert cafe in Naramachi where a foreign student can order easily by pointing at the picture menu, though staff communication is mainly in Japanese so a few basic phrases help.
caveats: Hours and open-days have shifted since opening: it began as weekends-only during the early-2023 soft opening and some listings still flag primarily weekend operation, while others list daily 11:00-19:00. The closed schedule is irregular, so check the cafe's Instagram before visiting. Seat count is small (~12-13), so waits are possible on busy days.

bio.background: KAYA cafe is a small group of tofu-sweets cafes that built its reputation at locations in Osaka's Nakazaki-cho and Kobe's Motomachi before opening this Naramachi branch. The Nara shop did a soft opening in late 2022 / January 2023 and held its grand opening in early 2023, settling into a tucked-away corner of the old townscape near Sarusawa Pond.
bio.philosophy: The whole concept is 'healthy yet creamy and delicious' — using tofu as the base for indulgent-tasting desserts so guests can enjoy sweets with less guilt.
bio.specialty: The signature is a handmade tofu tiramisu served in a wooden masu box, offered in flavors such as strawberry, cocoa, matcha, hojicha, black sesame and mango with customizable toppings like mochi and seasonal fruit. The menu also includes parfaits, pancakes, a Nara-exclusive matcha tofu kan (抹茶豆腐カン), specialty omurice, and milk-forward drinks.
bio.origin: Naramachi, Nara City (branch of an Osaka/Kobe-based tofu-sweets cafe)

anecdotes.text: The shop sits in an antique-decorated space designed to fit the traditional Naramachi atmosphere, with the masu-box presentation making the tofu tiramisu a popular photo subject.
The spring version of the tofu tiramisu layers in fresh strawberries, and the desserts are designed so the tofu reads as creamy rather than 'tofu-flavored.'

positives.text: Desserts are tofu-based, which can suit diners looking for lighter, plant-forward sweets — though dairy is present in the cream and milk drinks.

name_ja: BAKERY GEN（ベーカリー ゲン）
cuisine: Bakery (handmade bread, approx. 40 varieties) run by a husband-and-wife team
neighborhood: Sakyō (Heijō New Town / near Kintetsu Takanohara Station), northern Nara City
address_ja: 〒631-0801 奈良県奈良市左京2-3-1 ローレルスクエア高の原5番館4
geocode_note: Geocoded from 奈良市左京2-3-1 via GSI: [lng 135.804504, lat 34.712563], within the loose Nara box. AREA MISMATCH: target said きたまち（近鉄奈良駅北側）but the shop is in 左京 (Sakyō), the Heijō New Town danchi area near Kintetsu Takanohara Station — far northern Nara, ~8km from central Nara, not central/Kitamachi.
seats_text: Primarily a takeout bread shop; no documented seating / dine-in counts found.
hours_raw: 9:00-18:00 (closes early when bread sells out). 定休日: 毎週月曜日・金曜日 (Mondays and Fridays).
closed_days: Mondays and Fridays (毎週月曜日・金曜日)
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood bakery in a residential danchi far from the tourist center; little to no English, so point at what you want and pay at the counter — straightforward even with no Japanese.
gf_detail: This is a conventional wheat-flour bakery; the owners blend several types of wheat flour for their breads, and signature items (cream pan, milk bread, focaccia, kouign-amann) are all wheat-based. There is no gluten-free offering and no measures against cross-contamination documented. Not suitable for celiac or gluten-avoidant diners.
vegan_detail: Breads documented include custard-cream pan (eggs/dairy), butter-rich kouign-amann, and milk bread, so dairy/eggs/butter are common. One item noted is an egg-free milk bread, but it is not described as vegan (likely still contains dairy/butter), and no vegan range is advertised. Not a vegan destination; a vegan traveler would need to ask about specific loaves and assume most contain animal products.
caveats: Real, currently-operating bakery, but the curated target's area label is incorrect: it is in 左京 (Sakyō / Heijō New Town near Kintetsu Takanohara), NOT きたまち/central Nara. Closes when the day's bread sells out, so hours can end before 18:00. No Tabelog page was located; official site and local press confirm details. Closed both Mondays and Fridays. Free 28-space parking behind the shop; access by bus from Kintetsu Takanohara Station (Sakyō 6-chōme stop) or a 15-min walk from JR Heijōyama Station.

bio.background: BAKERY GEN is a small neighborhood bakery run by a husband-and-wife team in the Sakyo residential district of northern Nara, set among the Heijo New Town housing estates. The couple bake roughly 40 kinds of bread by hand each day and pride themselves on being close to their customers, often turning conversations at the counter into new products on the shelves.
bio.specialty: Their best-known item is the cream pan, filled with rich custard cream made fresh by hand every morning to pair with their dough. Other favorites include a salted potato focaccia, an egg-free fluffy milk bread, and a buttery kouign-amann.
bio.philosophy: They describe themselves as a bakery that stays close to the community, adjusting the blend of several wheat flours to suit each bread and recipe, drawing on years of accumulated technique, and developing new breads from customer feedback.

anecdotes.text: The name GEN comes from a beloved family dog the owner lived with as a child; the shop carries the wish that the family can always stay together.
The bakery is woven into the life of the surrounding Heijo-Sakyo housing estate, profiled as part of a feature on the relationship between bread shops and danchi communities.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Conventional bakery that blends multiple wheat flours for its breads; flour is used throughout, so cross-contamination is unavoidable and there is no gluten-free production.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Breads commonly use eggs, dairy, custard cream, and butter; no vegan line is advertised and shared equipment/surfaces would contact animal-derived ingredients.

cuisine: Italian cafe / creative kakigori (shaved ice)
neighborhood: Takabatake-cho, central Nara (near Nara Hotel / old Daijoin Garden area, within Kintetsu Nara Station range)
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町1028
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市高畑町1028 returned [135.836197, 34.677956], within the Nara box.
seats_text: About 11 seats (counter and table); a small intimate space, seat count may adjust for reservations.
hours_raw: Italian: 11:30- (L.O. 14:00), 18:00- (L.O. 20:30), dinner by reservation. Kakigori served irregularly (mainly summer) on days when dinner reservations are not taken; check Instagram for dates/times.
closed_days: Irregular closures (不定休); no fixed closing day. Operating days, especially for kakigori, vary — confirm via Instagram.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny owner-run hideaway where dinner is reservation-only and the kakigori schedule is posted irregularly on Instagram, so a foreign student will do best with some Japanese and a willingness to plan ahead.
gf_detail: This is an Italian kitchen built around pasta and Western dishes, so wheat is central and there is no indication of gluten-free accommodation; not suitable for celiacs. The kakigori may be naturally wheat-light but no gluten-free handling is documented.
vegan_detail: No vegan menu is documented. It is a creative Italian restaurant likely using meat, fish, dairy and eggs, and the signature kakigori use syrups, umeboshi paste and a tomato-and-sour-cream version that is not vegan. A vegan diner would need to ask directly about any plant-based options.
caveats: Hours and especially kakigori availability are irregular and announced on Instagram; dinner requires a reservation, the space is very small, and it is cash only. No official website was confirmed.

bio.background: The chef trained at prestigious Tokyo hotels and restaurants before opening this small Italian cafe in 2017 in Takabatake-cho, his wife's hometown, in a quiet corner of central Nara near the Nara Hotel.
bio.origin: Tokyo fine-dining background; settled in Nara.
bio.specialty: Creative Italian cooking using local Nara ingredients, plus a celebrated summer shaved ice. The signature is a Garden Plum Soda kakigori made from syrup pressed from the large plum tree growing on the property, layered with umeboshi paste for a sweet-tart-salty profile and a fizzing carbonated jelly at the bottom. His fine-dining background also produces savory, Italian-style kakigori such as a tomato version finished with basil and sour cream.
bio.philosophy: He frames the shaved ice in light of Nara's long history of worshipping the guardian deity of ice since the Heijo-kyo era, and treats kakigori as a dish rather than a sweet — building flavors with techniques borrowed from Western cooking. He offers it only irregularly, woven into the restaurant's schedule, valuing quality over convenience.

anecdotes.text: He wanted to help bring vitality to the neighborhood and took up shaved ice after receiving guidance from popular local kakigori shops, then developed his own approach using soup-like bases, salt and pepper alongside gentle sweetness.

The Garden Plum Soda kakigori is built from syrup made from the fruit of the large plum tree in the shop's own garden, with umeboshi paste adding a salty seasoning note said to help guard against summer heat exhaustion.

lead: お庭 シェイブアイス ミラコロ
name_ja: お庭 シェイブアイス ミラコロ
cuisine: かき氷（シェイブアイス）専門店
neighborhood: ならまち／旧大乗院庭園周辺（高畑町）
gf_detail: A shaved ice specialist where the base is ice and house-made fruit/sugar syrups, which are inherently wheat-free. However, the shop makes no documented gluten-free claim, toppings such as konpeito candy and seasonal extras have not been verified as GF, and there is no information on a gluten-free kitchen protocol. Coeliac visitors should ask directly about each topping. Not a dedicated gluten-free establishment.
vegan_detail: Many bowls rely on dairy elements such as crème d'ange (an egg/cream sauce) and milk-based toppings, so the menu is not vegan by default. Some fruit-syrup bowls (for example the salted coconut-pineapple) may be closer to plant-based, but ingredients change daily (about five varieties offered) and no vegan labelling is documented. Vegans should ask which of the day's bowls omit dairy and egg.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市高畑町974
geocode_note: GSI address search returned coordinates for 奈良県奈良市高畑町974, within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Around 9 interior seats (raw-wood and tatami, shoes off) plus 6 terrace seats facing the garden, per a published feature; small individual shop.
hours_raw: 水・木 10:00-16:00（L.O. 15:30）。他の曜日に臨時営業の日もあり。
closed_days: 月・火・金・土・日・祝（基本は水・木のみ営業、臨時営業あり）
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny shoes-off, garden-side shop run by one passionate owner with no English documented, so basic Japanese or a translation app helps for choosing among the daily bowls.
caveats: Open essentially only two days a week (Wed/Thu) with occasional irregular extra days; no reservations; daily menu rotates (about five kinds) and changes seasonally. Cards and e-money are not accepted but QR-code payment is. Confirm dietary needs in person as no GF/vegan claims are published.

bio.background: The owner left a career as a securities trader to pursue a long-held passion for kakigori, spending roughly a year of preparation before opening the shop in 2019 rather than launching before the quality met his standards.
bio.specialty: House-made shaved ice with from-scratch syrups: a salted coconut-pineapple bowl finished with Nara konpeito candy from a confectioner founded in 1861, a 'MAX Ginger / 本気の生姜' bowl built for intense ginger umami, and a seasonal 'ミラコロいちご' using Nara's premium Koto-ka (古都華) strawberries with crème d'ange.
bio.philosophy: Relentless quality control, with the owner describing himself as the harshest critic of his own shaved ice; the ice is shaved for a fluffy texture and served in vessels chosen to slow melting.

anecdotes.text: The shop sits just south of the historic Daijoin Garden in the Naramachi district, marked by a 'NARAMACHI GARDEN' sign, with a shoes-off interior of raw wood and tatami plus a small garden-facing terrace.

Opening on essentially only Wednesdays and Thursdays, the limited schedule has helped build a devoted following among local 'gōrā' (shaved-ice enthusiasts), with about five varieties shown by photo at the counter each day.

positives.text: Syrups are made in-house from fruit and sugar, and the core product is shaved ice rather than wheat- or batter-based, so several bowls may suit avoiders of gluten if toppings are checked.

name_ja: ラトリエ スリジェ
cuisine: 個人経営のパティスリー（焼き菓子・ケーキ、夏はかき氷）
neighborhood: ならまち・興福寺周辺（近鉄奈良駅圏内）
gf_detail: This is a French-style patisserie whose core products are wheat-based: cakes, galette bretonne, financiers and other baked goods all contain flour. No gluten-free items or accommodations are documented. Not suitable for celiacs. The shaved ice (kakigori) may use wheat-free syrups, but it is typically plated with cake/custard/baked elements, so cross-contact with wheat is very likely. Confirm directly before ordering if you have celiac disease.
vegan_detail: A pastry shop centered on butter, eggs, cream and dairy-rich custards (galette bretonne, financiers, fresh cakes, custard-and-cream shaved ice). No vegan items or plant-based options are documented. Not suitable for vegans.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西御門町11-5 タウンコート西御門 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from address 奈良県奈良市西御門町11-5; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: Small shop with a mix of table and counter seating accommodating both small groups and solo visitors; exact seat count not published.
hours_raw: Mon, Tue, Fri 11:00-19:00 (L.O. 18:30); Sat, Sun & holidays 11:00-18:00 (L.O. 17:30); closes early when sold out (売り切れ次第終了). Closed Wed & Thu.
closed_days: Wednesday, Thursday (may also close early when items sell out)
cultural_comfort_note: A small neighborhood sweets shop where pointing at the display case and a few polite Japanese words go a long way; staff likely speak limited English, so a translation app helps for the kakigori menu.
caveats: Baked goods and shaved ice can sell out, after which the shop closes early. No documented vegan or gluten-free options; everything is wheat- and dairy-based.

bio.background: Latelier Cerisier opened in July 2023, founded by pastry chef Masayoshi Yamaguchi, who previously honed his craft as a pâtissier at the hotel The Hilltop Terrace Nara before striking out on his own just steps from Kintetsu Nara Station.
bio.specialty: French-style baked sweets and fresh cakes — signatures include galette bretonne and financiers — and, in the warmer months, elaborate dessert-style shaved ice (kakigori). The flagship 'Puin à la Mode' shaved ice is built like a composed dessert, layering caramel and custard sauce over baked custard, house-made berry ice, and pistachio and strawberry syrups that shift in flavor as you eat.
bio.philosophy: The shop's stated aim is to make 'sweets that bring smiles and emotion,' pairing refined pâtisserie technique with careful, personal presentation.

anecdotes.text: The shop's name 'cerisier' is French for cherry tree, and cherry-themed creations — including a shaved ice using two kinds of cherries with a vanilla anglaise sauce — are part of its identity.
To counter the chill of eating shaved ice, the shop offers guests complimentary roasted barley tea.

lead: ルアン NIPPONIA HOTEL 奈良 ならまち
name_ja: ルアン NIPPONIA HOTEL 奈良 ならまち
name_en: LE UN NIPPONIA HOTEL Nara Naramachi
cuisine: フレンチ／大和野菜・大和牛・大和豚を使うイノベーティブ料理
neighborhood: ならまち
gf_detail: A French-based innovative restaurant; bread, flour, and dashi feature heavily in the cuisine, so it is not a dedicated or celiac-safe gluten-free venue. Allergies (which would include gluten/wheat) must be communicated at booking via the comments field or by phone at least 3 days in advance, as same-day accommodation is not possible. No documented dedicated gluten-free menu or fryer.
vegan_detail: A French restaurant centered on Yamato pork, Yamato beef, Yamato chicken, fresh fish, and dairy; documented signature dishes are meat- and fish-based, so this is not a vegan or vegetarian destination. No vegan/vegetarian menu is documented. Dietary requests must be made at booking or by phone at least 3 days ahead; verify directly whether a plant-based course is possible.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市西城戸町4 NIPPONIA HOTEL 奈良 ならまち
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良県奈良市西城戸町4; coordinates fall within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 16 seats (12 counter, 4 table)
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-15:00 (L.O. 14:00), Dinner 17:30-22:00 (L.O. 20:00). Tuesday is dinner only (no lunch).
closed_days: No regular full closing day documented; Tuesday lunch is closed (dinner only on Tuesday).
cultural_comfort_note: An English-language reservation site (TableCheck) and English menu descriptions exist, so a foreign student can book and dine comfortably, though this is a refined, higher-priced course restaurant rather than a casual spot.
caveats: Course-style dining at roughly 4,500-5,800 yen for lunch and around 8,000-12,500 yen for dinner, plus a 10% service charge; reservation strongly recommended and any dietary/allergy needs must be stated at least 3 days in advance. Confirm current vegan/gluten-free feasibility directly.

bio.background: LE UN occupies a converted former sake brewery storehouse of the Toyosawa Sake Brewery within NIPPONIA HOTEL Nara Naramachi, set among the Edo-to-Taisho-era townscapes of Naramachi. The dining room is an intimate counter-style space carved out of a renovated traditional house.
bio.specialty: Yamato French courses built on regional Nara ingredients: Yamato pork (served as confit and with young spring vegetables), Yamato beef, Yamato chicken, and seasonal local vegetables and fruit, alongside dishes such as steamed fresh fish with zucchini and mint, conger eel carpione, and new onion gratin. Sake lees from the on-site Toyosawa brewery are woven into the cooking, with courses designed to pair with local Japanese sake.
bio.philosophy: A local-production, local-consumption ethos: French technique used to draw out the true flavor of Nara's land and its traditional Yamato ingredients, with deliberate sake pairing.
roles: A Swiss-trained head chef leads the kitchen, described as bringing technique and refined sensibility.

anecdotes.text: The restaurant and hotel are housed in the renovated storehouses and former residence of the Toyosawa Sake Brewery, and the kitchen incorporates that brewery's sake lees into its dishes.

staff_allergy_handling.text: Guests with allergies must inform the restaurant either in the comments field at the time of online booking or by phone at least 3 days in advance; due to ingredient preparation, same-day accommodation is not possible.

positives.text: The restaurant has a stated process for handling allergies when notified at least 3 days ahead of the reservation.

name_ja: 奈良めし 板焚屋 三条店
cuisine: Nara-cuisine izakaya
neighborhood: Sanjo-dori / near Kintetsu Nara Station
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市下三条町44-3
geocode_note: GSI geocode of 奈良市下三条町44-3 returned [135.824203, 34.682201], within the central Nara box; matches the Sanjo-dori location near Kintetsu Nara Station.
seats_text: Tabelog and Gnavi list 64 seats (tatami, sunken-kotatsu, and counter; private sunken-kotatsu rooms for groups of 3-12); one aggregator lists 85 total/40-person private capacity, so total capacity may vary.
hours_raw: Sources differ. Tabelog: Mon-Fri 17:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30), Sat/Sun/holidays also lunch 11:30-14:00 plus 17:00-24:00; Gnavi: daily 17:00-24:00 (L.O. 23:30); one aggregator: Tue-Sun 17:00-23:00, closed Mon. Closed days reported inconsistently (年中無休 on Tabelog vs Monday elsewhere).
closed_days: Sources conflict — Tabelog lists open year-round (年中無休) while another listing shows closed Mondays plus an annual summer break (around Aug 19-21). Confirm directly before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: An English (and Chinese) menu is available and the place is used to both tourists and locals, so a foreign student can order comfortably even with limited Japanese.
gf_detail: No gluten-free program or labeling found on any page. This is a soy-sauce, sake, and tempura-heavy izakaya (fried items, grilled skewers with tare, simmered dishes), so wheat exposure is pervasive and it is not suitable for celiacs. No allergy or gluten information is published.
vegan_detail: A meat-and-fish izakaya centered on Yamato beef, pork, and chicken. It does feature Yamato heirloom vegetables and vegetable tempura, but no dedicated vegan menu exists and dashi/soy seasonings make most dishes non-vegan. A flexible vegan might find a few plain vegetable plates but should ask in advance; no vegan info is published.
caveats: Seats, hours, and closed days are reported inconsistently across listings; confirm directly. No gluten-free or vegan information is published anywhere. Operated by a small Nara restaurant group (株式会社イング) rather than being a solo/family-run mom-and-pop, hence categorized OMNI.

bio.background: Itadakiya sits on Sanjo-dori, the historic approach toward Kasuga Taisha that has served pilgrims into Nara for centuries, a few minutes' walk from Kintetsu Nara Station. It is run by Ing, a Nara-based restaurant group, under the easygoing concept of a 'teishoku eatery where you can also drink' — comfortable enough for a quiet dinner or a group banquet.
bio.specialty: Creative 'Nara-meshi' cooking that puts local Yamato ingredients front and center: Yamato chicken and Yamato pork grilled as skewers and over the robata, Yamato heirloom vegetables in tempura, clay-pot rice, and regional dishes like Asuka-nabe (a milk-based hot pot). A signature is a green-tea-broth shabu hot pot with Yamato pork, paired with sake brought in from Nara-area breweries.
bio.philosophy: Showcasing Nara's terroir — the kitchen selects vegetables daily and leans on nationally known Yamato heirloom varieties alongside local meats and sake, so a meal doubles as a tour of regional produce.

anecdotes.text: The house describes its food as creative cooking that makes the most of Nara ingredients, sourcing fresh local vegetables daily and building dishes around Yamato heirloom vegetables, Yamato chicken, and Yamato pork.
The owner's recommended course pairs the regional ingredients with two hours of free-flowing local sake, positioned as the kitchen's confidence pick for both quantity and quality.

gf_cross_contamination.text: No gluten-free handling or fryer information is published; the menu is built around soy-sauce-based grilling, tempura, and simmered dishes, so wheat cross-contact should be assumed throughout.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Yakitori/grilled and simmered izakaya dishes typically use standard wheat-based soy sauce and tare; no gluten-free seasoning is indicated.

vegan_cross_contact.text: Dishes are prepared in a meat-and-fish kitchen and most use dashi or soy seasoning, so vegetable items are not reliably vegan; no separation is documented.

positives.text: An English menu is available, making it easier for a non-Japanese-speaking diner to identify and ask about ingredients.

cuisine: 和の茶店・甘味処（自家焙煎コーヒー、大和茶、注文後に焼く自家製どら焼き・団子）
neighborhood: きたまち（奈良市押上町・近鉄奈良駅北側）
gf_detail: No gluten-free offering. The signature items are wheat-based: house dorayaki (made from wheat-flour pancake batter) and freshly baked toast. Dango are rice-based but are served with mitarashi/sweet tare typically built on wheat-containing soy sauce, and there is no documented gluten-free or allergy program. Not suitable for celiacs.
vegan_detail: No documented vegan menu. Drinks include Yamato tea (matcha/sencha/houjicha), specialty coffee, and non-alcoholic mocktails, several of which may be plant-based, and lattes can presumably be made with plant milk if available, but this is unconfirmed. Sweets such as dorayaki and dango commonly contain egg/dairy and the tare may contain non-vegan elements, so a vegan visitor must ask item by item. No vegan claims are made by the shop.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市押上町28-1
geocode_note: GSI address search returned [135.8349, 34.688503] for 奈良市押上町28; within the Nara box.
seats_text: Two floors: a modern ground floor and a tatami-mat second floor overlooking nearby shrines; exact seat count not documented.
hours_raw: Sun-Thu: around midday to 17:00 (last order 16:30); Fri-Sat: around midday to 21:00 (last order 20:30), with Nara wine and alcoholic drinks served on Fri/Sat nights. Opening time was still being finalized at the time of the April 2026 report.
closed_days: Irregular holidays (定休日不定休). Note: the weekly grid lists Monday as closed as a placeholder; the shop's actual closures are irregular — confirm before visiting.
cultural_comfort_note: A calm, design-forward Japanese tea cafe in the quiet Kitamachi district; a foreign student would be comfortable but should expect a Japanese-language menu and may want a translation app or a Japanese-speaking companion to navigate the tea and sweets options.
caveats: Opened spring 2026; very new, so hours were still being adjusted and no Tabelog/HappyCow listing exists yet. No documented allergen, gluten-free, or vegan program. Hours and irregular closures should be confirmed directly before visiting.

bio.background: 天迦久茶寮 opened in spring 2026 in a preserved merchant-house setting in Nara's historic Kitamachi quarter, on the old Kyoto highway just north of Kintetsu Nara Station near Todai-ji's Tegai Gate and Kaidan-in. It is the latest venue from the Nara coffee-and-tea group behind Amenokaku Coffee, conceived as a grown-up retreat where guests slow down over carefully chosen drinks and sweets in a tranquil Japanese space.
bio.specialty: Self-roasted specialty coffee and Yamato tea (matcha, sencha, houjicha) served alongside house-made wagashi and Western sweets baked to order, including the signature dorayaki and dango, plus freshly baked toast. Hot tea is poured into French cups and coffee and lattes are served in Akahada-yaki (Nara pottery) bowls with Nara motifs.
bio.philosophy: A tea house where adults can relax in a Japanese space, pairing locally rooted ingredients and craft with an unhurried, considered hospitality.

anecdotes.text: The interior spreads over two floors, with a modern ground floor and a tatami-mat second floor that looks out over neighboring shrines, while a small tsubo-niwa courtyard garden is tended by a landscaper who also maintains gardens at Kyoto's Taizo-in.
On Friday and Saturday evenings the cafe extends its hours and serves Nara wine and other alcoholic drinks, shifting from a daytime tea stop to an evening lounge.

gf_cross_contamination.text: Signature dorayaki are made from wheat-flour batter and toast is baked on-site, so wheat is handled throughout the kitchen; no gluten-free preparation or separation is documented.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Dango are typically served with mitarashi/sweet tare based on Japanese soy sauce, which usually contains wheat; the shop does not document wheat-free tare.

positives.text: Drinks lean toward tea and coffee, including Yamato tea and non-alcoholic mocktails, giving plant-based visitors some beverage options to ask about.

lead: おはなさんのおむすび屋さん
name_ja: おはなさんのおむすび屋さん
cuisine: Onigiri (rice balls) and breakfast set meals; morning-only specialist
neighborhood: Naramachi (Higashi-jōdo-chō)
gf_detail: Not gluten-free or celiac-safe. The single set meal includes miso soup (most miso/dashi seasonings are not declared gluten-free) plus grilled fish and side dishes; takeout items include tsukudani and seasoned kombu that typically use wheat-based soy sauce. While plain salted onigiri rice is naturally gluten-free, there is no gluten-free protocol, no documented separate prep, and the kitchen is not set up for celiac needs. No published allergen handling.
vegan_detail: Not a vegetarian/vegan destination. The one set (はな結び定食) comes with grilled fish, and several onigiri fillings are fish-based (salmon, mentaiko). The morning miso soup is made with dashi (not specified as kombu-only), so it is likely not vegan. A vegan could potentially eat plain rice onigiri or homemade kombu onigiri à la carte, but this is not confirmed as animal-free and would need to be asked about in person. There is no documented vegan menu.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市東城戸町32 無人書店ふうせんかずら内
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 奈良県奈良市東城戸町32; first feature coordinates [135.827255, 34.679142], within the central Nara box.
seats_text: 8 counter seats inside the bookstore, set behind the bookshelves overlooking an interior courtyard
hours_raw: 水・木・金・土曜日、時々日曜 7:30-10:00（ラストオーダー9:30）、テイクアウトは12:00頃まで
closed_days: Sunday–Tuesday (occasionally open Sunday); morning-only operation
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny solo-run morning counter inside a Japanese bookstore where little to no English is likely, so a few Japanese phrases and patience will make for a smooth, warm visit.

bio.background: Originally from Wajima in Ishikawa Prefecture, the owner known as Ohana-san worked as a sales representative in Osaka before moving to Nara in 2013 out of deep affection for the city. She spent about seven years working at a traditional Japanese restaurant in the Gakuen-mae area, then was invited to take over the unmanned bookstore Fuusenkazura, where she opened her onigiri shop in its kitchen in December 2020.
bio.specialty: A single set meal, the Hana-musubi teishoku (around 700 yen): two freshly hand-formed onigiri using Koshihikari rice from the Yamato Highlands, plus grilled fish, small side dishes, and miso soup. Fillings rotate among several options including homemade kombu, salmon, mentaiko, and pickled-greens combinations; takeout onigiri and homemade kombu/tsukudani are also sold.
bio.philosophy: Everything is made carefully, one item at a time. The onigiri are formed gently so the grains loosen easily on the tongue, and the miso soup is made fresh each morning from freshly drawn dashi, aiming for a simple, gentle, refined plate.

anecdotes.text: She moved to Nara in 2013 simply because she loved the city so much, after a career in Osaka sales and seven years at a Japanese restaurant in Gakuen-mae.
She opened the onigiri shop in December 2020 inside the kitchen of the unmanned bookstore Fuusenkazura after being invited to become its proprietor.
Each onigiri is served freshly made to order, one at a time, with the second piece brought out after an interval so it is eaten warm.
The miso soup is made every morning from freshly taken dashi, and the set is praised as a simple yet gentle and refined one-plate breakfast.

soy_sauce_wheat.text: Takeout offerings include homemade kombu and tsukudani, which are typically simmered in wheat-based soy sauce; soy sauce content is not declared gluten-free.

vegan_cross_contact.text: The set comes with grilled fish and the miso soup is made with morning dashi (not specified as kombu-only), so vegan separation is not assured.

positives.text: Rice is the focus: Koshihikari from the Yamato Highlands, with onigiri formed gently to order, so plain salted or kombu onigiri may be requestable à la carte.

lead: おむすび処 和が家
name_ja: おむすび処 和が家
cuisine: Onigiri (hand-formed rice balls) specialist serving a one-soup, three-side (一汁三菜) set; solo-run neighborhood shop using Hokkaido Yumepirika rice
gf_detail: Plain rice balls (especially the shio-musubi / salted onigiri) are naturally based on rice, but this is not a gluten-free destination and there is no documented allergen handling. The signature 一汁三菜 set is built around miso soup and assorted side dishes; miso typically contains barley or wheat, the soup uses dashi, and side dishes and certain fillings can contain wheat-based soy sauce. A celiac traveler would need to ask in detail, and no dedicated gluten-free practices are documented.
vegan_detail: Not a vegan destination. Several fillings are animal-based (salmon, etc.) and the popular sets include miso soup that almost certainly uses fish dashi, plus a rolled-omelet (dashimaki) option made with egg. A few items such as the plain salted rice ball or umboshi (plum) onigiri may be plant-based, but vegan suitability is not documented and would need to be confirmed directly.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市芝辻町11-43 シティホームズ奈良 1F
geocode_note: Geocoded via GSI from 奈良県奈良市芝辻町11-43; result [135.820557, 34.68544] falls within the central Nara box and matches the Funabashi shopping street / Shibatsuji-cho location.
neighborhood: Funabashi shopping street / Shibatsuji-cho, about a 7-minute walk from JR Nara and Kintetsu Nara stations
seats_text: Intimate space with 6 counter seats plus a raised tatami-style area seating about 7 (stocked with toys and picture books for young children), roughly 13 seats total.
hours_raw: 9:00–15:00 (L.O. 14:30, closes when sold out)
closed_days: Closed Wednesday, Thursday and Friday (open on public holidays). Note: closed-day listings vary slightly by source.
cultural_comfort_note: A tiny, warm solo-run neighborhood shop where ordering is simple but interaction is in Japanese, so a few basic phrases will help a foreign student feel at ease.
caveats: Hours are 9:00–15:00 and the shop closes early once items sell out, so arrive earlier rather than later. Cash only. Closed Wednesday through Friday per the most detailed local article, though closed-day information differs across listings, so confirm via the shop's Instagram before visiting.

bio.background: A small, hand-made-onigiri shop that opened in autumn 2023 on Nara's retro Funabashi shopping street. It is run single-handedly by a young proprietor who first opened the shop while still a fourth-year university student and has since graduated holding childcare and kindergarten-teaching qualifications, with an aspiration to one day work in child- and family-support services. The shop name Wagaya draws on the owner's own name and carries the wish that guests will treat the place like their own home.
bio.specialty: Hand-formed rice balls made from carefully chosen Hokkaido-grown Yumepirika rice, prized for its natural sweetness and for keeping its flavor even after it cools. The signature 一汁三菜 (one-soup, three-side) set pairs two rice balls of your choice with a miso soup and three small side dishes, and there is an equally popular set that swaps in a fluffy rolled-omelet (dashimaki). Roughly ten regular fillings rotate with the seasons, alongside a simple salted rice ball.
bio.philosophy: The owner chose this calm corner of the old shopping street specifically to give guests a peaceful, restful experience, reflecting the neighborhood spirit of being kind to both heart and body; a small raised seating area stocked with toys and picture books welcomes families with young children.

anecdotes.text: The owner tasted many rice varieties before settling on Hokkaido Yumepirika because its grains stay sweet and flavorful even once cooled, making it ideal for both dine-in and takeout rice balls.
Within months of opening, 30 to 40 percent of customers had become repeat visitors, a sign of how the cozy, home-like atmosphere resonated with locals.
The shop opened while its proprietor was still a university student, who has since graduated with childcare and kindergarten-teaching licenses and hopes to move into child and family support work.

positives.text: The owner is selective about ingredients, choosing a specific Hokkaido rice variety along with particular salt and miso, and works alone, so it is feasible to ask directly about how a dish is made.

cuisine: 日本料理・懐石（季節の和食、大和野菜・奈良の地酒）
gf_detail: A traditional Japanese kaiseki restaurant. Course menus rely heavily on soy sauce and dashi (typically fish-based), and dishes commonly include wheat-containing seasonings and items, so it is not a celiac-safe environment without direct confirmation. The hotel publishes an allergen-information document, so it is best to call ahead (0742-35-5819) and discuss gluten needs in advance.
vegan_detail: This is an omnivore kaiseki restaurant; most courses feature fish, fish-based dashi, and other animal products. While Yamato/Nara vegetables and Nara-pickle (奈良漬) feature in the cooking, no documented vegan menu exists. Vegetarian/vegan needs should be arranged in advance by phone, ideally when reserving.
address_ja: 奈良県奈良市三条本町8-1 ホテル日航奈良 3F
geocode_note: Geocoded from address_ja via GSI to chō-level (三条本町8番); coordinates fall within the central Nara box. Restaurant is on the 3rd floor of Hotel Nikko Nara, directly connected to JR Nara Station west exit.
seats_text: 64 seats total, including 3 private rooms (4–18 persons).
hours_raw: Lunch 11:30-14:30 / Dinner 17:30-19:30 (L.O.); closed Mondays.
closed_days: Mondays (月曜定休)
cultural_comfort_note: As a polished hotel restaurant directly connected to JR Nara Station, staff are used to guests and reservations can be made online, though a few words of Japanese and an advance call about any dietary needs will make the visit smoother.

bio.origin: Central Nara, on the third floor of the hotel that sits directly at the west exit of JR Nara Station.
bio.background: A full Japanese-cuisine dining room serving seasonal washoku and kaiseki built around carefully chosen ingredients. The kitchen leans into the local larder, featuring Yamato/Nara vegetables, Nara-pickle (nara-zuke), and the regional rice and produce that define Nara's table.
bio.specialty: Seasonal kaiseki and course menus that change with the four seasons, paired with a curated flight of Nara sake, offered as a three-type tasting up to a six-type selection in a nod to Nara being regarded as a birthplace of sake brewing.
bio.philosophy: The house describes its cooking as dishes woven from carefully examined, dependable ingredients and a spirit of hospitality that spares no effort (吟味した確かな食材と、手間を惜しまぬおもてなしの心で綴る逸品).

anecdotes.text: Diners come to experience the four seasons through Japanese cuisine, with monthly-changing courses and a tasting line-up of Nara local sake.

staff_allergy_handling.text: The hotel provides an allergen-information document, indicating that allergy inquiries are handled, though specific gluten/vegan handling details were not published.
